topics.title: Thermodynamics of Refrigeration: Carnot
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT523 - AUTOMOTIVE REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Thermodynamics of Refrigeration: Carnot

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1 – Thermodynamics of Refrigeration: Carnot Cycle

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Carnot Refrigeration Cycle (Reversed Carnot Cycle):**
> A *theoretical, ideal, completely reversible* thermodynamic cycle operating between two thermal reservoirs at constant temperatures $T_H$ (hot reservoir / condenser side) and $T_L$ (cold reservoir / evaporator side). It consists of **two isothermal processes** and **two isentropic (reversible adiabatic) processes**, and serves as the **upper benchmark** against which the performance of all *real* refrigeration cycles is measured.

In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme (Course Code: **PEAUT523 – Automotive Refrigeration and Air Conditioning**), the Carnot refrigeration cycle is the *reversed* Carnot power cycle. Because a refrigerator consumes work input ($W_{in}$) to pump heat $Q_L$ from a low-temperature region to a high-temperature region, the standard Carnot heat engine is operated in reverse.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – "The Sisyphus of Heat"

Imagine a bucket of water on a hill. A person (heat engine) lets the water fall, generating energy. Now reverse the process: imagine a person **carrying** a bucket of cold water *up* a hill (from $T_L$ to $T_H$). 

- The person must **spend energy** (work input $W_{in}$) to lift the water.
- The cold water absorbed at the bottom = $Q_L$ (refrigeration effect).
- The warm water dumped at the top = $Q_H$ (heat rejection at condenser).
- The Carnot cycle represents the **most efficient** way to do this — no energy wasted on friction, no water spilled (no entropy created).

### 1.3 The Four Cardinal States

> [!NOTE]
> The Carnot refrigeration cycle has four ideal processes. Memorize them — they are the *foundation* of every refrigeration analysis you will perform in KTU examinations.

| State | Process Entry | Process Exit | Physical Description |
|:-----:|:-------------:|:------------:|:---------------------|
| 1 | Evaporator exit | Compressor inlet | **Saturated vapor** at $T_L$ |
| 2 | Compressor exit | Condenser inlet | **Superheated vapor** at $T_H$ |
| 3 | Condenser exit | Expansion device inlet | **Saturated liquid** at $T_H$ |
| 4 | Expansion device exit | Evaporator inlet | **Two-phase mixture** at $T_L$ |

- **Temperatures:** $T_L = T_{evaporator}$ (typically **−5 °C to 5 °C** for automotive AC), $T_H = T_{condenser}$ (typically **40 °C to 55 °C**).
- **Pressures:** $P_L = P_{evaporator}$ (low side), $P_H = P_{condenser}$ (high side).
- **Universal Gas Constant:** $R_u = \mathbf{8.314 \; kJ/(kmol \cdot K)}$ — relevant when deriving relations using ideal gas assumptions (Carnot's original formulation).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown – The Four Processes

The Reversed Carnot cycle on a **Pressure–Enthalpy (p–h) / Temperature–Entropy (T–s)** diagram executes the following logic:

### Process 1 → 2 : Reversible Isentropic Compression
- The refrigerant vapor at state 1 (low $P$, low $T$) is compressed in a **frictionless, perfectly insulated compressor**.
- **Why isentropic?** No heat transfer ($Q = 0$) and reversible ($s = \text{constant}$).
- **How:** Work input $W_{comp}$ is consumed; entropy stays constant while temperature rises from $T_L$ to $T_H$.
- **Engineering reality:** Real compressors are *not* isentropic due to friction, leakage, and heat transfer — this is why real COP is *less* than Carnot COP.

### Process 2 → 3 : Reversible Isothermal Heat Rejection
- At state 2, the superheated vapor is in contact with the **hot reservoir** at $T_H$.
- Heat $Q_H$ is rejected to the surroundings at *constant* $T_H$.
- **Why isothermal?** Perfect thermal contact and infinitely slow process allow the refrigerant to remain at $T_H$ while releasing heat.
- **In p–h diagram:** A horizontal line (since temperature is constant for an ideal gas, and approximately so for real refrigerants in two-phase regions).

### Process 3 → 4 : Reversible Isentropic Expansion
- Saturated liquid at state 3 enters a **frictionless expansion device** (ideal turbine).
- Pressure drops from $P_H$ to $P_L$, temperature drops from $T_H$ to $T_L$.
- **In practice:** A throttling valve is used (isenthalpic, not isentropic) — this irreversibility is a *major* source of lost work in real systems.

### Process 4 → 1 : Reversible Isothermal Heat Absorption
- The two-phase mixture at $T_L$ absorbs heat $Q_L$ from the **cold reservoir** (the cabin/space to be cooled).
- This is the *useful refrigeration effect*.
- The fluid fully evaporates back to saturated vapor at state 1.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All temperatures in COP equations **MUST be absolute** (Kelvin or Rankine). This is a common KTU exam pitfall — students lose 2 marks for using °C.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|:-:|:---------:|:-------:|:-----:|:------|
| 1 | Refrigeration Effect | $RE = Q_L = T_L \, (s_1 - s_4)$ | kJ/kg | $s_1 = s_2$, $s_3 = s_4$ |
| 2 | Heat Rejection | $Q_H = T_H \, (s_2 - s_3)$ | kJ/kg | At constant $T_H$ |
| 3 | Net Work Input | $W_{net} = Q_H - Q_L$ | kJ/kg | From 1st Law: $\oint \delta W = \oint T \, ds$ |
| 4 | Carnot COP (Refrigerator) | $COP_{R,Carnot} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ | dimensionless | $T$ in K |
| 5 | Carnot COP (Heat Pump) | $COP_{HP,Carnot} = \dfrac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}$ | dimensionless | $T$ in K |
| 6 | Universal Relation | $COP_{HP,Carnot} = COP_{R,Carnot} + 1$ | dimensionless | Always true for ideal cycles |
| 7 | Alternate Form (Refrigerator) | $COP_{R,Carnot} = \dfrac{1}{\dfrac{T_H}{T_L} - 1}$ | dimensionless | Useful when only ratio given |
| 8 | Kelvin-Planck Consequence | $\eta_{Carnot,engine} = 1 - \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$ | dimensionless | Heat engine efficiency |
| 9 | Heat Pump to Refrigerator | $COP_{HP} = \dfrac{COP_R}{COP_R - 1}$ | dimensionless | Inverse of #6 |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Carnot COP formula is not just academic — it is the **design ceiling** for automotive AC engineers. When designing a vehicle AC system:

- **Cabin temperature target:** $T_L \approx 20 \;°C = 293 \;K$
- **Ambient (engine bay) temperature:** $T_H \approx 45 \;°C = 318 \;K$
- **Maximum possible COP:** $COP_{R,Carnot} = \frac{293}{318 - 293} = \frac{293}{25} \approx 11.72$

A real automotive R-134a system achieves a COP of only **2.0 to 3.5** — meaning the Carnot benchmark shows there is still **70%–80% theoretical improvement potential**, which drives research into ejector cycles, two-phase expansion, and variable displacement compressors.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Carnot Refrigeration COP

We will derive $COP_{R,Carnot} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ from the **First Law of Thermodynamics** and the **definition of COP for a refrigerator**.

### Step 1 – Define COP for a Refrigerator

By definition, the Coefficient of Performance of a refrigerator is the ratio of the *desired effect* (heat removed from the cold space) to the *work input* required.

$$
COP_R \;=\; \frac{\text{Desired Effect}}{\text{Work Input}} \;=\; \frac{Q_L}{W_{net,in}}
$$

This is the formal starting point — KTU examiners often award **1 mark** simply for writing this correctly.

### Step 2 – Apply Steady-Flow Energy Equation (1st Law) to the Cycle

For a complete cycle, the working fluid returns to its initial state, so $\Delta U = 0$ and $\Delta H = 0$. The **net work input** equals the difference between the heat rejected to the hot reservoir and the heat absorbed from the cold reservoir.

$$
\oint \delta Q \;=\; \oint \delta W
$$

$$
Q_H - Q_L \;=\; W_{net,in}
$$

This energy balance is the core of the First Law applied to a refrigeration cycle. It is the *second* foundational statement in any KTU derivation.

### Step 3 – Express Heat Transfers in Terms of Temperature and Entropy

For a **reversible** process, the Second Law of Thermodynamics (Clausius Equality) gives:

$$
\delta Q_{rev} \;=\; T \, ds
$$

Applying this to the two isothermal heat transfer processes (2 → 3 at $T_H$ and 4 → 1 at $T_L$):

$$
Q_H \;=\; T_H \, (s_2 - s_3)
$$

$$
Q_L \;=\; T_L \, (s_1 - s_4)
$$

Since the isentropic processes have $s_1 = s_2$ and $s_3 = s_4$, the entropy differences are equal. Let $\Delta s = s_1 - s_4 = s_2 - s_3$.

### Step 4 – Substitute into the COP Expression

We have $Q_L = T_L \cdot \Delta s$ and $Q_H = T_H \cdot \Delta s$. Substitute into $COP_R = Q_L / W_{net,in}$ where $W_{net,in} = Q_H - Q_L$:

$$
COP_R \;=\; \frac{T_L \, \Delta s}{T_H \, \Delta s - T_L \, \Delta s}
$$

### Step 5 – Simplify

The $\Delta s$ terms cancel (a non-zero quantity, since $T_H \neq T_L$ in any practical cycle):

$$
COP_R \;=\; \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;COP_{R,Carnot} \;=\; \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}\;}
$$

This is the **definitive Carnot refrigerator COP** — derive it from scratch in any KTU exam for full marks.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Carnot Heat Pump COP

By analogous logic, a heat pump's desired effect is $Q_H$ (heat delivered to the hot space, e.g., a cabin heater):

$$
COP_{HP} \;=\; \frac{Q_H}{W_{net,in}} \;=\; \frac{T_H \, \Delta s}{(T_H - T_L) \, \Delta s}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;COP_{HP,Carnot} \;=\; \frac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}\;}
$$

### Proof of the Universal Relation $COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1$

Starting from the definitions:

$$
COP_{HP} - COP_R \;=\; \frac{Q_H}{W} - \frac{Q_L}{W} \;=\; \frac{Q_H - Q_L}{W} \;=\; \frac{W}{W} \;=\; 1
$$

$$
\therefore \;COP_{HP,Carnot} \;=\; COP_{R,Carnot} + 1 \quad \blacksquare
$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Pattern)

> **Problem [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 style]:**
> A Carnot refrigerator operates between a cold reservoir at **−10 °C** and a hot reservoir at **35 °C**. Determine:
> (a) The COP of the refrigerator.
> (b) The COP of the heat pump operating between the same reservoirs.
> (c) The refrigeration effect if the heat rejection rate is **$Q_H = 60 \; kJ/min$**.

### Solution – Step by Step

**Convert to absolute temperatures (mandatory!):**

$$
T_L \;=\; -10 + 273 \;=\; 263 \;K
$$

$$
T_H \;=\; 35 + 273 \;=\; 308 \;K
$$

**Part (a) – Refrigerator COP:**

$$
COP_{R,Carnot} \;=\; \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} \;=\; \frac{263}{308 - 263} \;=\; \frac{263}{45} \;\approx\; 5.844
$$

**Part (b) – Heat Pump COP:**

$$
COP_{HP,Carnot} \;=\; \frac{T_H}{T_H - T_L} \;=\; \frac{308}{45} \;\approx\; 6.844
$$

Verification: $COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1 \;\Rightarrow\; 5.844 + 1 = 6.844$ ✓

**Part (c) – Refrigeration Effect:**

Given $Q_H = 60 \; kJ/min = 1 \; kJ/s = 1 \; kW$.

Since $COP_R = Q_L / W$ and $Q_H = Q_L + W$:

$$
Q_L \;=\; \frac{COP_R}{COP_R + 1} \times Q_H \;=\; \frac{5.844}{6.844} \times 1 \;\approx\; 0.854 \; kW
$$

Equivalently, $W = Q_H - Q_L = 1 - 0.854 = 0.146 \; kW$, and $COP_R = 0.854 / 0.146 = 5.844$ ✓.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture – Reversed Carnot Refrigeration Cycle

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **energy flow and state transitions** of the reversed Carnot cycle, illustrating the interaction between the four ideal components and the two thermal reservoirs.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[State 1: Saturated Vapor at T_L, P_L] -->|Process 1 to 2: Isentropic Compression| B[State 2: Superheated Vapor at T_H, P_H]
    B -->|Process 2 to 3: Isothermal Heat Rejection Q_H| C[State 3: Saturated Liquid at T_H, P_H]
    C -->|Process 3 to 4: Isentropic Expansion| D[State 4: Two Phase Mixture at T_L, P_L]
    D -->|Process 4 to 1: Isothermal Heat Absorption Q_L| A

    H[Hot Reservoir at T_H] -. Heat Rejection Q_H .-> B
    D -. Heat Absorption Q_L .-> L[Cold Reservoir at T_L]

    W[Work Input W_net] -->|Drives the cycle| A
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **square nodes** A, B, C, D represent the four thermodynamic states of the refrigerant.
- The **solid arrows** represent the direction of the working fluid through the cycle components (compressor → condenser → expander → evaporator).
- The **dotted arrows** show heat transfer to/from external reservoirs.
- The **bottom branch** (W) shows that work input is required to drive the cycle — this is the defining feature of a refrigerator.

## 4.2 T–s Diagram Representation (Block Schematic)

Since physical T–s plot rendering is complex, we provide a structured representation of the four states on the T–s plane, showing the rectangular shape characteristic of the Carnot cycle.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph T_s_Plane["Temperature Entropy Diagram of Reversed Carnot"]
        direction TB
        S1["State 1: High s, Low T_L — Sat. Vapor"]
        S2["State 2: High s, High T_H — Superheated Vapor"]
        S3["State 3: Low s, High T_H — Sat. Liquid"]
        S4["State 4: Low s, Low T_L — Two Phase"]
        S2 ---|Isothermal at T_H| S3
        S4 ---|Isothermal at T_L| S1
        S1 ---|Isentropic s=const| S2
        S3 ---|Isentropic s=const| S4
    end

    QL["Q_L = T_L x Delta s (Area under 4 to 1)"] --- S4
    QH["Q_H = T_H x Delta s (Area under 2 to 3)"] --- S2
    WN["W_net = Q_H minus Q_L (Rectangular Area)"] --- T_s_Plane
```

**Key Insight from the T–s Diagram:**
- The cycle traces a **rectangle** on the T–s plane (ideal case).
- **Area under 4→1 line** = $Q_L$ (refrigeration effect).
- **Area under 2→3 line** = $Q_H$ (heat rejection).
- **Area of the rectangle** = $W_{net,in} = Q_H - Q_L$.
- $COP_R = \frac{\text{Area under 4→1}}{\text{Area of rectangle}}$.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**Q:** Define the Coefficient of Performance (COP) of a refrigerator and state its relation to the COP of a heat pump operating between the same temperature limits.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **COP of a Refrigerator:** It is defined as the ratio of the desired refrigeration effect (heat extracted from the cold body) to the work input required to achieve it.
>
> $$COP_R \;=\; \frac{Q_L}{W_{net,in}}$$
>
> **COP of a Heat Pump:** It is the ratio of the desired heating effect (heat delivered to the hot body) to the work input.
>
> $$COP_{HP} \;=\; \frac{Q_H}{W_{net,in}}$$
>
> **Relation:** Since $Q_H = Q_L + W$ for a cyclic device, it follows that:
>
> $$COP_{HP} \;=\; COP_R + 1$$

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q:** Why is the Carnot cycle considered an *ideal* cycle? List any two reasons.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> 1. The Carnot cycle is composed entirely of **reversible processes** (two isothermal and two isentropic), meaning no entropy is generated within the system.
> 2. It operates between **two constant-temperature reservoirs** with no internal irreversibilities, so it establishes the **maximum possible COP** for any refrigerator or heat pump operating between the same temperatures — known as **Carnot's Theorem**.
> 3. (Optional third point) All real cycles are compared against it as a benchmark, making it the theoretical ceiling of refrigeration performance.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) – Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**Q:** A Carnot refrigerator is used to maintain a pharmaceutical cold storage at **−20 °C**. The surroundings are at **30 °C**. The refrigerator absorbs heat from the cold space at the rate of **$Q_L = 3.5 \; kW$**.

**(a)** Calculate the COP of the refrigerator and the power required to drive the compressor. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If the same device is used as a heat pump to heat a room at **30 °C** by extracting heat from the outside ambient at **20 °C**, determine the COP of the heat pump and compare it with the COP of the refrigerator. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a):** Absolute temperatures: $T_L = -20 + 273 = 253 \; K$, $T_H = 30 + 273 = 303 \; K$.

$$
COP_{R,Carnot} \;=\; \frac{253}{303 - 253} \;=\; \frac{253}{50} \;=\; 5.06
$$

Power required:

$$
W \;=\; \frac{Q_L}{COP_R} \;=\; \frac{3.5}{5.06} \;\approx\; 0.692 \; kW
$$

**Part (b):** For the heat pump, the new cold reservoir is outside at $20 \;°C = 293 \;K$ and the hot reservoir is the room at $30 \;°C = 303 \;K$.

$$
COP_{HP,Carnot} \;=\; \frac{T_H}{T_H - T_L} \;=\; \frac{303}{303 - 293} \;=\; \frac{303}{10} \;=\; 30.3
$$

Comparison: The heat pump COP (30.3) is significantly higher than the refrigerator COP (5.06) because the temperature lift (10 K) is much smaller. This is the principle behind **automotive heat pump cabin heating** — a small temperature lift yields enormous COP.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 – Alternative]

**Q:** With the help of a **block diagram**, explain the working of a Carnot refrigeration cycle. **(7 marks)**

**(a)** Derive an expression for the COP of a Carnot refrigerator in terms of the source and sink temperatures. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Carnot refrigerator operates between **−15 °C** and **40 °C**. If it produces **$Q_L = 200 \; kJ$** of refrigeration, calculate the work input and the heat rejected to the hot reservoir. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Block Diagram:** (Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes — the four states with heat/work flows.) Award **3 marks** for the diagram, **2 marks** for labeling the four processes, and **2 marks** for showing $Q_L$, $Q_H$, and $W$ directions.

**Derivation of COP:** (Refer to Section 3.1 — Steps 1 to 5.) Award full marks for the algebraic derivation culminating in $COP_R = T_L / (T_H - T_L)$.

**Part (b):** $T_L = -15 + 273 = 258 \; K$, $T_H = 40 + 273 = 313 \; K$.

$$
COP_{R,Carnot} \;=\; \frac{258}{313 - 258} \;=\; \frac{258}{55} \;\approx\; 4.691
$$

Work input:

$$
W \;=\; \frac{Q_L}{COP_R} \;=\; \frac{200}{4.691} \;\approx\; 42.63 \; kJ
$$

Heat rejected:

$$
Q_H \;=\; Q_L + W \;=\; 200 + 42.63 \;=\; 242.63 \; kJ
$$

Alternatively, using entropy:

$$
\Delta s \;=\; \frac{Q_L}{T_L} \;=\; \frac{200}{258} \;\approx\; 0.7752 \; kJ/K
$$

$$
Q_H \;=\; T_H \cdot \Delta s \;=\; 313 \times 0.7752 \;\approx\; 242.63 \; kJ \quad \checkmark
$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where KTU Students Typically Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Temperature Scale Error (–2 marks):** Using °C instead of Kelvin in the COP formula is the *single most common mistake* in KTU exams. Always convert: $T(K) = T(°C) + 273$.
> 2. **Confusing COP with Efficiency (–2 marks):** COP is **not** a percentage and **can exceed 1**. Never write "COP = 250%" — write "COP = 2.5".
> 3. **Heat Pump vs Refrigerator Mix-up (–2 marks):** For a refrigerator, $Q_L$ is the *desired* output. For a heat pump, $Q_H$ is the *desired* output. Mixing these up gives the wrong COP.
> 4. **Forgetting the Block Diagram (–3 marks):** In 7-mark descriptive questions, the block diagram of the Carnot cycle is **mandatory**. Drawing only a T–s diagram or no diagram at all costs 2–3 marks.
> 5. **Sign Convention Errors:** Heat absorbed by the refrigerant is **positive**; heat rejected is also **positive** but flows *out* of the system. State the convention explicitly.
> 6. **Reversibility assumption:** Always state that the Carnot cycle assumes *no friction, no pressure drop, and perfect heat transfer* — KTU examiners deduct marks for omitting these assumptions.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exams:**
>
> - **Carnot cycle** = Two isothermal + Two isentropic processes (memorize the sequence: 1→2 compress, 2→3 reject, 3→4 expand, 4→1 absorb).
> - **Reversed Carnot** is the benchmark for **all** refrigeration cycles — it gives the *maximum* possible COP.
> - $COP_{R,Carnot} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ — *the* most important formula in Module 1.
> - $COP_{HP,Carnot} = \dfrac{T_H}{T_H - T_L} = COP_R + 1$ — equally important for heat pump problems.
> - **Always use Kelvin** for temperatures in COP calculations.
> - **Net work** = Area of the rectangle on the T–s diagram = $Q_H - Q_L$.
> - **Refrigeration effect** = $Q_L = T_L \cdot \Delta s$ = Area under the 4→1 line on the T–s diagram.
> - **Limitations:** (i) Difficult to achieve isothermal processes in practice, (ii) Isentropic compression of wet vapor is impractical, (iii) Real compressors and expanders are not frictionless, (iv) Heat transfer across finite temperature difference is unavoidable.
> - **Real-world COP** of an automotive AC (R-134a) is typically **2.0 to 3.5**, while the Carnot COP can be **8 to 12** for the same temperature limits — highlighting the gap between theory and practice.
> - **Relation to 1st Law:** $Q_H = Q_L + W$ — the foundation energy balance for any cyclic refrigeration device.
> - **Relation to 2nd Law:** Carnot's theorem — no engine/refrigerator operating between two reservoirs can be more efficient than a reversible one — a **guaranteed 2-mark question** in every KTU exam.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reversed Carnot Cycle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAUT523 - AUTOMOTIVE REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Reversed Carnot Cycle # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Reversed Carnot Cycle** is a *theoretical, idealized* thermodynamic cycle executed in the **reverse direction** of the standard Carnot (heat engine) cycle. It serves as the **upper benchmark** of performance for **all refrigeration and heat pump systems** operating between two thermal reservoirs. The cycle comprises **four internally reversible processes**: - **Two Isothermal Processes** (constant temperature) - **Two Isentropic (Isentropic = Reversible Adiabatic) Processes** (constant entropy) In the context of automotive A/C, this represents the most efficient cooling cycle *impossible to achieve in practice*, but used as the reference standard against which real refrigerants (R-134a, R-1234yf) are compared. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bucket Relay" > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy: The Two-Story Elevator for Heat** Imagine a building where heat is an unwanted "package" that must be moved from the **cold ground floor** to the **hot top floor**: - An elevator (the **compressor**) lifts the heat-package up — but uses electricity (this is the **work input, W_in**). - At the top, the package is gently set on a tray and the tray is **cooled at constant high temperature** (this is the **isothermal heat rejection, Q_H**, in the condenser). - The empty tray is lowered down using a **frictionless pulley** (this is the **isentropic expansion**, the throttling-free expansion in an ideal turbine). - At the bottom, the tray picks up new heat-packages at **constant low temperature** (this is the **isothermal heat absorption, Q_L**, in the evaporator). The **Reversed Carnot Cycle** is the *perfectly frictionless, infinitely slow* version of this elevator — no wasted energy, no friction, no heat leaks. ## 1.3 The Four Corner States on T-s Diagram | State | Description | Typical Phase Condition (Refrigeration) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **State 1** | Saturated vapor at evaporator exit | Low pressure, low temperature, high enthalpy | | **State 2** | Superheated vapor at compressor exit | High pressure, high temperature, same entropy as 1 | | **State 3** | Saturated liquid at condenser exit | High pressure, high temperature, same entropy as 4 | | **State 4** | Two-phase mixture at turbine exit | Low pressure, low temperature, same entropy as 3 | ## 1.4 Why It Matters in Automotive A/C - Sets the **theoretical ceiling** for Coefficient of Performance (COP). - Allows engineers to evaluate **second-law efficiency** of real automotive systems. - Used to compute **maximum possible COP** for cabin cooling at, say, **T_L = 277 K (4 °C)** with ambient at **T_H = 318 K (45 °C)** under the scorching Kerala summer. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Process-by-Process Logical Breakdown The cycle is traversed in the direction **1 → 2 → 3 → 4 → 1** (clockwise on the P-h diagram, counter-clockwise on the T-s diagram). ### **Process 1 → 2 : Isentropic Compression** - **What happens:** Refrigerant vapor is compressed from low pressure (evaporator) to high pressure (condenser). - **Why:** Work must be supplied to lift the refrigerant to a temperature *higher* than the ambient, so it can reject heat. - **How (reversible):** No heat transfer across cylinder walls; entropy remains constant. - **Real-world analogy:** Pumping up a bicycle tire very slowly so the air inside doesn't heat up due to turbulence. $$s_1 = s_2$$ ### **Process 2 → 3 : Isothermal Heat Rejection (Condensation)** - **What happens:** Refrigerant, now at high pressure, releases heat to the surroundings (atmosphere + radiator fan). - **Why:** Heat flows only from hot to cold; we need the refrigerant *hotter* than ambient. - **How (reversible):** Heat is transferred through an *infinitely slow* temperature gradient. $$T_2 = T_3 = T_H \quad ; \quad \delta Q_H = T_H \cdot ds < 0$$ ### **Process 3 → 4 : Isentropic Expansion (Ideal Turbine)** - **What happens:** High-pressure liquid expands to low pressure. - **Why:** This is a *recovery* process — instead of throttling (which destroys exergy), the cycle recovers work. - **How (reversible):** Frictionless turbine; entropy constant. $$s_3 = s_4$$ ### **Process 4 → 1 : Isothermal Heat Absorption (Evaporation)** - **What happens:** Low-pressure liquid vaporizes by absorbing heat from the refrigerated space (car cabin). - **Why:** This is the *useful cooling effect* — the entire reason the A/C system exists. - **How (reversible):** Isothermal heat transfer from cabin air at T_L. $$T_4 = T_1 = T_L \quad ; \quad \delta Q_L = T_L \cdot ds > 0$$ ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > All temperatures **MUST** be in **absolute units** (Kelvin for °C input). Examiners explicitly check this — converting to Kelvin is the #1 reason students lose 1 mark. | # | Quantity | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | **COP of Refrigerator** | $COP_R = \dfrac{Q_L}{W_{net}}$ | Cooling effect per unit work | dimensionless | | 2 | **COP of Heat Pump** | $COP_{HP} = \dfrac{Q_H}{W_{net}}$ | Heating effect per unit work | dimensionless | | 3 | **Ideal COP (Reversed Carnot)** | $COP_{R,Carnot} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ | T in Kelvin | dimensionless | | 4 | **Ideal Heat Pump COP** | $COP_{HP,Carnot} = \dfrac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}$ | T in Kelvin | dimensionless | | 5 | **Relation between COPs** | $COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1$ | Always true for Carnot | dimensionless | | 6 | **Heat Absorbed (Q_L)** | $Q_L = T_L \cdot (s_1 - s_4)$ | Area under 4-1 on T-s | kJ/kg | | 7 | **Heat Rejected (Q_H)** | $Q_H = T_H \cdot (s_2 - s_3)$ | Area under 2-3 on T-s | kJ/kg | | 8 | **Net Work Input** | $W_{net} = Q_H - Q_L$ | First law applied to cycle | kJ/kg | | 9 | **Net Work (alternate)** | $W_{net} = (T_H - T_L) \cdot (s_1 - s_4)$ | Direct from T-s area | kJ/kg | | 10 | **Refrigerating Effect (RE)** | $RE = h_1 - h_4$ | Enthalpy drop during evaporation | kJ/kg | | 11 | **Second-Law Efficiency** | $\eta_{II} = \dfrac{COP_{actual}}{COP_{Carnot}}$ | Real vs. ideal | dimensionless | ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Benchmarking:** Automakers compute the *Carnot COP* and compare it with the *actual COP* of the R-134a system to report "second-law efficiency" of their A/C unit in technical white papers. - **Design Optimization:** Increasing T_L (cabin set point from 16 °C to 22 °C) significantly *boosts* COP — a key reason for "Eco-Mode" in modern EVs like Tata Nexon EV and MG ZS EV. - **Sizing Compressors:** Knowing the theoretical minimum work helps engineers select the *smallest compressor* that can still meet the cooling demand. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: COP of Reversed Carnot Refrigerator **Step 1 — Apply the First Law of Thermodynamics to the cyclic device.** For a closed system executing a cycle, the net work input equals the net heat transfer. $$W_{net,in} = Q_H - Q_L$$ **Step 2 — Express the heat transfers using the isothermal-reversible relationships.** For an isothermal reversible process on the T-s diagram, the heat transfer equals the area under the process curve. $$Q_L = \int_{4}^{1} T \cdot ds = T_L (s_1 - s_4) \quad \text{(positive, heat absorbed)}$$ $$Q_H = \int_{2}^{3} T \cdot ds = T_H (s_3 - s_2)$$ Since the cycle is rectangular on the T-s diagram, $(s_1 - s_4) = (s_2 - s_3)$, so: $$Q_H = T_H (s_1 - s_4)$$ **Step 3 — Substitute into the net work equation.** $$W_{net,in} = T_H (s_1 - s_4) - T_L (s_1 - s_4)$$ $$W_{net,in} = (T_H - T_L) (s_1 - s_4)$$ **Step 4 — Apply the definition of COP.** $$COP_R = \frac{\text{Desired Output}}{\text{Required Input}} = \frac{Q_L}{W_{net,in}}$$ $$COP_R = \frac{T_L (s_1 - s_4)}{(T_H - T_L)(s_1 - s_4)}$$ **Step 5 — Cancel the common entropy term and present the result.** $$\boxed{COP_{R,Carnot} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Physical Interpretation:** The smaller the temperature difference $(T_H - T_L)$, the larger the COP. This is why automotive A/C systems lose efficiency dramatically on hot days — the *gap* between cabin temperature and ambient temperature grows. ## 3.2 Derivation: COP of Reversed Carnot Heat Pump **Step 1 — Start from the heat pump definition.** $$COP_{HP} = \frac{Q_H}{W_{net,in}}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute the expressions derived above.** $$COP_{HP} = \frac{T_H (s_1 - s_4)}{(T_H - T_L)(s_1 - s_4)}$$ **Step 3 — Simplify.** $$\boxed{COP_{HP,Carnot} = \frac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}}$$ ## 3.3 Derivation: Linkage between COP_R and COP_HP **Step 1 — Subtract the two COPs.** $$COP_{HP} - COP_R = \frac{T_H}{T_H - T_L} - \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$$ **Step 2 — Combine over a common denominator.** $$COP_{HP} - COP_R = \frac{T_H - T_L}{T_H - T_L}$$ **Step 3 — Conclude the elegant identity.** $$\boxed{COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This relationship is a **favourite KTU question** in Part A. The +1 represents the *extra* heating effect because in a heat pump, the work input itself is eventually degraded to heat and added to the cabin. ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) > **Problem:** A car cabin is to be maintained at **5 °C** when the outside ambient is **40 °C**. The system operates on a reversed Carnot cycle and absorbs **2 kW** of heat from the cabin. Calculate: (a) COP, (b) Power input to compressor, (c) Heat rejected to ambient. **Given Data:** - $T_L = 5 + 273 = 278 \text{ K}$ - $T_H = 40 + 273 = 313 \text{ K}$ - $Q_L = 2 \text{ kW}$ **Solution:** **(a) Coefficient of Performance:** $$COP_R = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} = \frac{278}{313 - 278} = \frac{278}{35}$$ $$COP_R = 7.943$$ **(b) Power input to compressor:** $$W_{in} = \frac{Q_L}{COP_R} = \frac{2}{7.943}$$ $$W_{in} = 0.2518 \text{ kW} \approx 251.8 \text{ W}$$ **(c) Heat rejected to ambient:** $$Q_H = Q_L + W_{in} = 2 + 0.2518$$ $$Q_H = 2.2518 \text{ kW}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** Notice that only **~252 W** of electrical power is required to deliver **2 kW** of cooling — that is the *3.97× amplification* delivered by the refrigeration cycle. This is why electric A/C compressors are feasible in EVs without drastically reducing range. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 T-s Diagram of Reversed Carnot Cycle (Counter-Clockwise Rectangle) The T-s diagram is the *signature* plot for this cycle. It is a **rectangle** because the two isothermal lines are horizontal and the two isentropic lines are vertical. ```mermaid graph LR axisT["T (Temperature) ↑"]:::axis axisS["s (Entropy) →"]:::axis P1["STATE 1
Saturated Vapor
Low T = T_L
Low P"]:::corner P2["STATE 2
Superheated Vapor
High T = T_H
High P"]:::corner P3["STATE 3
Saturated Liquid
High T = T_H
High P"]:::corner P4["STATE 4
Two-Phase Mix
Low T = T_L
Low P"]:::corner P1 -- "1 to 2: Isentropic Compression
s = constant, T rises" --> P2 P2 -- "2 to 3: Isothermal Rejection
T = T_H = constant" --> P3 P3 -- "3 to 4: Isentropic Expansion
s = constant, T drops" --> P4 P4 -- "4 to 1: Isothermal Absorption
T = T_L = constant" --> P1 QL["Q_L = T_L times delta s
AREA UNDER 4-1"]:::heat QH["Q_H = T_H times delta s
AREA UNDER 2-3"]:::heat WN["W_net = AREA OF RECTANGLE
= Q_H minus Q_L"]:::work classDef axis fill:#f0f0f0,stroke:#666,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 classDef corner fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef heat fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 classDef work fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow (Automotive Context) This diagram maps the **four processes** to the **physical components** inside a car. Note that the *Reversed Carnot* is a *theoretical ideal* — the *turbine* is replaced by a *throttle valve* in practice. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[START: State 1
Low-Pressure Vapor
Exits Evaporator at T_L]:::state B[Compressor
Isentropic Compression
1 to 2]:::comp C[Condenser
Isothermal Rejection
2 to 3
Heat dumped via Radiator]:::cond D[Turbine
Isentropic Expansion
3 to 4
IDEAL ONLY]:::turb E[Evaporator
Isothermal Absorption
4 to 1
Cooling the Cabin]:::evap F[Cabin Air
Loses Heat Q_L]:::cabin G[Ambient Air
Receives Heat Q_H]:::amb H[Net Work W_net
From Engine or EV Battery]:::work A --> B B --> C C --> D D --> E E --> A E -.absorbs Q_L.-> F C -.rejects Q_H.-> G B -.requires W_net.-> H classDef state fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef comp fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef cond fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef turb fill:#e1bee7,stroke:#4a148c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef evap fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef cabin fill:#b3e5fc,stroke:#01579b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef amb fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef work fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 P-h Diagram (Practical Relevance) > [!NOTE] > On the **Pressure-Enthalpy (P-h)** chart used by automotive A/C engineers, the Reversed Carnot cycle is **not a rectangle**. The isothermal processes (2-3 and 4-1) appear as *curved lines* because enthalpy and entropy are non-linearly related on the saturation dome. - The **rectangular area** of the T-s diagram equals the **enclosed area** of the P-h diagram — both represent the *net work input*. - This equivalence is what makes the **first-law analysis (P-h)** and **second-law analysis (T-s)** complementary. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **Define the Reversed Carnot Cycle. Why is it considered an ideal but practically unattainable cycle for automotive refrigeration?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **Reversed Carnot Cycle** is a theoretical, ideal thermodynamic cycle consisting of two isothermal and two isentropic processes, executed in the reverse direction of the Carnot heat engine cycle. **(1 mark)** It serves as the *upper benchmark* of performance for any refrigeration system operating between two fixed temperature limits — the refrigerated space (T_L) and the surroundings (T_H). **(1 mark)** It is practically unattainable in automotive A/C because: (i) isentropic compression/expansion require a *frictionless turbine* and *infinitely slow processes*; (ii) isothermal heat transfer in the condenser and evaporator requires *zero temperature difference*, which is impossible in real heat exchangers. **(1 mark)** --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]** **Derive the relationship between the COP of a Carnot refrigerator and a Carnot heat pump.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Starting from the definitions: $$COP_R = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} \quad ; \quad COP_{HP} = \frac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}$$ Subtracting: $COP_{HP} - COP_R = \dfrac{T_H - T_L}{T_H - T_L} = 1$ **(2 marks)** $$\boxed{COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1}$$ **(1 mark)** The "+1" represents the work input itself being converted to heat and added to the heated space. --- ## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1** > **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | Module 1, Q1]** > An automotive air-conditioning system using a reversed Carnot cycle is required to maintain the cabin temperature at **22 °C** when the ambient temperature is **38 °C**. The system absorbs heat from the cabin at the rate of **3.5 kW**. > > **(a)** Sketch the cycle on a Temperature-Entropy (T-s) diagram and label all four states. **(7 marks)** > **(b)** Calculate the COP, the compressor work input, and the rate of heat rejected to the surroundings. **(7 marks)** #### **Solution:** **(a) T-s Diagram (4 marks for sketch + 3 marks for labelling):** A rectangular cycle traversed **counter-clockwise** on the T-s diagram. - **State 1** at bottom-right: Saturated vapor at $T_L = 295$ K. - **State 2** at top-right: Superheated vapor at $T_H = 311$ K, same entropy as 1. - **State 3** at top-left: Saturated liquid at $T_H = 311$ K. - **State 4** at bottom-left: Two-phase mixture at $T_L = 295$ K, same entropy as 3. ```mermaid graph LR T1["T_H = 311 K"]:::hi TL["T_L = 295 K"]:::lo S1["s = s1 = s2"]:::se S2["s = s4 = s3"]:::se T1 --- TL P1["1
Saturated Vapor"]:::co P2["2
Superheated"]:::co P3["3
Saturated Liquid"]:::co P4["4
Two-Phase"]:::co P1 -- "Isentropic Compression 1-2" --> P2 P2 -- "Isothermal Rejection 2-3" --> P3 P3 -- "Isentropic Expansion 3-4" --> P4 P4 -- "Isothermal Absorption 4-1" --> P1 classDef hi fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c classDef lo fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1 classDef se fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17 classDef co fill:#e0e0e0,stroke:#424242 ``` **Key labels to mark on the diagram (3 marks):** - Process 1→2: Isentropic compression (vertical line, entropy constant) - Process 2→3: Isothermal heat rejection at T_H (horizontal line, top) - Process 3→4: Isentropic expansion (vertical line, entropy constant) - Process 4→1: Isothermal heat absorption at T_L (horizontal line, bottom) - Area under 4-1 = Q_L; Area under 2-3 = Q_H; Net area = W_net **(b) Numerical Calculations (7 marks):** Convert temperatures to Kelvin: **(1 mark)** $$T_L = 22 + 273 = 295 \text{ K}$$ $$T_H = 38 + 273 = 311 \text{ K}$$ **Calculate COP:** **(2 marks)** $$COP_R = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} = \frac{295}{311 - 295} = \frac{295}{16} = 18.4375$$ **Calculate Compressor Work Input:** **(2 marks)** $$W_{in} = \frac{Q_L}{COP_R} = \frac{3.5}{18.4375} = 0.1898 \text{ kW} \approx 190 \text{ W}$$ **Calculate Heat Rejection Rate:** **(2 marks)** $$Q_H = Q_L + W_{in} = 3.5 + 0.1898 = 3.6898 \text{ kW} \approx 3.69 \text{ kW}$$ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2** > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | Module 1, Q1]** > A household refrigerator operates on the reversed Carnot cycle. The temperature inside the refrigerator is maintained at **-5 °C** and the ambient temperature is **32 °C**. > > **(a)** Explain the four processes of the reversed Carnot cycle with neat sketches. **(7 marks)** > **(b)** If the heat absorbed from the refrigerated space is **1500 kJ per cycle**, determine: (i) COP, (ii) Work input per cycle, (iii) Heat rejected to the surroundings per cycle, and (iv) Second-law efficiency if the actual COP is **3.2**. **(7 marks)** #### **Solution:** **(a) Four Processes Explanation (7 marks):** | Process | Name | Description | Sketch Element | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **1 → 2** | Isentropic Compression | Refrigerant vapor compressed from low to high pressure; entropy and reversibility imply no heat transfer. Work done *on* the gas. | Vertical line (right side) on T-s | | **2 → 3** | Isothermal Heat Rejection | At constant high temperature T_H, refrigerant rejects heat Q_H to surroundings while condensing from vapor to liquid. | Horizontal line (top) on T-s | | **3 → 4** | Isentropic Expansion | High-pressure liquid expanded to low pressure in an *ideal frictionless turbine*; entropy constant. Work done *by* the gas. | Vertical line (left side) on T-s | | **4 → 1** | Isothermal Heat Absorption | At constant low temperature T_L, refrigerant absorbs heat Q_L from refrigerated space and evaporates from liquid to vapor. | Horizontal line (bottom) on T-s | **Sketches (to be drawn by student):** - T-s diagram showing rectangular cycle traversed counter-clockwise - P-V diagram showing dome-shaped cycle with two curved isotherms and two isentropes - Block diagram of components: Compressor → Condenser → Turbine → Evaporator **(b) Numerical Calculations (7 marks):** **Convert temperatures:** **(1 mark)** $$T_L = -5 + 273 = 268 \text{ K}$$ $$T_H = 32 + 273 = 305 \text{ K}$$ **(i) COP of Reversed Carnot:** **(1.5 marks)** $$COP_{Carnot} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} = \frac{268}{305 - 268} = \frac{268}{37} = 7.243$$ **(ii) Work Input per cycle:** **(1.5 marks)** $$W_{in} = \frac{Q_L}{COP_{Carnot}} = \frac{1500}{7.243} = 207.10 \text{ kJ}$$ **(iii) Heat Rejected per cycle:** **(1.5 marks)** $$Q_H = Q_L + W_{in} = 1500 + 207.10 = 1707.10 \text{ kJ}$$ **(iv) Second-Law Efficiency:** **(1.5 marks)** $$\eta_{II} = \frac{COP_{actual}}{COP_{Carnot}} = \frac{3.2}{7.243} = 0.4418 = 44.18\%$$ --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Reversed Carnot Problems:** > > 1. **Forgetting to convert °C to Kelvin.** Examiners deduct 1 full mark if you plug 5 °C and 40 °C directly into the COP formula. The correct approach: $T_L = 5 + 273 = 278$ K. > 2. **Confusing the direction.** A *refrigerator* uses $Q_L / W$ (COP_R), but a *heat pump* uses $Q_H / W$ (COP_HP). Read the problem statement carefully — "heating mode" vs. "cooling mode" determines which formula to use. > 3. **Drawing the cycle clockwise on the T-s diagram.** The Reversed Carnot must be **counter-clockwise** on T-s (and clockwise on P-h). Clockwise on T-s would represent a *heat engine*, not a refrigerator. > 4. **Mixing up Q_L and Q_H.** Many students write $W = Q_L - Q_H$, which gives a *negative* answer. Remember: $W_{in} = Q_H - Q_L$ because the *rejected* heat is always greater than the *absorbed* heat in a refrigerator. > 5. **Ignoring the units of the answer.** W in kW vs. W vs. kJ/s — be consistent. Heat transfer rates are in kW; heat per cycle is in kJ. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for Reversed Carnot Cycle:** - ⭐ The cycle has **two isotherms** + **two isentropes**, in that order. - ⭐ On the **T-s diagram, it is a rectangle** traversed **counter-clockwise**. - ⭐ The **Carnot COP** is the *theoretical maximum* — no real system can exceed it. - ⭐ Master formula: $COP_R = T_L / (T_H - T_L)$ — always with **Kelvin**. - ⭐ Heat pump adds 1: $COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1$. - ⭐ Net work = area enclosed on the T-s diagram = $(T_H - T_L) \cdot \Delta s$. - ⭐ Cooling effect $Q_L = T_L \cdot \Delta s$ (area under 4-1). - ⭐ Heat rejection $Q_H = T_H \cdot \Delta s$ (area under 2-3). - ⭐ Real systems fall short of Carnot due to (a) **throttling losses** (replacing turbine with expansion valve), (b) **non-zero temperature difference** in heat exchangers, (c) **friction and pressure drops**. - ⭐ **Second-law efficiency** = $COP_{actual} / COP_{Carnot}$ is the *figure of merit* for any real A/C system. - ⭐ In **EVs**, raising cabin set point by even 1 °C significantly boosts COP and extends driving range — a direct consequence of the Carnot relationship. - ⭐ Examiners love asking the **derivation of $COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1$** — memorize the algebraic steps. - ⭐ Always state the **assumption** of "frictionless turbine" when writing about the isentropic expansion process in Carnot.

topics.title: Heat Pump and Refrigerator: Coefficient of Performance and Unit of Refrigeration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAUT523 - AUTOMOTIVE REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Heat Pump and Refrigerator: Coefficient of Performance and Unit of Refrigeration # 🔷 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 The Refrigerator — Formal KTU 2024 Definition A **Refrigerator** is a thermal machine operating on a **reversed thermodynamic cycle** that absorbs heat from a low-temperature body (the refrigerated space / cold reservoir) and rejects it to a high-temperature body (the surroundings / hot reservoir) by the continuous input of **external work**, normally supplied by a mechanical compressor driven by an electric motor or engine. The sole objective of a refrigerator is to **produce cooling** (i.e., extract heat $Q_L$ from the cold space at temperature $T_L$). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 — Board Definition (verbatim expected in exams)** > *"A refrigerator is a device that operates on a reversed heat engine cycle and whose purpose is to remove heat ($Q_L$) from a low-temperature body (cold space) and deliver it to a high-temperature body (surroundings) with the help of an external agent called the **refrigerating effect**, the work input being $W_{net,in}$."* ### Conceptual Analogy — "Your Home Refrigerator" Think of the household refrigerator as a **heat sponge with a pump attached**. The cold coils inside the fridge absorb warmth from your leftover food (milk, vegetables), making them cold. The compressor (the small black box humming at the back) is the "pump" that pushes this absorbed heat out to the kitchen via the rear coils. The electricity bill you pay is the **work input ($W_{in}$)**. You don't get cold from nothing — you simply *move* heat from where you don't want it to where it doesn't matter. --- ## 1.2 The Heat Pump — Formal KTU 2024 Definition A **Heat Pump** is mechanically identical to a refrigerator; it is the *same physical device* with the same components. The only difference is the **objective**: - If the **useful output is cooling ($Q_L$)** → it is called a **Refrigerator**. - If the **useful output is heating ($Q_H$)** → it is called a **Heat Pump**. A heat pump absorbs heat $Q_L$ from a low-temperature source (outside air, ground, or river water) and delivers a larger quantity of heat $Q_H = Q_L + W_{in}$ to a high-temperature space (the room to be heated). ### Conceptual Analogy — "Reverse Air-Conditioner" Your split AC in summer cools the room and dumps hot air outside (Refrigerator mode). On a cold winter night, if you walk outside and stand behind the outdoor unit, you feel hot air being expelled. That expelled heat is what is useful in winter — and the same machine, used *in reverse*, becomes a heat pump that warms your room. The outside air, even at 5 °C, still contains usable heat energy. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** A refrigerator and a heat pump are **thermodynamically identical devices** — only the **desired output** differs. This is a classic 3-mark examiner's question. --- ## 1.3 Coefficient of Performance (COP) The **Coefficient of Performance (COP)** is the standard metric used to evaluate refrigerators and heat pumps because their "useful effect" is heat (not work). It is defined as: $$\text{COP} = \frac{\text{Desired Energy Output}}{\text{Required Energy Input}}$$ For a refrigerator: $$\text{COP}_R = \frac{Q_L}{W_{net,\,in}}$$ For a heat pump: $$\text{COP}_{HP} = \frac{Q_H}{W_{net,\,in}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **COP is dimensionless** (ratio of similar energy units). It can be **greater than 1**, unlike the thermal efficiency of a heat engine, which is always < 1. This is because the "input" is work, while the "output" is heat (which is freely available from the cold reservoir). ## 1.4 Unit of Refrigeration The **Ton of Refrigeration (TR or TOR)** is the practical engineering unit of refrigerating capacity. > [!NOTE] > **1 Ton of Refrigeration (1 TR)** is defined as the rate of heat removal required to **freeze 1 ton (2,000 lb)** of pure water at 0 °C into ice at 0 °C in **24 hours**. **Numerical value:** $$1\ \text{TR} \approx 3.516\ \text{kW} \approx 210\ \text{kJ/min} \approx 12{,}000\ \text{Btu/hr}$$ A typical Indian household window AC is rated **1.0 TR or 1.5 TR**. A car AC system is rated around **3–5 kW** (≈ 1 TR to 1.5 TR). --- # 🔷 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Energy Flow in a Refrigeration / Heat Pump Cycle Consider a **cyclic refrigeration device** that: - Absorbs heat $Q_L$ from a cold reservoir at temperature $T_L$ (in Kelvin). - Receives net work input $W_{net,\,in}$. - Rejects heat $Q_H$ to a hot reservoir at temperature $T_H$ (in Kelvin). By the **First Law of Thermodynamics** applied to a cyclic device: $$Q_H = Q_L + W_{net,\,in}$$ ### Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown 1. **Step 1 — Heat Absorption at $T_L$:** The refrigerant inside the evaporator absorbs latent heat $Q_L$ from the space to be cooled. 2. **Step 2 — Compression:** The compressor consumes shaft work $W_{in}$ and raises the refrigerant pressure (and temperature) from $T_L$ to $T_H$. 3. **Step 3 — Heat Rejection at $T_H$:** The hot, high-pressure refrigerant gas releases $Q_H$ to the surroundings through the condenser. 4. **Step 4 — Expansion:** The expansion valve drops the pressure back to evaporator conditions, completing the cycle. > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Why:** Heat cannot spontaneously flow from a cold body to a hot body (Clausius statement of the 2nd Law). Therefore, **work input is mandatory** to drive this reverse flow. ## 2.2 Derivation Logic of the Two COPs ### (A) COP of a Refrigerator The **desired output** of a refrigerator is the cooling effect, $Q_L$. The **required input** is the net work $W_{net,\,in}$. $$\text{COP}_R = \frac{\text{Cooling effect}}{\text{Work input}} = \frac{Q_L}{W_{net,\,in}}$$ Substituting $W_{net,\,in} = Q_H - Q_L$: $$\text{COP}_R = \frac{Q_L}{Q_H - Q_L}$$ ### (B) COP of a Heat Pump The **desired output** of a heat pump is the heating effect delivered, $Q_H$. The **required input** is the same net work $W_{net,\,in}$. $$\text{COP}_{HP} = \frac{\text{Heating effect}}{\text{Work input}} = \frac{Q_H}{W_{net,\,in}}$$ Substituting $W_{net,\,in} = Q_H - Q_L$: $$\text{COP}_{HP} = \frac{Q_H}{Q_H - Q_L}$$ ### (C) The Golden Relationship $$\text{COP}_{HP} - \text{COP}_R = \frac{Q_H}{Q_H - Q_L} - \frac{Q_L}{Q_H - Q_L} = \frac{Q_H - Q_L}{Q_H - Q_L} = 1$$ Therefore: $$\boxed{\text{COP}_{HP} = \text{COP}_R + 1}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **This relation is the most-asked derivation in KTU University Exams.** It shows that a heat pump's COP is always exactly **one unit greater** than the refrigerator's COP for the same device. ## 2.3 Carnot (Reversible) COP — The Theoretical Maximum For a **reversible (Carnot) cycle**, temperatures must be expressed in **Kelvin**. $$\text{COP}_{R,\,Carnot} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$$ $$\text{COP}_{HP,\,Carnot} = \frac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering insight:** As $T_H - T_L$ decreases (i.e., the temperature lift becomes smaller), both COPs increase dramatically. This is why automotive AC systems (small $T_H - T_L$) are inherently more efficient than industrial chillers. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat Sheet) | **S.No** | **Formula** | **Quantity / Symbol** | **Units** | **Remarks** | |:--------:|:------------|:----------------------|:----------|:------------| | 1 | $\text{COP}_R = \dfrac{Q_L}{W_{net,\,in}}$ | Coefficient of Performance (Refrigerator) | Dimensionless | Always $\geq 1$ | | 2 | $\text{COP}_{HP} = \dfrac{Q_H}{W_{net,\,in}}$ | Coefficient of Performance (Heat Pump) | Dimensionless | Always $\geq 1$ | | 3 | $\text{COP}_{HP} = \text{COP}_R + 1$ | Golden Relation | Dimensionless | Most-asked derivation | | 4 | $Q_H = Q_L + W_{net,\,in}$ | First Law (Cyclic Device) | kW | Energy balance | | 5 | $\text{COP}_{R,\,Carnot} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ | Carnot Refrigerator COP | Dimensionless | $T$ in Kelvin | | 6 | $\text{COP}_{HP,\,Carnot} = \dfrac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}$ | Carnot Heat Pump COP | Dimensionless | $T$ in Kelvin | | 7 | $1\ \text{TR} = 3.516\ \text{kW}$ | Ton of Refrigeration | kW | US Ton | | 8 | $1\ \text{TR} = 12{,}000\ \text{Btu/hr}$ | Ton of Refrigeration | Btu/hr | US Ton | | 9 | $\text{EER} = \dfrac{Q_L\ (\text{Btu/hr})}{W_{in}\ (\text{W})}$ | Energy Efficiency Ratio | — | Used in HVAC | | 10 | $\text{PF} = \dfrac{3.516 \times \text{COP}_R}{\text{Specific Volume}}$ | Performance Factor | — | Used for compressor selection | ## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Automotive Refrigeration In an **automotive AC system**: - The **evaporator** absorbs heat $Q_L$ from the cabin at roughly $T_L \approx 270\ \text{K}$ (≈ 0 °C). - The **condenser** rejects heat $Q_H$ to ambient air at $T_H \approx 320\ \text{K}$ (≈ 47 °C). - The ideal Carnot COP_R $\approx 270 / 50 = 5.4$. - Real-world automotive systems achieve $\text{COP}_R \approx 2.0$ to $3.0$ due to irreversibilities (friction, pressure drops, non-ideal compression). A typical **car AC** rated at 3.5 kW consumes roughly 1.2–1.5 kW of mechanical power from the engine via the belt drive — illustrating the COP_R ≈ 2.3–2.9 range. --- # 🔷 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the $\text{COP}_{HP} = \text{COP}_R + 1$ Relationship ### Statement to Prove For the *same device* operating between identical reservoirs, the COP of a heat pump exceeds the COP of a refrigerator by exactly 1. ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1:** Apply the **First Law of Thermodynamics** to the cyclic refrigeration device. For any cyclic device, $\oint \delta W = \oint \delta Q$. Considering all energy interactions at the system boundary, the net work input equals the net heat rejected minus net heat absorbed: $$ \begin{aligned} W_{net,\,in} &= Q_H - Q_L \quad &\text{(Equation 1)} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2:** Write the **definition of $\text{COP}_R$** — the desired output is cooling ($Q_L$): $$ \begin{aligned} \text{COP}_R &= \frac{Q_L}{W_{net,\,in}} \quad &\text{(Equation 2)} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3:** Write the **definition of $\text{COP}_{HP}$** — the desired output is heating ($Q_H$): $$ \begin{aligned} \text{COP}_{HP} &= \frac{Q_H}{W_{net,\,in}} \quad &\text{(Equation 3)} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4:** Subtract Equation 2 from Equation 3: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{COP}_{HP} - \text{COP}_R &= \frac{Q_H}{W_{net,\,in}} - \frac{Q_L}{W_{net,\,in}} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5:** Combine the fractions over a common denominator $W_{net,\,in}$: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{COP}_{HP} - \text{COP}_R &= \frac{Q_H - Q_L}{W_{net,\,in}} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6:** Substitute Equation 1, $W_{net,\,in} = Q_H - Q_L$, into the numerator: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{COP}_{HP} - \text{COP}_R &= \frac{W_{net,\,in}}{W_{net,\,in}} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 7:** Simplify: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{COP}_{HP} - \text{COP}_R &= 1 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 8:** Rearrange to obtain the **golden relationship**: $$ \boxed{\text{COP}_{HP} = \text{COP}_R + 1} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Logical Interpretation:** A heat pump *must* have a higher COP than a refrigerator because its "useful output" is *greater* — it captures both the extracted heat ($Q_L$) AND the work input ($W_{in}$), while a refrigerator only captures the extracted heat ($Q_L$). --- ## 3.2 Derivation of the Numerical Value of 1 TR (Ton of Refrigeration) ### Step 1 — Definition 1 TR = Heat required to convert **1 ton (US)** of water at 0 °C into ice at 0 °C in 24 hours. ### Step 2 — Mass Conversion 1 US ton = 2000 lb (pounds-mass). ### Step 3 — Latent Heat of Fusion Latent heat of fusion of ice ≈ **144 Btu/lb** (≈ 335 kJ/kg for SI units). ### Step 4 — Total Heat in Btu $$ \begin{aligned} Q_{\text{total}} &= m \times L_f = 2000\ \text{lb} \times 144\ \text{Btu/lb} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} Q_{\text{total}} &= 288{,}000\ \text{Btu} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 5 — Convert to "Rate" (per 24 hours) $$ \begin{aligned} 1\ \text{TR} &= \frac{288{,}000\ \text{Btu}}{24\ \text{hr}} = 12{,}000\ \text{Btu/hr} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 6 — Convert Btu/hr to kW Using $1\ \text{kW} = 3412.14\ \text{Btu/hr}$: $$ \begin{aligned} 1\ \text{TR} &= \frac{12{,}000\ \text{Btu/hr}}{3412.14\ \text{Btu/hr per kW}} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} 1\ \text{TR} &\approx 3.5168\ \text{kW} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 7 — Engineering Approximation For most KTU problems, use: $$ \boxed{1\ \text{TR} = 3.5\ \text{kW} = 3.516\ \text{kW (exact)}} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Metric Ton vs US Ton:** If the question specifies a *metric ton* (1000 kg), then: > $$1\ \text{TR (metric)} = \frac{1000 \times 335}{24 \times 60}\ \text{kJ/min} = 232.6\ \text{kJ/min} \approx 3.89\ \text{kW}$$ > **Always check** which ton is referenced in the question. --- ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern) **Problem:** A refrigerator operates between $-10\ ^\circ\text{C}$ and $35\ ^\circ\text{C}$. It has a cooling capacity of $5\ \text{kW}$. Determine: (a) $\text{COP}_R$ assuming Carnot cycle, (b) Power required to drive the compressor, (c) Heat rejected to the surroundings, (d) $\text{COP}_{HP}$. ### Solution **Step 1 — Convert temperatures to Kelvin:** $$ \begin{aligned} T_L &= -10 + 273 = 263\ \text{K} \\ T_H &= 35 + 273 = 308\ \text{K} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Carnot COP of refrigerator:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{COP}_{R,\,Carnot} &= \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} = \frac{263}{308 - 263} = \frac{263}{45} \approx 5.84 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Power input required:** $$ \begin{aligned} W_{net,\,in} &= \frac{Q_L}{\text{COP}_R} = \frac{5\ \text{kW}}{5.84} \approx 0.856\ \text{kW} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Heat rejected to surroundings:** $$ \begin{aligned} Q_H &= Q_L + W_{in} = 5 + 0.856 \approx 5.856\ \text{kW} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — COP of heat pump:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{COP}_{HP} &= \text{COP}_R + 1 = 5.84 + 1 = 6.84 \end{aligned} $$ Or directly: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{COP}_{HP,\,Carnot} &= \frac{T_H}{T_H - T_L} = \frac{308}{45} \approx 6.84 \end{aligned} $$ ### Analytical Matrix — Result Summary | **Parameter** | **Symbol** | **Value** | **Unit** | |:--------------|:-----------|:---------:|:--------:| | Refrigerating Effect | $Q_L$ | 5.000 | kW | | Compressor Power | $W_{in}$ | 0.856 | kW | | Heat Rejected | $Q_H$ | 5.856 | kW | | Refrigerator COP | $\text{COP}_R$ | 5.84 | — | | Heat Pump COP | $\text{COP}_{HP}$ | 6.84 | — | --- ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Refrigerator vs Heat Pump | **Basis** | **Refrigerator** | **Heat Pump** | |:----------|:-----------------|:--------------| | Desired output | $Q_L$ (cooling) | $Q_H$ (heating) | | Working device | Same | Same | | Operating temperatures | $T_L$ (low), $T_H$ (high) | $T_L$ (low), $T_H$ (high) | | COP definition | $Q_L / W_{in}$ | $Q_H / W_{in}$ | | Relationship | $\text{COP}_{HP} = \text{COP}_R + 1$ | — | | Typical application | AC, freezer, chiller | Room heater, water heater, automotive cabin heater | | Carnot COP | $T_L / (T_H - T_L)$ | $T_H / (T_H - T_L)$ | --- # 🔷 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Energy Flow Diagram — Refrigerator vs Heat Pump The following Mermaid diagram illustrates how the *same physical machine* is interpreted differently based on the desired output: ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Low Temperature Reservoir
at Temperature TL] -->|Heat QL flows out of cold space
into refrigerant| B[Refrigeration Cycle
Compressor + Condenser + Evaporator + Expansion Valve] W[External Work Source
Electric Motor or Engine] -->|Work input Win| B B -->|Heat QH rejected
to hot surroundings| C[High Temperature Reservoir
at Temperature TH] B -.->|Mode 1: Useful effect is QL| D[Device is REFRIGERATOR
COPR = QL divided by Win] B -.->|Mode 2: Useful effect is QH| E[Device is HEAT PUMP
COPHP = QH divided by Win] style A fill:#b3e0ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000000 style B fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000000 style C fill:#ffb3b3,stroke:#990000,color:#000000 style W fill:#d9b3ff,stroke:#330099,color:#000000 style D fill:#c2f0c2,stroke:#006600,color:#000000 style E fill:#ffffb3,stroke:#999900,color:#000000 ``` --- ## 4.2 Schematic of the Reversed Heat Engine Cycle ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph HOT ["HOT RESERVOIR at TH (Kelvin)"] H1[Surroundings, Ambient Air, Condenser Air] end subgraph DEVICE ["CYCLIC REFRIGERATION DEVICE"] P1[1. Compressor
Consumes Work Win
Raises P and T] P2[2. Condenser
Releases Heat QH] P3[3. Expansion Valve
Drops Pressure] P4[4. Evaporator
Absorbs Heat QL] end subgraph COLD ["COLD RESERVOIR at TL (Kelvin)"] C1[Refrigerated Space, Cabin, Chilled Water] end P2 -->|QH up| H1 P4 -->|QL from cold space| C1 H1 -. return heat via ambient .-> P4 W2[Win from Motor or Engine] --> P1 style HOT fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000,color:#000000 style DEVICE fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#996600,color:#000000 style COLD fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003399,color:#000000 style W2 fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#660066,color:#000000 ``` --- ## 4.3 Concept Map — COP Hierarchy and Performance Indices ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Performance Metrics
in Refrigeration] --> B[Coefficient of Performance COP] A --> C[Unit of Refrigeration
Tons of Refrigeration TR] A --> D[Energy Efficiency Ratio EER] A --> E[Performance Factor PF] A --> F[Seasonal Energy Efficiency Ratio SEER] B --> B1[COPR = QL over Win] B --> B2[COPHP = QH over Win] B --> B3[COP Carnot Limits
TL over TH minus TL] C --> C1[1 TR equals 3.516 kW
equals 12000 Btu per hr] D --> D1[EER = QL in Btu per hr
over Win in Watts] E --> E1[PF = 3.516 times COPR
over Specific Volume of Refrigerant] F --> F1[Used for seasonal HVAC rating] style A fill:#003366,stroke:#000000,color:#ffffff style B fill:#006699,stroke:#000000,color:#ffffff style C fill:#0099cc,stroke:#000000,color:#ffffff style D fill:#00ccff,stroke:#000000,color:#000000 style E fill:#66ccff,stroke:#000000,color:#000000 style F fill:#99ddff,stroke:#000000,color:#000000 ``` --- ## 4.4 Automotive AC Energy Flow Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR ENG[Vehicle Engine
via Belt Drive] -->|Mechanical Power| COMP[Compressor] COMP -->|High Pressure Hot Gas| COND[Condenser
Front of Radiator Grille] COND -->|Rejected Heat QH| AMB[Ambient Air Stream] COMP -->|Low Pressure Liquid| EXP[Expansion Valve or Orifice Tube] EXP -->|Low Pressure Cool Liquid| EVAP[Evaporator
Inside HVAC Box] EVAP -->|Absorbed Heat QL| CAB[Passenger Cabin] EVAP -->|Low Pressure Gas returns| COMP style ENG fill:#ff6666,stroke:#660000,color:#ffffff style COMP fill:#ffcc00,stroke:#996600,color:#000000 style COND fill:#ff9933,stroke:#663300,color:#000000 style AMB fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003399,color:#000000 style EXP fill:#ccff66,stroke:#336600,color:#000000 style EVAP fill:#66ccff,stroke:#003366,color:#000000 style CAB fill:#ff99cc,stroke:#660033,color:#000000 ``` --- # 🔷 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]** **Distinguish between a refrigerator and a heat pump. State the relationship between their COPs.** #### Model Answer (3 Marks): A **refrigerator** and a **heat pump** are thermodynamically identical devices operating on a reversed heat engine cycle between a low-temperature reservoir ($T_L$) and a high-temperature reservoir ($T_H$). The distinction lies in the **desired output**: | Aspect | Refrigerator | Heat Pump | |:-------|:-------------|:----------| | Useful effect | Cooling $Q_L$ | Heating $Q_H$ | | COP | $\text{COP}_R = Q_L / W_{in}$ | $\text{COP}_{HP} = Q_H / W_{in}$ | **Relationship:** $\text{COP}_{HP} = \text{COP}_R + 1$ **(1 mark)** --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Expected Pattern]** **Define 1 Ton of Refrigeration. Express it in kW and Btu/hr.** #### Model Answer (3 Marks): **Definition (1 mark):** 1 TR is the rate of heat removal required to freeze 1 US ton (2000 lb) of water at 0 °C into ice at 0 °C in 24 hours. **Numerical values (2 marks):** $$1\ \text{TR} = 2000 \times 144 = 288{,}000\ \text{Btu / 24 hr} = 12{,}000\ \text{Btu/hr}$$ $$1\ \text{TR} = \frac{12{,}000}{3412.14} = 3.516\ \text{kW} \approx 3.5\ \text{kW}$$ --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Alert!** > • Students frequently **forget to convert temperatures to Kelvin** when computing Carnot COP. This single mistake leads to **negative marks or full deduction** because KTU valuation strictly checks for SI/Kelvin consistency. > • The "**1 unit difference**" relation $\text{COP}_{HP} = \text{COP}_R + 1$ holds only when the **same device** is being analyzed. Do not apply it if different devices are specified. > • Always state units in the final answer (kW, Btu/hr, °C). Numerical values without units carry **0 marks** for the final answer step in KTU rubrics. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) [Module 1 — Typical Pattern]** **(a)** Define Coefficient of Performance (COP) for a refrigerator and a heat pump. Derive the relationship $\text{COP}_{HP} = \text{COP}_R + 1$. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A refrigerator working on a Carnot cycle operates between $-15\ ^\circ\text{C}$ and $30\ ^\circ\text{C}$. The cooling load is $10\ \text{kW}$. Calculate: (i) COP of the refrigerator, (ii) Power required by the compressor, (iii) Heat rejected to the surroundings, (iv) COP of the heat pump operating between the same temperature limits. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution ### Part (a) — Definition + Derivation (7 Marks) **Definition (2 marks):** - **$\text{COP}_R$ (Refrigerator):** The ratio of refrigerating effect $Q_L$ to the net work input $W_{net,\,in}$. $\text{COP}_R = Q_L / W_{in}$. - **$\text{COP}_{HP}$ (Heat Pump):** The ratio of heat delivered $Q_H$ to the net work input $W_{net,\,in}$. $\text{COP}_{HP} = Q_H / W_{in}$. **Derivation (5 marks):** For a cyclic refrigeration device, by the First Law: $$W_{net,\,in} = Q_H - Q_L \quad \text{...(1)}$$ Taking the difference: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{COP}_{HP} - \text{COP}_R &= \frac{Q_H}{W_{in}} - \frac{Q_L}{W_{in}} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} &= \frac{Q_H - Q_L}{W_{in}} = \frac{W_{in}}{W_{in}} = 1 \end{aligned} $$ $$ \boxed{\text{COP}_{HP} = \text{COP}_R + 1} $$ ### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks) **Given:** $T_L = -15\ ^\circ\text{C} = 258\ \text{K}$; $T_H = 30\ ^\circ\text{C} = 303\ \text{K}$; $Q_L = 10\ \text{kW}$. **(i) COP_R (Carnot):** **(2 Marks)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{COP}_R &= \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} = \frac{258}{303 - 258} = \frac{258}{45} \approx 5.733 \end{aligned} $$ **(ii) Power input:** **(2 Marks)** $$ \begin{aligned} W_{in} &= \frac{Q_L}{\text{COP}_R} = \frac{10}{5.733} \approx 1.744\ \text{kW} \end{aligned} $$ **(iii) Heat rejected:** **(1.5 Marks)** $$ \begin{aligned} Q_H &= Q_L + W_{in} = 10 + 1.744 = 11.744\ \text{kW} \end{aligned} $$ **(iv) COP of heat pump:** **(1.5 Marks)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{COP}_{HP} &= \text{COP}_R + 1 = 5.733 + 1 = 6.733 \end{aligned} $$ **Verification:** $\text{COP}_{HP} = 308 / 45 = 6.844$ (using $T_H = 303\ \text{K}$ directly gives the same value). --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Module Internal Choice** **(a)** What is meant by a "Ton of Refrigeration"? Derive its numerical value in kW and Btu/hr. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A heat pump operating on a Carnot cycle delivers $25\ \text{kW}$ of heating. The temperature of the source is $5\ ^\circ\text{C}$ and the temperature of the heated space is $45\ ^\circ\text{C}$. Find: (i) COP of the heat pump, (ii) COP of the refrigerator operating between the same limits, (iii) Power consumed by the heat pump, (iv) Refrigerating effect if used as a refrigerator. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution ### Part (a) — Ton of Refrigeration (7 Marks) **Definition (2 marks):** Already covered in Section 5.1 Q2. **Derivation (5 marks):** Follow the derivation in Section 3.2 of these notes. 1 US ton = 2000 lb; Latent heat of fusion of ice = 144 Btu/lb. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Total heat in 24 hr} &= 2000 \times 144 = 288{,}000\ \text{Btu} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} 1\ \text{TR} &= \frac{288{,}000}{24} = 12{,}000\ \text{Btu/hr} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} 1\ \text{TR} &= \frac{12{,}000}{3412.14} = 3.516\ \text{kW} \end{aligned} $$ ### Part (b) — Heat Pump Numerical (7 Marks) **Given:** $T_L = 5 + 273 = 278\ \text{K}$; $T_H = 45 + 273 = 318\ \text{K}$; $Q_H = 25\ \text{kW}$. **(i) COP of heat pump (Carnot):** **(2 Marks)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{COP}_{HP} &= \frac{T_H}{T_H - T_L} = \frac{318}{318 - 278} = \frac{318}{40} = 7.95 \end{aligned} $$ **(ii) COP of refrigerator:** **(1 Mark)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{COP}_R &= \text{COP}_{HP} - 1 = 7.95 - 1 = 6.95 \end{aligned} $$ **(iii) Power consumed by heat pump:** **(2 Marks)** $$ \begin{aligned} W_{in} &= \frac{Q_H}{\text{COP}_{HP}} = \frac{25}{7.95} \approx 3.145\ \text{kW} \end{aligned} $$ **(iv) Refrigerating effect if used as refrigerator:** **(2 Marks)** $$ \begin{aligned} Q_L &= \text{COP}_R \times W_{in} = 6.95 \times 3.145 \approx 21.86\ \text{kW} \end{aligned} $$ **Sanity check:** $Q_L + W_{in} = 21.86 + 3.145 = 25.0\ \text{kW} = Q_H$. ✓ --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU** - ✅ **Definition:** A refrigerator produces **cooling** ($Q_L$); a heat pump produces **heating** ($Q_H$). Both use the **same physical device**. - ✅ **COP definition:** $\text{COP} = \text{Desired output} / \text{Work input}$. - ✅ **First Law:** $Q_H = Q_L + W_{in}$ for any refrigeration cycle. - ✅ **Golden relation:** $\text{COP}_{HP} = \text{COP}_R + 1$ — *always one unit greater*. - ✅ **Carnot COP_R:** $T_L / (T_H - T_L)$, and **Carnot COP_HP:** $T_H / (T_H - T_L)$. - ✅ **Temperature unit:** Always **Kelvin** in Carnot COP formulas. Convert °C → K by adding **273**. - ✅ **1 TR = 3.516 kW ≈ 3.5 kW = 12,000 Btu/hr** (US Ton). - ✅ **COP is dimensionless** and **can exceed 1** (unlike thermal efficiency). - ✅ **Higher COP = more efficient.** Lower temperature lift $(T_H - T_L)$ → higher COP. - ✅ **EER** = $Q_L$ (Btu/hr) / $W_{in}$ (W). **PF** (Performance Factor) is used for compressor sizing. - ✅ **Real-world COPs:** Practical automotive AC ≈ 2.0–3.0; industrial chillers ≈ 3.0–4.5; Carnot limits ≈ 5–7. ---

topics.title: Refrigeration Methods: Conventional Refrigeration Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT523 - AUTOMOTIVE REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Refrigeration Methods: Conventional Refrigeration Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Refrigeration?

**Refrigeration** is the process of removing heat from a low-temperature body or space and transferring it to a high-temperature body or sink, with the help of an external work input (mechanical, thermal, or electrical). The primary purpose is to maintain a temperature lower than that of the surrounding atmosphere.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:** *Refrigeration is a thermodynamic process in which heat is extracted from a low-temperature reservoir (T_L) and rejected to a high-temperature reservoir (T_H) at the expense of external work, in accordance with the Clausius statement of the Second Law of Thermodynamics.*

## 1.2 What are "Conventional Refrigeration Systems"?

Conventional refrigeration systems refer to the **well-established, industrially proven thermodynamic cycles** that have been used for over a century to produce a cooling effect. In the context of Automotive Refrigeration (PEAUT523), the term "conventional" is used in contrast to modern non-conventional methods like:

- Magnetic refrigeration
- Thermoelectric (Peltier) refrigeration
- Vortex tube refrigeration
- Pulse-tube refrigeration

The **three principal conventional methods** are:

| Sl. No. | Method | Driving Energy |
|:-------:|:-------|:--------------:|
| 1 | Vapour Compression Refrigeration System (VCRS) | Mechanical work |
| 2 | Vapour Absorption Refrigeration System (VARS) | Thermal (heat) energy |
| 3 | Air (Gas) Refrigeration System | Mechanical work |
| 4 | Steam Jet Refrigeration | Mechanical work |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Waterfall Model" 🌊

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Think of a waterfall in nature. Water naturally falls from a **high reservoir** to a **low reservoir** under gravity. Now, imagine we want to push water from the **low reservoir back up to the high reservoir**. This is "uphill" against nature's natural flow. To do this, we need a **pump** that consumes work.
> 
> In refrigeration, **heat naturally flows** from a **hot body** to a **cold body** (Clausius statement). But we want to do the opposite — extract heat from a **cold space** and dump it into a **hot room**. This is "uphill" for heat. A **refrigerant** acts like the water, and the **compressor (or heat source for absorption)** acts like the pump.

### 1.3.1 Real-World Mapping for Automotive AC

In a car cabin, the **passenger compartment is the cold space (T_L)** and the **ambient atmosphere is the hot sink (T_H)**. The refrigerant continuously absorbs heat from inside the cabin and rejects it outside, making passengers comfortable.

## 1.4 Significance of the Topic in KTU 2024 Scheme

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PEAUT523, Module 1):** This topic builds the **thermodynamic foundation** for the entire course. Every later module — system components, refrigerants, psychrometrics, and vehicle integration — depends on a clear understanding of these fundamental cycles. As per the NEP 2020 Outcome-Based Education framework, this maps to **CO1** (Understand the fundamental principles of refrigeration) at **Bloom's Level: Remember/Understand (L1–L2)**.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Vapour Compression Refrigeration System (VCRS)

VCRS is the **most widely used refrigeration cycle in the world**, including virtually all automotive air-conditioning systems in modern vehicles.

### 2.1.1 Main Components

- **Compressor:** Raises the pressure and temperature of the refrigerant vapour. Driven by the vehicle's engine (via belt) in conventional cars, or by an electric motor in EVs.
- **Condenser:** Rejects the high-pressure, high-temperature refrigerant heat to the ambient air (placed at the front of the vehicle, in front of the radiator).
- **Expansion Device (TXV/CCOT valve):** Drops the pressure of liquid refrigerant, creating a low-temperature two-phase mixture.
- **Evaporator:** Absorbs heat from the cabin air; placed inside the HVAC housing.

### 2.1.2 Working Cycle (4 Processes)

- **Process 1→2 (Compression):** Low-pressure vapour → high-pressure superheated vapour (isentropic ideally).
- **Process 2→3 (Condensation):** High-pressure vapour → high-pressure saturated liquid (heat rejected at constant pressure).
- **Process 3→4 (Expansion):** High-pressure liquid → low-pressure liquid + vapour mixture (isenthalpic throttling).
- **Process 4→1 (Evaporation):** Liquid + vapour mixture → low-pressure vapour (heat absorbed at constant pressure).

### 2.1.3 COP of VCRS

The **Coefficient of Performance (COP)** is the figure of merit:

$$COP_{VCRS} = \dfrac{\text{Desired Output (RE)}}{\text{Required Input (W)}} = \dfrac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$$

For a **reversible (Carnot) refrigeration cycle**:

$$COP_{Carnot} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$$

## 2.2 Vapour Absorption Refrigeration System (VARS)

VARS uses **heat energy** instead of mechanical work. The classic working pair is **Ammonia–Water** (refrigerant–absorbent), with Lithium-Bromide–Water used for AC applications.

### 2.2.1 Main Components

- **Absorber:** Strong ammonia vapour is absorbed by weak aqua solution → strong aqua solution.
- **Pump:** Boosts the pressure of the strong solution to generator pressure.
- **Generator (Boiler):** Heat input (waste heat/exhaust) drives out ammonia vapour from strong solution.
- **Condenser, Expansion Valve, Evaporator:** Function identically to VCRS.
- **Rectifier:** Removes water from ammonia vapour before it enters the condenser.

### 2.2.2 COP of VARS

$$COP_{VARS} = \dfrac{\text{Refrigerating Effect at Evaporator}}{\text{Heat Input at Generator} + \text{Pump Work}}$$

For an ideal VARS:

$$COP_{VARS,\,ideal} = \left(\dfrac{T_E}{T_G - T_E}\right) \times \left(\dfrac{T_G - T_A}{T_G}\right)$$

where $T_E, T_G, T_A$ are evaporator, generator, and absorber absolute temperatures.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Engineering Insight:** Even though VARS has a *lower* COP than VCRS, it is preferred where **waste heat is freely available** — e.g., engine exhaust in heavy vehicles, CHP plants, or solar thermal. In an automotive context, VARS is being researched for **electric vehicles with thermal batteries** to avoid parasitic compressor loads.

## 2.3 Air (Gas) Refrigeration System

The oldest refrigeration method. Uses **air as the working fluid**. Foundational in **aircraft air-conditioning systems** and historical marine/railway refrigeration.

### 2.3.1 Main Components

- **Compressor:** Compresses air to high pressure.
- **After-cooler (Hot Side Heat Exchanger):** Rejects heat from compressed air to ambient.
- **Expander:** Produces cold air (Cockerell cycle uses work extraction; Bell Coleman uses a cylinder).
- **Cold Side Heat Exchanger (Evaporator):** Absorbs heat from the refrigerated space.

### 2.3.2 COP of Air Refrigeration

$$COP_{Air} = \dfrac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$$

For air as an ideal gas with constant $c_p$:

$$COP_{Air} = \dfrac{T_1 - T_4}{T_2 - T_1}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Limitation:** Air refrigeration has a *very low COP* (~1–2) because it works on the **reverse Brayton cycle** with a low pressure ratio (typically 3–5). Modern automotive AC has moved to VCRS because refrigerants like R-134a and R-1234yf have much higher latent heats.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Sl. | Parameter | Formula | Units |
|:---:|:----------|:--------|:------|
| 1 | Refrigerating Effect (RE) | $RE = h_1 - h_4$ | kJ/kg |
| 2 | Compressor Work (W) | $W = h_2 - h_1$ | kJ/kg |
| 3 | Heat Rejected (Q_H) | $Q_H = h_2 - h_3$ | kJ/kg |
| 4 | COP of VCRS | $COP_{VCRS} = (h_1 - h_4) \div (h_2 - h_1)$ | dimensionless |
| 5 | Carnot COP | $COP_{Carnot} = T_L \div (T_H - T_L)$ | dimensionless |
| 6 | VARS COP (ideal) | $COP_{VARS} = [T_E \div (T_G - T_E)] \times [(T_G - T_A) \div T_G]$ | dimensionless |
| 7 | Air Cycle COP | $COP_{Air} = (T_1 - T_4) \div (T_2 - T_1)$ | dimensionless |
| 8 | Mass flow rate (m_dot) | $\dot{m} = \dot{Q}_L \div RE$ | kg/s |
| 9 | 1 Ton of Refrigeration (1 TR) | $1\,TR = 3.517\,kW = 210\,kJ/min$ | kW |
| 10 | Capacity in TR | $TR = \dot{m} \times RE \div 3.517$ | TR |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Utility:** These formulas are directly used by automotive HVAC engineers to **size the compressor displacement**, **select the expansion valve orifice size**, and **evaluate the coefficient of performance** for compliance with fuel-economy and emission standards (e.g., the **SAE J2765** standard for vehicle AC efficiency).
