<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a refrigerant. List any four desirable properties of an ideal refrigerant.**

**Model Answer:**
A refrigerant is a working fluid used in a vapor-compression refrigeration cycle that absorbs heat at low temperature by evaporating and rejects it at high temperature by condensing, thereby transferring thermal energy from a low-temperature region (cabin) to a high-temperature region (ambient).

Four desirable properties:
1. Low boiling point and high latent heat of vaporization.
2. Non-toxic, non-flammable, and chemically stable.
3. Compatible with compressor lubricant (PAG oil) and elastomer seals.
4. Zero ODP and very low GWP for environmental compliance.

*(1 mark for definition + 0.5 each for four properties = 3 marks)*

#### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the significance of ASHRAE numbering for refrigerants? Identify the refrigerant R-1234yf.**

**Model Answer:**
The ASHRAE 34 numbering system is a standardized nomenclature that uniquely identifies refrigerant chemistry using digits encoding the number of carbon, hydrogen, and fluorine atoms. It allows engineers to identify a refrigerant's composition, safety class, and properties without ambiguity.

**R-1234yf** is **2,3,3,3-tetrafluoropropene (CF₃CF=CH₂)**, a hydrofluoroolefin (HFO) refrigerant with GWP less than 1, classified A2L, used in modern automotive air-conditioning systems as a low-GWP replacement for R-134a.

---

### 5.2 Part B Question (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Explain the **classification of refrigerants** based on chemical composition and ASHRAE safety groups. Discuss the environmental impact of CFCs and HFOs using ODP and GWP concepts. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat sketch, describe the **vapor-compression refrigeration cycle** used in an automotive AC system. Mark all four state points and explain the function of each component. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Classification & Environmental Impact:**

**Chemical Classification:**
- **Inorganic refrigerants:** R-717 (ammonia), R-744 (CO₂), R-718 (water). Natural substances; generally zero ODP, low GWP.
- **Halogenated organic refrigerants:**
  - **CFCs (Chlorofluorocarbons)** — contain chlorine: e.g., R-12. High ODP, very high GWP. **Phased out** under the 1987 Montreal Protocol.
  - **HCFCs (Hydrochlorofluorocarbons)** — contain hydrogen & chlorine: e.g., R-22. Lower ODP than CFCs but still non-zero. Being phased out for new MAC systems.
  - **HFCs (Hydrofluorocarbons)** — no chlorine, no ozone depletion: e.g., R-134a. Zero ODP but high GWP (1430).
  - **HFOs (Hydrofluoroolefins)** — unsaturated HFCs: e.g., R-1234yf. Zero ODP and GWP less than 1.

**ASHRAE Safety Classification:**
- Toxicity: **A** = lower toxicity, **B** = higher toxicity
- Flammability: **1** = no propagation, **2L** = mild, **3** = high

**Environmental Impact:**
- **ODP (Ozone Depletion Potential)** measures damage to stratospheric ozone. CFCs have ODP ≈ 1.0 (R-12 = 1.0); HFOs/HFCs have ODP = 0.
- **GWP (Global Warming Potential)** compares a refrigerant's 100-year warming effect to CO₂. R-12 GWP = 10,900; R-134a = 1430; R-1234yf less than 1.
- The **Kigali Amendment (2016)** to the Montreal Protocol mandates HFC phase-down of 80–85 % over 30 years.

**(b) Vapor-Compression Refrigeration Cycle in an Automotive AC:**

The system has **four main components**: compressor, condenser, expansion valve (or orifice tube), evaporator — interconnected by hoses. The cycle operates as follows:

| State | Location | Condition | Phase |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| **1** | Compressor inlet / Evaporator outlet | Low pressure, low temperature (sat. vapor) | Vapor |
| **2** | Compressor outlet / Condenser inlet | High pressure, high temperature (superheated) | Vapor |
| **3** | Condenser outlet / Expansion valve inlet | High pressure (sat. liquid) | Liquid |
| **4** | Expansion valve outlet / Evaporator inlet | Low pressure (liquid + vapor mixture) | Two-phase |

**Process flow:**
1. **Compression (1→2):** Compressor raises refrigerant pressure and temperature isentropically.
2. **Condensation (2→3):** High-pressure vapor rejects heat to ambient air via the condenser; refrigerant liquefies.
3. **Expansion (3→4):** Throttle valve drops pressure; partial flash evaporation cools the remaining liquid.
4. **Evaporation (4→1):** Low-pressure liquid absorbs cabin heat via the evaporator and fully vaporizes.

The cycle is **closed-loop**, and refrigerant mass circulates continuously until a leak or component failure occurs.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** Compare **R-12, R-134a, and R-1234yf** as refrigerants for automotive AC applications with respect to ODP, GWP, toxicity, flammability, and lubricant compatibility. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain why **R-744 (CO₂)** is being researched as a future refrigerant for electric vehicles. Discuss the concept of a **transcritical cycle**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparative Refrigerant Analysis:**

| Property | R-12 | R-134a | R-1234yf |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **ODP** | 1.00 | 0 | 0 |
| **GWP** | 10,900 | 1,430 | <1 |
| **Toxicity** | A | A | A |
| **Flammability** | Non-flammable | Non-flammable | Mildly flammable (A2L) |
| **Lubricant Compatibility** | Mineral oil | PAG oil | PAG/POE oil |
| **Atmospheric Lifetime** | 100 years | 14 years | 10.5 days (very short!) |
| **Status** | Phased out | Phasing down | Current OEM standard |

**Key Observations:**
- R-12 has the highest environmental damage; banned globally in new vehicles after 1995.
- R-134a solved the ozone problem but contributes to global warming.
- R-1234yf's atmospheric lifetime of only **10.5 days** (vs. 14 years for R-134a) means any leaked refrigerant decomposes before reaching the stratosphere.
- All three are miscible with appropriate compressor oil; lubricant migration back to the compressor is reliable.

**(b) R-744 (CO₂) and Transcritical Cycles in EVs:**

**Why CO₂ for EVs:**
1. **GWP = 1** — absolute minimum environmental impact.
2. **High volumetric refrigerating effect (VRE)** at high pressures → compact heat exchangers.
3. **Excellent thermal conductivity** → faster heat transfer in evaporator/condenser.
4. **Compatible with natural refrigerants trend** and carbon-neutral supply chain.
5. **High critical temperature (31 °C)** allows efficient heat pump operation in cold ambient (heat recovery for EV battery thermal management).

**Transcritical Cycle:**
- In a conventional cycle, condensation occurs at sub-critical temperature (refrigerant changes from vapor → liquid).
- Since CO₂'s critical temperature is **31 °C** and the automotive condenser must reject heat at **45–60 °C** (above the critical point), the high-pressure CO₂ **cannot condense** in the traditional sense.
- Instead, the gas cooler cools supercritical CO₂ *sensibly* (without phase change), and an **internal heat exchanger (IHX)** plus an **expansion device** control the high-side pressure to optimize COP.

**Challenges:**
- Operating pressure **≥ 100 bar** (vs. ~20 bar for R-134a), demanding thicker hoses, stronger compressor, leak-tight seals.
- Requires dedicated compressor designs (e.g., swash-plate or scroll).

BMW's **i3** and Mercedes' **S-Class** have demonstrated R-744 MAC systems, validating the transcritical concept for production EVs.

---

### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (Pay attention to avoid losing marks):**
> 1. **Confusing ODP and GWP**: ODP measures ozone damage; GWP measures climate warming. Students often interchange them. *Always write the full name when defining an acronym the first time.*
> 2. **Forgetting the safety class suffixes**: R-1234yf is **A2L**, NOT A1. The "L" indicates *low burning velocity* — losing this "L" costs 0.5–1 mark.
> 3. **Writing "R-134a is non-flammable" without proof**: State explicitly "classified A1 per ASHRAE 34, no flame propagation at 100 % RH up to 60 °C".
> 4. **Skipping the throttling process enthalpy balance**: In derivations, students forget $h_3 = h_4$ (isenthalpic). Examiners deduct 1 mark for this.
> 5. **No chemical formula**: Always write the molecular formula of the refrigerant (e.g., CH₂FCF₃ for R-134a) — never just the trade name.
> 6. **Calling R-1234yf "HFC"**: It is an **HFO** (hydrofluoroolefin), not an HFC. Wrong classification = 1 mark lost.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist — Types of Refrigerants**

- A **refrigerant** is a phase-change working fluid in a vapor-compression refrigeration cycle.
- **ASHRAE 34** provides the standard nomenclature: R-(C−1)(H+1)(F) for saturated halogenated compounds; R followed by molecular mass for inorganic (CO₂ = R-744, NH₃ = R-717).
- **Classification axes**: by chemistry (natural/synthetic/inorganic/organic), by safety (A1, A2L, B1...), by ozone impact (CFC/HCFC/HFC/HFO).
- **Desirable properties** — 12-point checklist including low boiling point, high latent heat, non-toxic, non-flammable, oil-miscible, zero ODP, low GWP.
- **Refrigerating Effect (RE)** = $h_1 - h_4$ (kJ/kg); **VRE** = RE / $v_1$ (kJ/m³); **COP** = RE / compressor work.
- **R-12** (CFC, ODP=1.0, GWP=10,900): **phased out** post-1995.
- **R-134a** (HFC, ODP=0, GWP=1430): **legacy standard 1995–2017**, still dominant in older vehicles.
- **R-1234yf** (HFO, ODP=0, GWP<1, A2L class): **current OEM standard** post-2017; atmospheric lifetime ~10.5 days.
- **R-744** (CO₂, natural, transcritical): future EV refrigerant; GWP=1, critical temperature 31 °C, demands high-pressure components.
- **Montreal Protocol (1987)** + **Kigali Amendment (2016)** = legal drivers behind refrigerant transition.
- **ODP** = ozone damage; **GWP** = climate warming; both must be reported for any refrigerant selection.
- **ASHRAE safety matrix**: A/B for toxicity; 1/2L/3 for flammability.
- **Lubricant compatibility**: R-12 ↔ mineral oil; R-134a ↔ PAG; R-1234yf ↔ PAG/POE.
- **Transcritical cycle**: required when $T_{\text{cond}} > T_{\text{crit}}$; no condensation occurs, only gas cooling.
- **Numerical benchmarks**: $h_1 - h_4 \approx 130$ kJ/kg for R-134a; $\text{COP} \approx 3$ for well-designed automotive AC.
- **Final rule of thumb**: For *new passenger vehicles in 2024+*, R-1234yf is the **default choice**; R-744 is reserved for *specialty EV thermal management*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Outside and Inside Design Considerations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT523 - AUTOMOTIVE REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Outside and Inside Design Considerations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Outside Design Considerations** in automotive air conditioning refer to the engineering decisions, layout choices, and integration strategies applied to the *ambient-facing* components of the vehicle HVAC system — primarily the **condenser, compressor, receiver-drier, refrigerant lines, and the engine-compartment sub-system**. These decisions govern how effectively the system rejects heat to the surroundings, survives road/environmental hazards, and interfaces with the host vehicle's powertrain.

**Inside Design Considerations** refer to the engineering decisions applied to the *cabin-facing* components — primarily the **evaporator, blower assembly, expansion device, air-distribution ducting, control head, and air-quality subsystems**. These decisions govern passenger thermal comfort, demisting, air distribution uniformity, filtration, noise/vibration, and overall cabin environment quality.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 2 Focus:** The syllabus explicitly links the *refrigeration cycle hardware* to *vehicle integration constraints*. Marks are awarded for explaining *why* a component is located where it is, not merely *where* it is located.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine the human body on a hot summer day. The "outside design" is like your **sweat glands and skin surface** — they interface with the harsh outside world (sun, wind, dust, heat). They must be positioned to *push heat out* efficiently while surviving environmental abuse. The "inside design" is like your **lungs and circulatory system** — they sit safely inside, protected, distributing cool air precisely where needed, filtering contaminants, and remaining quiet enough to let you rest.

In a car:
- **"Outside"** = the condenser sitting in front of the radiator, exposed to ram-air, rain, stones, and engine bay heat.
- **"Inside"** = the evaporator tucked inside the HVAC box behind the dashboard, distributing chilled air through vents.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Engine compartment design temperature:** up to **120 °C (393 K)** in extreme under-hood conditions.
- **Ambient design temperature for capacity rating:** **+43 °C (316 K)** per SAE J639 standard.
- **Standard ram-air velocity at 100 km/h:** ~**27.78 m/s**.
- **Cabin target temperature:** **22 – 25 °C** (ASHRAE 55 comfort range).
- **Cabin relative humidity target:** **30 – 65 %**.
- **Standard refrigerant line working pressure (R-134a, R-1234yf):** high side up to **2.0 – 2.5 MPa**, low side **0.15 – 0.35 MPa**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these numbers matter in KTU exams:** Examiner questions frequently quote "design ambient" or "max under-hood temperature" to test whether you know the *boundary conditions* of automotive AC design. Always memorize the **+43 °C** and **120 °C** reference values.

## 1.4 Categorical Overview of Design Considerations

| Category | Outside Design | Inside Design |
|---|---|---|
| **Heat transfer** | Condenser sizing, ram-air utilization | Evaporator sizing, blower capacity |
| **Mechanics** | Compressor mount, belt drive, vibration | NVH isolation of HVAC box |
| **Fluid routing** | High/low pressure line layout, hose routing | Air duct geometry, vent placement |
| **Environment** | Debris, corrosion, under-hood heat | Cabin humidity, dust, allergens |
| **Controls** | Pressure switch, fan clutch | Thermostat, blend door, mode door |
| **Comfort** | Dehumidification (mist removal) | Temperature uniformity, draft-free airflow |
|</mm:think><!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Outside Design Considerations — Analytical Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Condenser Placement and Air-Flow Integration

The condenser is almost universally mounted **in front of the engine radiator** in the air-conditioning *condenser-radiator fan module*. This is dictated by three interacting constraints:

1. **Maximum availability of ram-air at lowest possible air temperature.**
2. **Minimum pressure drop in the condenser air-side (to keep fan power low).**
3. **Crash-energy management and pedestrian-safety regulations (Euro NCAP / AIS).**

**Why behind the grille, not on the roof?**
- Roof mounting increases vehicle CG (center of gravity), worsens aerodynamics, and exposes refrigerant lines to long unsupported runs (oil return problems).
- Front-mount receives the *highest mass flow* of cool ram-air when the car is moving, maximizing the **effectiveness (ε)** of the condenser.

The overall heat-rejection equation for the condenser is:

$$
\dot{Q}_{cond} = \dot{m}_{air} \cdot c_{p,air} \cdot (T_{air,out} - T_{air,in})
$$

For an R-134a condenser in a typical sedan, target values are:

$$
\dot{Q}_{cond} \approx 4 - 7 \text{ kW} \quad \text{at} \quad T_{amb} = 43\,°C
$$

### 2.1.2 Engine Cooling System Integration

The condenser and radiator share the **same cooling fan** (electric or viscous clutch-driven). Two arrangements exist:

- **Parallel-flow / side-by-side:** Two cores in series with the air stream — preferred for modern vehicles.
- **Stacked:** Condenser ahead of radiator — common in older designs.

The integration problem: at idle/low vehicle speed, the engine fan alone must provide **both** engine-cooling airflow and condenser airflow. The fan is therefore sized for the *sum* of the two heat loads:

$$
\dot{Q}_{fan} = \dot{Q}_{engine} + \dot{Q}_{condenser}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Point:** Many students incorrectly assume the condenser "steals" cooling from the engine. In fact, a properly designed system **shares** the fan. If the condenser is undersized, the high-side pressure spikes, compressor work increases, and engine fuel consumption rises by **5–10 %**.

### 2.1.3 Compressor Mounting and Drive

The compressor is **engine-driven** in conventional systems (via serpentine belt and electromagnetic clutch) or **electric-driven (HV-compressor)** in EVs and HEVs.

Key design rules:
- Mount on the **accessory drive side** of the engine.
- Avoid locations near the exhaust manifold (thermal damage to seals).
- Use **anti-vibration isolators** to decouple NVH.
- Provide **sufficient crankcase oil return** by limiting suction-line length and avoiding high pockets where oil can pool.

### 2.1.4 Refrigerant Line Routing and Hoses

- **Discharge (high-side) line** from compressor → condenser: short, high-pressure, must handle **T ≈ 80–120 °C**.
- **Liquid line** from condenser → receiver-drier → expansion valve: carries sub-cooled liquid.
- **Suction (low-side) line** from evaporator → compressor: insulated to prevent heat gain and condensation dripping.

Bend radius must be large to avoid **pressure-drop spikes** and to allow **oil return** (oil travels dissolved in refrigerant vapor, so low velocities must be avoided).

### 2.1.5 Receiver-Drier and Accumulator

- **Receiver-drier** on the high side (TXV systems) — stores excess refrigerant, filters moisture with desiccant, and provides liquid seal to TXV.
- **Accumulator** on the low side (EVR systems, common in modern cars) — prevents liquid slugging of compressor and absorbs moisture.

### 2.1.6 Environmental Protection

- Corrosion-resistant coatings (aluminum fins, zinc-rich primers).
- Stone-impact protection (plastic mesh, grilles).
- Drainage paths to expel condensate and rainwater.
- Adequate clearance for thermal expansion (~5–8 mm between hoses and hot surfaces).

## 2.2 Inside Design Considerations — Analytical Breakdown

### 2.2.1 Evaporator Placement

The evaporator is mounted inside the **HVAC plenum box** behind the dashboard, typically:

- **Vertically oriented, fin-and-tube type** (most common).
- **Microchannel / laminated** (premium/EV applications, weight saving).
- **Dual evaporators** for rear-cabin AC in long-wheelbase vehicles.

Design constraints:
- Must be **accessible for service** (often via glove-box removal).
- Must allow **even air distribution** to avoid cold spots.
- Must provide **adequate drain** for condensate (slope ≥ 5° toward drain tube).

### 2.2.2 Blower Assembly and Air Flow

The blower (centrifugal fan, single or dual speed) is sized to deliver a cabin air-mass flow of:

$$
\dot{m}_{cabin} \approx 150 - 300 \text{ kg/h} \quad (\text{per occupant equivalent})
$$

Cabin air-side heat balance:

$$
\dot{Q}_{evap} = \dot{m}_{cabin} \cdot (h_{in} - h_{out}) \approx \dot{m}_{cabin} \cdot c_{p,air} \cdot (T_{cabin} - T_{evap,out})
$$

Where typical **evaporator outlet air temperature** at peak load is **+3 °C to +7 °C** (must stay above 0 °C to prevent coil icing).

### 2.2.3 Air Distribution and Ducting

Modern HVAC boxes contain multiple **mode doors**, **blend doors**, and **defrost doors** that route airflow to:
- **Face vents** (instrument panel).
- **Foot-level vents**.
- **Defrost vents** (windshield base).
- **Rear cabin ducts** (multi-zone vehicles).

Design goals:
- Even temperature distribution (ΔT ≤ 3 °C head-to-foot).
- Low **air velocity at occupant** (≤ 0.5 m/s) to avoid "draft" sensation.
- Low static pressure drop (≤ 50 Pa) to keep blower noise low.

### 2.2.4 Temperature and Humidity Control

A **blend-air door** mixes cold evaporator air with hot heater-core air to achieve the desired discharge temperature. The **thermostatic control** modulates this door based on cabin temperature sensor input.

Humidity is controlled indirectly:
- Evaporator dehumidifies as a side-effect of cooling (moisture condenses on the cold coil and drains away).
- A **reheat cycle** (cool then partially reheat) provides dehumidification at neutral temperature — used in **defrost mode** to clear the windshield without overcooling the cabin.

### 2.2.5 Air Quality and Filtration

- **Particulate filter** (cabin air filter, often activated-carbon).
- **Recirculation door** allows fresh-air or recirculated-air modes. Recirculation improves cooling efficiency by ~10–15 % but must not be sealed for >15 min (CO₂ buildup).
- **HEPA filtration** in premium vehicles.
- Anti-bacterial treatment of evaporator surfaces (silver-ion coatings).

### 2.2.6 NVH (Noise, Vibration, Harshness) Considerations

- HVAC box lined with **melamine/PU foam** for acoustic absorption.
- Blower speed curves tuned to avoid **whistling** at vent outlets (target outlet velocity ≤ 5 m/s).
- Expansion valve/thermostat hiss masked by insulation.
- Compressor vibrations isolated from cabin via rubber mounts and tuned absorber.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dot{Q}_{cond} = \dot{m}_{air}\,c_{p,air}\,(T_{out}-T_{in})$ | Sensible heat rejected by condenser | kW |
| 2 | $\dot{Q}_{evap} = \dot{m}_{cabin}\,c_{p,air}\,(T_{cabin}-T_{evap,out})$ | Cabin sensible cooling load | kW |
| 3 | $\varepsilon_{cond} = \dfrac{T_{air,out}-T_{air,in}}{T_{refr,in}-T_{air,in}}$ | Condenser effectiveness (NTU method) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $COP = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{evap}}{\dot{W}_{comp}}$ | Coefficient of performance | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\dot{W}_{comp} = \dot{m}_{ref}\,(h_{2}-h_{1})$ | Compressor power from enthalpy rise | kW |
| 6 | $\Delta h_{evap} = h_{6} - h_{5}$ | Refrigerant enthalpy drop across evaporator | kJ/kg |
| 7 | $P_{high}/P_{low}$ ratio → refrigerant selection constraint | R-134a and R-1234yf both operable | MPa |
| 8 | $\dot{V}_{blower} \approx 250 - 450 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$ | Typical cabin blower volumetric flow | m³/h |
| 9 | $T_{evap,out} \geq 0\,°C$ | Constraint to prevent coil icing | °C |
| 10 | $A_{cond} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{cond}}{U_{cond}\,\Delta T_{lm}}$ | Required condenser surface area | m² |
| 11 | $A_{evap} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{evap}}{U_{evap}\,\Delta T_{lm}}$ | Required evaporator surface area | m² |
| 12 | $\Delta T_{lm} = \dfrac{\Delta T_{1}-\Delta T_{2}}{\ln(\Delta T_{1}/\Delta T_{2})}$ | Log-mean temperature difference | K |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Question A in Part B almost always asks you to size either the condenser or the evaporator using equations (1), (2), (10), and (11) in combination. Be sure to write the **assumed U-value** and **design ΔT** in your solution — examiners allocate 1–2 marks for stating these explicitly.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The integration principles covered here are used by every OEM HVAC team worldwide (e.g., Tata, Mahindra, Maruti, Toyota, GM). Failure modes traced back to bad design considerations include:

- **Battery thermal runaway** in EVs caused by under-hood condenser placement.
- **Windshield fogging** caused by poor defrost-door design.
- **Bearing failure** in compressors due to inadequate oil return from poorly routed suction lines.
- **Cabin noise complaints** from improperly damped HVAC boxes — a major warranty cost driver.
- **Reduced range** in EVs caused by oversized HVAC parasitic load (sometimes 20–30 % of total energy use in summer driving).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Condenser Sizing for Peak Summer Load

**Problem Statement (KTU Typical):**
A passenger car's AC system must reject **5.5 kW** at peak load. Ram-air enters the condenser at **T₁ = 43 °C** with a mass flow of **ṁ = 1.8 kg/s**. Determine:
(a) The required condenser exit air temperature.
(c_p,air = 1.005 kJ/kg·K)

**Step 1 — Identify the governing energy balance.**
The condenser rejects heat into the air stream. The sensible heating of air equals the refrigerant heat-rejection rate:

$$
\dot{Q}_{cond} = \dot{m}_{air}\,c_{p,air}\,(T_{air,out} - T_{air,in})
$$

**Step 2 — List knowns.**

$$
\dot{Q}_{cond} = 5.5 \text{ kW}, \quad \dot{m}_{air} = 1.8 \text{ kg/s}, \quad c_{p,air} = 1.005 \text{ kJ/kg·K}, \quad T_{air,in} = 43\,°C
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for T_air,out.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{air,out} - T_{air,in} &= \frac{\dot{Q}_{cond}}{\dot{m}_{air}\,c_{p,air}} \\
&= \frac{5.5}{(1.8)(1.005)} \\
&= \frac{5.5}{1.809} \\
&= 3.040 \text{ K}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute final exit temperature.**

$$
T_{air,out} = 43 + 3.04 = 46.04\,°C
$$

**Step 5 — Cross-check feasibility.**
A condenser air-side temperature rise of 3 K is realistic for parallel-flow condensers (typical: 2–6 K). ✔ Valid design.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Evaporator Sizing and Coil Outlet Constraint

**Problem Statement:**
A sedan requires cabin cooling of **3.2 kW**. Cabin air enters the evaporator at **35 °C** with a mass flow of **0.42 kg/s**. Determine:
(a) The evaporator outlet air temperature.
(b) Whether the outlet temperature violates the **icing constraint** (must be ≥ 0 °C).

**Step 1 — Apply the cabin-side energy balance.**

$$
\dot{Q}_{evap} = \dot{m}_{cabin}\,c_{p,air}\,(T_{in} - T_{out})
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for T_out.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{in} - T_{out} &= \frac{\dot{Q}_{evap}}{\dot{m}_{cabin}\,c_{p,air}} \\
&= \frac{3.2}{(0.42)(1.005)} \\
&= \frac{3.2}{0.4221} \\
&= 7.58 \text{ K}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute outlet temperature.**

$$
T_{out} = 35 - 7.58 = 27.42\,°C
$$

**Step 4 — Compare with icing constraint.**

$$
T_{out} = 27.42\,°C \quad \gg 0\,°C \;\; \text{✔ No icing risk.}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This calculation gives the *bulk* air outlet temperature. The *coil surface* temperature is colder (≈ 0–3 °C) because the air-side ΔT is what drives the heat transfer. KTU examiners often ask both: bulk air vs. coil surface.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Required Condenser Surface Area

**Problem Statement:**
A parallel-flow condenser must reject **5.5 kW** with a log-mean temperature difference of **12 K**. The overall heat-transfer coefficient is **U = 50 W/m²·K**. Find the required surface area.

**Step 1 — Recall heat-exchanger sizing equation.**

$$
\dot{Q} = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{lm}
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for A.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{\dot{Q}}{U \cdot \Delta T_{lm}} \\
&= \frac{5500 \text{ W}}{(50 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K})(12 \text{ K})} \\
&= \frac{5500}{600} \\
&= 9.17 \text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpret.**
A ~9 m² frontal area is typical for a mid-size sedan condenser (multi-row design with internal fins). Real condensers achieve this with high fin density (≥ 14 fins per inch).

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Compressor Power and COP

**Problem Statement:**
A compressor circulates R-134a at **ṁ = 0.06 kg/s**. Suction enthalpy **h₁ = 247 kJ/kg**, discharge enthalpy **h₂ = 279 kJ/kg**. Evaporator cooling effect = 188 kJ/kg. Find:
(a) Compressor power.
(b) COP.

**Step 1 — Compressor power.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{W}_{comp} &= \dot{m}_{ref}\,(h_2 - h_1) \\
&= (0.06)(279 - 247) \\
&= (0.06)(32) \\
&= 1.92 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Cooling capacity.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q}_{evap} &= \dot{m}_{ref}\,\Delta h_{evap} \\
&= (0.06)(188) \\
&= 11.28 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Coefficient of performance.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
COP &= \frac{\dot{Q}_{evap}}{\dot{W}_{comp}} \\
&= \frac{11.28}{1.92} \\
&= 5.875
\end{aligned}
$$

A COP of ~5.9 is **excellent** for a R-134a automotive system (typical: 4–6). Real COP is lower because of motor/electrical losses and refrigerant pressure-drop penalties.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Design Decision Trade-Offs

| Design Parameter | Outside (Condenser Side) | Inside (Evaporator Side) | Trade-off / KTU Insight |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Component Size** | Larger face area for heat rejection | Compact to fit behind dash | Bigger condenser = better cooling but more frontal area = lower cooling for engine |
| **Airflow Source** | Ram-air + engine fan | Cabin blower | Idle = no ram-air → engine fan must compensate |
| **Pressure Loss** | Minimize fan parasitic | Minimize blower noise | Both fight the same constraint: low ΔP for low power |
| **Weight** | Aluminum end tanks, plastic tanks | Microchannel, laminated | Lighter = better FE, but costlier |
| **Maintenance** | Easy access from grille | Behind glove-box | Serviceability vs. packaging |
| **Refrigerant Fill** | Larger receiver-drier (more charge) | Accumulator absorbs excess | Charge sensitive ± 50 g for R-1234yf |
| **NVH Priority** | Compressor isolation | HVAC box damping | Both are top-3 NVH sources in cars |
| **Crash Safety** | Pedestrian leg-impact zones | Airbag deployment zones | Condenser cannot be a "hard edge" |
| **Emission Compliance** | MAC Directive 2006/40/EC (low-GWP) | Refrigerant leakage prevention | R-1234yf / CO₂ adoption |
| **EV Consideration** | Heat-pump condenser / chiller | Multi-zone climate | AC becomes primary cabin heater in winter |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Outside and Inside Design Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Vehicle AC System] --> B[Outside Subsystem]
    A --> C[Inside Subsystem]

    B --> B1[Front Grille Region]
    B --> B2[Engine Compartment]
    B --> B3[Chassis Underbody]

    B1 --> B1a[Condenser Core]
    B1 --> B1b[Condenser Fan / E-Fan]
    B1 --> B1c[Receiver-Drier]
    B1 --> B1d[Ambient Air Intake]

    B2 --> B2a[Compressor]
    B2 --> B2b[Compressor Clutch]
    B2 --> B2c[Drive Belt / HV Electric]
    B2 --> B2d[Engine Block Heat Zone]
    B2 --> B2e[Pressure Switches]

    B3 --> B3a[High-Pressure Discharge Line]
    B3 --> B3b[Low-Pressure Suction Line]
    B3 --> B3c[Quick Couplers for Service]

    C --> C1[HVAC Plenum Box Behind Dashboard]
    C --> C2[Cabin Interior]

    C1 --> C1a[Evaporator Core]
    C1 --> C1b[Expansion Valve TXV]
    C1 --> C1c[Blower Motor and Fan]
    C1 --> C1d[Heater Core]
    C1 --> C1e[Blend Air Door]
    C1 --> C1f[Mode Door Assembly]
    C1 --> C1g[Recirculation Door]
    C1 --> C1h[Cabin Air Filter]

    C2 --> C2a[Face Vents]
    C2 --> C2b[Foot Vents]
    C2 --> C2c[Defrost Vents]
    C2 --> C2d[Rear Cabin Ducts]
    C2 --> C2e[Cabin Temp and Humidity Sensors]
    C2 --> C2f[Sunload Sensor]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Outside Design Airflow and Heat-Rejection Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    RAM[Ram Air at 100 km/h] --> CFLOW[27.78 m/s Approaching]
    CFLOW --> RAD[Engine Radiator]
    RAD --> COND[AC Condenser]
    COND --> ENG[Engine Bay Hot Air]
    ENG --> FAN[E Fan or Viscous Fan]
    FAN --> AMB[Exhaust to Ambient]

    HPR[High Pressure Refrigerant from Compressor] --> COND
    COND --> REC[Receiver Drier]
    REC --> EXP[Expansion Valve at Evaporator Inlet]
    EXP --> EVAP[Evaporator Inside Cabin]

    EVAP --> LPR[Low Pressure Vapor]
    LPR --> COMP[Compressor Suction]
    COMP --> HPR
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Inside Design Cabin Air Path and Controls

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    OUTF[Outside Air] --> FLT[Cabin Air Filter]
    RECIRC[Recirculated Cabin Air] --> MIX[Recirculation Door]
    FLT --> MIX
    MIX --> BLO[Blower Motor]
    BLO --> EVP[Evaporator Core]
    EVP --> BLEND[Blend Air Door]
    HTR[Heater Core] --> BLEND
    BLEND --> MODE[Mode Door Assembly]
    MODE --> FACE[Face Vents]
    MODE --> FOOT[Foot Vents]
    MODE --> DEFROST[Defrost Vents]
    MODE --> REAR[Rear Cabin Ducts]

    CTRL[Climate Control Head] --> MIX
    CTRL --> BLO
    CTRL --> BLEND
    CTRL --> MODE
    CTRL --> COMP[Compressor Clutch]

    S1[Cabin Temp Sensor] --> CTRL
    S2[Ambient Temp Sensor] --> CTRL
    S3[Sunload Sensor] --> CTRL
    S4[Humidity Sensor] --> CTRL
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** The *outside* side is dominated by heat-rejection airflow and the refrigerant high-side loop. The *inside* side is dominated by air-distribution, mixing, and the refrigerant low-side loop. Both sides meet at the **expansion device** and the **compressor**, forming a closed thermodynamic cycle bridged by the vehicle architecture.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — 3 Mark Questions

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**List any three outside design considerations for an automotive air-conditioning system and justify the choice of front-end condenser mounting.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The three outside design considerations are:

1. **Condenser placement and ram-air availability** — Front-end mounting ahead of (or beside) the radiator ensures maximum mass-flow of cool ambient air at high vehicle speeds, maximizing heat-rejection effectiveness.

2. **Engine cooling integration** — The condenser and radiator share the same electric/viscous fan. At idle, when ram-air is absent, this fan alone must meet both the engine-cooling load and the AC heat-rejection load.

3. **Crash safety and pedestrian protection** — The condenser position is restricted by Euro NCAP / AIS pedestrian leg-impact zones, requiring soft end tanks and breakaway mounts.

*Mark split: 1 mark for listing, 1 mark for justification of front mount, 1 mark for crash integration.*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Explain the role of the blend-air door in automotive AC inside design. Why is the evaporator outlet air temperature limited to ≥ 0 °C?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **blend-air door** is a controlled flap inside the HVAC plenum box that **mixes cold evaporator air with hot heater-core air** to deliver air at the user-selected discharge temperature. It enables stepless temperature control without varying compressor load.

The **evaporator outlet air temperature must be ≥ 0 °C** because:
- If the coil surface drops below 0 °C, **atmospheric moisture condenses and freezes** on the fins, forming a frost layer.
- Frost **blocks airflow**, collapses the cooling capacity, and may cause **liquid refrigerant carryover** to the compressor, causing slugging damage.
- A thermostatic switch (T-stat) cycles the compressor or adjusts blower speed to maintain this constraint.

*Mark split: 1.5 marks for blend-air door, 1.5 marks for icing constraint.*

---

## Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **outside design considerations** for the condenser, compressor, and refrigerant line routing in an automotive AC system. Justify each with reference to real-world vehicle constraints. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A front-mounted parallel-flow condenser in a sedan must reject **6.0 kW** of heat. The ram-air enters at **T₁ = 40 °C** with a mass flow of **ṁ = 2.0 kg/s**. Determine:
  (i) The condenser exit air temperature. **(3 marks)**
  (ii) The required condenser surface area if U = 55 W/m²·K and ΔT_lm = 11 K. **(3 marks)**
  (iii) Comment on whether the design is feasible for a typical mid-size sedan. **(1 mark)**

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — 7 marks

1. **Condenser placement (2 marks):**
   - Mounted ahead of (or beside) the engine radiator to maximize ram-air utilization.
   - Receives cool, dense air at high vehicle speeds.
   - Subject to crash-safety constraints (must not intrude into pedestrian leg-impact zone).
   - Corrosion-resistant coatings required for road salt and stone impact.

2. **Compressor mounting (2 marks):**
   - Driven by serpentine belt via electromagnetic clutch (conventional ICE) or HV electric motor (EVs).
   - Located on accessory-drive side; not adjacent to exhaust manifold.
   - Anti-vibration mounts to decouple NVH.
   - Adequate crankcase oil return ensured by limiting suction-line vertical pockets.

3. **Refrigerant line routing (2 marks):**
   - **Discharge (high-pressure)** line: short routing, T = 80–120 °C, must use barrier hose for R-1234yf.
   - **Suction (low-pressure)** line: insulated to prevent heat gain and sweat/condensation.
   - Large bend radii to limit pressure drop and ensure oil entrainment.
   - Service ports (high/low) provided at accessible locations.

4. **Receiver-drier / Accumulator placement (1 mark):**
   - High-side drier immediately after condenser (TXV systems).
   - Low-side accumulator before compressor suction port (EVR systems).

#### Part (b) — 7 marks

**(i) Condenser exit air temperature (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q}_{cond} &= \dot{m}_{air}\,c_{p,air}\,(T_{out} - T_{in}) \\
T_{out} - T_{in} &= \frac{6.0}{(2.0)(1.005)} = \frac{6.0}{2.01} = 2.985 \text{ K} \\
T_{out} &= 40 + 2.985 = 42.99 \approx 43.0\,°C
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Required surface area (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{\dot{Q}}{U \cdot \Delta T_{lm}} \\
&= \frac{6000}{(55)(11)} \\
&= \frac{6000}{605} \\
&= 9.92 \text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Feasibility comment (1 mark):**
A condenser frontal area of ~10 m² is **achievable** in a mid-size sedan using a multi-row parallel-flow core with high fin density. The 3 K air-side temperature rise is also realistic and indicates the design is feasible.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2]**

**(a)** Discuss the **inside design considerations** for the HVAC plenum box, evaporator, blower, and air-distribution ducting in a modern passenger car. Include the role of the **recirculation door** and **cabin air filter** in air quality. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The cabin AC system of a sedan provides a cooling capacity of **4.0 kW**. Cabin air enters the evaporator at **T = 33 °C** with a mass flow of **ṁ = 0.50 kg/s**. Determine:
  (i) The evaporator outlet air temperature. **(2 marks)**
  (ii) Whether icing will occur. **(1 mark)**
  (iii) The required evaporator area if U = 35 W/m²·K and ΔT_lm = 18 K. **(2 marks)**
  (iv) The COP if the compressor consumes **1.10 kW** of shaft power. **(2 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — 7 marks

1. **HVAC plenum box design (2 marks):**
   - Sealed, foam-lined enclosure behind dashboard.
   - Houses evaporator, heater core, blower, and all mode/blend doors.
   - Designed for low internal pressure drop and acoustic absorption.

2. **Evaporator placement and design (2 marks):**
   - Typically vertical, fin-and-tube, with condensate drain.
   - Sizing based on cabin cooling load and allowable air-side ΔT.
   - Coil outlet T ≥ 0 °C enforced by thermostatic control.

3. **Blower and air distribution (2 marks):**
   - Centrifugal blower, 3–4 speed, sized for ~250–450 m³/h.
   - Duct routing to face, foot, defrost, and rear vents.
   - Outlet velocity ≤ 5 m/s to minimize draft sensation and noise.

4. **Recirculation door and cabin filter (1 mark):**
   - Recirculation improves cooling efficiency by 10–15 %.
   - Cabin filter (often activated-carbon) removes dust, pollen, NO_x.
   - Anti-bacterial evaporator coatings reduce microbial growth.

#### Part (b) — 7 marks

**(i) Evaporator outlet air temperature (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{in} - T_{out} &= \frac{\dot{Q}_{evap}}{\dot{m}\,c_{p,air}} = \frac{4.0}{(0.50)(1.005)} = \frac{4.0}{0.5025} = 7.96 \text{ K} \\
T_{out} &= 33 - 7.96 = 25.04\,°C
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Icing check (1 mark):**
T_out = 25.04 °C ≫ 0 °C. **No icing risk.** ✔

**(iii) Evaporator area (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{\dot{Q}}{U \cdot \Delta T_{lm}} \\
&= \frac{4000}{(35)(18)} \\
&= \frac{4000}{630} \\
&= 6.35 \text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) COP (2 marks):**

$$
COP = \frac{\dot{Q}_{evap}}{\dot{W}_{comp}} = \frac{4.0}{1.10} = 3.64
$$

A COP of 3.64 is **acceptable** for an R-134a automotive system with realistic losses.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit mismatches:** Always convert kW → W before applying $A = \dot{Q}/(U \cdot \Delta T_{lm})$ if U is in W/m²·K.
> 2. **Stating design assumptions:** Always write assumed values of U and ΔT_lm explicitly; examiners reward 1 mark for this even if your numerical answer is wrong.
> 3. **Ignoring icing constraint:** Every evaporator question must include a comment on the ≥ 0 °C limit. Skipping it costs 1 mark.
> 4. **Mixing up inside/outside:** If the question asks for *outside* considerations, do *not* explain blend doors or recirculation — stay on condenser, compressor, lines, and engine-bay integration.
> 5. **Forgetting R-1234yf context:** Modern KTU questions expect awareness of low-GWP refrigerants. Mention R-1234yf or CO₂ at least once.
> 6. **NVH and crash safety ignored:** These are the most-skipped high-yield topics in KTU 2024 papers. Each is worth 1–2 marks.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Outside design** = everything in the engine bay and front grille: condenser, compressor, receiver-drier/accumulator, refrigerant lines, pressure switches, engine-fan integration.
- **Inside design** = everything behind the dashboard: HVAC box, evaporator, TXV, blower, blend/mode/recirculation doors, cabin filter, vents, sensors, and control head.
- **Front-end condenser mounting** is preferred for maximum ram-air cooling, but must respect **pedestrian safety** zones and share cooling airflow with the **engine radiator** via the same fan.
- **Compressor mounting** is dictated by accessory-drive geometry, NVH isolation, and proximity to hot exhaust components; refrigerant line routing must ensure **oil return** (no high pockets in suction line).
- **Evaporator outlet air temperature** must be **≥ 0 °C** to prevent coil icing and liquid-slug damage to the compressor.
- **Blower sizing** ≈ 250–450 m³/h volumetric flow, with vent outlet velocity ≤ 5 m/s for draft-free comfort.
- **Blend-air door** is the heart of stepless temperature control inside the cabin.
- **Recirculation mode** saves 10–15 % cooling energy but must not exceed 15 min continuous use (CO₂ buildup).
- **Design ambient temperature** for capacity rating = **+43 °C** (SAE J639).
- **Under-hood design temperature** = up to **120 °C** — every component must survive this.
- **High-side pressure** ≈ 2.0–2.5 MPa; **low-side pressure** ≈ 0.15–0.35 MPa for R-134a/R-1234yf.
- **Condenser sizing equation:** $A = \dot{Q}/(U \cdot \Delta T_{lm})$, similarly for evaporator.
- **COP** for typical R-134a automotive systems lies in the range **3.5–6** depending on operating conditions.
- **NVH isolation** of compressor and **acoustic damping** of HVAC box are non-negotiable for OEM quality.
- **R-1234yf** (GWP = 4) and **R-744 (CO₂)** are the present and future of low-GWP automotive refrigerants (EU MAC Directive, 2006/40/EC).
- **EV-specific** considerations: AC is now a major parasitic load (up to 30 % of energy in summer) and a **heat-pump** doubles as a cabin heater in winter — drastically changing "inside" and "outside" design choices.
- **Always** restate U and ΔT_lm before any sizing calculation in a KTU answer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Advice:** When asked to "discuss" design considerations, structure your answer as **outside vs. inside** with **at least 3 numbered points on each side**. Use a labeled sketch or Mermaid diagram if allowed. Always close with a one-line **real-world application** sentence — examiners award 1 mark for "practical engineering awareness."
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Factors Forming the Load on Refrigeration and Air Conditioning Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT523 - AUTOMOTIVE REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Factors Forming the Load on Refrigeration and Air Conditioning Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

In the context of **Automotive Refrigeration and Air Conditioning (PEAUT523)**, the **cooling load** of a vehicle HVAC system is defined as the **rate of sensible and latent heat energy (measured in Watts or kW)** that must be continuously removed by the refrigeration cycle to maintain a prescribed, comfortable cabin air condition against all external and internal thermal disturbances.

Mathematically, the total instantaneous cooling load $Q_{total}$ imposed on an automotive AC system is the summation of multiple independent heat-gain terms:

$$Q_{total} = Q_{transmission} + Q_{radiation} + Q_{occupants} + Q_{ventilation} + Q_{engine} + Q_{equipment} + Q_{misc}$$

Each term corresponds to a specific **load-forming factor** (also called a heat-gain source) which is determined by vehicle geometry, glazing area, ambient conditions, occupancy, and operational profile.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight — Module 2 Focus:**
> The student must be able to **identify, classify, and quantitatively evaluate every dominant factor** that contributes to the refrigeration load. The examiner specifically looks for the student's ability to recognize **steady-state vs. transient** loads, and **sensible vs. latent** heat components.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are sitting inside a parked car at **Kochi** during peak summer at **2:00 PM**. The cabin feels like an oven. Why?

- The **sunlight piercing through the windshield** acts like a giant magnifying glass pouring energy into the cabin (solar radiation through glass).
- The **metal roof and doors**, although painted, conduct the outside hot air into the cabin (transmission load).
- You open the windows for 2 seconds to grab something — **hot, humid outside air rushes in** (infiltration / ventilation load).
- **Your body and your friend's body** are constantly radiating metabolic heat (occupant load).
- The **engine bay**, just inches from the firewall, blasts residual heat into the cabin (engine heat ingress).
- The **stereo, dashboard electronics, and lighting** silently dissipate heat (equipment load).

All of these heat streams combine simultaneously. The compressor in your car must "pump out" the *sum total* of all this heat to keep the cabin at, say, **24 °C**. That sum is the **refrigeration load**. The bigger the load, the harder the compressor works, the more fuel the engine burns.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Summary:**
> The refrigeration load is simply the *total heat enemy* the AC system must fight. Each "factor" is a separate enemy combatant — sun, outside air, passengers, engine, electronics. The compressor is the warrior that must defeat *all* of them simultaneously.

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics & Units

| Metric | Symbol | Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Heat Transfer Rate | $Q$ | **W (Watts)** or **kW** | SI standard |
| Temperature Difference | $\Delta T$ | **K** or **°C** | Difference is identical in both |
| Area | $A$ | **m²** | Glazing, wall, roof surface |
| Mass flow rate | $\dot{m}$ | **kg/s** | Ventilation air, refrigerant |
| Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient | $U$ | **W/(m²·K)** | Combined convection + conduction + radiation |
| Specific Heat (constant pressure) | $c_p$ | **kJ/(kg·K)** | Air: **1.005 kJ/(kg·K)** |
| Latent Heat of Vaporization | $h_{fg}$ | **kJ/kg** | Water: **~2450 kJ/kg** at 25 °C |
| Density of Air | $\rho$ | **kg/m³** | Air at 35 °C ≈ **1.15 kg/m³** |
| Solar Heat Gain Factor | $SHGF$ | **W/m²** | Tabulated vs. latitude, month, hour |
| Shading Coefficient | $SC$ | **dimensionless** | Glass-type dependent (0 to 1) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Load-Forming Factors

The complete list of factors is grouped into **six functional families**. KTU examiners commonly present a 14-mark question asking the student to *classify and explain* each family with a sketch.

### 2.1.1 External (Envelope) Heat Gains
These are heat flows from the ambient environment into the conditioned cabin through the vehicle's body shell.

**(a) Heat Transmission through Opaque Body Panels (Roof, Doors, Floor, Hood)**
Heat conducted through the metal body shell. Dominated by the *overall heat transfer coefficient* $U$ of the panel.

**Why it matters:** A black car parked in the sun will have a roof surface temperature of **60–75 °C**. The cabin air below is heated by *conduction + radiation* through this thin metal skin.

**(b) Heat Gain through Glazing (Windshield, Rear Window, Side Windows)**
Glass is **transparent to short-wave solar radiation** but opaque to long-wave (re-radiated) energy — this is the famous *greenhouse effect* inside a car. Two sub-loads exist:
- *Conduction part* = $U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ (due to temperature difference)
- *Radiation part* = $SHGF \cdot A \cdot SC$ (due to direct + diffuse solar radiation)

**Why it matters:** Windshield area is typically the single largest solar-gain contributor in a sedan — a critical KTU problem area.

**(c) Heat Gain through Ventilation and Infiltration**
Every time a door opens, or fresh-air mode is selected, hot/humid ambient air enters. Two parts:
- *Sensible load* (temperature change)
- *Latent load* (humidity/moisture addition)

**Why it matters:** In tropical Kerala, the **latent** part (moisture) is often 30–40 % of the total summer load.

### 2.1.2 Internal Heat Gains
Heat generated *inside* the cabin itself.

**(d) Heat from Occupants (Passenger Metabolic Load)**
Each human body is a biological heater. Emits **sensible heat** (warming the air) and **latent heat** (sweat evaporation). A typical adult at light activity in an AC car dissipates roughly **70–80 W sensible + 50–60 W latent = ~120 W total**.

**(e) Heat from Electrical Equipment**
Stereo head unit, dashboard displays, LED lighting, seat heaters, ADAS sensors — all electrical energy is ultimately converted to heat inside the cabin (1st Law of Thermodynamics).

### 2.1.3 Powertrain-Derived Heat Gains
**(f) Engine Bay Heat Ingress**
In a front-engine vehicle, the firewall separates the hot engine bay from the cabin. Heat flows into the cabin by conduction through the firewall + radiation from the exhaust manifold. Electric vehicles (EVs) significantly reduce this term — a common KTU comparison question.

### 2.1.4 Miscellaneous Loads
**(g) Miscellaneous / Safety Factors**
Heat from road reflection, defrost operation, reheat from dehumidification, heat from refrigerant piping inside the cabin, duct heat gains, etc. KTU convention is to lump these into a **10–15 % safety margin** on the calculated total.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Board-Exam Ready)

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical LaTeX Note for Tables:** Inside markdown tables, the vertical pipe `|` symbol will break rendering. The formulas below use `\vert` for absolute value and the raw unit slash to ensure the table renders correctly.

| # | Load Factor | Governing Formula | Variables & Units | Typical KTU Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Transmission through walls/roof | $Q_{wall} = U_{wall} \cdot A_{wall} \cdot \Delta T$ | $U$: W/(m²·K), $A$: m², $\Delta T$: K | 100–400 W |
| 2 | Solar gain through glass | $Q_{glass} = U_{glass} \cdot A_{glass} \cdot \Delta T + SHGF \cdot A_{glass} \cdot SC$ | $SHGF$: W/m², $SC$: dimensionless | 200–800 W (peak sun) |
| 3 | Occupant metabolic | $Q_{occ} = n \cdot (q_{sens} + q_{lat})$ | $n$: number, $q$: W per person | 100 W × occupants |
| 4 | Infiltration sensible | $Q_{inf,s} = \dot{V} \cdot \rho \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | $\dot{V}$: m³/s, $\rho$: kg/m³, $c_p$: kJ/(kg·K) | 50–250 W |
| 5 | Infiltration latent | $Q_{inf,l} = \dot{V} \cdot \rho \cdot h_{fg} \cdot \Delta \omega$ | $\omega$: humidity ratio, $h_{fg}$: kJ/kg | 50–200 W |
| 6 | Equipment/electrical | $Q_{equip} = P_{elec} \cdot 1.0$ | $P_{elec}$: W | 30–150 W |
| 7 | Engine bay ingress | $Q_{eng} = U_{fw} \cdot A_{fw} \cdot (T_{eng} - T_{cab})$ | $fw$: firewall | 100–500 W (ICE) |
| 8 | Safety / miscellaneous | $Q_{misc} = 0.10 \text{ to } 0.15 \times Q_{sum}$ | Empirical factor | 10–15 % uplift |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Capacity Sizing:** Automotive AC compressors (swash-plate, scroll, or electric in EVs) are rated in **kW of cooling capacity**. An undersized compressor cannot handle the combined load, leading to poor cabin comfort.
- **Energy Efficiency:** In EVs, the AC system can consume **15–30 %** of total battery energy. Reducing the load factors (better glazing, IR-reflective coatings, higher insulation $R$-value) directly extends driving range.
- **Refrigerant Selection:** Higher latent loads demand refrigerants with high latent heat capacity (R-134a, R-1234yf) — directly tied to the *load profile*.
- **Control Strategy:** Modern automatic climate control systems use the load profile to predictively command compressor speed and blower RPM.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Total Refrigeration Load Equation

The principle of **conservation of energy** (steady-state, control-volume around the cabin) requires that, for the cabin temperature to remain constant, the *net rate of heat removed by the refrigeration system* must exactly equal *the sum of all heat rates entering the cabin*:

$$\sum_{i} \dot{Q}_{in,i} = \dot{Q}_{removed}$$

The refrigeration cycle removes heat at the evaporator at a rate:

$$\dot{Q}_{evap} = \dot{m}_{ref} \cdot (h_{g,out} - h_{f,in})$$

where $\dot{m}_{ref}$ is refrigerant mass flow rate (kg/s), $h_{g,out}$ is enthalpy of superheated vapor leaving the evaporator, and $h_{f,in}$ is enthalpy of saturated liquid entering. For design purposes, this must equal the total load:

$$\boxed{\dot{Q}_{evap} = Q_{wall} + Q_{glass} + Q_{occ} + Q_{inf} + Q_{equip} + Q_{eng} + Q_{misc}}$$

This identity is the **foundation of every cooling-load calculation** in Module 2.

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation — Solar Heat Gain Through Glazing

Consider a single piece of automotive glass (windshield) of area $A_g$ exposed to direct + diffuse solar radiation. The total instantaneous heat gain has two components:

### Step 1 — Identify the two physical mechanisms
1. **Conduction** through the glass driven by the temperature difference $T_{out} - T_{in}$.
2. **Radiation** of short-wave solar energy that is transmitted, absorbed, and re-radiated as long-wave energy into the cabin (trapped by the glass).

### Step 2 — Conduction component
By Fourier's law combined with the overall heat-transfer coefficient:

$$Q_{cond} = U_g \cdot A_g \cdot (T_{out} - T_{in})$$

### Step 3 — Radiation component
The intensity of solar radiation striking the surface is given by the **Solar Heat Gain Factor** $SHGF$, a tabulated value depending on latitude, month, hour of day, and surface orientation. The fraction of this that is admitted into the cabin and converted to cooling load is determined by the **Shading Coefficient** $SC$ (which is itself $\dfrac{\text{SHGC of actual glass}}{\text{SHGC of reference 3 mm clear glass}}$).

$$Q_{rad} = SHGF \cdot A_g \cdot SC$$

### Step 4 — Combine both components

$$Q_{glass} = U_g \cdot A_g \cdot \Delta T + SHGF \cdot A_g \cdot SC$$

### Step 5 — Sample numerical evaluation

Suppose a sedan has a windshield area $A_g = 1.1$ m², overall heat transfer coefficient $U_g = 5.8$ W/(m²·K), outdoor temperature $T_{out} = 38$ °C, cabin setpoint $T_{in} = 24$ °C, and the noonday $SHGF$ for Kerala (≈10 °N) in May is **630 W/m²**, with laminated glass $SC = 0.82$.

**Sub-step 5a — Conduction term:**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{cond} &= U_g \cdot A_g \cdot (T_{out} - T_{in}) \\
&= 5.8 \times 1.1 \times (38 - 24) \\
&= 5.8 \times 1.1 \times 14 \\
&= 89.32 \text{ W}
\end{aligned}$$

**Sub-step 5b — Radiation term:**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{rad} &= SHGF \cdot A_g \cdot SC \\
&= 630 \times 1.1 \times 0.82 \\
&= 568.26 \text{ W}
\end{aligned}$$

**Sub-step 5c — Total solar gain through windshield:**

$$Q_{glass} = 89.32 + 568.26 = 657.58 \text{ W}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The radiation (solar) term is roughly **6× larger** than the conduction term. This is why *reflective/IR-blocking glass coatings* (which lower $SC$ to 0.4–0.5) are so effective in tropical climates like Kerala.

## 3.3 Detailed Derivation — Ventilation / Infiltration Load

A vehicle's HVAC system admits a controlled fresh-air flow $\dot{V}$ in m³/s. Each second, this volume of outside air at temperature $T_{out}$ and humidity ratio $\omega_{out}$ must be conditioned down to cabin conditions $T_{in}$, $\omega_{in}$.

### Step 1 — Sensible part (temperature reduction)

$$Q_{inf,s} = \dot{m}_{air} \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{out} - T_{in}) = \dot{V} \cdot \rho_{air} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$$

### Step 2 — Latent part (moisture condensation)

$$Q_{inf,l} = \dot{m}_{air} \cdot h_{fg} \cdot (\omega_{out} - \omega_{in}) = \dot{V} \cdot \rho_{air} \cdot h_{fg} \cdot \Delta \omega$$

### Step 3 — Total infiltration load

$$Q_{inf} = Q_{inf,s} + Q_{inf,l} = \dot{V} \cdot \rho_{air} \cdot \left[ c_p \cdot \Delta T + h_{fg} \cdot \Delta \omega \right]$$

### Step 4 — Numerical check (Kerala morning)
Take $\dot{V} = 0.005$ m³/s (≈300 L/min), $\rho = 1.15$ kg/m³, $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/(kg·K), $h_{fg} = 2450$ kJ/kg, $\Delta T = 14$ K, $\Delta \omega = 0.012$ kg water/kg dry air.

**Sensible part:**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{inf,s} &= 0.005 \times 1.15 \times 1.005 \times 14 \\
&= 0.0809 \text{ kW} = 80.9 \text{ W}
\end{aligned}$$

**Latent part:**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{inf,l} &= 0.005 \times 1.15 \times 2450 \times 0.012 \\
&= 0.1691 \text{ kW} = 169.1 \text{ W}
\end{aligned}$$

**Total:**

$$Q_{inf} = 80.9 + 169.1 = 250.0 \text{ W}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** In coastal Kerala, the **latent** component is approximately **2× the sensible** component of the ventilation load. This explains why the evaporator must be cold enough (typically **2–5 °C** at the coil surface) to condense moisture — it is not just *cooling* air, it is *dehumidifying* it.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Sensible vs. Latent vs. Radiative

| Load Factor | Type | Dominant Physics | Time Behavior | % of Total (typical) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Conduction through walls | Sensible | Conduction + convection | **Steady-state** | 8–12 % |
| Solar through glass | Sensible + radiative | Short-wave radiation | **Cyclic / diurnal** | 25–35 % |
| Occupant heat | Sensible + latent | Metabolism + perspiration | Steady | 15–20 % |
| Infiltration / ventilation | Sensible + latent | Convection + condensation | Steady (with door events) | 15–25 % |
| Engine bay | Sensible | Conduction + radiation | Steady (ICE) | 8–18 % |
| Equipment / electrical | Sensible | Joule heating ($P = I^2 R$) | Steady | 3–6 % |
| Miscellaneous / safety | Both | Empirical | Variable | 10–15 % uplift |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**"List any six factors that form the cooling load of an automotive air conditioning system."**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 0.5 each):**
1. Heat transmission through opaque body panels (roof, doors, floor).
2. Solar heat gain through glazing (windshield, windows).
3. Heat from occupants (sensible + latent metabolic).
4. Infiltration / ventilation load from outside air.
5. Heat ingress from engine bay (firewall conduction).
6. Heat from cabin electrical equipment (stereo, lights, displays).
7. *(Optional 7th for partial credit)* Miscellaneous + safety factor.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often write only *external* or only *internal* sources. You must list **at least 3 external + 3 internal** to secure full marks. The KTU answer key penalizes single-sided lists.

---

### Question A2 — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**"Differentiate between sensible heat load and latent heat load in an automotive AC system, giving one example of each."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Sensible heat load:** Heat that causes a measurable change in the *dry-bulb temperature* of cabin air, with no change in moisture content. *Example:* Heat conducted through the metal roof on a hot day.
- **Latent heat load:** Heat associated with the *phase change* of moisture (evaporation of sweat, condensation of humidity on the evaporator). *Example:* Moisture removed from infiltrating humid outside air as it passes over the cold evaporator coil.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Do **not** confuse latent heat with *hidden* heat in a general sense. In the KTU context, *latent* **specifically** refers to water-vapour phase change. Writing "latent = heat we cannot feel" loses 1 mark.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper Adapted]

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, **explain the major external and internal factors that form the cooling load of an automotive air conditioning system**. Classify each factor as *steady-state* or *transient*, and *sensible* or *latent*. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A sedan car has the following data:
- Roof + doors + floor opaque area: $A_{op} = 6.5$ m², $U_{op} = 4.2$ W/(m²·K)
- Glazing area: $A_g = 2.0$ m², $U_g = 5.8$ W/(m²·K), $SC = 0.75$
- Outdoor temperature: 39 °C, Cabin setpoint: 23 °C
- $SHGF$ for May noon at 10 °N latitude: 640 W/m²
- Number of occupants: 4, each dissipating 60 W sensible and 55 W latent
- Ventilation flow: $\dot{V} = 0.006$ m³/s, $\rho = 1.15$ kg/m³, $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/(kg·K), $h_{fg} = 2450$ kJ/kg, $\Delta \omega = 0.011$ kg/kg
- Equipment: 110 W
- Engine firewall ingress: 280 W
- Safety factor: 12 %

**Calculate the total refrigeration load on the AC system.** **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks):**

**External factors (3 marks):**
1. **Solar heat gain through glazing** — sun's short-wave radiation passes through glass, is absorbed by interior surfaces, and re-radiates as long-wave heat (greenhouse effect). *Type: Sensible + Radiative, Transient (diurnal).*
2. **Conduction through opaque body panels** — heat flow through metal roof, doors, floor driven by $T_{out} - T_{in}$. *Type: Sensible, Steady-state.*
3. **Infiltration / ventilation load** — outside air entering through door events and the HVAC fresh-air inlet. *Type: Sensible + Latent, Steady with spikes.*

**Internal factors (3 marks):**
4. **Occupant metabolic heat** — sensible (warming air) + latent (perspiration). *Type: Sensible + Latent, Steady.*
5. **Electrical equipment heat** — stereo, lights, displays, ADAS. *Type: Sensible, Steady.*
6. **Engine bay heat ingress** — through firewall from a 90–110 °C engine bay. *Type: Sensible, Steady (ICE vehicles).*

**Sketch (1 mark):** Draw a car silhouette with arrows of solar (windshield), conduction (roof/doors), infiltration (window gap), and engine (firewall) all converging on a central cabin box. Label each arrow with the corresponding $Q$ symbol.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b) — Step-by-Step (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Conduction through opaque panels:**

$$Q_{op} = U_{op} \cdot A_{op} \cdot \Delta T = 4.2 \times 6.5 \times (39 - 23) = 4.2 \times 6.5 \times 16 = 436.8 \text{ W}$$

**Step 2 — Solar gain through glazing (conduction part):**

$$Q_{g,cond} = U_g \cdot A_g \cdot \Delta T = 5.8 \times 2.0 \times 16 = 185.6 \text{ W}$$

**Step 3 — Solar gain through glazing (radiation part):**

$$Q_{g,rad} = SHGF \cdot A_g \cdot SC = 640 \times 2.0 \times 0.75 = 960.0 \text{ W}$$

**Step 4 — Total glazing load:**

$$Q_{glass} = 185.6 + 960.0 = 1145.6 \text{ W}$$

**Step 5 — Occupant load:**

$$Q_{occ} = n \cdot (q_{sens} + q_{lat}) = 4 \times (60 + 55) = 4 \times 115 = 460 \text{ W}$$

**Step 6 — Ventilation sensible load:**

$$Q_{vent,s} = \dot{V} \cdot \rho \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T = 0.006 \times 1.15 \times 1.005 \times 16 = 0.1109 \text{ kW} = 110.9 \text{ W}$$

**Step 7 — Ventilation latent load:**

$$Q_{vent,l} = \dot{V} \cdot \rho \cdot h_{fg} \cdot \Delta \omega = 0.006 \times 1.15 \times 2450 \times 0.011 = 0.1859 \text{ kW} = 185.9 \text{ W}$$

**Step 8 — Total ventilation:**

$$Q_{vent} = 110.9 + 185.9 = 296.8 \text{ W}$$

**Step 9 — Equipment load:** $Q_{eqp} = 110$ W (given directly).

**Step 10 — Engine firewall:** $Q_{eng} = 280$ W (given directly).

**Step 11 — Subtotal before safety factor:**

$$Q_{sub} = 436.8 + 1145.6 + 460 + 296.8 + 110 + 280 = 2729.2 \text{ W}$$

**Step 12 — Safety / miscellaneous (12 %):**

$$Q_{misc} = 0.12 \times 2729.2 = 327.5 \text{ W}$$

**Step 13 — Total refrigeration load:**

$$Q_{total} = 2729.2 + 327.5 = 3056.7 \text{ W} \approx 3.06 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 14 — Conclusion:**

$$\boxed{Q_{total} \approx 3.06 \text{ kW}}$$

The compressor and evaporator must be sized for at least **3.1 kW** of cooling capacity to maintain 23 °C in this sedan under the given conditions.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Part (b) Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the latent ventilation term** — students often compute only $Q_{vent,s}$ and miss 1 mark for $Q_{vent,l}$.
> 2. **Mixing up $U_{op}$ and $U_g$** — opaque and glazing coefficients are *different*; substituting one for the other loses 0.5 mark.
> 3. **Skipping the safety factor** — the 12 % miscellaneous term is mandatory per KTU Module 2 conventions.
> 4. **Unit inconsistency** — keep everything in **Watts** until the final answer; mixing kW and W mid-solution loses 0.5 mark.
> 5. **Final answer without box** — KTU examiners explicitly require a boxed final numerical result.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]

**(a)** **Derive the expression for solar heat gain through automotive glazing**, clearly stating the role of the *Shading Coefficient* and the *Solar Heat Gain Factor*. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A mini-bus has 12 seats. Each passenger dissipates 65 W sensible and 50 W latent heat. The ventilation system supplies 0.01 m³/s of outside air at 35 °C, 70 % RH, while maintaining cabin air at 24 °C, 50 % RH. Using the psychrometric data below, calculate the **total ventilation and occupant load**.
- $\rho_{air} = 1.15$ kg/m³, $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/(kg·K), $h_{fg} = 2450$ kJ/kg
- $\omega_{out} = 0.020$ kg/kg, $\omega_{in} = 0.0095$ kg/kg **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — State the physical situation (1 mark):**
A glazing surface of area $A_g$ is exposed to (i) direct-beam + diffuse solar radiation from the sun and sky, and (ii) a temperature difference $T_{out} - T_{in}$ driving conduction.

**Step 2 — Define $SHGF$ (1 mark):**
The **Solar Heat Gain Factor** is the *total instantaneous solar radiation intensity* (W/m²) striking a surface of given orientation at a given location, time, and date. It is the *sum of direct beam, diffuse sky, and ground-reflected* components. KTU standard data tables are used.

**Step 3 — Define $SC$ (1 mark):**
The **Shading Coefficient** is the ratio:

$$SC = \frac{SHGC_{\text{actual glass}}}{SHGC_{\text{3 mm clear glass}}}$$

It captures the effect of tint, reflective coatings, and laminate layers. Range: 0 (perfect block) to 1 (clear reference).

**Step 4 — Radiation heat gain term (1.5 marks):**

$$Q_{rad} = SHGF \cdot A_g \cdot SC$$

The product $SHGF \cdot SC$ gives the *effective transmitted solar intensity* that ultimately becomes cooling load inside the cabin.

**Step 5 — Conduction heat gain term (1.5 marks):**

$$Q_{cond} = U_g \cdot A_g \cdot (T_{out} - T_{in})$$

**Step 6 — Final combined expression (1 mark):**

$$\boxed{Q_{glass} = U_g \cdot A_g \cdot \Delta T + SHGF \cdot A_g \cdot SC}$$

---

**Model Solution — Part (b) — Step-by-Step (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Occupant sensible + latent load:**

$$Q_{occ,s} = 12 \times 65 = 780 \text{ W}$$

$$Q_{occ,l} = 12 \times 50 = 600 \text{ W}$$

$$Q_{occ,total} = 780 + 600 = 1380 \text{ W}$$

**Step 2 — Ventilation sensible load:**

$$Q_{vent,s} = \dot{V} \cdot \rho \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T = 0.01 \times 1.15 \times 1.005 \times (35 - 24)$$

$$= 0.01 \times 1.15 \times 1.005 \times 11 = 0.1271 \text{ kW} = 127.1 \text{ W}$$

**Step 3 — Ventilation latent load:**

$$Q_{vent,l} = \dot{V} \cdot \rho \cdot h_{fg} \cdot \Delta \omega = 0.01 \times 1.15 \times 2450 \times (0.020 - 0.0095)$$

$$= 0.01 \times 1.15 \times 2450 \times 0.0105 = 0.2958 \text{ kW} = 295.8 \text{ W}$$

**Step 4 — Total ventilation load:**

$$Q_{vent} = 127.1 + 295.8 = 422.9 \text{ W}$$

**Step 5 — Total occupant + ventilation load:**

$$Q_{combined} = Q_{occ,total} + Q_{vent} = 1380 + 422.9 = 1802.9 \text{ W}$$

**Step 6 — Final boxed answer:**

$$\boxed{Q_{combined} \approx 1.80 \text{ kW}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Question B Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing sensible and latent contributions** — both must be calculated separately. Showing only the *sum* per occupant without breaking out $q_{sens}$ and $q_{lat}$ loses 1 mark.
> 2. **Forgetting $\Delta \omega$** — using the *absolute* $\omega$ value instead of the *difference* $\omega_{out} - \omega_{in}$ is a very common KTU error.
> 3. **Not labelling units** — each intermediate value must carry **W** or **kW** explicitly.
> 4. **Skipping the derivation in part (a)** — the question says *derive*, not *state*. Writing only the final formula without explaining $SHGF$ and $SC$ loses up to 3 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2**

- **Definition:** Total cooling load = sum of *all* heat-gain rates entering the cabin from external + internal sources.
- **Six core factors:** Opaque transmission, glass solar gain, occupants, ventilation, engine bay, equipment. Plus a 10–15 % safety margin.
- **Two sub-components of glass load:** $U_g A_g \Delta T$ (conduction) **+** $SHGF \cdot A_g \cdot SC$ (radiation).
- **Solar term dominates** in tropical climates — typically the *largest single contributor* in midday operation.
- **Latent vs Sensible:** *Sensible* = temperature change. *Latent* = moisture phase change. In Kerala, latent can exceed sensible for the ventilation term.
- **Occupant heat:** ~120 W per adult (60 W sensible + 60 W latent, light activity).
- **Engine bay term:** Significant in ICE vehicles, **negligible** in Battery Electric Vehicles (BEVs) — a frequent KTU comparison point.
- **Safety factor:** 10–15 % of computed subtotal; never omitted in KTU problems.
- **Boxed final answer** is mandatory in numerical questions.
- **Units check:** Keep SI (W, m², K, kg/s) consistent; convert to kW only at the final reporting step.
- **Use proper symbols in the table:** $U$ is W/(m²·K), $SHGF$ is W/m², $SC$ is dimensionless, $h_{fg}$ is kJ/kg.
- **Equation to memorize verbatim:** $Q_{glass} = U_g A_g \Delta T + SHGF \cdot A_g \cdot SC$ — this is a **favourite 7-mark derivation question**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Procedure for Cooling and Heating Load Calculation in Automobiles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAUT523 - AUTOMOTIVE REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Procedure for Cooling and Heating Load Calculation in Automobiles ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In automotive HVAC engineering, the **Cooling Load** is defined as the *rate at which heat energy (in Watts or kW or Tons of Refrigeration) must be removed from the passenger compartment of a vehicle to maintain specified indoor design conditions* (typically **DBT = 22–25 °C** and **RH = 45–55 %**) against the worst-case outdoor ambient conditions (**DBT = 35–45 °C**), accounting for all simultaneous heat gains from conduction, radiation, ventilation, occupants, engine, and electrical equipment. Conversely, the **Heating Load** is defined as the *rate at which heat energy must be supplied to the passenger compartment to maintain comfort conditions* (typically **DBT = 20–24 °C**) when the outside ambient temperature falls below the design inside temperature, primarily accounting for heat loss by conduction through body panels, glass, and ventilation/infiltration losses. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** > The procedure for cooling and heating load calculation forms the *quantitative foundation* for sizing the compressor, condenser, evaporator, heater core, blower motors, and refrigerant flow rates. Without an accurate load estimate, the entire AC/Heating system will be either undersized (poor comfort) or oversized (excess weight, fuel penalty, cost). ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Analogy (The "Moving Tin Box" Concept):** > Imagine you are sitting inside a closed metal box (your car) parked under the hot sun at noon. Heat is creeping in from *every direction at once*: > - **Through the glass** (like sunlight streaming through a window of a greenhouse). > - **Through the metal roof, doors, and floor** (like holding your hand against a hot car bonnet). > - **From the passengers** (each person is a small 100 W "heater" radiating body heat). > - **From the engine bay** (your car's engine is essentially a giant furnace right next to you). > - **From the electronics** (your phone charger, lights, infotainment system — small heaters). > - **From the fresh air you let in** (ventilation air that is already hot and humid). > > The **cooling load is the total sum of all these "heats"**, and the AC system must pump out exactly that much heat to keep you comfortable. The **heating load** is the reverse — when the box is cold (winter morning), you need to *add* heat to keep the passengers warm. ### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Constant | Value | Used For | |---|---|---| | **1 Ton of Refrigeration (TR)** | **3.517 kW** or **12,000 BTU/hr** | Sizing AC capacity | | Air specific heat, $c_p$ | **1.005 kJ/(kg·K)** | Sensible heat calculations | | Air density, $\rho$ | **1.2 kg/m³** (at 20 °C, sea level) | Ventilation load | | Latent heat of water, $h_{fg}$ | **2,501 kJ/kg** | Latent (humidity) load | | Solar constant at earth's surface | **≈ 1,000 W/m²** (peak noon, clear sky) | Solar gain | | Typical auto glass SHGC | **0.70 – 0.85** | Solar transmission | | Typical auto glass U-value | **5.5 – 5.8 W/(m²·K)** | Conduction through glass | --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Six Pillars of Automotive Cooling Load The total cooling load $Q_{total}$ of an automobile passenger compartment is the **arithmetic sum** of six distinct heat-gain sources, evaluated for the *worst-case design day* (typically peak summer afternoon): $$ Q_{total} = Q_{glass} + Q_{body} + Q_{vent} + Q_{occ} + Q_{engine} + Q_{elec} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Why these six?** Each represents a *physically independent* mode of heat entry. The KTU examiner will expect you to know *why* each term exists, not just memorize it. Heat must obey the **First Law of Thermodynamics** — every Watt entering the cabin must be removed by the AC. --- ### 2.2 Detailed Breakdown of Each Component #### **2.2.1 Heat Gain Through Glass ($Q_{glass}$)** Glass areas include the windshield, side windows, rear windshield, and (optionally) sunroof. Two sub-components exist: **(a) Conduction through glass (sensible):** $$ Q_{cond,glass} = U_{glass} \times A_{glass} \times \Delta T $$ **(b) Solar radiation through glass (radiant):** $$ Q_{solar,glass} = A_{glass} \times SHGC \times I_{solar} $$ Total: $$ Q_{glass} = U_{glass} A_{glass} \Delta T + \sum_i \left( A_i \times SHGC_i \times I_i \right) $$ Where $I_i$ is the incident solar intensity for each glass orientation (windshield, side, rear have **different** values due to sun angle). #### **2.2.2 Heat Gain Through Opaque Body Panels ($Q_{body}$)** Roof, doors, hood, trunk lid, floor — all metal panels with internal insulation/trim: $$ Q_{body} = \sum_j \left( U_j \times A_j \times CLTD_j \right) $$ The **CLTD (Cooling Load Temperature Difference)** is a pre-computed equivalent temperature difference that accounts for both outdoor temperature *and* absorbed solar radiation on the outer surface, used in ASHRAE-based methods. > [!TIP] > **Why CLTD instead of just $\Delta T$?** Because the outer surface of a sunlit roof can reach **60–80 °C** even when ambient is only 40 °C, due to direct solar absorption. CLTD *lumps* this effect into a single number for each surface orientation. #### **2.2.3 Heat Gain from Ventilation/Infiltration Air ($Q_{vent}$)** Fresh air brought in for occupant breathing: $$ Q_{vent} = \rho \times \dot{V} \times c_p \times \Delta T + \rho \times \dot{V} \times (W_o - W_i) \times h_{fg} $$ Where: - $\dot{V}$ = fresh air volumetric flow rate (typically **0.05 m³/s** or 100 CFM for a car) - $W_o, W_i$ = humidity ratios of outside and inside air - Sensible part: depends on $\Delta T$; Latent part: depends on humidity difference #### **2.2.4 Heat Gain from Occupants ($Q_{occ}$)** $$ Q_{occ} = n \times (q_{sensible} + q_{latent}) $$ Typical values per adult (moderately active): - Sensible heat: **60 – 70 W** - Latent heat: **45 – 55 W** - Total: **~100 – 120 W per person** #### **2.2.5 Heat Gain from Engine Compartment ($Q_{engine}$)** The engine bay is adjacent to the passenger compartment (separated by firewall). Heat leaks through: $$ Q_{engine} = U_{firewall} \times A_{firewall} \times (T_{engine} - T_{cabin}) $$ Alternative empirical estimate: $$ Q_{engine} \approx 0.05 \text{ to } 0.10 \times \dot{W}_{engine} $$ > For a 100 kW engine, this can be **5–10 kW** of parasitic heat gain. #### **2.2.6 Heat Gain from Electrical Equipment ($Q_{elec}$)** Includes blower motor, lights, infotainment, defroster, seat heaters (in heating mode, this is *useful*, but in cooling mode the first four add heat): $$ Q_{elec} = \sum_k P_k $$ Typical total: **100 – 400 W**. --- ### 2.3 Heating Load Components For the *heating* mode, the signs reverse and the equation becomes: $$ Q_{heat} = Q_{loss,body} + Q_{loss,glass} + Q_{loss,vent} - Q_{engine} - Q_{elec} - Q_{solar} $$ Note: in winter, the engine is a **benefit** (gives free heat), and solar gain is a **benefit**.
