The heater core must *supplement* the deficit to maintain comfort. $$ Q_{loss} = U \times A \times (T_{in} - T_{out}) $$ Where $T_{in} > T_{out}$ in winter. --- ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Component | Formula | Key Variable | Typical Unit | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Glass conduction | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ | $U \approx 5.8$ | W | | 2 | Glass solar gain | $Q = A \cdot SHGC \cdot I$ | $I \approx 700$ | W/m² | | 3 | Body panel | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot CLTD$ | $CLTD$ from table | W | | 4 | Ventilation sensible | $Q_s = \rho \dot{V} c_p \Delta T$ | $\dot{V} \approx 0.05$ | W | | 5 | Ventilation latent | $Q_l = \rho \dot{V} (W_o - W_i) h_{fg}$ | $h_{fg} = 2501$ | W | | 6 | Occupants | $Q = n \times q_{tot}$ | $q_{tot} \approx 110$ | W/person | | 7 | Engine bay | $Q \approx 0.05 - 0.10 \times \dot{W}$ | $\dot{W}$ in W | W | | 8 | Electrical | $Q = \sum P_k$ | Tabulated | W | | 9 | **Total Cooling Load** | $Q_{cool} = \sum_{i=1}^{6} Q_i$ | — | **W or kW** | | 10 | **TR Conversion** | $TR = Q_{cool}(kW) / 3.517$ | — | Tons | | 11 | Body heat loss (heating) | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot (T_{in} - T_{out})$ | $T_{in} - T_{out} > 0$ | W | | 12 | **Total Heating Load** | $Q_{heat} = Q_{loss} - Q_{gain}$ | — | **W or kW** | > [!NOTE] > **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE FOR STUDENTS:** In your KTU answer sheets, never write $\vert x \vert$ for absolute value inside tabular or aligned blocks — use $\lvert x \rvert$ or simply describe it in words ("magnitude of temperature difference"). This avoids LaTeX rendering errors and examiner confusion. --- ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Sizing the Compressor:** Compressor displacement (cc/rev) is directly selected from the cooling load in kW. - **Sizing the Evaporator:** Heat exchanger capacity must equal $Q_{total}$ at design conditions. - **Sizing the Heater Core:** Required to meet $Q_{heat}$ at minimum design outside temperature. - **Refrigerant Flow Rate:** $\dot{m}_{ref} = Q_{cool} / (h_{g,evap} - h_{f,evap})$ kg/s. - **Blower Selection:** Determined from the sensible load and required cabin air changes per hour (ACH). - **Refrigerant Charging:** Total refrigerant charge scales approximately linearly with the cooling load. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Worked Example: Complete Cooling Load for a Sedan Car **Given Vehicle Data (typical mid-size sedan):** | Parameter | Value | |---|---| | Windshield area | $A_{ws} = 0.50$ m² | | Rear glass area | $A_{rw} = 0.40$ m² | | Side glass (2 windows) | $A_{sw} = 2 \times 0.35 = 0.70$ m² | | Roof (painted steel + headliner) | $A_{rf} = 1.20$ m² | | Door panels (4 doors) | $A_{dr} = 4 \times 0.45 = 1.80$ m² | | Hood | $A_{hd} = 1.00$ m² | | Trunk lid | $A_{tk} = 0.60$ m² | | Floor pan | $A_{fl} = 1.40$ m² | | Outside DBT, $T_o$ | **40 °C** | | Inside design DBT, $T_i$ | **25 °C** | | Outside RH | 40 % | | Inside RH | 50 % | | Number of occupants, $n$ | 4 | | Engine power, $\dot{W}_{eng}$ | 80 kW | | Fresh air flow, $\dot{V}$ | 0.05 m³/s | | Solar intensity (windshield-facing) | 800 W/m² | | Solar intensity (side) | 400 W/m² | | Solar intensity (rear) | 200 W/m² | **Material Properties:** | Component | $U$ [W/(m²·K)] | $SHGC$ | |---|---|---| | Glass | 5.8 | 0.78 | | Roof | 4.5 | — | | Doors | 5.0 | — | | Hood | 5.5 | — | | Trunk | 5.0 | — | | Floor | 3.0 | — | --- ### 3.2 Detailed Step-by-Step Calculation #### **Step 1: Heat Gain Through Glass (Conduction + Solar)** **Sub-step 1a: Conduction (sensible)** For all glass, $\Delta T = T_o - T_i = 40 - 25 = 15$ K. $$ \begin{aligned} Q_{cond,glass} &= U_{glass} \times A_{total,glass} \times \Delta T \\ &= 5.8 \times (0.50 + 0.40 + 0.70) \times 15 \\ &= 5.8 \times 1.60 \times 15 \\ &= 139.2 \; \text{W} \end{aligned} $$ **Sub-step 1b: Solar radiation (radiant)** $$ \begin{aligned} Q_{solar,ws} &= A_{ws} \times SHGC \times I_{ws} = 0.50 \times 0.78 \times 800 = 312.0 \; \text{W} \\ Q_{solar,sw} &= A_{sw} \times SHGC \times I_{sw} = 0.70 \times 0.78 \times 400 = 218.4 \; \text{W} \\ Q_{solar,rw} &= A_{rw} \times SHGC \times I_{rw} = 0.40 \times 0.78 \times 200 = 62.4 \; \text{W} \end{aligned} $$ **Total glass load:** $$ \begin{aligned} Q_{glass} &= 139.2 + 312.0 + 218.4 + 62.4 \\ &= 732.0 \; \text{W} \end{aligned} $$ --- #### **Step 2: Heat Gain Through Opaque Body Panels** Using $\Delta T = 15$ K (assuming painted panels in shade or non-peak hours; for sunlit roof we could add solar absorbed, but here we keep it as conduction-only for clarity): $$ \begin{aligned} Q_{roof} &= 4.5 \times 1.20 \times 15 = 81.0 \; \text{W} \\ Q_{doors} &= 5.0 \times 1.80 \times 15 = 135.0 \; \text{W} \\ Q_{hood} &= 5.5 \times 1.00 \times 15 = 82.5 \; \text{W} \\ Q_{trunk} &= 5.0 \times 0.60 \times 15 = 45.0 \; \text{W} \\ Q_{floor} &= 3.0 \times 1.40 \times 15 = 63.0 \; \text{W} \end{aligned} $$ **Total body load:** $$ Q_{body} = 81.0 + 135.0 + 82.5 + 45.0 + 63.0 = 406.5 \; \text{W} $$ > [!TIP] > **Exam Tip:** If the question explicitly states "sunlit roof" or "west-facing wall", add a **solar absorbed** term: $Q_{solar,abs} = \alpha_s \cdot A \cdot I$ where $\alpha_s$ is the surface solar absorptivity (≈ 0.5 for light paint, 0.9 for black). --- #### **Step 3: Ventilation / Infiltration Load** At $T_o = 40\,^\circ\text{C}$, RH = 40 %: $W_o \approx 0.0167$ kg/kg (from psychrometric chart) At $T_i = 25\,^\circ\text{C}$, RH = 50 %: $W_i \approx 0.0099$ kg/kg **Sensible part:** $$ \begin{aligned} Q_{vent,s} &= \rho \times \dot{V} \times c_p \times \Delta T \\ &= 1.2 \times 0.05 \times 1005 \times 15 \\ &= 904.5 \; \text{W} \end{aligned} $$ **Latent part:** $$ \begin{aligned} Q_{vent,l} &= \rho \times \dot{V} \times (W_o - W_i) \times h_{fg} \\ &= 1.2 \times 0.05 \times (0.0167 - 0.0099) \times 2501000 \\ &= 1.2 \times 0.05 \times 0.0068 \times 2501000 \\ &= 1020.4 \; \text{W} \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Wow! Latent > Sensible?** Yes, in hot humid climates (like Kerala monsoon aftermath, 35 °C & 80 % RH), the **latent load dominates**. This is why KTU questions often give high humidity to make the latent term significant. **Total ventilation load:** $$ Q_{vent} = 904.5 + 1020.4 = 1924.9 \; \text{W} $$ --- #### **Step 4: Occupant Load** For 4 occupants at moderately active level (sensible = 70 W, latent = 50 W per person): $$ \begin{aligned} Q_{occ} &= n \times (q_s + q_l) \\ &= 4 \times (70 + 50) \\ &= 4 \times 120 \\ &= 480 \; \text{W} \end{aligned} $$ --- #### **Step 5: Engine Bay Heat Transfer** Using empirical estimate (5 % of engine power for an idling/light-load condition, since the firewall is partial insulation): $$ \begin{aligned} Q_{engine} &= 0.05 \times \dot{W}_{eng} \\ &= 0.05 \times 80{,}000 \\ &= 4000 \; \text{W} = 4.0 \; \text{kW} \end{aligned} $$ > [!WARNING] > This is a **huge** load — often the *single largest* heat source in a vehicle. This is why engine bay insulation and careful HVAC duct routing are critical KTU topics. --- #### **Step 6: Electrical Equipment Load** Estimated: - Blower motor: 150 W - Infotainment + lights + sensors: 100 W - Defroster (when active): 200 W - **Total: $Q_{elec} \approx 450$ W** --- #### **Step 7: Total Cooling Load** $$ \begin{aligned} Q_{total,cool} &= Q_{glass} + Q_{body} + Q_{vent} + Q_{occ} + Q_{engine} + Q_{elec} \\ &= 732.0 + 406.5 + 1924.9 + 480 + 4000 + 450 \\ &= 7993.4 \; \text{W} \\ &\approx 7.99 \; \text{kW} \end{aligned} $$ **Convert to Tons of Refrigeration:** $$ \begin{aligned} TR &= \frac{Q_{total}}{3.517} = \frac{7.99}{3.517} \approx 2.27 \; \text{TR} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > A typical sedan AC system is rated **1.5 – 2.5 TR**, so our calculated value is realistic. KTU board exam answers for cooling load of a standard sedan should fall in this range. --- ### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Cooling vs Heating Load | Aspect | Cooling Load | Heating Load | |---|---|---| | Direction of heat flow | **Into** cabin | **Out of** cabin | | Main sources | Solar, engine, outside air | Cold outside, cold glass | | Dominant term (typical) | **Engine + Ventilation** | **Glass + Ventilation** | | Equipment that handles it | Compressor + Evaporator | Heater core (uses engine coolant) | | Sign convention | Positive (to be removed) | Negative (to be supplied) | | Typical magnitude (sedan) | 6 – 10 kW | 3 – 6 kW | | Affected most by | Sun load, engine run-time | Wind speed, glass area | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram: Six Heat Sources in an Automobile Cabin ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Outside Ambient 40 C] --> B[Ventilation Air Intake] B --> B1[Sensible Heat
904.5 W] B --> B2[Latent Heat
1020.4 W] C[Sun Radiation] --> D1[Windshield
312 W] C --> D2[Side Glass
218 W] C --> D3[Rear Glass
62 W] E1[Glass Conduction
139 W] --> F D1 --> F D2 --> F D3 --> F F[Q GLASS = 732 W] G1[Roof 81 W] --> G2[Doors 135 W] G2 --> G3[Hood 82.5 W] G3 --> G4[Trunk 45 W] G4 --> G5[Floor 63 W] G5 --> H[Q BODY = 406.5 W] B1 --> I B2 --> I H --> I F --> I J[4 Occupants
480 W] --> I K[Engine Bay
4000 W] --> I L[Electrical Loads
450 W] --> I I[Q TOTAL = 7.99 kW approx 2.27 TR] M[AC Compressor Sizing] --> I N[Evaporator Sizing] --> I O[Refrigerant Flow] --> I ``` ### 4.2 Block Diagram: Cooling Load Calculation Procedure (Sequential Flow) ```mermaid flowchart TD S1[Step 1: Collect Vehicle Geometry
Glass areas, panel areas] --> S2[Step 2: Define Design Conditions
T inside, T outside, RH] S2 --> S3[Step 3: Estimate Material U values
and SHGC for glass] S3 --> S4[Step 4: Calculate Q glass
Conduction plus Solar] S4 --> S5[Step 5: Calculate Q body
Sum of U times A times CLTD] S5 --> S6[Step 6: Calculate Q vent
Sensible plus Latent] S6 --> S7[Step 7: Add Q occupants
n times q per person] S7 --> S8[Step 8: Add Q engine
5 to 10 percent of W engine] S8 --> S9[Step 9: Add Q electrical
Sum of component powers] S9 --> S10[Step 10: Total Q cool
Sum of all six] S10 --> S11[Step 11: Convert to TR
Divide by 3.517] S11 --> S12[Step 12: Select Compressor
and Evaporator Capacity] S12 --> S13[Output: System Design Specifications] ``` ### 4.3 Block Diagram: Heating Load Calculation (Winter Mode) ```mermaid flowchart LR H1[Outside Cold Air
T out = 5 C] --> H2[Ventilation Loss
rho V cp dT] H1 --> H3[Glass Heat Loss
U times A times dT] H4[Opaque Body Heat Loss
U times A times dT] --> H5[Q BODY LOSS] H2 --> H5 H3 --> H5 H5 --> H6[Subtract Free Heat Gains] H7[Engine Heat Reuse
Free benefit] --> H6 H8[Solar Through Glass
Reduced in winter] --> H6 H9[Electrical Equipment
Free benefit] --> H6 H6 --> H10[Net Heating Load Q heat] H10 --> H11[Heater Core Sizing
Blower Sizing
Coolant Flow Rate] ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]** *List any six sources of cooling load in an automobile passenger compartment and state the formula for one of them.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** The six sources of cooling load in an automobile are: 1. Heat gain through glass (windshield, windows, rear glass) — conduction + solar. 2. Heat gain through opaque body panels (roof, doors, hood, floor, trunk). 3. Heat gain from ventilation / fresh-air intake. 4. Heat gain from occupants (sensible + latent body heat). 5. Heat gain from the engine compartment (through the firewall). 6. Heat gain from electrical equipment (blower, lights, infotainment). **Formula example (glass solar gain):** $$ Q_{solar,glass} = A \times SHGC \times I_{solar} $$ where $A$ = glass area (m²), $SHGC$ = Solar Heat Gain Coefficient (≈ 0.78), and $I$ = incident solar intensity (W/m²). --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]** *Define "Ton of Refrigeration" and convert a cooling load of 8 kW into TR.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition:** A *Ton of Refrigeration (TR)* is the rate of heat removal required to freeze **1 ton (2000 lb = 907.18 kg) of water at 0 °C into ice at 0 °C in 24 hours**. In SI units: $$ 1 \; \text{TR} = \frac{907.18 \times 335 \; \text{kJ}}{24 \times 3600 \; \text{s}} = 3.517 \; \text{kW} $$ **Conversion of 8 kW:** $$ TR = \frac{8}{3.517} = 2.275 \; \text{TR} $$ --- ### 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) --- ### **Question A — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 14 Marks]** **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **step-by-step procedure for calculating the cooling load of an automobile passenger compartment**. List all heat sources with their governing formulas. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A car has the following data. Calculate the **total cooling load** in kW and convert it to **Tons of Refrigeration**: - Total glass area: 1.5 m², $U_{glass} = 5.8$ W/(m²·K), SHGC = 0.78, average solar intensity = 700 W/m² - Roof area: 1.2 m², $U_{roof} = 4.5$ W/(m²·K) - Door area (4 doors): 1.6 m², $U_{door} = 5.0$ W/(m²·K) - $\Delta T = 15$ K, Ventilation: 0.04 m³/s, 3 occupants @ 110 W each, Engine bay: 3.0 kW, Electrical: 400 W. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for Question A:** **(a) Step-by-step procedure (7 marks):** Refer to **Section 4.2 Block Diagram** for visual. The procedure is: 1. **Define geometry:** Measure glass and panel areas. 2. **Fix design conditions:** Inside DBT, RH; outside DBT, RH. 3. **Obtain material data:** $U$-values, SHGC, surface absorptivity. 4. **Compute glass load:** $Q_{glass} = U A \Delta T + A \cdot SHGC \cdot I$. 5. **Compute body load:** $Q_{body} = \sum U_i A_i CLTD_i$. 6. **Compute ventilation load:** $Q_{vent} = \rho \dot V c_p \Delta T + \rho \dot V (W_o - W_i) h_{fg}$. 7. **Add occupant load:** $Q_{occ} = n \times q_{tot}$. 8. **Add engine bay load:** $Q_{engine} \approx 0.05 - 0.10 \times \dot W_{eng}$. 9. **Add electrical load:** $Q_{elec} = \sum P_k$. 10. **Sum all:** $Q_{total} = Q_{glass} + Q_{body} + Q_{vent} + Q_{occ} + Q_{engine} + Q_{elec}$. 11. **Convert to TR** and size components. **(b) Numerical solution (7 marks):** **Step 1: Glass load** $$ Q_{glass} = (5.8 \times 1.5 \times 15) + (1.5 \times 0.78 \times 700) $$ $$ Q_{glass} = 130.5 + 819.0 = 949.5 \; \text{W} $$ **Step 2: Body load** $$ Q_{body} = (4.5 \times 1.2 \times 15) + (5.0 \times 1.6 \times 15) $$ $$ Q_{body} = 81.0 + 120.0 = 201.0 \; \text{W} $$ **Step 3: Ventilation load** (taking dry case, $W_o \approx W_i$, so latent ≈ 0 for simplicity) $$ Q_{vent} = 1.2 \times 0.04 \times 1005 \times 15 = 723.6 \; \text{W} $$ **Step 4: Occupant load** $$ Q_{occ} = 3 \times 110 = 330 \; \text{W} $$ **Step 5: Engine + Electrical** $$ Q_{eng+elec} = 3000 + 400 = 3400 \; \text{W} $$ **Step 6: Total** $$ \begin{aligned} Q_{total} &= 949.5 + 201.0 + 723.6 + 330 + 3400 \\ &= 5604.1 \; \text{W} \approx 5.60 \; \text{kW} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 7: Convert to TR** $$ TR = \frac{5.60}{3.517} \approx 1.59 \; \text{TR} $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students **forget the solar radiation term** in glass load — this is a 2-mark deduction each time. Also, students often take only sensible ventilation and ignore latent, losing 1–2 marks. **Always write down BOTH sensible AND latent** in your answer. --- ### **Question B — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 14 Marks]** **(a)** Differentiate between **cooling load and heating load** calculation procedures for an automobile. List the dominant terms in each. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Calculate the **heating load** for a car with the following winter design data: - Outside DBT = 5 °C, Inside design DBT = 22 °C, $\Delta T = 17$ K - Glass area: 1.6 m², $U_{glass} = 5.8$ W/(m²·K) - Opaque body area: 4.5 m², average $U = 5.0$ W/(m²·K) - Ventilation: 0.04 m³/s - Engine heat available to cabin: 3.5 kW - Electrical heat: 300 W - Solar gain (winter, weak): 200 W **Convert the net heating load to kW.** **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for Question B:** **(a) Differences (7 marks):** | Feature | Cooling Load | Heating Load | |---|---|---| | Heat flow direction | Into cabin | Out of cabin | | Outside condition | Hot (35–45 °C) | Cold (0–10 °C) | | Solar radiation | **Major source** (often largest) | Minor (small contribution) | | Engine bay | **Parasitic load** (adds heat) | **Free benefit** (re-used) | | Dominant terms | Engine + Ventilation latent | Glass loss + Ventilation sensible | | Equipment used | Compressor + Evaporator | Heater core (uses coolant) | | Sign in heat balance | Positive (to remove) | Positive (to supply) | **(b) Heating load calculation (7 marks):** **Step 1: Glass heat loss** $$ Q_{glass,loss} = 5.8 \times 1.6 \times 17 = 157.76 \; \text{W} $$ **Step 2: Body heat loss** $$ Q_{body,loss} = 5.0 \times 4.5 \times 17 = 382.5 \; \text{W} $$ **Step 3: Ventilation heat loss** (sensible only, winter air is dry) $$ Q_{vent,loss} = 1.2 \times 0.04 \times 1005 \times 17 = 820.1 \; \text{W} $$ **Step 4: Total losses** $$ Q_{loss,total} = 157.76 + 382.5 + 820.1 = 1360.4 \; \text{W} $$ **Step 5: Free heat gains** $$ Q_{gain,free} = 3500 + 300 + 200 = 4000 \; \text{W} $$ **Step 6: Net heating load** $$ \begin{aligned} Q_{heat,net} &= Q_{loss,total} - Q_{gain,free} \\ &= 1360.4 - 4000 \\ &= -2639.6 \; \text{W} \end{aligned} $$ **Interpretation:** The negative result means **the engine alone provides more than enough heat** — the heater core does not need to add any additional energy. In fact, the system would need to *blend in cold outside air* to avoid over-heating. In many real cars, the heating system is "free" (uses waste engine heat). > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #2 (Heating mode):** Students often **ignore free engine heat** and compute a positive heating load, then wrongly size the heater core. The correct KTU approach: **always subtract free gains first.** Also, the heating load in modern cars is *almost always smaller* than the cooling load, and may even be negative. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Revision Notes for KTU Board Exam:** - **Cooling Load = 6 components**: Glass, Body, Ventilation, Occupants, Engine, Electrical. - **Heating Load = Losses − Free Gains** (engine is your friend in winter). - **1 TR = 3.517 kW** (memorize this exactly — appears in almost every numerical). - **Engine bay heat ≈ 5–10 % of engine power** is the **single largest** heat source in many vehicles. - **Latent load** dominates in **hot-humid** climates (Kerala coast, Mumbai, Chennai) — never ignore it. - **Sensible load** dominates in **hot-dry** climates (Rajasthan, Delhi summer). - **SHGC of typical auto glass** is **0.70 – 0.85**; use **0.78** as default. - **U-value of glass** ≈ **5.8 W/(m²·K)**; painted metal panels ≈ **4.5 – 5.5 W/(m²·K)**. - **CLTD** is used for opaque sunlit panels (accounts for solar absorption on the outer skin). - **Ventilation flow** for a car: **0.04 – 0.06 m³/s** (≈ 80–120 CFM). - **Occupant load**: **~110 W per person** (sensible 60–70 W + latent 40–50 W). - **Compressor, evaporator, refrigerant flow rate, blower** — all sized from $Q_{total}$. - **Sizing sequence** in KTU answer: Compute load → Convert to TR → Select compressor displacement → Select evaporator & condenser → Calculate refrigerant mass flow rate. - **Solar orientation matters**: Windshield ≠ Side glass ≠ Rear glass (different solar intensities and SHGC). - **Typical sedan cooling load** = **6 – 10 kW (1.7 – 2.8 TR)**. - **Typical sedan heating load** = **0 – 3 kW** (often near zero or negative due to engine waste heat). - **ASHRAE method** uses CLTD/SHGF; the **functional method** (used above) is preferred for board exams as it is more intuitive. ---

topics.title: Effect of Air Conditioning Load on Engine Performance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT523 - AUTOMOTIVE REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Effect of Air Conditioning Load on Engine Performance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme)**
> The *Effect of Air Conditioning Load on Engine Performance* refers to the parasitic power demand imposed on the internal combustion (IC) engine when the automotive air conditioning compressor is engaged. This auxiliary load directly reduces the net usable brake power, increases specific fuel consumption (SFC), alters the effective torque delivery, and places additional thermal stress on the engine cooling system.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine you are cycling (pedaling) on a flat road at a comfortable speed. Now, imagine someone quietly sits on the back of your cycle (a *passenger*). You will immediately feel heavier, your legs must work harder, your speed drops slightly, and you burn more energy (calories) to maintain the same pace.

The automotive air conditioning compressor behaves **exactly like this passenger**. When you switch the car's AC "ON," the engine is forced to drive a belt or clutch-coupled compressor. This extra mechanical workload is drawn directly from the engine's crankshaft, reducing the power available at the wheels and increasing fuel consumption. In technical terms, the compressor acts as a **parasitic load** on the engine.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Metric Highlighted**
> - A typical R134a/R1234yf automotive AC compressor consumes between **3 kW to 7 kW** of engine power (≈ 4 to 10 hp) at full load.
> - The fuel consumption of a passenger car can increase by **5% to 20%** with AC operation.
> - The compressor typically absorbs **15% to 25%** of the total engine output under maximum cooling demand.

### Why This Topic is Exam-Critical for KTU
This topic is a **favourite Module 2 question** in KTU examinations because it directly links thermodynamics (refrigeration cycle work input) with internal combustion engine performance. KTU examiners test the student's ability to:
1. Quantify the parasitic power loss.
2. Relate compressor work input to fuel economy penalties.
3. Compute the revised BSFC and brake thermal efficiency with AC engaged.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Parasitic Load Concept (Structured Logic)

When the AC system is switched ON, the following chain of events occurs:

1. **Compressor Engagement**: The electromagnetic clutch couples the compressor pulley to the engine crankshaft via a serpentine/V-belt.
2. **Power Extraction**: The compressor draws mechanical work to circulate the refrigerant and raise its enthalpy/pressure (refrigeration effect).
3. **Shaft Torque Demand**: This mechanical work is subtracted from the engine's indicated power, reducing the brake power available at the wheels.
4. **Throttle Compensation**: The driver (or ECU) typically opens the throttle wider to maintain speed, increasing fuel injection quantity.
5. **Fuel Economy Penalty**: Because more fuel is burned for the same distance, the vehicle's km/litre (or mpg) drops measurably.
6. **Cooling System Load**: The condenser (front of the radiator) rejects the additional heat absorbed from the cabin, raising coolant temperatures and fan demand.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reality Check**
> Modern vehicles use a *clutchless variable-displacement compressor* (e.g., swash-plate type) which can modulate capacity. This significantly reduces the parasitic load at part-load cooling, but the engine still bears a measurable penalty.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---------|----------------|-------|-----------------|
| 1 | $W_{comp} = \dfrac{m_r \cdot \Delta h}{60 \cdot \eta_{mech} \cdot \eta_{mot}}$ | $W_{comp}$ = Compressor power, $m_r$ = refrigerant mass flow (kg/h), $\Delta h = h_2 - h_1$ (kJ/kg), $\eta$ = efficiencies | kW | Calculates parasitic power on engine |
| 2 | $P_{net} = P_{brake} - W_{comp}$ | $P_{net}$ = usable power, $P_{brake}$ = brake power w/o AC | kW | Net power available at wheels |
| 3 | $BSFC_{AC} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f \cdot 3600}{P_{net}}$ | $BSFC$ = Brake Specific Fuel Consumption | g/kWh | Quantifies fuel economy penalty |
| 4 | $\eta_{b,th} = \dfrac{P_{net}}{\dot{m}_f \cdot CV}$ | Brake thermal efficiency with AC | decimal / % | Overall engine efficiency |
| 5 | $T_{net} = T_{brake} - T_{comp}$ | $T_{net}$ = net torque, $T_{brake}$ = brake torque | N·m | Torque loss to wheels |
| 6 | $\Delta Fuel \% = \dfrac{FC_{AC} - FC_{noAC}}{FC_{noAC}} \times 100$ | % increase in fuel consumption | % | Exam-style fuel penalty calculation |
| 7 | $N_{comp} = N_{engine} \times R_{pulley}$ | Compressor speed from engine speed via pulley ratio | rpm | Compressor speed derivation |
| 8 | $Q_{cond} = Q_{evap} + W_{comp}$ | Energy balance on condenser (heat rejected = cooling + work) | kW | Condenser heat rejection increase |
| 9 | $\eta_{vol,comp} = \dfrac{m_r}{\rho_s \cdot V_s \cdot N_{comp}}$ | Volumetric efficiency of compressor | decimal | Compressor performance check |
| 10 | $P_{eng,req} = P_{base} \times \left(1 + k_{AC}\right)$ | $k_{AC}$ = AC load factor (0.05–0.20 typical) | kW | Quick estimation of engine demand |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hybrid/EV Design**: Understanding AC parasitic load is the foundational reason why electric vehicles require dedicated *electric compressors* (powered by the high-voltage battery) rather than belt-driven units.
- **Engine Downsizing**: Modern turbocharged 3-cylinder engines need to carefully manage AC engagement maps to avoid perceived "lag" when AC cycles on.
- **Heavy Vehicles**: In buses and trucks, AC load can reach **15–20 kW**, requiring auxiliary engine units (APU) or dedicated AC engine PTO drives.
- **Emissions & BS-VI/Euro 6**: Since AC increases fuel burned per km, it directly elevates $CO_2$ and $NO_x$ tailpipe emissions — a key regulatory concern.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Parasitic Power Demand on Engine

The compressor work per unit time (power) is derived directly from the steady-flow energy equation (1st Law of Thermodynamics) applied to the compressor control volume.

**Step 1 — First Law for a Compressor (Adiabatic, neglecting KE/PE):**

$$
\dot{W}_{in} = \dot{m}_r \,(h_2 - h_1)
$$

Where $h_1$ = enthalpy at compressor inlet (saturated vapor), $h_2$ = enthalpy at compressor outlet (superheated vapor).

**Step 2 — Convert refrigerant mass flow to SI units:**

Most engineering data lists refrigerant flow in kg/hr. To convert to kW, we use:

$$
\dot{W}_{comp} \,[\text{kW}] = \dfrac{\dot{m}_r \,[\text{kg/h}] \times (h_2 - h_1) \,[\text{kJ/kg}]}{3600}
$$

**Step 3 — Account for mechanical losses in the compressor and belt drive:**

The actual power drawn from the engine crankshaft is higher due to mechanical friction and belt slip:

$$
W_{engine} = \dfrac{\dot{W}_{comp}}{\eta_{mech,belt} \cdot \eta_{mech,comp}}
$$

Typical values: $\eta_{mech,belt} \approx 0.95$, $\eta_{mech,comp} \approx 0.85$.

**Step 4 — Net brake power available to the wheels:**

$$
P_{net} = P_{brake,no\,AC} - W_{engine}
$$

**Step 5 — Revised BSFC and thermal efficiency:**

$$
BSFC_{AC} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f \times 3600}{P_{net}}
$$

$$
\eta_{b,th,AC} = \dfrac{P_{net}}{\dot{m}_f \times CV}
$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 14-Mark Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement**
> A 4-cylinder petrol engine develops a brake power of **55 kW** at 3000 rpm when the AC is OFF. The AC compressor draws refrigerant at 18 kg/h, with inlet and outlet enthalpies $h_1$ = 247 kJ/kg and $h_2$ = 277 kJ/kg. Mechanical efficiency of compressor = 85%, belt drive efficiency = 90%, fuel consumption = 14 kg/h, calorific value = 44,000 kJ/kg. Calculate: (a) Compressor power drawn from engine, (b) Net brake power, (c) BSFC with AC ON, (d) Brake thermal efficiency with AC ON.

#### Part (a) — Compressor Theoretical Power

$$
\dot{W}_{comp} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_r \cdot (h_2 - h_1)}{3600} = \dfrac{18 \times (277 - 247)}{3600}
$$

$$
\dot{W}_{comp} = \dfrac{18 \times 30}{3600} = \dfrac{540}{3600} = 0.15 \text{ kW}
$$

#### Part (a-ii) — Actual Engine Power Drawn by Compressor

$$
W_{engine} = \dfrac{0.15}{0.85 \times 0.90} = \dfrac{0.15}{0.765} = 0.196 \text{ kW}
$$

#### Part (b) — Net Brake Power

$$
P_{net} = 55 - 0.196 = 54.804 \text{ kW}
$$

#### Part (c) — BSFC with AC ON

$$
BSFC_{AC} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f \times 3600}{P_{net}} = \dfrac{14 \times 3600}{54.804}
$$

$$
BSFC_{AC} = \dfrac{50{,}400}{54.804} = 919.6 \text{ g/kWh}
$$

Compare with AC OFF:

$$
BSFC_{no\,AC} = \dfrac{50{,}400}{55} = 916.4 \text{ g/kWh}
$$

$$
\Delta BSFC = 919.6 - 916.4 = 3.2 \text{ g/kWh increase}
$$

#### Part (d) — Brake Thermal Efficiency with AC

$$
\eta_{b,th,AC} = \dfrac{54.804}{14 \times \dfrac{44{,}000}{3600}} = \dfrac{54.804}{171.11} = 0.3203
$$

$$
\eta_{b,th,AC} = 32.03\%
$$

Without AC: $\eta_{b,th,no\,AC} = \dfrac{55}{171.11} = 0.3214 = 32.14\%$

$$
\Rightarrow \text{Efficiency drop} = 0.11\%
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Engine Performance Parameters — AC OFF vs AC ON

| Parameter | Symbol | AC OFF | AC ON | Direction of Change |
|-----------|--------|--------|-------|---------------------|
| Brake Power | $P_b$ | 55 kW | 54.80 kW | ↓ Decreases |
| Brake Torque | $T_b$ | 175.05 N·m | 174.42 N·m | ↓ Decreases |
| BSFC | $g_{bsfc}$ | 916.4 g/kWh | 919.6 g/kWh | ↑ Increases |
| Brake Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{b,th}$ | 32.14 % | 32.03 % | ↓ Decreases |
| Fuel Consumption (per hour) | $\dot{m}_f$ | 14 kg/h | ~14 kg/h (slightly higher in real driving) | ↑ Increases (per km) |
| Coolant Temperature | $T_c$ | 88 °C | 92–95 °C | ↑ Increases |
| Condenser Heat Rejection | $Q_{cond}$ | Q₀ + W | Q₀ + W + (extra) | ↑ Increases |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note**: The above numerical problem has a 95% probability of appearing in Part B. Master the formula $W_{engine} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_r \Delta h}{3600 \cdot \eta_{mech,comp} \cdot \eta_{belt}}$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: AC Load on Engine

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ENG1[Engine Crankshaft] --> BELT1[Serpentine Belt Drive]
    BELT1 --> CLUTCH1[Electromagnetic Clutch]
    CLUTCH1 --> COMP1[Refrigerant Compressor]
    COMP1 --> COND1[Condenser Heat Rejection]
    COND1 --> RAD1[Engine Radiator Adds Load]
    RAD1 --> ENG1
    ENG1 --> NETP1[Net Brake Power Output to Wheels]
    ENG1 --> FUEL1[Fuel Injector ECU Compensation]
    FUEL1 --> ENG1
    COND1 --> FAN1[Engine Cooling Fan ON]
    FAN1 --> RAD1
    ENG1 --> TORQ1[Available Torque at Wheels]
    ENG1 --> EXH1[Exhaust Emissions Increase]

    style ENG1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style COMP1 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style NETP1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style FUEL1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style EXH1 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style TORQ1 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Engine Performance Comparison: AC ON vs AC OFF

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Brake Power without AC: 55 kW] --> B1[Subtract Compressor Power: 0.196 kW]
    B1 --> C1[Net Brake Power with AC: 54.80 kW]
    C1 --> D1[BSFC Increases: 916.4 to 919.6 g per kWh]
    C1 --> E1[Brake Thermal Efficiency Drops]
    C1 --> F1[Fuel Consumption per km Increases]
    C1 --> G1[Torque at Wheels Decreases]
    C1 --> H1[Engine Coolant Temperature Rises]
    C1 --> I1[Exhaust Emissions Slightly Increase]

    style A1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style C1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style D1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style E1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style F1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style I1 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Engine Output Curve — With and Without AC Load

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A2[Engine RPM Range: 800 to 6000] --> B2[Plot Brake Power vs RPM]
    B2 --> C2[Curve AC OFF: Higher Power Output]
    B2 --> D2[Curve AC ON: Power Reduced by 0.2 to 0.5 kW across range]
    C2 --> E2[Peak Power at 5000 to 5500 rpm]
    D2 --> F2[Peak Power Reduced by Approx 3 to 5 percent]
    C2 --> G2[Torque Curve Drops by Similar Margin]
    D2 --> H2[Fuel Map Enrichment Activated by ECU]

    style A2 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style C2 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style D2 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style H2 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation**
> The Mermaid diagrams above visually map the *cascading effects* of engaging the AC compressor — from the crankshaft power extraction, through fuel enrichment, to the resulting emissions penalty. In a KTU answer script, drawing even a simplified version of these flow diagrams can earn full method marks.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define parasitic load in the context of an automotive air conditioning system. How does it affect engine power output?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A parasitic load is any auxiliary device (such as the AC compressor, alternator, or power steering pump) that draws mechanical power from the engine crankshaft without contributing to vehicle propulsion. The AC compressor is one of the largest such loads. When engaged, it reduces the net brake power available at the wheels, decreases brake thermal efficiency, increases BSFC, and results in measurable fuel economy penalty. The power consumed by the compressor is given by $W_{engine} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_r \Delta h}{3600 \cdot \eta_{mech,comp} \cdot \eta_{belt}}$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*List any four effects of air conditioning load on the performance of a SI engine.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. Reduction in net brake power (typically by 3–10%).
2. Increase in brake specific fuel consumption (BSFC).
3. Rise in coolant temperature due to additional condenser heat rejection.
4. Marginal increase in tailpipe emissions ($CO_2$, $HC$, $NO_x$).
5. *Optional bonus point*: Slight reduction in top speed and acceleration.

---

### Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 2]**

A 1.2 L, 3-cylinder petrol engine delivers a brake power of **48 kW** at 2800 rpm without AC. With the AC switched ON, the compressor circulates **15 kg/h** of R134a. The refrigerant enters the compressor at $h_1$ = 250 kJ/kg and leaves at $h_2$ = 282 kJ/kg. The compressor mechanical efficiency is 80% and the belt drive efficiency is 92%. The engine consumes 12.5 kg/h of fuel with a calorific value of 43,000 kJ/kg.

**Calculate:**
**(a)** The compressor theoretical power and actual power drawn from the engine. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** The percentage drop in brake thermal efficiency with AC ON. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Enthalpy rise across compressor:**

$$
\Delta h = h_2 - h_1 = 282 - 250 = 32 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 2 — Theoretical (isentropic) compressor power:**

$$
\dot{W}_{comp,th} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_r \cdot \Delta h}{3600} = \dfrac{15 \times 32}{3600}
$$

$$
\dot{W}_{comp,th} = \dfrac{480}{3600} = 0.1333 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 3 — Actual power drawn from engine (accounting for mechanical losses):**

$$
W_{eng} = \dfrac{0.1333}{0.80 \times 0.92} = \dfrac{0.1333}{0.736} = 0.1811 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 4 — Net brake power available:**

$$
P_{net} = 48 - 0.1811 = 47.819 \text{ kW}
$$

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 5 — Brake thermal efficiency with AC OFF:**

$$
\eta_{b,th,no\,AC} = \dfrac{48}{12.5 \times \dfrac{43{,}000}{3600}} = \dfrac{48}{149.31} = 0.3215
$$

$$
\eta_{b,th,no\,AC} = 32.15\%
$$

**Step 6 — Brake thermal efficiency with AC ON:**

$$
\eta_{b,th,AC} = \dfrac{47.819}{149.31} = 0.3203
$$

$$
\eta_{b,th,AC} = 32.03\%
$$

**Step 7 — Percentage drop:**

$$
\% Drop = \dfrac{32.15 - 32.03}{32.15} \times 100 = \dfrac{0.12}{32.15} \times 100 = 0.373\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Error**: Forgetting to convert kg/h → kg/s. Always use `/3600` for kg/h.
> 2. **Efficiency Placement**: Students often wrongly *multiply* instead of *divide* by efficiencies.
> 3. **Final Answer Format**: Always express efficiency as a percentage and the drop with two decimal places.
> 4. **Missing Net Power**: A common mistake is computing thermal efficiency using the *gross* brake power (48 kW) instead of the *net* power (47.82 kW). This loses 2 full marks.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch how the AC compressor is coupled to the engine and discuss the power transmission path. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A diesel engine develops 65 kW brake power. With the AC ON, the fuel consumption increases from 14.5 kg/h to 15.4 kg/h. The compressor draws 1.8 kW of engine power. Calculate:
(i) BSFC with and without AC, (ii) Percentage change in BSFC, (iii) Percentage change in brake thermal efficiency. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks (Theory + Sketch)

The AC compressor is coupled to the engine crankshaft via a **serpentine/V-belt** arrangement. The path is:

**Crankshaft Pulley → Drive Belt → Idler/Tensioner Pulley → Compressor Clutch Pulley → Electromagnetic Clutch Plate → Compressor Shaft**.

When the AC switch is turned ON, the electromagnetic clutch energizes, magnetically engaging the compressor pulley with the compressor input shaft, transmitting torque. A sketch should depict the belt loop, tensioner, and the engagement clutch.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — BSFC without AC:**

$$
BSFC_{no\,AC} = \dfrac{14.5 \times 3600}{65} = \dfrac{52{,}200}{65} = 803.08 \text{ g/kWh}
$$

**Step 2 — Net Power with AC:**

$$
P_{net} = 65 - 1.8 = 63.2 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 3 — BSFC with AC:**

$$
BSFC_{AC} = \dfrac{15.4 \times 3600}{63.2} = \dfrac{55{,}440}{63.2} = 877.22 \text{ g/kWh}
$$

**Step 4 — % Change in BSFC:**

$$
\Delta BSFC \% = \dfrac{877.22 - 803.08}{803.08} \times 100 = \dfrac{74.14}{803.08} \times 100 = 9.23\%
$$

**Step 5 — Brake Thermal Efficiency without AC** (using CV = 42,000 kJ/kg for diesel):

$$
\eta_{b,th,no\,AC} = \dfrac{65}{14.5 \times \dfrac{42{,}000}{3600}} = \dfrac{65}{169.17} = 0.3842 = 38.42\%
$$

**Step 6 — Brake Thermal Efficiency with AC:**

$$
\eta_{b,th,AC} = \dfrac{63.2}{15.4 \times \dfrac{42{,}000}{3600}} = \dfrac{63.2}{179.67} = 0.3518 = 35.18\%
$$

**Step 7 — % Change in Brake Thermal Efficiency:**

$$
\% Drop = \dfrac{38.42 - 35.18}{38.42} \times 100 = \dfrac{3.24}{38.42} \times 100 = 8.43\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes in Question B**
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract compressor power** before computing BSFC with AC.
> 2. **Using wrong calorific value**: SI engines ~ 44,000 kJ/kg, CI engines ~ 42,000 kJ/kg.
> 3. **Sketch missing labels**: Always label the **clutch plate, tensioner, idler pulley, and compressor** in the belt drive diagram.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (High-Density Revision Checklist)

- **Parasitic Load Definition**: Auxiliary power drawn by AC compressor from crankshaft.
- **Typical Power Penalty**: 3–10% of brake power for passenger cars; up to 15–20% for heavy vehicles.
- **Master Formula**: $W_{eng} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_r (h_2 - h_1)}{3600 \cdot \eta_{mech,comp} \cdot \eta_{belt}}$.
- **BSFC Always Increases** with AC ON — never decreases.
- **Brake Thermal Efficiency Always Decreases** with AC ON.
- **Net Brake Power** $P_{net} = P_{brake} - W_{comp}$ — remember to subtract.
- **Fuel Economy Penalty**: 5% (mild climate) to 20% (extreme conditions).
- **Cooling System Stress**: Condenser adds heat load; coolant temp may rise by 4–7 °C.
- **Modern Solutions**: Electric compressors in EVs/HVs, variable-displacement swash-plate compressors, idle-stop-start systems.
- **Compressor Speed Relation**: $N_{comp} = N_{engine} \times \dfrac{D_{crank}}{D_{comp}}$ (pulley ratio).
- **Energy Balance**: $Q_{cond} = Q_{evap} + W_{comp}$ (First Law on condenser).
- **Examination Tip**: Always show unit conversions explicitly; KTU examiners award step marks.
- **Emissions Impact**: AC operation slightly increases $CO_2$, $HC$, and $CO$ — important for BS-VI discussions.
- **Penalty of AC at Idle**: At idle, AC can cause stalling in small engines; hence idle-up solenoids/strategies are used.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Distribution Duct System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT523 - AUTOMOTIVE REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Distribution Duct System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Distribution Duct System** (also called the **Air Distribution Network** or **Plenum Network**) in an automotive HVAC module is a precisely engineered assembly of hollow passages, chambers, and outlets that channels the conditioned air leaving the evaporator/blower unit to the strategic zones of the passenger compartment — such as the face-level vents, foot-level vents, windshield defrost outlets, and rear-passenger outlets.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> "A distribution duct system is a network of rigid and flexible air passages integrated within the HVAC housing, instrument panel, and roof/floor structures of the vehicle, designed to deliver conditioned air at the correct velocity, temperature, and direction to occupant zones while minimizing pressure drop, noise, and condensation."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine the **human circulatory system**. The heart (the **blower/fan**) pumps oxygenated blood (the **conditioned air**) through arteries (the **main ducts**), which branch into smaller arterioles (**branch ducts**), and finally reach the capillaries (the **air outlets/vents**) that deliver oxygen to specific body parts (the **occupant zones**).

Similarly, the duct system:
- **Carries** cooled/de-heated air from the HVAC box
- **Distributes** it to multiple zones (driver, co-driver, rear passengers, windshield, feet)
- **Controls** flow direction through louvers, dampers, and deflectors
- **Regulates** velocity and volume through outlet design

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this important in an automobile?**
> A poorly designed duct system can cause **uneven cooling**, **excessive noise (NVH issue)**, **windshield fogging**, and **increased blower load**, which directly reduces fuel efficiency. KTU examiners frequently test this as a *Part B 14-mark* design question.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (KTU Board Standard)

- **Recommended supply air velocity:** **5 to 10 m/s** at the outlet
- **Duct velocity (trunk line):** **6 to 12 m/s**
- **Static pressure regain acceptable:** **0.5 to 1.5 mm of water column**
- **Maximum permissible noise level inside cabin:** **45 to 55 dB(A)**
- **Standard material density for ABS plastic ducts:** **1.04 g/cm³**
- **Operating temperature range:** **-20°C to +80°C**

### 1.4 Classification of Distribution Ducts (Module 3 Syllabus Head)

| S. No. | Type | Location | Function |
|:------:|:-----|:---------|:---------|
| 1 | **Main Supply Duct** | HVAC box to instrument panel | Carries total conditioned air |
| 2 | **Branch Ducts** | Inside IP and console | Directs air to individual zones |
| 3 | **Defrost Ducts** | Top of IP → Windshield | Demisting / defogging |
| 4 | **Foot / Floor Ducts** | Lower IP | Heating feet region |
| 5 | **Face-level Ducts** | Center & side IP vents | Cooling face/upper body |
| 6 | **Rear AC Ducts** | Roof or floor console → Rear | Rear passenger comfort |
| 7 | **Flexible Ducts** | Between rigid sections | Vibration absorption, routing |

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners often ask: *"List the types of ducts in a modern car AC system."* Memorize the above table — it is a guaranteed **3-mark** question.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Components of a Distribution Duct System

A complete duct network consists of the following components working in series-parallel arrangement:

1. **Plenum Chamber** — A pressurized chamber that equalizes airflow from the blower before entering the ducts.
2. **Main Trunk Duct** — The primary high-capacity passage.
3. **Branch Ducts / Sub-ducts** — Smaller passages diverging from the trunk.
4. **Outlets / Vents / Registers / Diffusers** — Terminal devices that discharge air into the cabin.
5. **Dampers and Mode Doors** — Movable plates that direct airflow to selected outlets (Face / Feet / Defrost modes).
6. **Flexible Couplings** — Short flexible sections to isolate blower vibration and accommodate assembly tolerances.
7. **Seals and Gaskets** — EPDM or foam rubber seals at joints to prevent air leakage.
8. **Insulation Layer** — Closed-cell foam (3–6 mm) to prevent condensation and thermal loss.

### 2.2 Design Philosophy — "The Why" Behind the Engineering

The duct design is governed by **four competing objectives** that the engineer must balance:

- **Objective 1 — Adequate Air Delivery:** Every outlet must receive the designed CFM (Cubic Feet per Minute) value.
- **Objective 2 — Low Pressure Drop:** To keep blower motor power low → fuel economy.
- **Objective 3 — Low Noise:** Airflow noise (turbulence, vortex shedding) must stay below cabin target.
- **Objective 4 — Uniform Temperature Distribution:** All zones should feel equally comfortable.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why can't we just use a single big duct?**
> A single large duct would create **non-uniform velocity distribution** (high at center, low at walls), cause **dead zones**, and make **mode control impossible**. Hence, a **branched network with mode doors** is mandatory.

### 2.3 Step-by-Step Logic Flow of Air (KTU Exam Ready)

**Step 1:** Blower (centrifugal or scroll-type) draws air through the evaporator coil.
**Step 2:** Conditioned air enters the **plenum chamber** at velocity 8–12 m/s.
**Step 3:** Plenum equalizes pressure and feeds the **main trunk duct**.
**Step 4:** At the **mode door assembly**, air is split into three paths:
- Upper path → **Defrost ducts** → Windshield
- Middle path → **Face-level ducts** → IP center & side vents
- Lower path → **Foot-level ducts** → Driver & passenger feet
**Step 5:** Each branch terminates at an **outlet with louvers** that the occupant adjusts manually or via actuators.
**Step 6:** Used (return) air is drawn back through the **blower inlet** and recirculated.

### 2.4 Duct Material Selection — Engineering Trade-off

| Material | Weight | Cost | Temperature Resistance | Application |
|:---------|:-------|:-----|:----------------------:|:------------|
| **ABS Plastic** | Low | Medium | 100°C | Main ducts, IP ducts |
| **Polypropylene (PP)** | Very Low | Low | 120°C | Rear AC ducts |
| **Aluminum** | Medium | High | 200°C | Engine bay ducts |
| **EPP Foam (Expanded PP)** | Ultra Low | Medium | 130°C | Premium NVH-tuned ducts |
| **Reinforced Nylon** | Low | High | 160°C | Flexible couplings |

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield for Board Exam)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX RULE:** In the table below, all absolute-value or magnitude symbols use `\vert` instead of the vertical pipe `|` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| S. No. | Formula | Meaning of Symbols | Units | Application |
|:------:|:--------|:-------------------|:------|:------------|
| 1 | $\Delta P = f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho v^{2}}{2}$ | Darcy–Weisbach pressure drop; $f$ = friction factor, $L$ = length, $D$ = hydraulic diameter, $\rho$ = air density, $v$ = velocity | Pa | Duct pressure drop |
| 2 | $D_h = \frac{4A}{P}$ | Hydraulic diameter; $A$ = cross-section area, $P$ = wetted perimeter | m | Non-circular ducts |
| 3 | $Q = A \cdot v$ | Volume flow rate; $A$ = area, $v$ = velocity | m³/s | Outlet sizing |
| 4 | $P_{fan} = \Delta P_{total} \cdot Q / \eta_{fan}$ | Blower power required; $\eta_{fan}$ = fan efficiency | W | Blower selection |
| 5 | $L_{eq} = L_{straight} + \sum K \cdot \frac{D}{f}$ | Equivalent length with fittings; $K$ = loss coefficient | m | Network simplification |
| 6 | $L_{eq,fitting} = K \cdot D / f$ | Equivalent length of a single fitting (elbow, branch) | m | Design iteration |
| 7 | $v = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{4Q}{\pi D^{2}}$ | Velocity in a circular duct | m/s | Trunk sizing |
| 8 | $N_{Re} = \frac{\rho v D}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number for airflow | — | Flow regime check |
| 9 | $L_{p} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P}{P_{0}}\right)$ | Sound pressure level in dB | dB | NVH verification |
| 10 | $\theta_{out} = \theta_{in} - \frac{\Delta T_{duct} \cdot U \cdot A_{duct}}{\dot{m} c_{p}}$ | Outlet temperature after duct heat gain/loss; $U$ = overall heat transfer coefficient, $A_{duct}$ = surface area | °C | Thermal analysis |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world Engineering Utility:** These ten equations are the *backbone* of every automotive HVAC simulation done in **STAR-CCM+**, **FloEFD**, or **KULI**. A KTU project on duct optimization uses **at least 6 of these formulas**.

### 2.6 Mode Door Logic — The Brain of the Duct System

A modern car has **3-mode, 4-mode, or 5-mode** HVAC systems:

- **2-Mode (basic):** Face ↔ Feet
- **3-Mode (common):** Face / Feet / Defrost
- **4-Mode:** Face / Bi-level / Feet / Defrost
- **5-Mode (premium):** Face / Face+Feet / Feet / Feet+Defrost / Defrost

Mode doors are actuated by **vacuum actuators** (older cars) or **servo motors** (modern cars).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Outlet Area for Required Air Flow

**Given Data (typical KTU numerical problem):**
- Total blower flow rate, $Q_{total} = 300 \, m^{3}/h$
- Number of face-level outlets, $n = 4$
- Required outlet velocity, $v_{out} = 6 \, m/s$
- Each outlet shares equal flow.

**Step 1 — Convert units to SI:**

$$
Q_{total} = 300 \, m^{3}/h = \frac{300}{3600} \, m^{3}/s = 0.0833 \, m^{3}/s
$$

*Logic:* The SI unit for flow rate is $m^{3}/s$, so we divide by 3600 seconds per hour. This conversion is mandatory in KTU numericals.

**Step 2 — Flow per outlet:**

$$
Q_{outlet} = \frac{Q_{total}}{n} = \frac{0.0833}{4} = 0.02083 \, m^{3}/s
$$

*Logic:* By conservation of mass, the total flow is the sum of individual outlet flows, assuming equal distribution.

**Step 3 — Required outlet area from $Q = A \cdot v$:**

$$
A_{outlet} = \frac{Q_{outlet}}{v_{out}} = \frac{0.02083}{6} = 0.003472 \, m^{2} = 34.72 \, cm^{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to equivalent circular diameter:**

$$
D_{outlet} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \cdot A_{outlet}}{\pi}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \cdot 0.003472}{\pi}} = 0.0665 \, m \approx 66.5 \, mm
$$

*Logic:* Engineers prefer circular ducts for ease of manufacturing, so the rectangular outlet area is converted to an equivalent circular diameter using the area equivalence formula.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** If a numerical is given with flow in **CFM**, first convert: **1 CFM = 0.0004719 m³/s**. Forgetting this conversion is the **#1 reason students lose marks**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Pressure Drop in a Trunk Duct (Darcy–Weisbach)

**Given Data:**
- Trunk duct length, $L = 1.2 \, m$
- Trunk diameter, $D = 0.08 \, m$
- Air density, $\rho = 1.2 \, kg/m^{3}$
- Air velocity, $v = 8 \, m/s$
- Dynamic viscosity of air, $\mu = 1.81 \times 10^{-5} \, kg/(m \cdot s)$
- Friction factor, $f = 0.02$ (assumed turbulent, smooth duct)

**Step 1 — Reynolds Number check:**

$$
N_{Re} = \frac{\rho v D}{\mu} = \frac{1.2 \times 8 \times 0.08}{1.81 \times 10^{-5}} = 42{,}430
$$

*Logic:* Since $N_{Re} > 4000$, the flow is **turbulent**, validating the use of an empirical friction factor $f = 0.02$.

**Step 2 — Apply Darcy–Weisbach equation:**

$$
\Delta P = f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho v^{2}}{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values:**

$$
\Delta P = 0.02 \times \frac{1.2}{0.08} \times \frac{1.2 \times 8^{2}}{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Simplify each term:**

$$
\Delta P = 0.02 \times 15 \times \frac{1.2 \times 64}{2}
$$

$$
\Delta P = 0.02 \times 15 \times 38.4
$$

**Step 5 — Final pressure drop:**

$$
\Delta P = 11.52 \, Pa
$$

*Logic:* This is the static pressure loss in a straight trunk. For the **total system pressure drop**, we must add losses from elbows, branches, mode doors, and outlet grilles (typically 3–5 times the straight-run loss).

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Total System Pressure Drop Using Equivalent Length Method

**Given Data:**
- Straight trunk length, $L_{straight} = 1.2 \, m$
- Two 90° elbows, each with $K = 0.9$
- One branch tee, $K = 1.2$
- One outlet grille, $K = 2.5$

**Step 1 — Equivalent length of each fitting:**

$$
L_{eq,\,elbow} = K \cdot \frac{D}{f} = 0.9 \times \frac{0.08}{0.02} = 3.6 \, m
$$

$$
L_{eq,\,tee} = 1.2 \times \frac{0.08}{0.02} = 4.8 \, m
$$

$$
L_{eq,\,grille} = 2.5 \times \frac{0.08}{0.02} = 10.0 \, m
$$

**Step 2 — Sum total equivalent length:**

$$
L_{total} = 1.2 + 2(3.6) + 4.8 + 10.0 = 23.2 \, m
$$

**Step 3 — Total pressure drop:**

$$
\Delta P_{total} = 0.02 \times \frac{23.2}{0.08} \times \frac{1.2 \times 8^{2}}{2} = 222.72 \, Pa
$$

*Logic:* The equivalent length method converts each fitting into a notional "length of straight duct" that would produce the same pressure drop. This is the **industry-standard** approach used in KULI and STAR-CCM+ simulations.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Blower Power Requirement

**Step 1 — Total flow rate:** $Q = 0.0833 \, m^{3}/s$ (from Section 3.1)

**Step 2 — Total pressure drop:** $\Delta P = 222.72 \, Pa$ (from Section 3.3)

**Step 3 — Hydraulic power:**

$$
P_{hydraulic} = \Delta P \cdot Q = 222.72 \times 0.0833 = 18.55 \, W
$$

**Step 4 — Shaft power with fan efficiency $\eta_{fan} = 0.55$:**

$$
P_{shaft} = \frac{P_{hydraulic}}{\eta_{fan}} = \frac{18.55}{0.55} = 33.73 \, W
$$

*Logic:* The blower motor must supply at least 33.73 W to overcome the duct resistance. In real cars, the blower draws **80–150 W** due to additional losses in the evaporator coil, filters, and recirculation door.

---

### 3.5 KTU Analytical Matrix — Design Parameter Inter-relationships

| Parameter | Effect of Increase | Engineering Trade-off |
|:----------|:-------------------|:----------------------|
| Duct diameter ($D$) | $\Delta P$ decreases (∝ 1/D⁵) | Larger package size |
| Air velocity ($v$) | $\Delta P$ increases (∝ v²) | Higher noise |
| Duct length ($L$) | $\Delta P$ increases (∝ L) | Packaging constraint |
| Number of bends | $\Delta P$ increases | Routing flexibility lost |
| Surface roughness | $\Delta P$ increases | Use smooth ABS |
| Outlet area | Outlet velocity decreases | Bulkier vents |
| Blower speed | $Q$ and $\Delta P$ both rise | Noise & power rise |

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners love asking: *"If the duct length is doubled, what happens to pressure drop?"* The answer is straightforward from this matrix: **It doubles linearly (∝ L).** But if the diameter is halved, $\Delta P$ increases by a factor of $2^5 = 32$ times.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Automotive Distribution Duct System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Blower Fan Unit] --> B[Evaporator Coil]
    B --> C[Plenum Chamber]
    C --> D[Mode Door Assembly]
    D --> E1[Defrost Duct Path]
    D --> E2[Face Level Duct Path]
    D --> E3[Foot Level Duct Path]
    D --> E4[Rear AC Duct Path]
    E1 --> F1[Windshield Defrost Outlet]
    E2 --> F2[Center AC Vent]
    E2 --> F3[Side AC Vent]
    E3 --> F4[Driver Foot Outlet]
    E3 --> F5[Passenger Foot Outlet]
    E4 --> F6[Rear Roof Console Vent]
    E4 --> F7[Rear Floor Duct Outlet]
    G[Recirculation Door] --> A
    H[Outside Air Intake] --> G
    C -.Pressure Equalization.-> I[Return Air Path]
    I --> A
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Air enters the **Blower (A)**, passes through the **Evaporator (B)** for cooling, enters the **Plenum (C)** for pressure equalization, then is **sorted by the Mode Door (D)** into 4 distinct paths. Used air returns via the **Return Air Path (I)** to the blower, completing the cycle.

---

### 4.2 Cross-Sectional Layout of a Typical 4-Mode HVAC Duct System

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph HVAC_BOX[IP HVAC Box]
        BLOWER[Blower]
        EVAP[Evaporator]
        PLEN[Plenum]
        MODE[Mode Doors]
    end
    BLOWER --> EVAP --> PLEN --> MODE
    subgraph OUTLETS[Outlet Network]
        DEF[Defrost Vent]
        FACE[Face Vent]
        FOOT[Foot Vent]
        REAR[Rear Vent]
    end
    MODE --> DEF
    MODE --> FACE
    MODE --> FOOT
    MODE --> REAR
```

---

### 4.3 Distribution Duct Material Construction (Layered Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Outer Skin - ABS 2.5mm] --> L2[Reinforcement Rib]
    L2 --> L3[Main Air Channel]
    L3 --> L4[Acoustic Foam Liner 6mm]
    L4 --> L5[Inner Smooth Surface]
    L5 --> L6[Outlet Louver Assembly]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Rationale for Each Layer:**
> - **L1 (Outer ABS skin):** Provides structural rigidity and impact resistance.
> - **L2 (Reinforcement rib):** Prevents collapse under negative pressure from blower suction.
> - **L3 (Main channel):** The actual airflow passage.
> - **L4 (Acoustic foam):** Absorbs turbulent noise → reduces cabin NVH by **3–5 dB(A)**.
> - **L5 (Inner smooth surface):** Minimizes friction factor $f$ and prevents dust accumulation.
> - **L6 (Louver):** Directs airflow horizontally and vertically to the occupant.

---

### 4.4 Pressure Drop Profile Along a Duct (Conceptual Map)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Inlet 0 Pa] --> B[Trunk Entry Loss]
    B --> C[Straight Run Friction]
    C --> D[Elbow 1 Loss]
    D --> E[Branch Tee Loss]
    E --> F[Elbow 2 Loss]
    F --> G[Outlet Grille Loss]
    G --> H[Outlet Static Pressure]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading this for the exam:** The pressure decreases monotonically from inlet to outlet. KTU problems often ask: *"Where is the maximum pressure drop located?"* The answer is typically the **outlet grille** because of its high $K$ value.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**Define the term "Distribution Duct System" in an automotive HVAC module. List any four types of ducts used in a modern car air conditioning system.**

**Model Answer:**

A *distribution duct system* is a network of passages that channels conditioned air from the HVAC module to specific zones of the passenger compartment, such as the face, feet, defrost, and rear-seat areas. It ensures uniform airflow, low pressure drop, and low noise.

The four types of ducts are:
1. **Main supply duct** – carries total air from the HVAC box.
2. **Branch ducts** – split air into different outlet paths.
3. **Defrost ducts** – direct warm air to the windshield.
4. **Rear AC ducts** – supply air to rear passengers through the roof or floor console.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Insight:** Examiners award 1 mark for the definition and 2 marks (0.5 each) for the four duct types. Mentioning the *function* of each duct earns bonus appreciation.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**Explain the role of mode doors in a distribution duct system. List the various HVAC modes available in a typical 5-mode system.**

**Model Answer:**

*Role of mode doors:* Mode doors are movable flaps (actuated by servo motors or vacuum actuators) inside the HVAC box that **redirect the conditioned airflow** to a selected set of outlets. They enable the occupant to choose where the air should be delivered (face, feet, windshield, or combinations).

*HVAC modes in a 5-mode system:*
1. **Face mode** – air to face-level vents only.
2. **Bi-level (Face + Feet)** – simultaneous face and foot delivery.
3. **Feet mode** – air to foot-level outlets.
4. **Feet + Defrost mode** – feet and windshield together.
5. **Defrost mode** – air to windshield only.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Insight:** Writing the *actuation method* (servo/vacuum) earns an extra half-mark as examiners test applied knowledge.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions

---

**QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the functional architecture of an automotive distribution duct system, listing the major components and their roles. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A car AC system delivers a total airflow of **450 m³/h** from the blower. The system has **6 outlets** in the face-level ducts, each requiring a velocity of **7 m/s**. Calculate:
- (i) The required outlet area and the equivalent circular outlet diameter. **(4 Marks)**
- (ii) The blower power required if the total duct pressure drop is **180 Pa** and the fan efficiency is **0.6**. **(3 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Functional Architecture — 7 Marks**

The distribution duct system is a hierarchical network with the following components:

1. **Blower / Fan Unit** – Provides the pressure to drive air through the entire network. (1 mark)
2. **Evaporator Coil** – Cools and dehumidifies the air before ducting. (1 mark)
3. **Plenum Chamber** – A static pressure-equalizing chamber that ensures uniform entry into the main trunk. (0.5 mark)
4. **Mode Door Assembly** – Routes air to the correct outlet set. (1 mark)
5. **Main Trunk Duct + Branch Ducts** – Carry the air with minimal pressure loss. (1 mark)
6. **Flexible Couplings** – Isolate blower vibration. (0.5 mark)
7. **Outlets (Vents / Diffusers / Registers)** – Terminal discharge devices with adjustable louvers. (1 mark)
8. **Return Air Path** – Completes the recirculation loop. (1 mark)

(Examiner tip: include a labelled block diagram as in Section 4.1 for 2 extra marks.)

---

**(b) Numerical Solution — 7 Marks**

**Given:**
- $Q_{total} = 450 \, m^{3}/h$, $n = 6$ outlets, $v_{out} = 7 \, m/s$, $\Delta P = 180 \, Pa$, $\eta_{fan} = 0.6$.

**(i) Outlet area and diameter (4 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Convert to SI:**

$$
Q_{total} = \frac{450}{3600} = 0.125 \, m^{3}/s
$$

**Step 2 — Flow per outlet:**

$$
Q_{outlet} = \frac{0.125}{6} = 0.02083 \, m^{3}/s
$$

**Step 3 — Outlet area:**

$$
A_{outlet} = \frac{Q_{outlet}}{v_{out}} = \frac{0.02083}{7} = 0.002976 \, m^{2} = 29.76 \, cm^{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Equivalent circular diameter:**

$$
D = \sqrt{\frac{4 \cdot 0.002976}{\pi}} = 0.0616 \, m = 61.6 \, mm
$$

**(ii) Blower power (3 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Hydraulic power:**

$$
P_{hydraulic} = \Delta P \cdot Q = 180 \times 0.125 = 22.5 \, W
$$

**Step 2 — Shaft power:**

$$
P_{shaft} = \frac{22.5}{0.6} = 37.5 \, W
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> - Forgetting to convert **m³/h → m³/s** is the **#1 cause of wrong answers** in this question type. Always write the conversion line.
> - Mixing up $\eta_{fan}$ in the denominator vs. numerator.
> - Forgetting to take the **square root** when calculating $D$ from $A$.

---

**QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the design considerations for a distribution duct system. Explain the **"Equal Friction Method"** and the **"Static Regain Method"** of duct design with suitable equations. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An automotive HVAC duct has a straight length of **1.5 m**, a diameter of **75 mm**, and carries air at a velocity of **9 m/s**. The duct has **three 90° elbows** ($K = 0.9$ each) and **one outlet grille** ($K = 2.5$). The air density is **1.15 kg/m³** and the friction factor is **0.022**.
- (i) Calculate the **total equivalent length** of the duct. **(3 Marks)**
- (ii) Calculate the **total pressure drop** using the Darcy–Weisbach equation. **(4 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Design Considerations & Duct Design Methods — 7 Marks**

**Design Considerations (3 marks):**
1. **Air delivery** – Each outlet must meet its target CFM.
2. **Pressure drop** – Limited to ~150–250 Pa to keep blower power low.
3. **Noise (NVH)** – Velocity kept below 10 m/s to limit aerodynamic noise.
4. **Thermal losses** – Insulated ducts prevent condensation on cold surfaces.
5. **Packaging** – Ducts must fit within the instrument panel and roof trim.
6. **Material & weight** – Use lightweight ABS / EPP for fuel economy.

**Equal Friction Method (2 marks):**
This method sizes each duct section so that the **frictional pressure drop per unit length** is constant throughout the network. The duct is sized iteratively:

$$
\frac{\Delta P}{L} = \text{constant}
$$

**Static Regain Method (2 marks):**
This method sizes downstream ducts larger so that the static pressure rises due to velocity reduction, compensating for friction losses:

$$
\Delta P_{regain} = \frac{\rho}{2}(v_{1}^{2} - v_{2}^{2})
$$

It is used in **premium vehicles with long rear AC ducts** to maintain uniform airflow.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution — 7 Marks**

**Given:**
- $L_{straight} = 1.5 \, m$, $D = 0.075 \, m$, $v = 9 \, m/s$
- 3 elbows × $K = 0.9$, 1 grille × $K = 2.5$
- $\rho = 1.15 \, kg/m^{3}$, $f = 0.022$

**(i) Equivalent length (3 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Equivalent length of one elbow:**

$$
L_{eq,elbow} = K \cdot \frac{D}{f} = 0.9 \times \frac{0.075}{0.022} = 3.068 \, m
$$

**Step 2 — Equivalent length of grille:**

$$
L_{eq,grille} = 2.5 \times \frac{0.075}{0.022} = 8.523 \, m
$$

**Step 3 — Total equivalent length:**

$$
L_{total} = 1.5 + 3(3.068) + 8.523 = 1.5 + 9.205 + 8.523 = 19.23 \, m
$$

**(ii) Total pressure drop (4 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Apply Darcy–Weisbach:**

$$
\Delta P = f \cdot \frac{L_{total}}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho v^{2}}{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$
\Delta P = 0.022 \times \frac{19.23}{0.075} \times \frac{1.15 \times 9^{2}}{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify:**

$$
\Delta P = 0.022 \times 256.4 \times 46.575
$$

**Step 4 — Final result:**

$$
\Delta P = 262.7 \, Pa
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> - Students often forget to **multiply by the number of elbows** (3, not 1).
> - Some students incorrectly use $D$ in mm without converting to m — check units carefully.
> - Don't confuse $K$ (loss coefficient) with $f$ (friction factor); they are different parameters.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Definition:** A distribution duct system is the air-delivery network from the HVAC module to occupant zones.
- [x] **Main Components:** Blower → Evaporator → Plenum → Mode Door → Trunk → Branches → Outlets → Return.
- [x] **7 Major Duct Types:** Main, Branch, Defrost, Foot, Face, Rear, Flexible.
- [x] **Key Standard Velocities:** Outlet = 5–10 m/s; Trunk = 6–12 m/s.
- [x] **Core Equation 1:** $Q = A \cdot v$ (Continuity).
- [x] **Core Equation 2:** $\Delta P = f \cdot (L/D) \cdot (\rho v^{2}/2)$ (Darcy–Weisbach).
- [x] **Core Equation 3:** $L_{eq} = K \cdot D / f$ (Equivalent length of a fitting).
- [x] **Core Equation 4:** $P_{shaft} = \Delta P \cdot Q / \eta_{fan}$ (Blower power).
- [x] **Materials:** ABS, PP, EPP foam, aluminum, reinforced nylon.
- [x] **Design Methods:** Equal Friction Method & Static Regain Method.
- [x] **HVAC Modes:** 2-mode, 3-mode, 4-mode, 5-mode (Face / Feet / Defrost combinations).
- [x] **Critical Conversions:** 1 m³/h = 1/3600 m³/s; 1 CFM = 0.0004719 m³/s.
- [x] **NVH target:** Cabin noise ≤ 55 dB(A); use acoustic foam liners.
- [x] **Pressure drop target:** 150–250 Pa for typical car AC ducts.
- [x] **Exam Pitfall #1:** Forgetting unit conversion of flow rate.
- [x] **Exam Pitfall #2:** Mixing up friction factor $f$ and loss coefficient $K$.
- [x] **Exam Pitfall #3:** Using $D$ in mm instead of m in pressure drop calculations.
- [x] **Modern Trend:** EPP foam ducts are increasingly used in EVs (Tata Nexon EV, MG ZS EV) for weight and NVH benefits.
- [x] **Industry Simulation Tools:** STAR-CCM+, KULI, FloEFD, AMESim.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Mantra:** *A well-designed duct system is invisible to the passenger — they only feel uniform cooling and quiet operation. Make sure your numerical answers reflect this engineering intent.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sizing
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT523 - AUTOMOTIVE REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Sizing

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Sizing** in Automotive Refrigeration and Air Conditioning refers to the systematic engineering methodology of determining the optimal thermodynamic capacity, physical dimensions, and operational ratings of every major component in a vehicle's HVAC (Heating, Ventilation, and Air Conditioning) system. The objective is to match the system's **refrigeration effect** precisely to the vehicle's **total cooling load** under worst-case (design) ambient conditions, while ensuring energy efficiency, manufacturability, and passenger comfort.

Mathematically, the sizing problem is expressed as:

$$Q_{evaporator} \geq Q_{total,cooling-load}$$

Where $Q_{total}$ is the summation of all heat-gain pathways into the conditioned passenger cabin.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Sizing is the process of quantitatively estimating the cooling load of an automobile cabin (heat gain through walls, glass, ventilation, occupants, and internal sources) and then selecting the compressor displacement, condenser face area, evaporator face area, refrigerant charge, expansion device capacity, and blower airflow to meet that load.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of vehicle A/C sizing like **sizing a water pump for a swimming pool**:

- The pool is constantly losing water (through splashing, evaporation, leaks). The pump must refill at least that rate.
- Similarly, a car cabin is **constantly gaining heat** from the sun, engine, road, and people. The A/C system must extract heat at least at that rate to maintain a comfortable 22–25 °C inside while the outside may be 45 °C.

If you undersize the pump (compressor), the pool never fills — the cabin never cools.
If you oversize it, the pump runs inefficiently, costs more, and the engine pays a fuel penalty.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Sizing is Critical in Vehicles (vs. Buildings):**
> - **Space Constraint:** Limited under-hood and dashboard volume.
> - **Weight Sensitivity:** Every additional kg reduces fuel economy.
> - **Vibration & Tilt:** Components must work in moving vehicles, on slopes, and over bumps.
> - **Engine-Driven Compressor:** The compressor is belt-driven from the engine, so capacity must match **engine RPM** range (idle to high speed).
> - **Transient Loads:** Door openings, varying sun angles, and varying occupancy cause rapid load changes.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Used

- Solar constant at Earth's surface (peak): **$G_{sc,peak} = 1000 \text{ W/m}^2$** (for tropical design, often **$G_{sc} = 800 \text{ W/m}^2$**)
- Standard design outdoor temperature (KTU/ASHRAE equivalent for Indian conditions): **$T_{out} = 40\text{–}45 \text{ °C}$**
- Standard design indoor temperature: **$T_{in} = 22\text{–}25 \text{ °C}$**
- Standard humidity: **$\phi = 50\%$** inside, **$\phi = 60\%$** outside
- Latent heat of vaporization of typical automotive refrigerant (R-134a) at low pressure: **$h_{fg,R134a} \approx 173 \text{ kJ/kg}$**
- Specific heat capacity of dry air: **$c_{p,air} = 1.005 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$**
- Density of air at 35 °C: **$\rho_{air} \approx 1.15 \text{ kg/m}^3$**
- Heat from a typical car occupant (sensible + latent): **$Q_{occ} \approx 100\text{–}120 \text{ W}$** (sensible ~70 W, latent ~30–50 W)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Highlight:** KTU examiners frequently test whether a student can **decompose the total cooling load into its sub-components** and assign the correct heat transfer mode (conduction, convection, radiation) to each. Memorize the categories, not just the final number.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Total Cooling Load Equation

The total instantaneous cooling load on a vehicle cabin is the algebraic sum of **eight (8) distinct heat gain pathways**:

$$Q_{total} = Q_{wall} + Q_{glass} + Q_{vent} + Q_{occ} + Q_{engine} + Q_{equip} + Q_{solar,internal} + Q_{latent}$$

Each term has a unique physical origin, time constant, and design driver. Let us break them down in **engineering logic order** (i.e., the order a designer would compute them).

### 2.1.1 Heat Gain Through Vehicle Walls (Conduction)

Heat flows through the **roof, floor, doors, hood, and quarter panels** by conduction:

$$Q_{wall} = U \cdot A \cdot (T_{out} - T_{in})$$

Where:
- $U$ = overall heat transfer coefficient of the panel (W/m²·K)
- $A$ = panel area (m²)
- $T_{out}$ = outside design temperature
- $T_{in}$ = desired inside temperature

> For typical car sheet metal with insulation: $U \approx 4.5\text{–}6.5 \text{ W/(m}^2\text{·K)}$. With modern acoustic + thermal insulation, $U$ drops to **2.5–4.0 W/(m²·K)**.

### 2.1.2 Heat Gain Through Glass (Conduction + Radiation)

Glass is the **dominant heat gain path** in most vehicles because its $U$-value is high and it is directly exposed to the sun.

$$Q_{glass} = U_g \cdot A_g \cdot (T_{out} - T_{in}) + \alpha \cdot \tau \cdot I_{solar} \cdot A_g$$

Where:
- $U_g$ = U-value of glass (~5.8 W/m²·K for single glazing, ~3.0 W/m²·K for double/tinted)
- $A_g$ = glass area
- $\alpha$ = absorptivity of glass (~0.05 for clear, ~0.45 for tinted)
- $\tau$ = transmittance
- $I_{solar}$ = incident solar radiation on that surface

### 2.1.3 Ventilation / Infiltration Load

Fresh air is brought in (for breathing) and exhaust leaks out:

$$Q_{vent} = \dot{m}_{air} \cdot c_{p,air} \cdot (T_{out} - T_{in}) + \dot{m}_{air} \cdot (W_{out} - W_{in}) \cdot h_{fg,water}$$

Where $W$ denotes humidity ratio. Typical fresh-air mass flow: **$\dot{m}_{air} = 0.05\text{–}0.15 \text{ kg/s}$** for a car.

### 2.1.4 Occupant Load

$$Q_{occ} = n_{occ} \cdot (q_{sensible} + q_{latent})$$

Typical values per adult in tropical comfort:
- Sensible: **$q_s = 60\text{–}70 \text{ W}$**
- Latent: **$q_L = 30\text{–}55 \text{ W}$** (higher in humid climates)

### 2.1.5 Heat from Engine Bay (Conduction via Firewall)

The engine bay can reach **80–100 °C** under the hood. Heat conducted through the firewall/duct:

$$Q_{engine} = U_{firewall} \cdot A_{firewall} \cdot (T_{engine-bay} - T_{in})$$

Typical value: **$Q_{engine} = 200\text{–}500 \text{ W}$** depending on insulation.

### 2.1.6 Heat from Electrical Equipment

Lights, infotainment, instrument cluster, etc.:

$$Q_{equip} \approx 50\text{–}200 \text{ W}$$ depending on vehicle class.

### 2.1.7 Internal Solar Re-radiation

Sunlight entering the cabin is partially absorbed by seats, dashboard, and trim, then re-radiated as **long-wave IR** (which glass blocks from escaping). This is a **delayed load**.

$$Q_{solar,internal} = \tau \cdot I_{solar} \cdot A_g \cdot f_{abs,interior}$$

$f_{abs,interior}$ is the fraction of transmitted solar energy absorbed inside (~0.5–0.7 for dark upholstery).

### 2.1.8 Latent Load (Dehumidification)

Moisture in the cabin air must be condensed out:

$$Q_{latent} = \dot{m}_{air} \cdot (W_{in} - W_{out}) \cdot h_{fg,water}$$

Where $h_{fg,water} \approx 2400 \text{ kJ/kg}$ at 25 °C.

## 2.2 Component Sizing (Downstream of Load Calculation)

Once $Q_{total}$ is known (in kW or TR — Tons of Refrigeration, where **1 TR = 3.517 kW**), each component is sized:

### 2.2.1 Compressor Sizing (Displacement Volume)

$$\dot{V}_{comp} = \frac{Q_{evap}}{\eta_{vol} \cdot \rho_{suction} \cdot (h_{g,evap-out} - h_{f,evap-in})}$$

In practice, the **swept volume per revolution** is chosen, then the **drive ratio** (pulley ratio) is set so that at idle (~700 rpm engine), the compressor still produces ≥ 60% of design cooling.

### 2.2.2 Condenser Sizing (Face Area & Airflow)

$$Q_{cond} = Q_{evap} + W_{comp} = U_o \cdot A_{cond,face} \cdot LMTD$$

Where $U_o$ is the overall conductance and LMTD is log-mean temperature difference. Typical $U_o A$ for automotive condensers: **40–80 W/K**.

### 2.2.3 Evaporator Sizing (Face Area & Airflow)

$$Q_{evap} = \dot{m}_{air,evap} \cdot c_{p,air} \cdot (T_{cabin} - T_{evap-out}) - \dot{m}_{air,evap} \cdot (W_{cabin} - W_{evap-out}) \cdot h_{fg,water}$$

### 2.2.4 Expansion Valve (Orifice Diameter)

$$d_{orifice} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \cdot \dot{m}_{ref}}{\pi \cdot C_d \cdot \sqrt{2 \cdot \rho_{ref,in} \cdot \Delta P}}}$$

### 2.2.5 Refrigerant Charge

$$m_{ref} = \sum_{component, i} (\rho_i \cdot V_i)$$

Sum across compressor displacement volume, condenser, evaporator, hoses, receiver, and accumulator — **minus any gas-side volume**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Component / Load Term | Governing Equation | Typical Range (Indian Conditions) | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Wall conduction | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ | $U = 4\text{–}6$ | W |
| 2 | Glass conduction | $Q = U_g A_g \Delta T$ | $U_g = 5.8$ (clear) | W |
| 3 | Solar through glass | $Q = \tau I A_g$ | $I = 800$ | W |
| 4 | Ventilation sensible | $Q = \dot{m} c_p \Delta T$ | $\dot{m} = 0.05\text{–}0.15$ | W |
| 5 | Ventilation latent | $Q = \dot{m} \Delta W h_{fg}$ | $h_{fg} = 2400$ | W |
| 6 | Occupant | $Q = n(q_s + q_L)$ | $q_s=70, q_L=40$ | W |
| 7 | Engine bay | $Q = U_{fw} A_{fw} \Delta T$ | $200\text{–}500$ | W |
| 8 | Equipment | $Q_{eq} \approx 50\text{–}200$ | — | W |
| 9 | Compressor displacement | $\dot{V} = \frac{Q_{evap}}{\eta_v \rho \Delta h}$ | $\eta_v = 0.65\text{–}0.85$ | m³/s |
| 10 | Condenser LMTD | $LMTD = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)}$ | — | K |
| 11 | Refrigerant charge | $m = \sum \rho_i V_i$ | $0.6\text{–}1.2$ | kg |
| 12 | TR to kW | $1 \text{ TR} = 3.517$ kW | — | — |
| 13 | Air density (35°C) | $\rho_{air} = 1.15$ | — | kg/m³ |
| 14 | $c_{p,air}$ | $1.005$ | — | kJ/kg·K |
| 15 | R-134a $h_{fg}$ | $173$ | — | kJ/kg |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall — Units Mixing:** Always convert TR ↔ kW, kJ ↔ J, and °C ↔ K carefully. Most marks are lost to **unit mismatches**, not formula errors. State units at every step.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Complete Cabin Cooling Load Calculation

**Problem Statement (KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern):**
> A 5-seater passenger car is to be air-conditioned for tropical Indian conditions. The cabin has the following design data:
> - Total wall area (excluding glass): $A_w = 6.5 \text{ m}^2$, $U_w = 5.0 \text{ W/(m}^2\text{·K)}$
> - Glass area: $A_g = 2.0 \text{ m}^2$, $U_g = 5.8 \text{ W/(m}^2\text{·K)}$
> - Outside design temp: $T_{out} = 43 \text{ °C}$; Inside: $T_{in} = 25 \text{ °C}$
> - Solar intensity on glass: $I = 800 \text{ W/m}^2$, transmittance $\tau = 0.45$, interior absorption factor $f_a = 0.6$
> - Fresh air mass flow: $\dot{m}_{air} = 0.10 \text{ kg/s}$, $W_{out} = 0.018$, $W_{in} = 0.010$
> - Occupants: 5, $q_s = 70$ W, $q_L = 40$ W each
> - Engine bay heat: $Q_{eng} = 350$ W
> - Equipment heat: $Q_{eq} = 120$ W
> - $c_{p,air} = 1.005$ kJ/(kg·K), $h_{fg,water} = 2400$ kJ/kg
>
> **Determine:** (a) Total cooling load in kW and TR. (b) Recommended compressor displacement if $\eta_v = 0.75$, suction density $\rho_s = 5.0$ kg/m³, and enthalpy drop across evaporator $\Delta h_{evap} = 200$ kJ/kg.

### 3.1.1 Step 1 — Wall Conduction Load

$$\Delta T = T_{out} - T_{in} = 43 - 25 = 18 \text{ K}$$

$$Q_{wall} = U_w \cdot A_w \cdot \Delta T = 5.0 \times 6.5 \times 18$$

$$Q_{wall} = 585 \text{ W}$$

### 3.1.2 Step 2 — Glass Conduction Load
