$$Q_{glass,cond} = U_g \cdot A_g \cdot \Delta T = 5.8 \times 2.0 \times 18 = 208.8 \text{ W}$$

### 3.1.3 Step 3 — Solar Gain Through Glass (Direct Transmission)

$$Q_{solar,direct} = \tau \cdot I \cdot A_g = 0.45 \times 800 \times 2.0 = 720 \text{ W}$$

### 3.1.4 Step 4 — Internal Solar Re-radiation (from absorbed sun on interior surfaces)

$$Q_{solar,internal} = \tau \cdot I \cdot A_g \cdot f_a = 720 \times 0.6 = 432 \text{ W}$$

### 3.1.5 Step 5 — Ventilation Sensible Load

$$Q_{vent,s} = \dot{m}_{air} \cdot c_{p,air} \cdot \Delta T = 0.10 \times 1.005 \times 18$$

$$Q_{vent,s} = 1.809 \text{ kW} = 1809 \text{ W}$$

### 3.1.6 Step 6 — Ventilation Latent Load

$$Q_{vent,L} = \dot{m}_{air} \cdot (W_{out} - W_{in}) \cdot h_{fg,water} = 0.10 \times (0.018 - 0.010) \times 2400$$

$$Q_{vent,L} = 0.10 \times 0.008 \times 2400 = 1.92 \text{ kW} = 1920 \text{ W}$$

### 3.1.7 Step 7 — Occupant Load

$$Q_{occ} = 5 \times (70 + 40) = 5 \times 110 = 550 \text{ W}$$

### 3.1.8 Step 8 — Engine Bay + Equipment

$$Q_{eng+eq} = 350 + 120 = 470 \text{ W}$$

### 3.1.9 Step 9 — Total Cooling Load

$$Q_{total} = Q_{wall} + Q_{glass,cond} + Q_{solar,direct} + Q_{solar,internal} + Q_{vent,s} + Q_{vent,L} + Q_{occ} + Q_{eng+eq}$$

$$Q_{total} = 585 + 208.8 + 720 + 432 + 1809 + 1920 + 550 + 470$$

$$Q_{total} = 6694.8 \text{ W} \approx 6.695 \text{ kW}$$

Converting to **Tons of Refrigeration (TR)**:

$$Q_{total} = \frac{6.695}{3.517} = 1.903 \text{ TR} \approx 1.9 \text{ TR}$$

### 3.1.10 Step 10 — Compressor Displacement (Part b)

$$\dot{V}_{comp} = \frac{Q_{evap}}{\eta_v \cdot \rho_s \cdot \Delta h_{evap}}$$

$$\dot{V}_{comp} = \frac{6.695 \text{ kW}}{0.75 \times 5.0 \text{ kg/m}^3 \times 200 \text{ kJ/kg}}$$

$$\dot{V}_{comp} = \frac{6.695}{0.75 \times 5.0 \times 200} = \frac{6.695}{750} = 8.93 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

Converting to **L/s**: $\dot{V}_{comp} = 8.93 \text{ L/s}$ or **$32.15 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr}$**.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Sensitivity & Design Margins

| Design Parameter | Increase Effect on $Q_{total}$ | KTU Design Rule of Thumb |
|---|---|---|
| Outdoor $T_{out}$ (per +1 K) | + (U·A) ≈ +30 W/K (walls) + 12 W/K (glass) | Use 43 °C, not 40 °C |
| Glass area $A_g$ | Solar grows linearly | Limit with $A_g/A_w \leq 0.30$ |
| Solar $I$ (per +100 W/m²) | + $A_g \times 0.45 \times 0.10 = 9$ W | Use 800 W/m² (not 1000) for east-west |
| Occupants (per +1) | +110 W | Design for full seating |
| Insulation | Reduces U | Add 25 mm foam → $U$ halves |
| Window tinting | Reduces $\tau$ | Tint film → $\tau$ halves |

## 3.3 Derivation — LMTD for Condenser Sizing

Given hot refrigerant enters condenser at $T_{h,in}$ and leaves at $T_{h,out}$, while air enters at $T_{c,in}$ and leaves at $T_{c,out}$:

$$\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out}, \quad \Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in}$$

$$\text{LMTD} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)}$$

The required face area follows from:

$$Q_{cond} = U_o \cdot A_{cond} \cdot \text{LMTD}$$

$$A_{cond} = \frac{Q_{cond}}{U_o \cdot \text{LMTD}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Counter-flow assumed** in the LMTD formula above. If the condenser is a **cross-flow** unit, apply the appropriate LMTD correction factor $F$ (typically 0.85–0.95 for automotive serpentine condensers).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Sizing Methodology Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["START: Vehicle Type and Design Conditions"]
    nodeB["Define T_out, T_in, RH, Solar I, Occupancy"]
    nodeC["Compute Q_wall = U A dT"]
    nodeD["Compute Q_glass = U_g A_g dT + tau I A_g"]
    nodeE["Compute Q_vent_s and Q_vent_L"]
    nodeF["Compute Q_occ = n qs plus n qL"]
    nodeG["Compute Q_engine and Q_equipment"]
    nodeH["Sum Q_total = 8 pathways"]
    nodeI["Convert to TR"]
    nodeJ["Select Compressor: Vdot = Q over eta_v rho dH"]
    nodeK["Select Condenser: A_c = Q_cond over U_o LMTD"]
    nodeL["Select Evaporator: A_e from airflow and Bypass factor"]
    nodeM["Select Expansion Valve: d_orif from m_dot"]
    nodeN["Compute Refrigerant Charge m = sum rho V"]
    nodeO["Check at Idle RPM: 60 percent of design cooling"]
    nodeP["END: Final Bill of Materials and Performance"]

    nodeA --> nodeB --> nodeC --> nodeH
    nodeB --> nodeD --> nodeH
    nodeB --> nodeE --> nodeH
    nodeB --> nodeF --> nodeH
    nodeB --> nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI --> nodeJ --> nodeK --> nodeL --> nodeM --> nodeN --> nodeO --> nodeP
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Heat Gain Pathways into the Cabin

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1["Sun I = 800 W per m2"] --> node2["Glass Transmission"]
    node2 --> node3["Cabin Interior"]
    node1 --> node4["Glass Absorption"]
    node4 --> node5["Re-radiation to Cabin"]
    node3 --> nodeH["Total Q_total"]
    node5 --> nodeH
    node6["Outside Air T_out = 43 C"] --> node7["Walls and Roof Conduction"]
    node6 --> node8["Glass Conduction"]
    node7 --> nodeH
    node8 --> nodeH
    node9["Engine Bay T = 90 C"] --> node10["Firewall Conduction"]
    node10 --> nodeH
    node11["Fresh Air Inlet m_dot"] --> node12["Ventilation Sensible plus Latent"]
    node12 --> nodeH
    node13["Passengers 5 adults"] --> node14["Sensible 70 W plus Latent 40 W per person"]
    node14 --> nodeH
    node15["Electrical Loads"] --> node16["Lights plus Infotainment"]
    node16 --> nodeH
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Component Sizing Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeX["Required Q_total from Load Calc"] --> nodeY["Compressor Sizing: Displacement Vdot_comp"]
    nodeY --> nodeZ["Condenser Sizing: A_cond and Airflow"]
    nodeY --> nodeAA["Evaporator Sizing: A_evap and Bypass Factor"]
    nodeY --> nodeAB["Expansion Device: d_orifice"]
    nodeZ --> nodeAC["Refrigerant Charge: Sum rho V"]
    nodeAA --> nodeAC
    nodeAB --> nodeAC
    nodeAC --> nodeAD["Validation: Idle RPM Performance"]
    nodeAD --> nodeAE["Final Component Selection"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Each box represents a sizing step. Arrows show the dependency — for example, you cannot size the condenser without first knowing $Q_{total}$ and the compressor's discharge enthalpy.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern]**
> **Q:** List any **six (6) sources of heat gain** in an automotive air-conditioned cabin.

**Model Answer:**

The six (6) sources of heat gain in an automobile A/C cabin are:

1. **Conduction through walls, roof, and floor** of the vehicle body — $Q = U A \Delta T$
2. **Conduction and solar radiation through glass** (windshield, side, rear) — $Q = U_g A_g \Delta T + \tau I A_g$
3. **Ventilation / infiltration** of outside air (both sensible and latent) — $Q = \dot{m} c_p \Delta T + \dot{m} \Delta W h_{fg}$
4. **Heat from occupants** (passengers' body heat — sensible ~70 W + latent ~40 W per person)
5. **Heat conduction from the engine bay** through the firewall and HVAC ducts (200–500 W)
6. **Heat from internal electrical equipment** (lights, infotainment, instrument cluster — 50–200 W)

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic — "WGS VOE"**: **W**alls, **G**lass, **S**olar re-radiation, **V**entilation, **O**ccupants, **E**ngine+Equipment.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Pattern]**
> **Q:** What is the difference between **1 TR** and **1 kW** of cooling? Why is the TR unit commonly used in A/C sizing?

**Model Answer:**

- **1 Ton of Refrigeration (1 TR)** is defined as the rate of heat removal required to freeze **1 ton (2000 lb = 907.185 kg) of water at 0 °C into ice at 0 °C in 24 hours**.
- Numerically: **1 TR = 3.517 kW** (or 12,000 BTU/hr).
- **Why TR is preferred in A/C sizing:**
  - Matches the practical range of cooling capacities for vehicles and buildings (small fractions to tens of TR).
  - Historically tied to the ice-making industry, providing intuitive scaling.
  - 1 TR corresponds roughly to the latent heat extracted from ~907 kg of water — an easily relatable physical anchor.
- For an automobile, a typical A/C system is rated **1.5 to 3 TR** (~5–10 kW).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) — Compressor & Load Calculation

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question 2024]**

> **(a)** Derive the expression for the **total cooling load** of a passenger car cabin, listing and explaining **all heat gain pathways** with their respective formulae. (7 Marks)
>
> **(b)** A sedan has a total wall area (excluding glass) of 7.0 m² with $U = 5.0$ W/(m²·K), and glass area of 2.5 m² with $U_g = 5.8$ W/(m²·K) and transmittance $\tau = 0.5$. Design temperatures are $T_{out} = 45$ °C and $T_{in} = 24$ °C. Solar intensity is 800 W/m². Fresh air mass flow is 0.12 kg/s with $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/(kg·K) and humidity ratio difference $\Delta W = 0.008$. Occupant load is 5 × 100 W. Engine + equipment load = 600 W. Calculate the **total cooling load in kW and TR**. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 Marks

The total cooling load is the **sum of eight (8) heat gain pathways** in a vehicle cabin:

**1. Conduction through opaque walls, roof, floor (2 marks):**

$$Q_1 = U_w \cdot A_w \cdot (T_{out} - T_{in})$$

This is Fourier's law applied to the composite vehicle body panels.

**2. Conduction through glass (1 mark):**

$$Q_2 = U_g \cdot A_g \cdot (T_{out} - T_{in})$$

Glass has a higher $U$-value than insulated walls, so it dominates conduction losses.

**3. Direct solar transmission through glass (1 mark):**

$$Q_3 = \tau \cdot I \cdot A_g$$

This is the instantaneous solar load — typically the **largest single term** in the daytime load.

**4. Internal solar re-radiation (0.5 mark):**

$$Q_4 = \tau \cdot I \cdot A_g \cdot f_a$$

Solar energy absorbed by interior surfaces (seats, dash) is re-radiated as long-wave IR, which the glass does not transmit back out — this is the **greenhouse effect** in vehicles.

**5. Ventilation — sensible (0.5 mark):**

$$Q_5 = \dot{m}_{air} \cdot c_{p,air} \cdot (T_{out} - T_{in})$$

Fresh air brought in for breathing must be cooled to cabin temperature.

**6. Ventilation — latent (0.5 mark):**

$$Q_6 = \dot{m}_{air} \cdot (W_{out} - W_{in}) \cdot h_{fg,water}$$

Moisture in incoming air must be condensed.

**7. Occupant metabolic load (0.5 mark):**

$$Q_7 = n_{occ} \cdot (q_s + q_L)$$

Each person emits ~60–70 W sensible + 30–55 W latent metabolic heat.

**8. Engine bay + equipment (1 mark):**

$$Q_8 = Q_{engine} + Q_{equipment}$$

Conducted through firewall (engine) and dissipated by electrical accessories.

**Total:**

$$\boxed{Q_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{8} Q_i}$$

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 Marks

Given: $A_w = 7.0$ m², $U_w = 5.0$ W/(m²·K), $A_g = 2.5$ m², $U_g = 5.8$ W/(m²·K), $\tau = 0.5$, $T_{out} = 45$ °C, $T_{in} = 24$ °C, $I = 800$ W/m², $\dot{m} = 0.12$ kg/s, $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/(kg·K), $\Delta W = 0.008$, $h_{fg} = 2400$ kJ/kg, $Q_{occ} = 500$ W, $Q_{eng+eq} = 600$ W.

**Step 1:** $\Delta T = 45 - 24 = 21$ K

**Step 2:** $Q_1 = 5.0 \times 7.0 \times 21 = 735$ W = **0.735 kW**

**Step 3:** $Q_2 = 5.8 \times 2.5 \times 21 = 304.5$ W = **0.305 kW**

**Step 4:** $Q_3 = 0.5 \times 800 \times 2.5 = 1000$ W = **1.000 kW**

**Step 5:** $Q_4$ (re-radiation) = assume $f_a = 0.6$ → $Q_4 = 1000 \times 0.6 = 600$ W = **0.600 kW** *(if not given, omit)*

**Step 6:** $Q_5 = 0.12 \times 1.005 \times 21 = 2.533$ kW = **2533 W**

**Step 7:** $Q_6 = 0.12 \times 0.008 \times 2400 = 2.304$ kW = **2304 W**

**Step 8:** $Q_7 = 500$ W = **0.500 kW**

**Step 9:** $Q_8 = 600$ W = **0.600 kW**

**Total:**

$$Q_{total} = 0.735 + 0.305 + 1.000 + 0.600 + 2.533 + 2.304 + 0.500 + 0.600 = 8.577 \text{ kW}$$

$$\boxed{Q_{total} \approx 8.58 \text{ kW} = \frac{8.58}{3.517} \approx 2.44 \text{ TR}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Component Sizing Alternative

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question 2024 — Alternative Choice]**

> **(a)** Explain the **step-by-step methodology** for sizing a **compressor** for an automotive A/C system. List the formulae and discuss the role of the **pulley drive ratio** in ensuring adequate cooling at engine idle. (7 Marks)
>
> **(b)** A vehicle A/C system has a total cooling load of 7.5 kW. The compressor has $\eta_v = 0.78$, suction density $\rho_s = 4.8$ kg/m³, and evaporator enthalpy drop $\Delta h = 195$ kJ/kg. The engine idle speed is 700 rpm and the maximum speed is 6000 rpm. The compressor must deliver at least 60% of design capacity at idle. Determine the **compressor swept displacement per revolution** and the **maximum pulley drive ratio** between engine and compressor. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Establish design cooling load (1 mark):**
The total cooling load $Q_{total}$ (in kW or TR) is calculated from the cabin heat balance, as derived in Q(A).

**Step 2 — Convert to mass flow of refrigerant (1 mark):**

$$\dot{m}_{ref} = \frac{Q_{evap}}{\Delta h_{evap}}$$

This gives the refrigerant mass flow rate required.

**Step 3 — Determine compressor displacement volume (2 marks):**

$$\dot{V}_{comp} = \frac{\dot{m}_{ref}}{\eta_v \cdot \rho_{suction}}$$

The **volumetric efficiency** $\eta_v$ (typically 0.65–0.85) accounts for re-expansion, leakage, and pressure-ratio effects.

**Step 4 — Relate to engine RPM via pulley ratio (1 mark):**

$$N_{comp} = N_{engine} \cdot R_{pulley}$$

Where $R_{pulley} = D_{crank-pulley} / D_{comp-pulley}$ is the drive ratio.

**Step 5 — Compute swept volume per revolution (1 mark):**

$$V_{swept/rev} = \frac{\dot{V}_{comp}}{N_{comp}}$$

**Step 6 — Validate idle performance (1 mark):**
The compressor must provide ≥ 60% of $Q_{design}$ at engine idle. This constrains the pulley ratio. If idle capacity is too low, the pulley ratio must be increased (or a larger compressor selected).

> **Key insight:** A higher pulley ratio gives more cooling at idle but limits the compressor's maximum RPM, which could **over-speed** the compressor at high engine speeds and cause bearing damage.

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** $Q_{total} = 7.5$ kW, $\eta_v = 0.78$, $\rho_s = 4.8$ kg/m³, $\Delta h = 195$ kJ/kg, $N_{idle} = 700$ rpm, $N_{max} = 6000$ rpm.

**Step 1: Compute refrigerant mass flow rate (1 mark):**

$$\dot{m}_{ref} = \frac{Q_{evap}}{\Delta h} = \frac{7.5}{195} = 0.03846 \text{ kg/s} = 38.46 \text{ g/s}$$

**Step 2: Compute compressor volumetric flow (1 mark):**

$$\dot{V}_{comp} = \frac{\dot{m}_{ref}}{\eta_v \cdot \rho_s} = \frac{0.03846}{0.78 \times 4.8} = 0.01027 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 3: Determine minimum compressor RPM to meet design load (1 mark):**

At design conditions, the compressor operates at some speed $N_{des}$ producing $\dot{V}_{comp}$. Typically, the design point is taken at $N_{des} = 3000$ rpm (mid-range). So:

$$V_{swept/rev} = \frac{\dot{V}_{comp}}{N_{des}/60} = \frac{0.01027}{50} = 2.054 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{rev} = 205.4 \text{ cc/rev}$$

**Step 4: Compute the required compressor RPM at idle (1.5 marks):**

We need at least 60% of $Q_{design}$ at idle, so required $\dot{V}_{idle} = 0.6 \times 0.01027 = 0.006162$ m³/s.

$$N_{comp,idle} = \frac{\dot{V}_{idle}}{V_{swept/rev}} = \frac{0.006162}{2.054 \times 10^{-4}} = 30 \text{ rev/s} = 1800 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 5: Compute the maximum allowable pulley ratio (1 mark):**

At engine idle (700 rpm), compressor must run at ≥ 1800 rpm:

$$R_{pulley,min} = \frac{1800}{700} = 2.57$$

**Step 6: Check maximum speed limit (1.5 marks):**
At engine max (6000 rpm), the compressor would run at $6000 \times 2.57 = 15,420$ rpm. This is **too high** for most automotive compressors (max safe ~9000 rpm).

**Therefore:** The pulley ratio must be reduced so compressor max RPM ≤ 9000:
$$R_{pulley,max} = \frac{9000}{6000} = 1.5$$

**Final answer:**
- $\boxed{V_{swept} = 205 \text{ cc/rev}}$
- $\boxed{R_{pulley} \in [1.5, \, 2.57]}$ → typical choice $R_{pulley} = 1.5$ for safe max-speed operation, accepting that idle capacity will be ~35% of design. To meet the 60% idle rule, a larger compressor (e.g., 290 cc) is needed.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Confusing $\dot{V}_{comp}$ (volumetric flow rate) with $V_{swept}$ (displacement per revolution). They differ by a factor of RPM.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting to convert $\Delta h$ from kJ/kg to J/kg when $Q$ is in watts.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Not checking the **max-RPM constraint** when sizing the pulley. A valid idle solution may produce over-speeding at redline.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Treating $Q_{vent,s}$ and $Q_{vent,L}$ as a single term. They must be added separately.
> - **Common Mistake 5:** Using 1 TR = 3.5169 kW inconsistently (some texts round to 3.5 or 3.52). For KTU, use **3.517**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Eight (8) heat gain pathways** must be summed: walls, glass conduction, solar direct, solar re-radiation, ventilation sensible, ventilation latent, occupants, engine + equipment.
- [x] **1 TR = 3.517 kW**; always state units clearly.
- [x] **Design outdoor conditions for India: $T_{out} = 43$–$45$ °C**, RH ≈ 50–60%, $I = 800$ W/m² (peak tropical noon).
- [x] **Design indoor conditions: $T_{in} = 22$–$25$ °C**, RH ≈ 50%.
- [x] **Typical car A/C capacity: 1.5 to 3 TR** (5–10 kW).
- [x] **Compressor sizing formula:** $\dot{V}_{comp} = \frac{Q_{evap}}{\eta_v \cdot \rho_s \cdot \Delta h_{evap}}$.
- [x] **Pulley drive ratio** must satisfy BOTH: ≥ 60% design cooling at idle AND ≤ compressor max safe RPM at redline.
- [x] **Condenser LMTD method** uses counter-flow assumption; cross-flow needs correction factor $F \approx 0.85$–$0.95$.
- [x] **Refrigerant charge** = sum of (density × volume) over all components — must account for liquid vs. vapor regions.
- [x] **Solar load is the dominant** daytime contributor in tropical climates — tinted glass and IR-reflective films reduce $\tau$ significantly.
- [x] **Occupant load is non-trivial**: 5 occupants contribute ~550 W (~5–10% of total load).
- [x] **Engine bay heat** is often under-estimated; design firewalls with ceramic-coated insulation for 200–500 W rejection.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision Mantra:**
> *"Q total = Eight paths. Compressor = Q over (eta V rho dH). 1 TR = 3.517 kW. Pulley ratio satisfies BOTH idle AND redline."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Supply and Return Ducts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAUT523 - AUTOMOTIVE REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Supply and Return Ducts # Supply and Return Ducts – Fundamentals & System Overview > [!NOTE] > **Module 3 Focus:** This topic bridges the gap between the evaporator unit (cooling source) and the passenger cabin. In an automobile, the **HVAC plenum** houses both the blower, evaporator, heater core, and the **ducting system** that finally delivers conditioned air to the occupant. --- ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Supply Duct** is a conduit (rigid, semi-rigid, or flexible) that transports **conditioned air** — i.e., air that has been cooled, dehumidified, and (in mixed-temperature mode) reheated — from the outlet of the HVAC housing (evaporator/heater core assembly) to the various discharge outlets located inside the passenger compartment (dashboard vents, floor outlets, windshield defrost nozzles, side window demisters, and rear-seat ducts). A **Return Duct** (also called the **recirculation duct**) is the conduit through which **warm cabin air** is drawn back into the evaporator inlet of the HVAC unit. It establishes the closed-loop airflow path necessary for: - Continuous re-cooling of cabin air. - Filtration (the return duct always terminates at or before the blower/evaporator inlet filter). - Mixing with a controlled proportion of fresh **outside air** (RAM air) via the fresh-air intake door. In automotive applications, these ducts are typically integrated into a single **plenum chamber** (also called the HVAC box) moulded from polypropylene (PP) or ABS plastic. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bloodstream of the Cabin" Think of the human cardiovascular system: - The **Supply Ducts** are the **arteries** — they carry fresh, cooled, oxygen-rich "blood" (conditioned air) outward from the heart (HVAC unit) to every organ (driver, passenger, defrost zone, floor). - The **Return Ducts** are the **veins** — they collect the used, warm, CO₂-rich "blood" (warm cabin air) and return it to the heart to be re-purified. - The **Blower Fan** is the **heart** that pumps the air. - Just as arteries branch out (aorta → arterioles → capillaries), supply ducts **branch and taper** to deliver the right volume of air to each vent. - The **return duct** is simpler — usually one large path, because air naturally returns to the lowest-pressure point (the blower suction). If the "arteries" are too narrow, the passenger feels **insufficient cooling** (poor supply); if the "veins" are blocked, the blower **sucks a vacuum** and cooling becomes noisy/inefficient (poor return). --- ## 1.3 Purpose and Functions — Quick Summary Box > [!IMPORTANT] > **Primary Functions of Supply Ducts** > 1. Deliver cooled (or heated) air to driver, front passenger, and rear occupants. > 2. Distribute air to windshield for **defrosting** and side windows for **demisting**. > 3. Direct air to the **floor** for heating mode (foot-well comfort). > 4. Maintain uniform **temperature distribution** throughout the cabin. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Primary Functions of Return Ducts** > 1. Collect warm air from the cabin and return it to the evaporator inlet. > 2. House the **cabin air filter** (particulate/charcoal filter). > 3. Provide the suction port for the **blower motor**. > 4. Enable **recirculation mode** (closing the fresh-air flap isolates the cabin from outside). --- ## 1.4 Standard Performance Metrics (KTU Board-Relevant) | Parameter | Typical Automotive Value | |---|---| | Supply air velocity at vent | **2.5 – 5.0 m/s** (face level) | | Supply duct velocity (inside duct) | **3.0 – 7.0 m/s** | | Return duct velocity | **2.0 – 4.0 m/s** (low velocity to minimise noise) | | Static pressure in supply plenum | **50 – 250 Pa** (gauge) | | Total cabin airflow | **150 – 250 CFM** (≈ 250 – 425 m³/h) | | Duct material | **Polypropylene (PP)**, **ABS**, **EPP foam** | | Standard noise target | **< 40 dB(A)** at the driver's ear | --- # Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Duct Classification ### 2.1.1 By Geometry 1. **Rectangular ducts** — most common in dashboards because they fit into flat cavities (e.g., behind the glove box, along the centre console). 2. **Circular ducts** — used for rear-seat extension lines and flexible hoses connecting the HVAC box to the rear AC unit. 3. **Oval / flat-oval ducts** — a compromise; gives the cross-sectional area of a circle in a flat package. ### 2.1.2 By Construction 1. **Rigid ducts** — injection-moulded PP or ABS. Used in the main plenum and high-volume paths. 2. **Semi-rigid ducts** — corrugated EPP (Expanded Polypropylene) foam. Excellent sound absorption. 3. **Flexible ducts** — spiral-wound aluminium or polyester with PVC coating. Used in the engine bay to isolate vibration. --- ## 2.2 Why "Equivalent Diameter" Matters A duct carrying a given volumetric flow rate $Q$ at velocity $V$ requires a cross-section: $$A = \frac{Q}{V}$$ But duct design tables (and friction-loss charts) are derived for **circular** ducts. To use them for rectangular ducts, we convert the rectangle $a \times b$ into an **equivalent circular diameter** $D_e$ that has: - the **same cross-sectional area**, OR - the **same hydraulic diameter**, OR (most commonly used) - the **same carrying capacity (friction loss)** at the same air quantity. The **equal-friction equal-capacity** equivalent diameter for a rectangular duct of sides $a$ and $b$: $$D_e = 1.30 \, \frac{(a \cdot b)^{0.625}}{(a + b)^{0.25}}$$ Where: - $D_e$ = equivalent circular diameter in **metres** - $a, b$ = sides of the rectangular duct in **metres** > [!NOTE] > **Hydraulic diameter** (used for non-circular pressure-drop calculations): > $$D_h = \frac{4A}{P} = \frac{2ab}{a+b}$$ --- ## 2.3 Core Governing Equations ### 2.3.1 Continuity (Mass Conservation) For an incompressible fluid (air at low Mach): $$Q = A \cdot V \quad \text{[m}^3\text{/s]}$$ ### 2.3.2 Darcy–Weisbach Friction Pressure Drop The friction loss in a straight duct run of length $L$ carrying air at density $\rho$: $$\Delta P_f = f \cdot \frac{L}{D_e} \cdot \frac{\rho V^2}{2}$$ Where: - $f$ = Darcy friction factor (for turbulent flow in smooth ducts ≈ 0.018 – 0.025) - $\rho$ = air density ≈ **1.20 kg/m³** at 20 °C, 1 atm - $V$ = mean air velocity [m/s] ### 2.3.3 Reynolds Number (to confirm flow regime) $$Re = \frac{V \cdot D_e}{\nu}$$ Where $\nu$ = kinematic viscosity of air ≈ **1.5 × 10⁻⁵ m²/s**. Turbulent flow is typically $Re > 4000$. ### 2.3.4 Dynamic Pressure (Velocity Pressure) $$P_v = \frac{\rho V^2}{2} \quad \text{[Pa]}$$ ### 2.3.5 Static Pressure Regain (Diffuser Effect) When air **decelerates** (e.g., expanding into a larger duct or at the vent outlet), part of the kinetic energy converts back to static pressure: $$P_{regain} = \frac{\rho (V_1^2 - V_2^2)}{2}$$ --- ## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Board Exam Quick Reference) | # | Equation | Application | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $Q = A \cdot V$ | Volumetric flow | m³/s | | 2 | $D_e = 1.30 \dfrac{(ab)^{0.625}}{(a+b)^{0.25}}$ | Rectangular → circular | m | | 3 | $D_h = \dfrac{4A}{P} = \dfrac{2ab}{a+b}$ | Hydraulic diameter | m | | 4 | $Re = \dfrac{V D_e}{\nu}$ | Flow regime check | — | | 5 | $\Delta P_f = f \dfrac{L}{D_e} \dfrac{\rho V^2}{2}$ | Friction loss in straight run | Pa | | 6 | $P_v = \dfrac{\rho V^2}{2}$ | Velocity pressure | Pa | | 7 | $P_{regain} = \dfrac{\rho (V_1^2 - V_2^2)}{2}$ | Static pressure regain | Pa | | 8 | $\text{NC} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{W}{W_0}\right)$ | Sound power level (Noise Criterion) | dB | | 9 | $\dot{m} = \rho Q$ | Mass flow rate | kg/s | | 10 | $P_{fan} = \dfrac{\Delta P_{total} \cdot Q}{\eta_{fan}}$ | Blower power demand | W | --- ## 2.5 Engineering Utility — Why This Matters in Real Vehicles - **Cabin comfort uniformity:** Proper duct sizing ensures that the rear passenger receives nearly the same airflow as the driver — a common complaint in poorly designed cars is "rear-seat AC is weak." - **NVH (Noise, Vibration, Harshness):** Automotive OEMs target cabin noise below 35 dB(A). Ducts are the **#1 source of HVAC noise** if sized below 3 m/s velocity or with sharp bends. - **Condensate management:** Return ducts route humid air over the cold evaporator → condensation forms on the fins → the water drains via the plenum drain tube. Proper duct routing prevents **water carry-over** into the cabin. - **Filter accessibility:** The return duct always houses the cabin filter (pollen / activated-carbon) — its location must allow service replacement. - **Crash safety:** Ducts behind the dashboard must not become projectiles in a frontal impact — hence EPP foam is preferred for its energy-absorbing crumple behaviour. --- # Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Equivalent Diameter (Equal Friction Method) We seek a circular diameter $D_e$ such that, for the **same air quantity $Q$**, it produces the **same friction loss per unit length** as a rectangular duct $a \times b$. ### Step 1 — Express Friction Loss per Unit Length For a duct of any shape: $$\frac{\Delta P_f}{L} = \frac{f}{D_h} \cdot \frac{\rho V^2}{2}$$ For a **circular** duct, $D_h = D$, and the velocity for a given $Q$ is: $$V_c = \frac{Q}{\pi D^2 / 4} = \frac{4Q}{\pi D^2}$$ For a **rectangular** duct, $D_h = \dfrac{2ab}{a+b}$, and: $$V_r = \frac{Q}{ab}$$ ### Step 2 — Equate the Two Friction Losses Setting $(\Delta P_f / L)_{circle} = (\Delta P_f / L)_{rect}$ and assuming the same $f$ (a justified assumption for fully rough turbulent flow in ducts of similar relative roughness): $$\frac{f}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho V_c^2}{2} = \frac{f}{D_h} \cdot \frac{\rho V_r^2}{2}$$ Cancelling $f$ and $\rho / 2$: $$\frac{V_c^2}{D} = \frac{V_r^2}{D_h}$$ ### Step 3 — Substitute $V_c$ and $V_r$ $$\frac{1}{D}\left(\frac{4Q}{\pi D^2}\right)^2 = \frac{1}{D_h}\left(\frac{Q}{ab}\right)^2$$ $$\frac{16 Q^2}{\pi^2 D^5} = \frac{Q^2}{D_h \cdot a^2 b^2}$$ Cancel $Q^2$ and rearrange: $$D^5 = \frac{16 \, D_h \, a^2 b^2}{\pi^2}$$ $$D = \left[\frac{16 \, D_h \, a^2 b^2}{\pi^2}\right]^{1/5}$$ ### Step 4 — Substitute $D_h = 2ab / (a+b)$ $$D = \left[\frac{16}{\pi^2} \cdot \frac{2ab}{a+b} \cdot a^2 b^2\right]^{1/5} = \left[\frac{32 \, a^3 b^3}{\pi^2 (a+b)}\right]^{1/5}$$ ### Step 5 — Numerical Coefficient Evaluating the constant: $$\left(\frac{32}{\pi^2}\right)^{1/5} = \left(\frac{32}{9.8696}\right)^{1/5} = (3.242)^{0.2} \approx 1.265 \approx 1.30$$ The exact KTU/ASHRAE tabulated value uses the slightly higher coefficient **1.30** (it accounts for the small variation in $f$ with shape). Therefore, the working formula is: $$\boxed{D_e = 1.30 \, \frac{(a \cdot b)^{0.625}}{(a + b)^{0.25}}}$$ This derivation is a frequent 7-mark question in KTU Module 3 examinations. --- ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Duct Sizing for Dashboard Vent **Problem:** A dashboard centre vent must deliver **30 CFM** of supply air. The duct feeding it is rectangular, **40 mm wide × 25 mm deep**. Compute: 1. Mean air velocity inside the duct. 2. Equivalent diameter. 3. Reynolds number. 4. Friction loss over a 0.8 m straight run. **Given data:** - $Q = 30 \text{ CFM} = 30 \times \dfrac{0.0004719}{1} \text{ m}^3\text{/s} \approx 0.01416 \text{ m}^3\text{/s}$ - $a = 0.040 \text{ m}, \quad b = 0.025 \text{ m}$ - $L = 0.8 \text{ m}, \quad \rho = 1.20 \text{ kg/m}^3, \quad \nu = 1.5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2\text{/s}, \quad f = 0.02$ ### Step 1 — Velocity $$A = a \times b = 0.040 \times 0.025 = 1.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$ $$V = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{0.01416}{1.0 \times 10^{-3}} = 14.16 \text{ m/s}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Velocity is too high** — KTU recommendation is 3–7 m/s inside ducts. 14.16 m/s will cause **noise + high friction loss**. Either (i) reduce CFM, (ii) increase duct cross-section, or (iii) use multiple parallel ducts. ### Step 2 — Equivalent Diameter $$D_e = 1.30 \times \frac{(0.040 \times 0.025)^{0.625}}{(0.040 + 0.025)^{0.25}}$$ $$(ab) = 0.001 \text{ m}^2 \Rightarrow (ab)^{0.625} = 0.001^{0.625}$$ $$\ln(0.001) = -6.9078 \Rightarrow 0.625 \times (-6.9078) = -4.317$$ $$(ab)^{0.625} = e^{-4.317} = 0.01334$$ $$(a+b)^{0.25} = (0.065)^{0.25} = 0.5044$$ $$D_e = 1.30 \times \frac{0.01334}{0.5044} = 1.30 \times 0.02645 = 0.0344 \text{ m} \approx 34.4 \text{ mm}$$ ### Step 3 — Reynolds Number $$Re = \frac{V \cdot D_e}{\nu} = \frac{14.16 \times 0.0344}{1.5 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{0.4871}{1.5 \times 10^{-5}} = 3.247 \times 10^{4}$$ $$Re \approx 32{,}470 \quad \text{(fully turbulent)}$$ ### Step 4 — Friction Loss $$\Delta P_f = f \cdot \frac{L}{D_e} \cdot \frac{\rho V^2}{2}$$ $$\frac{\rho V^2}{2} = \frac{1.20 \times (14.16)^2}{2} = \frac{1.20 \times 200.5}{2} = 120.3 \text{ Pa}$$ $$\frac{L}{D_e} = \frac{0.8}{0.0344} = 23.26$$ $$\Delta P_f = 0.02 \times 23.26 \times 120.3 = 55.96 \text{ Pa}$$ **Conclusion:** The supply duct causes ~56 Pa of static pressure drop, in addition to grille losses (~10–20 Pa) and bend losses. The blower must therefore deliver at least **80–100 Pa** to this vent. This is a realistic automotive value — modern brushless blowers provide 150–300 Pa. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Duct Design Methods | Design Method | Principle | When Used in Automotive A/C | |---|---|---| | **Equal Friction** | Same $\Delta P/L$ in every duct section | Most common for trunk + branches | | **Static Regain** | Each branch recovers $P_v$ lost in previous section | Long rear-duct extensions | | **Velocity Reduction** | Reduce $V$ progressively from trunk to branch | Quieter cabin (OEM preference) | | **T-Method (Total Pressure)** | Maintain constant total pressure | Rare — used in high-rise HVAC, not vehicles | | **Balanced Design** | Trial-and-error with manual dampers | Aftermarket / custom vehicles | --- # Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 HVAC Duct System Block Diagram (Automotive) ```mermaid graph TD nodeA[Outside Air Intake
RAM air at cowl] --> nodeB[Fresh Air Flap
Mixes outside and recirculated air] nodeC[Cabin Air Return
Recirculation duct] --> nodeB nodeB --> nodeD[Blower Motor
Centrifugal fan] nodeD --> nodeE[Evaporator Core
Cooling and dehumidification] nodeE --> nodeF[Heater Core
Hot coolant side] nodeF --> nodeG[Temperature Blend Door
Mixes cold and hot air] nodeG --> nodeH[Mode Door Actuator
Selects vent floor or defrost] nodeH --> nodeI[Plenum Chamber
Main supply manifold] nodeI --> nodeJ[Dashboard Centre Vent
30 to 40 CFM] nodeI --> nodeK[Side Window Demister Ducts
20 CFM each] nodeI --> nodeL[Floor Duct
60 to 80 CFM] nodeI --> nodeM[Windshield Defrost Ducts
40 to 60 CFM] nodeI --> nodeN[Rear Seat Extension Duct
40 CFM] nodeC -.-> nodeO[Cabin Air Filter
Pollen and carbon] nodeO -.-> nodeD nodeI --> nodeP[Condensate Drain Tube
Plenum to underbody] ``` ## 4.2 Pressure–Velocity Distribution Along a Typical Supply Duct ```mermaid graph LR stepA[HVAC Plenum
P_total = 250 Pa
V = 8 m per s] --> stepB[Straight Run 0.5 m
Delta P_f = 30 Pa
V = 8 m per s] stepB --> stepC[Bend 90 deg
Loss Coefficient C = 0.9
Loss = 0.9 times P_v] stepC --> stepD[Branch Tee
Split flow 60 percent to vent A
40 percent to vent B] stepD --> stepE[Centre Vent
P_static = 110 Pa
V_out = 4 m per s] stepD --> stepF[Floor Duct
P_static = 90 Pa
V_out = 5 m per s] ``` ## 4.3 Functional Architecture Flow — Air Path States ```mermaid graph TD mode1[Mode 1 MAX COOL] --> flowA[Blower HIGH speed] flowA --> flow2[Recirculation door CLOSED] flow2 --> flow3[Temperature door FULL COLD] flow3 --> flow4[Mode door to DASHBOARD VENTS only] mode5[Mode 2 DEFROST] --> flow6[Blower MEDIUM speed] flow6 --> flow7[Fresh air door OPEN] flow7 --> flow8[Temperature door FULL HOT] flow8 --> flow9[Mode door to WINDSHIELD only] mode10[Mode 3 BI-LEVEL] --> flow11[Blower MED speed] flow11 --> flow12[Fresh air partially open] flow12 --> flow13[Temp door MID position] flow13 --> flow14[Mode door split DASH plus FLOOR] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagrams:** In each mode the supply ducts to *defrost*, *dashboard*, and *floor* are opened or closed by the **mode door actuator**. The return duct always draws air from the cabin — only the **recirculation door** changes whether outside air joins it. --- # KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Differentiate between a supply duct and a return duct in an automotive HVAC system.** **Model Answer:** | Feature | Supply Duct | Return Duct | |---|---|---| | Air direction | From HVAC unit → cabin | From cabin → HVAC unit | | Air condition | Cooled / heated / mixed | Warm cabin air | | Temperature | 2 °C to 50 °C (varies with mode) | ~25 °C to 45 °C | | Velocity (typical) | 3–7 m/s | 2–4 m/s | | Function | Distribution to vents | Suction for blower | | Houses | Mode doors, blend door | Cabin air filter | **[3 Marks – awarded for 3 correct contrasting points]** --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **What is the significance of the "equivalent diameter" concept in rectangular duct design?** **Model Answer:** The equivalent diameter $D_e$ converts a rectangular duct into a hypothetical circular duct that produces the **same friction loss** for the **same air quantity**. This allows engineers to use the vast tabulated friction-loss data available only for circular ducts (Moody chart, ASHRAE tables). The standard formula is: $$D_e = 1.30 \, \frac{(a \cdot b)^{0.625}}{(a + b)^{0.25}}$$ Without this concept, every rectangular duct design would require expensive CFD or experimental calibration. **[3 Marks]** --- ## Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]` **(a)** Explain with neat sketches the **complete layout of supply and return ducts** in a typical sedan car HVAC system. List the materials used and justify the choice. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** A rectangular duct of cross-section **60 mm × 30 mm** supplies **45 CFM** of air to a dashboard vent. The duct run is **1.2 m long** with **two 90° bends** (loss coefficient $C = 0.9$ each). Compute the equivalent diameter, mean velocity, Reynolds number, and total static pressure loss (friction + bends). Take $f = 0.02$, $\rho = 1.20 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\nu = 1.5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2\text{/s}$. **[7 Marks]** --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Layout & Materials [7 Marks]** ``` [ASCII SKETCH] Outside Air ──┐ │ Fresh-air Flap Cabin Return ──┴── Cabin Filter ── Blower ── Evaporator ── │ ▼ Heater Core │ ▼ Temperature Blend Door │ ▼ Mode Door Actuator / | \ Defrost DASH FLOOR (Rear) Duct Vent Duct Duct (wind- (driver (foot) (rear shield) pass.) seat) ``` - **Supply side** branches into 4 ducts: defrost (windshield), dashboard (face), floor (foot), and rear-seat extension. - **Return side** is a single large duct located under the dashboard on the passenger side, terminating at the blower inlet with a **cabin air filter**. - **Materials:** - Main plenum → injection-moulded **Polypropylene (PP)** — lightweight, recyclable, low cost. - Dashboard ducts → **EPP foam** — sound absorption, crash energy absorption. - Rear extension → **flexible aluminium + PVC** — absorbs engine vibration. - Floor duct → **semi-rigid PP** — fits curved floor pan. **[Awarded 7 marks: 2 layout + 2 materials + 2 sketches/text + 1 justification]** --- **(b) Numerical Solution [7 Marks]** **Given:** $a = 0.060 \text{ m}, \, b = 0.030 \text{ m}, \, Q = 45 \text{ CFM} = 0.02124 \text{ m}^3\text{/s}, \, L = 1.2 \text{ m}$. **Step 1 — Equivalent Diameter** $$ab = 0.060 \times 0.030 = 1.8 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$ $$(ab)^{0.625} = (1.8 \times 10^{-3})^{0.625} = e^{0.625 \times \ln(1.8 \times 10^{-3})} = e^{-3.887} = 0.02051$$ $$(a+b)^{0.25} = (0.090)^{0.25} = 0.5493$$ $$D_e = 1.30 \times \frac{0.02051}{0.5493} = 1.30 \times 0.03734 = 0.04854 \text{ m} \approx 48.5 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 2 — Velocity** $$A = 0.060 \times 0.030 = 1.8 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$ $$V = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{0.02124}{1.8 \times 10^{-3}} = 11.80 \text{ m/s}$$ **Step 3 — Reynolds Number** $$Re = \frac{V D_e}{\nu} = \frac{11.80 \times 0.04854}{1.5 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{0.5728}{1.5 \times 10^{-5}} = 3.819 \times 10^4$$ $$Re \approx 38{,}190 \quad \text{(turbulent)}$$ **Step 4 — Friction Loss** $$\frac{\rho V^2}{2} = \frac{1.20 \times (11.80)^2}{2} = \frac{1.20 \times 139.24}{2} = 83.54 \text{ Pa}$$ $$\frac{L}{D_e} = \frac{1.2}{0.04854} = 24.72$$ $$\Delta P_{friction} = 0.02 \times 24.72 \times 83.54 = 41.30 \text{ Pa}$$ **Step 5 — Bend Losses (minor losses)** $$\Delta P_{bend} = 2 \times C \times \frac{\rho V^2}{2} = 2 \times 0.9 \times 83.54 = 150.37 \text{ Pa}$$ **Step 6 — Total Static Pressure Loss** $$\boxed{\Delta P_{total} = 41.30 + 150.37 = 191.67 \text{ Pa} \approx 192 \text{ Pa}}$$ **[Awarded 7 marks: 1 equivalent dia + 1 velocity + 1 Re + 2 friction + 2 bends + rounding]** --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **(a)** Discuss the **various materials** used for automotive supply and return ducts. Compare rigid PP, EPP foam, and flexible aluminium-PVC ducts in terms of weight, cost, NVH, and durability. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** Describe the **design considerations** for cabin air distribution — uniformity, noise, condensate management, and filter placement — with reference to the return duct layout. **[7 Marks]** --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Materials Comparison [7 Marks]** | Property | Rigid PP | EPP Foam | Flexible Alu-PVC | |---|---|---|---| | Density | 0.90 g/cm³ | 0.05–0.20 g/cm³ (very light) | 0.5–0.8 g/cm³ | | Weight (per moulded part) | Medium | **Lowest** | Medium | | Cost | Low | Medium | Medium-High | | NVH (noise absorption) | Poor (reflective) | **Excellent** | Fair | | Crash energy absorption | Brittle | **Excellent** | Poor | | Temperature resistance | Up to 100 °C | Up to 110 °C | Up to 80 °C | | Recyclability | High | High | Low | | Typical use | Plenum, main trunk | Dashboard ducts | Engine-bay transitions | **[3 marks for table + 2 marks for application mapping + 2 marks for trade-off reasoning]** --- **(b) Design Considerations [7 Marks]** 1. **Uniformity:** Use a **plenum chamber** at the HVAC outlet so that each vent sees the same static pressure. Larger vents are placed further away to balance friction losses. 2. **Noise (NVH):** Keep duct velocity **< 7 m/s**, use **gradual bends** (radius ≥ 1.5 × duct width), and line ducts with **EPP foam** for absorption. 3. **Condensate management:** The evaporator coil condenses moisture from return air. The plenum must slope toward a **drain tube** exiting below the firewall to prevent water carry-over into the supply side. 4. **Filter placement:** The cabin air filter is mounted at the **return duct inlet** (upstream of the blower) so that the blower draws air through the filter. This keeps the blower wheel clean and protects the evaporator fins. 5. **Serviceability:** The filter door must be accessible without tools — typically behind the glove box. 6. **Mode actuation:** A single **mode door actuator** (servo) rotates flaps that open/close ducts to defrost, dash, or floor positions. **[7 marks: 2 uniformity + 2 noise + 2 condensate + 1 filter]** --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common mistakes that cost marks in Module 3:** > 1. **Forgetting the $(a+b)^{0.25}$ denominator** in $D_e$ — students often write only $(ab)^{0.625}$. KTU strictly awards **zero** for missing the denominator. > 2. **Mixing up hydraulic diameter $D_h$ and equivalent diameter $D_e$** — $D_h$ is for non-circular Bernoulli/Moody use; $D_e$ is the equal-friction conversion. They are **not** equal. > 3. **Not converting CFM to m³/s** — board examiners instantly deduct **1 mark** if SI units are missing. > 4. **Omitting bend losses** in duct pressure drop — only straight-run friction gives **incomplete** answer. > 5. **Confusing supply with return** — the return duct is **always at lower pressure (suction side)**; never place a filter on the supply side. > 6. **Velocity limits:** Writing velocity > 10 m/s inside ducts is a red flag — it means the duct is undersized for that flow rate. --- ## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Supply duct** = conditioned air from HVAC box to vents (face, feet, defrost, rear). - **Return duct** = cabin air back to evaporator/blower; **always houses cabin filter**. - **Equivalent diameter** formula: $D_e = 1.30 \dfrac{(ab)^{0.625}}{(a+b)^{0.25}}$ — board-favourite equation. - **Standard air properties (memorise):** $\rho = 1.20 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\nu = 1.5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2\text{/s}$. - **Velocity limits:** 3–7 m/s inside ducts; 2.5–5 m/s at outlet vents. - **Friction loss equation:** $\Delta P_f = f \dfrac{L}{D_e} \dfrac{\rho V^2}{2}$. - **Bend loss:** $\Delta P = C \dfrac{\rho V^2}{2}$ with $C \approx 0.9$ for sharp 90°; $C \approx 0.3$ for long-radius bends. - **Total pressure loss** = friction loss + bend loss + grille/outlet loss + entrance/exit loss. - **Static pressure regain** happens when duct expands — useful in rear-duct design. - **Mode door actuator** controls which supply ducts (defrost/dash/floor) are active. - **Recirculation flap** controls whether return duct mixes with outside (fresh) air or is closed (recirculation mode for fast cooling). - **Condensate drain** must be located at the lowest point of the evaporator housing, NOT inside any supply duct. - **Materials:** PP for plenum, EPP for dash ducts, flexible Alu-PVC for engine-bay transitions. - **NVH rule of thumb:** if $V > 7 \text{ m/s}$, expect audible whistle → redesign duct. - **Reynolds number** must be checked — automotive ducts are always **turbulent** ($Re > 4000$).

topics.title: Types of Grills
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT523 - AUTOMOTIVE REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Types of Grills

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3 — TYPES OF GRILLS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Automotive Refrigeration and Air Conditioning (PEAUT523)**, a **Grill** (also spelled *grille* or *diffuser*) is defined as a **terminal air-distribution device** installed at the outlet of an HVAC duct, designed to deliver conditioned air into the vehicle cabin with a **controlled velocity, direction, and diffusion pattern**.

As per KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus terminology for Module 3 (Automotive HVAC Air Distribution and Ducting), grills are classified as the **final-stage delivery components** of an automotive HVAC plenum system, and they perform the critical function of converting **ducted laminar/streamlined flow** into a **low-velocity, well-mixed, occupant-friendly airflow**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 3):**
> A *grill* in automotive AC refers to the **outlet register** through which cooled, heated, dehumidified, or fresh air enters the passenger compartment. It governs the **throw**, **drop**, and **spread** of conditioned air, while also providing **adjustability** to suit occupant comfort.

### 1.2 Key Design Parameters of a Grill

A grill is not merely an opening — it is an engineered component characterized by the following quantifiable parameters:

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Unit | Physical Meaning |
|-----------|--------|---------------|------------------|
| **Effective Velocity** | $V_{eff}$ | **m/s** | Air speed measured at the grill face (typically **0.25 to 2.5 m/s** in cabin) |
| **Throw Distance** | $T$ | **m** | Horizontal distance the airstream travels before velocity drops to **0.25 m/s** (terminal velocity) |
| **Drop** | $D$ | **m** | Vertical fall of the airstream due to Coanda effect & buoyancy |
| **Spread** | $S$ | **m** | Lateral (sideways) diffusion width of the airstream |
| **Pressure Drop** | $\Delta P$ | **Pa** (or mm H₂O) | Static pressure loss across the grill face |
| **Discharge Coefficient** | $C_d$ | dimensionless | Ratio of actual to ideal mass flow (typically **0.6 – 0.8** for automotive grills) |
| **Noise Criterion** | NC | dB | Acoustic comfort limit (**NC 30 – 40** is typical cabin target) |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy:**
> Think of a grill like the **showerhead in your bathroom**. A showerhead takes high-pressure water from a pipe and breaks it into gentle, well-distributed droplets that fall comfortably on you. Similarly, an automotive AC grill takes high-velocity air from the evaporator/duct and spreads it gently across the cabin so passengers feel a soft breeze — not a blast of cold wind in the face.
>
> Now imagine if there were *no showerhead* — water would shoot out in a painful jet. That's exactly what happens in a car without proper grills: passengers get **drafts, cold spots, noise, and dust streaks** on the windshield.

### 1.4 Functional Role of Grills in the Automotive HVAC Loop

Grills serve **four essential engineering functions** simultaneously:

1. **Air Distribution** — Deliver conditioned air at the correct velocity and direction.
2. **Flow Regulation** — Allow occupants to **open, close, or redirect** the airflow (manual adjustability).
3. **Aesthetic Integration** — Blend with the dashboard/instrument-panel styling.
4. **Acoustic Dampening** — Reduce **HVAC noise** entering the cabin (target NC ≤ 35).

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & Classification Framework

### 2.1 Primary Classification Axes for Automotive Grills

Automotive AC grills (KTU Module 3 classification) can be categorized along **four orthogonal design axes**:

#### Axis A — Based on Function in HVAC Circuit

1. **Supply / Delivery Grills (SG)**
   - Push conditioned air *into* the cabin.
   - Located on dashboard, roof console, rear seat, B-pillar.
   - Must produce **non-uniform velocity profile** with rapid *spread* and *drop* for occupant comfort.

2. **Return / Recirculation Grills (RG)**
   - Pull cabin air *back* to the evaporator/blower for reconditioning.
   - Typically located near the **floor** or **base of dashboard**.
   - Operate at higher face velocity (**1.0 – 2.0 m/s**) to overcome return-duct resistance.

3. **Defrost Grills (DG)**
   - Direct warm air onto the **inner windshield surface** to remove frost/ice.
   - Located along the **upper edge of the dashboard**, aimed at the base of the windshield.
   - Produce a high-velocity, narrow-throw **sheet flow** that follows the curved glass.

4. **Demist / Ventilation Grills**
   - Low-velocity grills aimed at the **side windows** to prevent condensation.
   - Often combined with defrost grills in modern vehicles.

#### Axis B — Based on Directional Adjustability

| Type | Adjustability | Typical Use |
|------|---------------|-------------|
| **Fixed Grills** | None — fixed louver angle | Rear seat ducts, floor outlets |
| **Adjustable Louver Grills** | Manually rotatable slats (horizontal/vertical) | Dashboard center vents |
| **Rotating / Swivel Grills** | Pivoting spherical or cylindrical core | Side vents, premium cabins |
| **Motorized Grills** | Actuator-driven louvers | Luxury/electric vehicles |

#### Axis C — Based on Physical Geometry

| Geometry | Description | Airflow Character |
|----------|-------------|-------------------|
| **Linear Slot** | Long, narrow rectangular opening | High spread, low drop — ideal for windshield |
| **Louvered (Slat)** | Multiple parallel angled blades | Moderate spread, directional control |
| **Round / Circular** | Concentric ring or perforated disc | Radial diffusion — soft, omnidirectional |
| **Rectangular Perforated** | Flat plate with multiple holes | Low throw, excellent noise reduction |
| **Eye-Ball (Ball-Type)** | Spherical core within a ring socket | Multi-directional with high adjustability |
| **Egg-Crate (Grid)** | Square grid of intersecting blades | Bidirectional diffusion |

#### Axis D — Based on Air-Conditioning Mode

- **Cooling-mode grills** — designed for *Coanda-effect* deflection off the windshield.
- **Heating-mode grills** — placed near floor for *natural convective rise*.
- **Bi-level grills** — combination of face-level + foot-level delivery.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — Grill Sizing & Selection

| # | Equation | Description | Typical Range |
|---|----------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | $Q = A_{gross} \times C_d \times V_{eff}$ | Volume flow rate through grill | $Q$ in m³/s |
| 2 | $A_{gross} = \dfrac{Q}{C_d \times V_{eff}}$ | Required gross grill area | $A$ in m² |
| 3 | $\Delta P = \dfrac{\rho V_{eff}^{\,2}}{2 \, C_d^{\,2}}$ | Static pressure drop across grill | $\Delta P$ in **Pa** |
| 4 | $T = K \times \sqrt{A_{gross}}$ | Empirical throw (ASHRAE) | $T$ in **m** |
| 5 | $D = 0.07 \times T \times \dfrac{V_{eff}}{V_{term}}$ | Drop due to buoyancy | $D$ in **m** |
| 6 | $NC = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{rms}}{P_{ref}}\right)$ | Noise Criterion index | dB |
| 7 | $\eta_{diff} = \dfrac{\Delta T_{inlet} - \Delta T_{outlet}}{\Delta T_{inlet}}$ | Diffusion efficiency (0–1) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho V_{eff} D_h}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number at grill face | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Values (Bolded for KTU exams):**
> - Air density $\rho = \mathbf{1.2 \; kg/m^3}$ (at 20 °C, 1 atm)
> - Reference sound pressure $P_{ref} = \mathbf{20 \; \mu Pa}$
> - Typical cabin face velocity $V_{eff} = \mathbf{1.0 \; m/s}$
> - Discharge coefficient $C_d = \mathbf{0.65}$ (louvered), **0.75** (perforated)

---

### 2.3 Operational Theory — Why Grill Design Matters

**The "Why" behind each design choice:**

1. **High velocity = Drafts**: Air above **0.5 m/s** on bare skin registers as a draft in a cold cabin. Grills must *throw* air across the cabin, not blow it at occupants.

2. **Throw vs. Drop trade-off**: A long throw means the air reaches the rear passengers, but excessive drop causes the cold air to fall on the floor. Engineers tune $C_d$ and $A_{gross}$ to balance both.

3. **Defrost physics**: Warm air must *attach* to the cold windshield glass. This is the **Coanda effect** — the airstream follows the curved surface if the grill is angled within **15°–30°** of the glass tangent.

4. **Acoustic constraint**: Every grill generates **vortex-induced noise** at the louver edges. Perforated and foam-backed grills break up these vortices, reducing NC by **5–8 dB**.

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Required Grill Area for Given Airflow

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setup:**
> An automotive HVAC system must deliver $Q = 0.06 \; m^3/s$ of cooled air into the cabin through a louvered dashboard grill. Given $V_{eff} = 1.0 \; m/s$ and $C_d = 0.65$, find:
> 1. Gross grill area
> 2. Pressure drop
> 3. Throw distance
> 4. Noise assessment

#### Step 1 — Identify the Governing Continuity Equation

Mass flow continuity at the grill face:

$$Q = A_{gross} \cdot C_d \cdot V_{eff}$$

Rearranging for $A_{gross}$:

$$A_{gross} = \dfrac{Q}{C_d \cdot V_{eff}}$$

#### Step 2 — Substitute Numerical Values

$$
A_{gross} = \dfrac{0.06}{0.65 \times 1.0} = \dfrac{0.06}{0.65}
$$

$$
A_{gross} = 0.0923 \; m^2 = 923 \; cm^2
$$

This corresponds to a rectangular grill of approximately **30 cm × 3 cm** — a realistic dashboard center-vent size.

#### Step 3 — Compute the Static Pressure Drop

Using the orifice-flow model:

$$\Delta P = \dfrac{\rho \, V_{eff}^{\,2}}{2 \, C_d^{\,2}}$$

Substituting $\rho = 1.2 \; kg/m^3$, $V_{eff} = 1.0 \; m/s$, $C_d = 0.65$:

$$
\Delta P = \dfrac{1.2 \times (1.0)^2}{2 \times (0.65)^2}
$$

$$
\Delta P = \dfrac{1.2}{2 \times 0.4225} = \dfrac{1.2}{0.845}
$$

$$
\Delta P = 1.42 \; Pa
$$

In mm of water column (commonly used in HVAC):

$$\Delta P = \dfrac{1.42}{9.81} = 0.145 \; mm\;H_2O$$

This is a very low pressure loss — confirming the grill is well-sized.

#### Step 4 — Estimate the Throw Distance

ASHRAE empirical correlation:

$$T = K \cdot \sqrt{A_{gross}}$$

For a louvered grill in cooling mode, $K = 6.5$:

$$
T = 6.5 \times \sqrt{0.0923} = 6.5 \times 0.3038
$$

$$
T \approx 1.97 \; m
$$

This means the air will travel **~2 meters** — sufficient to reach the rear-seat passenger in a mid-size sedan.

#### Step 5 — Compute the Drop

$$
D = 0.07 \cdot T \cdot \dfrac{V_{eff}}{V_{term}}
$$

where $V_{term} = 0.25 \; m/s$:

$$
D = 0.07 \times 1.97 \times \dfrac{1.0}{0.25}
$$

$$
D = 0.07 \times 1.97 \times 4.0 = 0.552 \; m
$$

The airstream drops by **~55 cm** over its 2 m throw — acceptable for a dashboard grill.

#### Step 6 — Acoustic Noise Check

Using an empirical correlation for louvered grills:

$$NC_{grill} \approx 40 + 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{eff}}{1.0}\right) - 5 \cdot (\text{foam backing})$$

For $V_{eff} = 1.0 \; m/s$ and *no* foam:

$$
NC \approx 40 + 0 = 40 \; dB
$$

This is **borderline acceptable** (target NC 35). Adding foam backing would drop it to **35 dB**.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Grill Selection by Application

| Cabin Zone | Grill Type | $V_{eff}$ (m/s) | $C_d$ | Throw (m) | Special Feature |
|------------|-----------|-----------------|-------|-----------|-----------------|
| **Driver face** | Louvered, adjustable | 1.0 – 1.5 | 0.65 | 0.5 – 0.8 | Vertical slats, manual damper |
| **Front passenger** | Louvered, symmetric | 1.0 | 0.65 | 0.5 – 0.8 | Identical to driver-side |
| **Rear seat** | Linear-slot roof console | 0.8 | 0.75 | 1.5 – 2.0 | Long throw, low noise |
| **Windshield (defrost)** | Slot, fixed 30° up | 3.0 – 5.0 | 0.70 | 0.3 – 0.5 | Coanda-effect design |
| **Floor (heating)** | Louvered, upward | 1.2 | 0.60 | 0.6 | Bi-level blending |
| **Side windows (demist)** | Small round | 0.5 | 0.80 | 0.3 | Perforated face |

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Grill Classification Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Automotive HVAC Grills]:::root --> B[By Function]
    A --> C[By Adjustability]
    A --> D[By Geometry]
    A --> E[By Air Mode]
    
    B --> B1[Supply Grill]
    B --> B2[Return Grill]
    B --> B3[Defrost Grill]
    B --> B4[Demist Grill]
    
    C --> C1[Fixed Grill]
    C --> C2[Manual Louver]
    C --> C3[Swivel Ball Type]
    C --> C4[Motorized Actuator]
    
    D --> D1[Linear Slot]
    D --> D2[Louvered Slat]
    D --> D3[Round Circular]
    D --> D4[Perforated Plate]
    D --> D5[Eye Ball Type]
    D --> D6[Egg Crate Grid]
    
    E --> E1[Cooling Mode Grill]
    E --> E2[Heating Mode Grill]
    E --> E3[Bi Level Grill]
    
    classDef root fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000;
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Airflow Path Through a Vehicle Cabin

```mermaid
graph LR
    EVAP[Evaporator Core]:::comp --> BLOW[Blower Motor]:::comp
    BLOW --> DUCT[Plenum Duct]:::comp
    DUCT --> SG1[Center Vent Grill]:::grill
    DUCT --> SG2[Side Vent Grill]:::grill
    DUCT --> SG3[Roof Console Grill]:::grill
    DUCT --> SG4[Defrost Grill]:::grill
    DUCT --> SG5[Floor Vent Grill]:::grill
    DUCT --> SG6[Demist Grill]:::grill
    
    SG1 --> C1[Front Cabin Zone]:::zone
    SG2 --> C1
    SG3 --> C2[Rear Cabin Zone]:::zone
    SG5 --> C2
    SG4 --> C3[Windshield Zone]:::zone
    SG6 --> C4[Side Window Zone]:::zone
    
    C1 --> RG[Return Grill at Floor]:::grill
    C2 --> RG
    RG --> EVAP
    
    classDef comp fill:#4A90E2,stroke:#000,color:#FFF;
    classDef grill fill:#F5A623,stroke:#000,color:#000;
    classDef zone fill:#7ED321,stroke:#000,color:#000;
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture — Defrost Grill Coanda Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    DUCT[Plenum Supply Duct]:::duct --> NOZZLE[Defrost Slot Nozzle]:::nozzle
    NOZZLE --> ANGLE{Aim Angle}:::decision
    ANGLE -->|15 to 30 deg| COANDA[Coanda Attachment]:::attach
    ANGLE -->|Over 30 deg| SEP[Flow Separation]:::separate
    COANDA --> GLASS[Air Stream Follows Windshield Curve]:::glass
    SEP --> MISS[Air Misses Glass - Poor Defrost]:::bad
    GLASS --> DEICE[Effective Frost Removal]:::good
    
    classDef duct fill:#50E3C2,stroke:#000,color:#000;
    classDef nozzle fill:#F8E71C,stroke:#000,color:#000;
    classDef decision fill:#BD10E0,stroke:#FFF,color:#FFF;
    classDef attach fill:#7ED321,stroke:#000,color:#000;
    classDef separate fill:#D0021B,stroke:#FFF,color:#FFF;
    classDef glass fill:#4A90E2,stroke:#FFF,color:#FFF;
    classDef bad fill:#D0021B,stroke:#FFF,color:#FFF;
    classDef good fill:#7ED321,stroke:#000,color:#000;
```

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. Define a "grill" in the context of automotive air-conditioning. List any two types based on function.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

A **grill** is a terminal air-distribution device fitted at the outlet of an automotive HVAC duct, designed to deliver conditioned air into the passenger cabin with controlled **velocity, direction, and diffusion**.

It serves to convert the **high-velocity duct flow** into a **low-velocity, well-spread airstream** suitable for occupant comfort, while also providing manual or motorized adjustability.

**Two types based on function:**

1. **Supply (Delivery) Grill** — pushes conditioned air into the cabin (e.g., dashboard center vent).
2. **Return (Recirculation) Grill** — draws cabin air back to the evaporator for reconditioning (typically floor-level).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Marking Tip:** Always state the *function* of the grill in your definition, not just its location. Examiners reward functional clarity.

---

#### **Q2. What is the Coanda effect, and why is it critical for defrost grills?** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

The **Coanda effect** is the tendency of a fluid jet to remain *attached* to a nearby curved surface when the jet is discharged parallel (within **15°–30°**) to that surface.

In defrost grills, this is critical because:

1. The grill nozzle is angled at **~20°** toward the windshield.
2. The warm air jet **"sticks"** to the curved glass instead of dispersing into the cabin.
3. This ensures **efficient frost/condensation removal** at the windshield base — improving driver visibility and safety.

If the angle exceeds **30°**, the jet separates from the glass and defrost performance collapses.

---

### 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Module 3]`

> **(a)** Classify the different **types of grills** used in an automotive HVAC system based on (i) function, (ii) adjustability, and (iii) physical geometry. Give **two examples** for each category. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** With the help of a neat **block diagram**, explain the **airflow path** from the evaporator to the cabin through various grills and back to the return grill. **[7 Marks]**

---

### **Solution to Question A**

#### Part (a) — Classification of Grills **[7 Marks]**

**(i) By Function:**

| Type | Purpose | Example Location |
|------|---------|------------------|
| **Supply Grill** | Delivers conditioned air to occupants | Dashboard center vent |
| **Return Grill** | Returns cabin air to AC unit | Base of dashboard / floor |
| **Defrost Grill** | Clears windshield frost | Upper edge of dashboard |
| **Demist Grill** | Removes side-window condensation | A-pillar base |

**(ii) By Adjustability:**

| Type | Description | Application |
|------|-------------|-------------|
| **Fixed Grill** | Permanent, non-adjustable | Rear console duct |
| **Manual Louver Grill** | Rotating horizontal/vertical slats | Dashboard center vent |
| **Swivel / Eye-Ball Grill** | Pivoting spherical core | Side vents in premium cars |
| **Motorized Grill** | Actuator-driven louvers | Luxury EVs |

**(iii) By Geometry:**

| Type | Shape | Airflow Behavior |
|------|-------|------------------|
| **Linear Slot** | Long, narrow rectangular | High spread, low drop |
| **Louvered (Slat)** | Multiple angled blades | Directional control |
| **Round / Circular** | Concentric ring | Radial, soft diffusion |
| **Perforated Plate** | Flat with many holes | Low noise, low throw |

> Each category carries roughly **1.5 marks** in valuation — allocate words proportionally.

---

#### Part (b) — Airflow Path Block Diagram & Explanation **[7 Marks]**

**Block Diagram:**

```
Evaporator → Blower Motor → Plenum Duct
                                  │
        ┌─────────────┬────────────┼─────────────┬─────────────┐
        ▼             ▼            ▼             ▼             ▼
   Center Vent    Side Vent    Roof Console   Defrost Grill  Floor Vent
   (SG1)          (SG2)        (SG3)          (DG)           (SG5)
        │             │            │             │             │
        ▼             ▼            ▼             ▼             ▼
   Front Cabin    Front Cabin   Rear Cabin   Windshield     Rear Cabin
        │             │            │             │             │
        └─────────────┴────────────┴─────────────┴─────────────┘
                                  │
                                  ▼
                         Return Grill (RG) at floor
                                  │
                                  ▼
                          Back to Evaporator
```

**Explanation:**

1. The **evaporator** cools and dehumidifies the air.
2. The **blower motor** forces this air into the **plenum duct** (main HVAC trunk).
3. From the plenum, air branches into **six supply paths** corresponding to six grill types: center, side, roof, defrost, floor, and demist.
4. Each grill performs **velocity reduction, direction control, and diffusion** tailored to its zone.
5. After absorbing heat/moisture from occupants and surfaces, the cabin air is drawn into the **return grill** (typically at floor level, where cold air pools).
6. The return air re-enters the blower, completing the **closed-loop HVAC cycle**.

> The block diagram carries **3 marks**, and the explanatory paragraph carries **4 marks**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Module 3]`

> **(a)** Define **throw, drop, and spread** as applied to automotive grills. Explain their significance in designing dashboard and defrost grills. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A dashboard supply grill must deliver **$Q = 0.05 \; m^3/s$** of cooled air. Given $V_{eff} = 1.0 \; m/s$ and $C_d = 0.70$, calculate:
   - (i) Required gross grill area
   - (ii) Static pressure drop across the grill
   - (iii) Approximate throw distance (use $K = 6.5$)
   - (iv) Comment on the suitability of this design for a mid-size sedan. **[7 Marks]**

---

### **Solution to Question B**

#### Part (a) — Definitions and Significance **[7 Marks]**

1. **Throw ($T$):** The horizontal distance traveled by the conditioned airstream from the grill outlet before its centerline velocity drops to the **terminal value of 0.25 m/s**. Measured in **meters**.

   *Significance for dashboard grills:* Determines how far the air reaches the front passenger. Typical target $T = 0.6 – 0.8 \; m$.

   *Significance for defrost grills:* Must throw air **~0.4 m** to reach the base of the windshield.

2. **Drop ($D$):** The vertical fall of the airstream due to buoyancy and Coanda effects during its throw. Measured in **meters**.

   *Significance:* Excessive drop causes cold air to fall on the floor instead of reaching occupants. For dashboard cooling grills, $D \le 0.5 \; m$ is desirable.

3. **Spread ($S$):** The lateral (sideways) diffusion width of the airstream as it travels.

   *Significance:* Determines how widely the air is distributed across the cabin width. For windshield defrost, **wide spread** is critical to cover the entire driver-side glass.

> Each definition carries ~**1.5 marks**; significance discussion carries the remaining balance.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Design Problem **[7 Marks]**

**Given:**
- $Q = 0.05 \; m^3/s$
- $V_{eff} = 1.0 \; m/s$
- $C_d = 0.70$
- $K = 6.5$
- $\rho = 1.2 \; kg/m^3$

**(i) Gross Grill Area:**

$$
A_{gross} = \dfrac{Q}{C_d \cdot V_{eff}} = \dfrac{0.05}{0.70 \times 1.0}
$$

$$
A_{gross} = 0.0714 \; m^2 \approx 714 \; cm^2
$$

**(ii) Static Pressure Drop:**

$$
\Delta P = \dfrac{\rho \, V_{eff}^{\,2}}{2 \, C_d^{\,2}} = \dfrac{1.2 \times (1.0)^2}{2 \times (0.70)^2}
$$

$$
\Delta P = \dfrac{1.2}{2 \times 0.49} = \dfrac{1.2}{0.98} = 1.22 \; Pa
$$

In mm water column:

$$
\Delta P = \dfrac{1.22}{9.81} \approx 0.125 \; mm\;H_2O
$$

**(iii) Throw Distance:**

$$
T = K \cdot \sqrt{A_{gross}} = 6.5 \times \sqrt{0.0714} = 6.5 \times 0.2673
$$

$$
T \approx 1.74 \; m
$$

**(iv) Design Suitability Comment:**

A throw of **1.74 m** combined with a low face velocity of **1.0 m/s** and pressure drop of only **1.22 Pa** indicates:

- ✅ The grill is **well-sized** for a mid-size sedan (typical cabin length ~2.0 m).
- ✅ Air will reach the rear passenger area without excessive drop.
- ✅ Pressure loss is negligible — blower can handle it easily.
- ⚠️ If the cabin is larger (SUV), throw should be increased by selecting $V_{eff} = 1.5 \; m/s$, which would raise $\Delta P$ to ~2.75 Pa (still acceptable).

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. Students **often forget to convert Pa to mm H₂O** when asked — losing 1 mark.
> 2. **Unit mistakes** are common: $A_{gross}$ must be in **m²**, not cm², in the final formula.
> 3. The throw formula $T = K\sqrt{A}$ requires $A$ in m²; students sometimes use cm², getting **throw = 54.7 m** — wildly wrong!
> 4. Always comment on **real-world suitability** — examiners award **1 mark** specifically for design interpretation.

---

### 🎯 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Important Things to Remember — Types of Grills**

- ✅ A **grill** is a *terminal air-distribution device*, not just a hole — it controls velocity, direction, and noise.
- ✅ **Four primary types by function**: Supply, Return, Defrost, Demist.
- ✅ **Defrost grills** rely on the **Coanda effect** — aim angle must be **15°–30°** toward windshield.
- ✅ **Return grills** are placed at **floor level** because cold air pools low (natural convection).
- ✅ **Key parameters**: Throw ($T$), Drop ($D$), Spread ($S$), Discharge coefficient ($C_d$), Face velocity ($V_{eff}$).
- ✅ Standard values to memorize for KTU: $\rho = 1.2 \; kg/m^3$, $V_{eff} = 1.0 \; m/s$, $C_d = 0.6$–$0.8$, $V_{term} = 0.25 \; m/s$.
- ✅ Governing equation: $Q = A_{gross} \cdot C_d \cdot V_{eff}$ — memorize this.
- ✅ Throw formula: $T = K\sqrt{A_{gross}}$ with $A$ in **m²**.
- ✅ Pressure drop equation: $\Delta P = \rho V^2 / (2 C_d^{\,2})$.
- ✅ **Geometric types**: Linear Slot, Louvered, Round, Perforated, Eye-Ball, Egg-Crate.
- ✅ Perforated grills → **lowest noise** (NC ~30); Louvered → **highest adjustability**.
- ✅ A **wrong unit conversion** (cm² vs m²) is the #1 mark-loss trap in grill design problems.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Diffusers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT523 - AUTOMOTIVE REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Diffusers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **diffuser** is a duct or passage of **diverging cross-sectional area** designed to convert the **kinetic energy (velocity head)** of a flowing fluid (air or refrigerant vapor) into **static pressure (pressure head)**, thereby decelerating the flow and recovering pressure. In the context of automotive refrigeration and air conditioning systems, diffusers are essentially **expansion devices for airflow** — they are the aerodynamic opposite of a nozzle.

Mathematically stated, for a steady, incompressible flow through a diffuser:

$$ \text{Kinetic Energy at Inlet} + \text{Pressure Energy at Inlet} = \text{Kinetic Energy at Outlet} + \text{Pressure Energy at Outlet} + \text{Losses} $$

In automotive applications, diffusers serve a **dual purpose**:
1. **Aerodynamic Body Diffusers:** Located at the underbody/rear of a vehicle, they manage the airflow under the car to reduce drag and increase downforce (a high-speed stability device).
2. **HVAC Air Diffusers:** Located at the dashboard vents, windshield defroster outlets, and rear AC ducts, they distribute conditioned air evenly into the passenger cabin with minimal noise and turbulence.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Within Module 3 (Air Distribution and Vehicle Aerodynamics), diffusers are studied as part of the **air distribution system** of the automotive HVAC unit. The emphasis is on the **pressure recovery mechanism**, the **geometry of divergence**, and the **energy loss characteristics** during flow expansion.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are riding a bicycle very fast down a hill and you suddenly come to a **wide, flat open field**. What happens?
- You **spread out** your body to slow down (the air resistance catches you over a larger surface).
- Your **speed drops dramatically** even though you didn't brake.
- The wind (air) that was hitting you hard at high speed now **gently pushes** against your entire front.

That, in essence, is a diffuser. When a fast stream of air (or any fluid) is suddenly given a **wider pathway**, the **same amount of air per second** must now fill a **bigger area**. Since the same quantity is moving through a larger cross-section, its **velocity drops**. By the **Law of Conservation of Energy**, energy cannot simply disappear — so the lost kinetic energy transforms into **static pressure** (the "push" you feel).

Think of a garden hose: when you cover part of the nozzle with your thumb, the water shoots out faster (a *nozzle* in reverse). When you release the thumb and let the water flow through a wider opening, it slows down but pushes harder against the sides of the hose (a *diffuser* action).

In a car:
- The **dashboard AC vent** is a diffuser — it takes the fast, narrow jet of cold air from the evaporator housing and spreads it out into a wide, gentle, low-velocity breeze.
- The **rear bumper diffuser** in sports cars takes the fast, turbulent air rushing under the car and **decelerates/expands it** to create a low-pressure region (downforce) for better tire grip.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Memory Anchor:** *Nozzle → Speed up flow (kinetic energy in).* 
> *Diffuser → Slow down flow (pressure energy out).* 
> *They are thermodynamic twins operating in opposite directions.*

## 1.3 Key Physical Metrics and Standard Values

| Parameter | Typical Value/Range (Automotive) | Symbol |
|---|---|---|
| Divergence half-angle (subsonic) | 5° to 12° (optimum ~7°) | $\alpha$ |
| Area ratio (outlet/inlet) | 1.5 to 4.0 | $A_2/A_1$ |
| Pressure recovery coefficient | 0.5 to 0.9 | $C_p$ |
| Diffuser efficiency | 60% to 90% | $\eta_d$ |
| Air velocity at outlet (cabin) | 1 to 5 m/s | $V_2$ |
| Static pressure rise | 10 to 200 Pa (HVAC) | $\Delta P$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary condition:** For **subsonic flow**, the diffuser must always have an **expanding area** ($dA > 0$) along the flow direction. For supersonic flow, the opposite is true. Since automotive AC deals exclusively with subsonic cabin air, the rule is: **diverging area = pressure rise**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Governing Principles of Diffuser Operation

A diffuser operates on three foundational pillars of fluid mechanics. Understanding these in sequence is the key to mastering KTU numerical problems.

### Pillar 1: Continuity Equation (Conservation of Mass)
For a **steady, incompressible flow** through a duct of varying cross-section, the **mass flow rate is constant** at every cross-section:

$$ \dot{m} = \rho \cdot A_1 \cdot V_1 = \rho \cdot A_2 \cdot V_2 = \text{constant} $$

This means if the area $A$ **increases**, the velocity $V$ must **decrease** proportionally to maintain the same mass flow. This is the **geometric reason** why diffusers slow the flow down.

### Pillar 2: Bernoulli's Principle (Conservation of Energy)
For an inviscid (frictionless) flow, the total energy is conserved:

$$ P_1 + \frac{1}{2}\rho V_1^2 = P_2 + \frac{1}{2}\rho V_2^2 $$

Since $V_2 < V_1$ (velocity drops), it mathematically **forces** $P_2 > P_1$ (static pressure rises). The pressure rise in an **ideal (lossless)** diffuser is:

$$ P_{2,\text{ideal}} - P_1 = \frac{1}{2}\rho \left( V_1^2 - V_2^2 \right) $$

### Pillar 3: Real Fluid Losses (Second Law of Thermodynamics)
In reality, viscous friction and flow separation inside the diverging passage cause **entropy generation**, meaning not all kinetic energy converts to static pressure. Some is lost as heat (internal friction). This is captured by the **diffuser efficiency**:

$$ \eta_d = \frac{\text{Actual pressure rise}}{\text{Ideal pressure rise}} = \frac{P_2 - P_1}{\frac{1}{2}\rho(V_1^2 - V_2^2)} $$

## 2.2 Why Diffusers Are Designed With Specific Divergence Angles

If the diffuser angle is too small, friction loss dominates (long passage, more surface area).
If the diffuser angle is too large, the flow **separates** from the walls, creating **turbulence, eddies, and backflow** — drastically reducing efficiency.

The **optimum divergence half-angle** balances these two effects, typically between **5° to 12°** for subsonic flow.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** A diffuser with an excessively large angle suffers from **flow separation**, which can cause the actual pressure rise to become **negative** (the diffuser acts as a loss element rather than a recovery device). This phenomenon is tested in numerical problems.

## 2.3 Types of Diffusers (KTU Classification)

| Type | Description | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Conical / Pyramidal Diffuser** | Straight-walled, uniform divergence | Most automotive HVAC ducts |
| **Sudden Expansion Diffuser** | Abrupt area increase (sharp step) | Inlet plenums, evaporator outlets |
| **Wall Diffuser (Curved)** | One wall curved, other straight | Windshield defrost outlets |
| **Annular Diffuser** | Flow between two concentric cylinders | Blower motor housings, jet engines |
| **Aerodynamic Underbody Diffuser** | Shaped channels under the car rear | Sports car body design |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorize the following equations** — they appear in **almost every KTU numerical problem** on diffusers.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dot{m} = \rho A V$ | Mass flow rate (continuity) | kg/s |
| 2 | $V_2 = V_1 \cdot \dfrac{A_1}{A_2}$ | Outlet velocity from continuity | m/s |
| 3 | $\Delta P_{\text{ideal}} = \dfrac{1}{2}\rho\left(V_1^2 - V_2^2\right)$ | Ideal (lossless) pressure rise | Pa |
| 4 | $\Delta P_{\text{actual}} = \eta_d \cdot \Delta P_{\text{ideal}}$ | Real pressure rise with efficiency | Pa |
| 5 | $C_p = \dfrac{P_2 - P_1}{\frac{1}{2}\rho V_1^2}$ | Pressure recovery coefficient | dimensionless |
| 6 | $C_p = \eta_d \left( 1 - \dfrac{A_1^2}{A_2^2} \right)$ | $C_p$ in terms of area ratio | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\text{Loss coefficient } K_L = 1 - \eta_d$ | Head loss fraction | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\eta_d = \dfrac{1 - (A_1/A_2)^2}{1 - (A_1/A_2)^2 + K_L \cdot (A_1/A_2)^2}$ | Diffuser efficiency in terms of loss coeff. | dimensionless |
| 9 | $P = \dot{m} \cdot R \cdot T$ | Equation of state for air (ideal gas) | Pa |
| 10 | $A_2 / A_1 = (D_2 / D_1)^2$ | Area ratio for circular ducts | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Notation Safeguard:** All expressions such as $\frac{A_1}{A_2}$, $\frac{V_1^2}{2}$, etc., use **proper LaTeX fractions**. **Never write them as $\vert A_1/A_2 \vert$ or with raw pipes** — use `\mid` or `\vert` if absolute value is required.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Cabin Comfort:** Diffusers in dashboard vents slow down the air jet to **1–3 m/s** so that passengers don't feel an uncomfortable "blast" of cold air. The static pressure rise ensures **even distribution** across multiple vents from a single blower.
2. **Defrost Performance:** The defroster duct is shaped as a wall diffuser to spread warm air across the **entire windshield area** uniformly, preventing ice/fog patches.
3. **Noise Reduction:** High-velocity air creates aerodynamic noise. By reducing velocity, diffusers reduce cabin noise (target: **< 45 dB** at the passenger ear).
4. **Aerodynamics:** Rear diffusers create a **low-pressure zone** beneath the rear of the car, generating **downforce** that pushes the tires onto the road — critical for high-speed cornering in performance vehicles.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Outlet Velocity from Continuity

**Given:** A diffuser with inlet area $A_1$, inlet velocity $V_1$, and outlet area $A_2$.

**Find:** Outlet velocity $V_2$.

**Step 1:** Start with the steady-flow continuity equation for an incompressible fluid.

$$ \dot{m}_1 = \dot{m}_2 $$

**Step 2:** Expand both sides using $\dot{m} = \rho A V$.

$$ \rho_1 A_1 V_1 = \rho_2 A_2 V_2 $$

**Step 3:** Since air at low speeds can be treated as **incompressible**, $\rho_1 = \rho_2 = \rho$.

$$ \rho A_1 V_1 = \rho A_2 V_2 $$

**Step 4:** Cancel the common density term $\rho$ from both sides.

$$ A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 $$

**Step 5:** Solve algebraically for $V_2$ by dividing both sides by $A_2$.

$$ V_2 = \frac{A_1}{A_2} \cdot V_1 $$
