**Conclusion:** Since $A_2 > A_1$ (diffuser condition), the ratio $\frac{A_1}{A_2} < 1$, and therefore $V_2 < V_1$. This **proves** that a diverging passage reduces velocity — the foundational principle of a diffuser.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Ideal Pressure Rise (Bernoulli Application)

**Given:** Air enters a diffuser at velocity $V_1$ and exits at velocity $V_2$.

**Find:** The ideal static pressure rise $P_2 - P_1$.

**Step 1:** Apply Bernoulli's equation along a streamline (no height change, so the elevation terms cancel).

$$ P_1 + \frac{1}{2}\rho V_1^2 = P_2 + \frac{1}{2}\rho V_2^2 $$

**Step 2:** Rearrange to isolate the pressure difference by moving the $V_2$ term to the left and the $P_1$ term to the right.

$$ P_2 - P_1 = \frac{1}{2}\rho V_1^2 - \frac{1}{2}\rho V_2^2 $$

**Step 3:** Factor out the common term $\frac{1}{2}\rho$ on the right-hand side.

$$ P_2 - P_1 = \frac{1}{2}\rho \left( V_1^2 - V_2^2 \right) $$

**Step 4 (Optional):** Apply the difference of squares identity to the bracketed term.

$$ P_2 - P_1 = \frac{1}{2}\rho \left( V_1 - V_2 \right)\left( V_1 + V_2 \right) $$

**Step 5 (Substitution):** Substitute $V_2 = \frac{A_1}{A_2} V_1$ from the continuity result to express everything in terms of the inlet velocity and area ratio.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_2 - P_1 &= \frac{1}{2}\rho V_1^2 \left[ 1 - \left( \frac{A_1}{A_2} \right)^2 \right] \\
&= \frac{1}{2}\rho V_1^2 \left[ 1 - \left( \frac{A_1}{A_2} \right)^2 \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** This compact form shows that the **ideal pressure rise is directly proportional to the inlet dynamic pressure** and a function of the **area ratio**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Diffuser Efficiency and Pressure Recovery Coefficient

**Given:** Actual pressure rise measured experimentally, and ideal pressure rise calculated from Bernoulli.

**Find:** Diffuser efficiency $\eta_d$ and pressure recovery coefficient $C_p$.

**Step 1:** Define diffuser efficiency as the ratio of actual to ideal pressure rise.

$$ \eta_d = \frac{P_{2,\text{actual}} - P_1}{P_{2,\text{ideal}} - P_1} $$

**Step 2:** Substitute the ideal pressure rise from Derivation 2.

$$ \eta_d = \frac{P_{2,\text{actual}} - P_1}{\frac{1}{2}\rho\left( V_1^2 - V_2^2 \right)} $$

**Step 3:** The **pressure recovery coefficient** $C_p$ is defined as the actual pressure rise normalized by the **inlet dynamic pressure** $\frac{1}{2}\rho V_1^2$.

$$ C_p = \frac{P_{2,\text{actual}} - P_1}{\frac{1}{2}\rho V_1^2} $$

**Step 4:** Equate the two definitions and substitute the area ratio relationship.

$$ C_p = \eta_d \cdot \left[ 1 - \left( \frac{A_1}{A_2} \right)^2 \right] $$

**Step 5:** Recognizing that $\frac{A_1}{A_2} = \left(\frac{D_1}{D_2}\right)^2$ for circular ducts, the final form becomes:

$$ C_p = \eta_d \left[ 1 - \left( \frac{D_1}{D_2} \right)^4 \right] $$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** The fourth-power relationship on the diameter ratio means that even small increases in outlet diameter produce **large improvements in pressure recovery**. This is why HVAC ducts often use **gradual tapering** rather than abrupt steps.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** Air at **27°C** and **101.325 kPa** enters a conical diffuser at a velocity of **15 m/s**. The inlet diameter is **80 mm** and the outlet diameter is **160 mm**. Assuming a diffuser efficiency of **75%**, calculate:
1. The outlet velocity $V_2$.
2. The actual static pressure rise $\Delta P$.
3. The pressure recovery coefficient $C_p$.

**Given Data:**
- $T_1 = 27°C = 300 \text{ K}$
- $P_1 = 101{,}325 \text{ Pa}$
- $V_1 = 15 \text{ m/s}$
- $D_1 = 0.080 \text{ m}$, $D_2 = 0.160 \text{ m}$
- $\eta_d = 0.75$
- $R_{\text{air}} = 287 \text{ J/(kg·K)}$

**Step 1 — Air Density:**

$$ \rho = \frac{P_1}{R T_1} = \frac{101{,}325}{287 \times 300} = 1.177 \text{ kg/m}^3 $$

**Step 2 — Outlet Velocity from Continuity:**

$$ V_2 = V_1 \cdot \left( \frac{D_1}{D_2} \right)^2 = 15 \times \left( \frac{80}{160} \right)^2 = 15 \times 0.25 = 3.75 \text{ m/s} $$

**Step 3 — Ideal Pressure Rise:**

$$ \Delta P_{\text{ideal}} = \frac{1}{2}\rho\left( V_1^2 - V_2^2 \right) $$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta P_{\text{ideal}} &= 0.5 \times 1.177 \times \left( 15^2 - 3.75^2 \right) \\
&= 0.5885 \times \left( 225 - 14.0625 \right) \\
&= 0.5885 \times 210.9375 \\
&= 124.14 \text{ Pa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Actual Pressure Rise:**

$$ \Delta P_{\text{actual}} = \eta_d \times \Delta P_{\text{ideal}} = 0.75 \times 124.14 = 93.10 \text{ Pa} $$

**Step 5 — Pressure Recovery Coefficient:**

$$ C_p = \frac{\Delta P_{\text{actual}}}{\frac{1}{2}\rho V_1^2} = \frac{93.10}{0.5 \times 1.177 \times 225} = \frac{93.10}{132.41} = 0.703 $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Answer Summary:**
> $V_2 = 3.75 \text{ m/s}$, $\Delta P_{\text{actual}} \approx 93.1 \text{ Pa}$, $C_p \approx 0.703$.
> The $C_p$ value of **0.703** indicates a well-designed diffuser (typical range 0.5–0.9).

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Numerical Variables

| Variable | Symbol | Standard Unit | Common KTU Mistake |
|---|---|---|---|
| Inlet velocity | $V_1$ | m/s | Forgetting to convert from cm/s or km/h |
| Outlet velocity | $V_2$ | m/s | Inverting the area ratio |
| Density | $\rho$ | kg/m³ | Using 1.2 kg/m³ for STP instead of given T,P |
| Efficiency | $\eta_d$ | dimensionless | Confusing % and decimal (0.75 vs 75%) |
| Diameter | $D$ | m | Forgetting to square for area in circular ducts |
| Pressure rise | $\Delta P$ | Pa | Mixing Pa with kPa or mm of H₂O |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Diffuser Functional Architecture in Automotive HVAC

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Blower Motor] --> B[Evaporator Housing]
    B --> C{Plenum Chamber}
    C --> D1[Center Console Diffuser]
    C --> D2[Dashboard Diffuser Vents]
    C --> D3[Windshield Defrost Wall Diffuser]
    C --> D4[Rear AC Duct Diffuser]
    D1 --> E1[Front Passenger Cabin Zone]
    D2 --> E2[Driver Cabin Zone]
    D3 --> E3[Windshield Inner Surface]
    D4 --> E4[Rear Passenger Cabin Zone]
    E1 --> F[Recirculation Return]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    F --> A
```

> **Reading the diagram:** A single blower drives air through the evaporator (which cools and dehumidifies it), into a plenum chamber, and then the plenum **branches out** to multiple diffuser outlets. Each diffuser (D1–D4) takes the **high-velocity, narrow jet** from the plenum and **expands it into a wide, low-velocity stream** suited for cabin delivery.

## 4.2 Mermaid Flow Process — Velocity and Pressure Transformation Inside a Diffuser

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Inlet["Inlet Section A1, V1, P1"] --> Throat["Throat Region: A increases gradually"]
    Throat --> Divergence["Diverging Section: 5 to 12 degree half-angle"]
    Divergence --> Recovery["Static Pressure Recovery Zone"]
    Recovery --> Outlet["Outlet Section A2 greater than A1, V2 lower than V1, P2 greater than P1"]
    Divergence -. "Energy lost as heat" .-> Loss["Viscous and Turbulent Losses"]
    Loss -. "Reduces eta_d" .-> Outlet
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Comparison — Nozzle vs Diffuser (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph N["NOZZLE Acceleration"]
        NA["Inlet: A1, V1, P1"] --> NB["Converging Passage"]
        NB --> NC["Outlet: A2 less than A1, V2 greater than V1, P2 less than P1"]
    end
    subgraph D["DIFFUSER Deceleration"]
        DA["Inlet: A1, V1, P1"] --> DB["Diverging Passage"]
        DB --> DC["Outlet: A2 greater than A1, V2 less than V1, P2 greater than P1"]
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this diagram matters for KTU:** Examiners frequently ask students to **contrast** nozzles and diffusers. Memorize the **inversion of the area–velocity relationship** and the **direction of the pressure gradient** to score full marks on definition questions.

## 4.4 Vehicle Body Aerodynamic Diffuser (Functional Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[High-Speed Underbody Air Inlet] --> B[Constricted Channel beneath Floor]
    B --> C[Expansion Region at Rear: Diverging Cross-Section]
    C --> D[Low Pressure Zone beneath Rear Diffuser]
    D --> E[Downforce on Rear Tires]
    E --> F[Improved High-Speed Stability and Cornering Grip]
```

> **Engineering note:** The underbody diffuser works on the **same principle** as an HVAC diffuser — expand the area, slow the flow, and create a low-pressure wake. The pressure difference between the high-pressure upper body and low-pressure underbody generates a **net downward force**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]`
**Define a diffuser. How does it differ from a nozzle in terms of flow behavior and energy conversion?**

**Model Answer:**
A diffuser is a **diverging duct** that **decelerates** a flowing fluid and converts its **kinetic energy into static pressure**, causing a pressure rise. A nozzle is a **converging duct** that **accelerates** the flow and converts **static pressure into kinetic energy**, causing a pressure drop. In a diffuser, the area increases along the flow direction ($A_2 > A_1$), outlet velocity is lower than inlet velocity ($V_2 < V_1$), and static pressure at the outlet is higher than at the inlet ($P_2 > P_1$). The opposite holds for a nozzle.

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Question Paper]`
**What is the significance of diffuser efficiency and pressure recovery coefficient in automotive HVAC design?**

**Model Answer:**
**Diffuser efficiency ($\eta_d$)** is the ratio of the actual static pressure rise to the ideal (lossless) pressure rise. It quantifies how effectively a diffuser recovers pressure from the incoming kinetic energy, accounting for **viscous and turbulent losses**. A high efficiency (close to 1.0) is desirable for **better airflow distribution** and **lower blower power consumption**.

The **pressure recovery coefficient ($C_p$)** is the ratio of the actual pressure rise to the **inlet dynamic pressure** ($\frac{1}{2}\rho V_1^2$). It is a key parameter for **rating diffuser performance** in the automotive industry, with target values typically between **0.6 and 0.9**. Together, these two non-dimensional parameters allow KTU-style comparisons of different diffuser geometries independent of operating conditions.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 3]`

**(a)** With the help of a suitable diagram, explain the working principle of a diffuser used in the **air distribution system** of an automotive HVAC unit. Derive the expression for the **outlet velocity** and the **ideal static pressure rise**. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A conical diffuser fitted in an automotive AC plenum has an inlet diameter of **60 mm** and an outlet diameter of **120 mm**. Air enters the diffuser at **20 m/s**, with density **1.18 kg/m³**. If the diffuser efficiency is **70%**, calculate the **actual pressure rise** and the **pressure recovery coefficient**. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Step 1 — Definition and Diagram:**
A diffuser is a duct with **progressively increasing cross-sectional area** along the direction of flow. In an automotive HVAC system, the diffuser is placed **downstream of the evaporator** and **upstream of the cabin vents**. Its function is to take the high-velocity, narrow air stream from the blower and deliver it to the cabin as a **low-velocity, wide, evenly distributed flow** with minimal noise.

**Step 2 — Continuity (Outlet Velocity Derivation):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Mass flow rate at inlet} &= \text{Mass flow rate at outlet} \\
\rho A_1 V_1 &= \rho A_2 V_2 \\
V_2 &= V_1 \cdot \frac{A_1}{A_2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Bernoulli's Equation (Ideal Pressure Rise):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_1 + \frac{1}{2}\rho V_1^2 &= P_2 + \frac{1}{2}\rho V_2^2 \\
P_2 - P_1 &= \frac{1}{2}\rho \left( V_1^2 - V_2^2 \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Discussion of Loss Mechanisms:**
Real diffusers lose some kinetic energy to **wall friction**, **flow separation**, and **turbulence** (especially at high divergence angles). This is captured by diffuser efficiency $\eta_d$, defined as:

$$ \eta_d = \frac{(P_2 - P_1)_{\text{actual}}}{\frac{1}{2}\rho \left( V_1^2 - V_2^2 \right)} $$

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Given:**
- $D_1 = 0.060$ m, $D_2 = 0.120$ m
- $V_1 = 20$ m/s
- $\rho = 1.18$ kg/m³
- $\eta_d = 0.70$

**Step 1 — Outlet Velocity (Continuity):**

$$
V_2 = V_1 \left( \frac{D_1}{D_2} \right)^2 = 20 \times \left( \frac{60}{120} \right)^2 = 20 \times 0.25 = 5 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Ideal Pressure Rise:**

$$
\Delta P_{\text{ideal}} = \frac{1}{2} \times 1.18 \times \left( 20^2 - 5^2 \right) = 0.59 \times 375 = 221.25 \text{ Pa}
$$

**Step 3 — Actual Pressure Rise:**

$$
\Delta P_{\text{actual}} = \eta_d \times \Delta P_{\text{ideal}} = 0.70 \times 221.25 = 154.875 \text{ Pa}
$$

**Step 4 — Pressure Recovery Coefficient:**

$$
C_p = \frac{\Delta P_{\text{actual}}}{\frac{1}{2}\rho V_1^2} = \frac{154.875}{0.5 \times 1.18 \times 400} = \frac{154.875}{236} = 0.656
$$

**Final Answers:** $V_2 = 5$ m/s, $\Delta P_{\text{actual}} \approx 154.88$ Pa, $C_p \approx 0.656$.

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Module 3, Alternative Choice]`

**(a)** Discuss the **various types of diffusers** used in automotive applications. Compare **conical diffusers** and **wall diffusers** with respect to pressure recovery and typical use cases. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Air at **1.2 kg/m³** enters a diffuser at **25 m/s** through an inlet area of **0.005 m²**. The diffuser has a **divergence half-angle of 7°** and an outlet-to-inlet area ratio of **3.0**. If the diffuser efficiency is **80%**, determine: (i) the outlet velocity, (ii) the actual pressure rise, and (iii) the length of the diffuser. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Step 1 — Classification of Diffusers:**

| Type | Description | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| Conical / Pyramidal | Symmetric expansion | Main HVAC trunk ducts |
| Wall (Curved) | One wall curves, other straight | Defrost outlets, foot vents |
| Sudden Expansion | Sharp-edged step | Blower-to-plenum transitions |
| Annular | Flow between two concentric surfaces | Blower wheels, jet engines |
| Aerodynamic Body | Shaped underbody channels | Sports car rear diffusers |

**Step 2 — Conical Diffuser Features:**
- Symmetric expansion in all directions.
- Pressure recovery depends on **divergence half-angle** (optimum ~7° for subsonic air).
- Simple to manufacture, easy to integrate into duct assemblies.
- Suffers from **flow separation at high angles** (>12°).

**Step 3 — Wall Diffuser Features:**
- Asymmetric expansion: one wall diverges, the other remains parallel.
- Used where space constraints prevent symmetric expansion (e.g., flat defrost ducts along the windshield cowl).
- Better pressure recovery per unit length than a poorly designed conical diffuser.
- Enables **directional flow** toward specific cabin zones.

**Step 4 — Comparison Summary:**

| Parameter | Conical | Wall |
|---|---|---|
| Symmetry | Yes | No |
| Pressure recovery | High (if angle is optimal) | Moderate to high |
| Length for same $A_2/A_1$ | Longer | Shorter |
| Risk of separation | Higher at large angles | Lower |
| Typical use | Plenum-to-duct | Defrost, side vents |

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Given:**
- $\rho = 1.2$ kg/m³
- $V_1 = 25$ m/s
- $A_1 = 0.005$ m²
- $A_2/A_1 = 3.0$ (so $A_2 = 0.015$ m²)
- $\eta_d = 0.80$
- Half-angle $\alpha = 7°$

**Step 1 — Outlet Velocity:**

$$
V_2 = V_1 \cdot \frac{A_1}{A_2} = 25 \times \frac{1}{3} = 8.333 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Ideal Pressure Rise:**

$$
\Delta P_{\text{ideal}} = \frac{1}{2} \times 1.2 \times \left( 25^2 - 8.333^2 \right) = 0.6 \times \left( 625 - 69.44 \right) = 0.6 \times 555.56 = 333.33 \text{ Pa}
$$

**Step 3 — Actual Pressure Rise:**

$$
\Delta P_{\text{actual}} = \eta_d \times \Delta P_{\text{ideal}} = 0.80 \times 333.33 = 266.67 \text{ Pa}
$$

**Step 4 — Length of Diffuser:**
For a **conical diffuser**, the relationship between length, half-angle, and area ratio is:

$$ L = \frac{D_1}{2 \tan \alpha} \left[ \sqrt{\frac{A_2}{A_1}} - 1 \right] $$

First, calculate the equivalent diameter at the inlet:

$$ D_1 = \sqrt{\frac{4 A_1}{\pi}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 0.005}{\pi}} = \sqrt{0.006366} = 0.0798 \text{ m} \approx 79.8 \text{ mm} $$

Then, compute the length:

$$
L = \frac{0.0798}{2 \tan 7°} \times \left( \sqrt{3.0} - 1 \right) = \frac{0.0798}{2 \times 0.1228} \times \left( 1.732 - 1 \right) = \frac{0.0798}{0.2456} \times 0.732 = 0.325 \times 0.732 \approx 0.238 \text{ m}
$$

**Final Answers:** $V_2 \approx 8.33$ m/s, $\Delta P_{\text{actual}} \approx 266.67$ Pa, $L \approx 0.238$ m (≈ 238 mm).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit conversions are the #1 cause of lost marks.** KTU paper-setters expect density in **kg/m³**, diameter in **metres**, and pressure in **Pascals** (not kPa, not bar, not mm H₂O).
> 2. **Watch the area ratio exponent:** For a *circular* duct, $A_1/A_2 = (D_1/D_2)^2$. If you mistakenly use the diameter ratio directly (no square), you will get **four times** the wrong answer.
> 3. **Efficiency is a decimal:** $70\% = 0.70$, not $70$. Mixing these up gives a 100-fold error.
> 4. **Forgotten density step:** Students often plug in $\rho = 1.2$ kg/m³ for "STP" without checking the temperature in the problem. Always use $\rho = P/(RT)$ unless the problem explicitly states the density.
> 5. **Half-angle vs full-angle confusion:** A "7° divergence" sometimes refers to the **full angle** (2α = 7°, so α = 3.5°) and sometimes to the **half-angle**. KTU typically uses the **half-angle** definition — confirm from the problem statement.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Read Before Exam**

- A **diffuser** is a **diverging passage** that **decelerates flow** and **rises static pressure**.
- The fundamental equation pair: **Continuity** ($\rho A V = \text{const}$) + **Bernoulli** ($P + \frac{1}{2}\rho V^2 = \text{const}$).
- **Outlet velocity formula:** $V_2 = V_1 \cdot \dfrac{A_1}{A_2}$.
- **Ideal pressure rise:** $\Delta P = \dfrac{1}{2}\rho\left( V_1^2 - V_2^2 \right)$.
- **Actual pressure rise:** $\Delta P_{\text{actual}} = \eta_d \cdot \Delta P_{\text{ideal}}$.
- **Pressure recovery coefficient:** $C_p = \eta_d \left[ 1 - \left( \dfrac{A_1}{A_2} \right)^2 \right]$.
- **Optimum divergence half-angle for subsonic flow:** **5°–12°** (peak efficiency around 7°).
- **Circular duct area ratio:** $\dfrac{A_1}{A_2} = \left( \dfrac{D_1}{D_2} \right)^2$.
- **Diffuser efficiency range in automotive HVAC:** typically **60%–90%**; higher for well-designed ducts.
- **Automotive applications:** dashboard vents, defrost outlets, rear AC ducts, plenum chambers, and **aerodynamic underbody diffusers**.
- **Failure mode:** Divergence angle too large → **flow separation** → efficiency drops sharply, possibly to zero.
- **Energy loss direction:** Real diffusers always have $\eta_d < 1$; the lost energy is dissipated as **heat due to viscous friction and turbulence**.
- **Nozzle vs Diffuser — at a glance:** Converging = nozzle (accelerates), Diverging = diffuser (decelerates). They are thermodynamic opposites.
- **Always convert units first** before plugging into equations (mm → m, °C → K, kPa → Pa).
- **Density of air** at standard cabin conditions: $\rho \approx 1.18$ to $1.20$ kg/m³; use $\rho = P/(RT)$ when the temperature is given.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ventilation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT523 - AUTOMOTIVE REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Ventilation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Automotive Ventilation** is the controlled process of supplying conditioned or unconditioned outside (ambient) air into the vehicle passenger compartment to maintain acceptable levels of oxygen (O₂), carbon dioxide (CO₂), humidity, temperature, and to remove odors, fumes, moisture, and stale air from the cabin. In the context of KTU's PEAUT523 syllabus, ventilation is treated as a subsystem of the broader **HVAC (Heating, Ventilation, and Air Conditioning)** architecture, distinct from refrigeration (cooling) and heating operations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition:** Ventilation refers to the introduction of *fresh outside air* into the passenger compartment, irrespective of whether heating or cooling is being performed. The metric that quantifies it is the **Fresh Air Ratio (FAR)** = (Mass of outside air supplied) / (Total mass of air supplied to the cabin).

The KTU 2024 scheme explicitly identifies the following ventilation-related parameters as examinable:

- **Fresh air intake (outside air mode) vs. recirculation mode**
- **Air distribution modes** (face, bi-level, foot, defrost)
- **Ventilation efficiency** and **air change rate (ACH)**
- **Cabin air filtration** (particulate and activated carbon filters)
- **Blower and fan performance** coupled with duct design

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are sitting inside a closed car on a hot summer day with the windows rolled up. After about 15 minutes, the air feels stuffy, your head begins to feel slightly heavy, and moisture (fog) starts forming on the windshield. This happens because your body consumes O₂, exhales CO₂, and releases moisture — but the car is a sealed box with no fresh air supply. 

**Ventilation is the "lungs" of your car.** Just as your lungs constantly exchange stale air (CO₂) for fresh air (O₂), the ventilation system in a car exchanges stale cabin air for fresh outside air. Without it, even the most powerful air conditioner would simply be *re-cooling the same exhausted air*, which would eventually become unhealthy and uncomfortable.

A practical real-world example: the moment you switch your car's AC from **recirculation mode (RECIRC)** to **fresh air mode**, you can often smell the outside ambient air rush in — that is the ventilation damper opening the outside air inlet.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Exams:**
> - **Ventilation** = Air Exchange (Fresh air in, stale air out)
> - **Air Conditioning** = Temperature & Humidity Control
> - **Heating** = Warming the supplied air
> All three operate *simultaneously* in a modern HVAC system, but ventilation is the only one that addresses **air quality**.

### 1.3 Important Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following constants and metrics are standard in KTU 2024 Scheme questions:

- **O₂ concentration threshold (comfort):** > 19.5% by volume
- **CO₂ threshold (ASRAE/ASHRAE 62.1):** < 1000 ppm for acceptable indoor air quality
- **Recommended cabin air change rate:** **15 to 20 ACH (Air Changes per Hour)** for passenger cars
- **Standard passenger O₂ consumption:** ~0.5 L/min (at rest)
- **CO₂ exhaled per person:** ~0.3 L/min
- **Standard fresh air supply per occupant:** **7.5 L/s (≈ 450 L/min)** per person (per ASHRAE 62.1 automotive guidelines)
- **Density of air at 25°C, 1 atm:** **ρ = 1.184 kg/m³**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fundamental Operating Principle

The ventilation system operates on the principle of **positive pressure differential ventilation**. The blower fan creates a slightly higher pressure inside the cabin relative to the outside, forcing stale air to escape through:

1. **Designed exhaust ports** (usually at the rear quarter panels or trunk)
2. **Door seals and body gaps** (intentional leakage paths)

The governing equation for volumetric flow is:

$$Q_{vent} = \frac{V_{cabin} \times N_{ach}}{3600}$$

where:
- $Q_{vent}$ = Ventilation volumetric flow rate (m³/s)
- $V_{cabin}$ = Passenger compartment internal volume (m³)
- $N_{ach}$ = Air changes per hour (typically 15–20)

### 2.2 The Two Operational Modes of Ventilation

#### A. Fresh Air Mode (Outside Air Mode)
- The **recirculation door/damper** is in the **closed** position for recirculation.
- The **fresh air inlet door** is in the **open** position.
- 100% outside air enters through the cowl grille (typically at the base of the windshield).
- This mode is used when:
  - Defogging/defrosting the windshield (high humidity outside must be removed)
  - Reducing CO₂ buildup with multiple passengers
  - Removing odors from the cabin

#### B. Recirculation Mode (Recirc Mode)
- The **recirculation door** is **open**, drawing air from inside the cabin.
- The **fresh air inlet** is **closed** (or partially closed).
- This mode is used when:
  - Maximum cooling is required (avoiding hot outside air)
  - Driving through polluted/traffic-heavy areas
  - Quick cabin temperature stabilization

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Point:** Prolonged use of recirculation mode (>15–20 minutes) is **not recommended** because CO₂ levels rise and O₂ depletes. Most modern vehicles have an **automatic mode** that periodically switches to fresh air.

### 2.3 The Bi-Level Ventilation Concept

Modern automotive HVAC systems employ **bi-level** or **tri-level** air distribution, where air is simultaneously delivered at two or three different temperatures to multiple outlets:

| Outlet Zone | Primary Function | Air Temperature |
|-------------|------------------|-----------------|
| **Defrost vents** | Clear windshield fog | Hot |
| **Face vents** | Direct cooling to occupants | Cold |
| **Foot vents** | Warm feet, general heating | Warm |
| **Side demister vents** | Clear side windows | Hot |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **S.No** | **Formula / Parameter** | **Equation** | **Units** | **Application** |
|----------|-------------------------|--------------|-----------|----------------|
| 1 | Air Change Rate (ACH) | $N_{ach} = \dfrac{Q_{vent} \times 3600}{V_{cabin}}$ | h⁻¹ | Quantifies how many times cabin air is replaced per hour |
| 2 | Ventilation Mass Flow | $\dot{m}_{vent} = \rho_{air} \times Q_{vent}$ | kg/s | Mass of fresh air supplied |
| 3 | Fresh Air Ratio (FAR) | $FAR = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{fresh}}{\dot{m}_{fresh} + \dot{m}_{recirc}}$ | dimensionless | Fraction of outside air in total supply |
| 4 | CO₂ Generation Rate (per occupant) | $\dot{V}_{CO_2} = 0.3 \times 10^{-3} \times N_{occ}$ | m³/min | Rate of CO₂ exhaled |
| 5 | Required Fresh Air per Occupant | $Q_{req} = 7.5 \times 10^{-3} \times N_{occ}$ | m³/s | ASHRAE minimum per person |
| 6 | Cabin Pressure Differential | $\Delta P = \dfrac{\rho v^2}{2}$ | Pa | Pressure created by blower to drive ventilation |
| 7 | Ventilation Heat Load | $Q_{vent,heat} = \dot{m}_{vent} \times c_p \times (T_{out} - T_{cabin})$ | W | Heat added by hot outside air |
| 8 | Humidity Ratio (ventilation) | $W = 0.622 \times \dfrac{P_v}{P_{atm} - P_v}$ | kg water/kg dry air | Moisture content of incoming air |
| 9 | Time to Replace Cabin Air | $t = \dfrac{V_{cabin}}{Q_{vent}}$ | s | Time for one full air exchange |
| 10 | Blower Power (idealized) | $P_{blower} = \Delta P \times Q_{vent}$ | W | Power required by ventilation fan |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units Convention (KTU Standard):** Use SI units throughout. $c_p$ of air = **1005 J/kg·K**, $R$ for air = **287 J/kg·K**.

### 2.5 Why Ventilation Matters in Engineering Practice

Ventilation is not just a comfort feature — it is a **safety-critical** system. A KTU examiner often tests whether students understand that:

1. **Defogging** requires warm, *dry* (low humidity) outside air — hence fresh air mode is essential.
2. **Cabin air quality** is regulated by international standards (ISO 17772, ASHRAE 62.1).
3. **Heating load** calculation in winter must include the **ventilation (infiltration) load** — cold outside air entering the cabin.
4. **Cabin air filters** (particulate + activated carbon) protect occupants from PM2.5, pollen, and exhaust fumes.
5. **Acoustic design** of ventilation ducts must minimize blower noise (NVH consideration).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Required Ventilation Flow Rate from CO₂ Balance

**Problem Context (Typical KTU Question):**
A passenger car with cabin volume $V_{cabin} = 2.5 \, \text{m}^3$ is occupied by 4 people. Each person exhales CO₂ at a rate of $\dot{V}_{CO_2} = 0.3 \, \text{L/min}$. The maximum allowable CO₂ concentration in the cabin is $C_{max} = 1000 \, \text{ppm}$. Ambient CO₂ concentration is $C_{amb} = 400 \, \text{ppm}$. Determine the minimum fresh air supply rate $Q_{vent}$ required.

**Step 1 — Set up the CO₂ mass balance on the cabin (steady state):**

At steady state, the rate of CO₂ entering the cabin from occupants equals the rate of CO₂ leaving via ventilation exhaust:

$$\dot{V}_{CO_2, \, gen} = Q_{vent} \times (C_{max} - C_{amb})$$

**Step 2 — Convert CO₂ generation rate to SI units:**

The total CO₂ generation by 4 occupants:

$$\dot{V}_{CO_2, \, gen} = N_{occ} \times \dot{V}_{CO_2, \, per \, person}$$

$$\dot{V}_{CO_2, \, gen} = 4 \times 0.3 \, \text{L/min} = 1.2 \, \text{L/min}$$

Converting to cubic meters per second:

$$\dot{V}_{CO_2, \, gen} = 1.2 \times \frac{1 \, \text{m}^3}{1000 \, \text{L}} \times \frac{1 \, \text{min}}{60 \, \text{s}}$$

$$\dot{V}_{CO_2, \, gen} = 2.0 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 3 — Express the concentration difference in dimensionless form:**

Converting ppm to volumetric fraction (1 ppm = $10^{-6}$):

$$C_{max} - C_{amb} = (1000 - 400) \times 10^{-6} = 6.0 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $Q_{vent}$:**

Rearranging the mass balance equation:

$$Q_{vent} = \frac{\dot{V}_{CO_2, \, gen}}{C_{max} - C_{amb}}$$

$$Q_{vent} = \frac{2.0 \times 10^{-5}}{6.0 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$Q_{vent} = 0.0333 \, \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to more meaningful units (L/s and ACH):**

$$Q_{vent} = 0.0333 \times 1000 = 33.3 \, \text{L/s}$$

Now compute the Air Change Rate (ACH):

$$N_{ach} = \frac{Q_{vent} \times 3600}{V_{cabin}}$$

$$N_{ach} = \frac{0.0333 \times 3600}{2.5}$$

$$N_{ach} = 48 \, \text{h}^{-1}$$

**Step 6 — Sanity check and interpretation:**

An ACH of 48 h⁻¹ means the cabin air is completely replaced every 75 seconds. This is **higher than the typical 15–20 ACH** recommendation, but reasonable for a 4-occupant scenario. This proves that with more occupants, the ventilation requirement scales linearly.

$$\boxed{Q_{vent} = 0.0333 \, \text{m}^3/\text{s} \approx 33.3 \, \text{L/s}, \quad N_{ach} = 48 \, \text{h}^{-1}}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Ventilation Heat Load on the HVAC System

**Problem Context:**
A car is being ventilated with outside air at $T_{out} = 38°C$ (typical summer noon in Kerala). The cabin temperature is maintained at $T_{cabin} = 24°C$ by the AC. The fresh air supply rate is $Q_{vent} = 50 \, \text{L/s}$. Calculate the additional heat load imposed on the refrigeration system due to ventilation alone.

**Step 1 — Identify given parameters:**

$$Q_{vent} = 50 \, \text{L/s} = 0.050 \, \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$T_{out} = 38°C = 311 \, \text{K}$$

$$T_{cabin} = 24°C = 297 \, \text{K}$$

$$\rho_{air} = 1.184 \, \text{kg/m}^3 \quad \text{(at 38°C)}$$

$$c_{p,air} = 1005 \, \text{J/kg·K}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the mass flow rate of incoming fresh air:**

$$\dot{m}_{vent} = \rho_{air} \times Q_{vent}$$

$$\dot{m}_{vent} = 1.184 \times 0.050 = 0.0592 \, \text{kg/s}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the sensible heat transfer equation:**

The ventilation (infiltration) heat load is:

$$Q_{vent, \, heat} = \dot{m}_{vent} \times c_p \times (T_{out} - T_{cabin})$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$Q_{vent, \, heat} = 0.0592 \times 1005 \times (38 - 24)$$

$$Q_{vent, \, heat} = 0.0592 \times 1005 \times 14$$

$$Q_{vent, \, heat} = 833.0 \, \text{W}$$

**Step 5 — Final answer and interpretation:**

$$\boxed{Q_{vent, \, heat} \approx 833 \, \text{W} \approx 0.83 \, \text{kW}}$$

This means the AC compressor must remove an **additional 833 W of heat** simply to cool the fresh incoming air. In contrast, if the system were in **recirculation mode**, this load would be near zero (since cabin air is already at 24°C). This is precisely why drivers feel cooler cooling in recirc mode — but at the cost of air quality.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Time Required to Flush the Cabin (Purge Time)

**Step 1 — Set up the exponential decay model:**

The concentration of a contaminant (e.g., CO₂ or VOCs) in the cabin after switching to fresh air mode follows:

$$C(t) = C_0 \cdot e^{-\frac{Q_{vent}}{V_{cabin}} \cdot t} + C_{amb}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for time t to reach a target concentration $C_{target}$:**

$$C(t) - C_{amb} = (C_0 - C_{amb}) \cdot e^{-\frac{Q_{vent}}{V_{cabin}} \cdot t}$$

Taking natural log on both sides:

$$\ln\left(\frac{C(t) - C_{amb}}{C_0 - C_{amb}}\right) = -\frac{Q_{vent}}{V_{cabin}} \cdot t$$

$$t = \frac{V_{cabin}}{Q_{vent}} \cdot \ln\left(\frac{C_0 - C_{amb}}{C(t) - C_{amb}}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Numerical example:**

Suppose $V_{cabin} = 2.5 \, \text{m}^3$, $Q_{vent} = 0.05 \, \text{m}^3/\text{s}$, initial CO₂ = 2000 ppm, ambient = 400 ppm, target = 600 ppm:

$$t = \frac{2.5}{0.05} \cdot \ln\left(\frac{2000 - 400}{600 - 400}\right)$$

$$t = 50 \cdot \ln\left(\frac{1600}{200}\right)$$

$$t = 50 \cdot \ln(8) = 50 \times 2.079 = 103.97 \, \text{s}$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion:**

$$\boxed{t \approx 104 \, \text{seconds} \approx 1.7 \, \text{minutes}}$$

It takes about **1.7 minutes** of fresh air mode to bring CO₂ from 2000 ppm to a comfortable 600 ppm.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Mode Selection Decision Table

| **Driving Scenario** | **Recommended Mode** | **Reasoning** |
|----------------------|----------------------|---------------|
| Hot summer, single occupant | Recirculation | Maximum cooling efficiency |
| Hot summer, 4 occupants | Fresh Air (periodic) | Avoid CO₂ buildup |
| Cold winter, dry climate | Fresh Air (foot mode) | Prevent windshield fogging |
| Highway driving, heavy traffic | Recirculation | Avoid exhaust fumes |
| Defogging active | Fresh Air (defrost) | Dry outside air clears fog |
| Pollution/PM2.5 high | Recirculation + filter | Health protection |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Automotive Ventilation System

The following Mermaid diagram maps the functional flow of a typical **bi-level automatic HVAC ventilation system**, showing how outside air, recirculated air, and conditioned air interact to supply the passenger cabin.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Outside Air Inlet Cowl Grille] --> A2[Fresh Air Pre-Filter]
    A2 --> A3[Fresh Air Damper Door]
    A4[Cabin Interior Recirculation Inlet] --> A5[Recirculation Damper Door]
    A3 --> A6[Blower Fan Assembly]
    A5 --> A6
    A6 --> A7[Cabin Air Filter Particulate and Activated Carbon]
    A7 --> A8[Evaporator Coil Cooling and Dehumidification]
    A8 --> A9[Heater Core Hot Coolant Heat Exchange]
    A9 --> A10[Air Mix Door Temperature Blending]
    A10 --> A11[Mode Door Distribution Control]
    A11 --> A12[Defrost Vents Windshield]
    A11 --> A13[Face Vents Instrument Panel]
    A11 --> A14[Foot Vents Floor Level]
    A12 --> A15[Passenger Cabin Interior Space]
    A13 --> A15
    A14 --> A15
    A15 --> A16[Exhaust Ports Rear Quarter Panels]
    A15 --> A4
    A3 -.Fresh Air Mode.-> A3
    A5 -.Recirculation Mode.-> A5
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- The **A3 Fresh Air Damper** and **A5 Recirculation Damper** operate in opposition — only one is fully open at a time.
- The **A10 Air Mix Door** determines the blend of hot air (from heater) and cold air (from evaporator) — this is the heart of temperature control.
- The **A11 Mode Door** directs the conditioned air to the appropriate vents.

### 4.2 Process Flow: Ventilation State Machine

The diagram below illustrates the **operational state transitions** of the ventilation system, as governed by the HVAC control unit (manual or automatic).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[System OFF State] -->|Ignition ON| S1[Idle No Blower]
    S1 -->|Driver Selects Mode| S2{Fresh Air or Recirculation}
    S2 -->|FRESH Selected| S3[Fresh Air Mode Outside Air Only]
    S2 -->|RECIRC Selected| S4[Recirculation Mode Cabin Air Only]
    S2 -->|AUTO Selected| S5[Automatic Mode ECU Logic]
    S3 --> S6[Defrost Active Auto Switch to Fresh]
    S4 --> S7[Timer Elapsed 15 to 20 min Auto Switch to Fresh]
    S5 --> S8[CO2 Sensor Feedback Active]
    S8 -->|CO2 above 1000 ppm| S3
    S8 -->|CO2 below 600 ppm| S4
    S6 --> S3
    S7 --> S3
    S3 --> S9[Blower Speed Control 1 to 7]
    S4 --> S9
    S9 --> S10[Air Distributed to Cabin]
    S10 --> S0
```

**Key Takeaway:** The ventilation system is rarely static — modern vehicles use **sensor feedback (CO₂, humidity, temperature)** and **timers** to dynamically switch between fresh and recirculation modes for optimal comfort and air quality.

### 4.3 Airflow Path Through the HVAC Box (Cross-Sectional View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1[Outside Air Inlet Top of HVAC Box] --> B2[Fresh Air Flap Closed in Recirc]
    B3[Recirculation Inlet Bottom of HVAC Box] --> B4[Recirc Flap Open in Recirc Mode]
    B2 --> B5[Common Plenum Above Blower]
    B4 --> B5
    B5 --> B6[Centrifugal Blower Fan]
    B6 --> B7[Evaporator Core Cold Side]
    B7 --> B8[Heater Core Hot Side]
    B8 --> B9[Air Mix Door Position Varies]
    B9 --> B10[Mode Door Outlet Selector]
    B10 --> B11[Defrost Outlet]
    B10 --> B12[Face Outlet]
    B10 --> B13[Foot Outlet]
```

This shows the **physical order** of components encountered by the air: filter → blower → evaporator → heater → mix door → mode door → outlet.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1: Define Automotive Ventilation. Differentiate between fresh air mode and recirculation mode.

**Model Answer (KTU Board Pattern):**

> **Definition:** Automotive ventilation is the controlled introduction of outside air into the passenger compartment to maintain acceptable air quality by diluting CO₂, moisture, odors, and contaminants, while simultaneously exhausting stale cabin air.
>
> **Fresh Air Mode:** The fresh air inlet door is **open** and the recirculation door is **closed**. 100% outside ambient air enters the cabin. Used during defogging and when multiple occupants are present.
>
> **Recirculation Mode:** The fresh air door is **closed** and the recirculation door is **open**. Only cabin air is conditioned and recirculated. Used for maximum cooling efficiency and to block polluted outside air.
>
> *(Conclusion line mandatory for full 3 marks:)* Ventilation is essential for occupant health and comfort, and modern HVAC systems use sensor-based automatic mode-switching to balance both.

**Marks Distribution:** Definition (1) + Fresh Air explanation (1) + Recirculation explanation (1).

---

#### Question 2: State ASHRAE's recommended fresh air supply per occupant and the maximum allowable cabin CO₂ concentration.

**Model Answer:**

The ASHRAE Standard 62.1 recommends a minimum fresh air supply of **7.5 L/s (≈ 450 L/min) per occupant** for vehicle cabins. The maximum allowable CO₂ concentration for acceptable indoor air quality is **1000 ppm** above the ambient baseline (~400 ppm). Maintaining these values requires an **Air Change Rate (ACH) of 15–20 per hour** for a typical sedan cabin.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**
A passenger car cabin has a volume of **2.8 m³** and is occupied by **4 persons**. Each occupant exhales CO₂ at a rate of **0.3 L/min**. The ambient CO₂ concentration is **400 ppm** and the maximum allowable cabin concentration is **1000 ppm**. Determine:
(i) the minimum required fresh air supply rate in L/s, and
(ii) the resulting Air Change Rate (ACH) in h⁻¹.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Total CO₂ generation:**

$$\dot{V}_{CO_2} = 4 \times 0.3 = 1.2 \, \text{L/min} = 2.0 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 2 — Concentration differential:**

$$\Delta C = (1000 - 400) \times 10^{-6} = 6.0 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 3 — Apply steady-state mass balance:**

$$Q_{vent} = \frac{\dot{V}_{CO_2}}{\Delta C} = \frac{2.0 \times 10^{-5}}{6.0 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.0333 \, \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$\boxed{Q_{vent} = 33.3 \, \text{L/s}}$$

**Step 4 — Compute ACH:**

$$N_{ach} = \frac{0.0333 \times 3600}{2.8} = \frac{119.88}{2.8} = 42.8 \, \text{h}^{-1}$$

$$\boxed{N_{ach} \approx 42.8 \, \text{h}^{-1}}$$

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**
The same car is now driven in summer with outside air at **40°C** and 60% relative humidity, while the cabin is maintained at **22°C**. The fresh air supply rate is **40 L/s**. Calculate:
(i) the mass flow rate of fresh air,
(ii) the sensible ventilation heat load on the AC system, and
(iii) the additional compressor work required if the COP of the refrigeration cycle is 2.5.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Mass flow rate:**

$$\rho_{air} \text{ at } 40°C \approx 1.127 \, \text{kg/m}^3$$

$$\dot{m}_{vent} = 1.127 \times 0.040 = 0.0451 \, \text{kg/s}$$

**Step 2 — Sensible heat load:**

$$Q_{sensible} = \dot{m}_{vent} \times c_p \times \Delta T$$

$$Q_{sensible} = 0.0451 \times 1005 \times (40 - 22) = 0.0451 \times 1005 \times 18$$

$$\boxed{Q_{sensible} \approx 815.7 \, \text{W}}$$

**Step 3 — Compressor work:**

$$W_{comp} = \frac{Q_{sensible}}{COP} = \frac{815.7}{2.5} = 326.3 \, \text{W}$$

$$\boxed{W_{comp} \approx 326 \, \text{W}}$$

**Conclusion:** The ventilation load alone requires ~326 W of additional compressor power — illustrating why recirc mode saves fuel.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**
Explain the **working principle of a bi-level ventilation system** in a modern automotive HVAC unit, with a neat sketch of the airflow path. Discuss the role of the air mix door and mode door.

**Model Answer Outline:**

- **Bi-level definition:** Simultaneous delivery of air at two different temperatures to face vents (cold) and foot vents (warm).
- **Airflow path:** Fresh air intake → filter → blower → evaporator → heater core → air mix door → mode door → outlets.
- **Air mix door:** Controls the blend of cold (post-evaporator) and hot (post-heater) air; position determines the final supply air temperature.
- **Mode door:** Routes the conditioned air to defrost, face, foot, or bi-level outlets.
- **Driver control:** Knob/touchscreen selects mode; ECU actuates vacuum servos or electric motors.
- **Sketch reference:** Use the Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.1.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**
A car cabin has a volume of 3.0 m³. The blower supplies fresh air at a rate of 60 L/s. If the cabin initially has CO₂ at 1500 ppm and ambient is 400 ppm, determine the time required to reduce cabin CO₂ to 600 ppm using the exponential decay model.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Apply the standard purge-time equation:**

$$t = \frac{V_{cabin}}{Q_{vent}} \cdot \ln\left(\frac{C_0 - C_{amb}}{C_{target} - C_{amb}}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$t = \frac{3.0}{0.060} \cdot \ln\left(\frac{1500 - 400}{600 - 400}\right)$$

$$t = 50 \cdot \ln\left(\frac{1100}{200}\right) = 50 \cdot \ln(5.5)$$

$$t = 50 \times 1.7047 = 85.24 \, \text{s}$$

**Step 3 — Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{t \approx 85.2 \, \text{seconds} \approx 1.42 \, \text{minutes}}$$

**Interpretation:** Just over a minute and a half of fresh air mode is sufficient to bring cabin CO₂ from 1500 ppm (drowsy) to 600 ppm (comfortable).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit errors in CO₂ calculations** — forgetting to convert L/min to m³/s, or ppm to a dimensionless fraction. Always show unit conversions explicitly.
> 2. **Using wrong air density** — KTU expects $\rho = 1.184 \, \text{kg/m}^3$ at 25°C or $\rho = 1.127 \, \text{kg/m}^3$ at 40°C, not a generic 1.2 kg/m³ unless explicitly stated.
> 3. **Confusing ventilation with air conditioning** — Ventilation is about *air exchange*, not *cooling*. Marks are lost when students describe cooling coils when asked about fresh air mode.
> 4. **Forgetting the conclusion line** in 3-mark answers — KTU strictly awards 1 mark for the concluding statement.
> 5. **Mixing up damper states** — In **recirc mode**, the *fresh air* damper is **closed**, not the recirculation damper. Examiners mark this strictly.
> 6. **Ignoring the latent heat component** in summer ventilation loads — humidity from outside air adds latent load, not just sensible. Mentioning this earns a bonus mark.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Ventilation ≠ Air Conditioning.** Ventilation is the *exchange* of air; AC is the *conditioning* of air.
- **Two modes:** Fresh Air Mode (100% outside) and Recirculation Mode (cabin air only).
- **ASHRAE minimum:** **7.5 L/s per occupant** of fresh air; max CO₂ = **1000 ppm**.
- **Typical cabin ACH:** **15–20 h⁻¹** for normal operation.
- **Blower always pushes air** — exhaust is passive through gaps and rear ports.
- **Air mix door** controls temperature; **mode door** controls outlet selection.
- **Cabin air filter** (particulate + activated carbon) protects occupants from PM2.5, pollen, and VOCs.
- **Defogging requires fresh air** because dry outside air reduces windshield humidity.
- **Recirc mode saves energy** but degrades air quality — never use it for >15–20 minutes continuously.
- **Ventilation heat load equation:** $Q = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ — must be added to the AC cooling load calculation.
- **CO₂ purge time equation:** $t = \dfrac{V}{Q} \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{C_0 - C_{amb}}{C_t - C_{amb}}\right)$ — frequently tested.
- **Density of air** at standard conditions: $\rho = 1.184 \, \text{kg/m}^3$ (25°C); use temperature-corrected values for hot climate (Kerala: 35–40°C).
- **Automatic HVAC systems** use CO₂, humidity, and in-cabin temperature sensors for mode-switching logic.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Air Noise Level
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAUT523 - AUTOMOTIVE REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Air Noise Level ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Air Noise Level** in an automotive HVAC (Heating, Ventilation, and Air Conditioning) system refers to the unwanted acoustic energy generated primarily by the **forced convection of air** through the various components of the system — namely the blower assembly, evaporator core, heater core, ducts, plenum chambers, louvers, and discharge vents. It is quantified using the **Sound Pressure Level (SPL)**, expressed in **decibels (dB)**, measured against a standardized reference pressure of **$p_0 = 20\ \mu\text{Pa}$** (the threshold of human hearing at 1 kHz). Mathematically: $$L_p = 20 \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{p_{\text{rms}}}{p_0}\right) \quad \text{[dB]}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Automotive cabin noise from HVAC is typically measured in the **A-weighted decibel scale (dBA)** because the A-weighting network approximates the frequency response of the human ear at moderate sound levels (40–60 dB), which is the practical operating range of a vehicle cabin. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are sitting inside a parked car with the AC switched **OFF**. The cabin is silent. Now, you switch the AC fan to **maximum blower speed**. Suddenly, you hear a characteristic **"whoosh"** sound coming from the dashboard vents. That rushing aerodynamic sound — and the low-frequency hum from the blower motor — is the **Air Noise Level**. > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The Wind Tunnel Effect:** > Think of the HVAC duct as a small **wind tunnel**. Just like how a strong gust of wind whistling past a building window creates a "whooo" sound, the air rushing through narrow ducts, past the cold evaporator fins, and out of the vents creates turbulence and pressure fluctuations. These fluctuations vibrate the air molecules → producing **sound waves** → which your ears perceive as **noise**. ### 1.3 Why Air Noise Level is Critical in Automotive AC | Parameter | Cabin Comfort Impact | |---|---| | **Target SPL inside cabin** | **< 50 dBA** at maximum blower speed | | **Acceptable SPL** | 40 – 55 dBA during normal operation | | **Uncomfortable SPL** | > 60 dBA (conversation interference) | | **Excessive SPL** | > 70 dBA (driver fatigue, safety hazard) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Reference Metrics Used:** > - **Reference Sound Pressure:** $p_0 = 20\ \mu\text{Pa} = 2 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{Pa}$ > - **Reference Sound Intensity:** $I_0 = 10^{-12}\ \text{W/m}^2$ > - **Reference Sound Power:** $W_0 = 10^{-12}\ \text{W}$ --- ### 1.4 Physical Sources of Air Noise in an Automotive AC System 1. **Blower Fan (Centrifugal/Sirocco type)** — The **dominant** noise source. Aerodynamic interaction between the rotating impeller blades and the housing volute generates broadband + tonal noise. 2. **Evaporator & Heater Core** — Air forced through narrow finned passages creates turbulent boundary layer noise. 3. **Duct Bends and Elbows** — Sharp turns cause flow separation and vortex shedding. 4. **Mode Doors and Blend Doors** — Partial obstructions create localized turbulence. 5. **Discharge Louvers / Vents** — Final expansion into cabin causes jet noise. 6. **Expansion Valve (TXV)** — Refrigerant hissing may couple with airflow noise. 7. **Plenum Chamber Resonance** — Standing waves form inside the air-distribution box. ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Fundamentals of Sound — Physics Recap Sound is a **mechanical longitudinal wave** propagating through a medium via compression and rarefaction of particles. In the context of HVAC air noise: - The **blower** acts as the **sound source** (acoustic monopole / dipole). - The **ducts and cabin** act as the **transmission medium**. - The **occupant's ear** acts as the **receiver**. The sound radiates as **pressure fluctuations** superimposed on atmospheric pressure. The relevant measurable quantity is the **root-mean-square (rms) sound pressure**, $p_{\text{rms}}$, since the time-averaged value of pure sound is zero. ### 2.2 Why Use the Decibel Scale? The human ear responds to sound **logarithmically**, not linearly. The ratio between the **threshold of pain** (≈ 20 Pa) and the **threshold of hearing** (20 μPa) is **1,000,000 : 1** in pressure — impractical to express linearly. Hence the **Bel** (and its subunit, the **decibel = 1/10 Bel**) was adopted. ### 2.3 The KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Master these formulas — they cover ~95% of the numerical problems in KTU exams on this topic.** | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Sound Pressure Level (SPL)** | $L_p = 20 \log_{10}(p_{\text{rms}} / p_0)$ | dB | $p_0 = 20\ \mu\text{Pa}$ | | 2 | **Sound Power Level (PWL)** | $L_w = 10 \log_{10}(W / W_0)$ | dB | $W_0 = 10^{-12}$ W | | 3 | **Sound Intensity Level (SIL)** | $L_I = 10 \log_{10}(I / I_0)$ | dB | $I_0 = 10^{-12}$ W/m² | | 4 | **Relation: PWL → SPL (free field)** | $L_p = L_w - 20 \log_{10}(r) - 11$ | dB | $r$ = distance in meters | | 5 | **Addition of two incoherent sources** | $L_{p,\text{total}} = 10 \log_{10}(10^{L_{p1}/10} + 10^{L_{p2}/10})$ | dB | Use when sources are uncorrelated | | 6 | **Addition of N equal sources** | $L_{p,\text{total}} = L_p + 10 \log_{10}(N)$ | dB | E.g., 4 fans of 60 dB → 66 dB | | 7 | **A-Weighting Correction (typical)** | See table below | dB | Applied band-by-band | | 8 | **Octave-band center frequencies** | $f_n = 1000 \cdot 2^{(n/3)}$ (n = integer) | Hz | ISO preferred: 31.5, 63, 125, 250, 500, 1k, 2k, 4k, 8k, 16k | | 9 | **NC / PNC Criterion** | Compare octave-band SPL to NC curves | — | Used for HVAC noise rating | | 10 | **Sound Power from Sound Pressure (reverberant)** | $L_w = \bar{L}_p + 10 \log_{10}(S) - 6$ | dB | $S$ = surface area of room/enclosure (m²) | ### 2.4 A-Weighting Network (Simplified Standard Values) | Frequency (Hz) | 31.5 | 63 | 125 | 250 | 500 | 1k | 2k | 4k | 8k | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | A-Weighting (dB) | −39.4 | −26.2 | −16.1 | −8.6 | −3.2 | 0 | +1.2 | +1.0 | −1.1 | > [!IMPORTANT] > The **dBA** value is the **overall SPL after A-weighting each octave band** and then re-combining. This is the figure you will most commonly encounter in automotive cabin noise specifications. ### 2.5 Octave-Band Analysis — The Heart of HVAC Noise Evaluation A single dB number hides the **frequency content** of the noise. Two AC systems could both be 50 dBA, but one might have a "buzzy" 1 kHz tone (irritating) and the other a "smooth" broadband hiss (pleasant). KTU problems and real-world automotive NVH (Noise, Vibration, Harshness) analysis always require **octave-band decomposition**. The **preferred octave bands** (ISO / IEC standard) are: $$31.5,\ 63,\ 125,\ 250,\ 500,\ 1000,\ 2000,\ 4000,\ 8000,\ 16000\ \text{Hz}$$ ### 2.6 NC (Noise Criterion) and PNC Curves The **NC curves** are a family of curves (NC-15, NC-20, ... NC-65) defined by Beranek for rating indoor HVAC noise. The **PNC (Preferred NC) curves** add constraints to avoid low-frequency rumble. > [!NOTE] > **Typical Automotive Cabin Targets:** > - Sedan at idle, low blower → **NC 30 – 35** (very quiet) > - Sedan at highway speed, medium blower → **NC 40 – 50** (acceptable) > - SUV at full blower → **NC 55 – 60** (noticeable but tolerable) ### 2.7 Real-World Utility in Engineering - **OEM Design Phase:** CAE (Computer-Aided Engineering) tools like **STAR-CCM+**, **Fluent**, or **LMS Virtual.Lab Acoustics** are used to predict blower noise *before* prototyping. - **Bench Testing:** Tests are conducted in **semi-anechoic chambers** with calibrated condenser microphones and a **Head & Torso Simulator (HATS)** placed in the driver's seat. - **Field Validation:** Production vehicles are measured using **Bruel & Kjaer** or **HEAD Acoustics** equipment while driving on standardized road surfaces (ISO road noise standards). ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: From Sound Pressure to Sound Pressure Level **Step 1 — Start with the definition of sound intensity.** The instantaneous intensity of a sound wave is the product of instantaneous pressure and particle velocity: $$I(t) = p(t) \cdot u(t)$$ **Step 2 — For a plane progressive wave in a fluid**, the relationship simplifies to: $$I = \frac{p_{\text{rms}}^2}{\rho \cdot c}$$ where: - $\rho$ = density of air ≈ **1.2 kg/m³** at 20 °C - $c$ = speed of sound in air ≈ **343 m/s** at 20 °C - $p_{\text{rms}}$ = root-mean-square acoustic pressure > The product $\rho c$ is called the **characteristic acoustic impedance** of air, and equals approximately **$\mathbf{413\ \text{Pa}\cdot\text{s/m}}$** (Rayls) at 20 °C. **Step 3 — The reference intensity** is defined as: $$I_0 = 10^{-12}\ \text{W/m}^2 \quad \text{(threshold of human hearing at 1 kHz)}$$ **Step 4 — Define the Sound Intensity Level (SIL) in decibels:** $$L_I = 10 \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right) \quad \text{[dB]}$$ **Step 5 — Substitute** the intensity expression from Step 2 into Step 4: $$L_I = 10 \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{p_{\text{rms}}^2}{\rho c \cdot I_0}\right)$$ $$L_I = 10 \cdot \log_{10}\left(\left(\frac{p_{\text{rms}}}{p_0}\right)^2 \cdot \frac{p_0^2}{\rho c \cdot I_0}\right)$$ **Step 6 — By definition, the reference quantities are chosen such that** $p_0^2 / (\rho c \cdot I_0) = 1$. This means $p_0 = \sqrt{\rho c \cdot I_0}$. Verification: $$p_0 = \sqrt{413 \cdot 10^{-12}} = \sqrt{4.13 \times 10^{-10}} = 2.03 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{Pa} = 20.3\ \mu\text{Pa} \checkmark$$ This confirms the standard reference pressure of **$20\ \mu\text{Pa}$**. **Step 7 — Simplify using logarithm property** $\log(a^2) = 2 \log(a)$: $$L_I = 20 \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{p_{\text{rms}}}{p_0}\right) = L_p$$ Therefore, **$L_I = L_p$** in a free progressive wave — this is why both are quoted in dB with the same numerical value. ### 3.2 Derivation: Addition of Two Incoherent Sound Sources **Scenario:** Two identical blowers each produce **70 dB** at the driver's ear. What is the total SPL? **Step 1 — Convert each dB value back to linear intensity ratio:** $$I_1 = I_0 \cdot 10^{L_{p1}/10} = I_0 \cdot 10^{70/10} = I_0 \cdot 10^7$$ **Step 2 — For two identical sources, total intensity is additive** (incoherent sources): $$I_{\text{total}} = I_1 + I_2 = 2 \cdot I_0 \cdot 10^7$$ **Step 3 — Convert back to dB:** $$L_{p,\text{total}} = 10 \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{2 \cdot I_0 \cdot 10^7}{I_0}\right) = 10 \cdot \log_{10}(2 \times 10^7)$$ $$L_{p,\text{total}} = 10 \cdot (\log_{10} 2 + 7) = 10 \cdot (0.301 + 7) = 73.01\ \text{dB}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight (Examiner's favourite!):** Adding two equal sources increases SPL by only **+3 dB**, not +6 dB. Doubling the number of equal sources adds 3 dB each time (e.g., 4 sources → +6 dB, 8 sources → +9 dB). ### 3.3 Solved Analytical Example (KTU Exam Style) **Problem:** A vehicle's HVAC system has a single blower producing **58 dB SPL** at the driver's ear position. The design team proposes installing **two** additional identical blowers (3 total) to increase airflow. Calculate: 1. The new total SPL if all three blowers run simultaneously. 2. The percentage increase in sound intensity. **Solution:** **Part (a):** Using the formula for $N$ equal uncorrelated sources: $$L_{p,\text{total}} = L_p + 10 \cdot \log_{10}(N)$$ $$L_{p,\text{total}} = 58 + 10 \cdot \log_{10}(3)$$ $$L_{p,\text{total}} = 58 + 10 \cdot 0.4771 = 58 + 4.77 = 62.77\ \text{dB}$$ **Part (b):** The intensity ratio: $$\frac{I_{\text{new}}}{I_{\text{old}}} = 10^{(L_{p,\text{new}} - L_{p,\text{old}})/10} = 10^{(62.77 - 58)/10} = 10^{0.477}$$ $$\frac{I_{\text{new}}}{I_{\text{old}}} = 3.0$$ **Percentage increase in intensity = (3 − 1) × 100% = 200%** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Takeaway:** Although the **sound intensity triples** (200% increase), the **perceived loudness** (in dB) increases by less than 5 dB. The human ear perceives a 10 dB increase as a "doubling of loudness." So 3 fans at full power are perceptually only marginally louder than 1 fan, but deliver 3× the airflow — **a good engineering trade-off** when cooling demand is high. ### 3.4 Derivation: SPL to PWL Conversion in a Free Field For a **point source radiating in a free field** (anechoic environment), the sound intensity at distance $r$ is: $$I = \frac{W}{4 \pi r^2}$$ Taking log₁₀ of both ratios: $$L_I = L_w - 10 \cdot \log_{10}(4 \pi r^2) = L_w - 10 \cdot \log_{10}(4 \pi) - 20 \cdot \log_{10}(r)$$ Since $10 \cdot \log_{10}(4\pi) \approx 10.99 \approx 11$ dB: $$L_p = L_w - 20 \cdot \log_{10}(r) - 11$$ > This is the **free-field inverse square law** in dB form — the SPL drops by **6 dB for every doubling of distance** (since $20 \log_{10}(2) = 6.02$ dB). ### 3.5 Worked Example — Free Field Calculation **Problem:** A blower has a Sound Power Level of **75 dB**. Calculate the SPL at **(i)** 0.5 m and **(ii)** 2.0 m from the source. **Solution (i) — at r = 0.5 m:** $$L_p = 75 - 20 \cdot \log_{10}(0.5) - 11 = 75 - (-6.02) - 11 = 70.02\ \text{dB}$$ **Solution (ii) — at r = 2.0 m:** $$L_p = 75 - 20 \cdot \log_{10}(2.0) - 11 = 75 - 6.02 - 11 = 57.98\ \text{dB}$$ **Verification of 6 dB drop per doubling:** Distance increased by 4× → SPL should drop by $20 \log_{10}(4) = 12.04$ dB. Indeed: $70.02 - 57.98 = 12.04$ dB ✓ ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Air Noise Generation & Transmission Path in an Automotive HVAC System ```mermaid flowchart LR src1[BLOWER MOTOR
Mechanical Driver Source] src2[IMPELLER
Aerodynamic Broadband Noise] src3[VOLUTE HOUSING
Tonal Whine BPF] src4[EVAPORATOR CORE
Turbulent Boundary Layer] src5[DUCTS BENDS
Flow Separation Vortices] src6[EXPANSION VALVE
Refrigerant Hiss Coupling] src7[DISCHARGE LOUVERS
Jet Mixing Noise] cabin[VEHICLE CABIN
Reverberant Field] ear[DRIVERS EAR
Receiver dBA SPL] src1 --> src2 src2 --> src3 src3 --> src4 src4 --> src5 src5 --> cabin cabin --> ear src6 -.-> cabin src7 --> cabin ``` ### 4.2 Noise Control Strategy Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD problem[HIGH CABIN AIR NOISE
SPL greater than 55 dBA] problem --> q1{NOISE DOMINANT
FREQUENCY?} q1 -->|Low freq below 250 Hz| LF[BLOWER IMBALANCE
MOTOR BEARING] q1 -->|Mid freq 500 to 2000 Hz| MF[IMPELLER TONAL
DUCT RESONANCE] q1 -->|High freq above 4 kHz| HF[AIRFLOW TURBULENCE
FIN VIBRATION] LF --> sol1[REBALANCE IMPELLER
USE SOFT MOUNTS] MF --> sol2[HARMONIC ABSORBER
INCREASE DUCT WALL STIFFNESS
ADD HELMHOLTZ RESONATOR] HF --> sol3[FOAM LINING IN DUCT
SMOOTH AEROFOIL LOUVERS
USE BLOWER AT LOWER RPM] sol1 --> check[RECALCULATE NC RATING] sol2 --> check sol3 --> check check --> verdict{NC BELOW 45?} verdict -->|YES| done[DESIGN ACCEPTED] verdict -->|NO| problem ``` ### 4.3 Octave-Band NC Curve Mapping (Conceptual) ```mermaid flowchart LR freq31[31.5 Hz] --> band[OCTAVE BAND
SPL MEASUREMENT] freq63[63 Hz] --> band freq125[125 Hz] --> band freq250[250 Hz] --> band freq500[500 Hz] --> band freq1k[1000 Hz] --> band freq2k[2000 Hz] --> band freq4k[4000 Hz] --> band freq8k[8000 Hz] --> band band --> awgt[A WEIGHTING
APPLIED PER BAND] awgt --> total[SUMMATION
dBA VALUE] total --> nc[NC RATING
COMPARED TO BERANEK CURVES] ``` ### 4.4 Blower Noise vs RPM Characteristic (Conceptual Block Representation) ```mermaid flowchart LR rpm1[LOW RPM
1500 rpm] --> spl1[SPL APPROX 42 dBA
NC 30] rpm2[MEDIUM RPM
2500 rpm] --> spl2[SPL APPROX 52 dBA
NC 40] rpm3[HIGH RPM
3500 rpm] --> spl3[SPL APPROX 62 dBA
NC 50] rpm4[PEAK RPM
4500 rpm] --> spl4[SPL APPROX 72 dBA
NC 60] spl1 --> cabin1[QUIET CRUISE] spl2 --> cabin2[NORMAL CITY DRIVING] spl3 --> cabin3[HOT DAY FULL COOLING] spl4 --> cabin4[UNACCEPTABLE
NEEDS NVH FIX] ``` ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Define Sound Pressure Level (SPL). What is the standard reference sound pressure used in air acoustics, and why is the decibel scale used instead of a linear scale?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** Sound Pressure Level (SPL) is the logarithmic measure of the rms sound pressure of a sound wave, defined as: $$L_p = 20 \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{p_{\text{rms}}}{p_0}\right) \quad \text{dB}$$ **Reference pressure:** $p_0 = 20\ \mu\text{Pa} = 2 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{Pa}$, which corresponds to the threshold of human hearing at 1 kHz. **Why decibel scale (2 marks):** 1. The human ear responds to sound **logarithmically**, not linearly. 2. The range from threshold of hearing (20 μPa) to threshold of pain (20 Pa) spans a factor of 10⁶ in pressure — impractical on a linear scale. 3. The decibel compresses this enormous range into manageable numbers (0 dB to 120 dB) that match human perception. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *What is the A-weighted decibel (dBA)? Why is it preferred over the linear dB scale for rating automotive cabin HVAC noise?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** **dBA definition:** dBA is the sound pressure level measured after passing the signal through the **A-weighting filter**, an electronic filter that mimics the frequency response of the human ear at moderate sound levels. The A-weighting attenuates very low (< 500 Hz) and very high (> 8 kHz) frequencies and emphasizes the mid-range (1–4 kHz) where the human ear is most sensitive. **Why preferred for automotive HVAC (2 marks):** 1. It correlates better with the **perceived loudness** of noise by the vehicle occupants. 2. It matches the **psychoacoustic response** of the ear at typical cabin SPLs (40–70 dB), making it the standard for regulatory and customer-satisfaction metrics. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Pattern --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / May 2024 pattern]** **Q. (a)** With the help of a suitable block diagram, explain the various **sources of air noise** in an automotive HVAC system. Discuss the **dominant source** and the physical mechanism responsible for its noise generation. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer (7 marks):** The major sources of air noise in an automotive HVAC system are: 1. **Blower Fan (Dominant Source – 4 marks):** The centrifugal/sirocco blower is the principal noise generator. Noise is produced by: - **Broadband turbulence noise** from unsteady flow over the rotating impeller blades. - **Tonal noise** at the **Blade Passage Frequency (BPF)** given by: $$f_{\text{BPF}} = \frac{N \cdot n}{60}$$ where $N$ = number of blades, $n$ = blower RPM. - **Vortex shedding** from blade trailing edges (related to Strouhal number). 2. **Evaporator and Heater Core (1 mark):** Narrow fin passages cause turbulent boundary layer separation; fin vibration may also produce buzz, squeal, or moan. 3. **Ducts, Bends, and Plenums (1 mark):** Sudden area changes and sharp turns produce flow separation, vortex shedding, and standing-wave resonances inside plenum chambers. 4. **Mode/Blend Doors and Louvers (1 mark):** Partially closed doors obstruct airflow; final discharge from louvers creates aerodynamic jet noise. **Diagram:** (Refer to Section 4.1 block diagram — students should redraw the complete noise-source-to-cabin chain.) --- **Q. (b)** A vehicle's HVAC blower produces a Sound Power Level of **78 dB re 10⁻¹² W**. Calculate the SPL at the driver's ear, located **0.8 m** from the blower, assuming free-field conditions. If the design team installs a **muffler (silencer)** that reduces the SPL by **8 dB**, what is the new Sound Power Level radiated by the blower + muffler assembly? **(7 marks)** **Model Answer (7 marks):** **Given:** - $L_w = 78$ dB - $r = 0.8$ m - Free-field condition **Step 1 — Use free-field equation (3 marks):** $$L_p = L_w - 20 \cdot \log_{10}(r) - 11$$ $$L_p = 78 - 20 \cdot \log_{10}(0.8) - 11$$ $$L_p = 78 - 20 \cdot (-0.0969) - 11 = 78 + 1.938 - 11$$ $$\boxed{L_p = 68.94\ \text{dB} \approx 69\ \text{dB}}$$ **Step 2 — After 8 dB attenuation (2 marks):** $$L_{p,\text{new}} = 68.94 - 8 = 60.94\ \text{dB}$$ **Step 3 — Find new Sound Power Level (2 marks):** Rearranging the free-field equation: $$L_{w,\text{new}} = L_{p,\text{new}} + 20 \cdot \log_{10}(r) + 11$$ $$L_{w,\text{new}} = 60.94 + 20 \cdot \log_{10}(0.8) + 11$$ $$L_{w,\text{new}} = 60.94 - 1.938 + 11 = 70.00\ \text{dB}$$ **Result:** The muffler reduces the radiated Sound Power Level from 78 dB to **70 dB** — a reduction of 8 dB in both SPL and PWL (since the muffler is a passive dissipative device that reduces acoustic power radiated into the cabin). **Verification using intensity ratio:** $$\frac{W_{\text{new}}}{W_{\text{old}}} = 10^{(70 - 78)/10} = 10^{-0.8} = 0.158$$ So the muffler dissipates approximately **84% of the original sound power** as heat. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]** **Q. (a)** Explain the **A-weighting network** and the **octave-band analysis** method. Why is octave-band analysis preferred over a single dBA number for detailed HVAC noise diagnostics? **(7 marks)** **Model Answer (7 marks):** **A-Weighting Network (3 marks):** The A-weighting network is an electronic frequency filter standardized in **IEC 61672** that approximates the equal-loudness contours (Fletcher-Munson curves) of the human ear at low to moderate SPL. It applies frequency-dependent corrections (in dB) to each octave band: - Strong attenuation below 250 Hz (e.g., −26.2 dB at 63 Hz) - Peak sensitivity near 1–4 kHz - Slight attenuation above 8 kHz **Octave-Band Analysis (2 marks):** The audible frequency spectrum (20 Hz – 20 kHz) is divided into bands such that the upper frequency of each band is **twice** the lower. The center frequencies follow: $$f_n = 1000 \cdot 2^{(n-7)/3} \quad \text{Hz}$$ Standard preferred bands: 31.5, 63, 125, 250, 500, 1k, 2k, 4k, 8k, 16k Hz. **Why preferred over single dBA (2 marks):** 1. **Two systems with the same dBA can sound very different.** Octave-band data reveals whether the energy is in annoying 1–2 kHz tones or pleasant broadband distribution. 2. **Targeted noise control:** If the 500 Hz band is 10 dB above target but 4 kHz is fine, the engineer designs a **Helmholtz resonator tuned to 500 Hz** — a fix not possible with only a single dBA number. --- **Q. (b)** An automotive AC system has two independent noise sources at the dashboard vent: a blower producing **62 dB SPL** and an expansion valve hiss producing **57 dB SPL**. Assuming the two sources are **incoherent**, calculate the total SPL. If a new blower with a 5 dB lower rating (57 dB) replaces the old one, what is the new total SPL? Comment on the engineering significance. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer (7 marks):** **Case 1 — Two original sources (4 marks):** Convert to linear intensity ratios: $$I_1 / I_0 = 10^{62/10} = 10^{6.2} = 1.585 \times 10^6$$ $$I_2 / I_0 = 10^{57/10} = 10^{5.7} = 5.012 \times 10^5$$ Sum: $$I_{\text{total}} / I_0 = 1.585 \times 10^6 + 5.012 \times 10^5 = 2.086 \times 10^6$$ $$L_{p,\text{total}} = 10 \cdot \log_{10}(2.086 \times 10^6) = 10 \cdot 6.3194 = 63.19\ \text{dB}$$ $$\boxed{L_{p,\text{total}} \approx 63.2\ \text{dB}}$$ **Case 2 — New blower at 57 dB (2 marks):** $$I_1' / I_0 = 10^{57/10} = 5.012 \times 10^5$$ $$I_2 / I_0 = 5.012 \times 10^5$$ Sum: $$I_{\text{total}}' / I_0 = 1.002 \times 10^6$$ $$L_{p,\text{total}}' = 10 \cdot \log_{10}(1.002 \times 10^6) = 60.01\ \text{dB}$$ **Engineering Significance (1 mark):** Replacing the blower reduced the total SPL from **63.2 dB → 60.0 dB** (a 3.2 dB drop), which is **perceptually noticeable** (a 3 dB change is just at the threshold of human detectability). The expansion valve hiss, previously masked, now becomes the dominant source. **Lesson:** Once a loud source is quieted, secondary sources (which were below threshold) become audible — the so-called "**unmasking effect**." Designers must address all significant sources, not just the loudest. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Unit Errors:** Students often write $L_p = 20 \log(p/p_0)$ without specifying that $p_0 = 20\ \mu\text{Pa}$. Always state the reference. > 2. **dB vs dBA Confusion:** dB is unweighted; dBA is A-weighted. They are NOT the same. KTU problems specifically ask for dBA in cabin noise questions. > 3. **Adding dB values linearly:** **A 60 dB + 60 dB source ≠ 120 dB.** It is 63 dB. This is the #1 mistake in numerical problems. > 4. **Forgetting the −11 dB term:** In free-field SPL-from-PWL problems, students often drop the $-11$ term, losing 2 marks. > 5. **Sign of $\log_{10}(r)$:** For $r < 1$ m, the log is negative, and the term becomes a **gain**, not a loss. Many students mark it as a subtraction. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Air Noise Level** - ✅ **Sound Pressure Level (SPL):** $L_p = 20 \log_{10}(p_{\text{rms}} / p_0)$ with $p_0 = 20\ \mu\text{Pa}$. - ✅ **Sound Power Level (PWL):** $L_w = 10 \log_{10}(W / W_0)$ with $W_0 = 10^{-12}$ W. - ✅ **Free-field relation:** $L_p = L_w - 20 \log_{10}(r) - 11$. - ✅ **A-Weighting (dBA):** Frequency-weighted SPL matching human ear response — **the standard for automotive cabin noise**. - ✅ **Octave bands (preferred):** 31.5, 63, 125, 250, 500 Hz, 1, 2, 4, 8, 16 kHz. - ✅ **Incoherent source addition:** $L_{p,\text{total}} = 10 \log_{10}(\sum 10^{L_{pi}/10})$. - ✅ **N equal sources:** $+10 \log_{10}(N)$ dB increment. - ✅ **6 dB drop per doubling of distance** (free field). - ✅ **3 dB rule:** Two equal incoherent sources → +3 dB; ten equal sources → +10 dB. - ✅ **10 dB rule:** Perceived as "doubling of loudness" by human ear. - ✅ **NC/PNC rating:** NC 30–35 is luxury cabin target; NC 50 is acceptable upper limit. - ✅ **Dominant noise source in automotive AC:** Centrifugal blower (aerodynamic + tonal BPF). - ✅ **Noise control techniques:** Helmholtz resonators, duct wall foam, smooth bends, balanced impeller, soft motor mounts, aerodynamic louver profiles. - ✅ **Blade Passage Frequency (BPF):** $f_{\text{BPF}} = N \cdot n / 60$ — primary tonal noise of blower. - ✅ **Strouhal Number for vortex shedding:** $f_s = \text{St} \cdot V / D$ — governs duct and fin tonal noise. --- **End of Notes — Air Noise Level | Module 3 | PEAUT523** *Prepared in alignment with KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020) examination pattern.*

topics.title: Layout of Duct Systems for Automobiles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT523 - AUTOMOTIVE REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Layout of Duct Systems for Automobiles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Duct System (Automotive Air Conditioning Context)**
> A *duct system* in an automobile is an integrated network of hollow passages, plenums, hoses, chambers, louvers, and outlets engineered to distribute conditioned air (cooled, heated, dehumidified, or fresh) from the HVAC unit to every occupant zone of the vehicle cabin at the correct volumetric flow rate, temperature, velocity, and pressure — while minimizing noise, pressure drop, and thermal losses.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the duct system forms the **terminal side of the air-handling unit (AHU)** of the vehicle and is responsible for *air distribution, mixing, and delivery*. It links the **evaporator / heater core outlet** to the **cabin outlets** (face vents, foot vents, defrost vents, side window demisters, and rear-seat ducts in premium cars).

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Veins & Arteries" of the Cabin

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world Analogy**
> Imagine the human circulatory system:
> - The **HVAC box (evaporator + blower)** is the **heart**.
> - The **blower motor** is the **pumping action**.
> - The **ducts** are the **arteries and veins** carrying conditioned "blood" (air).
> - The **vents/louvers** are the **capillaries** that release this conditioned air precisely where it is needed — face, feet, windshield.
>
> Just as a cardiologist worries about *clogged arteries* (pressure drop, turbulence), an automotive HVAC engineer worries about *duct restrictions, sharp bends, undersized hoses, and excessive length* that starve certain cabin zones of airflow.

### 1.3 Functional Objectives of an Automotive Duct System

The duct layout must simultaneously satisfy **six competing design constraints**, often referred to in KTU valuation as the *KTU Six-Point Duct Design Mandate*:

1. **Uniform Air Distribution** — every occupant receives a comparable volume of conditioned air (typically **120 to 200 m³/h per front seat** for thermal comfort).
2. **Low Static Pressure Drop** — total duct pressure drop should be ≤ **125 Pa** to avoid overloading the blower.
3. **Acoustic Comfort (NVH)** — airflow-induced noise below **45 dB(A)** in the cabin at full blower speed.
4. **Thermal Insulation** — chilled air duct surfaces must be insulated (closed-cell foam, **6 mm to 13 mm** thickness) to prevent cabin condensation.
5. **Lightweight & Compact Packaging** — ducts must conform to the irregular geometry of the dashboard, roof, and floor.
6. **Manufacturability & Cost** — injection-moulded PP, EPP foam, or fabric-reinforced flexible hoses.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Metric (KTU / SAE Highlight)**
> Industry benchmark airflow per occupant zone: **0.06 to 0.10 m³/s** (= **216 to 360 m³/h**) for a typical sedan. Premium vehicles with rear-seat comfort packages push this to **0.13 m³/s**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Ducts by Construction

The KTU Module-3 syllabus categorizes automotive ducts into three families based on **stiffness and material**:

| Sl. No. | Duct Type | Material | Typical Location | Advantage | Limitation |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | **Rigid Duct** | Injection-moulded PP / ABS / EPP foam | HVAC box, plenum, central console | Lowest pressure drop, precise geometry | Expensive tooling, hard to package in tight curves |
| 2 | **Flexible Duct** | Reinforced PVC / EPDM over helix wire | Foot-level, rear cabin, doors | Easy routing, vibration isolation | Higher pressure drop (~30 % more) |
| 3 | **Semi-Rigid Fabric Duct** | Non-woven polyester over aluminium core | Premium rear-seat comfort | Even diffusion, premium feel | Higher cost |

### 2.2 Classification by Airflow Path (Layout Patterns)

The layout dictates *which cabin zones receive conditioned air* and is a **high-yield KTU question**:

#### (a) Single-Zone (Front-Only) Layout
- Used in **economy hatchbacks and entry sedans**.
- Single evaporator, single blower, ducts serve only **front face vents + foot vents + defrost**.
- Rear passengers receive air only through *stratification* (warm air rises).

#### (b) Dual-Zone Layout
- Two electronically controlled blend doors — driver and front passenger get **independent temperatures**.
- Requires a **dual-shaft actuator** and split ducts after the heater core.
- Found in mid-segment sedans (e.g., Honda City, Hyundai Verna).

#### (c) Tri-Zone / Four-Zone Layout
- Independent rear-cabin ducting with roof-mounted or floor-channel outlets.
- Found in luxury vehicles (Mercedes S-Class, BMW 7-Series).

#### (d) Rear-AC Module Layout
- A **secondary evaporator + blower unit** mounted under the front passenger seat or in the roof console.
- Long flexible ducts route air to **roof-mounted rear vents**.

### 2.3 Cabin Outlet Map (KTU Board Favourite)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Four Strategic Outlet Zones (Memorize for ESE)**
> 1. **Face-Level Vents (Centre & Side)** — primary cooling; aim at occupant's torso height.
> 2. **Foot-Level Vents** — heating mode (warm air rises only works in winter stratification).
> 3. **Windshield Defrost Vents** — directed at base of windshield to clear fog (demist mode).
> 4. **Side Window Demister Vents** — fine nozzle slots along A-pillar trim.

### 2.4 Air Intake Modes — Recirculation vs Fresh Air

A duct layout must handle **two distinct air sources** feeding the blower inlet:

- **Fresh Air Mode (FAM)** — cabin draws ambient air through a **snorkel / cowl intake** at the base of the windshield.
- **Recirculation Mode (RAM)** — cabin air is pulled back through a **filter behind the glove box** to save cooling load (saves up to **7–10 %** compressor work).

Most modern cars have an **actuated blend door** allowing a *bi-modal or mixed mode*.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **directly testable** in KTU ESE / Internal Assessment for Module-3 duct topics. SI units throughout.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | **Volumetric Flow Rate** | $Q = A \cdot v$ | $Q$ in $\mathrm{m^3/s}$, $A$ in $\mathrm{m^2}$, $v$ in $\mathrm{m/s}$ |
| 2 | **Mass Flow Rate** | $\dot{m} = \rho \cdot Q = \rho \cdot A \cdot v$ | $\rho \approx 1.20 \,\mathrm{kg/m^3}$ for air at **20 °C, 1 atm** |
| 3 | **Equivalent Diameter (Rectangular Duct)** | $D_e = \dfrac{2 \cdot a \cdot b}{a + b}$ | $a, b$ are side lengths in $\mathrm{m}$ |
| 4 | **Hagen–Poiseuille Pressure Drop (Laminar)** | $\Delta P = \dfrac{128 \cdot \mu \cdot L \cdot Q}{\pi \cdot D^{4}}$ | $\mu$ dynamic viscosity ($\mathrm{Pa \cdot s}$) |
