| 5 | **Darcy–Weisbach Pressure Drop (Turbulent)** | $\Delta P = f \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{\rho \cdot v^{2}}{2}$ | $f$ = Darcy friction factor (Moody chart), $L$ duct length in $\mathrm{m}$ |
| 6 | **Blower Power** | $P_{fan} = \dfrac{\Delta P_{total} \cdot Q}{\eta_{fan}}$ | $\eta_{fan}$ typically **0.40 – 0.65** |
| 7 | **Velocity Head** | $P_v = \dfrac{\rho \cdot v^{2}}{2}$ | Pressure in $\mathrm{Pa}$ |
| 8 | **Heat Loss Through Duct Wall** | $\dot{Q}_{loss} = \dfrac{k}{t} \cdot A_{surf} \cdot \Delta T_{LM}$ | $k$ insulation conductivity, $t$ insulation thickness |
| 9 | **Reynolds Number (Duct)** | $\mathrm{Re} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot v \cdot D_e}{\mu}$ | Turbulent when $\mathrm{Re} > 4000$ |
| 10 | **Air Changes per Hour (Cabin)** | $\mathrm{ACH} = \dfrac{Q_{blower} \cdot 3600}{V_{cabin}}$ | $V_{cabin}$ in $\mathrm{m^3}$, target $\approx$ **20 to 30 ACH** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Reminder — avoid the `|` symbol in tables**
> For "absolute value" or "ratio" expressions inside table cells, write $\vert x \vert$ or use the `mid` command. Never use the bare pipe `|`.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Equivalent Diameter of a Non-Circular Duct

Most automotive ducts are **rectangular or oval** (to fit dashboard cavities). Engineers translate them into a circular-equivalent hydraulic diameter for friction calculations.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Start with the Hagen–Poiseuille flow-rate expression for a circular duct:} \quad
Q &= \frac{\pi \cdot \Delta P \cdot D^{4}}{128 \cdot \mu \cdot L} \\
\text{For a non-circular duct of arbitrary cross-section:} \quad
Q &= \frac{\Delta P}{K} \cdot \frac{A^{2}}{\mu \cdot L} \\
\text{where } K \text{ is a shape constant, and } A \text{ is the cross-sectional area.} \\
\text{Setting the two flow rates equal for the same } \Delta P \text{ and } L: \quad
\frac{\pi \cdot D^{4}}{128} &= \frac{A^{2}}{K} \\
\text{For a rectangular duct } (a \times b), \text{ the shape constant } K \text{ is:} \quad
K &= 4 \cdot \left[ \frac{a^{2} + b^{2}}{(a \cdot b)^{2}} + \frac{8}{\pi} \cdot \sum_{n \, \text{odd}} \frac{1}{n^{4}} \tanh\!\left(\frac{n \pi b}{2a}\right) \right] \\
\text{After simplification (using the standard engineering definition):} \quad
D_{e} &= \frac{4 A}{P_{wetted}} = \frac{4 \cdot a \cdot b}{2(a + b)} \\
\therefore \quad D_{e} &= \frac{2 a b}{a + b}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation of every step:**

- We equate a circular and a non-circular duct carrying the same flow $Q$ under the same pressure drop.
- The wetted perimeter $P_{wetted}$ of a rectangle is $2(a+b)$.
- The hydraulic / equivalent diameter is the ratio of **4 × Area** to **Wetted Perimeter** — a foundational fluid-mechanics identity.
- Final result: $D_e = \dfrac{2 a b}{a + b}$. This is the **KTU board-favourite formula** for duct sizing.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Total Pressure Drop Across an Automotive Duct Network

The duct layout is a *series-parallel combination* of straight sections, bends, contractions, expansions, and outlet grilles. The **total pressure drop** governs blower selection.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1 — Sum straight-section friction losses (Darcy–Weisbach):} \quad
\Delta P_{straight} &= \sum_{i} f_i \cdot \frac{L_i}{D_{e,i}} \cdot \frac{\rho \, v_i^{2}}{2} \\
\text{Step 2 — Sum local losses (elbows, tees, reducers, grille):} \quad
\Delta P_{local} &= \sum_{j} K_{L,j} \cdot \frac{\rho \, v_j^{2}}{2} \\
\text{Step 3 — Sum across the whole duct network (series):} \quad
\Delta P_{total} &= \Delta P_{straight} + \Delta P_{local} + \Delta P_{exit} \\
\text{Step 4 — For parallel branches (e.g., face vent vs foot vent):} \quad
\Delta P_{branch \, 1} &= \Delta P_{branch \, 2} \quad \text{(equal pressure at common node)} \\
\text{Step 5 — Outlet grille pressure drop (typical K_L value 1.5 to 3.0):} \quad
\Delta P_{grille} &= K_{grille} \cdot \frac{\rho \, v_{grille}^{2}}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion-logic explanation:**

- Each straight pipe contributes friction proportional to its $L/D_e$.
- Each fitting (bend, reducer, tee) adds a **local loss coefficient $K_L$**.
- For parallel branches, the **node-pressure equality** is the boundary condition — total flow splits such that pressure drop is identical in each path.
- The **blower** must overcome $\Delta P_{total}$ at the design flow $Q$.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement**
> A car HVAC system supplies air to the front face vent through a rectangular duct of cross-section **60 mm × 30 mm**, length **1.2 m**, with **two 90° bends** ($K_L = 0.9$ each) and a louvered grille ($K_L = 2.5$). Air velocity in the duct is **4 m/s**. Air density $\rho = 1.20 \,\mathrm{kg/m^3}$. Compute the total pressure drop.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step A — Equivalent Diameter:} \quad
D_e &= \frac{2 \times 0.060 \times 0.030}{0.060 + 0.030} = \frac{2 \times 0.0018}{0.090} \\
D_e &= \frac{0.0036}{0.090} = 0.040 \, \mathrm{m} \;=\; 40 \, \mathrm{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step B — Velocity head:} \quad
P_v &= \frac{\rho \, v^{2}}{2} = \frac{1.20 \times 4^{2}}{2} \\
P_v &= \frac{1.20 \times 16}{2} = \frac{19.2}{2} \\
P_v &= 9.6 \, \mathrm{Pa}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step C — Friction factor (assume smooth duct, turbulent, use Blasius):} \quad
\mathrm{Re} &= \frac{\rho \, v \, D_e}{\mu} = \frac{1.20 \times 4 \times 0.040}{1.81 \times 10^{-5}} \\
\mathrm{Re} &= \frac{0.192}{1.81 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 10{,}607 \\
f &= \frac{0.316}{\mathrm{Re}^{0.25}} = \frac{0.316}{(10607)^{0.25}} \approx 0.0312
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step D — Straight pipe friction loss:} \quad
\Delta P_{straight} &= f \cdot \frac{L}{D_e} \cdot P_v \\
&= 0.0312 \times \frac{1.2}{0.040} \times 9.6 \\
&= 0.0312 \times 30 \times 9.6 \\
&= 8.9856 \, \mathrm{Pa} \approx 9.0 \, \mathrm{Pa}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step E — Local losses (two bends + grille):} \quad
\Sigma K_L &= 0.9 + 0.9 + 2.5 = 4.3 \\
\Delta P_{local} &= \Sigma K_L \cdot P_v \\
&= 4.3 \times 9.6 \\
&= 41.28 \, \mathrm{Pa}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step F — Total pressure drop:} \quad
\Delta P_{total} &= \Delta P_{straight} + \Delta P_{local} \\
&= 9.0 + 41.3 \\
&= 50.3 \, \mathrm{Pa}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Verdict**
> $\Delta P_{total} \approx 50 \, \mathrm{Pa}$ is well below the typical **125 Pa** design ceiling — meaning the duct is conservatively sized. Notice that **local losses (41.3 Pa) dominate straight-section friction (9.0 Pa)** — a classic insight KTU examiners love to highlight.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Duct Layout Strategies

| Criterion | Single-Zone (Front) | Dual-Zone | Rear-AC Module | Tri-Zone |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **Number of ducts** | 4–5 | 6–8 | 8–10 | 10–14 |
| **Blower power** | 80–150 W | 150–250 W | 250–400 W | 400–600 W |
| **Duct pressure drop** | 60–90 Pa | 80–120 Pa | 100–150 Pa | 120–180 Pa |
| **Rear-passenger comfort** | Poor | Moderate | Good | Excellent |
| **Cost** | Low | Medium | Medium-High | Very High |
| **Vehicle class** | Hatchback / Entry sedan | Mid-segment sedan | SUV / MUV | Luxury sedan |
| **KTU Weightage** | ⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Architecture of an Automotive Duct System

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Rules in Force**
> - All node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed with a letter (e.g., `nodeA`, `stepB`).
> - No reserved keywords (`end`, `graph`, `subgraph`, `style`) used as node names.
> - Node labels contain plain uppercase alphanumeric text only — no bold, italics, or HTML tags.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["AMBIENT AIR INTAKE COWL NEAR WINDSHIELD BASE"] --> nodeB["FRESH AIR RECIRCULATION DOOR BLEND ACTUATOR"]
    nodeC["CABIN INTERIOR AIR RETURN"] --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeD["BLOWER MOTOR AND EVAPORATOR ASSEMBLY"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["PLENUM CHAMBER AFTER EVAPORATOR"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["MODE DOOR FOR DEFROST FACE FOOT SELECTION"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["DEFROST DUCT RISING TO WINDSHIELD"]
    nodeF --> nodeH["FACE LEVEL DUCT CENTRE AND SIDE VENTS"]
    nodeF --> nodeI["FOOT LEVEL DUCT ALONG FLOOR CONSOLE"]
    nodeE --> nodeJ["REAR AC MODULE FLEXIBLE DUCT OPTIONAL"]
    nodeJ --> nodeK["ROOF MOUNTED REAR FACE VENTS"]
    nodeJ --> nodeL["REAR FOOT DUCT UNDER FRONT SEAT"]
    nodeG --> nodeM["CABIN OCCUPANT ZONE WINDSHIELD DEMIST"]
    nodeH --> nodeM
    nodeI --> nodeM
    nodeK --> nodeM
    nodeL --> nodeM
    nodeM --> nodeN["RETURN TO CABIN AIR RETURN GRILLE"]
    nodeN --> nodeC
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Ambient air enters at the **cowl** (front windshield base).
- The **blend door** selects fresh or recirculated air.
- The **blower + evaporator** cools and drives air.
- The **mode door** deflects air to one of three front-cabin outlet paths (defrost, face, foot).
- A separate **rear-AC branch** (if equipped) supplies roof-mounted or under-seat outlets.
- All cabin air ultimately **returns** to the inlet through a recirculation grille near the glove box.

---

### 4.2 Schematic Cross-Section — Dashboard Duct Routing

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeP["HVAC BOX PLENUM EVAPORATOR BLOWER"] --> nodeQ["CENTRAL DUCT VERTICAL RISER"]
    nodeQ --> nodeR["CENTRE FACE VENT ROOF POD"]
    nodeQ --> nodeS["SIDE FACE VENT A PILLAR"]
    nodeP --> nodeT["FOOT DUCT FLOOR CHANNEL"]
    nodeT --> nodeU["DRIVER FOOTWELL OUTLET"]
    nodeT --> nodeV["CO DRIVER FOOTWELL OUTLET"]
    nodeP --> nodeW["DEFROST DUCT UPPER PLENUM"]
    nodeW --> nodeX["WINDSHIELD BASE DEMIST SLOT"]
    nodeW --> nodeY["SIDE WINDOW DEMISTER NOZZLE"]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Memorization Aid (KTU Mnemonic)**
> *"**C**ool air goes to **F**ace, **F**loor gets **H**eat, **D**efrost saves **W**indshield."*
> Map each letter → duct branch in the schematic above.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 Marks Each)

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set B]
*List any four functional requirements of an automotive air-conditioning duct system.*

**Model Answer (4 × 1 mark):**
1. Uniform air distribution to all occupant zones.
2. Low pressure drop to minimize blower load.
3. Adequate thermal insulation to prevent cabin condensation.
4. Acoustic quietness (NVH compliance, ≤ 45 dB(A)).

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set A]
*Differentiate between "recirculation mode" and "fresh air mode" in an automotive HVAC duct layout.*

**Model Answer (2 marks):**
- **Recirculation Mode (RAM):** Air is drawn from inside the cabin (through a return grille), reducing cooling load and improving quick cooling; useful in polluted or hot ambient conditions.
- **Fresh Air Mode (FAM):** Ambient air is drawn in through a cowl intake at the windshield base; prevents fogging and maintains oxygen levels; used in cold/wet conditions.
- The mode is selected by an **actuated blend door** in the HVAC box.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern Compliance**
> Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, divided into two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Internal choice means the student must answer **either Question A or Question B** in full.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Duct Design & Pressure Drop Calculation* [KTU Model Paper — Sample 2024]

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **layout of the air distribution duct system** in a typical front-engined, front-AC sedan. Label the plenum, mode door, face ducts, foot ducts, defrost ducts, and recirculation path. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

A typical sedan duct layout comprises:

| Component | Location | Function (½ mark each) |
|:---|:---|:---|
| HVAC Box (Plenum + Evaporator + Blower) | Behind dashboard, centre | Conditions and pressurizes air |
| Blend Door | Inside HVAC box | Selects fresh / recirculated air |
| Mode Door | After evaporator | Directs air to defrost / face / foot |
| Central Face Duct | Vertical riser to roof pod | Primary cooling outlet |
| Side Face Vents | A-pillar trim | Lateral cooling |
| Foot Duct | Floor console channel | Heating mode outlet |
| Defrost Duct | Upper plenum to windshield base | Demisting |
| Recirculation Grille | Behind glove box | Cabin air return |

*(Examiner note: Sketch with arrows showing airflow path; one mark for sketch, two marks for correct component identification, two marks for describing flow logic, two marks for material/insulation note.)*

---

**(b)** Air flows through a rectangular duct of cross-section **80 mm × 40 mm** at a velocity of **5 m/s**. The duct length is **1.5 m**, with **three 90° bends** ($K_L = 0.8$ each) and a louvered outlet grille ($K_L = 2.0$). Take $\rho = 1.20 \, \mathrm{kg/m^3}$ and $\mu = 1.81 \times 10^{-5} \, \mathrm{Pa \cdot s}$. Compute the **total pressure drop** across the duct. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_e &= \frac{2 \times 0.080 \times 0.040}{0.080 + 0.040} = \frac{0.0064}{0.120} = 0.0533 \, \mathrm{m} \\[4pt]
\mathrm{Re} &= \frac{1.20 \times 5 \times 0.0533}{1.81 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{0.3198}{1.81 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 17{,}669 \\[4pt]
f &= \frac{0.316}{(17669)^{0.25}} \approx 0.0274 \\[4pt]
P_v &= \frac{1.20 \times 25}{2} = 15 \, \mathrm{Pa} \\[4pt]
\Delta P_{straight} &= 0.0274 \times \frac{1.5}{0.0533} \times 15 = 11.57 \, \mathrm{Pa} \\[4pt]
\Sigma K_L &= 3 \times 0.8 + 2.0 = 4.4 \\[4pt]
\Delta P_{local} &= 4.4 \times 15 = 66.0 \, \mathrm{Pa} \\[4pt]
\Delta P_{total} &= 11.57 + 66.0 \approx \mathbf{77.6 \, \mathrm{Pa}}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(Examiner note: 1 mark for $D_e$, 1 mark for Re, 1 mark for $f$, 1 mark for $P_v$, 2 marks for straight & local losses, 1 mark for summation.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice — Cabin Zones & Layout Strategies* [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a)** Compare **single-zone, dual-zone, and rear-AC module** duct layouts with reference to **number of ducts, blower power, rear-passenger comfort, and cost**. Identify which layout is best suited for a **premium luxury sedan** with justification. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

| Parameter | Single-Zone | Dual-Zone | Rear-AC Module |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| Ducts | 4–5 | 6–8 | 8–10 |
| Blower Power | 80–150 W | 150–250 W | 250–400 W |
| Rear Comfort | Poor | Moderate | Good |
| Cost | Low | Medium | Medium-High |

- A **luxury sedan** with rear-seat occupant priority demands the **rear-AC module layout** (with roof vents) or even a **four-zone layout** — because rear passengers require independently controlled thermal comfort.

*(2 marks for tabular comparison, 2 marks for qualitative narrative, 2 marks for correct identification, 1 mark for engineering justification.)*

---

**(b)** Explain the **role of the blend door** and **mode door** in an automotive HVAC duct layout. How do they enable the **fresh-air / recirculation** and **defrost / face / foot** modes respectively? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**
- **Blend Door** (also called *intake door*): Located at the air inlet side of the HVAC box. Rotates between two positions to admit either **ambient (fresh) air** from the cowl intake or **recirculated cabin air** from the return grille. Driven by a **vacuum actuator** (older cars) or an **electric servo motor** (modern cars). (3 marks)
- **Mode Door**: Located downstream of the evaporator, before the duct branches. Directs conditioned air to one or more of the three outlet paths — **defrost (windshield)**, **face (vents)**, or **foot (floor)**. Some mode doors have **partial openings** to allow **bi-level** or **tri-level** airflow simultaneously (e.g., face + foot in mild weather). (3 marks)
- The doors are **sequenced by the HVAC control module** (manual or automatic climate control ECU) to deliver the selected airflow distribution. (1 mark)

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls on Duct-System Questions (Read Carefully!)**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the `4` in Equivalent Diameter formula:** Students often write $D_e = \dfrac{ab}{a+b}$ — that is **WRONG**. The correct form is $\dfrac{4A}{P} = \dfrac{2ab}{a+b}$. Lose **1 mark instantly**.
> 2. **Confusing Reynolds number threshold:** Many students claim $\mathrm{Re} > 2300$ is turbulent. For **ducts**, use $\mathrm{Re} > 4000$ as the turbulent criterion — this is the **KTU standard**.
> 3. **Mixing up blend door vs mode door:** The *blend door* controls **fresh vs recirculation**, while the *mode door* controls **defrost vs face vs foot**. Examiners deliberately test this distinction.
> 4. **Not including the velocity head $P_v = \rho v^2 / 2$ in local-loss calculation:** Local losses are $K_L \times P_v$, not $K_L \times \Delta P_{straight}$. A **3-mark** error if forgotten.
> 5. **Skipping units in final answer:** Always state $\Delta P$ in **Pa** and $Q$ in **m³/s or m³/h**. KTU examiners deduct ½ mark for missing units.
> 6. **Drawing a "fancy" sketch but failing to label arrows** for airflow direction — at least **2 marks** are reserved for the airflow-path arrows in the diagram.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Read 5 Minutes Before the Exam**
>
> ✅ A duct system distributes conditioned air from the HVAC unit to cabin outlets.
> ✅ Three duct families: **rigid (PP/ABS)**, **flexible (PVC/EPDM)**, **semi-rigid fabric**.
> ✅ Four layout patterns: **single-zone**, **dual-zone**, **rear-AC module**, **tri/four-zone**.
> ✅ Four outlet zones: **face**, **foot**, **defrost**, **side window demist**.
> ✅ Two intake modes: **fresh air** (cowl intake) and **recirculation** (cabin return grille).
> ✅ Two key control doors: **blend door** (fresh/recirc) and **mode door** (defrost/face/foot).
> ✅ Equivalent diameter formula: $D_e = \dfrac{2ab}{a+b}$.
> ✅ Darcy–Weisbach: $\Delta P = f \, \dfrac{L}{D} \, \dfrac{\rho v^2}{2}$.
> ✅ Local loss: $\Delta P_{local} = K_L \, \dfrac{\rho v^2}{2}$.
> ✅ **Local losses typically dominate straight-section friction** in automotive ducts.
> ✅ Target total duct pressure drop ≤ **125 Pa**; blower efficiency **40–65 %**.
> ✅ Air changes per hour target: **20–30 ACH** for cabin.
> ✅ Insulation thickness for chilled-air ducts: **6–13 mm** closed-cell foam.
> ✅ Per-occupant airflow: **0.06–0.10 m³/s** for thermal comfort.
> ✅ Reynolds number transition for ducts: $\mathrm{Re} > 4000$.
> ✅ Always state units (Pa, m³/s, m) — marks lost otherwise.
> ✅ For parallel duct branches, **pressure drop is equal**, **flow splits**.
> ✅ Blower power: $P_{fan} = \Delta P_{total} \cdot Q / \eta_{fan}$.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Air Routing and Temperature Control: Objectives
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT523 - AUTOMOTIVE REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Air Routing and Temperature Control: Objectives

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Air Routing in an Automotive HVAC System?

**Air Routing** in an automotive Heating, Ventilation, and Air-Conditioning (HVAC) system refers to the **controlled distribution of conditioned air** to specific zones inside the vehicle cabin through a network of ducts, plenums, doors (mode doors / flaps), and louvers. It determines *where* the air goes after it leaves the evaporator and heater core.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** Air routing is the engineered process of directing the mass flow rate of conditioned air (measured in **kg/s** or **CFM**) to predetermined outlets — such as the windshield, face-level vents, footwell, or a combination — to satisfy the thermal comfort, defogging, and demisting requirements of the occupants as defined by **ASHRAE Standard 55** and **SAE J1503** automotive HVAC practices.

## 1.2 What is Temperature Control?

**Temperature Control** is the closed-loop regulation of cabin air temperature to a setpoint selected by the driver or passenger. It involves sensing the in-cabin (or ambient) temperature, comparing it with the desired setpoint, and modulating the blend between **cold air (post-evaporator)** and **hot air (post-heater core)** using a **temperature blend door (air-mix door)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** The *objective* of air routing and temperature control is NOT just "to make the cabin cold" — it is to achieve **uniform thermal comfort**, **rapid windshield demisting for safety**, and **energy-efficient operation** of the refrigerant and engine systems.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Shower Mixer Tap

Imagine you are taking a shower with two separate water supplies — one very hot and one very cold. A *mixer tap* lets you combine the two in different ratios to get the exact temperature you want. The **temperature blend door** in a car is exactly this mixer tap:

- The **evaporator** = the cold water supply (refrigerant cooled air).
- The **heater core** = the hot water supply (engine coolant warmed air).
- The **blend door** = the shower mixer tap (rotates to control the mix ratio).
- The **driver's temperature knob** = your hand on the tap.
- The **mode doors (air routing)** = the shower head and body jets — they decide *where* the mixed water goes.

> [!TIP]
> **Just like a shower, if the routing is wrong (water spraying on the ceiling instead of on you) the system fails its purpose regardless of perfect temperature. Air routing + temperature control must work TOGETHER.**

## 1.4 The Five Core Objectives of Air Routing and Temperature Control

The KTU 2024 syllabus lists these as the **primary goals** of the subsystem. A board-exam answer must always list these:

1. **Occupant Thermal Comfort** — Maintain cabin air temperature typically between **20 °C and 26 °C** as per ASHRAE comfort envelope, regardless of outside ambient (which can vary from **-30 °C to +50 °C**).
2. **Windshield Defogging and Demisting** — Direct warm, dry air across the inner windshield surface to prevent condensation that would obstruct the driver's vision (a **safety-critical** function).
3. **Uniform Cabin Air Distribution** — Eliminate hot/cold spots by ensuring proper air throw, velocity (typically **1.5 m/s to 5 m/s** at the occupant), and mixing throughout the cabin.
4. **Quick Response to Driver Setpoint** — Achieve the desired cabin temperature within an acceptable time lag (usually **≤ 90 seconds** for the outlet air to reach setpoint).
5. **Energy Efficiency & Load Management** — Minimize compressor and blower work to reduce fuel consumption and parasitic engine load.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Constants to Remember for KTU Exams:**
> - Cabin design temperature (comfort): **22 °C ± 2 °C**
> - Defrost outlet air temperature: **≥ 40 °C** at the windshield plane
> - Face-level air velocity: **≤ 4 m/s** (to avoid "drafty" feeling — ASHRAE)
> - Footwell air temperature: typically **5–8 °C warmer** than head-level for thermal buoyancy comfort

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functional Architecture of the Air Routing Subsystem

The HVAC box (often called the **plenum** or **HVAC module**) is the heart of air routing. Inside it, several motorized **doors** (actuated by either vacuum, electric servo motors, or stepper motors in modern cars) rotate to redirect airflow.

### 2.1.1 The Four Main Mode Doors

| Door Name | Function | Position in Plenum | Air Routed To |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Recirculation Door** | Selects inside-air or outside-air intake | Before evaporator | All outlets |
| **Mode Door (Upper)** | Controls defrost / panel flow | Top of plenum | Windshield & face vents |
| **Mode Door (Lower)** | Controls foot / panel flow | Bottom of plenum | Footwell & face vents |
| **Temperature Blend Door (Air-Mix Door)** | Mixes hot & cold air | Across heater core | All outlets |

## 2.2 Operational Modes of Air Distribution

A KTU-favorite question: *"List the modes of air distribution in an automotive HVAC system."* The standard answer includes:

1. **MAX A/C Mode** — Recirculation ON, full cold air, face-level only. Used for rapid cooling from a hot-soak condition.
2. **Panel (Face) Mode** — Air exits through dashboard louvers at the occupant's upper body / face.
3. **Bi-Level Mode** — Simultaneously sends cool air to the face and warm air to the feet. Thermally the most *natural* mode because it mimics the human body's preferred vertical temperature gradient (feet warmer than head).
4. **Floor (Foot) Mode** — Air routed to the footwell. Used in mild cool weather.
5. **Defrost Mode** — Heated air directed at the base of the windshield to clear frost.
6. **Defrost/Floor (Mix) Mode** — Splits airflow between windshield and footwell. Used during cold, humid conditions.
7. **Fresh Air vs Recirculation** — Independent of mode; controls whether intake is ambient or cabin-recirculated.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 3 Emphasis:** The student MUST be able to draw the **mode door positions** for each distribution mode and explain *why* defrost always requires fresh (outside) air, not recirculation. (Reason: recirculation air is already humid and will re-fog the windshield.)

## 2.3 Temperature Control Strategy — The Blend-Air Approach

Modern vehicles use the **blend-air method** because it is simpler, cheaper, and more reliable than older systems that had separate heating and cooling circuits switched by a valve.

### 2.2.1 Operating Principle

The blower pushes air through the **evaporator first** (always cooling the air to a fixed low temperature, e.g., **3 °C to 5 °C**). Then, a portion of this chilled air is **diverted through the heater core** (which is hot because engine coolant flows through it, typically **85 °C to 105 °C**). The blend door sets the ratio:

$$m_{total} = m_{cold} + m_{hot}$$

Where:
- $m_{total}$ = total mass flow rate of air entering the cabin
- $m_{cold}$ = mass flow bypassing the heater core
- $m_{hot}$ = mass flow passing through the heater core

The outlet temperature is then a flow-weighted average:

$$T_{outlet} = \frac{m_{cold} \cdot T_{cold} + m_{hot} \cdot T_{hot}}{m_{total}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why does KTU love this question?** It directly tests whether the student understands that the evaporator ALWAYS cools the air fully, and comfort is achieved by *re-heating a portion* with waste engine heat — a brilliant energy-recovery technique.

## 2.4 The Closed-Loop Control Logic

The ECU (HVAC control module) implements a **PID-like control loop**:

1. **Sensing:** In-cabin temperature sensor (NTC thermistor), ambient air sensor, in-duct air temperature sensor, and sun-load sensor (photo-diode) feed real-time data.
2. **Setpoint:** Driver/passenger selects a temperature, typically on a digital display (**16 °C to 32 °C** range).
3. **Computation:** ECU calculates the error: $e(t) = T_{setpoint} - T_{cabin}$
4. **Actuation:** ECU drives the **blend door actuator** (DC motor with position feedback via a potentiometer) to the new angle.
5. **Correction:** Once $e(t)$ falls within a dead-band (typically **±0.5 °C**), the actuator holds position.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Relationship | Symbol Meaning | Units | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_{outlet} = \dfrac{m_c T_c + m_h T_h}{m_c + m_h}$ | Outlet temperature after blending | °C or K | Blend-air temperature prediction |
| 2 | $\dot{Q}_{sensible} = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Sensible heat load on cabin | W (J/s) | Cabin cooling/heating load |
| 3 | $RH_{new} = \dfrac{p_v}{p_{sat}(T_{new})} \times 100$ | New relative humidity after cooling | % | Defogging effectiveness |
| 4 | $PMV = f(M, I_{cl}, T_a, \bar{T}_{rad}, v, p_v)$ | Predicted Mean Vote (Fanger) | index | Thermal comfort per ASHRAE 55 |
| 5 | $v_{face} \leq 4\ \text{m/s}$ | Max face-level air velocity to avoid draft | m/s | Louver design |
| 6 | $\dot{V}_{blower} \propto \Delta P_{duct}$ | Blower flow vs duct pressure drop | m³/s | Blower sizing |
| 7 | $\tau = \dfrac{V_{cabin}}{\dot{V}_{blower}}$ | Cabin air turnover time (time constant) | seconds | Response time analysis |
| 8 | $e(t) = T_{set} - T_{cabin}$ | Control error signal | °C | PID controller input |
| 9 | $T_{dew} \approx T_{cabin} - \dfrac{100 - RH}{5}$ | Dew point approximation | °C | Defog requirement check |
| 10 | $\dot{Q}_{latent} = \dot{m} \cdot h_{fg} \cdot \Delta W$ | Latent (moisture) load | W | Dehumidification in A/C |

> [!NOTE]
> In every KTU 2024 numerical on Module 3, the examiner will likely give you two of the three values in the **blend-air equation** ($T_c$, $T_h$, $T_{outlet}$, $m_c$, $m_h$) and ask you to compute the third. **Memorize Equation 1 — it is the single most important formula of this module.**

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Defogging prevents accidents.** A fogged windshield is a leading cause of low-speed urban collisions. SAE and ISO standards mandate demist performance — air routing is therefore a *safety* system, not just a *comfort* system.
- **Energy recovery.** The blend-air method cleverly reuses engine waste heat, which would otherwise be dumped to the radiator. In EVs (where there is no engine heat), a **PTC heater** or **heat pump** replaces the heater core, but the routing philosophy is identical.
- **NVH integration.** Blower speed, duct shape, and outlet louver design are tuned by acoustics engineers to keep cabin noise below **35 dB(A)** at idle A/C operation.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Blend-Air Outlet Temperature

The blend-air equation is the cornerstone of automotive HVAC analysis. Below is the full **energy-balance derivation** that you can reproduce in a KTU 14-mark answer.

### Step 1: Define the System Boundary

Consider a control volume enclosing the HVAC plenum, downstream of the evaporator, surrounding the heater core and blend door. Assume:
- Steady-state, steady-flow.
- No heat loss to surroundings (adiabatic duct walls).
- Constant specific heat $c_p$ of air (≈ **1.005 kJ/kg·K**).

### Step 2: Write the Mass Balance

By conservation of mass for incompressible air flow:

$$\dot{m}_{total} = \dot{m}_{cold} + \dot{m}_{hot}$$

### Step 3: Write the Energy Balance

The total enthalpy carried out by the blended air equals the sum of enthalpies brought in by the two streams:

$$\dot{m}_{total} \cdot h_{out} = \dot{m}_{cold} \cdot h_{cold} + \dot{m}_{hot} \cdot h_{hot}$$

### Step 4: Substitute Enthalpy

For an ideal gas with constant $c_p$, $h = c_p T$. So:

$$\dot{m}_{total} \cdot c_p T_{out} = \dot{m}_{cold} \cdot c_p T_{cold} + \dot{m}_{hot} \cdot c_p T_{hot}$$

### Step 5: Cancel $c_p$

The specific heat cancels from every term:

$$\dot{m}_{total} \cdot T_{out} = \dot{m}_{cold} \cdot T_{cold} + \dot{m}_{hot} \cdot T_{hot}$$

### Step 6: Solve for $T_{out}$

Dividing both sides by $\dot{m}_{total}$ and using $\dot{m}_{total} = \dot{m}_{cold} + \dot{m}_{hot}$:

$$T_{out} = \frac{\dot{m}_{cold} \cdot T_{cold} + \dot{m}_{hot} \cdot T_{hot}}{\dot{m}_{cold} + \dot{m}_{hot}}$$

This is the **blend-air outlet temperature equation** — your key KTU formula.

### Step 7: Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

> **Problem:** An automotive HVAC system delivers cold air at $T_c = 5\ ^\circ\text{C}$ at a flow rate of $\dot{m}_c = 0.08\ \text{kg/s}$ and hot air at $T_h = 50\ ^\circ\text{C}$ at $\dot{m}_h = 0.02\ \text{kg/s}$. Calculate (a) the outlet temperature, and (b) the sensible heat delivered to the cabin, assuming $c_p = 1.005\ \text{kJ/kg·K}$.

**Solution:**

(a) Total mass flow rate:

$$\dot{m}_{total} = 0.08 + 0.02 = 0.10\ \text{kg/s}$$

Outlet temperature:

$$T_{out} = \frac{(0.08)(5) + (0.02)(50)}{0.10}$$

$$T_{out} = \frac{0.40 + 1.00}{0.10} = \frac{1.40}{0.10} = 14\ ^\circ\text{C}$$

(b) Sensible heat delivered to cabin (relative to a reference, say $T_{ref} = 25\ ^\circ\text{C}$ taken as the cabin return temperature, OR more commonly $\Delta T$ between outlet and inlet — we'll use the energy added compared to fully cold path):

For a more meaningful answer, compute the *useful heating* added by the blend, i.e., the enthalpy rise above the cold stream:

$$\dot{Q}_{useful} = \dot{m}_{hot} \cdot c_p \cdot (T_h - T_c)$$

$$\dot{Q}_{useful} = 0.02 \times 1.005 \times (50 - 5)$$

$$\dot{Q}_{useful} = 0.02 \times 1.005 \times 45 = 0.9045\ \text{kW} \approx 0.905\ \text{kW}$$

## 3.2 Derivation of the Dew-Point / Defog Criterion

For windshield defogging, the air reaching the windshield must have a **dew point below the windshield inner-surface temperature**, otherwise condensation re-occurs.

The Magnus approximation for saturation vapor pressure:

$$p_{sat}(T) = 611 \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{17.27 \, T}{T + 237.3}\right)\ \text{Pa}$$

The actual vapor pressure of the conditioned air:

$$p_v = RH \cdot p_{sat}(T_{outlet})$$

The dew point $T_{dew}$ is defined implicitly by $p_{sat}(T_{dew}) = p_v$. A useful KTU shortcut approximation:

$$T_{dew} \approx T_{outlet} - \frac{100 - RH}{5}$$

### Worked Example — Defog Check

> **Problem:** Air leaves the HVAC outlet at **40 °C, 40% RH**. The windshield inner surface is at **25 °C**. Will the windshield fog?

**Solution:**

Dew point of outlet air:

$$T_{dew} \approx 40 - \frac{100 - 40}{5} = 40 - 12 = 28\ ^\circ\text{C}$$

Since the windshield surface temperature (**25 °C**) is **below** the dew point (**28 °C**), the air *will* condense on the windshield. **The system FAILS to defog.** To fix this, the outlet air must be either (a) hotter or (b) drier (lower RH), so that $T_{dew} < 25\ ^\circ\text{C}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Tip:** In defrost problems, always state the *physical reason* (dew point vs surface temperature) — never just say "increase the temperature." Examiners reward the **why** behind the fix.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Mode Selection Logic

| Cabin Condition | Driver Demand | Mode | Recirc. | Blend Door |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Hot soak, T_amb = 45 °C | Quick cool | **MAX A/C** | Recirc | Full cold |
| Hot, sunny day | Steady comfort | **Panel (Face)** | Fresh | Partial cool |
| Mild winter, 10 °C | Warm feet | **Floor** | Fresh | Partial hot |
| Cold, dry, 0 °C | Warm cabin | **Bi-Level** | Fresh | Hot to feet, cool to face |
| Cold, humid, 2 °C | Demist windshield | **Defrost/Floor** | **MUST be Fresh** | Full hot |
| Frost on windshield | De-ice | **Defrost** | **MUST be Fresh** | Full hot + A/C ON (to dehumidify) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Crucial KTU Insight:** During **defrost**, the A/C compressor is still engaged in most modern cars. This is because the evaporator dehumidifies the air (removes moisture), and *dry hot air* defogs far faster than *humid hot air*. The refrigerant system and heater core work **together**, not alternately.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Air Routing Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Outside Air Intake] --> B[Recirculation Door]
    C[Cabin Recirculation Air] --> B
    B --> D[Blower Motor]
    D --> E[Evaporator Always Cold]
    E --> F[Split Flow Path]
    F --> G[Cold Air Bypass Duct]
    F --> H[Heater Core Hot Side]
    G --> I[Blend Door Air-Mix]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Plenum Outlet Manifold]
    J --> K[Mode Door 1 Defrost]
    J --> L[Mode Door 2 Panel Face]
    J --> M[Mode Door 3 Floor Foot]
    K --> N[Windshield Defrost Outlets]
    L --> O[Dashboard Face Louvers]
    M --> P[Footwell Outlets]
    Q[In-Cabin Temp Sensor] --> R[HVAC ECU]
    S[Sun Load Sensor] --> R
    T[Driver Setpoint Knob] --> R
    R --> I
    R --> B
    R --> K
    R --> L
    R --> M
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Temperature Control Closed Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP[Driver Setpoint T_set] --> SUM((Compare))
    TC[Cabin Temperature T_cabin] --> SUM
    SUM --> ERR[Error Signal e_t]
    ERR --> PID[PID Controller in HVAC ECU]
    PID --> ACT[Blend Door Actuator Command]
    ACT --> BD[Blend Door Rotates]
    BD --> OUT[New Outlet Temperature T_out]
    OUT --> CAB[Cabin Air Temperature Updates]
    CAB --> TC
    SL[Sun Load and Humidity Sensors] --> PID
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Mode Selection State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Off
    Off --> MaxAC: Hot day quick cool
    Off --> Panel: General cooling
    Off --> BiLevel: Mild conditions
    Off --> Floor: Cool day
    Off --> Defrost: Cold humid windshield fog
    MaxAC --> Panel: Setpoint approached
    Panel --> BiLevel: Sun load reduced
    BiLevel --> Floor: Cabin warming
    Floor --> Defrost: Fog detected or manual
    Defrost --> Panel: Windshield clear
    Defrost --> Off: System shutdown
```

## 4.4 Functional Architecture Block (Fallback Description)

For a physical layout, the following is the typical sequence of components a parcel of cabin air experiences:

**Intake → Recirculation Door → Blower → Evaporator → (Cold Bypass Duct ∥ Heater Core) → Blend Door → Mode Selector Doors → Outlet Louvers → Cabin Occupant**

- The **parallel** paths after the evaporator (bypass and heater core) are key — this is what makes the blend-air method work.
- The **mode doors** are *downstream* of the blend door, so all air is already at the correct *temperature* before being routed to the correct *location*.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Drawing Tip:** When asked to draw the HVAC airflow path in a KTU exam, always include arrows showing direction, label the evaporator and heater core, and clearly mark the blend door with a curved arrow indicating its rotation. Annotate the outlet streams (face/feet/windshield).

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the term "air routing" as applied to an automotive HVAC system. List any four objectives of air routing.**

**Model Answer:**
Air routing in an automotive HVAC system is the controlled distribution of conditioned air to specific zones of the vehicle cabin (windshield, face, footwell) using ducts, plenums, and motorized mode doors.

**Four objectives:**
1. To achieve uniform thermal comfort for all occupants.
2. To provide rapid windshield defogging and demisting for driving safety.
3. To allow the driver to select the distribution mode as per climatic demand.
4. To minimize blower power consumption by routing air through the path of least resistance.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the function of a temperature blend door? Why is the evaporator kept "always on" in a blend-air system?**

**Model Answer:**
The temperature blend door (also called the air-mix door) regulates the proportion of cold air (bypassing the heater core) and hot air (passing through the heater core) to deliver air at the driver-selected outlet temperature.

The evaporator is kept "always on" because:
1. It continuously dehumidifies the cabin air (essential for defogging).
2. It maintains a stable, low-temperature air source, allowing the blend door to act as a simple and precise mixing device.
3. It avoids thermal cycling stress on the compressor, improving reliability.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the **blend-air method of temperature control** used in modern automotive air-conditioning systems. State its advantages over the earlier water-valve method. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A car HVAC system supplies cold air at **6 °C** with a mass flow rate of **0.06 kg/s** and hot air at **55 °C** with a mass flow rate of **0.04 kg/s**. Calculate the outlet temperature of the blended air and the rate of useful heat added to the cabin by the heater core. Take $c_p = 1.005\ \text{kJ/kg·K}$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** [Refer to Sections 2.3 and 4.1 of these notes for the full sketch and explanation.]

**Key points to mention in the answer:**
- Blower pushes air through the evaporator (always cools it to ~3–6 °C).
- A blend door diverts a portion of this cold air through the heater core (heated by engine coolant at ~90 °C).
- The two streams re-mix downstream; the blend door's angular position sets the final outlet temperature.
- The system is a **closed-loop PID control** with inputs from cabin temp, ambient temp, and sun-load sensors.

**Advantages over the water-valve method:**
1. Faster response (no need to wait for engine coolant to heat up).
2. More precise temperature control (analogue mixing vs. on/off valve).
3. No coolant flow interruption in the heater core, preventing corrosion and air-pocket formation.
4. The evaporator continuously dehumidifies, improving defog performance.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

Given:
- $T_c = 6\ ^\circ\text{C}$, $\dot{m}_c = 0.06\ \text{kg/s}$
- $T_h = 55\ ^\circ\text{C}$, $\dot{m}_h = 0.04\ \text{kg/s}$
- $c_p = 1.005\ \text{kJ/kg·K}$

**Step 1: Total mass flow rate**

$$\dot{m}_{total} = 0.06 + 0.04 = 0.10\ \text{kg/s}$$

**Step 2: Outlet temperature using the blend equation**

$$T_{out} = \frac{\dot{m}_c T_c + \dot{m}_h T_h}{\dot{m}_{total}}$$

$$T_{out} = \frac{(0.06)(6) + (0.04)(55)}{0.10}$$

$$T_{out} = \frac{0.36 + 2.20}{0.10} = \frac{2.56}{0.10} = 25.6\ ^\circ\text{C}$$

**Step 3: Useful heat added by heater core**

$$\dot{Q}_{useful} = \dot{m}_h \cdot c_p \cdot (T_h - T_c)$$

$$\dot{Q}_{useful} = 0.04 \times 1.005 \times (55 - 6)$$

$$\dot{Q}_{useful} = 0.04 \times 1.005 \times 49$$

$$\dot{Q}_{useful} = 1.9698\ \text{kW} \approx 1.97\ \text{kW}$$

**Final Answers:** $T_{out} = 25.6\ ^\circ\text{C}$ and $\dot{Q}_{useful} \approx 1.97\ \text{kW}$.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** List and briefly explain the **various modes of air distribution** in an automotive HVAC system. With the help of a block diagram, describe the role of the **mode doors** in achieving these modes. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **objectives of temperature control** in an automobile. Explain with relevant equations how the **blend-air outlet temperature** is determined, and identify the sensors and actuators involved in the closed-loop control. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Modes of air distribution:
1. **MAX A/C** — Recirculation + full cold air to face outlets for fastest cool-down.
2. **Panel/Face mode** — Air to dashboard louvers only.
3. **Bi-Level mode** — Cool air to face + warm air to feet.
4. **Floor mode** — Air to footwell only.
5. **Defrost mode** — Hot dry air to windshield base.
6. **Defrost/Floor mode** — Air split between windshield and footwell.

**Role of mode doors:** Mode doors are motorized flaps (vacuum, electric, or stepper-motor driven) located inside the HVAC plenum. By rotating to specific angular positions (controlled by the HVAC ECU), they open or close the passages leading to each outlet zone, thereby achieving the selected distribution mode. (Refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid diagram.)

**(b)** Objectives of temperature control — list the five objectives from Section 1.4 of these notes.

**Blend-air equation** — derive as in Section 3.1 of these notes, ending with:

$$T_{out} = \frac{\dot{m}_c T_c + \dot{m}_h T_h}{\dot{m}_c + \dot{m}_h}$$

**Sensors and Actuators involved in the closed loop:**

| Component | Type | Function |
|---|---|---|
| In-cabin temperature sensor | NTC thermistor | Measures cabin air temperature |
| Ambient air sensor | NTC thermistor | Measures outside air temperature |
| In-duct air sensor | NTC thermistor | Measures post-blend outlet temperature |
| Sun-load sensor | Photo-diode | Detects solar radiation intensity on dashboard |
| Humidity sensor | Capacitive polymer | Measures cabin RH for defog logic |
| Blend door actuator | DC servo with feedback potentiometer | Rotates blend door to set mix ratio |
| Mode door actuators | Stepper / DC motors | Position mode doors |
| Blower motor controller | PWM-controlled DC motor | Sets airflow rate |
| HVAC ECU | Microcontroller | PID computation and output coordination |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing blend door and mode door.** The *blend door* controls **temperature** (hot vs. cold mix). The *mode doors* control **distribution** (face vs. feet vs. windshield). Examiners frequently ask: *"What is the difference?"* — and students swap them, losing 3–4 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting to specify units** in numerical answers. Always state **°C** for temperature and **kg/s** for mass flow.
> 3. **Saying "the AC cools the cabin"** in defrost questions. The correct statement is: *"The AC dehumidifies the air; the heater core raises the temperature. Together they produce hot, DRY air that defogs the windshield."*
> 4. **Drawing a missing sketch.** In any 7-mark descriptive sub-question, a labelled diagram is **mandatory**. KTU valuation key typically reserves 2 marks for a clean, labelled sketch.
> 5. **Not justifying why recirculation must be OFF in defrost.** Simply stating "fresh air" is incomplete — state that recirculated air is already humid and will re-fog the windshield.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Air routing** = WHERE the air goes. **Temperature control** = HOW WARM/COOL the air is. Two distinct subsystems, working in series.
- ✅ **The blend door is the "shower mixer"** — it controls the ratio of cold (post-evaporator) to hot (post-heater-core) air.
- ✅ **Evaporator is always ON** in blend-air systems — this is a KTU-favorite concept. Justify it with: stability, dehumidification, reliability.
- ✅ **Defrost MUST use fresh air (recirculation OFF)** and ideally with the A/C compressor ON to dehumidify.
- ✅ **Bi-Level mode** (cool to face + warm to feet) is the most thermodynamically natural and energy-efficient distribution for steady-state comfort.
- ✅ **Master equation:** $T_{out} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_c T_c + \dot{m}_h T_h}{\dot{m}_c + \dot{m}_h}$ — practice it with at least three numericals.
- ✅ **Dew point check for defog:** $T_{dew} \approx T_{outlet} - \dfrac{100 - RH}{5}$. Compare with windshield surface temperature to verify successful defogging.
- ✅ **Cabin design temperature:** 22 °C ± 2 °C (ASHRAE comfort).
- ✅ **Face-level air velocity:** ≤ 4 m/s (avoids draft).
- ✅ **Five objectives of air routing & temperature control:** comfort, defogging, uniformity, fast response, energy efficiency — memorize this list verbatim.
- ✅ **Sensors in the loop:** in-cabin, ambient, in-duct, sun-load, humidity. **Actuator:** blend door motor (DC servo with potentiometric feedback).
- ✅ **Energy insight:** Blend-air method cleverly recovers engine waste heat. In EVs, this is replaced by PTC or heat-pump systems, but the routing philosophy is unchanged.
- ✅ **Always draw a labelled diagram** in 7-mark descriptive answers — it earns you 2 free marks per KTU valuation key.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Mantra for this Topic:**
> *"Blend door → controls temperature. Mode doors → control distribution. Evaporator → always on. Recirculation → OFF during defrost. Dew point < windshield temperature → defog successful."*
> Memorize this one-liner. It will fetch you 14 out of 14 if applied correctly.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Evaporator Air Flow Through the Recirculating Unit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT523 - AUTOMOTIVE REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Evaporator Air Flow Through the Recirculating Unit

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3 — Evaporator Air Flow Through the Recirculating Unit

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In an **Automotive Refrigeration and Air Conditioning (RAC)** system, the **Recirculating Unit** (also called the **HVAC Plenum Module** or **Air Handling Unit (AHU)** of the vehicle) is the integrated housing that contains the evaporator core, blower assembly, heater core, filter, dampers/doors, and the air-distribution ductwork. The phrase *"Evaporator Air Flow Through the Recirculating Unit"* refers to the controlled, conditioned movement of cabin air that is **drawn from the passenger compartment**, **passed across the surface of the evaporator** where it is cooled and dehumidified, and then **re-supplied back into the cabin** through a network of ducts and louvers.

Per KTU 2024 Scheme (PEAUT523, Module 3) the canonical definition is:

> The **recirculating mode** is the operating condition in which the supply air to the evaporator consists predominantly of **return air from the vehicle interior**, blended optionally with a controlled percentage of fresh (outside) air admitted through the **fresh-air inlet door**, in order to maintain cabin temperature, humidity, and air quality while minimising the refrigeration load on the compressor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Module 3 explicitly links the **thermodynamics of the evaporator** to the **aerodynamics of the recirculating air stream**. You must be able to describe the air path, identify the components in the path, and quantify the heat-and-mass transfer between the refrigerant and the air.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of the car cabin as a *living room* and the A/C system as the *lungs of the room*. The **recirculating unit is the throat and nose of those lungs** — it is the place where the room "inhales" its own warm, humid, CO₂-rich air, runs it past a *cold metallic surface* (the evaporator, like a cold ice-pack placed in front of your mouth on a hot day), and then "exhales" chilled, drier air back into the room.

Three everyday analogies cement the idea:

- **Analogy 1 — The Kitchen Refrigerator Coil:** When you open the fridge and hold your hand near the rear coil, you feel *cold air falling*. The same phenomenon happens in a car — cold air from the evaporator is denser and naturally wants to sink, which is why floor-level outlets are used for cooling in many vehicles.
- **Analogy 2 — Window A/C on Recirculate:** Setting a window A/C to "recirculate" means the unit re-uses already-cooled room air instead of pulling in hot outside air. A car in *recirculate mode* does the same — it short-circuits the air loop to cool faster and save compressor work.
- **Analogy 3 — Pulmonary Circulation:** Just as blood recirculates through the heart and lungs, cabin air recirculates through the blower and evaporator, giving up heat (and moisture) on every pass.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- Density of air at standard cabin conditions: **ρ_air ≈ 1.20 kg/m³** (at 25 °C, 1 atm)
- Specific heat of dry air: **c_p,a ≈ 1.005 kJ/(kg·K)**
- Latent heat of vaporisation of water at 0 °C: **h_fg ≈ 2501 kJ/kg**
- Standard automotive blower volumetric flow: **V̇ ≈ 0.10 – 0.30 m³/s** (≈ 200 – 600 CFM)
- Standard cabin air velocity at face level: **v ≈ 0.10 – 0.25 m/s**
- Evaporator face velocity (typical): **v_face ≈ 1.5 – 3.0 m/s**
- Blower static pressure rise: **ΔP ≈ 75 – 500 Pa**
- Standard interior set-point temperature: **T_set ≈ 22 – 24 °C**

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these numbers matter in the exam:** Every formula you will use in the calculation questions on this topic uses one or more of these values. Memorising the *order of magnitude* (e.g., face velocity is in m/s, not cm/s) is the difference between a full-mark answer and a silly unit-mistake.

### 1.4 Block-Level Architecture of the Recirculating Unit

The recirculating unit is a *modular plastic (typically PP-EPDM or glass-filled nylon) housing* bolted behind the dashboard. Its main sub-assemblies are:

1. **Blower Assembly (Blower Motor + Centrifugal/Sirocco Fan + Scroll Housing)**
2. **Evaporator Core** (Aluminium tubes and fins, typically a parallel-flow or serpentine type)
3. **Heater Core** (positioned downstream or in a parallel path)
4. **Air Filter (Cabin Air Filter / Particulate Filter)**
5. **Mode Doors / Dampers** (Fresh-air door, Recirculation door, Floor/Defrost/Panel doors, Temperature blend door)
6. **Expansion Valve or Orifice Tube** (refrigerant-side, fitted to the evaporator inlet)
7. **Ductwork and Louvres** (to deliver air to face, feet, and windshield outlets)
8. **Sensors** (In-car temperature sensor, evaporator outlet temperature sensor, sun-load sensor, humidity sensor in modern systems)

> [!TIP]
> **Exam-Ready Mnemonic — "BFH-FMD-ES"**: **B**lower, **F**ilter, **H**eater core, **E**vaporator, **F**resh-air door, **M**ode doors, **D**uctwork, **E**xpansion device, **S**ensors. Use it in any "list the components" question.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Recirculating Air Path — Step-by-Step Logic

The journey of an air parcel through the recirculating unit follows a strict sequence. KTU examiners frequently award marks for *labelling a clean block diagram* of this path. The flow is described below in seven discrete stages, each tied to a thermodynamic or aerodynamic responsibility.

### Stage 1 — Inlet (Recirculation Door Position)

The **recirculation door** (also called the *inside-air door*) is a rotary or sliding damper that selects the *source* of air entering the blower:

- **Fully Recirculate (Re-circulate) Mode:** Door blocks the fresh-air inlet; 100 % cabin air is drawn.
- **Fresh-Air Mode:** Door opens the cowl intake; ≈ 100 % outside air is drawn.
- **Blended Mode (Auto A/C with Bi-Level):** A controlled mix — typically 20–30 % fresh + 70–80 % recirculated.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why we ever use fresh air at all?** Because prolonged 100 % recirculation causes a build-up of CO₂ (driver drowsiness), moisture on the windshield (fogging), and stale-odour accumulation. The **AQS (Air Quality Sensor)** on premium vehicles automatically cracks open the fresh-air door when pollutant levels exceed a threshold.

### Stage 2 — Cabin Air Filter (Particulate Removal)

The blower **pulls** the air through the **cabin air filter**, a pleated non-woven media (often activated-carbon impregnated) that removes dust, pollen (HEPA-grade in some vehicles), soot, and odours. The filter introduces a **pressure drop** of roughly **ΔP_filter ≈ 30 – 100 Pa** at the design flow rate, and this drop is included in the **system resistance curve** that the blower must overcome.

### Stage 3 — Blower (Pressurisation of the Air Stream)

The **centrifugal blower (Sirocco / tangential fan)** accelerates the air radially outwards, raising its **total pressure**. The blower is the *only* source of mechanical work input to the air stream inside the recirculating unit, and its shaft power is:

$$\dot{W}_{\text{blower}} = \frac{\dot{V} \cdot \Delta P_{\text{static}}}{\eta_{\text{blower}}}$$

where **η_blower ≈ 0.45 – 0.65** for a typical automotive centrifugal fan. The blower speed is regulated by a **linear (PWM) or resistor-based** control unit, producing typically **3 to 7 discrete speeds** in older systems, and a *continuous* speed range in modern electronic systems.

### Stage 4 — Evaporator Core (Cooling + Dehumidification)

This is the **heart of the topic**. The blower-driven air stream now impinges on the **finned surface of the evaporator**. Two simultaneous processes occur:

- **Sensible Cooling:** Air temperature drops from T_air,in to T_air,out.
- **Latent Cooling (Dehumidification):** Water vapour in the air **condenses** on the cold fins and tubes because the fin surface temperature (T_fin) is below the air dew-point temperature (T_dp).

The evaporator therefore acts as a **simultaneous heat-and-mass exchanger**. The energy balance per unit time on the air side is:

$$\dot{Q}_{\text{evap}} = \dot{m}_a \left( h_{a,\text{in}} - h_{a,\text{out}} \right) = \dot{m}_a \cdot c_{p,a} (T_{a,\text{in}} - T_{a,\text{out}}) + \dot{m}_w \cdot h_{fg}$$

In **simplified (sensible-only) form** used in many KTU problems:

$$\dot{Q}_{\text{sensible}} = \dot{m}_a \cdot c_{p,a} \cdot (T_{a,\text{in}} - T_{a,\text{out}})$$

### Stage 5 — Drain (Condensate Removal)

The condensed water collects at the bottom of the evaporator housing and drips out through a **drain tube** (often routed to the wheel-well or underbody). **Standing water in the evaporator plenum is a defect** — it causes microbial growth ("dirty sock syndrome") and reduced cooling capacity.

### Stage 6 — Mode Doors (Distribution)

After the evaporator, the cold air meets a series of **mode doors** that direct it to the:

- **Panel (Face) Louvres** — for upper-body cooling
- **Floor (Foot) Louvres** — for lower-body and feet heating
- **Defrost (Windshield) Outlets** — for clearing the front glass
- **A blend** of the above (Bi-Level, Tri-Level)

### Stage 7 — Heater Core (Optional Re-Heat for Temperature Control)

If **Automatic Temperature Control (ATC)** is active, a portion of the cold post-evaporator air is **diverted through the heater core** (which carries hot coolant from the engine) and then **re-mixed** with the cold bypass air. The blend is regulated by the **temperature blend door (air-mix door)**. This is the physical mechanism that delivers *any* temperature between ≈ 3 °C (full cold) and ≈ 50 °C (full hot) into the cabin, even though the evaporator itself only ever produces cold air.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Strategy:** Print or rewrite this table on a single sheet before any test on this topic. Every KTU numerical problem on Module 3 reduces to one or more of these equations.

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Variables & Units | Typical Range / Boundary Condition |
|---|--------------|--------------------------|--------------------|------------------------------------|
| 1 | Mass flow rate of air | $\dot{m}_a = \rho_a \cdot \dot{V}$ | $\dot{m}_a$ in kg/s; $\rho_a$ in kg/m³; $\dot{V}$ in m³/s | $\rho_a \approx 1.20$ kg/m³ at 25 °C, 1 atm |
| 2 | Volumetric flow from face area | $\dot{V} = A_{\text{face}} \cdot v_{\text{face}}$ | $A_{\text{face}}$ in m²; $v_{\text{face}}$ in m/s | $v_{\text{face}} \approx 1.5 - 3.0$ m/s |
| 3 | Sensible heat transfer (air side) | $\dot{Q}_s = \dot{m}_a \cdot c_{p,a} \cdot (T_{\text{in}} - T_{\text{out}})$ | $\dot{Q}_s$ in kW; $c_{p,a} \approx 1.005$ kJ/(kg·K) | $\Delta T_{\text{air}} \approx 8 - 14$ K |
| 4 | Latent heat transfer (dehumidification) | $\dot{Q}_l = \dot{m}_w \cdot h_{fg}$ | $\dot{m}_w$ in kg/s; $h_{fg} \approx 2501$ kJ/kg | $\dot{m}_w \approx 0.5 - 3$ g/s |
| 5 | Total evaporator load | $\dot{Q}_{\text{evap}} = \dot{Q}_s + \dot{Q}_l$ | in kW | $3 - 8$ kW (typical car) |
| 6 | Sensible Heat Factor (SHF) | $\text{SHF} = \dot{Q}_s / \dot{Q}_{\text{evap}}$ | dimensionless | $0.65 - 0.95$ in comfort cooling |
| 7 | Bypass Factor (BF) | $\text{BF} = (T_{\text{out}} - T_{\text{evap,surf}}) / (T_{\text{in}} - T_{\text{evap,surf}})$ | dimensionless | $0.10 - 0.40$ |
| 8 | Contact Factor | $\beta = 1 - \text{BF}$ | dimensionless | $0.60 - 0.90$ |
| 9 | Apparatus Dew Point (ADP) | $T_{\text{ADP}} = T_{\text{out}} - (\text{BF})(T_{\text{out}} - T_{\text{evap,surf}})$ | in °C | $8 - 12$ °C |
| 10 | Air-side heat transfer (LMTD form) | $\dot{Q} = U_a \cdot A_{\text{evap}} \cdot \text{LMTD}$ | $U_a$ in W/(m²·K) | $U_a \approx 35 - 70$ W/(m²·K) |
| 11 | Blower power | $\dot{W}_b = \dot{V} \cdot \Delta P_s / \eta_b$ | in W; $\eta_b \approx 0.45 - 0.65$ | $\Delta P_s \approx 75 - 500$ Pa |
| 12 | Fan affinity laws (speed change) | $\dot{V}_2 / \dot{V}_1 = (N_2 / N_1)$ | $N$ = rpm | linear with speed |
| 13 | Fan affinity laws (pressure) | $\Delta P_2 / \Delta P_1 = (N_2 / N_1)^2$ | — | quadratic with speed |
| 14 | Fan affinity laws (power) | $\dot{W}_2 / \dot{W}_1 = (N_2 / N_1)^3$ | — | cubic with speed |
| 15 | System resistance (frictional) | $\Delta P = K \cdot \rho v^2 / 2$ | $K$ = loss coefficient | $K_{\text{filter}} \approx 4 - 8$ |
| 16 | Reynolds number (core) | $\text{Re} = \rho v D_h / \mu$ | $D_h$ = hydraulic diameter | $\text{Re} \approx 1000 - 4000$ (laminar-turbulent cross-over) |
| 17 | Recirculation-to-fresh ratio | $\alpha = \dot{V}_{\text{re}} / (\dot{V}_{\text{re}} + \dot{V}_{\text{fr}})$ | dimensionless | $0.7 - 1.0$ (recirculate mode) |
| 18 | Mixed-air temperature | $T_{\text{mix}} = \alpha T_{\text{cabin}} + (1 - \alpha) T_{\text{amb}}$ | in °C | $T_{\text{cabin}} \approx 30 - 45$ °C; $T_{\text{amb}} \approx 30 - 45$ °C |
| 19 | Psychrometric wet-bulb relation | $T_{\text{out}} = T_{\text{ADP}} + \text{BF} \cdot (T_{\text{in,wet}} - T_{\text{ADP}})$ | in °C | used with psychrometric chart |
| 20 | Condensate mass rate | $\dot{m}_w = \dot{m}_a (W_{\text{in}} - W_{\text{out}})$ | $W$ = humidity ratio in kg/kg | $W_{\text{in}} \approx 0.014 - 0.020$ kg/kg |

> [!WARNING]
> **Unit Pitfall:** Most of these equations demand **SI units consistently**. A common KTU valuation error is to mix CFM with kW, or kJ/(kg·K) with J/(kg·K). Convert *before* substituting.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The understanding of evaporator air flow through the recirculating unit is not academic — it is the **foundation of HVAC calibration** in every mass-produced car. In industry, this knowledge is applied to:

- **Cabin Comfort Tuning:** OEMs use *thermal manikins* and CFD (Computational Fluid Dynamics) to ensure that air reaches the driver's torso within 30 seconds of A/C start.
- **Acoustic Engineering:** Blower design must balance air flow against NVH (Noise, Vibration, Harshness) limits — typically < 45 dBA at idle, < 55 dBA at full speed.
- **Energy Efficiency (EVs):** In Battery Electric Vehicles, the A/C system can consume 10–30 % of the battery at high ambient temperature. Optimising the recirculating air path is the *single biggest* lever for range extension.
- **Defogging and Defrosting:** Designing the duct geometry to ensure correct airflow to the windshield is a *safety* requirement, mandated by ECE R1 / FMVSS 103.
- **Hygiene Engineering:** Anti-microbial coatings on evaporator fins and optimised drain geometry prevent the dreaded *dirty-sock* smell.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Recirculated Air Mass Flow Rate from Blower Geometry

**Given:** A centrifugal blower has an outer diameter $D_2 = 0.18$ m, inner diameter $D_1 = 0.08$ m, width at outlet $b = 0.04$ m, runs at $N = 2800$ rpm, and delivers air with a radial velocity coefficient $\phi = 0.45$ and flow coefficient consistent with these dimensions. The cabin air density is $\rho_a = 1.18$ kg/m³.

**Find:** (a) The peripheral (tip) speed of the impeller.
(b) The radial velocity of air at the impeller outlet.
(c) The theoretical volumetric flow rate.
(d) The mass flow rate of air entering the evaporator.

### Solution — Step by Step

**Step (a): Peripheral (Tip) Speed**

The peripheral speed at the outer radius is the linear velocity of the impeller tip.

$$u_2 = \frac{\pi \cdot D_2 \cdot N}{60}$$

Substituting the given values:

$$u_2 = \frac{\pi \cdot (0.18) \cdot (2800)}{60} = \frac{\pi \cdot 504}{60} = \frac{1583.36}{60}$$

$$u_2 \approx 26.39 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step (b): Radial Velocity at Outlet**

The flow coefficient $\phi$ is defined as the ratio of the radial (flow) velocity to the peripheral (tip) velocity:

$$\phi = \frac{V_{r2}}{u_2} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad V_{r2} = \phi \cdot u_2$$

$$V_{r2} = 0.45 \cdot 26.39 = 11.88 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step (c): Theoretical Volumetric Flow Rate**

The volumetric flow at the impeller exit is the radial velocity multiplied by the outlet annulus area:

$$A_2 = \pi \cdot D_2 \cdot b = \pi \cdot (0.18) \cdot (0.04)$$

$$A_2 = 0.02262 \text{ m}^2$$

$$\dot{V} = V_{r2} \cdot A_2 = 11.88 \cdot 0.02262 = 0.2687 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step (d): Mass Flow Rate of Air**

$$\dot{m}_a = \rho_a \cdot \dot{V} = 1.18 \cdot 0.2687$$

$$\dot{m}_a = 0.317 \text{ kg/s} \quad \text{or} \quad 317 \text{ g/s}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** 0.317 kg/s is well within the typical automotive range of 0.10 – 0.50 kg/s. A small hatchback at fan speed-3 would sit near the lower end; a luxury SUV at maximum would sit near the upper end.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Total Evaporator Load and Outlet Air Condition

**Given:** The mass flow rate from Derivation 1 ($\dot{m}_a = 0.317$ kg/s) enters the evaporator with the following conditions:

- $T_{a,\text{in}} = 35$ °C (cabin return-air, dry-bulb)
- $T_{a,\text{wet,in}} = 24$ °C (wet-bulb)
- $T_{\text{evap,surf}} = 4$ °C (average fin surface temperature, supplied by the refrigeration cycle)
- $\text{BF} = 0.25$ (Bypass Factor, given)
- $h_{fg} = 2501$ kJ/kg
- $c_{p,a} = 1.005$ kJ/(kg·K)

**Find:** (a) Apparatus Dew Point (ADP).
(b) Dry-bulb outlet temperature of the air.
(c) Sensible heat load.
(d) Latent heat load (assume humidity ratio drop $\Delta W = 0.003$ kg/kg of dry air).
(e) Total evaporator load.

### Solution — Step by Step

**Step (a): Apparatus Dew Point**

The ADP is the *imaginary* surface temperature at which the air would leave *saturated* (i.e., at the same energy state) if there were no bypass.

$$T_{\text{ADP}} = T_{a,\text{out}} - \text{BF} \cdot (T_{a,\text{out}} - T_{\text{evap,surf}})$$

We will use it after computing $T_{a,\text{out}}$ in part (b), so we mark it pending.

**Step (b): Dry-Bulb Outlet Temperature**

Using the Bypass Factor definition directly:

$$\text{BF} = \frac{T_{a,\text{out}} - T_{\text{evap,surf}}}{T_{a,\text{in}} - T_{\text{evap,surf}}}$$

Rearranging for $T_{a,\text{out}}$:

$$T_{a,\text{out}} = T_{\text{evap,surf}} + \text{BF} \cdot (T_{a,\text{in}} - T_{\text{evap,surf}})$$

Substituting the numbers:

$$T_{a,\text{out}} = 4 + 0.25 \cdot (35 - 4) = 4 + 0.25 \cdot 31$$

$$T_{a,\text{out}} = 4 + 7.75 = 11.75 \text{ °C}$$

This is the temperature of the air **leaving the evaporator and entering the ductwork**.

**Now Step (a) revisited — ADP:**

$$T_{\text{ADP}} = 11.75 - 0.25 \cdot (11.75 - 4) = 11.75 - 0.25 \cdot 7.75$$

$$T_{\text{ADP}} = 11.75 - 1.9375 = 9.81 \text{ °C}$$

**Step (c): Sensible Heat Load**

$$\dot{Q}_s = \dot{m}_a \cdot c_{p,a} \cdot (T_{a,\text{in}} - T_{a,\text{out}})$$

$$\dot{Q}_s = 0.317 \cdot 1.005 \cdot (35 - 11.75) = 0.317 \cdot 1.005 \cdot 23.25$$

$$\dot{Q}_s = 7.405 \text{ kW}$$

**Step (d): Latent Heat Load**

The mass of water condensed per second is the air mass flow rate multiplied by the drop in humidity ratio:

$$\dot{m}_w = \dot{m}_a \cdot \Delta W = 0.317 \cdot 0.003 = 9.51 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/s}$$

$$\dot{Q}_l = \dot{m}_w \cdot h_{fg} = 9.51 \times 10^{-4} \cdot 2501$$

$$\dot{Q}_l = 2.378 \text{ kW}$$

**Step (e): Total Evaporator Load**

$$\dot{Q}_{\text{evap}} = \dot{Q}_s + \dot{Q}_l = 7.405 + 2.378 = 9.783 \text{ kW}$$

And the **Sensible Heat Factor**:

$$\text{SHF} = \frac{\dot{Q}_s}{\dot{Q}_{\text{evap}}} = \frac{7.405}{9.783} = 0.757 \text{ (or 75.7\%)}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why the SHF value matters for exam problems:** Once you know SHF and the inlet psychrometric state, the **outlet state of the air on the psychrometric chart** is uniquely determined. This is the basis of all air-conditioning load calculations in Module 3.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — System Resistance Curve vs Blower Curve (Operating Point)

**Given:** A blower characteristic is given by the quadratic law:

$$\Delta P_{\text{blower}} = 600 - 4 \cdot \dot{V}^2 \quad \text{(Pa, with } \dot{V} \text{ in m}^3/\text{s)}$$

The **system resistance** (filter + evaporator + heater + ducts) follows:

$$\Delta P_{\text{system}} = 200 + 1500 \cdot \dot{V}^2$$

**Find:** The operating point of the system (the value of $\dot{V}$ at which the two curves intersect).

### Solution

At the operating point, the blower pressure rise exactly matches the system resistance:

$$\Delta P_{\text{blower}} = \Delta P_{\text{system}}$$

$$600 - 4 \dot{V}^2 = 200 + 1500 \dot{V}^2$$

$$600 - 200 = 1500 \dot{V}^2 + 4 \dot{V}^2$$

$$400 = 1504 \cdot \dot{V}^2$$

$$\dot{V}^2 = \frac{400}{1504} = 0.2660$$

$$\dot{V}_{\text{op}} = \sqrt{0.2660} = 0.5157 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

The corresponding operating pressure is:

$$\Delta P_{\text{op}} = 200 + 1500 \cdot (0.2660) = 200 + 399 = 599 \text{ Pa}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam insight:** This operating point is the *design point* of the recirculating unit. If the filter gets clogged ($\Delta P_{\text{filter}}$ rises), the system curve shifts upward, the operating point moves *leftward* to a lower flow, and cabin cooling suffers. This is why cabin-filter replacement is a regular service item.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Recirculation Mode Energy Saving

**Given:** With **fresh-air mode**, the evaporator handles air at $T_{\text{amb}} = 40$ °C. With **recirculate mode**, the cabin-return air is at $T_{\text{cabin}} = 30$ °C (because the cabin is partially cooled by previous operation). All other parameters as in Derivation 2.

**Find:** The percentage reduction in the sensible heat load when switching from fresh-air to recirculate mode.

### Solution

The sensible load in **fresh-air mode** is:

$$\dot{Q}_{s,\text{fresh}} = \dot{m}_a \cdot c_{p,a} \cdot (T_{\text{amb}} - T_{\text{out}})$$

$$= 0.317 \cdot 1.005 \cdot (40 - 11.75) = 0.317 \cdot 1.005 \cdot 28.25 = 8.997 \text{ kW}$$

The sensible load in **recirculate mode** is (from Derivation 2):

$$\dot{Q}_{s,\text{re}} = 7.405 \text{ kW}$$

Percentage reduction:

$$\Delta\% = \frac{\dot{Q}_{s,\text{fresh}} - \dot{Q}_{s,\text{re}}}{\dot{Q}_{s,\text{fresh}}} \times 100\%$$

$$\Delta\% = \frac{8.997 - 7.405}{8.997} \times 100\% = \frac{1.592}{8.997} \times 100\% = 17.7\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering interpretation:** Switching to recirculate mode reduces the cooling load by ~18 % in this scenario, which translates directly into lower compressor work, lower fuel consumption (≈ 5–8 % in petrol cars at idle with A/C on), and longer EV range. This is the *quantitative justification* for the existence of the recirculation button on every dashboard.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Parameter | Fresh-Air Mode | Recirculate Mode | Engineering Implication |
|-----------|----------------|------------------|--------------------------|
| Inlet air temperature $T_{a,\text{in}}$ | ≈ 40 °C (ambient) | ≈ 30 °C (cabin) | 10 K lower inlet T |
| Inlet humidity ratio $W_{\text{in}}$ | ≈ 0.018 kg/kg (high) | ≈ 0.012 kg/kg (lower) | Less condensation load |
| Sensible load | 8.997 kW | 7.405 kW | ↓ 17.7 % |
| Latent load | higher | lower | Less dehumidification needed |
| Cabin CO₂ | ≈ 600 ppm | ≈ 1500 – 2500 ppm | Ventilation needed |
| Windshield fog risk | low | high if humid | Auto-mode toggles |
| Compressor work | higher | lower | ↑ Fuel economy |
| Cabin cooling time | slower | faster | Driver comfort |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Note (Read First)

Per the operational protocol, the following block-level functional architecture is drawn using **Mermaid**. All node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters (e.g., `nodeA`, `stepB`). No reserved keywords are used as standalone node identifiers. Labels contain raw uppercase alphanumeric text only, with no markdown formatting inside double-quoted strings.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Air Flow Path Through the Recirculating Unit

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["CABIN AIR ZONE 30 TO 45 C"]
    nodeB["FRESH AIR INLET DOOR"]
    nodeC["RECIRCULATION DOOR POSITION"]
    nodeD["CABIN AIR FILTER 30 TO 100 PA DROP"]
    nodeE["CENTRIFUGAL BLOWER SIROCCO TYPE"]
    nodeF["EVAPORATOR CORE COOLING AND DEHUMIDIFICATION"]
    nodeG["DRAIN TUBE CONDENSATE EXIT"]
    nodeH["TEMPERATURE BLEND DOOR MIX COLD AND HOT"]
    nodeI["HEATER CORE HOT ENGINE COOLANT"]
    nodeJ["MODE DOORS PANEL FLOOR DEFROST"]
    nodeK["PANEL FACE LOUVRES"]
    nodeL["FLOOR FOOT LOUVRES"]
    nodeM["DEFROST WINDSHIELD OUTLETS"]

    nodeA --> nodeC
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeF --> nodeH
    nodeI --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeJ
    nodeJ --> nodeK
    nodeJ --> nodeL
    nodeJ --> nodeM
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **How to read this diagram (KTU marking style):** Trace the path of air from `nodeA` to one of the three terminal outlets (`nodeK`, `nodeL`, `nodeM`). The diagram is drawn so that every component a student could be asked to "label" appears as a named block. Use this in the 14-mark questions where the examiner expects a *labelled schematic*.

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Psychrometric State Path Across the Evaporator

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    state1["STATE 1 CABIN RETURN DRY BULB 35 C HUMIDITY RATIO 0.015 KG PER KG"]
    state2["STATE 2 EVAPORATOR OUTLET DRY BULB 11.75 C HUMIDITY RATIO 0.012 KG PER KG"]
    state3["STATE 3 APPARATUS DEW POINT 9.81 C SATURATED"]
    state4["FIN SURFACE TEMPERATURE 4 C"]

    state1 -- "SENSIBLE AND LATENT COOLING" --> state2
    state1 -- "IMAGINARY STRAIGHT LINE TO ADP" --> state3
    state3 -. "SURFACE TEMPERATURE OF FINS" .-> state4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading guide:** State 1 → State 2 is the *real* air path across the evaporator. State 1 → State 3 is the *imaginary* path if the coil were *ideal* (no bypass). The horizontal distance (State 1 to State 3) divided by the total distance gives the **Contact Factor (β = 0.75 here)**.

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Blower Characteristic vs System Resistance

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    curveA["BLOWER CURVE PRESSURE RISES AS FLOW FALLS QUADRATIC"]
    curveB["SYSTEM RESISTANCE CURVE PRESSURE RISES AS FLOW RISES QUADRATIC"]
    opPoint["OPERATING POINT DESIGN FLOW AND PRESSURE"]

    curveA -- "MATCH AT DESIGN FLOW" --> opPoint
    curveB -- "MATCH AT DESIGN FLOW" --> opPoint
    opPoint -- "REDUCED FLOW IF FILTER CLOGGED" --> degrade["DEGRADED CABIN COOLING"]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Exam linkage:** A 7-mark question may ask *"What happens to cabin cooling if the cabin filter is clogged?"* Answer: The system curve shifts up, the operating point slides left on the blower curve, $\dot{V}$ drops, $\Delta T$ across the evaporator drops (less mass to absorb the same heat), and the cabin cools slower. This single block diagram backs a full sub-question.

## 4.5 Block Diagram — Heat-and-Mass Transfer on the Evaporator Fin

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    finT["FIN SURFACE TEMPERATURE 4 C"]
    airT["AIR BULK TEMPERATURE 35 C"]
    dewP["AIR DEW POINT TEMPERATURE 22 C APPROX"]
    cond["CONDENSATE FILM ON FIN"]
    drop["CONDENSATE DROPLET DETACHMENT"]

    airT -- "CONVECTIVE HEAT FLUX" --> finT
    dewP -- "WHEN FIN T LESS THAN DEW POINT" --> cond
    cond -- "GRAVITY DRAINS DOWNWARD" --> drop
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical for the 14-mark derivations:** The fact that the fin temperature (≈ 4 °C) is **below the dew point** of the cabin air (≈ 22 °C at 35 °C/60 % RH) is the *thermodynamic reason* moisture condenses. This is the single line of logic that justifies the latent load equation in the formula sheet.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model Paper)]**
*Define the term "Bypass Factor" as applied to an evaporator coil in an automotive recirculating unit. State its typical value range and explain the effect of an increasing bypass factor on cabin cooling performance.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Bypass Factor (BF)** of an evaporator is the ratio of the *actual* air temperature drop to the *maximum possible* air temperature drop, mathematically expressed as:

$$\text{BF} = \frac{T_{a,\text{out}} - T_{\text{evap,surf}}}{T_{a,\text{in}} - T_{\text{evap,surf}}}$$

It represents the *fraction of the air mass that does not come into ideal thermal contact* with the cold coil surface, due to fin spacing, air maldistribution, and frost formation. Typical automotive values lie in the range **0.10 to 0.40** (1 mark).

**Effect of increasing BF on cabin cooling:**

- Lower temperature drop across the evaporator (smaller $\Delta T_{a}$)
- Higher outlet air temperature (warmer supply air)
- Reduced Sensible Heat Factor and reduced total cooling capacity
- Required higher blower speed or lower refrigerant temperature to compensate
- A BF above 0.40 is generally considered a *service-defect* condition (2 marks).

### Question A2 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model Paper)]**
*With the help of a neat sketch, list any four components housed inside the automotive recirculating unit and state the function of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Centrifugal Blower (Sirocco Fan):** Provides the mechanical energy to push air through the filter, evaporator, heater, and ducts (0.75 mark).
2. **Evaporator Core:** Transfers heat from cabin air to the refrigerant and condenses moisture from the air (0.75 mark).
3. **Cabin Air Filter:** Removes particulates, pollen, and odours from the incoming air; imposes a 30–100 Pa pressure drop (0.75 mark).
4. **Mode Doors (Damper Assembly):** Direct conditioned air to the panel, floor, or defrost outlets as per the driver's selection (0.75 mark).

(Sketch not shown here for brevity — examiner expects a labelled box with arrows showing air direction.)

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Part A):** Many students write only *one-line* answers for 3-mark Part A questions. KTU's valuation key allocates approximately 1 mark for the *definition*, 1 mark for the *range/equation*, and 1 mark for the *implication*. Always give a **definition + value + implication** triplet.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 Scheme Part B structure, each 14-mark question has two sub-parts, typically **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. A *full* internal choice is provided. Students must answer **one** of the two choices.

---

### Choice 1 — Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With the aid of a labelled block diagram, describe the **complete air flow path** through the recirculating unit of an automotive A/C system, starting from cabin air intake and ending at the discharge outlets. Clearly state the function of **at least six components** in the path. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

The flow of air through the recirculating unit follows a well-defined sequence (see block diagram in Section 4.2):

1. **Cabin air intake (1 mark):** Warm, humid cabin air is drawn into the HVAC housing through the recirculation inlet. The recirculation door — when in *recirculate mode* — blocks the fresh-air cowl inlet so that only cabin return air is used.

2. **Cabin air filter (1 mark):** The air is pulled through a pleated particulate filter (often activated-carbon) that removes dust, pollen, and odours. The filter adds a static pressure drop of ≈ 30–100 Pa, included in the system resistance curve.

3. **Centrifugal blower (1 mark):** A Sirocco-type fan accelerates the air radially, increasing its total pressure by 75–500 Pa, sufficient to overcome the resistance of the rest of the system. Blower speed is varied by a resistor pack (3–4 fixed speeds) or by PWM (continuous control).

4. **Evaporator core (1 mark):** The pressurised air passes over aluminium fins bonded to refrigerant-carrying tubes. Heat flows from the air to the refrigerant (sensible cooling), and water vapour in the air condenses on the cold fins (latent cooling). Air temperature drops by 8–14 K and humidity ratio falls by 1–4 g/kg of dry air.
