5. **Drain tube (0.5 mark):** Condensed water collects at the bottom of the evaporator housing and drains out through a flexible tube routed to the underbody.

6. **Temperature blend door and heater core (1 mark):** In automatic A/C systems, a blend door divides the cold post-evaporator air into two streams. One stream passes through the heater core (carrying hot engine coolant at 90 °C) and the streams re-mix downstream, allowing any intermediate supply temperature to be achieved.

7. **Mode doors and discharge outlets (1.5 marks):** The conditioned air is finally directed by rotary or sliding mode doors to:
   - Panel (face) louvres — for upper-body cooling
   - Floor (foot) louvres — for lower-body and feet comfort
   - Defrost outlets — for windshield clearing and de-icing
   - A blend of the above (Bi-Level for simultaneous face and feet, or Tri-Level for defrost + face + feet).

The entire assembly is enclosed in a plastic (PP-EPDM) plenum that mounts behind the dashboard and connects to the ductwork feeding each outlet.

**(b)** A car cabin has a recirculating air mass flow rate of **0.25 kg/s** entering the evaporator at **32 °C DBT** and **50 % RH**. The evaporator surface temperature is **5 °C** and the Bypass Factor is **0.20**. Compute:

(i) The dry-bulb temperature of the air leaving the evaporator. **[3 marks]**
(ii) The Sensible Heat Load on the evaporator. **[2 marks]**
(iii) The Apparatus Dew Point (ADP) of the cooling coil. **[2 marks]**

**Given:**
- $\dot{m}_a = 0.25$ kg/s
- $T_{a,\text{in}} = 32$ °C
- $T_{\text{evap,surf}} = 5$ °C
- $\text{BF} = 0.20$
- $c_{p,a} = 1.005$ kJ/(kg·K)

**Solution:**

**(i) Outlet dry-bulb temperature:**

$$T_{a,\text{out}} = T_{\text{evap,surf}} + \text{BF} \cdot (T_{a,\text{in}} - T_{\text{evap,surf}})$$

$$T_{a,\text{out}} = 5 + 0.20 \cdot (32 - 5) = 5 + 0.20 \cdot 27$$

$$T_{a,\text{out}} = 5 + 5.4 = 10.4 \text{ °C}$$

**(ii) Sensible Heat Load:**

$$\dot{Q}_s = \dot{m}_a \cdot c_{p,a} \cdot (T_{a,\text{in}} - T_{a,\text{out}})$$

$$\dot{Q}_s = 0.25 \cdot 1.005 \cdot (32 - 10.4) = 0.25 \cdot 1.005 \cdot 21.6$$

$$\dot{Q}_s = 5.427 \text{ kW}$$

**(iii) Apparatus Dew Point:**

$$T_{\text{ADP}} = T_{a,\text{out}} - \text{BF} \cdot (T_{a,\text{out}} - T_{\text{evap,surf}})$$

$$T_{\text{ADP}} = 10.4 - 0.20 \cdot (10.4 - 5) = 10.4 - 0.20 \cdot 5.4$$

$$T_{\text{ADP}} = 10.4 - 1.08 = 9.32 \text{ °C}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning (Part B – 14 marks):** A common student error is to use the **inlet air temperature** instead of the **outlet air temperature** when calculating ADP. The ADP formula *always* uses $T_{a,\text{out}}$ as the reference, not $T_{a,\text{in}}$. Marks are deducted if the formula is inverted. Also, the **ADP must lie between $T_{\text{evap,surf}}$ and $T_{a,\text{out}}$** — if your answer violates this inequality, the substitution is wrong.

---

### Choice 1 — Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 (Alternative)]**

**(a)** Explain the construction and working of a **centrifugal (Sirocco) blower** used in an automotive recirculating unit. Derive the relationship between blower speed, volumetric flow rate, static pressure, and shaft power using the **Fan Affinity Laws**. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction (2.5 marks):** A Sirocco blower (also called a *multi-blade centrifugal fan* or *tangential fan*) consists of:

- A **forward-curved or backward-curved impeller** (typically with 30–60 blades) mounted on a motor shaft.
- A **scroll-shaped housing** (volute) that collects the air leaving the impeller periphery and converts its kinetic energy into static pressure.
- An **inlet ring** at the centre (eye) of the impeller where air enters axially.
- A **DC or BLDC motor** with an integrated resistor or PWM controller that varies the rotational speed from ≈ 1200 rpm (low) to 3500 rpm (high).

The blower is mounted on the **air-inlet side of the evaporator**, pulling the cabin air through the filter and pushing it across the evaporator core.

**Working (2.5 marks):** As the impeller rotates, the blades fling the air outwards by centrifugal force. The air gains kinetic energy inside the impeller passage and then enters the scroll housing, where the divergent cross-section causes a *gradual* deceleration. This deceleration converts kinetic energy into **static pressure** (per Bernoulli's principle). The pressurised air leaves the scroll tangentially and enters the evaporator. The flow through the blower is determined by the intersection of the **blower characteristic curve** (pressure vs. flow) and the **system resistance curve** (also pressure vs. flow).

**Fan Affinity Laws (2 marks):** When a blower's rotational speed $N$ is changed while the impeller geometry is held constant, the following three relationships hold:

$$\frac{\dot{V}_2}{\dot{V}_1} = \frac{N_2}{N_1} \quad \text{(Flow scales linearly with speed)}$$

$$\frac{\Delta P_2}{\Delta P_1} = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^2 \quad \text{(Pressure scales as the square of speed)}$$

$$\frac{\dot{W}_2}{\dot{W}_1} = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^3 \quad \text{(Power scales as the cube of speed)}$$

These are extremely useful in the field: doubling the blower speed (2× flow) requires *eight times* the shaft power, which is why very high fan speeds in electric cars are battery-expensive.

**(b)** A blower delivers **0.20 m³/s** of air at a static pressure rise of **150 Pa** at a particular speed. The blower efficiency is **55 %**. The cabin air is at **30 °C DBT** and **50 % RH**, and the evaporator surface temperature is **4 °C**. If the blower speed is increased such that the flow rate becomes **0.30 m³/s**, compute:

(i) The new static pressure rise. **[2 marks]**
(ii) The new shaft power input. **[2 marks]**
(iii) The new outlet air temperature (assume the same BF = 0.25 holds). **[3 marks]**

**Given:** $\dot{V}_1 = 0.20$ m³/s, $\Delta P_1 = 150$ Pa, $\eta_b = 0.55$, $T_{a,\text{in}} = 30$ °C, $T_{\text{evap,surf}} = 4$ °C, $\text{BF} = 0.25$, $\rho_a = 1.165$ kg/m³ (at 30 °C).

**Solution:**

**(i) New static pressure rise using the affinity laws:**

$$\frac{\Delta P_2}{\Delta P_1} = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{\dot{V}_2}{\dot{V}_1}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{0.30}{0.20}\right)^2 = (1.5)^2 = 2.25$$

$$\Delta P_2 = 2.25 \cdot 150 = 337.5 \text{ Pa}$$

**(ii) New shaft power input:**

$$\dot{W}_2 = \frac{\dot{V}_2 \cdot \Delta P_2}{\eta_b} = \frac{0.30 \cdot 337.5}{0.55} = \frac{101.25}{0.55} = 184.1 \text{ W}$$

**(iii) New outlet air temperature:**

First, compute the new mass flow rate:

$$\dot{m}_a = \rho_a \cdot \dot{V}_2 = 1.165 \cdot 0.30 = 0.3495 \text{ kg/s}$$

This value is *not* needed for the dry-bulb temperature itself (the Bypass Factor relation does not depend on $\dot{m}_a$), but the question's wording justifies showing it.

The outlet dry-bulb temperature is:

$$T_{a,\text{out}} = T_{\text{evap,surf}} + \text{BF} \cdot (T_{a,\text{in}} - T_{\text{evap,surf}})$$

$$T_{a,\text{out}} = 4 + 0.25 \cdot (30 - 4) = 4 + 0.25 \cdot 26 = 4 + 6.5 = 10.5 \text{ °C}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning (Part B – Alternative):** A common trap in this question is to **compute the new pressure by linear scaling** rather than *quadratic* scaling. Marks are lost instantly. Always re-derive the affinity-law expression in your answer sheet to earn the method marks even if the arithmetic slips.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Read this the night before the exam.**

- **Definition Box:** *Recirculating unit* = integrated HVAC plenum containing blower + filter + evaporator + heater + doors + ducts.
- **Air path mnemonic:** *BFH-FMD-ES* — Blower, Filter, Heater, Evaporator, Fresh/Recirc door, Mode doors, Ducts, Expansion device, Sensors.
- **Two simultaneous processes on the evaporator:** *Sensible cooling* ($\Delta T$) and *latent cooling* (dehumidification). The evaporator is a **simultaneous heat-and-mass exchanger**, not just a heat exchanger.
- **Bypass Factor (BF):** $0.10$ to $0.40$ for a clean automotive coil. > 0.40 → service required. Lower BF = better coil.
- **Contact Factor:** $\beta = 1 - \text{BF}$.
- **Apparatus Dew Point (ADP):** *Always* lies between the fin surface temperature and the air outlet temperature.
- **Blower:** Sirocco / centrifugal. Affinity laws — flow ∝ N, pressure ∝ N², power ∝ N³.
- **Recirculate mode** saves ≈ 15–20 % of the sensible load compared to fresh-air mode; the trade-off is CO₂ build-up and fog risk.
- **Cabin filter** adds 30–100 Pa drop; a clogged filter shifts the operating point left and reduces cooling.
- **Standard SI values to remember:** $\rho_a \approx 1.20$ kg/m³, $c_{p,a} \approx 1.005$ kJ/(kg·K), $h_{fg} \approx 2501$ kJ/kg.
- **Standard answer structure (Part B):** (1) State the given data, (2) State the governing equation, (3) Substitute with units, (4) Compute step by step, (5) State the final answer with units and a physical sanity check.
- **Do NOT** mix units (CFM vs m³/s, kW vs W, kJ vs J). Convert *before* substituting.
- **Do NOT** confuse BF with SHF. BF is a *coil property*; SHF is a *room load property*.
- **Do NOT** forget the latent-load term if the question says "total" cooling load or "dehumidification."
- **Always** draw a labelled block diagram for any 7-mark descriptive sub-question.
- **For SHF and ADP problems,** use the *psychrometric chart* if the data is given as wet-bulb — but for KTU 2024 Module 3, the Bypass Factor method is preferred as it bypasses the chart.

---

> **END OF NOTES — Evaporator Air Flow Through the Recirculating Unit | PEAUT523 | Module 3**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Automatic Temperature Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT523 - AUTOMOTIVE REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Automatic Temperature Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Automatic Temperature Control (ATC)** in an automotive HVAC system is a closed-loop feedback control architecture that continuously monitors in-cabin thermal conditions through a network of solid-state sensors and automatically regulates the refrigeration circuit (compressor, evaporator blower, and blend-air doors) to maintain the user-defined setpoint temperature regardless of external load disturbances.

In strict KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, ATC is defined as a **microprocessor-based multi-input/multi-output (MIMO) control system** that integrates the functions of:
- Cabin air temperature regulation
- Air distribution mode selection
- Blower speed modulation
- Compressor ON/OFF cycling

into a single operator-transparent loop governed by a **PI (Proportional-Integral) or PID (Proportional-Integral-Derivative) algorithm** implemented in the HVAC control module (also called the *HVAC ECU* or *Climate Control Unit*).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Unlike a manual A/C system where the driver independently sets fan speed, temperature, and mode, an ATC system treats the *temperature setpoint* as the only independent variable, and *derives* all other actuator commands algorithmically from sensor feedback.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Thermostat" in Your Car

Imagine driving a car on a hot Kerala summer afternoon. In a **manual A/C**, you would constantly fiddle with three knobs — temperature (hot/cold blend), blower speed, and fan direction — to avoid feeling too cold now and too hot two minutes later. It is a *job* the driver must do continuously.

**Automatic Temperature Control is like hiring an invisible chauffeur who ONLY cares about one number — the temperature you asked for (say, 22 °C).** This chauffeur:

1. **Feels** the cabin air (via the in-car temperature sensor).
2. **Feels** how hard the sun is baking the windshield (sun load sensor).
3. **Feels** how hot it is outside (ambient sensor).
4. **Feels** how cold the evaporator is getting (evaporator sensor — to prevent icing).
5. **Decides** how much to open the cold-air door, how fast to spin the blower, and whether to engage the compressor — *all by itself, in real time, every few hundred milliseconds.*

That is Automatic Temperature Control.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key physical constant** governing all ATC behaviour: the **heat capacity of air at standard conditions = 1.005 kJ/kg·K** (often rounded to **1.0 kJ/kg·K** in textbook problems) and the **latent heat of vaporisation of R-134a = 197.86 kJ/kg**. These constants appear in nearly every quantitative KTU problem on refrigeration capacity.

## 1.3 Core Subsystem Map of an ATC

| Subsystem | Function | Realization in the Vehicle |
|---|---|---|
| **Input (Sensing)** | Measure state variables | In-car temp, ambient, sun load, evaporator, coolant sensors |
| **Controller (Logic)** | Compare error, compute correction | HVAC ECU (microcontroller running PI/PID) |
| **Output (Actuation)** | Apply correction to the plant | Blend door actuator, blower motor driver, compressor clutch relay |
| **Plant (Load)** | Physical cabin + refrigerant loop | Vehicle cabin thermal mass + refrigeration system |
| **Disturbances** | Uncontrolled external inputs | Solar gain, occupant body heat, infiltration, ambient drift |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — The Closed-Loop Logic

The ATC system operates on a **negative feedback closed loop**. The control sequence executed every control cycle (typically **100 ms to 1 s** in modern ECUs) is:

1. **Acquisition** — Read all sensor voltages / NTC resistances.
2. **Conditioning** — Convert raw ADC counts to engineering units (°C, W/m²).
3. **Computation** — Calculate the *effective* (target) cabin temperature using:

$$T_{\text{target,eff}} = T_{\text{setpoint}} + \Delta T_{\text{sun}} + \Delta T_{\text{ambient}} + \Delta T_{\text{offset}}$$

where the offsets are pre-calibrated correction factors (look-up tables) stored in the HVAC ECU's EEPROM to compensate for solar loading, ambient extremes, and sensor placement error.

4. **Error Generation** — Compute the control error:

$$e(t) = T_{\text{target,eff}} - T_{\text{cabin,measured}}$$

5. **Actuator Command Generation** — Apply a PI (or PID) law to derive the blend-door position and blower duty cycle.
6. **Actuation** — Drive the blend-air door motor (via PWM or LIN/CAN bus) and the blower MOSFET.
7. **Loop Reset** — Wait the cycle period, return to step 1.

> [!NOTE]
> The *effective* target temperature step (3) is the most testable concept in KTU Module 3. Examiners frequently award 2–3 marks just for correctly writing the corrected setpoint equation with at least two compensation terms.

## 2.2 Sensor Inventory and Their Roles

| Sensor | Variable Measured | Typical Range | Why It Is Needed |
|---|---|---|---|
| **In-car (Cabin) Temperature Sensor** | $T_{\text{cabin}}$ | $-10\,°C$ to $60\,°C$ | Primary feedback for the closed loop |
| **Ambient (Outside) Temperature Sensor** | $T_{\text{amb}}$ | $-30\,°C$ to $80\,°C$ | Detects the air entering through fresh-air door; compensates for HVAC unit efficiency drop |
| **Sun Load (Solar) Sensor** | Solar irradiance on windshield | $0$ to $1000$ W/m² (approx.) | Adds large disturbance to the cabin; must be compensated *before* the cabin drifts |
| **Evaporator Temperature Sensor** | $T_{\text{evap}}$ (typically $-10\,°C$ to $20\,°C$) | Prevents evaporator icing; throttles compressor |
| **Engine Coolant Temperature Sensor** | $T_{\text{cool}}$ | Used for heater blend door logic in winter | Enables the *heater-only* mode when $T_{\text{cool}}$ is high and $T_{\text{amb}}$ is low |

## 2.3 Actuator Inventory

| Actuator | Type | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Blend-Air Door Actuator** | Stepper motor or DC servo | Mixes hot heater-core air with cold evaporator air to fine-tune $T_{\text{outlet}}$ |
| **Mode Door Actuator** | Servo / Vacuum | Selects face / foot / defrost / bi-level airflow paths |
| **Recirculation Door Actuator** | Servo | Toggles between fresh and recirculated air |
| **Blower Motor (with Resistor / FET)** | PWM-driven DC motor | Sets airflow rate |
| **Compressor Clutch Relay (or Variable Displacement Solenoid)** | Electromagnetic | Engages refrigerant flow; in variable compressors, modulates displacement |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarises every equation KTU 2024 expects students to recognise or apply in Module 3 problems.

| # | Equation | Meaning | Typical Use in KTU Problems |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Sensible heat transfer rate (W) | Compute cabin cooling load |
| 2 | $Q_{\text{lat}} = \dot{m} \cdot h_{fg}$ | Latent heat transfer rate (W) | Compute moisture removal by evaporator |
| 3 | $Q_{\text{total}} = Q_{\text{sens}} + Q_{\text{lat}}$ | Total cooling load | Plant sizing for ATC sizing |
| 4 | $\dot{m}_{\text{ref}} = \dfrac{Q_{\text{total}}}{h_1 - h_4}$ | Refrigerant mass flow rate | Relate compressor capacity to cabin load |
| 5 | $e(t) = T_{\text{set}} - T_{\text{measured}}$ | Control error (°C) | PI/PID input |
| 6 | $u(t) = K_p \cdot e(t) + K_i \!\int\! e(t)\,dt + K_d \dfrac{de}{dt}$ | PID control law | Derive actuator command |
| 7 | $T_{\text{eff}} = T_{\text{set}} + K_s \cdot I_{\text{sun}} + K_a (T_{\text{amb}} - 25)$ | Effective target temperature | **Most-tested ATC equation** |
| 8 | $T_{\text{outlet}} = (1-\alpha)\,T_{\text{cold}} + \alpha\,T_{\text{hot}}$ | Blend-door mixing | Where $\alpha$ is the blend door fraction ($0 \le \alpha \le 1$) |
| 9 | $\Delta T_{\text{approach}} = T_{\text{evap,surface}} - T_{\text{cabin,air,inlet}}$ | Evaporator approach | Evaporator sizing / icing margin |
| 10 | $N_{\text{blower}} \propto \dfrac{Q_{\text{load}}}{c_p \,(T_{\text{set}} - T_{\text{evap}})}$ | Blower speed law | Modulating airflow for steady state |

> **Note on units:** All temperatures in °C (or K, with identical differences), $K_p, K_i, K_d$ in appropriate engineering units, irradiance $I_{\text{sun}}$ in W/m². The constants $K_s$ and $K_a$ are ECU-calibrated gains (typically $K_s \approx 0.02\,°C\cdot m^2/W$ and $K_a \approx 0.3$ to $0.5$).

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

ATC systems have moved from a luxury feature (1990s W140 S-Class) to a near-universal mid-segment feature by 2024. Modern implementations use:
- **LIN bus or CAN bus** communication between the HVAC control head and the blower/actuator modules.
- **Auto-defog logic** that compares dew-point of cabin air to windshield temperature to prevent condensation.
- **Pre-conditioning** over the telematics channel, where the cabin is brought to setpoint *before* the driver enters (relevant in EVs to preserve range).

The control strategy directly determines **refrigerant consumption, fuel/energy economy, and occupant comfort index (PMV-PPD)** — making it a high-value topic for any automotive engineer.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Effective Target Temperature Equation

This is the most frequently asked derivation in KTU Module 3. We derive the equation:

$$T_{\text{target,eff}} = T_{\text{setpoint}} + K_s \cdot I_{\text{sun}} + K_a\,(T_{\text{amb}} - T_{\text{ref}})$$

### Step 1 — Identify the Disturbances

The cabin experiences three primary disturbances:
- **Solar gain** through the glazing (proportional to irradiance $I_{\text{sun}}$).
- **Ambient air temperature drift** (proportional to $T_{\text{amb}} - T_{\text{ref}}$ where $T_{\text{ref}}$ is a reference, conventionally $25\,°C$).
- **Internal heat sources** (occupants, infotainment) — ignored in this derivation for clarity.

### Step 2 — Linearise the Disturbance Contribution

Because the ATC compensator must run cheaply on a microcontroller, the controller's response to each disturbance is *linearised* about the calibration point. For sun load:

$$\Delta T_{\text{sun}} = K_s \cdot I_{\text{sun}}$$

For ambient:

$$\Delta T_{\text{amb}} = K_a\,(T_{\text{amb}} - T_{\text{ref}})$$

### Step 3 — Sum the Disturbance Corrections

The corrected setpoint (the "effective" temperature the cabin should *aim for* to *feel* like the user's setpoint) is:

$$T_{\text{target,eff}} = T_{\text{setpoint}} + \Delta T_{\text{sun}} + \Delta T_{\text{amb}}$$

Substituting:

$$\boxed{T_{\text{target,eff}} = T_{\text{setpoint}} + K_s \cdot I_{\text{sun}} + K_a\,(T_{\text{amb}} - T_{\text{ref}})}$$

### Step 4 — Numerical Example (KTU-style)

Given: $T_{\text{setpoint}} = 22\,°C$, $I_{\text{sun}} = 700\,W/m^2$, $T_{\text{amb}} = 38\,°C$, $T_{\text{ref}} = 25\,°C$, $K_s = 0.02\,°C\cdot m^2/W$, $K_a = 0.4$.

**Step 4.1** — Compute solar term:

$$\Delta T_{\text{sun}} = 0.02 \times 700 = 14\,°C$$

**Step 4.2** — Compute ambient term:

$$\Delta T_{\text{amb}} = 0.4 \times (38 - 25) = 0.4 \times 13 = 5.2\,°C$$

**Step 4.3** — Effective target:

$$T_{\text{target,eff}} = 22 + 14 + 5.2 = 41.2\,°C$$

**Interpretation:** To make the *occupant feel* 22 °C when the sun is blazing at 700 W/m² through the windshield on a 38 °C day, the ATC must cool the cabin air down to roughly **41 °C below ambient** (effectively very cold) until the compensated error is satisfied. After cabin thermal mass settles, the blend door modulates so that *sensed* cabin temperature equals 22 °C — but the air *leaving* the evaporator is much colder because of the compensation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's insight:** A common student error is to *add* $T_{\text{ref}}$ to the equation or use $T_{\text{set}} + K_s I_{\text{sun}} - K_a T_{\text{amb}}$. The correct sign convention is that the offset term is **positive** when the disturbance pushes the cabin *hotter* than comfortable.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — PI Control Law for Blend-Door Position

We derive the *actuator command* $u(t)$ sent to the blend-air door using a Proportional-Integral law.

### Step 1 — Define the Control Error

$$e(t) = T_{\text{target,eff}}(t) - T_{\text{cabin,measured}}(t)$$

### Step 2 — Write the Generic PI Law

$$u(t) = K_p \cdot e(t) + K_i \int_{0}^{t} e(\tau)\,d\tau$$

### Step 3 — Map $u(t)$ to Physical Blend-Door Position

The blend door position $\alpha(t)$ (with $0 = $ full cold, $1 = $ full hot) is:

$$\alpha(t) = \alpha_{\min} + \dfrac{u(t) - u_{\min}}{u_{\max} - u_{\min}}\;(\alpha_{\max} - \alpha_{\min})$$

In most practical ATCs, $\alpha_{\min} = 0$, $\alpha_{\max} = 1$, and $u$ is normalised to $[0, 1]$ so that:

$$\alpha(t) = K_p \cdot e(t) + K_i \int_{0}^{t} e(\tau)\,d\tau$$

clamped between 0 and 1.

### Step 4 — Outlet Air Temperature from the Blend Door

With the blend door at fraction $\alpha$:

$$T_{\text{outlet}} = (1 - \alpha)\,T_{\text{evap,air}} + \alpha\,T_{\text{heater,air}}$$

**Sample numerical:** $T_{\text{evap,air}} = 5\,°C$, $T_{\text{heater,air}} = 60\,°C$, $\alpha = 0.25$.

$$T_{\text{outlet}} = (1 - 0.25) \times 5 + 0.25 \times 60 = 3.75 + 15 = 18.75\,°C$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Steady-State Blower Speed Requirement

For steady state, the blower must deliver enough air to absorb the cabin load $Q_{\text{load}}$ and bring the air from evaporator outlet temperature up to the setpoint:

$$Q_{\text{load}} = \dot{m}_{\text{air}} \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{\text{set}} - T_{\text{evap,out}})$$

Solving for mass flow rate:

$$\dot{m}_{\text{air}} = \dfrac{Q_{\text{load}}}{c_p\,(T_{\text{set}} - T_{\text{evap,out}})}$$

Blower speed $N$ is approximately proportional to $\dot{m}_{\text{air}}$ for a fixed duct geometry:

$$\boxed{N \;\approx\; N_{\max} \cdot \dfrac{\dot{m}_{\text{air}}}{\dot{m}_{\text{air,max}}}}$$

**Worked sample:** A small car has a steady-state cooling load of $Q_{\text{load}} = 4.2\,kW$. $T_{\text{set}} = 22\,°C$, $T_{\text{evap,out}} = 5\,°C$, $c_p = 1.005\,kJ/kg\cdot K$.

**Step 1** — Temperature difference:

$$\Delta T = 22 - 5 = 17\,°C \;=\; 17\,K$$

**Step 2** — Required mass flow:

$$\dot{m}_{\text{air}} = \dfrac{4.2}{1.005 \times 17} = \dfrac{4.2}{17.085} = 0.2459\;kg/s$$

**Step 3** — Convert to volumetric flow at $1.2\,kg/m^3$ (standard cabin air density):

$$\dot{V} = \dfrac{0.2459}{1.2} = 0.205\,m^3/s \;\approx\; 12.3\,m^3/min$$

(For a small car cabin, this is a reasonable supply airflow at full blower.)

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Typical KTU Problem Types

| KTU Problem Pattern | Equations Used | Common Mark Distribution |
|---|---|---|
| "Compute effective setpoint given sun and ambient" | Eq. 7 | 7 marks |
| "Compute outlet temperature from blend door position" | Eq. 8 | 3–4 marks |
| "Compute refrigerant flow rate for given cabin load" | Eq. 1, 2, 4 | 7–8 marks |
| "Design PI gains for a given step-response" | Eq. 6 + Ziegler-Nichols | 7–10 marks |
| "Evaluate defog/dew-point logic" | $T_{\text{dew}} = T_{\text{air}} - \dfrac{100 - \phi}{5}$ | 3–5 marks |

> [!NOTE]
> **Equations are referenced from the KTU Formula Sheet in Section 2.4.**

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — ATC Closed-Loop Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram depicts the *complete* ATC closed-loop control system, including all five sensors, the controller, and all four actuator paths.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DIST["Disturbances"]
        sun["SUN LOAD SENSOR"]:::dist
        amb["AMBIENT SENSOR"]:::dist
        occ["OCCUPANT HEAT"]:::dist
    end

    subgraph SENS["Input Sensors"]
        inCab["IN CABIN TEMP SENSOR"]:::sens
        evap["EVAPORATOR TEMP SENSOR"]:::sens
        cool["COOLANT TEMP SENSOR"]:::sens
    end

    ECU["HVAC ECU / CLIMATE CONTROLLER"]:::ctrl
    SET["DRIVER SETPOINT KNOB"]:::input

    subgraph ACT["Actuators"]
        blend["BLEND DOOR ACTUATOR"]:::act
        blower["BLOWER MOTOR PWM DRIVER"]:::act
        mode["MODE DOOR ACTUATOR"]:::act
        recirc["RECIRC DOOR ACTUATOR"]:::act
        comp["COMPRESSOR CLUTCH RELAY"]:::act
    end

    PLANT["VEHICLE CABIN + REFRIGERATION PLANT"]:::plant
    FEED["CABIN AIR TEMPERATURE FEEDBACK"]:::feed

    SET --> ECU
    sun --> ECU
    amb --> ECU
    inCab --> ECU
    evap --> ECU
    cool --> ECU

    ECU --> blend
    ECU --> blower
    ECU --> mode
    ECU --> recirc
    ECU --> comp

    blend --> PLANT
    blower --> PLANT
    mode --> PLANT
    recirc --> PLANT
    comp --> PLANT

    occ --> PLANT
    PLANT --> FEED
    FEED --> inCab

    classDef dist fill:#ffe5b4,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000
    classDef sens fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef ctrl fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000
    classDef act  fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000
    classDef plant fill:#e2d6f3,stroke:#4a148c,color:#000
    classDef feed fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000
    classDef input fill:#d1ecf1,stroke:#0c5460,color:#000
```

### How to Read This Diagram

- **Yellow nodes (DIST)** are *uncontrolled* disturbances entering the plant.
- **Blue nodes (SENS)** are *measured* state variables.
- **Green node (ECU)** is the *controller* — it sees both the driver setpoint and the sensor feedback.
- **Red nodes (ACT)** are the *actuator* commands issued by the ECU.
- **Purple node (PLANT)** is the *physical system* (cabin + refrigerant loop).
- **Yellow feedback node (FEED)** closes the loop from the cabin back to the in-cabin sensor.

## 4.2 Mermaid Sequence — One Control Cycle of the ATC

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant D as Driver
    participant ECU as HVAC ECU
    participant Sun as Sun Sensor
    participant Amb as Ambient Sensor
    participant InCab as In-Cabin Sensor
    participant Evap as Evaporator Sensor
    participant Blend as Blend Door
    participant Blower as Blower Motor
    participant Comp as Compressor

    D->>ECU: Set 22 deg C
    ECU->>Sun: Read irradiance
    Sun-->>ECU: 700 W per m2
    ECU->>Amb: Read ambient
    Amb-->>ECU: 38 deg C
    ECU->>InCab: Read cabin
    InCab-->>ECU: 35 deg C
    ECU->>Evap: Read evap
    Evap-->>ECU: 3 deg C
    ECU->>ECU: Compute effective setpoint and PI law
    ECU->>Blend: Move to 18 percent
    ECU->>Blower: Set PWM to 70 percent
    ECU->>Comp: Clutch ON
    Note over ECU,Comp: Cycle repeats every 500 ms
```

## 4.3 Physical Layout (Block-Level Description)

Although a *physical* duct and sensor layout cannot be rendered in Mermaid, the architectural flow is:

- **Front centre fascia** → Driver setpoint knob + display → sends setpoint over **LIN bus** to HVAC ECU behind dashboard.
- **HVAC ECU** → drives 3–5 actuators over **LIN/CAN**:
  - *Blend-air door* (behind glove box area)
  - *Blower motor* (under passenger footwell)
  - *Mode door* (top of HVAC box)
  - *Recirculation door* (driver side cowl area)
  - *Compressor clutch relay* (engine bay, via engine ECU)
- **Sensor wiring** → Sun sensor on top of dashboard, in-cabin sensor behind steering column or in headliner, ambient sensor in front grille, evaporator sensor in HVAC box on evaporator fins, coolant sensor on engine coolant rail.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**List the four main sensors used in an Automatic Temperature Control (ATC) system and state the function of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Sensor | Function |
|---|---|
| **In-cabin temperature sensor** | Provides the primary feedback of cabin air temperature to the HVAC ECU, closing the control loop. |
| **Ambient (outside) temperature sensor** | Detects external air temperature; used to compensate the effective setpoint and decide fresh-air versus recirculation mode. |
| **Sun load sensor** | Measures solar irradiance on the windshield; used to pre-cool the cabin before the solar load disturbs it. |
| **Evaporator temperature sensor** | Detects the temperature of the evaporator fins; used to prevent evaporator icing and to throttle the compressor clutch. |

*(3 marks — 1 mark for listing sensors, 2 marks for correctly stating functions.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is meant by "effective target temperature" in an ATC system? Why is it different from the driver's setpoint?**

**Model Answer:**

The **effective target temperature** is the *corrected* setpoint that the controller actually aims to achieve at the in-cabin sensor, after applying pre-calibrated offsets for solar load and ambient temperature. It differs from the driver's setpoint because the *sensed* cabin temperature is not the same as the *felt* temperature — solar radiation and high ambient air heat the cabin shell and occupants, so the controller must drive the cabin *colder* than the setpoint numerically to make the occupant *feel* the chosen setpoint. Mathematically:

$$T_{\text{target,eff}} = T_{\text{setpoint}} + K_s \cdot I_{\text{sun}} + K_a\,(T_{\text{amb}} - 25)$$

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for physical reason, 1 for the equation.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** For Part B questions, students frequently:
> 1. Forget to include the **sun load compensation term** and lose 3–4 marks.
> 2. Mix up the *blend door fraction* $\alpha$ — using $0$ as "full hot" instead of "full cold".
> 3. Forget to **convert °C to K** when applying $c_p$ (the difference is the same numerically, but KTU examiners look for the awareness).
> 4. Do not state the **assumption of steady state** before writing $\dot{m} c_p \Delta T$ — always state it explicitly.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of an Automatic Temperature Control (ATC) system used in a modern car. List all the sensors and actuators used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The driver sets the cabin temperature to 24 °C. The sun load sensor reads 800 W/m² and the ambient temperature is 35 °C. Given $K_s = 0.025\,°C\cdot m^2/W$ and $K_a = 0.45$, with reference temperature $T_{\text{ref}} = 25\,°C$, determine the **effective target temperature** that the HVAC ECU should target. Also compute the **blend door fraction** $\alpha$ if the heater air is at 55 °C and the evaporator outlet air is at 4 °C and the desired outlet air temperature is 18 °C. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Block Diagram and Working (7 marks):**

Working of ATC:

1. The driver sets the desired cabin temperature (setpoint) on the climate control panel. The HVAC ECU reads the setpoint.
2. The ECU reads all sensor inputs simultaneously: **in-cabin temperature, ambient temperature, sun load, evaporator temperature, and engine coolant temperature**.
3. The ECU computes the **effective target temperature** by adding compensation terms for sun load and ambient drift to the driver setpoint.
4. The ECU calculates the **control error** $e(t) = T_{\text{target,eff}} - T_{\text{cabin,measured}}$.
5. A **PI or PID algorithm** in the ECU converts this error into commands for the **blend door actuator, blower motor, mode door actuator, recirculation door, and compressor clutch**.
6. The actuators adjust the refrigeration and airflow to drive the cabin temperature toward the effective target.
7. The cycle repeats every 100–500 ms continuously.

**Sensors:** in-cabin temperature sensor, ambient temperature sensor, sun load sensor, evaporator temperature sensor, engine coolant temperature sensor. *(5 sensors, list for 2 marks)*

**Actuators:** blend door actuator, mode door actuator, recirculation door actuator, blower motor (with PWM driver), compressor clutch relay. *(5 actuators, list for 2 marks)*

**Block diagram:** Draw the standard closed-loop ATC block diagram showing sensors → ECU → actuators → plant (cabin + refrigeration) → feedback to in-cabin sensor. *(3 marks for diagram, with all labels)*

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Part (i) — Effective Target Temperature (4 marks):**

$$T_{\text{target,eff}} = T_{\text{setpoint}} + K_s \cdot I_{\text{sun}} + K_a\,(T_{\text{amb}} - T_{\text{ref}})$$

**Step 1** — Solar term:

$$K_s \cdot I_{\text{sun}} = 0.025 \times 800 = 20\,°C$$

**Step 2** — Ambient term:

$$K_a\,(T_{\text{amb}} - T_{\text{ref}}) = 0.45 \times (35 - 25) = 0.45 \times 10 = 4.5\,°C$$

**Step 3** — Sum:

$$T_{\text{target,eff}} = 24 + 20 + 4.5 = 48.5\,°C$$

**Part (ii) — Blend Door Fraction (3 marks):**

The blend door mixing equation:

$$T_{\text{outlet}} = (1 - \alpha)\,T_{\text{evap}} + \alpha\,T_{\text{heater}}$$

Substituting $T_{\text{outlet}} = 18\,°C$, $T_{\text{evap}} = 4\,°C$, $T_{\text{heater}} = 55\,°C$:

$$18 = (1 - \alpha) \times 4 + \alpha \times 55$$

$$18 = 4 - 4\alpha + 55\alpha$$

$$18 = 4 + 51\alpha$$

$$51\alpha = 14$$

$$\alpha = \dfrac{14}{51} = 0.2745 \;\approx\; 0.275 \;\;(27.5\%)$$

**Conclusion:** The blend door must be **27.5% open toward the heater** to deliver 18 °C air to the cabin.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the role of a **PI controller** in the ATC system. With reference to the control law $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int e(t)\,dt$, describe the meaning of $K_p$ and $K_i$ and explain what happens if $K_p$ is set too high or too low. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A car cabin has a steady-state cooling load of **5 kW**. The driver has set the temperature to 21 °C. The evaporator outlet air temperature is 3 °C. Assuming $c_p = 1.005\,kJ/kg\cdot K$, calculate:
- (i) The required mass flow rate of air through the evaporator.
- (ii) The volumetric flow rate in $m^3/min$ at a cabin air density of $1.2\,kg/m^3$.
- (iii) If the maximum blower delivers $0.4\,kg/s$, what blower duty cycle (in %) is required? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Role of the PI Controller (7 marks):**

The **Proportional-Integral (PI) controller** in an ATC system converts the cabin temperature *error* — the difference between the effective target temperature and the measured cabin temperature — into a *command* for the actuators (typically the blend door position and the blower duty cycle). It does this by computing:

$$u(t) = K_p \cdot e(t) + K_i \int_{0}^{t} e(\tau)\,d\tau$$

- **Proportional term $K_p$:** produces an actuator command *proportional to the current error*. A large $K_p$ gives a *fast* response but risks *overshoot* and *oscillation* around the setpoint. A small $K_p$ gives a *slow*, *sluggish* response that may never reach the setpoint (steady-state offset).
- **Integral term $K_i$:** accumulates *past* errors. Its purpose is to **eliminate the steady-state offset** that a pure proportional controller would leave behind. The integral "remembers" how long the cabin has been too hot or too cold and steadily pushes the actuator harder until the error goes to zero.
- **If $K_p$ is too high:** the system *oscillates* (hunts) and the cabin temperature swings above and below the setpoint; occupants feel uncomfortable.
- **If $K_p$ is too low:** the system responds *too slowly*; the cabin may take many minutes to reach the setpoint and the controller may not react quickly enough to disturbances like a sudden change in solar load.
- **If $K_i$ is too high:** the system exhibits *integral windup* — after a long error, the integrator saturates the actuator and causes large overshoot.
- **If $K_i$ is too low:** steady-state offset is never eliminated.

In automotive ATC, the *derivative* term $K_d$ is rarely used because the cabin thermal time constant is large (10–20 minutes) and derivative action amplifies sensor noise. Hence **PI** is the standard choice.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Mass flow rate of air (3 marks):**

At steady state, the air absorbs the entire cabin cooling load:

$$Q_{\text{load}} = \dot{m}_{\text{air}} \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{\text{set}} - T_{\text{evap}})$$

$$\dot{m}_{\text{air}} = \dfrac{Q_{\text{load}}}{c_p \,(T_{\text{set}} - T_{\text{evap}})} = \dfrac{5}{1.005 \times (21 - 3)} = \dfrac{5}{1.005 \times 18} = \dfrac{5}{18.09}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{m}_{\text{air}} = 0.2764\;kg/s}$$

**(ii) Volumetric flow rate (2 marks):**

$$\dot{V} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{\text{air}}}{\rho_{\text{air}}} = \dfrac{0.2764}{1.2} = 0.2303\;m^3/s$$

Converting to m³/min:

$$\dot{V} = 0.2303 \times 60 = 13.82\;m^3/min$$

**(iii) Blower duty cycle (2 marks):**

Assuming linear scaling with maximum flow:

$$\text{Duty} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{\text{air}}}{\dot{m}_{\text{max}}} \times 100\% = \dfrac{0.2764}{0.4} \times 100\% = 69.1\%$$

**Conclusion:** The blower must operate at approximately **69% duty cycle** to maintain the cabin at 21 °C under the given 5 kW load.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use this checklist the night before the exam.

- ☐ **ATC = closed-loop, multi-input/multi-output** control; only the *temperature setpoint* is the user input.
- ☐ **Five core sensors** — in-cabin, ambient, sun load, evaporator, coolant. Always draw all five in a labelled block diagram.
- ☐ **Five core actuators** — blend door, mode door, recirculation door, blower, compressor clutch.
- ☐ **Effective target temperature** equation: $T_{\text{target,eff}} = T_{\text{set}} + K_s I_{\text{sun}} + K_a (T_{\text{amb}} - 25)$.
- ☐ **Blend door mixing** equation: $T_{\text{outlet}} = (1 - \alpha) T_{\text{evap}} + \alpha T_{\text{heater}}$.
- ☐ **PI law** is standard (not PID): $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int e\,dt$.
- ☐ **Cabin load equation** (steady state): $Q = \dot{m} c_p \Delta T$ with $c_p = 1.005\,kJ/kg\cdot K$.
- ☐ **Refrigerant flow**: $\dot{m}_{\text{ref}} = Q_{\text{load}} / (h_1 - h_4)$.
- ☐ **Evaporator sensor role** = icing prevention; do not let evaporator fall below ~0 °C.
- ☐ **Sun load is the dominant disturbance** in Indian/Kerala driving conditions; compensation is *mandatory* in any ATC system.
- ☐ **Sign convention for $\alpha$:** $\alpha = 0$ means *full cold* (100% evaporator air); $\alpha = 1$ means *full hot* (100% heater air).
- ☐ **Steady-state assumption** must be stated explicitly before using $\dot{m} c_p \Delta T$.
- ☐ **Blower modulation** follows load: more load → higher duty cycle.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Duct System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT523 - AUTOMOTIVE REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Duct System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (PEAUT523 / Module 3):**
> A **Duct System** in an automotive air-conditioning (AAC) application is an engineered network of rigid, semi-rigid, and flexible conduits that channel conditioned air from the **evaporator housing (HVAC plenum)** to strategic discharge outlets located at the **driver's face level, foot level, windshield defrost, and rear cabin zone**, while maintaining specified air velocity, static pressure, temperature uniformity, and acoustic comfort thresholds.

The duct system is the *circulatory highway* of any vehicle HVAC module. It accepts cold, dehumidified air from the evaporator coil outlet and distributes it across multiple zones, with selective blending of **fresh air (outside air)** and **recirculated air (inside air)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Building Plumbing of Cabin Air":**
> Think of a duct system exactly like the water supply network of a large hotel. The **evaporator** is the central water tank, the **blower** is the pump, and the **ducts** are the pipelines. Each room (driver, co-passenger, rear seats, windshield) has its own "tap" (outlet nozzle). Just as a plumber must ensure equal water pressure on the 1st floor and the 10th floor, an automotive HVAC engineer must ensure the front and rear passengers receive *equal volume* of cold air, with *minimal noise* and *minimal pressure loss*. A poorly designed duct = weak airflow at far outlets + whistling noise + hot spots in the cabin.

## 1.3 Primary Functional Objectives of an Automotive Duct System

| # | Objective | Engineering Metric |
|---|-----------|---------------------|
| 1 | Uniform air distribution to all zones | Air velocity at outlet: **3 – 6 m/s** |
| 2 | Minimal blower power consumption | Static pressure drop < **250 Pa** (typical) |
| 3 | Low aerodynamic noise (NC rating) | NC ≤ **45** inside cabin at 2500 RPM blower |
| 4 | Rapid cabin pull-down | Time to reach 25 °C from 50 °C ≤ **15 min** |
| 5 | Proper mode switching (face/foot/defrost) | Damper leakage ≤ **5 %** of rated flow |
| 6 | No condensation drips on passenger | Duct surface temp > **dew point** of cabin air |

## 1.4 Classification of Duct Systems (KTU 2024 Module 3 Syllabus Mapping)

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus-Aligned Classification:**

1. **Based on Air Routing Architecture**
   - Direct Expansion (DX) type duct — air is cooled in evaporator then ducted
   - Central Duct System (most common in sedans)
   - Side / Lateral Duct System (SUVs and commercial vehicles)
   - Rear Duct / Roof Duct (buses, vans, MPVs)
   - Mixed / Hybrid Duct System (premium multi-zone vehicles)

2. **Based on Construction Material**
   - **EPP (Expanded Polypropylene)** foam ducts — most common, lightweight
   - **PP (Polypropylene)** injection-molded ducts
   - **ABS (Acrylonitrile Butadiene Styrene)** hard ducts
   - **Aluminum** ducts (premium / off-road applications)
   - **Fabric / textile-reinforced flexible ducts** (rear cabin of MPVs)

3. **Based on Air Temperature Inside Duct**
   - Cold side ducts (downstream of evaporator) — risk of **sweating / condensation**
   - Hot side ducts (downstream of heater core) — used in winter heating mode
   - Mixed ducts (between mode doors and outlets)

## 1.5 Standard Design Inputs (KTU Board Numerical Reference Values)

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value (Sedan) | Unit |
|-----------|--------|----------------------|------|
| Cabin volume (compact sedan) | $V_{cab}$ | **2.8 – 3.5** | m³ |
| Total airflow (blower) | $\dot{V}$ | **300 – 450** | m³/h |
| Number of outlets | $n$ | **6 – 9** | – |
| Outlet velocity (face) | $v_{out}$ | **3 – 5** | m/s |
| Equivalent outlet area | $A_{out}$ | **0.0015 – 0.0035** | m² |
| Main trunk diameter | $D_{trunk}$ | **60 – 90** | mm |
| Maximum allowable pressure loss | $\Delta P_{max}$ | **150 – 250** | Pa |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Typical Sedan Duct System

The duct path begins at the **HVAC module (plenum + evaporator + heater core + blower)** and terminates at one of these outlet groups:

1. **Center / Side Face Vents (A-pillar outlets)** — direct cool air onto driver's chest and face
2. **Foot-Level Ducts** — supply air to front foot-well
3. **Defrost Ducts** — direct hot/dry air to windshield base to clear fog/ice
4. **Rear Cabin Ducts (under-seat or roof-mounted)** — supply to rear passengers

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Focus:** Mode doors (also called *air distribution flaps* or *temperature doors*) are NOT part of the duct *itself*, but they are studied alongside ducts because they determine WHICH ducts receive air in WHICH mode (Face / Bi-Level / Foot / Defrost / Defrost-Foot).

## 2.2 Governing Fluid Mechanics (Step-by-Step Logic)

### Step 1 — Continuity of Mass Flow
Air (treated as incompressible at low Mach numbers, $\text{Ma} < 0.3$) follows the continuity equation across the duct cross-section.

### Step 2 — Energy Equation (Bernoulli with losses)
The total mechanical energy lost between the blower outlet and any duct outlet equals the *static pressure drop* across that duct segment.

### Step 3 — Friction along the duct wall
Surface roughness of the duct material (EPP is *not* hydraulically smooth) creates skin friction which the Darcy-Weisbach equation captures.

### Step 4 — Local losses at fittings
Every bend, reducer, expansion, grille, and mode door contributes a *minor* (local) loss expressed as a fraction of the dynamic pressure $\rho v^2/2$.

### Step 5 — Acoustic constraint
High air velocity inside ducts (typically > **8 m/s**) generates **whistling, popping, and hissing** noises. Designers therefore *prefer larger duct cross-sections over higher velocities* to stay acoustically silent.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Print-Ready Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULA TABLE — DUCT SYSTEM DESIGN**

| # | Equation Name | LaTeX Formula | SI Units | Application |
|---|---------------|---------------|----------|-------------|
| 1 | Volume flow rate | $\dot{V} = A \cdot v$ | m³/s | Duct sizing |
| 2 | Mass flow rate | $\dot{m} = \rho \cdot A \cdot v$ | kg/s | Energy balance |
| 3 | Continuity | $A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2$ | – | Branch analysis |
| 4 | Darcy-Weisbach (friction loss) | $\Delta P_f = f \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{\rho v^2}{2}$ | Pa | Straight duct segment |
| 5 | Local (minor) loss | $\Delta P_L = K \cdot \dfrac{\rho v^2}{2}$ | Pa | Bends, diffusers, grilles |
| 6 | Total pressure drop | $\Delta P_{tot} = \sum \Delta P_f + \sum \Delta P_L$ | Pa | Full duct network |
| 7 | Hydraulic (equivalent) diameter | $D_h = \dfrac{4A}{P_{wet}}$ | m | Non-circular ducts |
| 8 | Reynolds number | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v D}{\mu}$ | – | Flow regime check |
| 9 | Friction factor (Blasius) | $f = 0.0791 \cdot Re^{-0.25}$ | – | Turbulent, smooth pipe, $Re < 10^5$ |
| 10 | Friction factor (Colebrook) | $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = -2 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\varepsilon/D}{3.7} + \dfrac{2.51}{Re\sqrt{f}}\right)$ | – | Generic, with roughness |
| 11 | Outlet discharge velocity | $v_{out} = \dfrac{\dot{V}_{out}}{A_{out}}$ | m/s | Nozzle design |
| 12 | Outlet throw distance (semi-empirical) | $L_{throw} = k \cdot v_{out} \cdot d_{out}$ | m | HVAC reach |
| 13 | Air density (approx, 40 °C) | $\rho \approx 1.127$ | kg/m³ | Standard input value |
| 14 | Dynamic viscosity of air (40 °C) | $\mu \approx 1.91 \times 10^{-5}$ | Pa·s | Re calculation |

### Local Loss Coefficients $K$ (Engineering Reference)

| Fitting | Typical $K$ Value |
|---------|-------------------|
| 90° smooth bend, $R/D = 1.5$ | **0.20 – 0.30** |
| 90° sharp bend | **0.90 – 1.10** |
| 45° bend | **0.30 – 0.40** |
| Sudden expansion ($A_2/A_1 = 2$) | **0.56** |
| Sudden contraction ($A_2/A_1 = 0.5$) | **0.32** |
| Louvered grille (open) | **1.5 – 2.5** |
| Bellmouth inlet | **0.05** |
| Mode door (partially open) | **2.0 – 6.0** |

## 2.4 Engineering Utility (Why This Matters in KTU Context)

- **HVAC OEM Design:** Every global automaker (Maruti, Hyundai, Tata, Toyota, Mahindra) has a dedicated *Duct and Plenum CAE team* using STAR-CCM+ and Fluent to optimize the same equations above.
- **NVH Engineering:** The **whistle tone** at highway speeds is often a duct resonance issue — controlled by choosing the right duct length-to-diameter ratio.
- **Energy Efficiency:** Every **10 Pa** of extra duct pressure drop requires **~1 % more blower power**, reducing EV range by 2–3 km in an electric vehicle.
- **Condensation Control:** Cold-side ducts need either EPP closed-cell insulation or **anti-sweat paint** to prevent cabin floor wetness.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Note:** A 14-mark Module 3 question will almost always involve a *numerical on duct pressure drop* using Darcy-Weisbach PLUS local losses, then conclude with an *outlet velocity check* against the 3–6 m/s comfort range.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivative 1 — Pressure Drop in a Straight Rectangular Duct

### Statement
A rectangular EPP duct has cross-section **120 mm × 60 mm** and length **1.8 m**. Air at **40 °C** flows at **5 m/s**. Compute the friction pressure drop using the Blasius formula. Take roughness $\varepsilon = 0$ (EPP smooth).

### Given Data
- $a = 0.120$ m, $b = 0.060$ m, $L = 1.8$ m
- $v = 5$ m/s, $\rho = 1.127$ kg/m³ (at 40 °C)
- $\mu = 1.91 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s
- $\varepsilon \approx 0$ mm (EPP)

### Step 1 — Cross-sectional area

$$A = a \times b = 0.120 \times 0.060 = 0.0072 \;\text{m}^2$$

### Step 2 — Wetted perimeter

$$P_{wet} = 2(a + b) = 2(0.120 + 0.060) = 0.360 \;\text{m}$$

### Step 3 — Hydraulic diameter

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_h &= \frac{4A}{P_{wet}} \\
    &= \frac{4 \times 0.0072}{0.360} \\
    &= \frac{0.0288}{0.360} \\
    &= 0.080 \;\text{m} \;=\; 80\;\text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Reynolds number

$$
\begin{aligned}
Re &= \frac{\rho \, v \, D_h}{\mu} \\
   &= \frac{1.127 \times 5 \times 0.080}{1.91 \times 10^{-5}} \\
   &= \frac{0.4508}{1.91 \times 10^{-5}} \\
   &= 23{,}602
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $Re \gg 4000$, the flow is **fully turbulent**.

### Step 5 — Friction factor (Blasius, valid for $Re < 10^5$)

$$
\begin{aligned}
f &= 0.0791 \cdot Re^{-0.25} \\
  &= 0.0791 \times (23{,}602)^{-0.25} \\
  &= 0.0791 \times 0.0807 \\
  &= 0.00638
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Friction pressure drop (Darcy-Weisbach)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta P_f &= f \cdot \frac{L}{D_h} \cdot \frac{\rho v^2}{2} \\
           &= 0.00638 \times \frac{1.8}{0.080} \times \frac{1.127 \times 5^2}{2} \\
           &= 0.00638 \times 22.5 \times 14.0875 \\
           &= 2.022 \;\text{Pa}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Result
$\Delta P_f \approx 2.02$ Pa — extremely low, confirming EPP ducts are *aerodynamically excellent*. The dominant losses will come from **fittings, not straight runs**.

---

## 3.2 Derivative 2 — Total Pressure Drop with Bends and Grille

### Statement
Add to the duct above: **two 90° bends (R/D = 1.5)** and **one louvered grille outlet ($K = 2.0$)**. Compute total pressure drop.

### Local Loss Data
- $K_{bend} = 0.25$ (smooth 90° bend with R/D = 1.5)
- $K_{grille} = 2.0$

### Step 1 — Dynamic pressure term

$$
\frac{\rho v^2}{2} = \frac{1.127 \times 25}{2} = 14.09 \;\text{Pa}
$$

### Step 2 — Sum of local loss coefficients

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum K &= 2 \times K_{bend} + K_{grille} \\
       &= 2 \times 0.25 + 2.0 \\
       &= 2.5
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Total local loss

$$
\Delta P_L = \sum K \cdot \frac{\rho v^2}{2} = 2.5 \times 14.09 = 35.22 \;\text{Pa}
$$

### Step 4 — Total pressure drop (friction + local)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta P_{tot} &= \Delta P_f + \Delta P_L \\
               &= 2.02 + 35.22 \\
               &= 37.24 \;\text{Pa}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Result
$\Delta P_{tot} \approx 37.2$ Pa — well within the **150–250 Pa** allowable range for a single duct branch.

---

## 3.3 Derivative 3 — Blower Power Penalty

### Statement
If the entire HVAC module has a total pressure drop of **220 Pa** at 400 m³/h airflow, what is the **blower shaft power** required? Assume blower efficiency $\eta_b = 0.55$.

### Step 1 — Convert volume flow

$$
\dot{V} = \frac{400}{3600} = 0.1111 \;\text{m}^3/\text{s}
$$

### Step 2 — Hydraulic (shaft) power

$$
P_{shaft} = \frac{\dot{V} \cdot \Delta P_{tot}}{\eta_b} = \frac{0.1111 \times 220}{0.55}
$$

$$
P_{shaft} = \frac{24.44}{0.55} = 44.44 \;\text{W}
$$

### Result
About **44 W** of electrical power is consumed by the blower. For an EV with 100 kWh battery, this is **0.044 % of pack capacity per hour of continuous AC use** — a real engineering constraint!

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Design Trade-Off Summary

> [!NOTE]
> **The Six Duct Design Levers (KTU High-Yield):**

| Lever ↑ | Pressure Drop | Noise | Material Cost | Pull-Down Speed |
|--------|---------------|-------|---------------|-----------------|
| Larger duct diameter | ↓ | ↓ | ↑ | ↓ slightly |
| Higher blower RPM | ↑ | ↑↑ | same | ↑↑ |
| More bends | ↑↑ | ↑ | same | neutral |
| Smoother bends (R/D ↑) | ↓ | ↓ | slightly ↑ | neutral |
| Louvers / grilles on outlets | ↑↑ | ↑ | cheap | neutral |
| EPP material (vs hard plastic) | ↓ | ↓ | ↑ | neutral |

**The optimal design point =** largest reasonable duct + minimum bend count + smooth radii + EPP closed-cell foam for the cold side.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set A]**

**Q1. List any three primary functions of the duct system in an automotive air-conditioning module.**

**Model Answer (3 points):**
1. To distribute conditioned air to all cabin zones with uniform velocity (**3–6 m/s**).
2. To minimize blower pressure drop and NVH (target $\Delta P \le 250$ Pa, NC $\le 45$).
3. To route airflow across multiple modes (Face / Foot / Defrost) via mode doors.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set B]**

**Q2. Why is EPP (Expanded Polypropylene) the most preferred duct material in modern passenger cars?**

**Model Answer (3 points):**
1. **Lightweight:** Density **30–90 kg/m³** saves vehicle mass and improves FE/EV range.
2. **Closed-cell structure:** Prevents condensation on the cold side (acts as built-in insulation).
3. **Acoustic damping:** Its porous structure absorbs blower whine, lowering cabin NC rating by 3–5 dB.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3, Full Question Pattern]**

### ⭐ QUESTION A — (14 Marks)

**Q (a)** Explain the different types of duct systems used in automotive applications with neat sketches. Discuss the criteria for material selection. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The major categories of automotive duct systems are:

1. **Central Duct System** — A single trunk runs from the HVAC module centre to the dashboard top, branching into left and right face outlets. Used in **sedans and hatchbacks** (e.g., Maruti Swift, Hyundai i20).
2. **Side/Lateral Duct System** — Ducts run along the A-pillars; common in **SUVs** where a tall dashboard requires long lateral routing.
3. **Rear Cabin Duct System** — Under-seat or roof-mounted flexible ducts; mandatory in **MPVs and buses** (e.g., Toyota Innova) for rear passenger comfort.
4. **Mixed/Hybrid System** — Combines central + side + rear; used in **premium cars** (e.g., Toyota Camry, Tata Safari).

**Material Selection Criteria:**

- Lightweight (target < **1.5 kg** per full duct set)
- Low thermal conductivity (to prevent sweating, $k \le 0.04$ W/m·K for EPP)
- Acoustic damping (loss factor $\eta \ge 0.05$ at 1 kHz)
- Recyclability (EPP is **100 % recyclable**)
- Cost (< **₹250 per duct** for compact car segment)

---

**Q (b)** Air at 40 °C flows at 5 m/s through a 100 mm × 80 mm rectangular EPP duct of length 1.5 m. Calculate the friction pressure drop using the Blasius equation. Take $\rho = 1.127$ kg/m³ and $\mu = 1.91 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Cross-section and hydraulic diameter**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= 0.100 \times 0.080 = 0.0080 \;\text{m}^2 \\
P_{wet} &= 2(0.100 + 0.080) = 0.360 \;\text{m} \\
D_h &= \frac{4A}{P_{wet}} = \frac{4 \times 0.0080}{0.360} = 0.0889 \;\text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Reynolds number**

$$
Re = \frac{1.127 \times 5 \times 0.0889}{1.91 \times 10^{-5}} = 26{,}229
$$

**Step 3 — Blasius friction factor**

$$
f = 0.0791 \times (26{,}229)^{-0.25} = 0.0791 \times 0.0786 = 0.00622
$$

**Step 4 — Friction pressure drop**

$$
\Delta P_f = 0.00622 \times \frac{1.5}{0.0889} \times \frac{1.127 \times 25}{2} = 1.48 \;\text{Pa}
$$

**Result:** $\Delta P_f \approx 1.48$ Pa (very low — EPP is excellent).

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B — (14 Marks — ALTERNATIVE)

**Q (a)** With a suitable sketch, describe the working of the central duct system of a typical sedan. Explain the role of mode doors in air distribution. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The **central duct system** in a sedan (refer to Section 4.1 diagram) consists of:

1. An **HVAC plenum** mounted behind the dashboard centre, housing the blower, evaporator, and heater core.
2. A **central trunk duct** (typically Ø 70–80 mm) that carries cooled air upward and forward to the dashboard top.
3. **Branch ducts** that split this central flow into:
   - Center face vent
   - Driver and co-driver side face vents
   - Foot-level ducts (downward branch)
   - Defrost ducts (uppermost branch toward windshield)

**Mode doors** (also called *air distribution flaps*) are motorized or cable-actuated plates that rotate to open/close specific duct branches. They decide the **mode** of operation:

| Mode Door Position | Cabin Air Path |
|-------------------|----------------|
| Face (default) | Air → face vents only |
| Bi-Level | Air → face + foot |
| Foot | Air → foot only |
| Defrost | Air → windshield slot |
| Defrost + Foot | Air → windshield + foot |

Mode doors are typically made of **glass-filled PP** for thermal stability and seal integrity.

---

**Q (b)** A blower delivers 360 m³/h of air through a duct network. The trunk duct is 80 mm circular and has three 90° bends and one outlet grille. If the total pressure loss in the system is 200 Pa, calculate the blower shaft power assuming blower efficiency of 60 %. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Convert volume flow**

$$
\dot{V} = \frac{360}{3600} = 0.10 \;\text{m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute shaft power using the energy balance**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{shaft} &= \frac{\dot{V} \cdot \Delta P_{tot}}{\eta_b} \\
          &= \frac{0.10 \times 200}{0.60} \\
          &= 33.33 \;\text{W}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Trunk duct velocity check**

$$
A_{trunk} = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.080)^2 = 0.00503 \;\text{m}^2
$$

$$
v_{trunk} = \frac{0.10}{0.00503} = 19.9 \;\text{m/s}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. Forgetting to convert **m³/h to m³/s** — a single line error costs **1 mark**.
> 2. Confusing **shaft power** with **air power** — the efficiency must be in the denominator. Skipping $\eta_b$ = full **7-mark question marked wrong**.
> 3. Not commenting on the trunk velocity of **19.9 m/s** — this is *acoustically unacceptable*; you should recommend increasing trunk diameter to **≥ 100 mm**.
> 4. Writing Reynolds number calculation *after* pressure drop — wrong sequence; examiners deduct **0.5 mark** for logical flow.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **DUCT SYSTEM — KTU Module 3 Rapid Recall (Pin This Before Exam)**

- ⭐ **Definition:** A duct system is the airflow highway between HVAC module and cabin outlets.
- ⭐ **Main functions:** Distribution, mode control, NVH control, dehumidification routing.
- ⭐ **Outlet velocity range:** **3–6 m/s** (face vents), **5–7 m/s** (defrost), **2–4 m/s** (rear).
- ⭐ **Pressure drop budget:** $\Delta P_{tot} \le$ **250 Pa** for full network.
- ⭐ **Most common material:** **EPP (Expanded Polypropylene)** — closed-cell, light, silent.
- ⭐ **Other materials:** PP, ABS, aluminum, fabric-flex.
- ⭐ **Core formulas (must memorize):**
  - $\dot{V} = A v$
  - $\Delta P_f = f \dfrac{L}{D_h} \dfrac{\rho v^2}{2}$
  - $\Delta P_L = K \dfrac{\rho v^2}{2}$
  - $D_h = \dfrac{4A}{P_{wet}}$
  - $f = 0.0791 Re^{-0.25}$ (Blasius)
- ⭐ **Duct types:** Central, Side, Rear, Hybrid.
- ⭐ **Mode doors** control **Face / Foot / Defrost / Bi-Level** modes.
- ⭐ **Condensation rule:** Cold-side duct inner wall temp > cabin dew point (typically use EPP with $k \le 0.04$ W/m·K).
- ⭐ **Blower power link:** Every 10 Pa extra drop = ~1 % extra blower power.
- ⭐ **Acoustic rule:** $v_{duct} \le 8$ m/s to avoid whistle tones.
- ⭐ **Reynolds number check:** Always $Re > 4000$ → turbulent; use Blasius for $Re < 10^5$.
- ⭐ **Exam trick question:** "Why not use metal ducts?" → Answer: **weight + condensation + cost + NVH**.
- ⭐ **Syllabus-aligned question expected:** Numerical on $\Delta P$ + short note on duct types + comparison of materials.

---

### 🔚 End of Notes — Duct System (PEAUT523 / Module 3)

*Compiled per KTU 2024 Scheme Board Examination Pattern. All numerical solutions verified for dimensional consistency and unit integrity.*

topics.title: Controlling Flow
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAUT523 - AUTOMOTIVE REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Controlling Flow # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Definition **Controlling Flow** in an automotive refrigeration and air conditioning (RAC) system refers to the regulation of refrigerant mass flow rate between the high-pressure side (condenser outlet) and the low-pressure side (evaporator inlet). The flow control device is the **expansion device** (also called the **metering device** or **throttling device**), which is the heart of the vapor-compression refrigeration cycle. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition:** > The *flow control device* is the component responsible for: > 1. **Pressure reduction** — dropping refrigerant pressure from condenser pressure ($P_c$) to evaporator pressure ($P_e$). > 2. **Temperature reduction** — achieving the required flash gas / low temperature for heat absorption in the evaporator. > 3. **Metering** — controlling the exact mass of refrigerant entering the evaporator to match the cooling load. Mathematically, the device enforces the **First Law of Throttling** (isenthalpic process): $$h_3 = h_4$$ where subscripts denote the inlet (just before expansion, after condenser) and outlet (just after expansion, before evaporator). ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The Kitchen Tap:** > Imagine a high-pressure water pipeline entering your kitchen. The tap (expansion valve) restricts flow. When you open it slightly, only a small trickle flows out — pressure drops, and water becomes cool as it sprays out (Joule–Thomson effect). Open it more, and more water flows; the temperature drop is less pronounced. The tap **meters** how much water enters your bucket (evaporator), and the **act of squeezing** the flow causes a pressure drop. > > In a car AC, the **expansion valve does exactly this** — but with high-pressure, hot refrigerant vapor on one side and a fine mist of cold, low-pressure refrigerant on the other. **Why is flow control critical?** - **Too much refrigerant** → liquid slugging at the compressor → mechanical damage. - **Too little refrigerant** → evaporator starved → poor cooling. - **Just right** → maximum coefficient of performance (COP) and cabin comfort. ## 1.3 Types of Flow Control Devices in Automotive AC | Device | Use Case | Operation | |---|---|---| | **Thermostatic Expansion Valve (TXV / TEV)** | Older / higher-end cars, bus AC, truck refrigeration | Modulates flow via evaporator outlet temperature/pressure feedback | | **Fixed Orifice Tube (FOT) / Expansion Valve Tube (EVT)** | Modern mass-produced cars (e.g., most sedans, hatchbacks) | Fixed-size orifice, flow varies with system pressure differential | | **Receiver–Drier** | Used with FOT — stores excess refrigerant, removes moisture | Storage + filter + desiccant (NOT a metering device) | | **Accumulator** | Used with FOT — prevents liquid from reaching compressor | Suction-line storage + desiccant | | **Electronic Expansion Valve (EEV)** | Modern hybrid/EV AC, climate-controlled systems | ECU-controlled PWM stepper motor modulation | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Focus:** The syllabus primarily emphasizes the **TXV** and **Fixed Orifice Tube (FOT)**, with mention of related accessories (Receiver-Drier and Accumulator). EEV is increasingly important in modern vehicles and is covered briefly. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Throttling Process — Why Pressure Drops When refrigerant passes through a narrow restriction, the **continuity equation** demands that velocity increases dramatically. By **Bernoulli's principle**, this increase in velocity corresponds to a drop in static pressure. The refrigerant experiences an **isenthalpic expansion** ($h_{in} = h_{out}$), which means no heat is exchanged with the surroundings (adiabatic) and no work is done. The refrigerant enters the valve as a **subcooled or saturated liquid** at high pressure and exits as a **two-phase mixture** (low-quality vapor + liquid) at low pressure. The fraction of refrigerant that flashes to vapor is called the **flash gas fraction**, $x$. ## 2.2 Operating Principle of a Thermostatic Expansion Valve (TXV) The TXV is a **self-actuated, modulating** metering device. It continuously senses the **evaporator outlet temperature** (via a remote bulb charged with the same refrigerant or an inert gas) and adjusts refrigerant flow to maintain a constant **superheat** at the evaporator exit. ### Key Components of a TXV 1. **Remote sensing bulb** — clamped to the evaporator outlet pipe, senses suction-line temperature. 2. **Diaphragm / Power head** — converts bulb pressure into mechanical force. 3. **Pinhole / Equalizer port** — senses evaporator outlet pressure. 4. **Needle & seat** — the actual metering restriction. 5. **Spring** — provides opposing force to set the static superheat. 6. **Adjustment stem** — sets the spring preload (superheat setting). ### Force Balance on the Diaphragm The TXV diaphragm floats in a state of **dynamic equilibrium**. The downward force (opening the valve) is: $$F_{open} = P_{bulb} \cdot A_{diaphragm}$$ The upward force (closing the valve) is: $$F_{close} = P_{evap,out} \cdot A_{diaphragm} + P_{spring} \cdot A_{eff} + W_{diaphragm}$$ At equilibrium: $$P_{bulb} = P_{evap,out} + P_{spring} \cdot \frac{A_{eff}}{A_{diaphragm}}$$ In practice, this gives a constant superheat of **4 °C to 8 °C** at the evaporator outlet. > [!NOTE] > **External Equalizer vs. Internal Equalizer:** > - **Internal Equalizer TXV** — senses evaporator inlet pressure. Used for small evaporators with low pressure drop. > - **External Equalizer TXV** — senses evaporator outlet pressure via a separate tube. Used for large evaporators (bus AC, multi-pass) to compensate for internal pressure drop and prevent hunting. ## 2.3 Operating Principle of a Fixed Orifice Tube (FOT) The FOT is a **non-modulating, fixed-restriction** device. It is a brass tube with a precisely sized orifice (typically **0.5 mm to 1.5 mm** in diameter) located inside a plastic/strainer body. The orifice is sized for a *nominal* operating condition; refrigerant flow varies with the pressure differential across it. ### Mass Flow Through a FOT (Idealized) For an isenthalpic orifice with subcooled liquid inlet: $$\dot{m} = C_d \cdot A_o \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho_{in} (P_{in} - P_{out})}$$ where: - $C_d$ = discharge coefficient (typically 0.6 to 0.8) - $A_o$ = orifice area (m²) - $\rho_{in}$ = inlet liquid density (kg/m³) - $P_{in} - P_{out}$ = pressure drop (Pa) > [!IMPORTANT] > Because the FOT cannot modulate, the system must use a **receiver–drier** (on the high side) or **accumulator** (on the low side) to handle varying cooling loads and to prevent liquid refrigerant from reaching the compressor. ## 2.4 Receiver–Drier vs. Accumulator | Feature | Receiver–Drier | Accumulator | |---|---|---| | **Location** | Between condenser and FOT | Between evaporator and compressor | | **Function** | Stores excess liquid refrigerant + filters + desiccates | Prevents liquid slugging + desiccates + stores excess oil/refrigerant | | **Used with** | Fixed Orifice Tube (FOT) | Fixed Orifice Tube (FOT) | | **NOT used with** | TXV (TXV systems don't need it) | TXV (TXV systems don't need it) | | **Internals** | Desiccant bag (silica gel / molecular sieve), filter screen, outlet tube | Desiccant bag, U-tube or standpipe, filter | > [!NOTE] > **Key Distinction (Frequently asked in KTU):** A TXV system uses **neither** a receiver–drier nor an accumulator in the suction line, because the TXV itself ensures only vapor leaves the evaporator. However, many OEM TXV systems still include a small **receiver** as a safety reservoir. ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula / Relation | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $h_3 = h_4$ | Isenthalpic expansion (throttling) | J/kg | | 2 | $x = \dfrac{h_4 - h_f}{h_{fg}}$ at $P_e$ | Flash gas quality after expansion | — | | 3 | $P_{bulb} = P_{evap,out} + \Delta P_{spring}$ | TXV force balance | kPa | | 4 | $SH = T_{evap,out} - T_{sat}(P_{evap,out})$ | Superheat definition | K or °C | | 5 | $\dot{m} = C_d A_o \sqrt{2 \rho_{in} \Delta P}$ | FOT mass flow rate | kg/s | | 6 | $\Delta T_{flash} = T_{cond,sat} - T_{evap,sat}$ | Useful temperature lift | K | | 7 | $COP = \dfrac{Q_L}{W_{comp}} = \dfrac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$ | Coefficient of performance | — | | 8 | $\dot{Q}_L = \dot{m}(h_1 - h_4)$ | Cooling capacity | kW | | 9 | $h_4 = h_{f,evap} + x \cdot h_{fg,evap}$ | Two-phase enthalpy post-expansion | J/kg | | 10 | $T_{bulb} = T_{evap,out} + SH$ | Bulb sensing relation | °C | > [!IMPORTANT] > **All temperatures in thermodynamic formulas are absolute (K) or contextual (°C difference).** Never confuse gauge pressure with absolute pressure in a KTU numerical — they have cost marks in valuation. ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Cabin thermal comfort** — proper flow control ensures stable evaporator temperatures between **0 °C and 5 °C**, preventing coil freeze-up. - **Compressor protection** — prevents liquid refrigerant (which is incompressible) from entering the compressor, where it would cause **valve plate damage, slugging, or bearing washout**. - **Energy efficiency** — optimized superheat (via TXV) maximizes COP. Excess superheat wastes evaporator capacity; insufficient superheat risks liquid carryover. - **System stability** — modulating devices (TXV, EEV) handle dynamic loads (engine idle vs. highway, sun load changes) gracefully; fixed devices rely on system inertia. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Flash Gas Fraction After Throttling **Given:** Refrigerant enters the expansion device as a saturated (or subcooled) liquid at state 3 and exits at state 4, partially vaporized, at evaporator pressure $P_e$. **Step 1 — Throttling Assumption (Isenthalpic)** The throttling process is rapid, with negligible heat transfer and no work exchange. Applying the steady-flow energy equation: $$\dot{Q} - \dot{W} = \dot{m}\left[(h_2 - h_1) + \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2} + g(z_2 - z_1)\right]$$ With $\dot{Q} = 0$, $\dot{W} = 0$, and negligible kinetic and potential energy changes: $$h_3 = h_4 \quad \text{(First Law of Throttling)}$$ **Step 2 — Identify States on P–h Diagram** - State 3: Saturated liquid at $P_c$ → $h_3 = h_{f,3} \approx h_{f}(P_c)$ - State 4: Two-phase mixture at $P_e$ → $h_4 = h_f(P_e) + x_4 \cdot h_{fg}(P_e)$ **Step 3 — Equate the Enthalpies** $$h_f(P_c) = h_f(P_e) + x_4 \cdot h_{fg}(P_e)$$ **Step 4 — Solve for Flash Gas Quality $x_4$** $$x_4 = \frac{h_f(P_c) - h_f(P_e)}{h_{fg}(P_e)}$$ **Step 5 — Numerical Example (R-134a)** Assume condenser pressure $P_c = 1.2$ MPa, evaporator pressure $P_e = 0.18$ MPa. - $h_f(P_c) = h_f(1.2 \text{ MPa}) = 117.77$ kJ/kg (saturated liquid at 46.3 °C) - $h_f(P_e) = h_f(0.18 \text{ MPa}) = 38.42$ kJ/kg (saturated liquid at -12.7 °C) - $h_{fg}(P_e) = 197.16$ kJ/kg at 0.18 MPa Substituting: $$x_4 = \frac{117.77 - 38.42}{197.16} = \frac{79.35}{197.16} = 0.4025$$ **Conclusion:** Approximately **40.25 %** of the refrigerant flashes to vapor immediately after the expansion device. The remaining **59.75 %** is liquid, which evaporates in the evaporator by absorbing cabin heat. > [!NOTE] > **Physical interpretation:** The flash gas is "inert" from a cooling standpoint — it does not absorb additional latent heat until it is condensed. Hence, excessive flash gas reduces the effective cooling capacity of the system. This is why **subcooling the liquid** before expansion is thermodynamically beneficial. ## 3.2 Derivation: TXV Superheat and Bulb Pressure Relation The TXV is designed to maintain a **constant superheat (SH)** at the evaporator outlet. Superheat is defined as the temperature of the refrigerant vapor above its saturation temperature at the operating pressure. **Step 1 — Superheat Definition** $$SH = T_{evap,out} - T_{sat}(P_{evap,out})$$ **Step 2 — Bulb Pressure Relation** The remote bulb contains a charge (often the same refrigerant as the system, or an inert gas like nitrogen). At equilibrium, the bulb pressure equals the saturation pressure of the bulb charge at the bulb temperature: $$P_{bulb} = P_{sat}(T_{bulb}) = P_{sat}(T_{evap,out})$$ (since the bulb is clamped to the suction line, $T_{bulb} \approx T_{evap,out}$). **Step 3 — Force Balance Equating** From the diaphragm equilibrium: $$P_{bulb} = P_{evap,out} + P_{spring}$$ The spring pressure is calibrated such that it equals the pressure equivalent of the design superheat: $$P_{spring} = P_{sat}(T_{sat} + SH_{design}) - P_{sat}(T_{sat})$$ **Step 4 — Final Expression** For a fixed superheat setting $SH$: $$P_{sat}(T_{sat} + SH) = P_{sat}(T_{sat}) + P_{spring}$$ This is a transcendental relation — solved numerically or via refrigerant tables. For small superheats (3 °C to 8 °C), the relation is approximately linear. ## 3.3 Derivation: Mass Flow Rate Through a Fixed Orifice Tube **Step 1 — Bernoulli Equation Across the Orifice** For an incompressible fluid across an idealized orifice: $$\frac{P_{in}}{\rho} + \frac{V_{in}^2}{2} = \frac{P_{out}}{\rho} + \frac{V_{out}^2}{2}$$ With $V_{in} \ll V_{out}$ (inlet is large bore, outlet is restricted): $$V_{out,ideal} = \sqrt{\frac{2(P_{in} - P_{out})}{\rho}}$$ **Step 2 — Apply Discharge Coefficient** Real orifices suffer vena contracta and friction losses: $$V_{out,actual} = C_d \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2(P_{in} - P_{out})}{\rho}}$$ **Step 3 — Mass Flow Rate** $$\dot{m} = \rho \cdot A_o \cdot V_{out,actual} = C_d \cdot A_o \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho (P_{in} - P_{out})}$$ **Step 4 — Numerical Example (FOT Sizing)** Given: - $A_o = \pi/4 \cdot (1.0 \times 10^{-3})^2 = 7.85 \times 10^{-7}$ m² - $\rho = 1200$ kg/m³ (subcooled R-134a at 40 °C) - $\Delta P = 1.2 \text{ MPa} - 0.18 \text{ MPa} = 1.02 \times 10^6$ Pa - $C_d = 0.65$ Substituting: $$\dot{m} = 0.65 \times 7.85 \times 10^{-7} \times \sqrt{2 \times 1200 \times 1.02 \times 10^6}$$ $$= 0.65 \times 7.85 \times 10^{-7} \times \sqrt{2.448 \times 10^9}$$ $$= 0.65 \times 7.85 \times 10^{-7} \times 4.948 \times 10^4$$ $$= 0.65 \times 7.85 \times 10^{-7} \times 4.948 \times 10^4$$ $$= 25.24 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s} = 0.0252 \text{ kg/s} \approx 90.9 \text{ kg/hr}$$ **Conclusion:** A 1 mm diameter FOT delivers approximately **90.9 kg/hr** of refrigerant under these conditions. ## 3.4 Comparative Analysis Matrix — TXV vs. FOT | Parameter | TXV | FOT | |---|---|---| | **Modulation** | Yes — modulates with load | No — fixed | | **Superheat** | Maintained constant (4–8 °C) | Variable with load | | **Evaporator utilization** | High (full coil wet under all loads) | Lower at low loads (overfeed) | | **Compressor protection** | Excellent (no liquid carryover) | Good (with accumulator) | | **Sensitivity to charge** | Low (tolerates some undercharge) | High (sensitive to charge) | | **Cost** | Higher | Lower | | **Use case** | Large/thermostatic systems, bus AC, truck refrigeration | Mass-market car AC (Toyota, Honda, Ford, Maruti) | | **Hunting risk** | Yes (especially with internal equalizer) | None | | **Response to load change** | Fast | Slow (relies on system pressures to equalize) | ## 3.5 Hunting in TXV — Causes and Remedies **Hunting** is the cyclic opening/closing of a TXV at low loads, causing unstable evaporator temperatures. **Causes:** - Oversized TXV (valve hunting because small flow variations cause large superheat swings). - Internal equalizer used with multi-pass evaporator (high pressure drop). - Low load conditions (very small evaporator flow rates). **Remedies:** - Use **external equalizer** TXV. - Use a TXV with smaller capacity rating. - Add a **distributor** (refrigerant distributor) ahead of the evaporator for uniform feed. - Replace the TXV with an **EEV** for very low load applications. # 4. [KTU University Exam – June 2024] **Differentiate between a Thermostatic Expansion Valve (TXV) and a Fixed Orifice Tube (FOT). Mention one application of each in automotive AC systems.** **Model Answer:** | Feature | TXV | FOT | |---|---|---| | Modulation | Modulates with cooling load | Fixed restriction | | Superheat | Maintains constant SH (4–8 °C) | Variable superheat | | Feedback | Senses evap outlet via remote bulb | No feedback | | Cost | Higher | Lower | | Application | Bus AC, large truck refrigeration | Mass-market car AC (e.g., Maruti Swift, Honda City) | > [!NOTE] > **Valuation Tip:** Listing the modulation and superheat features alone is sufficient for 3 marks. Avoid vague descriptions like "TXV is better" — KTU examiners reward *specific differentiation*. ### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **State the function of a receiver–drier in an automotive AC system. Why is it used with a FOT and not with a TXV?** **Model Answer:** - The receiver–drier (i) **stores excess liquid refrigerant**, (ii) **filters** solid contaminants, and (iii) **removes moisture** via a desiccant (silica gel or molecular sieve). - It is used with a **FOT** because the FOT cannot modulate — at low loads, excess refrigerant must be stored to prevent overfeed of the evaporator. The TXV, by contrast, modulates flow precisely, so excess liquid does not build up in the high side; hence a receiver–drier is unnecessary. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### Question A — [KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper 2024 Scheme] **(a)** Explain the working principle of a **Thermostatic Expansion Valve (TXV)** with a neat force-balance diagram.
