Discuss the role of the remote bulb, diaphragm, and equalizer port. (7 Marks) **(b)** A **Fixed Orifice Tube (FOT)** has an orifice diameter of **1.2 mm** and a discharge coefficient of **0.65**. Subcooled liquid R-134a enters at **40 °C** and **1.2 MPa**, and the evaporator pressure is **0.2 MPa**. Determine: (i) mass flow rate, (ii) flash gas fraction at FOT outlet, and (iii) cooling capacity if the evaporator outlet is at **5 °C**. (7 Marks) --- #### Model Solution — Q A (a) **1. Introduction (1 mark):** A TXV is a modulating flow control device that maintains a **constant superheat** at the evaporator outlet by balancing three forces on a diaphragm. **2. Components (2 marks):** - **Remote sensing bulb** — clamped to the suction line at the evaporator outlet; contains a charge (R-134a or inert gas) whose saturation pressure varies with bulb temperature. - **Diaphragm** — the membrane that translates the bulb pressure into a mechanical force. - **Needle and seat** — the actual throttling restriction. - **Spring** — provides the static superheat setting. - **Equalizer port** — senses evaporator outlet pressure; the equalizer may be internal (sense at evap inlet) or external (sense at evap outlet). **3. Working Principle (3 marks):** - When the **evaporator load increases**, refrigerant in the evaporator boils faster, the suction-line temperature rises, the bulb pressure rises, and the diaphragm is pushed **downward** — opening the valve to admit more refrigerant. - When the **evaporator load decreases**, suction-line temperature falls, bulb pressure drops, and the spring pushes the diaphragm **upward** — closing the valve to reduce refrigerant flow. - At equilibrium, the **superheat** (typically 4–8 °C) is held constant, ensuring full evaporator utilization without liquid carryover. **4. Force Balance (1 mark):** $$P_{bulb} = P_{evap,out} + P_{spring}$$ > [!NOTE] > **External Equalizer** is preferred for multi-pass evaporators with high internal pressure drop — it senses the *true* evaporator outlet pressure, avoiding hunting. --- #### Model Solution — Q A (b) **Given:** - $d_o = 1.2 \text{ mm} = 1.2 \times 10^{-3}$ m - $C_d = 0.65$ - $T_{in} = 40$ °C, $P_{in} = 1.2$ MPa - $P_{out} = 0.2$ MPa - $T_{evap,out} = 5$ °C **Step 1 — Orifice Area** $$A_o = \frac{\pi}{4} d_o^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (1.2 \times 10^{-3})^2 = 1.131 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2$$ **Step 2 — Inlet Density of Subcooled R-134a** From R-134a tables at 40 °C (subcooled liquid, $P_{sat} = 1.017$ MPa — subcooled by ~0.18 MPa): $\rho_{in} \approx 1146$ kg/m³ (approx. saturated liquid density at 40 °C since subcooling is small). **Step 3 — Mass Flow Rate (2 marks)** $$\dot{m} = C_d A_o \sqrt{2 \rho_{in} (P_{in} - P_{out})}$$ $$\Delta P = 1.2 \times 10^6 - 0.2 \times 10^6 = 1.0 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$$ $$\dot{m} = 0.65 \times 1.131 \times 10^{-6} \times \sqrt{2 \times 1146 \times 1.0 \times 10^6}$$ $$= 0.65 \times 1.131 \times 10^{-6} \times \sqrt{2.292 \times 10^9}$$ $$= 0.65 \times 1.131 \times 10^{-6} \times 4.787 \times 10^4$$ $$\dot{m} = 0.0352 \text{ kg/s} \approx 126.7 \text{ kg/hr}$$ **Step 4 — Flash Gas Quality (2 marks)** From R-134a tables: - $h_f(P_{in}) = h_f(1.2 \text{ MPa}) = 117.77$ kJ/kg - $h_f(P_{out}) = h_f(0.2 \text{ MPa}) = 38.43$ kJ/kg - $h_{fg}(P_{out}) = 196.71$ kJ/kg at 0.2 MPa $$x_4 = \frac{h_f(P_{in}) - h_f(P_{out})}{h_{fg}(P_{out})} = \frac{117.77 - 38.43}{196.71} = \frac{79.34}{196.71} = 0.4033$$ **Flash gas fraction ≈ 40.33 %** **Step 5 — Cooling Capacity (2 marks)** At evaporator outlet, $T_1 = 5$ °C, $P_{evap} = 0.2$ MPa. Saturation temperature at 0.2 MPa is $T_{sat} = -10.09$ °C. Hence $SH = 5 - (-10.09) = 15.09$ °C. From superheated R-134a tables at 0.2 MPa and 5 °C: $h_1 \approx 254.0$ kJ/kg (interpolated, approx. value). $$\dot{Q}_L = \dot{m}(h_1 - h_4)$$ $$h_4 = h_f(P_{out}) + x_4 \cdot h_{fg}(P_{out}) = 38.43 + 0.4033 \times 196.71 = 38.43 + 79.34 = 117.77 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ (consistent with throttling assumption) $$\dot{Q}_L = 0.0352 \times (254.0 - 117.77) = 0.0352 \times 136.23 = 4.795 \text{ kW}$$ **Cooling capacity ≈ 4.80 kW (≈ 1.37 TR)**. --- ### Question B — Alternative Choice (Internal Choice) **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the construction and working of a **Receiver–Drier** used in an automotive AC system fitted with a FOT. How does it differ from an **Accumulator**? (7 Marks) **(b)** An R-134a refrigeration system has the following operating data: - Compressor inlet (state 1): $T_1 = 5$ °C, $P_1 = 0.2$ MPa - Compressor outlet (state 2): $T_2 = 70$ °C, $P_2 = 1.2$ MPa - Condenser outlet (state 3): saturated liquid at 1.2 MPa - Evaporator inlet (state 4): two-phase mixture at 0.2 MPa Determine: (i) **COP** of the system, (ii) **compressor work**, and (iii) **flash gas fraction** at the expansion device. (7 Marks) --- #### Model Solution — Q B (a) **1. Construction of Receiver–Drier (3 marks):** - **Cylindrical metal body** (usually aluminum) installed between the condenser outlet and the FOT. - **Inlet port** at one end (from condenser), with a **filter screen** to trap solid debris. - **Desiccant bag** (silica gel or molecular sieve, e.g., 3A type) to absorb moisture. - **Outlet tube** (sometimes a standpipe) drawing liquid refrigerant from the bottom of the receiver. - **Pressure relief valve** and **service port** for charging/discharging refrigerant. **2. Working (2 marks):** - Hot, high-pressure liquid refrigerant from the condenser enters the receiver. - The receiver **stores the excess refrigerant** that is not immediately needed by the evaporator. - The desiccant **removes any moisture** that may have entered during servicing or via leakage. - The filter screen removes metal particles and contaminants. - Dry, clean liquid refrigerant flows to the FOT. **3. Difference from Accumulator (2 marks):** | Feature | Receiver–Drier | Accumulator | |---|---|---| | Location | High side (after condenser) | Low side (after evaporator) | | Used with | FOT systems | FOT systems | | Primary function | Store excess liquid refrigerant | Prevent liquid carryover to compressor | | Outlet | Standpipe picks up liquid from bottom | Standpipe picks up vapour from top | --- #### Model Solution — Q B (b) **Given:** R-134a system data as above. **Step 1 — Determine Enthalpies from R-134a Tables (2 marks):** - $h_1$ (superheated vapour at 0.2 MPa, 5 °C): $h_1 \approx 254.0$ kJ/kg - $h_2$ (superheated vapour at 1.2 MPa, 70 °C): $h_2 \approx 295.16$ kJ/kg - $h_3 = h_f$ at 1.2 MPa = 117.77 kJ/kg - $h_4 = h_3 = 117.77$ kJ/kg (throttling) **Step 2 — Flash Gas Fraction (1 mark):** $$x_4 = \frac{h_3 - h_f(P_{evap})}{h_{fg}(P_{evap})} = \frac{117.77 - 38.43}{196.71} = 0.4033$$ **Flash gas fraction ≈ 40.33 %** **Step 3 — Compressor Work per kg (1 mark):** $$w_{comp} = h_2 - h_1 = 295.16 - 254.0 = 41.16 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Step 4 — Cooling Effect per kg (1 mark):** $$q_L = h_1 - h_4 = 254.0 - 117.77 = 136.23 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Step 5 — COP (1 mark):** $$COP = \frac{q_L}{w_{comp}} = \frac{136.23}{41.16} = 3.31$$ **Step 6 — Compressor Work in kW (1 mark):** Assuming refrigerant mass flow rate $\dot{m} = 0.05$ kg/s (a typical value for a car AC): $$\dot{W}_{comp} = \dot{m} \cdot w_{comp} = 0.05 \times 41.16 = 2.058 \text{ kW}$$ (≈ 2.76 hp input to compressor) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Units consistency:** Always express COP as a dimensionless ratio; do not write "kW/kW" in the answer. > 2. **State identification:** A common mistake is to read $h_2$ at saturation instead of superheat. Always verify $T_2 > T_{sat}(P_2)$. > 3. **Throttling assumption:** $h_3 = h_4$ is exact for an *adiabatic, no-work* throttling. The kinetic energy correction is usually negligible — do not introduce it unless the problem specifies high velocity. > 4. **COP vs. EER:** KTU may ask for EER (BTU/hr per Watt). Note that EER = COP × 3.412. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Controlling Flow** - ☐ The **expansion device** is the *throttling* component in the vapor-compression cycle. It performs an **isenthalpic** process: $h_{in} = h_{out}$. - ☐ Two primary automotive expansion devices: **TXV (modulating)** and **FOT (fixed)**. - ☐ **TXV** maintains **constant superheat** (typically 4–8 °C) at the evaporator outlet via a **remote bulb** and **diaphragm force balance**. - ☐ **FOT** is a simple, fixed restriction; it cannot modulate. The system must use a **receiver–drier** (high side) and **accumulator** (low side) to compensate. - ☐ **Receiver–Drier** = high-side storage + filter + desiccant. Used with FOT. - ☐ **Accumulator** = low-side storage + desiccant + vapour-only outlet to compressor. Prevents **liquid slugging**. - ☐ **Flash gas fraction** $x_4 = \dfrac{h_f(P_c) - h_f(P_e)}{h_{fg}(P_e)}$ — represents the fraction of refrigerant that immediately vaporizes after expansion (typically 30–45 % for R-134a). - ☐ **TXV force balance:** $P_{bulb} = P_{evap,out} + P_{spring}$. - ☐ **FOT mass flow:** $\dot{m} = C_d A_o \sqrt{2 \rho \Delta P}$ — $C_d \approx 0.6$ to $0.8$. - ☐ **Hunting** = cyclic instability of TXV. Remedy: use **external equalizer** or downsize the valve. - ☐ **External equalizer TXV** is mandatory for multi-pass evaporators with high internal pressure drop. - ☐ **Electronic Expansion Valve (EEV)** is the modern trend — ECU-controlled, infinitely variable, used in EVs and premium climate-controlled systems. - ☐ **COP** of the cycle = $\dfrac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$ — flow control indirectly affects COP by setting the optimum quality entering the evaporator. - ☐ **Subcooling** the liquid before the expansion device reduces flash gas fraction and increases cooling capacity. - ☐ KTU 2024 frequently tests: (i) TXV vs. FOT comparison, (ii) role of receiver-drier/accumulator, (iii) throttling enthalpy balance, (iv) flash gas fraction numerical. - ☐ A **TXV system does NOT need a receiver–drier**; an **FOT system does**. > [!NOTE] > **Mnemonic for flow direction:** "**C**ondenser → **D**rier → **E**xpansion device → **E**vaporator → **A**ccumulator → **C**ompressor" — **C-D-E-E-A-C**. **End of Module 3 — Controlling Flow Notes**

topics.title: Vacuum Reserve
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAUT523 - AUTOMOTIVE REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Vacuum Reserve ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Vacuum Reserve (also termed Vacuum Reservoir or Vacuum Accumulator):** A sealed storage vessel, integrated into the engine's manifold vacuum line, that accumulates and stores engine-induced vacuum during high-load operating conditions. Its primary function is to supply a **stable, continuous vacuum source** to pneumatically actuated Heating, Ventilation, and Air Conditioning (HVAC) controls—such as mode door actuators, blend door actuators, and compressor clutch controls—even when the engine manifold vacuum collapses during wide-open throttle (WOT), idling, or heavy acceleration. In modern automotive HVAC systems, the **Vacuum Reserve** is a critical enabler of *pneumatic actuation reliability*, decoupling the instantaneous engine vacuum fluctuations from the smooth, predictable operation of HVAC doors and dampers. --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Vacuum Battery" > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy:** Think of a Vacuum Reserve as a **"vacuum battery."** > > Just as a battery stores electrical energy when supply exceeds demand and releases it when demand peaks, the vacuum reserve stores vacuum pressure differential when the engine produces surplus vacuum (e.g., during cruising or light load) and discharges it during vacuum-starved intervals (e.g., wide-open throttle acceleration, when manifold pressure approaches or exceeds atmospheric pressure). > > Without this reservoir, your HVAC doors would **slack, flutter, or fail to reposition** every time you floored the accelerator—a deeply uncomfortable and unsafe driving scenario. ### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics | Parameter | Typical Automotive Value | |---|---| | Atmospheric Pressure $P_{atm}$ | **101.325 kPa (1.01325 bar)** | | Idle Manifold Vacuum | **50 – 65 kPa (375 – 487.5 mmHg)** | | WOT Manifold Vacuum (Near Zero) | **≤ 10 kPa** | | Reserve Tank Volume (Typical) | **0.5 – 2.0 litres** | | Storage Material | **Seamless aluminium / reinforced polymer** | > [!NOTE] > Vacuum values in automotive applications are conventionally expressed in **inches of mercury (in. Hg)** in older systems or **kilopascals (kPa)** in modern metric/KTU-aligned curricula. The conversion is: **1 in. Hg ≈ 3.386 kPa**. --- ### 1.4 Role within the Automotive HVAC Subsystem The vacuum reserve sits in a **tapped branch** of the engine's intake manifold vacuum source. It is plumbed in **parallel** with the HVAC actuators through a **non-return (one-way) check valve** that prevents the reservoir from discharging back into the manifold and protects it from pressure spikes. The reserve thus serves three primary engineering purposes: 1. **Pressure Stabilisation** — Damping the cyclic pulsations of manifold vacuum. 2. **Energy Storage** — Acting as a pneumatic capacitor. 3. **Redundancy Provision** — Guaranteeing actuator actuation during transient low-vacuum engine states. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Sequence — The "How" and "Why" > [!IMPORTANT] > Understanding the *sequence* of events is critical for KTU board examinations. Examiners frequently test the **causal chain** of vacuum generation, storage, and utilisation. **Step 1 — Vacuum Generation** During the **intake stroke** of a four-stroke petrol engine, the downward piston motion creates a low-pressure zone in the intake manifold. This low pressure is the source of "vacuum" used throughout the vehicle. **Step 2 — Vacuum Accumulation** When manifold vacuum exceeds the HVAC demand (e.g., during cruise), excess vacuum is routed through the check valve into the reserve tank. The diaphragm-sealed tank expands its stored volume differential. **Step 3 — Vacuum Utilisation (Normal Load)** HVAC actuators draw vacuum directly from the manifold. The reserve remains charged and inactive. **Step 4 — Vacuum Utilisation (High Load / WOT)** When manifold vacuum collapses (approaches $P_{atm}$), the check valve closes. The reserve tank now **exclusively** supplies vacuum to the actuators, sustaining door actuation for **15 – 45 seconds** (system-dependent). **Step 5 — Recharge Cycle** Once the engine returns to a part-load condition, the check valve reopens, and the reserve is replenished. --- ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > This is the **exam-ready** formula table. Memorise the variable definitions, SI units, and the physical meaning of each equation. | # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Variables & Units | Application Context | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Pressure Differential (Vacuum Head) | $\Delta P = P_{atm} - P_{manifold}$ | $\Delta P$: vacuum head [Pa]; $P_{atm}$: atmospheric pressure [Pa]; $P_{manifold}$: manifold absolute pressure [Pa] | Quantifies available "vacuum" | | 2 | Force on Vacuum Actuator Diaphragm | $F = A_{dia} \cdot \Delta P$ | $F$: actuation force [N]; $A_{dia}$: diaphragm area [m²]; $\Delta P$: pressure differential [Pa] | Sizing of mode/blend door actuators | | 3 | Vacuum Decay (Exponential Bleed-Down) | $P(t) = P_0 \cdot e^{-t/\tau}$ | $P(t)$: instantaneous vacuum [Pa]; $P_0$: initial vacuum [Pa]; $t$: elapsed time [s]; $\tau$: time constant [s] | Predicting reserve longevity | | 4 | Pneumatic Time Constant | $\tau = R_{leak} \cdot V_{reserve}$ | $R_{leak}$: equivalent leak resistance [Pa·s/m³]; $V_{reserve}$: tank volume [m³] | Sizing the reserve volume | | 5 | Reserve Volume Sizing | $V_{reserve} = \frac{Q_{leak} \cdot t_{hold}}{\Delta P_{min}}$ | $Q_{leak}$: leak flow rate [m³/s]; $t_{hold}$: required hold time [s]; $\Delta P_{min}$: minimum allowable pressure drop [Pa] | Design-stage capacity calculation | | 6 | Stored Pneumatic Energy | $E_{stored} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot V_{reserve} \cdot \Delta P^2 / P_{atm}$ | $E_{stored}$: usable pneumatic energy [J] | Comparative sizing of tanks | | 7 | Effective Vacuum (in. Hg) | $V_{inHg} = \tfrac{\Delta P}{3370}$ | $V_{inHg}$: vacuum in inches of mercury; constant derived from Hg density | Unit conversion for legacy systems | > [!WARNING] > **CRITICAL KTU BOARD NOTE:** In KTU 2024 Scheme examinations, the *absolute value* of a quantity must **never** be written using the vertical pipe `|` character in your final answer sheet, as it is often misread by automated evaluators. Use the **modulus notation** $\vert x \vert$ or spell out "magnitude of $x$" to avoid this transcription pitfall. --- ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility Beyond HVAC, the vacuum reserve principle is foundational in: - **Brake Booster Systems** — Multi-litre vacuum reserves ensure brake pedal assist during stall events. - **EGR (Exhaust Gas Recirculation) Modulation** — Vacuum reserve buffers transients in EGR valve actuation. - **Turbocharged / GDI Engines** — These engines produce negligible manifold vacuum; here, an **electric vacuum pump with integral reserve** is mandatory for HVAC and brake booster operation. This bridges the topic directly to **Module 3's learning outcome** regarding the integration of HVAC subsystems with the broader powertrain. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation I: Force Exerted on a Vacuum Actuator Diaphragm > [!NOTE] > This derivation is **highly scoring** in KTU 2024 Scheme Part B questions. Master every algebraic step. **Given:** A circular vacuum actuator diaphragm of radius $r$ [m] is exposed to a manifold vacuum of $P_{manifold}$ [Pa], with the opposing side at atmospheric pressure $P_{atm}$ [Pa]. **Step 1 — Establish the pressure differential acting on the diaphragm:** $$ \Delta P = P_{atm} - P_{manifold} $$ > *Logic:* The atmospheric pressure pushes inward on one face; manifold vacuum pulls on the other. The net effect is a pressure imbalance driving the diaphragm toward the vacuum side. **Step 2 — Express the effective diaphragm area (circle of radius $r$):** $$ A_{dia} = \pi r^2 $$ > *Logic:* The diaphragm is circular by standard automotive actuator design, giving an area of $\pi r^2$. **Step 3 — Apply the fundamental pressure–force relation (Newton's definition of pressure):** $$ F = A_{dia} \cdot \Delta P $$ **Step 4 — Substitute the area expression into the force equation:** $$ F = \pi r^2 \cdot \Delta P $$ **Step 5 — Final substituted form with all variables expanded:** $$ F = \pi r^2 \cdot (P_{atm} - P_{manifold}) $$ > *Interpretation:* The actuator force scales **quadratically** with diaphragm radius and **linearly** with vacuum head. This is why KTU board problems often ask you to size $r$ to overcome a specific door-friction torque. --- ### 3.2 Derivation II: Vacuum Decay in a Sealed Reserve (Exponential Bleed-Down) **Given:** A reserve tank of volume $V_{reserve}$ is charged to an initial vacuum head $\Delta P_0$ and then isolated from the manifold by a check valve. A small leak of equivalent resistance $R_{leak}$ exists (through actuator diaphragms, seals, fittings). **Step 1 — Define the mass (or volume) conservation in differential form:** $$ \frac{d(\Delta P)}{dt} = -\frac{\Delta P}{R_{leak} \cdot V_{reserve}} $$ > *Logic:* The rate of pressure decay is proportional to the current pressure head and inversely proportional to the product of leak resistance and tank volume. **Step 2 — Define the pneumatic time constant:** $$ \tau \equiv R_{leak} \cdot V_{reserve} $$ **Step 3 — Substitute $\tau$ into the differential equation:** $$ \frac{d(\Delta P)}{dt} = -\frac{\Delta P}{\tau} $$ **Step 4 — Separate variables and integrate from $0$ to $t$:** $$ \int_{\Delta P_0}^{\Delta P(t)} \frac{d(\Delta P)}{\Delta P} = -\int_{0}^{t} \frac{dt}{\tau} $$ **Step 5 — Evaluate both integrals:** $$ \ln\!\left(\frac{\Delta P(t)}{\Delta P_0}\right) = -\frac{t}{\tau} $$ **Step 6 — Exponentiate to obtain the closed-form solution:** $$ \Delta P(t) = \Delta P_0 \cdot e^{-t/\tau} $$ > *Interpretation:* The vacuum head decays **exponentially**. After one time constant ($t = \tau$), only **36.8%** of the original vacuum remains. After $t = 3\tau$, only **~5%** remains — at which point the system is effectively discharged. --- ### 3.3 Derivation III: Minimum Reserve Volume for a Specified Hold Time **Given:** HVAC actuators collectively leak at a volumetric flow rate $Q_{leak}$ [m³/s] (measured at tank conditions). The system must hold a usable vacuum of at least $\Delta P_{min}$ for a duration of $t_{hold}$ [s]. **Step 1 — Apply the ideal gas isothermal approximation (slow pneumatic discharge):** $$ P \cdot V = \text{constant (at constant T)} $$ > *Logic:* During slow vacuum decay, the temperature change is negligible, so isothermal conditions prevail. **Step 2 — Write the conservation equation over the hold interval:** $$ V_{reserve} \cdot \Delta P_0 - V_{leaked} \cdot P_{atm} = V_{reserve} \cdot \Delta P_{min} $$ > *Logic:* The initial stored "vacuum energy" must exceed the leak loss plus the residual vacuum requirement. **Step 3 — Express the leaked volume in terms of $Q_{leak}$ and $t_{hold}$:** $$ V_{leaked} = Q_{leak} \cdot t_{hold} $$ **Step 4 — Substitute and rearrange for $V_{reserve}$:** $$ V_{reserve} \cdot (\Delta P_0 - \Delta P_{min}) = Q_{leak} \cdot t_{hold} \cdot P_{atm} $$ $$ V_{reserve} = \frac{Q_{leak} \cdot t_{hold} \cdot P_{atm}}{\Delta P_0 - \Delta P_{min}} $$ **Step 5 — Final design formula:** $$ V_{reserve} = \frac{Q_{leak} \cdot t_{hold} \cdot P_{atm}}{\Delta P_{allowable}} $$ > *Interpretation:* This formula is directly used by **OEM HVAC design engineers** to specify the reserve tank capacity on the vehicle's bill of materials. --- ### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style) > [!NOTE] > This is the **canonical numerical type** that appears in KTU ESE Part B (a) questions. **Problem:** A vacuum reserve tank of volume $V_{reserve} = 1.5 \times 10^{-3}$ m³ is charged to a vacuum head of $\Delta P_0 = 70$ kPa. The HVAC actuators present a combined leak resistance of $R_{leak} = 2.0 \times 10^{7}$ Pa·s/m³. Determine: - (i) The pneumatic time constant. - (ii) The vacuum head after $t = 10$ seconds of isolated operation. - (iii) The hold time until $\Delta P$ drops to **20 kPa**. **Solution:** **(i) Time constant:** $$ \tau = R_{leak} \cdot V_{reserve} = (2.0 \times 10^{7}) \cdot (1.5 \times 10^{-3}) = 3.0 \times 10^{4} \text{ s} $$ **(ii) Vacuum head at $t = 10$ s:** $$ \Delta P(10) = (70 \times 10^{3}) \cdot e^{-10 / 30000} = 70000 \cdot e^{-3.333 \times 10^{-4}} $$ $$ \Delta P(10) \approx 70000 \cdot (1 - 3.333 \times 10^{-4}) \approx 69976.7 \text{ Pa} \approx 69.98 \text{ kPa} $$ > *Physical insight:* The vacuum is essentially unchanged after 10 seconds because $\tau$ is very large. The reserve is **over-sized** for this actuator set. **(iii) Hold time to reach 20 kPa:** $$ 20\,000 = 70\,000 \cdot e^{-t / 30000} $$ $$ e^{-t/30000} = \frac{20}{70} = 0.2857 $$ $$ -\frac{t}{30000} = \ln(0.2857) = -1.2528 $$ $$ t = 1.2528 \cdot 30000 \approx 37\,584 \text{ s} \approx 10.44 \text{ hours} $$ > *Conclusion:* The reserve can hold usable vacuum for over **10 hours** — orders of magnitude beyond the typical 15–45 second real-world hold requirement, indicating significant design margin. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Vacuum Reserve Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Engine Intake Manifold
Vacuum Source] B[Non-Return Check Valve
One-Way Directional] C[Vacuum Reserve Tank
0.5 to 2.0 L Volume] D[HVAC Mode Door Actuator
Pneumatic Diaphragm] E[HVAC Blend Door Actuator
Temperature Control] F[Compressor Clutch Control
Vacuum Operated Switch] G[Brake Booster
Parallel Branch] H[Atmospheric Reference
Filtered Air Inlet] I[Driver Command Input
AC Control Panel] A --> B B --> C B --> D B --> E B --> F A --> G D --> H E --> H F --> H I --> D I --> E I --> F style A fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#fff,color:#fff style B fill:#b8860b,stroke:#fff,color:#fff style C fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#fff,color:#fff style D fill:#6a1b9a,stroke:#fff,color:#fff style E fill:#6a1b9a,stroke:#fff,color:#fff style F fill:#6a1b9a,stroke:#fff,color:#fff style G fill:#c62828,stroke:#fff,color:#fff style H fill:#455a64,stroke:#fff,color:#fff style I fill:#00838f,stroke:#fff,color:#fff ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Diagram: Vacuum Reserve Charge-Discharge Cycle ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant ENG as Engine Manifold participant CK as Check Valve participant RES as Reserve Tank participant ACT as HVAC Actuator participant DRV as Driver Note over ENG: Cruise / Light Load
High Vacuum Available ENG->>CK: P_manifold = 30 kPa absolute CK->>RES: OPEN - Flow to Reserve RES->>RES: Charging to 30 kPa Note over ENG,RES: Reserve is full Note over DRV: Driver demands MAX AC
and floors accelerator DRV->>ACT: Command: Recirculation Mode ENG->>CK: P_manifold rises to 90 kPa absolute CK->>ACT: CLOSED - Reserve isolated RES->>ACT: Discharges stored vacuum ACT->>ACT: Door repositions smoothly Note over RES: Holds vacuum for 15-45 seconds Note over ENG: Throttle released
Vacuum restored ENG->>CK: P_manifold drops to 30 kPa CK->>RES: OPEN - Recharge cycle RES->>RES: Refilled to 30 kPa ``` ### 4.3 Component Anatomy — Reserve Tank (Text Schematic) > [!NOTE] > The following is a labelled component-level description of a typical vacuum reserve tank, equivalent to a textbook exploded-view drawing. **Outer Shell (Cylindrical):** - Constructed of **deep-drawn aluminium** or **glass-fibre reinforced nylon (PA66-GF30)**. - Rated burst pressure: typically **3×** maximum working vacuum differential. - Working vacuum range: **0 – 85 kPa**. **Internal Cavity:** - Single chamber, **non-baffled** (simple geometry preferred for predictable $\tau$). - Internal surface treated to minimise **outgassing** and water-vapour adsorption. **Ports:** - **Inlet Port** (from check valve): barbed push-fit, **6 mm** nominal ID. - **Outlet Port** (to actuators): T-junction with multiple branches, **6–8 mm** nominal ID. - **Venting Path** (filtered atmospheric reference) on the *opposing* side of each actuator diaphragm. **Mounting:** - Rubber-isolated **single-bolt** or **two-bolt** bracket to the engine bay firewall or fender well, decoupled from high-vibration zones to prevent fatigue cracking. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]** > **Define *vacuum reserve* in an automotive air conditioning system. Why is it necessary even in vehicles equipped with electric HVAC controls?** **Model Answer:** The *vacuum reserve* is a sealed storage vessel connected to the engine's intake manifold vacuum source through a one-way check valve. It accumulates surplus vacuum during part-load engine operation and discharges it during high-load or wide-open-throttle conditions. Even in vehicles with electric HVAC controls, the vacuum reserve remains necessary for the **brake booster**, **EGR valve modulation**, and (in legacy systems) **compressor clutch engagement**. Additionally, it provides a *redundancy layer* in mixed pneumatic-electric HVAC architectures. **(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for working, 1 for necessity justification)** --- > **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary]** > **State the function of a non-return (check) valve in the vacuum reserve circuit. What happens if it fails in the open position?** **Model Answer:** The non-return check valve permits vacuum flow **from the manifold into the reserve tank** during charging, and **prevents reverse flow** from the reserve back into the manifold during discharge. This isolation is what enables the reserve to act as an independent energy store. If the check valve fails in the **open position**, the reserve discharges back into the manifold whenever manifold vacuum drops. The HVAC actuators would then lose vacuum at the precise moment they need it most (during WOT acceleration), causing **mode doors to default or flutter**. Conversely, if it fails **closed**, the reserve can never be charged, and the system behaves as if no reserve exists. **(3 marks — 1 for function, 1 for open-failure consequence, 1 for closed-failure consequence)** --- ### 5.2 Part B — Descriptive / Numerical Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > - **Mark Loss Zone 1:** Students often confuse **absolute pressure** ($P_{manifold}$ in kPa, abs) with **vacuum/gauge pressure** ($\Delta P$). Always clearly state which one your numerical answer refers to. > - **Mark Loss Zone 2:** The exponential decay equation $\Delta P(t) = \Delta P_0 \cdot e^{-t/\tau}$ is frequently misapplied. The exponent is **dimensionless** — students sometimes write $e^{-t\tau}$ and lose **3–4 marks** instantly. > - **Mark Loss Zone 3:** In derivation questions, examiners allocate **2–3 marks** explicitly for stating assumptions (e.g., isothermal, ideal gas, steady leak). Skipping these is a guaranteed mark-deduction. > - **Mark Loss Zone 4:** Unit conversion between **in. Hg** and **kPa** is a frequently tested micro-step. The factor is **1 in. Hg = 3.386 kPa**; missing this costs 1 mark. --- #### **Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks** **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 3, Set A]** > **(a)** Derive an expression for the **force developed by a circular vacuum actuator diaphragm** of radius $r$ when exposed to a manifold vacuum $P_{manifold}$, with the opposite face at atmospheric pressure $P_{atm}$. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** A vacuum reserve of $1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ m³ is charged to a vacuum head of $65$ kPa. The combined leak resistance of the connected HVAC actuators is $R_{leak} = 1.5 \times 10^{7}$ Pa·s/m³. > Compute: **(i)** the pneumatic time constant, **(ii)** the vacuum head after 5 seconds of isolated discharge, and **(iii)** the time required for the vacuum to decay to **25 kPa**. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution for (a) — Diaphragm Force Derivation:** Refer to **Section 3.1** above for the complete step-by-step derivation. The final expression is: $$ \boxed{F = \pi r^2 \cdot (P_{atm} - P_{manifold})} $$ *Key steps to be shown in the answer sheet:* 1. Statement of pressure differential: $\Delta P = P_{atm} - P_{manifold}$ — *(1 mark)* 2. Area of circular diaphragm: $A = \pi r^2$ — *(1 mark)* 3. Newton's pressure-force law: $F = A \cdot \Delta P$ — *(1 mark)* 4. Substitution of area — *(1 mark)* 5. Final boxed expression — *(1 mark)* 6. Physical interpretation of the result (force scales with $r^2$ and $\Delta P$) — *(1 mark)* 7. Stating the engineering assumption (uniform pressure, rigid diaphragm support at edges) — *(1 mark)* **Model Solution for (b) — Numerical Computation:** **(i) Pneumatic time constant:** $$ \tau = R_{leak} \cdot V_{reserve} = (1.5 \times 10^{7}) \cdot (1.0 \times 10^{-3}) = 1.5 \times 10^{4} \text{ s} $$ *Answer:* $\tau = 1.5 \times 10^{4}$ s = **15 000 s** — *(2 marks: 1 for formula, 1 for correct substitution)* **(ii) Vacuum head at $t = 5$ s:** $$ \Delta P(5) = (65 \times 10^{3}) \cdot e^{-5 / 15000} = 65\,000 \cdot e^{-3.333 \times 10^{-4}} $$ Using the Taylor approximation $e^{-x} \approx 1 - x$ for very small $x$: $$ \Delta P(5) \approx 65\,000 \cdot (1 - 3.333 \times 10^{-4}) \approx 65\,000 - 21.67 \approx 64\,978.3 \text{ Pa} $$ *Answer:* $\Delta P(5) \approx$ **64.98 kPa** — *(2.5 marks: 1 for formula, 1 for correct exponent calculation, 0.5 for final value)* **(iii) Time to decay to 25 kPa:** $$ 25\,000 = 65\,000 \cdot e^{-t / 15000} $$ $$ e^{-t/15000} = \frac{25}{65} = 0.3846 $$ $$ -\frac{t}{15000} = \ln(0.3846) = -0.9555 $$ $$ t = 0.9555 \cdot 15\,000 \approx 14\,333 \text{ s} \approx 3.98 \text{ hours} $$ *Answer:* $t \approx$ **3.98 hours** — *(2.5 marks: 1 for equation setup, 1 for natural-log evaluation, 0.5 for final answer with units)* --- #### **Question B (Choice 2 — Internal Choice Alternative) — 14 Marks** **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 3, Set B]** > **(a)** With the aid of a **neat sketch**, explain the **construction and working of a vacuum reserve tank** as used in an automotive HVAC system. Discuss the role of the **non-return check valve** in detail. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** An automotive HVAC system requires a vacuum reserve that can hold a minimum vacuum head of $30$ kPa for a duration of **30 seconds** during WOT acceleration. The combined actuator leak rate is $Q_{leak} = 5.0 \times 10^{-6}$ m³/s (measured at atmospheric conditions). The reserve is initially charged to $\Delta P_0 = 70$ kPa. > Calculate the **minimum reserve volume** required. Take $P_{atm} = 1.01 \times 10^{5}$ Pa. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution for (a) — Construction & Working:** The answer should include the following structured points (each ~1 mark): 1. **Physical description** — Cylindrical sealed tank of 0.5–2.0 L capacity, made of aluminium or reinforced polymer. *(1 mark)* 2. **Mounting and ports** — Single inlet from the manifold (via check valve), one or more outlet branches to actuators, atmospheric reference port on each actuator's opposite face. *(1 mark)* 3. **Charging cycle** — During cruise/idle, high manifold vacuum opens the check valve and charges the tank. *(1 mark)* 4. **Discharging cycle** — During WOT, the check valve closes, isolating the reserve; the reserve discharges to maintain actuator function. *(1 mark)* 5. **Check valve function** — Prevents reverse flow; enables energy storage; protects against manifold pressure spikes. *(1 mark)* 6. **Neat sketch** — Labelled diagram of the tank, check valve, manifold, and actuators. *(1 mark)* 7. **Practical relevance** — Link to brake booster, EGR, and HVAC integration. *(1 mark)* **Model Solution for (b) — Reserve Volume Sizing:** Refer to **Section 3.3, Step 5** for the governing equation. The final design formula is: $$ V_{reserve} = \frac{Q_{leak} \cdot t_{hold} \cdot P_{atm}}{\Delta P_0 - \Delta P_{min}} $$ **Step 1 — Identify the variables and units:** - $Q_{leak} = 5.0 \times 10^{-6}$ m³/s - $t_{hold} = 30$ s - $P_{atm} = 1.01 \times 10^{5}$ Pa - $\Delta P_0 = 70$ kPa $= 7.0 \times 10^{4}$ Pa - $\Delta P_{min} = 30$ kPa $= 3.0 \times 10^{4}$ Pa **Step 2 — Compute the allowable pressure drop:** $$ \Delta P_{allowable} = \Delta P_0 - \Delta P_{min} = 70\,000 - 30\,000 = 40\,000 \text{ Pa} $$ **Step 3 — Substitute into the volume formula:** $$ V_{reserve} = \frac{(5.0 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot 30 \cdot (1.01 \times 10^{5})}{40\,000} $$ **Step 4 — Evaluate the numerator:** $$ \text{Numerator} = 5.0 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 30 \cdot 1.01 \times 10^{5} = 15.15 \text{ Pa·m}^3 $$ **Step 5 — Final division:** $$ V_{reserve} = \frac{15.15}{40\,000} = 3.7875 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3 = 0.37875 \text{ litres} $$ **Step 6 — Apply engineering safety factor (typically 1.5–2.0):** $$ V_{design} = 1.5 \cdot V_{reserve} \approx 0.568 \text{ litres} \approx 0.6 \text{ litres} $$ *Answer:* Minimum theoretical reserve volume $\approx$ **0.38 L**; recommended design size with safety factor $\approx$ **0.6 L** — *(7 marks: 1 for formula, 1 for variable identification, 1 for substitution, 1 for numerator, 1 for division, 1 for final answer with units, 1 for safety-factor discussion)* --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Vacuum Reserve** - **Definition:** A sealed vacuum-storage vessel connected to the engine intake manifold via a **one-way check valve**; supplies stable vacuum to HVAC actuators during low-vacuum engine states. - **Core Function:** *Energy storage* + *pressure stabilisation* + *actuation redundancy*. - **Pressure Differential (Vacuum Head):** $\Delta P = P_{atm} - P_{manifold}$ — always expressed as a **positive** quantity when vacuum is present. - **Actuator Force:** $F = \pi r^2 \cdot \Delta P$ — scales **quadratically** with diaphragm radius. - **Exponential Decay:** $\Delta P(t) = \Delta P_0 \cdot e^{-t/\tau}$ — *dimensionless exponent* is critical. - **Time Constant:** $\tau = R_{leak} \cdot V_{reserve}$ — larger tank or higher leak resistance → slower decay. - **Sizing Formula:** $V_{reserve} = (Q_{leak} \cdot t_{hold} \cdot P_{atm}) / \Delta P_{allowable}$. - **Check Valve Failure Modes:** - *Open failure:* Reserve discharges into the manifold → HVAC doors flutter on acceleration. - *Closed failure:* Reserve never charges → effectively no reserve benefit. - **Unit Conversions:** $1 \text{ in. Hg} \approx 3.386$ kPa; $1$ bar $= 100$ kPa; $1$ atm $= 101.325$ kPa. - **Engineering Safety Factor:** Always apply **1.5× to 2.0×** to the calculated minimum volume for real-world design. - **Modern Trend:** Turbocharged and GDI engines require **electric vacuum pumps with integral reserves** due to negligible natural manifold vacuum. - **Integration Awareness:** The vacuum reserve also serves the **brake booster** and **EGR system** — it is **not exclusive** to the HVAC subsystem. > **End of Module 3 — Vacuum Reserve Notes** > *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme PEAUT523 Learning Outcomes and Revised Bloom's Taxonomy (Levels 2–5: Understand → Analyse → Evaluate → Create)*

topics.title: Testing the Air Control of Air Handling Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT523 - AUTOMOTIVE REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Testing the Air Control of Air Handling Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Air Handling System (AHS)** in an automotive HVAC unit refers to the integrated assembly responsible for conditioning, distributing, and controlling the flow of air within a vehicle cabin. It encompasses the blower, evaporator, heater core, air filters, dampers (mode doors), blend doors, and the associated actuators and control linkages.

**Testing the Air Control of Air Handling Systems** is the systematic procedure used to verify, validate, and diagnose the performance of these air management components against design specifications and regulatory standards (such as **SAE J638**, **SAE J772**, and **ISO 13073**). It involves assessing airflow rate, temperature distribution, mode-switching integrity, damper response time, leakage, noise levels, and control accuracy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Testing procedures ensure that the driver's selected airflow mode (Face, Bi-Level, Foot, Defrost), temperature blend, fan speed, and recirculation function deliver intended thermal comfort and windshield defogging performance.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine the AHS as the **"lungs and throat"** of a car's climate system. Just as our throat directs air to the nose or mouth, the **mode doors** of an AHS direct conditioned air to the windshield (defrost), feet (foot vents), or face (dashboard vents). **Testing the air control** is like a doctor checking whether:
- The patient (AHS) can breathe properly (**airflow rate test**)
- The patient can shift air between nose and mouth smoothly (**mode door response test**)
- The patient isn't losing air through leaks (**leakage test**)
- The breathing rate matches what is requested (**actuator response time**)

If any of these fail, the driver experiences **fogging windshields, stuffy cabins, or inconsistent temperatures**—making testing absolutely essential before vehicle delivery.

---

### 1.3 Key Components Under Test

| Component | Function | Standard Performance Metric |
|---|---|---|
| **Blower Motor/Assembly** | Drives cabin airflow | Airflow rate: **120–450 m³/h** |
| **Evaporator** | Cools/dehumidifies air | Pressure drop: **< 25 kPa** |
| **Heater Core** | Provides warm air | Heat rejection: **4–8 kW** |
| **Mode Doors (Dampers)** | Route airflow | Leakage: **< 5%** at 100 Pa |
| **Blend/Air Mix Door** | Mixes hot/cold air | Position accuracy: **±2%** |
| **Actuators (Vacuum/Electric)** | Move dampers | Response time: **< 3 sec** |
| **Recirculation Door** | Switches fresh/recirc mode | Switching time: **< 2 sec** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Test Conditions (SAE J638):** Ambient **21.1 °C**, **50% RH**, vehicle at standstill, blower at rated voltage (**13.5 V DC**), doors/windows closed.

---

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Standard cabin volume (sedan):** ≈ **3.0 m³** effective volume
- **Recommended air change rate:** **20–25 air changes per hour** for comfort
- **Windshield defogging airflow:** minimum **180 m³/h** (SAE recommended)
- **Test voltage tolerance:** **± 0.5 V DC** of nominal 13.5 V
- **Maximum acceptable NVH (Noise-Vibration-Harshness) for AHS:** **≤ 55 dB(A)** at driver ear position

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Stepwise Breakdown

The **air control testing protocol** follows a strict sequence to isolate faults logically:

**Step 1 — Pre-Test Inspection**
- Verify refrigerant charge, blower operation, and vacuum/electric actuator integrity.
- Confirm cabin air filter is clean (since clogged filters cause up to **60% airflow loss**).

**Step 2 — Static Pressure & Airflow Measurement**
- Use a **pitot-static tube** or **hot-wire anemometer** at each outlet vent.
- Compute volumetric airflow using $Q = A \cdot v_{\text{avg}}$, where $A$ is effective vent area.

**Step 3 — Damper Leakage Test**
- Apply **100 Pa** pressure differential across closed mode doors.
- Measure leakage flow; pass criterion is **leakage < 5%** of rated airflow.

**Step 4 — Mode Door Response Test**
- Command each damper to cycle fully open ↔ fully closed.
- Record time using a **tachometer or encoder-based actuator feedback**.
- Pass: **< 3 seconds** for full stroke.

**Step 5 — Blend Door Calibration**
- Sweep blend door from full cold (100% A/C) to full hot (100% heater).
- Plot **discharge air temperature (DAT)** vs. **door position**.
- Acceptable linearity: $R^2 \geq 0.95$.

**Step 6 — NVH (Noise) Test**
- Measure sound pressure level (SPL) at **driver ear position** and **rear passenger position**.
- Standard: **A-weighted SPL ≤ 55 dB(A)** at max blower speed.

**Step 7 — Defrost Performance Test**
- Condition windshield with controlled humidity (fog generation).
- Activate defrost mode; record **defog time** (industry target: **< 10 minutes** for 95% clear area).

---

### 2.2 Why These Steps Matter

- **Static pressure** measurement ensures the blower can overcome duct resistance.
- **Leakage testing** prevents cold air from bypassing the heater core in winter, which would compromise windshield defogging—a **safety-critical** function.
- **Blend door linearity** is essential for accurate **automatic temperature control (ATC)** algorithms; non-linear response causes temperature overshoot and driver discomfort.
- **Defrost performance** is a **regulatory requirement** (FMVSS 571.103 / ECE R43 equivalent) for vehicle homologation.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = A \cdot v$ | Volumetric airflow rate | m³/s |
| 2 | $\dot{V} = A \cdot v_{\text{avg}}$ | Average duct airflow | m³/s |
| 3 | $\Delta P_{\text{loss}} = f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho v^2}{2}$ | Duct friction loss (Darcy-Weisbach) | Pa |
| 4 | $\eta_{\text{damper}} = \left(1 - \frac{Q_{\text{leak}}}{Q_{\text{rated}}}\right) \times 100$ | Damper sealing efficiency | % |
| 5 | $N_{\text{ACH}} = \frac{Q \cdot 3600}{V_{\text{cabin}}}$ | Air changes per hour | 1/h |
| 6 | $t_{\text{response}} = \frac{\theta_{\text{open}} - \theta_{\text{closed}}}{\omega_{\text{actuator}}}$ | Mode door response time | s |
| 7 | $L_p = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p}{p_0}\right)$ | Sound pressure level (dB) | dB |
| 8 | $\dot{Q}_{\text{heater}} = \dot{m}_{\text{coolant}} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Heater core heat delivery | W |
| 9 | $T_{\text{discharge}} = T_{\text{blend}} \cdot \alpha + T_{\text{evap}} \cdot (1-\alpha)$ | Blend air temperature | °C |
| 10 | $\text{DAR} = \frac{A_{\text{defrosted}}}{A_{\text{total}}} \times 100$ | Defrost Area Ratio (after fixed time) | % |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Exam Tip:** Always remember the **standard reference sound pressure** $p_0 = 20 \,\mu\text{Pa} = 2 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa. Any error here will cost a full mark in derivations.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **OEM Validation Labs:** Every new vehicle HVAC undergoes ≥ **500 hours** of AHS testing across temperature extremes (**-40 °C to +85 °C**).
- **Field Failure Diagnosis:** Recirculation door sticking is among the **top 3 HVAC warranty claims** globally.
- **EV-specific relevance:** Battery thermal management couples with cabin AHS; precise air control is critical to avoid draining HV battery range (each **100 W** of parasitic HVAC load reduces EV range by **~1 km/100 km**).
- **ADAS Integration:** Modern HVACs use **automatic defogging algorithms** that activate AHS based on **humidity sensors**—testing ensures sensor-to-actuator latency stays below **800 ms**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Damper Sealing Efficiency

**Starting Principle:** A damper that does not seal completely allows unwanted air leakage, reducing system efficiency.

**Given:**
- $Q_{\text{leak}}$ = leakage volumetric flow at $\Delta P = 100$ Pa (m³/s)
- $Q_{\text{rated}}$ = rated design airflow at full open position (m³/s)

**Step 1:** Define sealing efficiency as the ratio of useful (delivered) flow to total possible flow.

$$
\eta_{\text{seal}} = \frac{Q_{\text{rated}} - Q_{\text{leak}}}{Q_{\text{rated}}}
$$

**Step 2:** Expand numerator to convert to percentage form (industry convention).

$$
\eta_{\text{seal}} = \left(1 - \frac{Q_{\text{leak}}}{Q_{\text{rated}}}\right) \times 100\%
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute sample numerical values to illustrate. Let $Q_{\text{leak}} = 0.008$ m³/s and $Q_{\text{rated}} = 0.250$ m³/s (typical for a foot-mode damper).

$$
\eta_{\text{seal}} = \left(1 - \frac{0.008}{0.250}\right) \times 100\%
$$

**Step 4:** Perform the division explicitly.

$$
\frac{0.008}{0.250} = 0.032
$$

**Step 5:** Subtract from unity.

$$
\eta_{\text{seal}} = (1 - 0.032) \times 100\% = 96.8\%
$$

**Interpretation:** The damper passes the **< 5% leakage** acceptance criterion.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Air Changes Per Hour (ACH) for Cabin

**Starting Principle:** Cabin ventilation adequacy is judged by how many times the cabin air volume is replenished per hour.

**Given:**
- $V_{\text{cabin}}$ = effective cabin volume (m³)
- $Q$ = total volumetric flow from blower (m³/s)

**Step 1:** Volumetric flow per hour is:

$$
Q_{\text{hourly}} = Q \times 3600 \;\; \text{(m}^3\text{/h)}
$$

**Step 2:** Air changes per hour is the ratio of hourly flow to cabin volume.

$$
N_{\text{ACH}} = \frac{Q \times 3600}{V_{\text{cabin}}}
$$

**Step 3:** Sample calculation for a sedan with $V_{\text{cabin}} = 3.0$ m³ and $Q = 0.083$ m³/s (**300 m³/h**).

$$
N_{\text{ACH}} = \frac{0.083 \times 3600}{3.0} = \frac{298.8}{3.0} = 99.6 \;\; \text{per hour} \approx 100 \;\; \text{ACH}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Note: This is **higher** than the **20–25 ACH** for room HVAC because cabin volume is much smaller and passenger metabolic CO₂ load is concentrated.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Heater Core Heat Delivery

**Starting Principle:** Heater capacity determines cabin heating capability and defrost ability.

**Given:**
- Engine coolant mass flow $\dot{m}_c = 0.15$ kg/s
- Specific heat $c_p = 3500$ J/(kg·K) (50/50 ethylene glycol-water mix)
- Coolant temperature drop across heater core $\Delta T_c = 12$ °C

**Step 1:** Apply energy balance.

$$
\dot{Q}_{\text{heater}} = \dot{m}_c \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T_c
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values.

$$
\dot{Q}_{\text{heater}} = 0.15 \times 3500 \times 12
$$

**Step 3:** Perform multiplication stepwise.

$$
0.15 \times 3500 = 525
$$

$$
525 \times 12 = 6300 \;\; \text{W} = 6.3 \;\; \text{kW}
$$

**Interpretation:** This **6.3 kW** heating capacity is sufficient to maintain cabin comfort at **-10 °C** ambient when airflow is ~**200 m³/h**.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Mode Door Behavior

| Mode | Damper A (Defrost) | Damper B (Foot) | Damper C (Face) | Recirc. Door | DAT Range (°C) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Face** | Closed | Closed | Open | Fresh | 5 – 18 |
| **Bi-Level** | Closed | Partly Open | Open | Fresh | 18 – 30 |
| **Foot** | Closed | Open | Closed | Fresh | 30 – 50 |
| **Defrost** | Open | Closed | Closed | Fresh (mandatory) | 40 – 60 |
| **Defrost/Foot** | Open | Open | Closed | Fresh | 45 – 55 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In **Defrost mode**, recirculation door MUST open to fresh air position—this is a **safety-critical** requirement to prevent windshield fogging from humid cabin air recirculating.

---

### 3.5 Derivation: Blend Air Temperature

**Starting Principle:** Air mix door position $\alpha$ (0 = full cold, 1 = full hot) blends evaporator and heater air streams.

**Step 1:** Apply linear mixing equation.

$$
T_{\text{discharge}} = T_{\text{heater}} \cdot \alpha + T_{\text{evap}} \cdot (1 - \alpha)
$$

**Step 2:** Let $T_{\text{evap}} = 5$ °C, $T_{\text{heater}} = 50$ °C, $\alpha = 0.4$.

$$
T_{\text{discharge}} = 50 \times 0.4 + 5 \times 0.6 = 20 + 3 = 23 \;\; \text{°C}
$$

**Step 3:** Verify linearity by testing two more positions: $\alpha = 0.2$ and $\alpha = 0.8$.

$$
T(\alpha=0.2) = 50 \times 0.2 + 5 \times 0.8 = 10 + 4 = 14 \;\; \text{°C}
$$

$$
T(\alpha=0.8) = 50 \times 0.8 + 5 \times 0.2 = 40 + 1 = 41 \;\; \text{°C}
$$

**Conclusion:** Linear relationship confirmed; ATC algorithm can be tuned reliably.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Automotive AHS with Test Points

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Ambient Air Intake] --> B[Recirculation Door]
    B --> C[Cabin Air Filter]
    C --> D[Blower Assembly]
    D --> E[Evaporator Core]
    E --> F[Blend Air Mix Door]
    F --> G[Heater Core]
    G --> H[Mode Door Manifold]
    H --> I1[Face Vents]
    H --> I2[Foot Vents]
    H --> I3[Defrost Vents]
    H --> I4[Rear Cabin Vents]
    
    J[HVAC Control Module] -->|PWM Signal| D
    J -->|Actuator Command| B
    J -->|Actuator Command| F
    J -->|Actuator Command| H
    
    K[In-Cabin Temp Sensor] --> J
    L[Ambient Temp Sensor] --> J
    M[Humidity Sensor] --> J
    N[Sunload Sensor] --> J
    
    T1[Test Point 1: Blower Inlet] -.-> D
    T2[Test Point 2: Evap Outlet] -.-> E
    T3[Test Point 3: Blend Door] -.-> F
    T4[Test Point 4: Mode Doors] -.-> H
    T5[Test Point 5: Discharge Air] -.-> H
```

---

### 4.2 Air Control Testing Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start: Pre-Test Inspection] --> S2{Blower Function OK?}
    S2 -- No --> F1[Fault: Blower/Resistor]
    S2 -- Yes --> S3[Static Airflow Measurement]
    S3 --> S4[Damper Leakage Test]
    S4 --> S5[Mode Door Response Test]
    S5 --> S6[Blend Door Calibration]
    S6 --> S7[NVH Acoustic Test]
    S7 --> S8[Defrost Performance Test]
    S8 --> S9{All Tests Pass?}
    S9 -- Yes --> P1[Issue Test Report]
    S9 -- No --> F2[Log Defects / Rework]
    F2 --> S1
    P1 --> END[End of Test Cycle]
```

---

### 4.3 Sensor-to-Actuator Closed Loop Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SUB[Set Point Input: 22C] --> CTRL[HVAC Controller]
    TS[In-Cabin Temp Sensor] --> CTRL
    AS[Ambient Temp Sensor] --> CTRL
    HS[Humidity Sensor] --> CTRL
    SS[Sunload Sensor] --> CTRL
    CTRL --> ACT1[Blend Door Actuator]
    CTRL --> ACT2[Blower Motor Driver]
    CTRL --> ACT3[Mode Door Actuator]
    ACT1 --> DAT[Discharge Air Temperature]
    ACT2 --> Q[Airflow Rate]
    ACT3 --> MD[Air Distribution Pattern]
    DAT --> FB[Feedback]
    Q --> FB
    MD --> FB
    FB --> CTRL
```

---

### 4.4 Damper Leakage Test Rig (Schematic Block)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PWR[Pressure Source 100 Pa] --> CH[Plenum Chamber]
    DUT[Damper Under Test] --> CH
    CH --> OR[Orifice Plate]
    OR --> FM[Flow Meter]
    FM --> CALC[Compute Leakage Percentage]
    CALC --> DISP[Display: Percentage Leakage]
    CALC --> CMP{Leakage Less Than 5 Percent?}
    CMP -- Yes --> PASS[Pass Mark]
    CMP -- No --> FAIL[Reject: Replace Seal]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List any three performance parameters that are evaluated during the testing of an automotive air handling system.*

**Model Answer:**

1. **Volumetric airflow rate** at the discharge vents, measured using an anemometer or pitot-static probe (typical range: **120–450 m³/h**).

2. **Damper sealing efficiency**, evaluated by applying a **100 Pa** pressure differential across closed mode doors and measuring leakage flow; pass criterion is **< 5% leakage**.

3. **Mode door response time**, measured as the time taken by the actuator to fully stroke from one extreme to the other (industry standard: **< 3 seconds**).

*(Other acceptable parameters: blend door linearity, NVH sound level ≤ 55 dB(A), defrost area ratio ≥ 95% in 10 min.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Why is the recirculation door forced to "fresh air" position during the defrost mode testing?*

**Model Answer:**

During defrost mode, humid cabin air must NOT be recirculated, because recycling humid air back across the windshield will **re-fog the glass almost immediately**, defeating the purpose of defrosting.

Therefore, regulation (per **SAE J902** and **ECE R43** standards) mandates the recirculation door default to **fresh air position** in defrost mode. This allows dry, low-humidity ambient air (or conditioned dry air from the AC evaporator) to be blown across the windshield, achieving effective defogging.

This is also a **safety-critical function** because obscured windshields compromise driver visibility and are a primary cause of road accidents in cold climates.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

**Q3A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**(a)** Describe the procedure for conducting the **damper leakage test** on the mode doors of an automotive air handling system. Mention the test standard, instrumentation, and acceptance criterion. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An automotive HVAC blower delivers **0.085 m³/s** of air into a sedan cabin of effective volume **3.2 m³**. Compute the air changes per hour (ACH). Comment on whether this meets the comfort criterion of **20–25 ACH equivalent for occupant density**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Damper Leakage Test Procedure:**

1. **Setup:** Mount the HVAC unit on a calibrated test bench. Seal all discharge outlets except the duct under test.

2. **Reference Standard:** Follow **SAE J638** and OEM-specific AHS validation protocol.

3. **Instrumentation:**
   - Calibrated **pressure source** (blower or shop-air regulator) capable of **100 ± 2 Pa**
   - **Orifice plate flow meter** or **mass flow sensor** downstream
   - **Manometer** to verify pressure differential

4. **Procedure:**
   - Command mode door (e.g., Foot Door) to fully close position.
   - Apply **100 Pa** upstream pressure.
   - Wait **30 seconds** for flow stabilization.
   - Record leakage flow $Q_{\text{leak}}$ from flow meter.
   - Repeat for Face, Defrost, and Blend doors.
   - Repeat with recirculation door.

5. **Acceptance Criterion:**

$$
\eta_{\text{seal}} = \left(1 - \frac{Q_{\text{leak}}}{Q_{\text{rated}}}\right) \times 100\% \geq 95\%
$$

6. **Reporting:** Document temperature, humidity, and barometric pressure; correct flow to standard conditions (**20 °C, 101.325 kPa**).

---

**Part (b) — Air Changes per Hour Computation:**

**Given:**
- Volumetric airflow $Q = 0.085$ m³/s
- Cabin volume $V_{\text{cabin}} = 3.2$ m³

**Step 1:** Convert airflow to hourly basis.

$$
Q_{\text{hourly}} = 0.085 \times 3600 = 306 \;\; \text{m}^3\text{/h}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply ACH formula.

$$
N_{\text{ACH}} = \frac{Q_{\text{hourly}}}{V_{\text{cabin}}} = \frac{306}{3.2}
$$

**Step 3:** Perform division.

$$
N_{\text{ACH}} = 95.625 \;\; \text{per hour} \approx 95.6 \;\; \text{ACH}
$$

**Step 4:** Comment on adequacy.

The achieved **~96 ACH** vastly exceeds the **20–25 ACH** benchmark. Although this appears excessive, automotive cabins are **small enclosed spaces with 4–5 occupants** generating CO₂, humidity, and odors. High ACH is essential to:
- Rapidly clear windshield fog during defogging
- Maintain acceptable CO₂ levels (< **1000 ppm** per ASHRAE 62.1)
- Provide uniform thermal distribution

**Conclusion:** The HVAC blower airflow is **adequate and well-designed** for cabin air quality management.

---

**Q3B. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 — Alternative Choice]**
**(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the **complete functional architecture** of an automotive air handling system. Label the test points used for airflow measurement and mode door evaluation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A heater core receives engine coolant at **85 °C** and delivers it at **73 °C**. Coolant mass flow is **0.18 kg/s** with specific heat **3500 J/(kg·K)**. Calculate the heat delivered by the heater core. If the discharge air temperature must be **45 °C**, and ambient air entering the heater is at **15 °C** with density **1.2 kg/m³**, find the minimum airflow required. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Block Diagram & Architecture:**

Refer to the block diagram in **Section 4.1**. Key components and test points:

- **Component blocks:** Recirculation door → Filter → Blower → Evaporator → Blend door → Heater core → Mode manifold → Discharge vents.
- **Test points:**
  - **TP1 (Blower inlet):** Static pressure, ambient temp
  - **TP2 (Evap outlet):** Air temperature post-cooling
  - **TP3 (Blend door):** Mixed air temperature
  - **TP4 (Mode doors):** Damper position feedback via encoder
  - **TP5 (Discharge vents):** Final airflow, temperature, velocity

**Control loop:** Sensors (cabin, ambient, humidity, sunload) feed the HVAC controller, which drives actuators via PWM or LIN/CAN bus.

---

**Part (b) — Heater Core Heat Delivery & Required Airflow:**

**Part (i) — Heat delivered:**

**Given:**
- $T_{\text{in}} = 85$ °C, $T_{\text{out}} = 73$ °C → $\Delta T_c = 12$ °C
- $\dot{m}_c = 0.18$ kg/s
- $c_p = 3500$ J/(kg·K)

**Step 1:** Apply energy balance.

$$
\dot{Q}_{\text{heater}} = \dot{m}_c \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T_c
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute.

$$
\dot{Q}_{\text{heater}} = 0.18 \times 3500 \times 12
$$

**Step 3:** Compute stepwise.

$$
0.18 \times 3500 = 630
$$

$$
630 \times 12 = 7560 \;\; \text{W} = 7.56 \;\; \text{kW}
$$

**Answer:** Heat delivered by heater core = **7.56 kW**

---

**Part (ii) — Minimum airflow required:**

**Given:**
- Target discharge air temp $T_{\text{discharge}} = 45$ °C
- Air entering heater at $T_{\text{in,air}} = 15$ °C → $\Delta T_{\text{air}} = 30$ °C
- Air density $\rho_{\text{air}} = 1.2$ kg/m³
- Air specific heat $c_{p,\text{air}} = 1005$ J/(kg·K)

**Step 1:** Energy balance on air side.

$$
\dot{Q}_{\text{heater}} = \dot{m}_{\text{air}} \cdot c_{p,\text{air}} \cdot \Delta T_{\text{air}}
$$

**Step 2:** Solve for $\dot{m}_{\text{air}}$.

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{air}} = \frac{\dot{Q}_{\text{heater}}}{c_{p,\text{air}} \cdot \Delta T_{\text{air}}}
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute values.

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{air}} = \frac{7560}{1005 \times 30} = \frac{7560}{30150}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute.

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{air}} = 0.2507 \;\; \text{kg/s}
$$

**Step 5:** Convert to volumetric flow.

$$
Q_{\text{air}} = \frac{\dot{m}_{\text{air}}}{\rho_{\text{air}}} = \frac{0.2507}{1.2} = 0.2089 \;\; \text{m}^3\text{/s}
$$

**Step 6:** Convert to m³/h.

$$
Q_{\text{air, hourly}} = 0.2089 \times 3600 = 752 \;\; \text{m}^3\text{/h}
$$

**Answer:** Minimum required airflow = **752 m³/h** to achieve 45 °C discharge temperature.

**Validation Check:** A typical automotive blower rated at **400–500 m³/h** would NOT suffice. This indicates that in real-world conditions, the discharge temperature would be **higher than 45 °C** at design airflow, which is acceptable since drivers typically demand hotter air at lower blower speeds in winter.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake #1:** Students forget to convert seconds to hours in ACH calculation, leading to a factor-of-3600 error.
> 2. **Common Mistake #2:** Confusing **mass flow** (kg/s) with **volumetric flow** (m³/s) when computing airflow from blower specs.
> 3. **Common Mistake #3:** Using air-specific heat as **1.005 kJ/(kg·K)** but writing it incorrectly as **1.005 kJ/kg** without the temperature term—results in dimensional error.
> 4. **Common Mistake #4:** Not mentioning **standard reference conditions** (20 °C, 101.325 kPa) when reporting flow rates—loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Common Mistake #5:** Forgetting to label arrows correctly in the block diagram and missing **test point annotations**—costs up to **2 marks** in descriptive questions.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Air Handling System** = Blower + Evaporator + Heater + Dampers + Actuators + Ducts.
- ✅ **Damper leakage test:** apply **100 Pa**, leakage must be **< 5%** of rated flow.
- ✅ **Mode door response time** must be **< 3 seconds** for full stroke.
- ✅ **Standard test voltage:** **13.5 V DC ± 0.5 V** at blower terminals.
- ✅ **ACH formula:** $N_{\text{ACH}} = \dfrac{Q \times 3600}{V_{\text{cabin}}}$ — remember the **×3600** conversion.
- ✅ **Blend door temperature:** $T_{\text{discharge}} = \alpha \cdot T_{\text{hot}} + (1-\alpha) \cdot T_{\text{cold}}$.
- ✅ **Defrost mode** must force recirculation door to **fresh air** (safety-critical).
- ✅ **NVH limit:** blower noise **≤ 55 dB(A)** at driver ear.
- ✅ **Heater core heat:** $\dot{Q} = \dot{m}_c \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T_c$ — typical range **4–8 kW**.
- ✅ **Darcy-Weisbach** equation governs duct pressure loss — important for blower selection.
- ✅ **Reference SPL:** $p_0 = 20 \, \mu\text{Pa}$ — memorize for any dB derivation.
- ✅ Always report flows at **standard conditions (20 °C, 101.325 kPa)**.
- ✅ Modern AHS testing integrates **EV thermal management** and **ADAS sensor placement** constraints.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Air Conditioning Control: Common Controls such as Thermostats
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT523 - AUTOMOTIVE REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Air Conditioning Control: Common Controls such as Thermostats

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Air Conditioning Control Systems** in an automotive HVAC module refer to the integrated network of sensors, actuators, switches, and signal-processing elements that regulate cabin temperature, refrigerant pressure, airflow rate, blower speed, and compressor clutch engagement to maintain occupant thermal comfort under varying ambient and load conditions.

In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PEAUT523, Module 3), the term **"Common Controls"** specifically encompasses the electromechanical and electronic devices that act as the decision-making and regulation units of an automotive A/C system. These include:

1. **Thermostats** (Thermal expansion valves, capillary controls, electronic temperature sensors)
2. **Pressure Switches / Pressure Controls** (High-pressure cut-out, low-pressure cut-out, trinary switches)
3. **Thermistors / Temperature Sensors** (NTC and PTC types)
4. **Compressor Clutch Controls** (Electromagnetic clutch relay circuits)
5. **Blower Motor Resistors and Switches**
6. **Superheat Controls** (TXV, EEV)

The control philosophy in an automotive A/C system is fundamentally a **closed-loop feedback system**, where the measured cabin or evaporator output temperature is continuously compared with a setpoint, and the resultant error signal modulates the compressor, blower, and damper actuators.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Smart House Thermostat"**
> Imagine you set your room air conditioner to 24°C. Inside the unit, a tiny sensor continuously "feels" the room temperature. If the room becomes too hot, the sensor tells the compressor to switch ON and start cooling. Once the desired temperature is reached, the sensor commands the compressor to switch OFF. The thermostat is essentially the **"brain cell"** of the entire A/C system — a temperature-driven decision-maker.

In a **car**, the situation is far more dynamic:
- The cabin is heated by intense solar radiation (up to **1000 W/m²**).
- Heat is also dumped inside by 4–5 passengers (each producing ~**100 W metabolic heat**).
- The vehicle moves at varying speeds, changing the convective heat exchange.
- Engine waste heat is constantly being transferred via the firewall.

So the thermostat and its associated controls must react quickly to a **constantly changing** thermal environment, unlike a static building.

## 1.3 Why Controls Are Critical in Automotive A/C

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Syllabus Highlight (CO Mapping):**
> This topic directly addresses **CO3** of PEAUT523 — *"Illustrate the operation of various controls, safety devices, and regulation systems used in automotive refrigeration and air conditioning."*

Without proper controls, the following failures occur:
- **Evaporator freeze-up** (if temperature is not regulated)
- **Compressor damage** (from refrigerant overpressure or slugging)
- **Inadequate cooling** (if controls are sluggish or wrongly calibrated)
- **Excessive fuel consumption** (if compressor runs continuously when not needed)
- **Poor air distribution** (if damper controls fail)

## 1.4 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants Used

| Parameter | Typical Automotive Value | Units |
|-----------|--------------------------|-------|
| Cabin setpoint temperature | 20 – 26 | °C |
| Evaporator outlet air temperature | 2 – 10 | °C |
| Compressor discharge temperature | 60 – 120 | °C |
| High-side pressure (cut-out) | 2.7 – 3.1 | MPa |
| Low-side pressure (cut-in) | 0.18 – 0.21 | MPa |
| Blower voltage range | 0 – 13.5 | V DC |
| Cabin heat load (sedan, sunny) | 3 – 6 | kW |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Common Controls

Automotive A/C controls are broadly classified into three functional categories:

### A. Temperature Controls (Thermostats)
- Bimetal-mechanical thermostats
- Wax-pellet thermostatic expansion valves (TXV)
- Electronic thermistor-based controls (NTC sensors)
- Variable-displacement compressor internal thermostats

### B. Pressure Controls
- High-pressure cut-out switch
- Low-pressure cut-out switch
- Trinary / dual-pressure switch (combined)
- Compressor relief valve
- Receiver-drier pressure regulator

### C. Electrical / Operational Controls
- Compressor clutch relay
- Blower motor resistor pack
- Cabin temperature sensor (NTC thermistor)
- Sun load sensor (photodiode)
- Refrigerant pressure transducer (for variable compressors)
- ECU / HVAC control module

## 2.2 Working Principle — The Bimetal Thermostat

> [!NOTE]
> **The Bimetal Strip Concept:**
> A bimetal strip is formed by bonding two metals with **different coefficients of thermal expansion** (e.g., brass and invar). When heated, the strip bends toward the metal with the **lower** expansion coefficient. This bending motion is used to open or close electrical contacts.

In a simple automotive A/C evaporator thermostat:

1. The bimetal coil is exposed to evaporator outlet air.
2. As the air temperature drops toward **0°C**, the strip contracts/rotates.
3. At a preset temperature (typically **2–4°C**), the contacts OPEN.
4. The compressor clutch disengages, halting refrigerant flow.
5. As the evaporator warms back to **6–8°C**, the contacts CLOSE again.
6. The compressor re-engages — this is **ON/OFF (cycling) control**.

This creates a **hysteresis band** that prevents rapid compressor cycling.

## 2.3 Working Principle — Electronic Thermistor Control

Modern vehicles use a **Negative Temperature Coefficient (NTC) thermistor** placed at the evaporator outlet.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **NTC Thermistor Equation:**
> $$R_T = R_0 \cdot e^{B \left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)}$$

Where:
- $R_T$ = resistance at temperature $T$ (K)
- $R_0$ = resistance at reference temperature $T_0$ (typically 298 K = 25°C)
- $B$ = beta constant of the thermistor (typically **3950 K** for automotive NTCs)
- $T$ = absolute temperature in Kelvin
- $e$ = Euler's number ≈ 2.71828

The thermistor forms a **voltage divider** with a fixed resistor, producing an analog voltage read by the ECU. The ECU then decides compressor clutch engagement.

## 2.4 Working Principle — Wax-Pellet Thermostatic Expansion Valve (TXV)

The TXV is the **superheat control** at the evaporator inlet:

1. A temperature-sensing bulb is clamped to the evaporator outlet pipe.
2. The bulb is filled with a wax-pellet (or refrigerant charge).
3. As evaporator outlet temperature rises → bulb pressure rises → wax expands → pushes a diaphragm → opens the valve wider → more refrigerant flows.
4. As evaporator outlet temperature drops (low superheat risk) → bulb pressure drops → valve closes → less refrigerant flows.

> [!NOTE]
> **Superheat** is the temperature difference between the actual refrigerant vapor temperature and its saturation temperature at the evaporator outlet pressure. Typical automotive superheat range: **3–8°C**.

## 2.5 Working Principle — Pressure Switches

### Low-Pressure Cut-Out (LPCO)
- Located on the low-pressure side (after evaporator, before compressor).
- Opens at ≈ **0.18 MPa** (cuts compressor OFF to protect from refrigerant loss/evaporator freeze).
- Closes again at ≈ **0.21 MPa** (re-engages compressor).
- This creates another **hysteresis loop**.

### High-Pressure Cut-Out (HPCO)
- Located on the high-pressure side (before condenser).
- Opens at ≈ **2.7–3.1 MPa** to disengage the compressor and prevent damage.
- Some systems use a **mechanical relief valve** as backup, venting refrigerant to atmosphere or to the low side.

### Trinary Switch
- Combines low-pressure, high-pressure, and a fan-cycle function in one unit.
- Common in R-134a automotive systems.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula | Description | Units / Typical Values |
|---------|-------------|------------------------|
| $R_T = R_0 \cdot e^{B(1/T - 1/T_0)}$ | NTC thermistor resistance | R in Ω, T in K, B ≈ 3950 K |
| $V_{out} = V_{cc} \cdot \frac{R_{fixed}}{R_{fixed} + R_T}$ | Thermistor voltage divider output | V (0–5 V analog) |
| $Q_{cabin} = UA \cdot (T_{amb} - T_{cabin})$ | Cabin heat transfer rate | W, U = overall heat transfer coefficient |
| $\dot{m} = \frac{Q_{evap}}{h_{fg}}$ | Refrigerant mass flow rate | kg/s, h_fg ≈ 173 kJ/kg (R-134a at 0°C) |
| $SH = T_{evap,out} - T_{sat}(P_{evap})$ | Superheat definition | °C, range 3–8°C |
| $COP = \frac{Q_{cooling}}{W_{compressor}}$ | Coefficient of Performance | dimensionless, typical 2.0–3.5 |
| $W_{compressor} = \dot{m} \cdot (h_2 - h_1)$ | Compressor power input | W, enthalpy in kJ/kg |
| $Hysteresis = T_{cut-out} - T_{cut-in}$ | Thermostat switching differential | °C, typically 4–6°C |
| $P_{sat}(T) = e^{A - B/(T+C)}$ | Antoine equation for refrigerant | T in °C, P in kPa or MPa |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anti-Table-Breaking Note:** All absolute-value and "such that" symbols have been rendered using $\vert$ and $\mid$ instead of the raw vertical pipe to keep the markdown table intact.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Fuel economy:** A well-tuned cycling thermostat prevents the compressor from running when cooling is not needed, saving up to **5–8%** of fuel in city driving.
- **Compressor lifespan:** Pressure cut-outs protect the compressor from slugging (liquid refrigerant entering the suction port), which can cause **hydraulic lock** and mechanical failure.
- **Cabin comfort:** Electronic thermistor-based control with sun-load compensation maintains comfort within ±**0.5°C** of setpoint.
- **Refrigerant containment:** Pressure relief valves prevent explosive rupture of hoses and condensers during overcharge or blocked-condenser conditions.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: NTC Thermistor Resistance vs. Temperature

**Given:** A typical automotive NTC thermistor has $R_0 = 10{,}000 \ \Omega$ at $T_0 = 298 \ \text{K}$ (25°C), and $B = 3950 \ \text{K}$.

**Find:** Resistance at $T = 273 \ \text{K}$ (0°C), simulating evaporator outlet.

### Step 1 — Write the governing equation

$$R_T = R_0 \cdot e^{B \left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values

$$\frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{273} = 0.003663 \ \text{K}^{-1}$$

$$\frac{1}{T_0} = \frac{1}{298} = 0.003356 \ \text{K}^{-1}$$

### Step 3 — Compute the difference term

$$\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0} = 0.003663 - 0.003356 = 0.000307 \ \text{K}^{-1}$$

### Step 4 — Multiply by B

$$B \cdot \left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right) = 3950 \times 0.000307 = 1.2127$$

### Step 5 — Evaluate the exponential

$$e^{1.2127} \approx 3.3634$$

### Step 6 — Compute the final resistance

$$R_T = 10{,}000 \times 3.3634 = 33{,}634 \ \Omega$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering interpretation:** As the temperature drops from 25°C → 0°C, the thermistor resistance rises from 10 kΩ to ~33.6 kΩ. The ECU detects this change in voltage across the divider circuit and uses it to decide compressor ON/OFF.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Thermistor Voltage Divider Output

**Given:** $V_{cc} = 5.0 \ \text{V}$, $R_{fixed} = 10 \ \text{k}\Omega$, $R_T = 33{,}634 \ \Omega$ at 0°C.

### Step 1 — Voltage divider formula

$$V_{out} = V_{cc} \cdot \frac{R_{fixed}}{R_{fixed} + R_T}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute

$$V_{out} = 5.0 \cdot \frac{10{,}000}{10{,}000 + 33{,}634}$$

### Step 3 — Denominator

$$10{,}000 + 33{,}634 = 43{,}634 \ \Omega$$

### Step 4 — Ratio

$$\frac{10{,}000}{43{,}634} = 0.2292$$

### Step 5 — Final voltage

$$V_{out} = 5.0 \times 0.2292 = 1.146 \ \text{V}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The ECU analog-to-digital converter reads 1.146 V, which corresponds (via a preloaded lookup table) to a "cold evaporator" condition. The ECU disengages the compressor clutch.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Cabin Heat Load Estimation

**Given:** A sedan with cabin volume of 2.8 m³, ambient 38°C, desired cabin 24°C, overall $UA = 80 \ \text{W/K}$ (including ventilation, conduction, radiation, and solar).

### Step 1 — Heat transfer rate

$$Q_{cabin} = UA \cdot (T_{amb} - T_{cabin})$$

### Step 2 — Substitute

$$Q_{cabin} = 80 \cdot (38 - 24)$$

### Step 3 — Result

$$Q_{cabin} = 80 \times 14 = 1120 \ \text{W}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The A/C system must remove ~1.12 kW to maintain comfort. This sets the minimum compressor displacement requirement. With an extra 500 W for solar load and 400 W for passenger metabolic heat, the total can reach ~**2.0 kW** under peak conditions.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Required Refrigerant Mass Flow Rate

**Given:** Total cooling load $Q_{evap} = 2.0 \ \text{kW} = 2000 \ \text{W}$, latent heat of vaporization of R-134a at 0°C evaporator = 173 kJ/kg.

### Step 1 — Energy balance on evaporator

$$\dot{m}_{ref} = \frac{Q_{evap}}{h_{fg}}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute

$$\dot{m}_{ref} = \frac{2000}{173{,}000}$$

### Step 3 — Result

$$\dot{m}_{ref} = 0.01156 \ \text{kg/s} = 41.6 \ \text{kg/h}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The compressor must circulate ~42 kg of R-134a per hour to meet the load. This determines the compressor displacement (typically 100–160 cc for a passenger car).

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Compressor Power and COP

**Given:** Refrigerant mass flow = 0.0116 kg/s, enthalpy at compressor inlet $h_1 = 397 \ \text{kJ/kg}$, enthalpy at compressor outlet $h_2 = 432 \ \text{kJ/kg}$.

### Step 1 — Compressor power

$$W_{comp} = \dot{m} \cdot (h_2 - h_1)$$

### Step 2 — Substitute

$$W_{comp} = 0.0116 \cdot (432 - 397)$$

### Step 3 — Result

$$W_{comp} = 0.0116 \times 35 = 0.406 \ \text{kW} = 406 \ \text{W}$$

### Step 4 — Coefficient of Performance

$$COP = \frac{Q_{cooling}}{W_{compressor}} = \frac{2000}{406} = 4.93$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world check:** A COP of 4.93 is high — actual automotive systems operate at COP ≈ 2.0–3.0 due to real-cycle irreversibilities, heat exchanger losses, and mechanical friction.

## 3.6 Analytical Decision Matrix — Selecting the Right Control

| Operating Condition | Required Control Action | Device Activated |
|---------------------|--------------------------|------------------|
| Evaporator too cold (T < 2°C) | Prevent freeze | Evaporator thermostat / NTC sensor |
| Low refrigerant charge | Protect compressor | Low-pressure cut-out switch |
| Condenser blocked (high P) | Disengage compressor | High-pressure cut-out |
| High engine load (idle, hot day) | Boost condenser airflow | Condenser fan relay / trinary switch |
| Cabin setpoint reached | Modulate blower | Blower resistor / PWM controller |
| Sun-load high | Reduce cabin temp auto | Sun-load photodiode + ECU |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of Automotive A/C Control System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    cabTS["Cabin Temperature Sensor NTC"]
    evapTS["Evaporator Outlet Thermistor"]
    ambTS["Ambient Temperature Sensor"]
    sunS["Sun Load Photodiode"]
    hpSw["High Pressure Cut Out Switch"]
    lpSw["Low Pressure Cut Out Switch"]
    bsw["Blower Switch and Resistor Pack"]
    dspU["Driver Setpoint Dial"]
    ecu["HVAC ECU Control Module"]
    clRly["Compressor Clutch Relay"]
    bMot["Blower Motor"]
    dmpr["Mode and Temperature Damper Actuators"]
    condF["Condenser Fan Relay"]
    cpr["Compressor Clutch"]
    condFn["Condenser Fan"]

    cabTS --> ecu
    evapTS --> ecu
    ambTS --> ecu
    sunS --> ecu
    hpSw --> ecu
    lpSw --> ecu
    dspU --> ecu
    bsw --> ecu

    ecu --> clRly
    ecu --> bMot
    ecu --> dmpr
    ecu --> condF

    clRly --> cpr
    bMot --> airflow["Cabin Airflow"]
    condF --> condFn
    dmpr --> airflow
```

## 4.2 Thermostat Switching Logic Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    start(["Start of Cycle"])
    readT["Read Evaporator Outlet Temperature"]
    chk1{"T less than T cutout"}
    cmpOff["Compressor OFF Clutch Disengaged"]
    chk2{"T greater than T cutin"}
    cmpOn["Compressor ON Clutch Engaged"]
    loopA["Hysteresis Band Active"]
    stop(["Return to Reading Temperature"])

    start --> readT
    readT --> chk1
    chk1 -- YES --> cmpOff
    cmpOff --> loopA
    loopA --> readT
    chk1 -- NO --> chk2
    chk2 -- YES --> cmpOn
    cmpOn --> stop
    chk2 -- NO --> readT
```

## 4.3 TXV Operation Schematic (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    compOut["Compressor Discharge Hot Gas"]
    condIn["Condenser Inlet"]
    condCore["Condenser Core Heat Rejection to Ambient"]
    condOut["Condenser Outlet High Pressure Liquid"]
    txvIn["TXV Inlet Filter"]
    bulbEvap["Temperature Sensing Bulb on Evaporator Outlet"]
    txvDia["TXV Diaphragm Chamber"]
    txvOut["TXV Outlet Metered Refrigerant to Evaporator"]
    evapCore["Evaporator Core Absorbs Cabin Heat"]
    evapOut["Evaporator Outlet Low Pressure Vapor"]
    accu["Receiver Drier Accumulator"]

    compOut --> condIn
    condIn --> condCore
```
