Substituting $\Delta P = 500 \text{ kPa} = 5 \times 10^{5}$ Pa, $A_b = 2.545 \times 10^{-4}$ m², $k = 800$ N/m, $L = 0.030$ m:

$$\Delta \theta = \dfrac{(5 \times 10^{5})(2.545 \times 10^{-4})}{800 \times 0.030}$$

$$\Delta \theta = \dfrac{127.25}{24} = 5.30 \text{ rad}$$

Wait — that is unrealistically large. Re-checking units: the lever arm length is given in **mm** in the problem. Re-interpreting $L = 30$ mm $= 0.030$ m is correct, but the formula assumes the angle is in radians and small-angle approximation, giving an unreasonably large result. The correct interpretation is that the actual throw angle is small (≈ 5–15°), meaning the problem expects a **bellows area much smaller** or a **larger $L$**. For pedagogical purposes, the formal answer is:

$$\boxed{\,\Delta \theta \approx 5.30 \text{ rad} \approx 304^{\circ}\,}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students frequently forget to convert spring pre-compression from mm to m, leading to a 1000× error. Always convert **all** length units to SI (metres, Pa, N) before substituting into any formula.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Quick Comparison Table

| Feature | HPCO | LPCO |
|---|---|---|
| Location in circuit | Between compressor discharge & condenser | Between evaporator outlet & compressor suction |
| Typical trip pressure (R-134a) | 2.45 MPa (gauge) | 0.12 MPa (gauge) |
| Failure mode protected | Condenser blockage, fan failure, overcharge | Refrigerant leak, expansion valve blockage, filter choke |
| Switch action on trip | OPEN (breaks clutch/contactor) | OPEN (breaks clutch/contactor) |
| Reset pressure | Trip − Differential | Trip + Differential |
| Reset type (auto/manual) | Usually auto (in DPS); some trucks manual | Usually auto |
| Sensor element | Bellows (high-pressure body) | Bellows (low-pressure body) |
| Typical differential | 0.5 MPa | 0.08 MPa |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Dual Pressure Switch (DPS) in an Automotive Refrigeration Circuit

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates how the HPCO and LPCO functions are integrated into a single Dual Pressure Switch and how it interacts with the compressor clutch, ECU, and refrigerant circuit.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Compressor Discharge Line] --> B[HPCO Bellows Sensor]
    B --> C{HPCO Trip Logic}
    C -->|Pressure > 2.45 MPa| D[Switch Contact OPEN]
    C -->|Pressure < 1.96 MPa| E[Switch Contact CLOSED]
    D --> F[Series-OR Logic Node]
    E --> F
    G[Evaporator Outlet / Suction Line] --> H[LPCO Bellows Sensor]
    H --> I{LPCO Trip Logic}
    I -->|Pressure < 0.12 MPa| J[Switch Contact OPEN]
    I -->|Pressure > 0.20 MPa| K[Switch Contact CLOSED]
    J --> F
    K --> F
    F --> L[Compressor Clutch Relay Coil]
    L -->|Energised| M[Compressor RUNNING]
    L -->|De-energised| N[Compressor OFF]
    O[Vehicle ECU] -.->|Diagnostic Signal| F
    P[Dashboard Warning Lamp] <-.-> O
    F -.->|Fault Code| O
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- The HPCO and LPCO contacts are wired in **series** within the clutch coil circuit. If *either* contact opens, the clutch is de-energised and the compressor stops.
- The ECU monitors the switch state (dashed lines) and triggers a dashboard warning if the compressor remains OFF for an abnormal duration.
- The **Series-OR behaviour** is intentional: a single failure mode (over-pressure *or* under-pressure) is sufficient to protect the system.

### 4.2 Cross-Sectional Schematic of a Pressure Cut-Out Switch (Bellows–Lever–Snap-Action)

The following block diagram abstracts the internal mechanical architecture of a single-pole pressure cut-out. It is drawn as a **functional architecture flow** rather than a literal cross-section, in line with the engine's Mermaid safety rule (no complex mechanical drawings attempted).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Refrigerant Pressure Port] --> B1[Welded Metallic Bellows]
    B1 -->|Axial expansion| L1[Pushrod / Lever]
    L1 --> S1[Adjustable Calibrated Spring]
    S1 -->|Opposes motion| L1
    L1 --> M1[Snap-Action Micro-switch]
    M1 --> T1{Threshold Reached?}
    T1 -->|Yes| O1[Electrical Contact OPENS]
    T1 -->|No| C1[Electrical Contact CLOSED]
    O1 --> X1[Compressor Clutch Circuit BROKEN]
    C1 --> X2[Compressor Clutch Circuit INTACT]
    ADJ[Adjustment Screw] -.->|Sets preload| S1
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- Refrigerant pressure enters the port and acts on the bellows.
- The bellows expansion is opposed by the calibrated spring (whose preload is set by the adjustment screw during factory calibration).
- At the trip threshold, the snap-action micro-switch breaks the electrical contact, de-energising the compressor clutch.
- On pressure recovery (HPCO: pressure falls; LPCO: pressure rises), the spring returns the lever, and the micro-switch re-closes.

### 4.3 Integration of Pressure Cut-Outs in a Complete Automotive AC Refrigeration Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    COMP[Compressor] -->|High-pressure vapor| DSH[Discharge Line]
    DSH --> COND[Condenser]
    COND --> RCVR[Receiver-Drier]
    RCVR --> EXV[Expansion Valve]
    EXV --> EVAP[Evaporator]
    EVAP --> SUC[Suction Line]
    SUC --> COMP
    DSH -.->|Sensing port| HPCO[HPCO Switch]
    SUC -.->|Sensing port| LPCO[LPCO Switch]
    HPCO --> CLUTCH[Compressor Clutch Relay]
    LPCO --> CLUTCH
    CLUTCH --> COMP
    ECU[Vehicle ECU] -.->|Monitors| HPCO
    ECU -.->|Monitors| LPCO
    ECU --> DASH[Dashboard Warning]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define a pressure cut-out switch. Differentiate between a high-pressure cut-out (HPCO) and a low-pressure cut-out (LPCO) in an automotive refrigeration system.*

**Model Answer:**

A pressure cut-out is a pressure-actuated safety control device that automatically opens an electrical circuit when the refrigerant pressure in the system deviates beyond a preset safe limit, thereby protecting the compressor and other components from damage.

| Aspect | HPCO | LPCO |
|---|---|---|
| Location | High-side (compressor discharge) | Low-side (compressor suction) |
| Function | Prevents excessive discharge pressure (e.g., condenser blockage) | Prevents compressor running with insufficient refrigerant |
| Typical trip pressure (R-134a) | ~ 2.45 MPa | ~ 0.12 MPa (gauge) |
| Reset direction | Pressure must *fall* to reset | Pressure must *rise* to reset |

*(3 marks — definition 1 mark, comparison table 2 marks.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sample Paper]**
*What is meant by the "differential" of a pressure cut-out switch? Why is it necessary, and what is its typical value for an automotive HPCO?*

**Model Answer:**

The differential of a pressure cut-out is the pressure difference between the cut-out (trip) pressure and the cut-in (reset) pressure. For an HPCO, the differential is $P_{trip} - P_{reset}$, and for an LPCO, it is $P_{reset} - P_{trip}$.

It is necessary to prevent rapid cycling (chattering) of the compressor near the trip threshold, which would cause motor winding overheating, oil foaming, and clutch wear. The typical differential for an automotive HPCO using R-134a is approximately **0.5 MPa (≈ 70 psi)**.

*(3 marks — definition 1 mark, necessity 1 mark, typical value 1 mark.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** *With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of a high-pressure cut-out (HPCO) switch used in an automotive air-conditioning system. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *A pressure cut-out switch has a bellows of effective diameter 22 mm and a spring constant of 1200 N/m. The spring is pre-compressed by 10 mm. If the snap-action micro-switch reaction force is 2 N, calculate the cut-out pressure in kPa and bar. (7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Construction and Working of HPCO (7 marks):**

The HPCO switch consists of the following parts (list 3 marks; working explanation 3 marks; diagram 1 mark):

1. **Pressure port:** Threaded fitting connected to the compressor discharge line.
2. **Metallic bellows:** Welded stainless-steel or phosphor-bronze bellows that axially expand with rising pressure.
3. **Lever / pushrod:** Transfers bellows motion to the switch contact.
4. **Calibrated spring:** Opposes bellows motion; preload sets the trip pressure.
5. **Adjustment screw:** Used to fine-tune the trip set-point during calibration.
6. **Snap-action micro-switch (SPDT):** Provides the electrical make/break action.
7. **Electrical terminals:** Connect to the compressor clutch relay coil.

**Working:** Refrigerant discharge pressure enters the bellows. As long as the pressure is below the trip set-point (e.g., 2.45 MPa), the spring force dominates, and the micro-switch contact remains closed, allowing the compressor clutch to be energised. When discharge pressure exceeds the trip set-point (due to condenser blockage, fan failure, or overcharge), the bellows force overcomes the spring preload, the snap-action micro-switch opens, the clutch relay coil is de-energised, and the compressor stops. As the high-side pressure gradually falls (heat rejection to ambient continues), it eventually reaches the cut-in (reset) pressure $P_{trip} - \Delta P$. At this point, the spring overcomes the bellows force, the contact re-closes, and the compressor restarts.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given data:**

- Effective bellows diameter: $d = 22$ mm $= 0.022$ m
- Spring constant: $k = 1200$ N/m
- Pre-compression: $x_{pre} = 10$ mm $= 0.010$ m
- Snap-switch reaction: $R_{sw}^{*} = 2$ N

**Step 1 — Effective bellows area:**

$$A_b = \dfrac{\pi}{4} d^2 = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (0.022)^2 = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times 4.84 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$A_b = 3.801 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Spring force at trip:**

$$F_s = k \cdot x_{pre} = 1200 \times 0.010 = 12.0 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3 — Total resisting force:**

$$F_{resist} = F_s + R_{sw}^{*} = 12.0 + 2.0 = 14.0 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4 — Cut-out pressure:**

$$P_{trip} = \dfrac{F_{resist}}{A_b} = \dfrac{14.0}{3.801 \times 10^{-4}} = 36{,}832 \text{ Pa}$$

$$\boxed{\,P_{trip} \approx 36.8 \text{ kPa (gauge)} \approx 0.368 \text{ bar (gauge)}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on the numerical result:** The obtained cut-out pressure of 36.8 kPa is much lower than a real automotive HPCO trip pressure (≈ 2.45 MPa) because the problem uses a *small-diameter bellows with a soft spring* — these are pedagogical values chosen by the examiner to keep the arithmetic simple, not real-world values. The *method* is what earns full marks.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** *Explain the function of a Dual Pressure Switch (DPS) in a modern automotive AC system. How does it differ from a trinary switch? (7 marks)*

**(b)** *An LPCO switch trips at 0.12 MPa (gauge) and has a differential of 0.08 MPa. Determine the cut-in pressure. If the suction line contains 0.5 kg of R-134a vapor at 5 °C with a specific volume of 0.05 m³/kg, estimate the time taken for the suction pressure to rise from trip to cut-in, assuming a refrigerant leak of 5 g/min into the suction line from the evaporator outlet. State the key assumption. (7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Function of DPS and Comparison with Trinary Switch (7 marks):**

**Function of DPS (4 marks):** A Dual Pressure Switch is a compact, single-housing combination of a High-Pressure Cut-Out and a Low-Pressure Cut-Out, both wired in **series** in the compressor clutch relay coil circuit. It performs two safety functions simultaneously:

- Disengages the compressor clutch if the **discharge pressure** exceeds the HPCO set-point (e.g., 2.45 MPa for R-134a), preventing condenser-side overpressure.
- Disengages the compressor clutch if the **suction pressure** falls below the LPCO set-point (e.g., 0.12 MPa gauge), preventing compressor operation with insufficient refrigerant charge.

The series wiring ensures that **either** over-pressure *or* under-pressure will independently break the clutch circuit, providing fail-safe protection with a single component.

**Differences from a Trinary Switch (3 marks):**

| Feature | Dual Pressure Switch (DPS) | Trinary Switch |
|---|---|---|
| Number of functions | 2 (HPCO + LPCO) | 3 (HPCO + LPCO + Condenser fan cycling) |
| Fan control | No | Yes — cycles condenser fan at mid-pressure |
| Typical pressure port | High-side and low-side | Receiver outlet / liquid line |
| Use case | Simple on/off automotive AC | Systems with electric condenser fans |

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Cut-in pressure of LPCO:**

For an LPCO, cut-in pressure is *higher* than the trip pressure:

$$P_{reset,L} = P_{trip,L} + \Delta P_L = 0.12 + 0.08 = 0.20 \text{ MPa (gauge)}$$

$$\boxed{\,P_{reset,L} = 0.20 \text{ MPa (gauge)}\,}$$

**Step 2 — Pressure rise to be achieved:**

$$\Delta P_{rise} = 0.20 - 0.12 = 0.08 \text{ MPa} = 80 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 3 — Mass of refrigerant needed to raise suction pressure by 80 kPa:**

Volume of suction line: $V = m \cdot v = 0.5 \times 0.05 = 0.025$ m³

Using ideal-gas approximation at constant volume and temperature, the mass needed to raise pressure by $\Delta P$ is:

$$\Delta m = \dfrac{V \cdot \Delta P}{R_{specific} \cdot T}$$

However, since the problem provides a *leak rate* into the suction line, the time taken can be estimated directly as:

$$t = \dfrac{\Delta m_{required}}{\dot{m}_{leak}}$$

**Step 4 — Key assumption:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Assumption:** The pressure rise is *linearly proportional* to the mass of refrigerant added, and the leak rate is constant at 5 g/min. Temperature is assumed constant at 5 °C during the pressure rise.

**Step 5 — Estimate of $\Delta m$ using $PV = mRT$:**

For R-134a, $R_{specific} = \dfrac{R_{universal}}{M} = \dfrac{8.314}{0.10203} = 81.5$ J/(kg·K)

$T = 5 + 273 = 278$ K

$$\Delta m = \dfrac{P \cdot \Delta V \cdot M}{R \cdot T}$$

Alternative: $\Delta m = V \cdot \Delta \rho$, with $\rho = P/(R_{specific} T)$.

Initial $\rho_1 = \dfrac{120{,}000}{81.5 \times 278} = 5.30$ kg/m³

Final $\rho_2 = \dfrac{200{,}000}{81.5 \times 278} = 8.83$ kg/m³

$$\Delta \rho = 8.83 - 5.30 = 3.53 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

$$\Delta m = V \cdot \Delta \rho = 0.025 \times 3.53 = 0.0883 \text{ kg} = 88.3 \text{ g}$$

**Step 6 — Time to rise:**

$$t = \dfrac{88.3 \text{ g}}{5 \text{ g/min}} = 17.66 \text{ min}$$

$$\boxed{\,t \approx 17.7 \text{ minutes}\,}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse cut-in and cut-out directions. For an **LPCO**, the cut-in pressure is *higher* (pressure must *rise* to reset). For an **HPCO**, the cut-in pressure is *lower* (pressure must *fall* to reset). Mixing this up is a guaranteed loss of 1–2 marks. Also, do not forget to convert gauge pressure to absolute if using ideal-gas law. Since the problem gives values in kPa gauge and the absolute is needed for gas law, add atmospheric pressure (101.325 kPa) to gauge values when applying $PV = mRT$. In the above solution, the *delta* in pressure cancels the atmospheric offset, so the gauge-difference approach is valid; however, students should explicitly state this assumption.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Read This the Night Before the Exam**

- **Pressure cut-out** = pressure-actuated safety switch that opens an electrical circuit on abnormal pressure.
- **HPCO (High-Pressure Cut-Out):** Located on the discharge line; trips when pressure is *too high*; typical trip ≈ **2.45 MPa** for R-134a.
- **LPCO (Low-Pressure Cut-Out):** Located on the suction line; trips when pressure is *too low*; typical trip ≈ **0.12 MPa (gauge)** for R-134a.
- **Differential ($\Delta P$):** Dead-band between trip and reset; prevents compressor short-cycling. HPCO $\Delta P \approx 0.5$ MPa; LPCO $\Delta P \approx 0.08$ MPa.
- **DPS (Dual Pressure Switch):** Combines HPCO + LPCO in one housing, contacts in *series* with compressor clutch.
- **Trinary Switch:** Adds condenser-fan cycling control (3 functions in one unit).
- **Force-balance equation:** $P_{trip} = (k \cdot x_{pre} + R_{sw}^{*}) / A_b$.
- **Differential equation:** $\Delta P = (k \cdot L \cdot \Delta \theta) / A_b$.
- **Cut-in / Cut-out direction rules:** HPCO → cut-in is *lower*; LPCO → cut-in is *higher*. This is the most-tested single fact in Module 3 viva voce.
- **Unit conversions:** 1 bar = 100 kPa = 14.504 psi; always convert mm → m, cm² → m² before substituting.
- **Real-world protection:** HPCO guards against condenser blockage/overcharge; LPCO guards against refrigerant loss/undercharge.
- **Reset type:** Most automotive DPS units are *automatic reset*; some heavy-truck HPCOs are *manual reset* for safety.
- **Sensor element:** Welded metallic bellows (legacy) or diaphragmatic capsule (modern, lower cost).
- **Snap-action micro-switch:** Provides quick make/break to prevent contact arcing.
- **Diagnostic integration:** Modern ECUs read the DPS state and log fault codes (P0530, P0531, P0532, P0533, P0534 for pressure sensor/switch issues).
- **Exam tip:** When asked "where is it located?" — answer *physically* (which line) and *electrically* (clutch circuit) for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Relays
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT523 - AUTOMOTIVE REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Relays

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Relay:** An electrically operated switch that uses an electromagnet to mechanically control one or more sets of contacts. It allows a low-power control circuit to switch a high-power load circuit, providing both electrical isolation and current amplification. In automotive air-conditioning systems, relays are essential electromechanical control elements that act as remote-controlled switches, enabling the ECU or dashboard switches to handle high compressor-clutch, blower-motor, and condenser-fan currents safely.

A relay fundamentally consists of two electrically isolated circuits:
1. **Control (Primary) Circuit** — the low-current coil energizing circuit driven by the switch/ECU.
2. **Load (Secondary) Circuit** — the high-current load circuit switched through the contacts.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Water-Tap Amplifier:**
> Imagine you want to open a giant industrial water valve (carrying 1000 litres/min) but you can only push with your little finger. You attach a long lever to the valve handle. Your finger pushes the lever easily (small force), and the lever amplifies it to open the heavy valve. **A relay works exactly like this lever** — a tiny current from a dashboard switch pushes an electromagnet, which in turn mechanically pulls a heavy contact pair to switch on a high-current device like the compressor clutch coil.

### 1.3 Why Relays Are Used in Automotive AC Systems

Modern automotive AC systems demand switching currents in the range of **20 A to 40 A** (compressor clutch, blower motors, condenser fans). The dashboard switches, ECU output transistors, and wiring harnesses are designed for low-current logic signals (typically **< 1 A**). Directly routing compressor current through a dashboard switch would cause:

- **Contact arcing and switch welding** (insufficient contact mass)
- **Voltage drops** (thin wires heating up)
- **Fire hazard** in the passenger cabin

A relay solves all of these by placing the high-current arc *inside an under-hood relay box*, far from the driver.

### 1.4 Standard Performance Metrics (Industry-Standard)

| Metric | Typical Value (Automotive) |
|---|---|
| Coil Operating Voltage | **12 V DC** (cars) / **24 V DC** (trucks/heavy vehicles) |
| Coil Resistance | **50 Ω – 200 Ω** |
| Coil Current Draw | **60 mA – 240 mA** |
| Contact Rating (Resistive) | **30 A – 40 A** at **14 V DC** |
| Operate Time | **5 ms – 15 ms** |
| Release Time | **2 ms – 10 ms** |
| Mechanical Life | **10⁷ operations** |
| Electrical Life | **10⁵ operations** at full load |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Module 3 explicitly tests the **functional role**, **construction**, **contact arrangement (SPST/SPDT/DPDT)**, and **automotive AC applications** of relays. Marks are frequently awarded for correctly identifying the relay type and its placement in the AC electrical schematic.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Detailed Construction of an Electromagnetic Relay

A standard automotive relay consists of the following parts:

- **Yoke (Frame):** Soft iron core that completes the magnetic circuit. Provides a low-reluctance path for flux.
- **Core / Pole Piece:** The central iron rod around which the coil is wound. Concentrates magnetic flux at the armature air gap.
- **Coil (Solenoid):** A bobbin wound with fine-gauge copper wire (typically **AWG 32 – AWG 38**). When energised, it produces a magnetomotive force (MMF).
- **Armature:** A pivoted soft-iron lever that is attracted toward the core when the coil is energised. It carries the moving contact.
- **Spring (Return Spring):** A spring that pulls the armature back to its resting (de-energised) position when the coil current is removed.
- **Contacts (Movable & Stationary):** Heavy-duty silver-alloy (AgSnO₂ or AgCdO) contact pairs that physically open/close the load circuit.
- **Terminals:** Standard automotive terminals — typically **85** (coil +), **86** (coil −), **30** (common), **87** (NO output), **87a** (NC output) per the **ISO 7588** standard pin-out.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Memory Trick — "86 85 30 87":**
> - **85 & 86** = Coil terminals (polarity-agnostic for non-diode relays)
> - **30** = Common feed (Battery +12 V input)
> - **87** = Normally Open (NO) output to load
> - **87a** = Normally Closed (NC) output to load

### 2.2 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step

1. **At Rest (Coil De-energised):**
   The spring holds the armature away from the core. The **Normally Closed (NC)** contact is closed, and the **Normally Open (NO)** contact is open.
2. **Energisation Phase:**
   When the control switch is closed, current $I_c$ flows through the coil, producing a magnetomotive force:
   
   $$F_{mmf} = N \cdot I_c$$
   
   where $N$ is the number of turns and $I_c$ is the coil current in amperes.
3. **Flux Generation:**
   The MMF drives magnetic flux $\Phi$ through the yoke–core–armature air-gap loop. Flux density at the air gap is:
   
   $$B = \frac{\Phi}{A_g}$$
   
   where $A_g$ is the cross-sectional area of the air gap.
4. **Electromagnetic Force on Armature:**
   The Maxwell stress at the air gap produces a pull force:
   
   $$F_{pull} = \frac{B^2 \cdot A_g}{2 \mu_0}$$
   
   where $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \text{ H/m}$ is the permeability of free space.
5. **Contact Actuation:**
   When $F_{pull} > F_{spring} + F_{contact\,friction}$, the armature snaps in, the NC contact opens, and the NO contact closes.
6. **De-energisation:**
   When the control switch is opened, $I_c \to 0$, the magnetic field collapses, and the return spring restores the armature to the resting position.

### 2.3 Types of Relays Used in Automotive AC

| Relay Type | Symbol | Function in AC System |
|---|---|---|
| **SPST (Single-Pole Single-Throw)** | NO or NC, one circuit | Compressor clutch relay, condenser fan relay |
| **SPDT (Single-Pole Double-Throw)** | Changeover (87 + 87a) | Blower speed changeover, fresh/recirculation air mode |
| **DPST (Double-Pole Single-Throw)** | Two simultaneous NO contacts | Dual-fan systems, paired loads |
| **DPDT (Double-Pole Double-Throw)** | Two changeover contacts | Reversing damper motors, mode door actuators |
| **Reed Relay** | Sealed glass contacts | Low-power AC sensor signals (e.g., low-pressure switch) |
| **Solid-State Relay (SSR)** | Triac/MOSFET output | PWM blower control, variable-speed compressor control |

### 2.4 Automotive AC Relay Applications — Deep Dive

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 3 Focus:** The KTU syllabus places heavy emphasis on the **role of relays in the AC electrical architecture**. The following applications appear repeatedly in past question papers.

#### 2.4.1 Compressor Electromagnetic Clutch Relay
- **Function:** Energises the compressor clutch coil (~**3 A – 5 A** at **12 V**) to engage the belt-driven compressor.
- **Triggered by:** ECM/AC ECU after verifying refrigerant pressure (via high/low-pressure switches) and evaporator temperature (via thermistor).
- **Contactor rating:** Typically **20 A** (derated to 70 % for inductive load).

#### 2.4.2 Blower Motor Relay
- **Function:** Supplies battery voltage to the blower fan motor (**8 A – 25 A** depending on speed).
- **Configuration:** Often used in conjunction with a **blower resistor pack** (for low/medium speeds) and direct battery feed (for high speed).

#### 2.4.3 Condenser / Radiator Fan Relay(s)
- **Function:** Switches the **12 V DC** cooling fan motor (often **40 A – 60 A** peak inrush, **15 A – 20 A** running).
- **Dual-relay configuration:** Common in vehicles with separate condenser and radiator fans, allowing independent control based on refrigerant pressure and engine coolant temperature.

#### 2.4.4 Auxiliary / Defrost Relay
- **Function:** Routes current to the rear-window defroster grid and AC blend door actuators.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Coil Resistance (Ohm's Law) | $R_c = \dfrac{V_c}{I_c}$ | Ω | Verify coil health |
| 2 | Coil Power Dissipation | $P_c = V_c^2 / R_c = I_c^2 \cdot R_c$ | W | Thermal design of relay box |
| 3 | Magnetomotive Force | $F_{mmf} = N \cdot I_c$ | Ampere-turns (At) | Magnetic pull strength |
| 4 | Magnetic Flux Density at Air Gap | $B = \dfrac{\mu_0 \cdot N \cdot I_c}{l_g}$ | Tesla (T) | Pull force calculation |
| 5 | Electromagnetic Pull Force | $F_{pull} = \dfrac{B^2 \cdot A_g}{2 \mu_0}$ | Newtons (N) | Spring preload design |
| 6 | Inductance Energy Stored in Coil | $E = \dfrac{1}{2} L I_c^2$ | Joules (J) | Coil suppression/snubber design |
| 7 | Contact Voltage Drop | $V_{drop} = I_{load} \cdot R_{contact}$ | Volts (V) | Power loss at contacts |
| 8 | Power Loss at Contacts | $P_{loss} = I_{load}^2 \cdot R_{contact}$ | W | Thermal rating check |
| 9 | Relay Operate Time (approx.) | $t_{op} \approx \dfrac{L}{R_c} \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{V_s}{V_s - V_{pickup}}\right)$ | seconds (s) | Time-delay relay tuning |
| 10 | Coil Inductance (solenoid) | $L = \dfrac{\mu_0 \cdot \mu_r \cdot N^2 \cdot A}{l}$ | Henries (H) | Coil design |

> [!NOTE]
> **Symbols used:** $V_c$ = coil voltage, $I_c$ = coil current, $N$ = number of turns, $l_g$ = air-gap length, $A_g$ = air-gap area, $\mu_r$ = relative permeability of core, $V_{pickup}$ = relay pull-in voltage, $V_s$ = supply voltage.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

In modern vehicles, relays are increasingly being **replaced by solid-state switches (MOSFETs/IGBTs)** controlled by the BCM (Body Control Module). However, electromechanical relays still dominate the **under-bonnet relay box** for the compressor clutch, fans, and blower motor due to their **low on-state resistance (typically < 50 mΩ)**, **high surge current capability**, and **galvanic isolation**. KTU students are expected to be able to **read and interpret relay wiring diagrams** and trace signals from the AC switch to the actuator through the relay coil.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivative 1 — Pull Force on a Relay Armature

We derive the force that the energised coil exerts on the armature at the air gap.

**Step 1: Define the system.** A relay armature presents an air gap of length $l_g$ in the magnetic circuit when de-energised. When the coil carries current $I_c$ through $N$ turns, it produces a magnetomotive force.

$$F_{mmf} = N \cdot I_c$$

**Step 2: Apply Hopkinson's law (reluctance concept).** The total reluctance $\mathcal{R}$ of the magnetic circuit is dominated by the air gap because the iron path has very high permeability.

$$\mathcal{R} = \frac{l_g}{\mu_0 \cdot A_g}$$

**Step 3: Compute the magnetic flux.** Flux equals MMF divided by total reluctance.

$$\Phi = \frac{F_{mmf}}{\mathcal{R}} = \frac{N \cdot I_c \cdot \mu_0 \cdot A_g}{l_g}$$

**Step 4: Compute the flux density at the air gap.**

$$B = \frac{\Phi}{A_g} = \frac{\mu_0 \cdot N \cdot I_c}{l_g}$$

**Step 5: Apply the Maxwell stress equation.** The pressure (force per unit area) at the air gap is given by the magnetic energy density.

$$p = \frac{B^2}{2 \mu_0}$$

**Step 6: Multiply by gap area to get the total pull force.**

$$\boxed{F_{pull} = \frac{B^2 \cdot A_g}{2 \mu_0} = \frac{\mu_0 \cdot N^2 \cdot I_c^2 \cdot A_g}{2 l_g^2}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** The pull force is **proportional to the square of the coil current** and **inversely proportional to the square of the air gap length**. This is why the armature snaps in suddenly once the pull-in voltage is reached — the air gap collapses, the force increases, and the system becomes positively self-energising.

### 3.2 Derivative 2 — Coil Resistance and Power Dissipation (Worked Example)

> **Problem:** A 12 V automotive relay has a coil rated at 80 mA. Calculate (a) coil resistance, (b) coil power dissipation, (c) number of turns if the coil bobbin has a mean turn length of 30 mm and uses AWG-36 copper wire (resistance ≈ **1.4 Ω/m**).

**Solution:**

**Step (a): Coil Resistance**

$$R_c = \frac{V_c}{I_c} = \frac{12 \text{ V}}{0.080 \text{ A}} = 150 \, \Omega$$

**Step (b): Power Dissipation in the Coil**

$$P_c = V_c \cdot I_c = 12 \times 0.080 = 0.96 \text{ W}$$

**Step (c): Number of Turns**

Total wire length $l_{wire} = R_c / r_{per\,m} = 150 / 1.4 \approx 107.1 \text{ m}$.

Length per turn $l_{turn} = 0.030 \text{ m}$.

$$N = \frac{l_{wire}}{l_{turn}} = \frac{107.1}{0.030} \approx 3570 \text{ turns}$$

### 3.3 Derivative 3 — Contact Voltage Drop Analysis

> **Problem:** A compressor clutch relay carries 4 A through silver-oxide contacts with measured contact resistance of **8 mΩ**. Calculate (a) voltage drop across contacts, (b) power lost as heat at the contacts.

**Solution:**

**Step (a): Voltage Drop**

$$V_{drop} = I_{load} \cdot R_{contact} = 4 \times 0.008 = 0.032 \text{ V} = 32 \text{ mV}$$

**Step (b): Power Loss at Contacts**

$$P_{loss} = I_{load}^2 \cdot R_{contact} = 4^2 \times 0.008 = 0.128 \text{ W}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This 128 mW of heat per relay is dissipated inside the under-bonnet relay box. A vehicle may have 4–6 AC relays running simultaneously, so relay-box thermal design (heat sinks, ventilation, derating) is critical for reliability.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Relay Selection Criteria for AC Loads

| Load Type | Inrush Current | Steady Current | Recommended Relay Type | Contact Derating |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Compressor Clutch Coil | ~ 6 A (first 50 ms) | 4 A | SPST-NO, 20 A contact | 80 % of rating → 16 A usable |
| Blower Motor (low) | 18 A | 8 A | SPST-NO, 30 A contact | 70 % → 21 A usable |
| Blower Motor (high) | 35 A | 22 A | SPST-NO, 40 A contact | 70 % → 28 A usable |
| Condenser Fan Motor | 50 A | 18 A | SPST-NO, 40 A contact (with inrush margin) | 60 % for inductive |
| Evaporator Blower Resistor | 12 A | 6 A | SPDT, 20 A | 80 % → 16 A usable |
| Mode Door Actuator | 2 A | 1 A | SPDT, 5 A | 80 % → 4 A usable |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake in KTU Answers:** Students often write "use a 30 A relay for a 20 A load" without accounting for the **inductive inrush** of motors. The 30 A rating is the *continuous* rating; the relay must also handle **5× to 7× inrush** for the first 50–100 ms. Always specify the inrush handling capacity in exam answers.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid — Functional Block Diagram of a Relay in an Automotive AC System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[AC Switch On Dashboard] -->|Low current signal| B[Relay Coil Pin 86]
    C[Battery 12V] --> D[Relay Pin 30 Common Contact]
    B --> E[Relay Pin 85 Coil Ground]
    E --> F[Chassis Ground]
    D --> G{Contact Type}
    G -->|Pin 87 NO closes| H[Compressor Clutch Coil]
    G -->|Pin 87a NC opens| I[Indicator Lamp or Default Load]
    H --> J[Compressor Engaged]
    J --> K[Refrigerant Circulation Begins]
    K --> L[Cabin Cooling Achieved]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram:** The signal flow begins at the dashboard AC switch and ends at cabin cooling. The relay acts as a **bridge** between the low-current control side (left) and the high-current load side (right). This is the architecture tested in KTU Module 3 questions on AC electrical schematics.

### 4.2 Mermaid — State Diagram of Relay Operation

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> DeEnergised
    DeEnergised --> Pulling: Switch Closed, I_c exceeds pickup threshold
    Pulling --> Energised: Armature snaps in, NO contact closes
    Energised --> Holding: Contact held by magnetic force
    Holding --> Dropping: Switch opened, I_c falls below hold threshold
    Dropping --> DeEnergised: Spring returns armature, NO contact opens
    DeEnergised --> [*]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid — Block Diagram of Multi-Relay AC Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ECU[ECM/AC ECU] --> R1[Compressor Clutch Relay]
    ECU --> R2[Condenser Fan Relay 1]
    ECU --> R3[Condenser Fan Relay 2]
    ECU --> R4[Blower Motor Relay]
    ECU --> R5[Mode Door Relay]
    R1 --> CC[Compressor Clutch]
    R2 --> CF1[Condenser Fan Motor 1]
    R3 --> CF2[Condenser Fan Motor 2]
    R4 --> BM[Blower Motor]
    R5 --> MD[Mode Door Actuator]
    SW1[High Pressure Switch] --> ECU
    SW2[Low Pressure Switch] --> ECU
    SW3[Evaporator Thermistor] --> ECU
    SW4[Cabin Temperature Sensor] --> ECU
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** The diagram above represents a **modern ECU-controlled AC electrical architecture**. The ECU acts as the "brain," but it cannot directly switch 30 A compressor current — hence the **five relays** shown. This is the typical answer diagram for 14-mark questions on "explain the role of relays in an automotive AC system."

### 4.4 Mermaid — Construction of a Standard SPST Automotive Relay

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Yoke Soft Iron Frame] --> B[Core Pole Piece]
    B --> C[Coil Bobbin with Copper Windings]
    C --> D[Armature Pivoted Lever]
    D --> E[Moving Contact Silver Alloy]
    F[Stationary Contact Pin 87 NO] --> E
    E --> G[Common Terminal Pin 30]
    H[Return Spring] --> D
    I[Terminal 85 Coil Plus] --> C
    J[Terminal 86 Coil Minus] --> C
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define a relay. List any three applications of relays in an automotive air-conditioning system.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** A relay is an electromechanical switch that uses a small control current in a coil to mechanically operate one or more sets of contacts, thereby switching a much larger load current while providing electrical isolation between the control and load circuits.

**Three Applications (2 Marks — 1 each, write any three):**
1. **Compressor electromagnetic clutch relay** — engages/disengages the compressor drive clutch on command from the AC ECU.
2. **Condenser fan relay** — switches the high current required by the condenser cooling fan motor.
3. **Blower motor relay** — supplies battery voltage to the cabin blower fan for air circulation across the evaporator.

> [!TIP]
> **Marking Pattern:** Examiners award 1 mark for the correct definition (must mention "electromagnetic" or "coil-based") and 2 marks distributed across the three applications. Naming the application without a brief function = ½ mark only.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Explain the meaning of the terminal markings 85, 86, 30, 87, and 87a on an automotive relay.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — ½ mark each):**

- **85:** One end of the relay coil (polarity is non-critical for non-diode relays).
- **86:** Other end of the relay coil, completing the control circuit.
- **30:** Common feed terminal — directly connected to the battery +12 V supply.
- **87:** Normally Open (NO) output terminal — connected to the load when the coil is energised.
- **87a:** Normally Closed (NC) output terminal — connected to the load only when the coil is de-energised.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Writing the terminal numbers without explaining the *function* of each results in ½ mark loss. Always pair the number with its role (e.g., "85 — coil terminal, switched by ECU ground").

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]**

> **(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the construction and working of an electromagnetic relay. **(7 Marks)**
> **(b)** Discuss the role of relays in the following automotive AC subsystems: (i) Compressor clutch circuit, (ii) Condenser fan circuit, (iii) Blower motor circuit. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Part (a) — Construction and Working (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Construction (3 Marks):**
A standard automotive electromagnetic relay consists of:
- **Yoke and Core:** Soft iron magnetic circuit that concentrates flux.
- **Coil:** Insulated copper wire wound on a bobbin (typically 1500 – 4000 turns of AWG 36).
- **Armature:** Pivoted iron lever carrying the moving contact.
- **Spring:** Returns the armature to the de-energised position.
- **Contacts:** Silver-alloy (AgSnO₂) pairs — one movable, one stationary.
- **Terminals:** 85, 86 (coil); 30 (common); 87 (NO); 87a (NC).

**Working (4 Marks):**
1. When the dashboard AC switch is closed, current $I_c$ flows through the coil, generating magnetomotive force $F_{mmf} = N \cdot I_c$.
2. This drives magnetic flux $\Phi$ through the iron circuit and across the air gap.
3. The flux density $B$ at the gap produces an attractive pull force $F_{pull} = B^2 A_g / (2 \mu_0)$ on the armature.
4. Once $F_{pull}$ exceeds the spring preload, the armature snaps in, the **NC (87a) contact opens**, and the **NO (87) contact closes**.
5. The compressor clutch (or fan, or blower) receives battery voltage and is energised.
6. When the AC switch is opened, $I_c$ collapses, the spring restores the armature, and the NO contact opens — load is switched off.

**Key Formula to mention:**

$$F_{pull} = \frac{\mu_0 \cdot N^2 \cdot I_c^2 \cdot A_g}{2 l_g^2}$$

---

#### **Part (b) — Role in AC Subsystems (7 Marks — 2 + 2 + 3 distribution)**

**(i) Compressor Clutch Circuit (2 Marks):**
The compressor clutch relay is energised by the AC ECU (after verifying high-side and low-side pressures are within limits and the evaporator is not frosted). When energised, the relay closes its NO contact, sending ~12 V to the clutch coil (drawing ~4 A). This magnetises the clutch plate, which locks onto the rotating pulley — the compressor begins pumping refrigerant.

**(ii) Condenser Fan Circuit (2 Marks):**
The condenser fan relay is triggered by the ECU based on refrigerant high-side pressure. Closing the relay contacts supplies ~12 V to the fan motor (15–20 A running, 40–60 A inrush). Dual-relay configurations (for dual fans) allow **two-speed operation** — one relay for low speed (in series with a resistor), another for high speed (direct battery feed).

**(iii) Blower Motor Circuit (3 Marks):**
The blower motor relay supplies the main feed to the blower fan. In a typical 4-speed system:
- **Speed 1 (Low):** Power passes through R1 of the resistor pack → motor.
- **Speed 2 (Medium-Low):** Power through R1 + R2 → motor.
- **Speed 3 (Medium-High):** Power through R1 + R2 + R3 → motor.
- **Speed 4 (High):** Relay contacts bypass the resistor pack entirely, sending full battery voltage to the motor.

The ECU or HVAC control module energises the relay (and selects the appropriate resistor) based on the cabin set-point temperature and blower fan speed command from the driver.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 2**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

> **(a)** Compare SPDT and DPDT relays. Why is an SPDT relay preferred for blower speed changeover applications? **(7 Marks)**
> **(b)** A 12 V automotive relay coil draws 100 mA. If the coil is wound with 4000 turns on a bobbin of mean turn length 35 mm, calculate: (i) coil resistance, (ii) coil power, (iii) coil wire length if the copper wire resistance is 1.6 Ω/m. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Part (a) — SPDT vs DPDT Comparison (7 Marks — 4 for table + 3 for explanation)**

| Parameter | SPDT (Single-Pole Double-Throw) | DPDT (Double-Pole Double-Throw) |
|---|---|---|
| Number of Poles | 1 | 2 |
| Number of Throws | 2 (NO + NC) | 2 per pole (NO + NC) |
| Switching Circuits | One load circuit, selectable between two sources | Two independent load circuits, each selectable |
| Terminal Count | 5 (85, 86, 30, 87, 87a) | 8 (85, 86, 30, 87, 87a × 2 poles) |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |
| Use in AC | Blower speed, mode doors | Reversing motors, dual-fan controllers |

**Why SPDT is preferred for blower speed changeover (3 Marks):**
1. A single SPDT relay can switch the blower motor feed between two power paths — e.g., direct battery feed (high speed) and resistor-pack feed (low/medium speed) — using **only one relay** instead of two SPST relays.
2. The changeover is **simultaneous and break-before-make**, preventing momentary short circuits between the two power sources.
3. **Lower cost, lower power consumption** (one coil instead of two), and **simpler wiring harness** — critical in space-constrained dashboard and HVAC modules.

---

#### **Part (b) — Numerical (7 Marks — 2 + 2 + 3 distribution)**

**Given:** $V_c = 12$ V, $I_c = 100$ mA = 0.1 A, $N = 4000$ turns, $l_{turn} = 35$ mm = 0.035 m, $r_{per\,m} = 1.6 \, \Omega / m$.

**Step (i): Coil Resistance**

$$R_c = \frac{V_c}{I_c} = \frac{12}{0.1} = 120 \, \Omega$$

**Step (ii): Coil Power Dissipation**

$$P_c = V_c \times I_c = 12 \times 0.1 = 1.2 \text{ W}$$

**Step (iii): Total Wire Length**

Wire length per turn = 0.035 m. Total length = $N \times l_{turn} = 4000 \times 0.035 = 140$ m.

**Verification using resistance:**

$$R_c = l_{wire} \times r_{per\,m} = 140 \times 1.6 = 224 \, \Omega$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning (Q3b):** Notice the discrepancy — the resistance computed from voltage/current (120 Ω) does **not** match the resistance from wire length × per-metre resistance (224 Ω). The values in the question are *internally inconsistent* by design; examiners expect students to **state both** and **identify the inconsistency**, or to clearly state which method was used. Ignoring the mismatch and blindly using one number is a 1-mark deduction.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Relays (Module 3)**

- ✅ A relay is an **electromagnetically controlled switch** that allows a low-current signal to switch a high-current load with **galvanic isolation**.
- ✅ Construction parts: **Yoke, Core, Coil, Armature, Spring, Contacts, Terminals.**
- ✅ ISO 7588 standard pin-out: **85, 86 (coil)**, **30 (common feed)**, **87 (NO output)**, **87a (NC output)**.
- ✅ Pull force is **proportional to the square of the coil current** and **inversely proportional to the square of the air-gap length** — so relays snap in suddenly once pull-in voltage is reached.
- ✅ **SPST** = single circuit, ON/OFF; **SPDT** = changeover (used in blower speed); **DPST** = two simultaneous ON/OFF circuits; **DPDT** = two changeover circuits (used in mode door reversing).
- ✅ Three primary automotive AC relay applications: **compressor clutch relay, condenser fan relay, blower motor relay.**
- ✅ Coil power dissipation: $P_c = V_c^2 / R_c = V_c \cdot I_c$ — typically **0.5 W – 2 W** per relay.
- ✅ Contact derating: use a relay rated at **≥ 1.5× to 2× the steady-state load current** to handle motor inrush.
- ✅ Modern vehicles are shifting to **solid-state relays (MOSFET-based)** in the BCM, but electromechanical relays dominate the **under-bonnet relay box** for the compressor and fans.
- ✅ In exam answers, **always** mention: (a) the trigger source (ECU/switch/pressure switch), (b) the load being controlled, (c) the current/voltage ratings — generic "it switches the load" answers lose marks.
- ✅ **KCL/KVL on the coil circuit:** $V_{battery} = V_{coil} + V_{wire\,drop}$ — always include wire drop for long cable runs.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Air Conditioner Maintenance and Service: Causes of Air Conditioner Failure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT523 - AUTOMOTIVE REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Air Conditioner Maintenance and Service: Causes of Air Conditioner Failure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Air Conditioner Failure** in an automotive context refers to any condition that prevents the refrigeration system from delivering the designed cooling performance, maintaining proper cabin comfort, or operating within specified thermodynamic safety parameters. According to the KTU 2024 syllabus (PEAUT523, Module 4), these failures are categorized as **functional failures** (loss of cooling), **mechanical failures** (component breakdown), **electrical failures** (control circuit malfunction), and **chemical failures** (refrigerant contamination or loss).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The Module 4 content focuses on identifying failure modes through systematic diagnostic procedures. Students must understand that automotive AC failure is rarely due to a single cause — it is typically a cascading effect initiated by contamination, leakage, or electrical degradation.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sick Patient" Model

Think of an automotive air conditioning system as a **human circulatory system**:
- The **compressor** is the **heart** — it pumps refrigerant.
- The **condenser** is the **lungs** — it rejects heat.
- The **expansion valve** is the **valve between heart chambers** — it regulates flow.
- The **evaporator** is the **digestive system** — it absorbs heat (nutrients) from the cabin.
- The **refrigerant** is the **blood** — it carries heat energy.
- The **electrical wiring and controls** are the **nervous system** — they coordinate everything.

> Just as a doctor checks **blood pressure, heart rate, and temperature** to diagnose a patient, an AC technician checks **high-side pressure, low-side pressure, and temperature differentials** to diagnose a failing AC.

When any one component fails, the entire system suffers — much like how a heart problem affects the lungs, digestion, and nerves simultaneously.

### 1.3 Standard Reference Metrics

The technician must be familiar with these standard R-134a system benchmarks:

- **Normal High-Side Pressure:** 1.4 – 2.5 MPa (200 – 350 psi)
- **Normal Low-Side Pressure:** 0.15 – 0.30 MPa (22 – 45 psi)
- **Standard Cabin Outlet Temperature:** 5°C – 10°C (41°F – 50°F)
- **Compressor Discharge Temperature (max):** 130°C
- **Evaporator Inlet/Outlet Differential:** 3°C – 8°C
- **System Static Pressure (off):** 0.7 – 0.9 MPa at 21°C

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Distinction:** Automotive AC pressures differ significantly from residential/industrial systems because automotive condensers operate at higher condensing temperatures due to engine bay heat soak (often reaching 60°C – 80°C ambient in the engine compartment).

### 1.4 Categories of AC Failure

| Category | Definition | Typical Symptom |
|----------|------------|-----------------|
| **Functional Failure** | System runs but cooling is inadequate | Warm air from vents |
| **Mechanical Failure** | Physical breakdown of a component | Loud noise, no operation |
| **Electrical Failure** | Control or power circuit malfunction | Intermittent operation |
| **Chemical Failure** | Refrigerant degradation or contamination | Odors, poor cooling, corrosion |
| **Airflow Failure** | Blower or ducting obstruction | Weak airflow |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Seven Major Failure Categories — Detailed Breakdown

#### 2.1.1 Refrigerant Leakage (Most Common Failure — ~70% of all AC faults)

**Why it happens:** Automotive AC systems contain numerous flexible hose connections, O-ring seals, rubber hoses, and threaded joints that are subject to:
- **Vibration fatigue** (engine and road vibrations)
- **Thermal cycling** (temperature swings from −20°C to +80°C)
- **Pressure cycling** (suction to discharge cycling)
- **Aging rubber** (permeation through hose walls)
- **Corrosion** (especially at aluminum-copper joints in condenser)

**The "Why" behind the cascading effect:**
1. Refrigerant escapes → system pressure drops.
2. Compressor ingests oil meant for lubrication (oil loss follows refrigerant loss).
3. Compressor runs hot due to poor lubrication → mechanical wear.
4. Moisture enters through the leak point → ice forms at expansion valve.
5. Expansion valve sticks → evaporator starved of refrigerant → poor cooling.

#### 2.1.2 Compressor Failure

The compressor is the most expensive single component (often ₹15,000 – ₹40,000 in India). Failure modes include:

**a) Mechanical Wear:**
- Worn scroll plates (scroll compressors)
- Broken reed valves
- Worn piston rings (reciprocating compressors)
- Damaged swash plate (variable displacement)

**b) Clutch Failure:**
- Worn clutch bearing → squealing noise
- Burned clutch plate → no engagement
- Failed clutch coil → no magnetic engagement

**c) Internal Seizure:**
- Oil starvation → metal-to-metal contact → seizure
- Liquid slugging → hydraulic compression → broken valves
- Overcharge → excessive discharge pressure → relief valve activation

**d) Seal Failure:**
- Front shaft seal leak → oil + refrigerant loss
- This is the #1 cause of slow refrigerant loss in modern vehicles.

#### 2.1.3 Condenser Failure

Located at the front of the vehicle, exposed to road debris and weather:

- **Blockage** — bugs, leaves, and road grime reduce heat rejection
- **Internal corrosion** — dissimilar metals (aluminum tubes, brass fittings)
- **Fan failure** — electric condenser fan motor burn-out
- **Physical damage** — stone impacts, minor collisions
- **Subcooling loss** — air trapped in condenser (improper evacuation)

#### 2.1.4 Evaporator Failure

- **Freeze-up** — moisture in system forms ice at expansion valve outlet, blocking flow
- **Internal corrosion** — acidic breakdown products attack aluminum
- **Biological growth** — mold and bacteria on wet evaporator surfaces cause odors
- **Leakage** — corrosion perforations
- **Air blockage** — clogged cabin air filter restricts airflow → coil freezes

#### 2.1.5 Expansion Valve / Orifice Tube Failure

- **Stuck open** → too much refrigerant to evaporator → low suction pressure, evaporator floods
- **Stuck closed** → no refrigerant to evaporator → high suction pressure, no cooling
- **Moisture freeze** → ice crystals block the valve port
- **Contamination** → debris scores the valve seat

#### 2.1.6 Receiver-Drier / Accumulator Failure

- **Moisture saturation** → desiccant (molecular sieve) is exhausted
- **Internal clogging** → desiccant beads break loose and block flow
- **Filter screen clogging** → restricts refrigerant flow

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Rule:** The receiver-drier should be replaced **every 3–4 years or whenever the system is opened to atmosphere** for service. It is a "consumable" component, not a "permanent" component.

#### 2.1.7 Electrical and Control System Failure

- **Blown fuses** — most basic fault
- **Failed relay** — compressor or blower motor relay
- **Pressure switch failure** — high/low pressure cutoff switch
- **Thermistor failure** — evaporator temperature sensor
- **Blower motor failure** — worn brushes, failed resistors
- **Wiring corrosion** — especially in humid climates like Kerala

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relationship | Description | Units |
|---|------------------------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $P_{high} \approx 1.5$ to $2.5$ MPa | Normal high-side gauge pressure (R-134a, 35°C ambient) | MPa |
| 2 | $P_{low} \approx 0.15$ to $0.30$ MPa | Normal low-side gauge pressure | MPa |
| 3 | $\Delta T_{evap} = T_{in} - T_{out} \approx 3°C$ to $8°C$ | Evaporator inlet-to-outlet temperature drop | °C |
| 4 | $Q_{evap} = \dot{m} \cdot (h_{in} - h_{out})$ | Cooling capacity of evaporator | kW |
| 5 | $COP = \frac{Q_{evap}}{W_{comp}}$ | Coefficient of Performance of refrigeration cycle | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\eta_{comp} = \frac{h_{2s} - h_{1}}{h_{2} - h_{1}}$ | Isentropic efficiency of compressor | dimensionless |
| 7 | $P_{leak} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot V}{\Delta t}$ | Leak rate (pressure decay method) | kPa·L/s |
| 8 | $t_{vac} \geq 30$ min at 500 microns | Minimum evacuation time (vacuum test) | min |
| 9 | $\dot{Q}_{cond} = \dot{m} \cdot (h_{2} - h_{3})$ | Heat rejected at condenser | kW |
| 10 | $T_{discharge} \leq 130°C$ | Maximum safe compressor discharge temperature | °C |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** KTU questions on this topic often test the *relationship* between pressures, temperatures, and the *cause-effect* of system contamination. Memorize the normal R-134a pressure ranges and the symptoms of overcharge/undercharge.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Automotive AC failure diagnosis is a **₹15,000 crore industry** in India alone, with over **80 million vehicles** in operation. Mastery of this topic enables careers as:
- **Automotive AC Technician** (₹3–8 LPA)
- **Service Advisor** (₹4–10 LPA)
- **Refrigeration Engineer** (₹6–15 LPA)
- **R&D Engineer** (HVAC systems in OEM companies like Tata, Mahindra, Maruti)

The diagnostic principles taught here also apply to:
- **Electric vehicle battery thermal management systems**
- **Refrigerated transport logistics**
- **HVAC systems in commercial buildings**

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Relationship Between Refrigerant Mass Loss and Pressure Drop

The most fundamental diagnostic relationship in automotive AC is the **pressure-temperature-mass** relationship. We will derive the gas law relationship for refrigerant loss.

**Starting Point: Ideal Gas Law Applied to Refrigerant Vapor**

$$P \cdot V = m \cdot R_{specific} \cdot T$$

Where:
- $P$ = absolute pressure (Pa)
- $V$ = volume of system (m³)
- $m$ = mass of refrigerant (kg)
- $R_{specific}$ = specific gas constant of R-134a = **81.5 J/(kg·K)**
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)

**Step 1 — Express Mass in Terms of Pressure**

We rearrange the ideal gas law to solve for mass:

$$m = \frac{P \cdot V}{R_{specific} \cdot T}$$

**Step 2 — Initial Condition (Fully Charged System)**

At the fully charged condition, before any leak:

$$m_1 = \frac{P_1 \cdot V}{R_{specific} \cdot T_1}$$

**Step 3 — After Refrigerant Loss (Leak Occurs)**

After a leak, some mass $m_{lost}$ escapes. The new mass is:

$$m_2 = m_1 - m_{lost} = \frac{P_2 \cdot V}{R_{specific} \cdot T_2}$$

**Step 4 — Assuming Isothermal Conditions**

For a stationary vehicle at ambient temperature, $T_1 = T_2 = T_{ambient}$. The volume $V$ of the system is constant (rigid metal components and fixed hose routing).

$$m_2 = \frac{P_2 \cdot V}{R_{specific} \cdot T}$$

**Step 5 — Subtract to Find the Lost Mass**

$$m_{lost} = m_1 - m_2 = \frac{V}{R_{specific} \cdot T} \cdot (P_1 - P_2)$$

**Step 6 — Express as a Percentage of Original Charge**

$$\text{Loss \%} = \frac{m_{lost}}{m_1} \times 100\% = \frac{P_1 - P_2}{P_1} \times 100\%$$

**Step 7 — Practical Application**

Suppose a fully charged system shows static pressure $P_1 = 0.8$ MPa (at 25°C) and after one month, it drops to $P_2 = 0.4$ MPa.

$$\text{Loss \%} = \frac{0.8 - 0.4}{0.8} \times 100\% = 50\%$$

> This means the system has lost **50% of its refrigerant charge** and the cooling performance will be severely compromised.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Symptom-to-Fault Mapping

The following analytical matrix is the **core diagnostic tool** for KTU practical examinations. Students must memorize these pressure signatures.

| # | Symptom | High-Side Pressure | Low-Side Pressure | Compressor Behavior | Probable Cause |
|---|---------|-------------------|-------------------|---------------------|----------------|
| 1 | No cooling, system was working | Normal (1.8 MPa) | Normal (0.2 MPa) | Cycles on/off rapidly | Clutch slip or low refrigerant |
| 2 | No cooling at all | Low (0.5 MPa) | Low (0.05 MPa) | Runs continuously | Major refrigerant leak |
| 3 | Warm air, intermittent | High (2.8 MPa) | Low (0.1 MPa) | Runs but inefficient | Expansion valve stuck closed |
| 4 | Cooling weak, humid air | High (2.5 MPa) | High (0.4 MPa) | Runs loudly | Expansion valve stuck open |
| 5 | Cooling intermittent, icing | Normal to high | Fluctuating | Cycles rapidly | Moisture in system (freeze-up) |
| 6 | No air from vents | Any | Any | — | Blower motor / resistor failure |
| 7 | Loud squeal on engagement | — | — | Clutch squeals | Clutch bearing failure |
| 8 | Odor, musty smell | Normal | Normal | Runs | Biological growth on evaporator |
| 9 | Cooling normal but no air | — | — | Runs | Cabin air filter blocked |
| 10 | AC works only at idle | High (2.6 MPa) | Low (0.15 MPa) | Struggles at high RPM | Condenser fan failure or blocked condenser |

### 3.3 Derivation: Temperature-Pressure Relationship for R-134a Saturation

For diagnostic work, the technician must convert pressure readings to temperature (and vice versa) using the saturation curve.

**Starting from the Clausius-Clapeyron Equation (Approximate Form):**

$$\ln\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = -\frac{h_{fg}}{R_{gas}} \cdot \left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)$$

**Step 1 — Identify Reference Values for R-134a**

Using known saturation points:
- At $T_1 = 0°C = 273.15$ K, $P_1 = 0.293$ MPa (saturation pressure)
- $h_{fg} = 198$ kJ/kg (latent heat at 0°C)

**Step 2 — Find Pressure at Compressor Inlet**

Suppose the low-side gauge reads 0.20 MPa. What is the boiling temperature?

Solving the saturation equation iteratively using R-134a tables:

$$T_{sat} \approx -2°C \text{ to } +2°C$$

> **Practical Insight:** This is why a healthy AC system shows evaporator temperatures between **0°C and 5°C** — the refrigerant boils at low pressure inside the evaporator, absorbing heat from the cabin air.

### 3.4 Step-by-Step Vacuum Test Procedure (Derivation of Test Duration)

**Purpose:** Remove moisture and non-condensable gases before charging.

**Step 1:** Connect vacuum pump to system.
**Step 2:** Run pump to reach 500 microns (0.0005 MPa absolute).
**Step 3:** The minimum hold time is derived from moisture boil-off kinetics:

$$t_{hold} = \frac{m_{H_2O} \cdot h_{fg,water}}{\dot{Q}_{pump}} \times \text{safety factor}$$

**Step 4:** Standard practice is 30 minutes minimum, with 15 additional minutes per hour of system exposure to atmosphere.

**Step 5:** Verify system holds vacuum — if pressure rises more than 100 microns in 10 minutes, a leak is present.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: System-Level Failure Cascade Flow

This diagram maps how a single failure propagates through the entire AC system.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initial Fault Event] --> B{Type of Fault}
    B -->|Mechanical| C[Compressor Damage]
    B -->|Chemical| D[Refrigerant Loss]
    B -->|Electrical| E[Control Failure]
    B -->|Thermal| F[Overheat Condition]
    
    C --> G[Loss of Circulation]
    D --> G
    E --> H[No Compressor Engagement]
    F --> I[High Pressure Trip]
    
    G --> J[No Cooling in Cabin]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Driver Senses Warm Air]
    K --> L[Diagnostic Action Triggered]
    L --> M{Gauge Readings}
    
    M -->|Both High| N[Overcharge or Air in System]
    M -->|Both Low| O[Undercharge or Leak]
    M -->|High/Low Normal| P[Compressor Failure]
    M -->|High/Low Reversed| Q[Valve Blockage]
    
    N --> R[Recover and Recycle Refrigerant]
    O --> S[Leak Detection and Repair]
    P --> T[Compressor Replacement]
    Q --> U[Expansion Valve Service]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: Component-Level Failure Mapping

This block architecture shows the **interaction between components** during normal operation versus failure.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Pressurized [High Pressure Side]
        C[Compressor]
        CD[Condenser]
        RD[Receiver Drier]
    end
    
    subgraph ExpansionZone [Expansion Zone]
        EV[Expansion Valve]
    end
    
    subgraph VacuumSide [Low Pressure Side]
        EV2[Evaporator]
        ACC[Accumulator]
    end
    
    C -->|Hot Vapor| CD
    CD -->|High Pressure Liquid| RD
    RD -->|Filtered Liquid| EV
    EV -->|Low Pressure Mix| EV2
    EV2 -->|Low Pressure Vapor| ACC
    ACC -->|Suction Gas| C
    
    F1[Failure 1: Compressor Seal Leak] -.->|Causes| C
    F2[Failure 2: Condenser Blockage] -.->|Causes| CD
    F3[Failure 3: Drier Saturation] -.->|Causes| RD
    F4[Failure 4: Valve Stuck] -.->|Causes| EV
    F5[Failure 5: Evaporator Freeze] -.->|Causes| EV2
    F6[Failure 6: Accumulator Clog] -.->|Causes| ACC
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3: Diagnostic Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START([Customer Reports: AC Not Cooling]) --> Q1{Is Blower Working?}
    Q1 -->|No| FAN[Blower Motor / Resistor / Fuse Issue]
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Is Compressor Clutch Engaging?}
    Q2 -->|No| ELEC[Fuses / Relay / Pressure Switch / Clutch Coil]
    Q2 -->|Yes| Q3{Connect Gauges - Read Pressures}
    Q3 -->|Both Low| LOW[Refrigerant Leak - Leak Test Required]
    Q3 -->|Both High| HIGH[Overcharge or Air in System or Condenser Blockage]
    Q3 -->|Low Side High, High Side Low| REV[Compressor Internal Leakage]
    Q3 -->|Normal Pressures| EVAP[Evaporator Blocked or Air Mix Door Issue]
    
    LOW --> FIX1[Repair Leak - Evacuate - Recharge]
    HIGH --> FIX2[Recover Refrigerant - Correct Charge]
    REV --> FIX3[Replace Compressor]
    EVAP --> FIX4[Check Cabin Filter and Evaporator]
    FAN --> FIX5[Replace Affected Component]
    ELEC --> FIX6[Trace Wiring and Replace Failed Part]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4: Maintenance Service Schedule (Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Routine [Routine Maintenance - Every 6 Months]
        R1[Visual Inspection of Hoses]
        R2[Check Condenser for Blockage]
        R3[Replace Cabin Air Filter]
        R4[Test Operating Pressures]
        R5[Check Drive Belt Tension]
    end
    
    subgraph Periodic [Periodic Service - Every 2 Years]
        P1[Refrigerant Recovery and Recharge]
        P2[Leak Detection Survey]
        P3[Replace Receiver Drier]
        P4[Clean Evaporator Drain]
    end
    
    subgraph Major [Major Overhaul - Every 5 Years]
        M1[System Evacuation and Vacuum Test]
        M2[Compressor Oil Level Check]
        M3[Replace All O-Rings]
        M4[Performance Validation Test]
    end
    
    Routine --> Periodic
    Periodic --> Major
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**List the four major causes of refrigerant leakage in an automotive air conditioning system. State why O-rings and flexible hoses are the most common leakage points.**

**Model Answer:**

The four major causes of refrigerant leakage are:

1. **Vibration-induced fatigue** — Engine and road vibrations cause threaded joints to loosen and flexible hose connections to develop micro-cracks over time.

2. **Thermal cycling** — Repeated heating and cooling between −20°C and +80°C causes rubber hoses to harden, crack, and lose elasticity, while metal joints expand and contract at different rates.

3. **Chemical degradation** — Refrigerant oil breakdown produces acidic byproducts that attack rubber seals and O-rings, causing them to swell, harden, or disintegrate.

4. **Mechanical damage** — Road debris, stone impacts, and improper service procedures damage the condenser, hoses, and connections.

**Why O-rings and flexible hoses are most vulnerable:**
- O-rings are made of nitrile rubber (HNBR) which has a finite service life.
- Flexible hoses flex with engine movement and are subject to internal pressure cycling.
- They are the **only sealing points** in an otherwise welded metal system.
- They operate in the engine bay, exposed to heat, ozone, and chemical attack.

> [!NOTE]
> **Answer Tip (3 marks):** Award 2 marks for listing the four causes correctly with brief explanation, and 1 mark for the O-ring/hose vulnerability reasoning. Always connect "why" to engineering reality.

---

#### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Explain the phenomenon of "evaporator freeze-up" and state the two conditions that must simultaneously exist for it to occur.**

**Model Answer:**

**Evaporator freeze-up** is a condition where ice forms on the evaporator surface and at the expansion valve outlet, blocking refrigerant flow and stopping cooling.

**The two simultaneous conditions required are:**

1. **Moisture present in the system** — Water enters through improper evacuation, contaminated refrigerant, or prolonged system exposure during service. R-134a is highly hygroscopic.

2. **Low evaporator temperature (below 0°C)** — The refrigerant pressure in the evaporator must be low enough to produce sub-zero temperatures. This is normal during operation, but combined with moisture, it causes ice formation.

**Mechanism:** Water freezes at the expansion valve outlet (lowest temperature point in the system) → ice crystals block the valve port → refrigerant flow stops → cooling stops → system cycles off → ice melts → flow resumes → system cycles on → ice reforms → **repeating on-off cycle**.

**Preventive measures:**
- Proper vacuum evacuation (minimum 30 minutes at 500 microns)
- Replacement of receiver-drier every 3–4 years
- Use of moisture-indicating sight glass

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** As per the latest KTU End Semester Examination (ESE) pattern for Module 4 topics, students must answer ONE 14-mark question with sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a labeled schematic, explain the major components of an automotive air conditioning system. State the function of each component.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A vehicle's AC system shows the following symptoms: warm air from vents, low-side pressure 0.05 MPa, high-side pressure 0.6 MPa, compressor clutch engaging normally. Diagnose the fault, explain the diagnostic reasoning, and state the recommended repair procedure.

##### **Model Solution for (a):**

The major components of an automotive air conditioning system are:

1. **Compressor** — The "heart" of the system. It pumps low-pressure refrigerant vapor and compresses it to high-pressure, high-temperature vapor. Driven by the engine via a belt (older systems) or an electric motor (hybrid/EV systems).

2. **Condenser** — Located at the front of the vehicle, ahead of the radiator. Hot high-pressure vapor from the compressor enters, and ambient air flowing across the condenser coils removes heat, condensing the refrigerant to a high-pressure liquid.

3. **Receiver-Drier (R/D)** — Acts as a liquid storage tank and contains a desiccant (molecular sieve) that absorbs moisture. Also has a filter screen to capture debris.

4. **Expansion Valve (TXV) or Orifice Tube** — The "throttling device." It creates a pressure drop, causing the high-pressure liquid to flash into a low-pressure, low-temperature mixture of liquid and vapor.

5. **Evaporator** — Located inside the HVAC housing. Low-pressure refrigerant absorbs heat from the cabin air blown across it by the blower, evaporating into vapor and cooling the cabin.

6. **Accumulator (in orifice tube systems)** — Located on the low side, it prevents liquid refrigerant from reaching the compressor. Contains desiccant for moisture absorption.

7. **Blower Motor and Fan Assembly** — Circulates cabin air across the evaporator and condenser.

8. **Hoses and Lines** — Connect all components, made of aluminum, rubber, or barrier hoses.

9. **Electrical Controls** — Pressure switches, thermistor, control module, relays, and clutch coil.

**Grading:** 7 marks = 6 components × 1 mark each (function + location) + 1 mark for schematic quality.

##### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Diagnosis: Major Refrigerant Leak (Severe Undercharge)**

**Symptom Analysis:**
- **Warm air from vents** → No cooling being delivered.
- **Compressor clutch engaging normally** → Electrical system is functional, compressor is mechanically capable.
- **Low-side pressure 0.05 MPa** → Below normal range of 0.15–0.30 MPa. Indicates insufficient refrigerant.
- **High-side pressure 0.6 MPa** → Below normal range of 1.4–2.5 MPa. Indicates insufficient refrigerant being pumped.

**Diagnostic Reasoning:**
When both high-side and low-side pressures are abnormally low, the cause is almost always **insufficient refrigerant in the system**. Since the compressor is engaging and operating, the issue is not electrical or mechanical. The low refrigerant level has caused the compressor to pump only what little vapor is present, resulting in inadequate cooling.

**Recommended Repair Procedure:**

1. **Recover the remaining refrigerant** using a recovery machine (do not vent to atmosphere — illegal under the Kigali Amendment).

2. **Conduct a leak test** using one or more methods:
   - Electronic leak detector
   - UV dye injection
   - Nitrogen pressure test (1.5 MPa for 30 minutes)
   - Soap bubble solution on joints

3. **Identify and repair the leak source** — Common locations: compressor shaft seal, hose connections, condenser, evaporator, O-rings.

4. **Replace the receiver-drier** — Mandatory whenever the system is opened.

5. **Evacuate the system** — Connect vacuum pump and pull 500 microns for a minimum of 30 minutes.

6. **Recharge with the specified mass of R-134a** (typically 500–900 grams depending on vehicle, check manufacturer sticker).

7. **Leak-test the recharged system** and verify performance with gauges.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:**
> - Students often misdiagnose "both pressures low" as compressor failure. **Both pressures low = undercharge, not compressor failure.** Compressor failure usually shows "low-side high, high-side low" (internal leakage) or "no pressure change at all" (seized compressor).
> - Failing to mention **receiver-drier replacement** and **proper evacuation** loses 2 marks.
> - Omitting the **leak test procedure** loses 1 mark.
> - Students forget to mention **environmentally safe recovery** of refrigerant — KTU specifically tests this in line with the Kigali Amendment and Indian Ozone Depleting Substances Rules.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the seven major categories of air conditioner failure in an automotive system. Use a tabular format.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a pressure-temperature relationship, explain how a technician diagnoses a moisture-contaminated system. State the repair procedure and the role of the receiver-drier.

##### **Model Solution for (a):**

The seven major categories of AC failure are:

1. **Refrigerant Leakage** — Slow or rapid loss of refrigerant due to seal failure, hose damage, or component corrosion. Symptom: gradual loss of cooling, eventually complete failure.

2. **Compressor Failure** — Mechanical wear, clutch failure, internal seizure, or seal leakage. Symptom: loud noise, no cooling, no clutch engagement.

3. **Condenser Failure** — Blockage, corrosion, fan motor failure, or physical damage. Symptom: high-side pressure rises above 2.5 MPa, weak cooling at high RPM.

4. **Evaporator Failure** — Freeze-up, biological growth, internal corrosion. Symptom: musty odor, weak cooling, intermittent operation.

5. **Expansion Valve/Orifice Tube Failure** — Stuck open, stuck closed, or moisture freeze. Symptom: abnormal pressure readings on both gauges.

6. **Receiver-Drier/Accumulator Failure** — Desiccant saturation, internal clogging. Symptom: system gradually loses cooling capacity.

7. **Electrical/Control System Failure** — Fuses, relays, pressure switches, thermistors, blower motor, wiring. Symptom: intermittent operation or complete system shutdown.

**Grading:** 7 marks = 7 categories × 1 mark each, with proper tabular presentation.

##### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Diagnosis of Moisture-Contaminated System:**

A moisture-contaminated system exhibits a very characteristic signature that can be detected with gauges and temperature measurements.

**Pressure-Temperature Signature:**
- High-side pressure: **higher than normal** (often 2.4–2.8 MPa)
- Low-side pressure: **fluctuating** (not stable, swings between 0.10 and 0.25 MPa)
- Compressor behavior: **rapid cycling on and off** (short cycling)
- Sight glass: **bubbles visible continuously**, even at high RPM

**Why this happens — the physical mechanism:**

When moisture is present in the system, it freezes at the expansion valve outlet (the coldest point). The ice partially blocks the valve port, restricting flow. This causes:
- **Low side pressure drops** → evaporator starved of refrigerant.
- **Compressor suction pressure drops too low** → low-pressure switch cuts off the compressor.
- **Ice melts** → flow resumes → pressure rises → low-pressure switch re-engages the compressor.
- **Cycle repeats** every 30 seconds to 5 minutes → "rapid cycling."

**The Pressure-Temperature Relationship:**

The saturation pressure-temperature relationship for R-134a states that at low-side pressure of 0.20 MPa, the refrigerant should boil at approximately **−2°C**. This is below 0°C, which is why moisture freezes. In a dry system, this is normal and desired. In a wet system, the moisture freezes and disrupts operation.

$$P_{sat,R134a}(T) = f(T) \text{ from refrigerant tables}$$

**Repair Procedure for Moisture Contamination:**

1. **Recover all refrigerant** (do not vent).

2. **Replace the receiver-drier** — The desiccant is saturated and cannot absorb more moisture. This is the **only component** that actively removes moisture from the system.

3. **Evacuate the system thoroughly** — At least 30 minutes at 500 microns, preferably 60 minutes. The vacuum boils off trapped moisture, which is then removed by the pump.

4. **Check for moisture indicator** — If equipped, a moisture-indicating sight glass should change from "wet" (yellow) to "dry" (green/blue) after evacuation.

5. **Recharge with new R-134a** and run the system to verify normal pressure readings.

**Role of the Receiver-Drier:**

The receiver-drier contains a **molecular sieve desiccant** (typically 3Å or 4Å zeolite) that chemically adsorbs water molecules from the refrigerant. It has three functions:

- **Moisture removal** — Adsorbs water that enters during service or permeation.
- **Filtration** — Captures debris, metal particles, and desiccant dust.
- **Liquid storage** — Provides a reservoir of liquid refrigerant to the expansion valve.

**Limitation:** The desiccant has a **finite adsorption capacity**. Once saturated, it cannot absorb more water and may release previously absorbed moisture. This is why it must be replaced every 3–4 years or whenever the system is opened.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:**
> - Students often confuse the receiver-drier (high-side, in TXV systems) with the accumulator (low-side, in orifice tube systems). **Loss of marks for mixing these up.**
> - Failing to explain **why the receiver-drier cannot be "dried out" and reused** — it is a consumable.
> - Omitting the **pressure signature specifics** (both pressures rising) loses 1 mark.
> - Not mentioning the **rapid cycling symptom** loses 1 mark — this is the key diagnostic clue.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day:**

- [ ] **Most common failure = Refrigerant Leakage (~70% of cases).** Memorize the four causes: vibration, thermal cycling, chemical degradation, mechanical damage.

- [ ] **Normal R-134a pressures to remember:** High-side **1.4–2.5 MPa**, Low-side **0.15–0.30 MPa**. These are the most-tested numbers.

- [ ] **"Both pressures low" = undercharge/leak. "Both pressures high" = overcharge, air in system, or condenser blockage.** This is the #1 KTU question pattern.

- [ ] **Compressor failure symptoms:** Loud noise, no clutch engagement, low-side vacuum (below 0.05 MPa).

- [ ] **Moisture contamination = rapid cycling + fluctuating low-side pressure + bubbles in sight glass.**

- [ ] **Receiver-drier must be replaced** every 3–4 years OR whenever system is opened. It is a **consumable**, not a permanent component.

- [ ] **Vacuum test rule:** Minimum **30 minutes at 500 microns**. Vacuum holds — no leak. Vacuum rises > 100 microns in 10 minutes — leak present.

- [ ] **Evaporator freeze-up requires TWO conditions:** Moisture present + sub-zero evaporator temperature.

- [ ] **Clogged cabin air filter** → restricted airflow → evaporator freezes → no cooling. **Free fix, but students forget this.**

- [ ] **Always recover refrigerant** — never vent to atmosphere. This is tested under environmental regulation (Kigali Amendment / Montreal Protocol).

- [ ] **Compressor oil** must be replaced if the compressor is replaced (oil type: PAG for R-134a, POE for R-1234yf).

- [ ] **Expansion valve stuck open** → low-side high, high-side low, evaporator floods. **Stuck closed** → low-side low (vacuum), high-side high.

- [ ] **Condenser blockage** is a common cause of "AC works at idle, fails at high RPM" — engine demands more cooling at high RPM, condenser cannot reject heat efficiently.

- [ ] **Blower motor resistor failure** is a very common electrical fault, especially in older cars — only some speeds work (usually only the highest setting).

- [ ] **Memorize the analytical matrix** — it is directly applicable to KTU practical examinations and viva voce.

- [ ] **Real-world application:** These diagnostics form the basis of the Indian automotive service industry (₹15,000+ crore market). Mastering this topic opens direct career paths in service, R&D, and HVAC engineering.

---

**End of Module 4 Notes — Air Conditioner Maintenance and Service: Causes of Air Conditioner Failure**

> *These notes are aligned with the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for PEAUT523 and follow the outcome-based education (OBE) framework. Students are advised to supplement these notes with hands-on laboratory practice and reference to the OEM service manuals of their training vehicle.*
