# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Leak Testing** in an automotive refrigeration and air conditioning system is a systematic diagnostic procedure employed to identify, locate, and quantify the escape of refrigerant (R-134a, R-1234yf, or HFO blends) from any sealed component of the closed-loop vapor compression cycle. It is performed whenever there is a suspected loss of cooling performance, after component replacement, during scheduled service, or as part of mandatory pre-charging verification.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, leak testing is classified as a **Service Bay Diagnostic Procedure (SBDP)** under the broader domain of **HVAC Service & Maintenance**. It bridges the gap between *symptom-based diagnosis* (e.g., weak cooling) and *corrective action* (e.g., repairing a hose fitting).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it matters for KTU exams:** Module 4 of PEAUT523 explicitly tests the student's ability to differentiate between *qualitative* leak detection (finding WHERE the leak is) and *quantitative* leak rate measurement (finding HOW FAST refrigerant is escaping). Examiners frequently pose questions asking for the *correct sequence* of tests and the *sensitivity threshold* of each instrument.

## 1.2 The Refrigerant Equation: A Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **bicycle tube** filled with air.

- You pump it up to a certain pressure — like charging an A/C system with refrigerant.
- A few days later, the tube goes flat. You don't know *where* the puncture is, only that air is escaping.
- You dunk the tube in a bucket of water — **bubbles** form at the leak point. This is exactly the **soap bubble test** for refrigerants.
- If the hole is too small to see bubbles, you listen with a stethoscope (your ear pressed against the tube) — this is the principle behind **ultrasonic leak detection**.
- For very tiny holes, you use a special spray that glows under UV light — the **fluorescent dye method**.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Core Intuition:** Refrigerant molecules in a modern A/C system are under high pressure (typically **1.4 MPa to 2.8 MPa** on the high side) and the molecules are far smaller than air molecules can pass through. A leak path that won't even let water through can still let refrigerant escape — this is why *soapy water* often fails, and *specialized detectors* are mandatory.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

- **Standard automotive system charge:** **0.5 kg to 1.2 kg** of R-134a (varies by vehicle class)
- **High-side operating pressure (R-134a):** **1.4 – 2.8 MPa**
- **Low-side operating pressure (R-134a):** **0.15 – 0.35 MPa**
- **Industry-standard leak rate threshold:** **≤ 14 g/year** (per SAE J2791 for R-134a; **≤ 8 g/year** for R-1234yf systems)
- **Atmospheric reference pressure:** **101.325 kPa = 1 atm**
- **Speed of sound in air at 20°C:** **343 m/s** (relevant for ultrasonic detectors)
- **GWP (Global Warming Potential) of R-134a:** **1430** — meaning **1 kg leaked = 1.43 tonnes of CO₂ equivalent** released into the atmosphere

## 1.4 Why Leak Testing is Non-Negotiable

| Reason | Engineering Impact |
|---|---|
| **Environmental compliance** | Refrigerants are potent greenhouse gases; leakage is regulated under the **Kigali Amendment (2016)** to the Montreal Protocol. |
| **System performance** | A **10% undercharge** reduces cooling capacity by approximately **15–20%** and increases compressor work. |
| **Compressor lubrication** | Refrigerant carries **PAG oil** (typically **PAG 46 or PAG 100**); a leak causes oil starvation and premature compressor failure. |
| **Moisture ingress** | Each leak event allows atmospheric humidity into the system, leading to **acid formation** and **bearing corrosion**. |
| **Legal / warranty** | Automotive OEMs void A/C warranty if leaks exceed SAE J2791 thresholds. |

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Leak Testing Methods

Leak detection methods fall into **three families** based on the physical principle exploited:

### Family A — *Visual / Chemical Methods* (Qualitative)
1. **Visual Inspection** (oil stain, dye residue)
2. **Soap Bubble Test** (saponified solution over pressurized region)
3. **Fluorescent Dye Test** (UV-reactive dye + black light)

### Family B — *Electronic Methods* (Quantitative, high sensitivity)
1. **Heated Diode Leak Detector (HDL)**
2. **Infrared (NDIR) Leak Detector**
3. **Heated Pentode Sensor**

### Family C — *Pressure-Based Methods* (Quantitative, system-level)
1. **Nitrogen Pressure Decay Test**
2. **Vacuum Decay Test**
3. **Standing Pressure Test (with electronic gauge)**

### Family D — *Acoustic Methods*
1. **Ultrasonic Leak Detector**
2. **Acoustic Emission Monitoring**

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of a Standard Leak Test Procedure

The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly lists the **OEM-recommended test sequence**. A student should memorize and be able to justify the order:

- **Step 1 — Recover any residual refrigerant** using an EPA-certified recovery machine. *Why?* Venting refrigerant is illegal and prevents accurate leak measurement.

- **Step 2 — Visual & tactile inspection.** *Why?* Approximately **60% of all automotive A/C leaks** are at accessible fittings, O-rings, and hose crimps. A simple visual check is the cheapest test.

- **Step 3 — Pressure-rise / standing-pressure test** with **dry nitrogen (N₂)** at regulated pressure. *Why N₂?* It is non-flammable, inert, dry, and inexpensive. Never use compressed air (contains moisture → corrosion) or oxygen (fire hazard).

- **Step 4 — Soap solution test** on accessible joints. *Why here?* It is fast but only detects gross leaks (> **50 g/year**).

- **Step 5 — Electronic leak detector scan** along all sealed joints, the compressor shaft seal, condenser tubes, and evaporator. *Why last?* It is the most sensitive (< **4 g/year**) and would be wasted on obvious leaks.

- **Step 6 — Dye injection and UV inspection** if the leak is intermittent or below detector sensitivity. *Why last?* Dye is invasive (stays in the system) and is committed only when other methods fail.

- **Step 7 — Document the leak location, leak rate estimate, and corrective action** in the service log.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** The order is *Visual → Pressure → Soap → Electronic → Dye*. Examiners will frame this as either a direct question or a scenario where a student must justify *why* a particular method is used in a specific position in the sequence.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relationship | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{1} V_{1} = P_{2} V_{2}$ | Boyle's Law (isothermal) — used in vacuum decay test | Pa, m³ |
| 2 | $\dot{m}_{leak} = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot V}{R_{specific} \cdot T \cdot \Delta t}$ | Mass leak rate from pressure decay | kg/s |
| 3 | $Q_{leak} = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot V \cdot M_{ref}}{R_{u} \cdot T \cdot \Delta t}$ | Molar leak rate | mol/s |
| 4 | $L_{annual} = \dot{m}_{leak} \times 365 \times 24 \times 3600$ | Annual refrigerant loss | kg/year or g/year |
| 5 | $CO_{2,eq} = m_{leak} \times GWP$ | Environmental impact | tonnes CO₂-eq |
| 6 | $S_{HDL} = \dfrac{C_{min}}{C_{atm}}$ | Sensor sensitivity ratio | dimensionless |
| 7 | $f_{ultrasonic} = \dfrac{v_{sound}}{d_{orifice}}$ | Dominant acoustic frequency at a leak orifice | Hz |
| 8 | $t_{vac,hold} = \dfrac{P_{rise}}{\Delta P_{allowed}}$ | Vacuum hold time criterion | hours |
| 9 | $P_{N_2,test} = 1.1 \times P_{operating,max}$ | Nitrogen test pressure (10% above max working) | MPa |
| 10 | $\eta_{cooling} = 1 - \left(\dfrac{m_{charge,actual}}{m_{charge,nominal}}\right)^{0.6}$ | Empirical cooling efficiency derating | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Note:** When writing these formulas in an exam, always state the *assumed conditions* (isothermal, ideal gas, dry nitrogen, no phase change) and convert to SI units *before* substituting. Examiners deduct 1 mark per unit error in numerical problems.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **SAE J2791 (R-134a) and SAE J2912 (R-1234yf)** define the maximum permissible leak rate for automotive A/C systems.
- **EPA Section 608 (USA)** and **CPCB regulations (India)** legally require leak testing before any refrigerant charge exceeding **227 g**.
- **Automotive OEM service manuals** (Toyota, Maruti, Hyundai, Tata) all reference the same test sequence taught in KTU Module 4.
- **Electric vehicle (EV) heat pump systems** have *even tighter* leak tolerances because of the higher refrigerant toxicity and GWP of R-1234yf.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Quantitative Leak Rate from Pressure Decay Test

This is the single most important derivation for KTU 2024 Part B (14-mark) questions on leak testing.

### Statement of the Problem
A sealed automotive A/C system, after recovery of refrigerant, is pressurized with **dry nitrogen** to an initial gauge pressure of $P_1$ at temperature $T$. After holding for a time interval $\Delta t$, the pressure falls to $P_2$ at the same temperature. Determine the **mass leak rate** $\dot{m}_{leak}$ and the **equivalent annual refrigerant loss** $L_{annual}$.

### Step 1 — Define the System and Assumptions
- The system internal volume $V$ is constant (rigid metal/rubber hoses and components).
- Nitrogen behaves as an **ideal gas** at the test conditions (low pressure, room temperature).
- The leak is the only mass transfer pathway; temperature is isothermal at $T$ [K].
- Use the **ideal gas equation of state**: $PV = mRT_{specific}$

### Step 2 — Express the Mass Before and After the Test
Initial mass of nitrogen inside the system:

$$m_1 = \dfrac{P_1 \cdot V}{R_{specific} \cdot T}$$

Final mass of nitrogen remaining after time $\Delta t$:

$$m_2 = \dfrac{P_2 \cdot V}{R_{specific} \cdot T}$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Mass That Escaped
The mass lost through the leak is the difference:

$$\Delta m = m_1 - m_2 = \dfrac{V}{R_{specific} \cdot T} \cdot (P_1 - P_2)$$

### Step 4 — Define the Mass Leak Rate
The mass leak rate is the escaped mass per unit time:

$$\dot{m}_{leak} = \dfrac{\Delta m}{\Delta t} = \dfrac{V \cdot (P_1 - P_2)}{R_{specific} \cdot T \cdot \Delta t}$$

### Step 5 — Convert to an Annual Loss
Multiplying by the number of seconds in a year:

$$L_{annual} = \dot{m}_{leak} \times (365.25 \times 24 \times 3600)$$

Substituting the values, $L_{annual}$ becomes:

$$L_{annual} = \dfrac{V \cdot (P_1 - P_2) \cdot 3.156 \times 10^{7}}{R_{specific} \cdot T \cdot \Delta t} \;\; \text{[kg/year]}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic (Step 5):** The factor $3.156 \times 10^7$ is the number of seconds in one Julian year ($365.25 \times 86400$). Multiplying kg/s by this factor converts the leak rate from a per-second basis to a per-year basis, which is the **industry standard unit (g/year or kg/year)** for comparing against SAE J2791 thresholds.

### Step 6 — Worked Numerical Example
**Given:**
- System internal volume: $V = 6.0 \text{ litres} = 6.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3$
- Initial gauge pressure: $P_1 = 1.5 \text{ MPa (gauge)} = 1.5 \times 10^6 + 101325 \text{ Pa} = 1.6013 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa (absolute)}$
- Final pressure after 10 minutes: $P_2 = 1.55 \text{ MPa (gauge)} = 1.6513 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa (absolute)}$ ... *(assume slight gain; for a leak test, $P_2 < P_1$)*
- Let us reframe: $P_1 = 1.65 \text{ MPa abs}$, $P_2 = 1.55 \text{ MPa abs}$, so $\Delta P = 0.10 \text{ MPa} = 1.0 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$
- Temperature: $T = 298 \text{ K}$
- Hold time: $\Delta t = 600 \text{ s}$
- Specific gas constant for nitrogen: $R_{specific} = 296.8 \text{ J/(kg·K)}$

**Substitution:**

$$\dot{m}_{leak} = \dfrac{6.0 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 1.0 \times 10^{5}}{296.8 \cdot 298 \cdot 600}$$

Numerator: $6.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.0 \times 10^{5} = 600$ [Pa·m³] = 600 [J]

Denominator: $296.8 \times 298 \times 600 = 5.307 \times 10^{7}$ [J/kg]

$$\dot{m}_{leak} = \dfrac{600}{5.307 \times 10^{7}} = 1.131 \times 10^{-5} \text{ kg/s}$$

**Convert to annual loss:**

$$L_{annual} = 1.131 \times 10^{-5} \times 3.156 \times 10^{7} = 357.0 \text{ kg/year}$$

This is **massively above** the SAE J2791 threshold of **14 g/year** — the system would fail catastrophically and the leak would be obvious without an instrument.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Vacuum Decay Test Sensitivity

In a vacuum decay test, the system is evacuated to a very low absolute pressure, isolated, and the pressure rise is monitored.

### Step 1 — Use Boyle's Law for the Initial Vacuum
After evacuation, the pressure is $P_{vac}$ (very low, say **100 Pa abs**). The mass of residual gas plus any *in-leakage* over a hold time $t$ is calculated using the ideal gas law in differential form.

### Step 2 — Express Leak Rate from Pressure Rise
If the pressure rises by $\Delta P$ over a hold time $t_{hold}$:

$$\dot{m}_{leak,vac} = \dfrac{V \cdot \Delta P}{R_{specific} \cdot T \cdot t_{hold}}$$

### Step 3 — Sensitivity Threshold
A typical vacuum decay criterion for an automotive A/C system is:

$$P_{rise,max} = 50 \text{ Pa in 15 minutes} = 3.33 \times 10^{-2} \text{ Pa/s}$$

Any rise faster than this is considered a **fail**.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix of Leak Detection Methods

| Method | Sensitivity (g/year) | Test Medium | Best Use Case | Cost | Skill Level |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Visual Inspection** | > 1000 | None | Accessible gross leaks | Free | Low |
| **Soap Bubble** | 50 – 500 | Soapy water | Quick gross leak check at fittings | Very low | Low |
| **Nitrogen Pressure Decay** | 5 – 20 | Dry N₂ | System-level integrity | Low | Medium |
| **Heated Diode Detector** | 4 – 14 | R-134a in circuit | Field service, OEM-approved | Medium | Medium |
| **NDIR (Infrared) Detector** | 1 – 5 | R-1234yf / R-134a | High-precision, modern vehicles | High | Medium |
| **Ultrasonic Detector** | 10 – 30 | Any pressurized gas | Hard-to-reach areas, NVH check | Medium | High |
| **Fluorescent Dye (UV)** | 5 – 20 | Dye + refrigerant | Intermittent or very slow leaks | Medium | Medium |
| **Halide Torch** | 50 – 200 | R-12 (historical) | Obsolete — taught for context | Low | Low |

## 3.4 Derivation 3: Acoustic Frequency from a Leak Orifice

> [!NOTE]
> **Background:** Ultrasonic leak detectors operate on the principle that gas escaping a small orifice generates broadband acoustic noise with a peak frequency that depends on the **orifice diameter** and the **speed of sound** in the gas.

### Step 1 — Relate Orifice Diameter to Acoustic Wavelength
For a circular orifice of diameter $d$, the dominant acoustic wavelength is approximately:

$$\lambda \approx 3.5 \cdot d$$

This is a well-known empirical relationship for orifice-generated turbulence spectra.

### Step 2 — Use the Wave Equation
The frequency is related to wavelength and speed of sound:

$$f = \dfrac{v_{sound}}{\lambda} = \dfrac{v_{sound}}{3.5 \cdot d}$$

### Step 3 — Worked Example
For a typical automotive A/C O-ring leak with an effective orifice $d = 0.1 \text{ mm} = 1.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}$, and $v_{sound} = 343 \text{ m/s}$ in air:

$$f = \dfrac{343}{3.5 \times 1.0 \times 10^{-4}} = \dfrac{343}{3.5 \times 10^{-4}} = 9.8 \times 10^{5} \text{ Hz} \approx 980 \text{ kHz}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic (Step 3):** The frequency falls in the **ultrasonic range (> 20 kHz)**, which is inaudible to humans. This is why ultrasonic detectors convert the signal to a lower, audible frequency for the technician to hear — typically a **headphone output** with heterodyne conversion to the **2 – 10 kHz** range.

This explains why ultrasonic detectors are excellent for finding leaks that are *invisible* and *silent* to humans but generate strong high-frequency turbulence noise.

## 3.5 Safety and Procedural Derivations

> [!WARNING]
> **Safety First:** Nitrogen test pressure must NEVER exceed the **lowest-rated component's Maximum Allowable Working Pressure (MAWP)** in the system. For a typical automotive A/C, MAWP is approximately **3.0 MPa**. The standard rule is:

$$P_{N_2,test} = 1.1 \times P_{operating,max}$$

If the maximum operating pressure is **2.8 MPa**, then the test pressure is:

$$P_{N_2,test} = 1.1 \times 2.8 = 3.08 \text{ MPa}$$

This sits *just below* MAWP with a **3.3% safety margin**. The KTU examiner will test whether the student knows to apply this **10% rule** rather than blindly pressurizing to a fixed value.

## 4.1 Master Leak Testing Workflow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **complete leak testing decision flow** that a KTU 2024 student must understand and reproduce in an exam. Every node uses a safe alphanumeric identifier and clean uppercase text labels.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Service Vehicle Arrives]) --> stepB{Refrigerant\nPresent?}
    stepB -- Yes --> stepC[Recover Refrigerant\nusing EPA Machine]
    stepB -- No --> stepD[Skip to Visual Check]
    stepC --> stepD
    stepD --> stepE{Visible Oil Stain\nor Dye Residue?}
    stepE -- Yes --> stepF[Mark Suspect Location\nand Proceed to Pressure Test]
    stepE -- No --> stepG[Proceed to Pressure Test\nwith Dry Nitrogen]
    stepF --> stepG
    stepG --> stepH{Pressure Holds\nwithin 5 percent}
    stepH -- No --> stepI[Apply Soap Solution\nat Fittings and Hoses]
    stepH -- Yes --> stepJ[System is Tight at Macro Level]
    stepI --> stepK{Bubbles Form?}
    stepK -- Yes --> stepL[Repair Identified Joint]
    stepK -- No --> stepM[Use Electronic Leak Detector]
    stepL --> stepN[Re-test After Repair]
    stepM --> stepO{Detector Triggers?}
    stepO -- Yes --> stepP[Pinpoint Leak with\nNarrow Probe Tip]
    stepO -- No --> stepQ[Inject Fluorescent Dye\nand Run System 15 min]
    stepQ --> stepR[UV Scan for Glow]
    stepR --> stepS{Glow Detected?}
    stepS -- Yes --> stepP
    stepS -- No --> stepT[No Leak Found - System OK]
    stepP --> stepN
    stepN --> stepU([Document and Return Vehicle])
    stepJ --> stepU
    stepT --> stepU
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, PEAUT523]`
**Define leak testing in the context of an automotive air conditioning system. List any four leak detection methods used in automotive service.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Leak testing is the systematic procedure used to detect, locate, and quantify the escape of refrigerant from any sealed component of an automotive vapor compression refrigeration system. It is performed whenever there is a suspected loss of cooling performance, after component replacement, or as part of pre-charge verification to ensure the system holds the design refrigerant charge (typically 0.5 to 1.2 kg of R-134a or R-1234yf).

The four common leak detection methods used in automotive service are:
1. **Visual inspection** for oil stains or dye residue at fittings and joints.
2. **Soap bubble test** using saponified water sprayed over a pressurized system.
3. **Electronic leak detection** using a heated diode or NDIR sensor probe.
4. **Fluorescent dye injection** followed by UV light inspection of the system.

*(Award 1 mark for the definition, 2 marks for the four methods with one-line justifications.)*

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, PEAUT523]`
**State the SAE J2791 maximum permissible leak rate for an R-134a automotive A/C system and explain why a 10 percent safety margin is applied to the nitrogen test pressure.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The SAE J2791 standard specifies a maximum permissible leak rate of **14 grams per year** for an R-134a automotive air conditioning system. For R-1234yf systems, the corresponding limit under SAE J2912 is approximately **8 grams per year** because of the higher GWP of R-1234yf.

A **10 percent safety margin** is applied to the nitrogen test pressure for the following reasons:
1. To prevent the test pressure from exceeding the **Maximum Allowable Working Pressure (MAWP)** of the weakest component, typically rated at **3.0 MPa**, which could rupture hoses, O-rings, or the compressor housing.
2. To ensure that any pre-existing micro-defects are not propagated into catastrophic failures during the test itself.
3. To compensate for **adiabatic temperature rise** when nitrogen is rapidly charged, which can transiently spike local pressures above the regulator setpoint.

*(Award 1 mark for the SAE value, 1 mark for the 10% rule statement, 1 mark for the reasoning.)*

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern Reminder:** Module-level Part B questions are 14 marks with internal choice between two 7+7 sub-part questions. The model answers below assume a structured response with diagrams, justifications, and any required numerical work.

### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, PEAUT523, Module 4]`

**(a)** Explain the complete step-by-step procedure for performing a leak test on an automotive A/C system after the refrigerant has been recovered. Justify the order in which the tests are conducted. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An automotive A/C system has an internal volume of **8.0 litres**. It is pressurized with dry nitrogen to an absolute pressure of **1.80 MPa** at a temperature of **30°C**. After a 15-minute hold, the absolute pressure drops to **1.77 MPa** at the same temperature. Calculate the mass leak rate in kg/s and the equivalent annual refrigerant loss in kg/year. Comment on whether the system passes the SAE J2791 standard. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part A(a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Refrigerant Recovery (1 mark)**
Connect an EPA-certified recovery machine to both service ports. Run the machine until the manifold gauges show a sustained vacuum, indicating complete recovery. Venting refrigerant to atmosphere is illegal under the Kigali Amendment.

**Step 2 — Visual and Tactile Inspection (1 mark)**
Inspect all accessible joints, hose crimps, the compressor shaft seal area, and the condenser for oil residue. Approximately 60 percent of all automotive A/C leaks are visible as oily streaks.

**Step 3 — Nitrogen Pressure Test (1 mark)**
Pressurize the system with **dry nitrogen** at **$P_{test} = 1.1 \times P_{operating,max}$** (typically around 3.0 MPa for R-134a systems). Hold for at least 10 minutes. Never use compressed air (introduces moisture) or oxygen (fire hazard).

**Step 4 — Soap Bubble Test (1 mark)**
Apply soap solution to accessible fittings and joints. Bubbles indicate gross leaks (> 50 g/year). This method is fast and inexpensive for the macro-leak level.

**Step 5 — Electronic Leak Detection (1 mark)**
Use a heated diode or NDIR detector to scan all joints, the compressor shaft seal, and the condenser. Sensitivity is 1 to 14 g/year, suitable for finding leaks below the soap test threshold.

**Step 6 — Fluorescent Dye and UV Inspection (1 mark)**
If the previous methods fail to identify the leak, inject UV dye into the system, run the compressor for 15 minutes, and scan with a UV lamp. The dye glows at the leak point.

**Step 7 — Documentation (1 mark)**
Record the leak location, estimated leak rate, and corrective action in the service log. Re-test the system after any repair to verify.

**Justification of Order:** The order proceeds from **cheapest and least invasive** (visual) to **most sensitive and most invasive** (dye). This minimizes time and cost for the most common leak types before committing to more elaborate procedures.

#### Model Solution to Part A(b) — 7 marks

**Given Data:**
- Internal volume: $V = 8.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3$
- Initial absolute pressure: $P_1 = 1.80 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$
- Final absolute pressure: $P_2 = 1.77 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$
- Pressure drop: $\Delta P = 0.03 \times 10^6 = 3.0 \times 10^4 \text{ Pa}$
- Temperature: $T = 30 + 273 = 303 \text{ K}$
- Hold time: $\Delta t = 15 \times 60 = 900 \text{ s}$
- Specific gas constant of nitrogen: $R_{specific} = 296.8 \text{ J/(kg·K)}$

**Step 1 — Apply the Mass Leak Rate Formula (2 marks)**

$$\dot{m}_{leak} = \dfrac{V \cdot \Delta P}{R_{specific} \cdot T \cdot \Delta t}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the Values (2 marks)**

$$\dot{m}_{leak} = \dfrac{8.0 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 3.0 \times 10^{4}}{296.8 \cdot 303 \cdot 900}$$

Numerator: $8.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 3.0 \times 10^{4} = 240$ [Pa·m³] = 240 [J]

Denominator: $296.8 \times 303 \times 900 = 8.094 \times 10^{7}$ [J/kg]

$$\dot{m}_{leak} = \dfrac{240}{8.094 \times 10^{7}} = 2.965 \times 10^{-6} \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to Annual Loss (2 marks)**

$$L_{annual} = 2.965 \times 10^{-6} \times 3.156 \times 10^{7} = 93.6 \text{ kg/year}$$

Converting to grams: $L_{annual} = 93,600 \text{ g/year}$

**Step 4 — Compare against SAE J2791 (1 mark)**

The acceptable limit is **14 g/year**. The measured leak rate of **93,600 g/year** is approximately **6,685 times higher** than the threshold. The system **FAILS** the SAE J2791 test and requires immediate repair.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part A — 14 marks):**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students forget to convert gauge pressure to **absolute pressure** before applying the ideal gas law. Deduct 1 mark.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Students forget to convert the hold time from minutes to **seconds**. Deduct 1 mark.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Students use the universal gas constant $R_u = 8.314$ J/(mol·K) but forget to divide by the molar mass of nitrogen (28 g/mol) to get $R_{specific}$. Deduct 1 mark.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Students write the answer in kg/s but forget to convert to **g/year** for the SAE comparison. Deduct 0.5 mark.
> - **Common Mistake 5:** Students skip the "Comment on pass/fail" line. Deduct 0.5 mark.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, PEAUT523, Module 4]`

**(a)** With the help of a block diagram, describe the working principle of a **heated diode leak detector**. List its sensitivity range and the refrigerants it can detect. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A technician uses a soap bubble test on a pressurized A/C line and observes a bubble stream at the rate of **one bubble every 3 seconds**. Each bubble has an approximate volume of **20 mm³**. The refrigerant in the line is R-134a at an absolute pressure of **1.0 MPa** and temperature of **25°C**. Estimate the mass leak rate and decide if the leak is acceptable under SAE J2791. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part B(a) — 7 marks

**Working Principle of Heated Diode Leak Detector (5 marks):**

A heated diode leak detector is a portable electronic instrument used to sense refrigerant molecules in the air near a suspected leak point. Its working principle involves the following steps:

1. **Air Sampling (1 mark):** A small built-in pump draws ambient air through a probe tip held close to the suspected leak joint. The air sample may contain trace refrigerant molecules if there is a leak.

2. **Sensor Heating (1 mark):** The air sample passes over a **heated diode sensor** maintained at approximately **400°C**. At this temperature, the sensor's electrical resistance is highly sensitive to the presence of halogen-based refrigerant molecules.

3. **Ionization and Current Change (1 mark):** The refrigerant molecules (containing chlorine or fluorine atoms) decompose on the hot diode surface and alter the diode's current-voltage characteristic. This produces a measurable change in **forward current** proportional to the refrigerant concentration.

4. **Audio-Visual Indication (1 mark):** The instrument converts the current change into an **audible beep** (frequency increases with leak rate) and a **bar-graph or numerical readout** on the display.

5. **Calibration (1 mark):** The instrument is calibrated against a known reference leak source (typically a calibrated capillary leak of **14 g/year**) before each use.

**Block Diagram (1 mark for drawing, included in the 5-mark allocation above):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    probeA[Probe Tip Near Leak] --> pumpA[Built-in Sampling Pump]
    pumpA --> filterA[Particulate Filter]
    filterA --> diodeA[Heated Diode Sensor\n400 degrees C]
    diodeA --> ampA[Amplifier Circuit]
    ampA --> audioA[Audible Beep Speaker]
    ampA --> dispA[Bar Graph Display]
    diodeA --> refA[Reference Diode\nfor Drift Compensation]
```

**Sensitivity Range and Refrigerants (2 marks):**
- Sensitivity: **4 to 14 g/year** (per SAE J2791)
- Detects: **R-12, R-134a, R-1234yf, R-22, HCFC and HFC refrigerants** containing halogen atoms
- Does **not** detect natural refrigerants like **R-744 (CO₂)** or **R-290 (propane)** because they lack halogen atoms

#### Model Solution to Part B(b) — 7 marks

**Given Data:**
- Bubble frequency: $f = 1 \text{ bubble} / 3 \text{ s} = 0.333 \text{ bubbles/s}$
- Bubble volume: $v_{b} = 20 \text{ mm}^3 = 2.0 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^3$ per bubble
- Absolute pressure inside line: $P = 1.0 \text{ MPa} = 1.0 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$
- Temperature: $T = 25 + 273 = 298 \text{ K}$
- Refrigerant: R-134a, molar mass $M = 102.03 \text{ g/mol}$
- Universal gas constant: $R_u = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$

**Step 1 — Compute the Volumetric Leak Rate (2 marks)**

The total volume of refrigerant escaping per second is the bubble volume times the bubble frequency:

$$Q_{leak} = v_{b} \times f = 2.0 \times 10^{-8} \times 0.333 = 6.667 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Ideal Gas Law to Convert to Molar Flow (2 marks)**

Using $PV = nRT$, the molar flow rate is:

$$\dot{n}_{leak} = \dfrac{P \cdot Q_{leak}}{R_u \cdot T}$$

$$\dot{n}_{leak} = \dfrac{1.0 \times 10^{6} \cdot 6.667 \times 10^{-9}}{8.314 \cdot 298}$$

Numerator: $6.667 \times 10^{-3}$ [Pa·m³/s] = $6.667 \times 10^{-3}$ [J/s]

Denominator: $8.314 \times 298 = 2477.6$ [J/mol]

$$\dot{n}_{leak} = \dfrac{6.667 \times 10^{-3}}{2477.6} = 2.691 \times 10^{-6} \text{ mol/s}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to Mass Flow Rate (2 marks)**

$$\dot{m}_{leak} = \dot{n}_{leak} \times M = 2.691 \times 10^{-6} \times 102.03 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/mol}$$

$$\dot{m}_{leak} = 2.745 \times 10^{-7} \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to Annual Loss (0.5 mark)**

$$L_{annual} = 2.745 \times 10^{-7} \times 3.156 \times 10^{7} = 8.66 \text{ kg/year} = 8,660 \text{ g/year}$$

**Step 5 — Compare to SAE J2791 (0.5 mark)**

The acceptable limit is **14 g/year**. The measured leak is **8,660 g/year**, which is **~618 times** the threshold. The system **FAILS** catastrophically — a single visible bubble stream in 3 seconds is already a major gross leak.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part B — 14 marks):**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students often forget that bubble volume is a *real volumetric displacement* and treat the bubble frequency as a dimensionless count. The bubble volume must be multiplied by frequency to get a volumetric flow rate. Deduct 1.5 marks.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Confusion between the use of $R_{specific}$ (for mass) and $R_u$ (for moles). The problem gives a molar mass, indicating the **molar route** is intended. Deduct 1 mark.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Unit conversion errors between mm³, cm³, and m³. The factor is $10^{-9}$ for mm³ to m³. Deduct 1 mark.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Forgetting to convert the molar mass of R-134a from g/mol to **kg/mol** when used with SI base units. Deduct 0.5 mark.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Print and Memorize Before Exam**

- **SAE J2791 limit for R-134a:** **14 g/year**. SAE J2912 limit for R-1234yf: **8 g/year**.
- **Test pressure rule:** $P_{N_2,test} = 1.1 \times P_{operating,max}$, never exceeding the **lowest-rated component MAWP**.
- **Test sequence (memorize in order):** Visual → Pressure → Soap → Electronic → Dye.
- **Test medium:** Always use **dry nitrogen**. **Never** use oxygen, compressed air, or refrigerant for the pressure decay test.
- **Recovery first:** Always recover refrigerant with an EPA machine before any leak test — venting is illegal.
- **Sensitivity ranking (best to worst):** NDIR Infrared → Heated Diode → Dye/Pressure Decay → Ultrasonic → Soap → Visual.
- **Heated diode operating temperature:** approximately **400°C**, sensitive to **halogen-bearing refrigerants only**.
- **Ultrasonic detector operating principle:** Orifice turbulence produces a peak acoustic frequency $f = \dfrac{v_{sound}}{3.5 \cdot d}$, typically in the **40 kHz to 100 kHz** range, heterodyned down to **2 – 10 kHz** for human hearing.
- **Leak rate formulas (master these):**
  - Mass leak rate: $\dot{m}_{leak} = \dfrac{V \cdot \Delta P}{R_{specific} \cdot T \cdot \Delta t}$
  - Annual loss: $L_{annual} = \dot{m}_{leak} \times 3.156 \times 10^{7}$ [s/year]
  - Molar leak rate: $\dot{n}_{leak} = \dfrac{P \cdot Q_{leak}}{R_u \cdot T}$
  - CO₂ equivalent: $CO_{2,eq} = m_{leak} \times GWP$ (R-134a GWP = 1430, R-1234yf GWP = 4)
- **Vacuum decay criterion:** Pressure rise must not exceed **50 Pa in 15 minutes** for a typical automotive system.
- **Compressor shaft seal accounts for ~25%** of all automotive A/C leaks — always inspect this area.
- **PAG oil viscosity grades:** PAG 46 (R-134a) and PAG 100 (R-1234yf) — colour-coded for identification.
- **Always re-test** the system after any repair to verify the leak has been eliminated and the system holds the design charge.
- **Documentation is mandatory:** Leak location, leak rate estimate, repair method, and post-repair test results must be recorded in the service log for warranty and regulatory compliance.
- **Moisture ingress consequence:** A single leak event can introduce enough atmospheric moisture to saturate the desiccant in the receiver-drier, requiring its replacement.
- **Halide torch is obsolete** — taught for historical context only. R-134a does not change the flame colour of a halide torch, rendering it ineffective for modern refrigerants.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Discharging the System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT523 - AUTOMOTIVE REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Discharging the System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Discharging the System** is a controlled, regulated service operation in automotive air conditioning in which the entire refrigerant charge contained within the high-pressure (liquid) and low-pressure (vapor) sides of the refrigeration circuit is deliberately extracted, captured, and transferred to an external certified storage cylinder using a **Recovery, Recycling, and Recharging (RRR) station**. This procedure is mandated by environmental legislation (EPA Section 609 under the Clean Air Act in the USA and equivalent Indian CPCB/MoEFCC norms following the **Montreal Protocol**, 1987) before any automotive A/C component is opened to atmosphere for repair, replacement, or decommissioning.

In modern R-134a and R-1234yf systems, discharging is **never** performed by venting the refrigerant into the open air. The term is retained in KTU 2024 Scheme syllabi purely for engineering pedagogy and refers to the *closed-loop recovery process*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Module 4**
> The phrase "Discharging the System" must be understood as **"Refrigerant Recovery"**, not uncontrolled venting. The examiner expects candidates to articulate environmental, legal, and thermodynamic reasons for the procedure.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine your car's A/C system as a **pressurised water bottle** containing a special "ice-making liquid" (refrigerant). If you want to repair the bottle, you cannot just "pop the cap and let the liquid spill onto the floor" — it is toxic, valuable, and environmentally damaging. Instead, you use a **syringe-like pump** (the recovery machine) that gently sucks the liquid out into a clean, sealed container, where it can later be cleaned and reused.

That suction-and-store operation is exactly what "discharging the system" means in an automotive A/C workshop.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terms to Memorize**
> - **Recovery** → Extracting refrigerant and storing it (may be contaminated).
> - **Recycling** → Cleaning the recovered refrigerant for reuse (oil removal, filtration, dehydration).
> - **Reclaiming** → Reprocessing to *virgin* ARI 700 purity standards (lab-tested).
> - **Discharging** → Generic term covering the entire above flow when the system is being emptied for service.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bold-Flagged)

- **Refrigerant saturation pressure of R-134a at 25 °C = 0.665 MPa (≈ 6.65 bar)**.
- **Refrigerant saturation pressure of R-134a at 0 °C ≈ 0.292 MPa (≈ 2.92 bar)**.
- **Standard discharge rate of an automotive RRR machine = 0.25 kg/min to 0.50 kg/min** for R-134a.
- **Maximum allowable refrigerant release into atmosphere = 0 kg** (zero-tolerance, as per EPA 40 CFR Part 82).
- **Minimum atmospheric dew point for recovered refrigerant reuse = −40 °C (≈ 60 ppm moisture by weight)**.
- **High-side service port SAE thread = M16 × 1.5 (older R-12) or M14 × 1.5 (R-134a)**.
- **Low-side service port SAE thread = M13 × 1.5 (R-134a)**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Must a System Be Discharged?

The engineering reasons for discharging a refrigeration system prior to service are fourfold:

1. **Environmental Protection** — CFCs (R-12) and HCFCs (R-22) have Ozone Depletion Potential (ODP); HFCs (R-134a) and HFOs (R-1234yf) have high Global Warming Potential (GWP). R-134a GWP = **1430**; R-1234yf GWP = **4**.
2. **Safety** — Refrigerant at high-side can be at **1.4–2.5 MPa gauge** and > 60 °C; uncontrolled release causes frostbite and pressure hazards.
3. **Component Protection** — Moisture ingress during repair would form ice at the TXV/orifice tube, destroying the compressor.
4. **Economic Recovery** — 1 kg of virgin R-134a costs roughly ₹1,500–2,500; recovery saves cost.

## 2.2 Thermodynamic Phases of Refrigerant Inside a Parked (Off) System

When the engine and compressor are switched off and the system has thermally stabilised to ambient, the refrigerant **does not simply sit as a liquid pool**. Because the condenser and evaporator are interconnected, the refrigerant **migrates and equalises** until the entire circuit reaches a single saturation pressure corresponding to ambient temperature.

This equalised pressure (gauge) is approximately:

$$P_{eq} = P_{sat}(T_{amb}) - P_{atm}$$

where $P_{sat}$ is read from the refrigerant's saturated pressure–temperature table.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** A common error is assuming the high-side and low-side pressures remain at their *running* values when the engine is off. They will *equalise* through the refrigerant migration — the discharge procedure must therefore be performed with this equalised, static pressure in mind.

## 2.3 Two Methodologies of Discharge

| Method | Description | Equipment Used | When Applied |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Liquid Refrigerant Recovery** | Liquid is pushed out of the high-side first, while the system is still in liquid/vapour equilibrium. Faster. | RRR machine + liquid-line adaptor | Large charged systems, fast workshops |
| **Vapour Refrigerant Recovery** | The system is first run in A/C mode, then recovered as vapour from the low-side. Slower but safer for oil carry-over. | RRR machine only | Standard automotive service |
| **Push-Pull (Hybrid) Method** | Liquid is pushed out by an external pressure source; vapour recovery completes the job. | Recovery cylinder + RRR machine | Deep recovery, large industrial chillers |

In **automotive A/C service**, the **vapour recovery method** is the standard because the refrigerant charge is small (typically **0.5–1.2 kg for a passenger car**) and the procedure must prevent lubricant (PAG oil) carry-over into the recovery cylinder.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Saturation pressure | $P_{sat} = f(T_{sat})$ — read from refrigerant table | kPa, bar, psi | Determines static system pressure before discharge |
| 2 | Mass of refrigerant recovered | $m_{rec} = V_{cyl}(ρ_{liq,cyl} - ρ_{vap,cyl})$ | kg | Theoretical check on recovery completeness |
| 3 | Recovery time estimate | $t_{rec} \approx \dfrac{m_{sys}}{\dot{m}_{RRR}}$ | minutes | $ \dot{m}_{RRR} = 0.25–0.50$ kg/min |
| 4 | Pressure ratio at compressor | $r_p = \dfrac{P_{cond,abs}}{P_{evap,abs}}$ | dimensionless | Must be < 8 for R-134a in normal running |
| 5 | Moisture in recovered refrigerant | $W = \dfrac{p_{vap}}{P_{sys}} \times 10^6$ | ppm | Should be < 60 ppm for reuse |
| 6 | Oil carry-over rate | $η_{oil} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{oil}}{\dot{m}_{ref}}$ | % | Should be < 0.5 % during vapour recovery |
| 7 | GWP equivalent discharge | $G_{eq} = m_{ref} \times GWP$ | kg CO₂-eq | Used for compliance reporting |
| 8 | Low-side service pressure threshold (discharge complete) | $P_{LS,abs} \leq 0$ kPa gauge | kPa | Modern RRR machines auto-stop here |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL EXAM RULE:** Inside markdown tables, never use the bare `|` symbol for absolute value — always use $\vert$ or $\mid$. Example: $|P_{LS}|$ → $\vert P_{LS} \vert$.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Workshops** — Every A/C service begins and ends with discharge and recharge cycles; understanding them is essential for service-bay profitability.
- **Insurance Assessment** — Adjuster must know if refrigerant was legally recovered before the claim is settled.
- **Emission Audits** — Transport corporations (KSRTC, BEST) need quantified refrigerant accounting for carbon credit compliance.
- **Design Validation** — OEM refrigerant mass declarations on the vehicle's under-hood label are verified through mass-balance discharge.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Procedure, Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Standard 12-Step Automotive A/C Discharge Procedure (Vapour Recovery Method)

The following exhaustive procedure is the **KTU 2024 Scheme expected model answer** for any practical / descriptive question on this topic.

### **Pre-Discharge Phase (Steps 1–4)**

**Step 1 — Identify the Refrigerant Type.**
Visually inspect the under-hood refrigerant specification label and the service port thread sizes.

$$M16\times1.5 \Rightarrow R\text{-}12 \quad ; \quad M14\times1.5 \Rightarrow R\text{-}134a \quad ; \quad M12\times1.5 \Rightarrow R\text{-}1234yf$$

This identification is non-negotiable because mixing refrigerants in the recovery cylinder causes fractionation and pressure spikes.

**Step 2 — Verify RRR Machine Certification.**
The recovery station must conform to **SAE J2788** (for R-134a) or **SAE J2843** (for R-1234yf). Confirm that the recovery cylinder is within its hydrostatic test date (typically 5-year validity).

**Step 3 — Connect the Recovery Station.**
Connect the **red (high-side) hose** of the manifold to the high-side service port, and the **blue (low-side) hose** to the low-side service port. The **yellow (centre) hose** goes to the RRR machine inlet.

> [!NOTE]
> Service port caps must be removed *only* with the engine OFF. Cracking a port under pressure releases refrigerant.

**Step 4 — Open the Manifold Valves and Start the Recovery Machine.**
With the engine OFF, slowly open both manifold hand valves. The RRR machine's internal compressor pulls refrigerant vapour from the low side, liquid from the high side, and condenses it into the recovery cylinder.

### **Active Discharge Phase (Steps 5–8)**

**Step 5 — Monitor the High-Side and Low-Side Gauges.**
The recovery machine will display (or the manifold will indicate) a falling low-side gauge reading. As the refrigerant depletes:

$$P_{LS}(t) = P_{eq} \cdot e^{-t/\tau}$$

where $\tau$ is the recovery time constant, depending on RRR machine flow rate $\dot{m}_{RRR}$ and system volume $V_{sys}$.

**Step 6 — Allow Pressure Equalisation.**
When the low-side reading approaches **−100 kPa gauge (vacuum)**, the RRR machine will auto-shut off (per SAE J2788 protocol). The system is now considered "discharged" to **≤ 0 kPa gauge**.

**Step 7 — Wait for the "Soak Period".**
Close the manifold valves, shut off the RRR machine, and wait 5 minutes. If pressure re-rises, refrigerant was trapped in an oil-logged section (typically the evaporator or accumulator). Restart the machine to pull the residual charge.

**Step 8 — Record the Recovered Mass.**
The recovery cylinder's internal scale (or sight glass) indicates the recovered refrigerant mass:

$$m_{rec} = m_{final,cyl} - m_{initial,cyl}$$

This must be within **± 30 g of the vehicle's under-hood label mass** for accountability.

### **Post-Discharge Phase (Steps 9–12)**

**Step 9 — Isolate the System.**
Close the manifold valves, disconnect the hoses, and immediately re-cap the service ports to prevent moisture ingress.

**Step 10 — Verify Vacuum (Optional Evacuation Test).**
Attach a vacuum pump to both ports and pull **−100 kPa gauge** for **30 minutes**. A rise > 0.3 kPa in 30 min indicates a leak — repair before recharge.

**Step 11 — Tag the Cylinder.**
Label the recovery cylinder with **refrigerant type, date, vehicle registration, technician ID, and contamination status** (clean vs. contaminated).

**Step 12 — Dispose/Recycle as per Regulation.**
Return to a certified reclamation centre. R-12 must be destroyed; R-134a may be recycled; R-1234yf must be reclaimed due to low GWP economics.

## 3.2 Analytical Derivation: Mass of Refrigerant Remaining After Recovery

Consider a vehicle A/C system of total internal volume $V_{sys}$ at the moment the low-side gauge reaches $P_{LS,final}$ absolute.

**Governing relation:**

The mass remaining $m_{rem}$ equals the mass of refrigerant vapour occupying the system volume at the final low pressure and the final temperature $T_{final}$:

$$m_{rem} = \rho_{vap}(P_{LS,abs},\, T_{final}) \times V_{sys}$$

For an ideal-gas approximation (valid at low pressures):

$$\rho_{vap} = \dfrac{P \cdot M_{ref}}{R_u \cdot T}$$

where $M_{ref}$ is the refrigerant's molar mass and $R_u = 8.314$ J/(mol·K) is the universal gas constant.

**Substituting:**

$$m_{rem} = \dfrac{P_{LS,abs} \cdot M_{ref} \cdot V_{sys}}{R_u \cdot T_{final}}$$

**Plug in KTU Sample Numbers** — Take a small car with $V_{sys} = 0.015$ m³, $P_{LS,abs} = 0.5$ kPa (i.e., 0.0005 MPa, after deep recovery), $T_{final} = 303$ K, and $M_{R-134a} = 102.03$ g/mol.

$$m_{rem} = \dfrac{(500\,\text{Pa})(0.10203\,\text{kg/mol})(0.015\,\text{m}^3)}{(8.314\,\text{J/mol·K})(303\,\text{K})}$$

**Numerator:**

$$(500)(0.10203)(0.015) = 0.7652\,\text{Pa·m}^3\,\text{kg/mol}$$

**Denominator:**

$$(8.314)(303) = 2519.1\,\text{J/mol}$$

**Division:**

$$m_{rem} = \dfrac{0.7652}{2519.1} = 3.04 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{kg} = 0.304\,\text{g}$$

This confirms that a properly executed deep recovery leaves **less than 1 gram** of refrigerant — well within the EPA 40 CFR §82.150 threshold of **15 grams** maximum residual for a passenger car system.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Discharge Decision Tree

| Observed Symptom | Probable Cause | Discharge Required? | Subsequent Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| Compressor clutch not engaging | Electrical fault / low charge | NO (diagnose first) | Check refrigerant *level only* with UV dye |
| Visible oil streaks at hose joint | Hose leak | YES (full recovery) | Replace hose, evacuate, recharge |
| System contaminated with PAG oil + debris | Compressor failure | YES (full recovery) | Flush with approved flush solvent |
| Receiver-drier saturated | Moisture ingress | YES (full recovery) | Replace drier, deep evacuate < 100 Pa |
| Odour of vinegar | Formic acid formation | YES (full recovery + reclaim) | Replace all rubber components |
| No fault, scheduled 2-year service | — | YES (full recovery) | Recharge with fresh lubricant |

## 3.4 Derivative Insight: Why the Equalised Pressure Determines Recovery Difficulty

When the engine is off, the equalised pressure is determined entirely by ambient. If ambient is **40 °C** (typical Kerala summer):

$$P_{eq,R134a}(40°C) \approx 1.017\,\text{MPa gauge}$$

This is a substantial pressure pushing refrigerant *out* of the system through any leak path. Conversely, if ambient is **20 °C**:

$$P_{eq,R134a}(20°C) \approx 0.572\,\text{MPa gauge}$$

The RRR machine must overcome this back-pressure, and the recovery time roughly follows:

$$t_{rec} \approx \dfrac{m_{sys}}{\dot{m}_{RRR}} \left( 1 + \dfrac{P_{eq}}{P_{atm}} \right)$$

For a system with $m_{sys} = 0.8$ kg, $\dot{m}_{RRR} = 0.35$ kg/min, $P_{eq} = 1.017$ MPa, $P_{atm} = 0.101$ MPa:

$$t_{rec} \approx \dfrac{0.8}{0.35}\left( 1 + \dfrac{1.017}{0.101} \right) = 2.286 \times 11.07 \approx 25.3\,\text{minutes}$$

This explains why **summer service in Kerala (high ambient) prolongs discharge times by ~ 10×** compared with cool-weather service.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Refrigerant Recovery, Recycling & Recharging (RRR) Machine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Vehicle A and C System] -->|High Side Hose Red| B[Manifold Gauge Set]
    A2[Vehicle A and C System 2] -->|Low Side Hose Blue| B

    D --> E[Condenser Coil in RRR]
    E --> F[Receiver Cylinder with Sight Glass]
    F --> G[Recycling Filter Acid and Moisture]
    G --> H[Vacuum Pump Stage for Deep Recovery]
    H --> I[Recharging Flow Meter]
    I --> J[Vehicle A and C System Recharge]
    F --> K[Storage Cylinder Tagged]
    K --> L[Reclaim Centre Reprocessor]

    class C process

```

## 4.2 Flowchart — Standard Discharge Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Vehicle Arrives for A and C Service] --> B{Refrigerant Type Identified}
    B -->|R-12| C[Use Legacy Recovery Machine]
    B -->|R-134a| D[Use SAE J2788 RRR Station]
    B -->|R-1234yf| E[Use SAE J2843 RRR Station with New Oil PO]
    D --> F[Connect Red Blue Yellow Hoses to Service Ports]
    F --> G[Open Manifold Valves Slowly]
    G --> H[Start RRR Machine in Recovery Mode]
    H --> I{Monitor Low Side Gauge}
    I -->|Falling| J[Continue Recovery]
    I -->|Stuck Above Zero| K[Check for Blocked Hose]
    J --> L{Pressure Below minus 100 kPa gauge}
    L -->|No| I
    L -->|Yes| M[Auto Shutoff by RRR]
    M --> N[Wait 5 min Soak Period]
    N --> O{Pressure Rises Above minus 50 kPa gauge}
    O -->|Yes| H
    O -->|No| P[Record Recovered Mass]
    P --> Q[Close Valves Disconnect Cap Ports]
    Q --> R[Tag Cylinder and File Service Record]
    R --> S[End: System Discharged]

    classDef decision fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9,stroke-width:2px
    classDef process fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#06c,stroke-width:2px
    classDef terminal fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#0a0,stroke-width:2px

    class J process

```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Manifold Gauge Set Connection to A/C System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define the term "discharging the system" in the context of automotive air conditioning. Why is the procedure mandatory under modern environmental regulations?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

*Discharging the system* refers to the **controlled, closed-loop extraction of the refrigerant charge** from an automotive A/C circuit into a certified external storage cylinder using a Recovery, Recycling, and Recharging (RRR) machine. (1 mark)

The procedure is mandatory because:
- Release of refrigerant vapour into the atmosphere is prohibited by the **Montreal Protocol (1987)** and the **EPA Section 609 of the Clean Air Act (USA)**, and the **Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB)** in India. (1 mark)
- Refrigerants such as R-12, R-134a, and R-1234yf have **Ozone Depletion Potential (ODP)** and **Global Warming Potential (GWP)** values that demand capture. R-134a GWP = **1430**, meaning 1 kg vented equals 1.43 tonnes of CO₂. (1 mark)

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**List any three safety precautions that must be observed while discharging an automotive A/C system.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. **Wear Personal Protective Equipment (PPE)** — safety goggles, refrigerant-rated gloves, and a face shield; liquid refrigerant contacting skin causes frostbite. (1 mark)
2. **Verify the engine and ignition are OFF** before connecting manifold hoses, and **never** crack a service port under residual pressure. (1 mark)
3. **Use only refrigerant-matched RRR equipment** (SAE J2788 for R-134a, SAE J2843 for R-1234yf) to avoid cross-contamination and pressure-vessel accidents. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Part B Module-4 question carries 14 marks. Two alternatives (A or B) are offered; the student answers **one**. Each alternative is split into sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the complete **step-by-step procedure** for discharging an automotive A/C system using the **vapour recovery method**, with the aid of a labelled block diagram.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A passenger car A/C system contains **0.85 kg of R-134a**. The vehicle has just been switched off in a workshop where ambient temperature is **38 °C**. Determine:
- (i) The equalised static system pressure immediately after switch-off.
- (ii) The minimum theoretical mass of refrigerant that will remain inside the system after a deep recovery that pulls the low side to **0.5 kPa absolute**, given the total internal volume of the system is **0.018 m³** and the system temperature has dropped to **25 °C** during recovery.
- (iii) Comment on whether this residual meets the EPA threshold of 15 g for automotive systems.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Step-by-step vapour recovery procedure (7 marks):**

1. **Identify refrigerant** by reading the under-hood label and verifying service port thread size. R-134a uses M14 × 1.5 high-side and M13 × 1.5 low-side. (1 mark)
2. **Inspect RRR machine** for SAE J2788 certification, recovery cylinder test date validity, and sufficient empty volume. (1 mark)
3. **Connect the manifold gauge set:** red hose to high-side service port, blue hose to low-side service port, yellow centre hose to the RRR machine inlet. (1 mark)
4. **Slowly open manifold valves** with the engine OFF, then switch on the RRR machine in *Recovery* mode. (1 mark)
5. **Monitor low-side gauge**; vapour is drawn from the low side while liquid migrates from the high side via the connecting lines. (1 mark)
6. **Wait for auto-shutoff** when low-side reaches **−100 kPa gauge** (or per SAE J2788 specification). (1 mark)
7. **Perform soak test** for 5 minutes; if pressure re-rises, restart recovery. Record recovered mass and **cap the service ports** to prevent moisture ingress. (1 mark)

*(Labelled block diagram: refer to Section 4.1 of these notes for the recovery machine architecture.)*

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Equalised static pressure at 38 °C (2 marks):**

From the R-134a saturated pressure table (or using Antoine equation approximation):

$$P_{sat,R134a}(38°C) \approx 0.920\,\text{MPa absolute}$$

Therefore, gauge pressure:

$$P_{eq,gauge} = 0.920 - 0.101 = 0.819\,\text{MPa gauge} \approx 8.19\,\text{bar}$$

**(ii) Residual mass calculation (4 marks):**

Using the ideal-gas relation derived in Section 3.2:

$$m_{rem} = \dfrac{P_{LS,abs} \cdot M_{ref} \cdot V_{sys}}{R_u \cdot T_{final}}$$

Substituting the given values:
- $P_{LS,abs} = 0.5$ kPa $= 500$ Pa
- $M_{ref}$ (R-134a) $= 102.03 \times 10^{-3}$ kg/mol $= 0.10203$ kg/mol
- $V_{sys} = 0.018$ m³
- $R_u = 8.314$ J/(mol·K)
- $T_{final} = 25 + 273 = 298$ K

**Numerator:**

$$(500)(0.10203)(0.018) = 0.9183$$

**Denominator:**

$$(8.314)(298) = 2477.6$$

**Division:**

$$m_{rem} = \dfrac{0.9183}{2477.6} = 3.71 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{kg} = 0.371\,\text{g} \approx 0.37\,\text{g}$$

**(iii) EPA Compliance Comment (1 mark):**

The computed residual mass of **0.37 g** is far below the EPA Section 82.150 maximum permissible release of **15 g** per vehicle. The discharge procedure **meets environmental compliance** with a very large safety margin, and the system is legally safe to open for service.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [Internal Choice Alternative]**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare the **vapour recovery method** and the **liquid recovery method** of discharging an automotive A/C system. Discuss their relative advantages, limitations, and typical applications.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat flowchart, explain the **discharge decision logic** that a service technician should follow when a vehicle arrives with an A/C complaint. Highlight the points at which the technician must decide *whether* to discharge, and *which* type of recovery to employ.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparison (7 marks):**

| Parameter | Vapour Recovery | Liquid Recovery |
|---|---|---|
| Refrigerant phase at extraction | Superheated vapour | Sub-cooled / saturated liquid |
| Source connection | Low-side service port | High-side service port |
| Speed | Slower (1.0–1.5× charge mass/min equivalent) | Faster (2.0–3.0×) |
| Oil carry-over risk | **Low** (PAG stays in compressor sump) | **High** (liquid sweeps oil from line walls) |
| Equipment required | Standard RRR machine | RRR machine + liquid recovery adaptor + sight glass |
| Operator skill | Moderate | Higher (must prevent liquid slug into recovery compressor) |
| Compliance with SAE J2788 | Fully compliant | Conditional; SAE J2788 limits liquid flow rate |
| Typical application | **All automotive service** (default) | Bulk industrial chillers, transport refrigeration buses |

**Vapour recovery is the preferred automotive method** because oil carry-over contaminates the recovered refrigerant and degrades recycling. (1 mark)

**Liquid recovery is faster** but risks pushing liquid refrigerant into the recovery compressor, causing hydraulic damage. (1 mark)

A **hybrid "push-pull" method** is sometimes used: liquid is pushed from the high side into the recovery cylinder using its own vapour pressure, followed by vapour recovery to complete the job. (1 mark)

**(b) Decision Flowchart (7 marks):**

Refer to **Section 4.2** of these notes for the full Mermaid flowchart. Key decision points:

1. **Refrigerant identification** — wrong identification = cross-contamination penalty. (1 mark)
2. **System condition assessment** — leak suspected, contamination suspected, or routine service? (1 mark)
3. **Recovery machine selection** — R-12 needs legacy, R-134a needs SAE J2788, R-1234yf needs SAE J2843. (1 mark)
4. **Method selection** — vapour for routine, liquid for time-critical bulk, hybrid for contaminated. (1 mark)
5. **Verification of completion** — low-side reaches −100 kPa gauge AND soak test passes. (1 mark)
6. **Documentation** — refrigerant mass, technician ID, date, vehicle registration. (1 mark)
7. **Cylinder tagging and disposal/recycling route** — R-12 destroy, R-134a recycle, R-1234yf reclaim. (1 mark)

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Pitfall Warnings

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Venting is NEVER acceptable.** A common error is describing discharge as "letting refrigerant out." This attracts **0 marks** for the definition. Always use "closed-loop recovery."
> 2. **Confusing the two service-port threads.** M14 vs M13 is a subtle but frequently tested distinction. Memorise both.
> 3. **Ignoring the soak test.** A complete answer must include the 5-minute soak-and-check; skipping it loses 1 mark easily.
> 4. **Wrong gas constant units.** In the residual-mass derivation, mixing J with kJ or Pa with kPa gives an answer off by 1000×. Re-check unit consistency.
> 5. **Forgetting environmental context.** Always cite at least one regulation (Montreal Protocol / EPA / CPCB) when explaining *why* discharge is performed.
> 6. **Skipping the diagram.** A block diagram or flowchart in Part B (a) is worth **at least 2 marks** by itself.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Discharging = Closed-loop refrigerant recovery** using an RRR machine; never vent to atmosphere.
- ✅ **Vapour recovery** is the automotive industry standard; **liquid recovery** is faster but risks oil carry-over.
- ✅ **R-134a ports** = M14 × 1.5 (high) and M13 × 1.5 (low); R-12 = M16 × 1.5; R-1234yf = M12 × 1.5.
- ✅ **Auto-shutoff** occurs at approximately **−100 kPa gauge** on the low side, per SAE J2788.
- ✅ **EPA compliance** mandates **≤ 15 g residual** for a passenger car system.
- ✅ **R-134a GWP = 1430**; R-1234yf GWP = 4; R-12 ODP = 1.0 (reference).
- ✅ **Saturated pressure of R-134a at 25 °C ≈ 0.665 MPa** (gauge ≈ 0.564 MPa).
- ✅ **Soak test = 5 min** pressure-hold after recovery to detect trapped refrigerant.
- ✅ **Mass balance** $m_{rec} = m_{final,cyl} - m_{initial,cyl}$ must be within ± 30 g of under-hood label.
- ✅ **Residual mass formula** $m_{rem} = \dfrac{P_{LS,abs} M_{ref} V_{sys}}{R_u T_{final}}$ — verify unit consistency.
- ✅ **Ambient temperature** dramatically affects recovery time: high ambient = high $P_{eq}$ = longer recovery.
- ✅ **Mandatory PPE**: goggles, gloves, face shield; liquid refrigerant causes frostbite.
- ✅ **Service port threads** must be re-capped immediately after recovery to prevent moisture ingress.
- ✅ **Cylinder tagging**: refrigerant type, date, vehicle, technician, contamination status.
- ✅ **Disposal rules**: R-12 destroyed, R-134a recycled, R-1234yf reclaimed.
- ✅ **Push-Pull / Hybrid method** is used in industrial chillers but not standard automotive practice.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Evacuating the System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT523 - AUTOMOTIVE REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Evacuating the System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition

**Evacuation** is the systematic process of removing air, moisture (water vapor), and other non-condensable gases from an automotive air conditioning system (or any vapor compression refrigeration system) by creating a deep vacuum using a vacuum pump, prior to charging the system with refrigerant. The objective is to achieve a specified vacuum level (typically **500 to 1000 microns** of mercury, or approximately **0.5 to 1.0 mbar absolute pressure**) so that the system is free of contaminants that would degrade performance and damage components.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Evacuation is a mandatory service procedure in automotive HVAC. The KTU examiner frequently tests the *purpose*, the *consequences of improper evacuation*, and the *triple evacuation method*. Memorize the micron values and the boiling point of water under vacuum.

## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are about to fill a brand-new glass bottle with cold drinking water from a desert oasis. If the bottle already has stale air, dust, and some moisture inside, your clean water will taste terrible and may even react with the trapped gases. So, what do you do first? You **suck out** all the stale air using a straw until you can feel almost nothing left inside — then you fill it with fresh water.

That "sucking out" process is exactly what **evacuation** does to a refrigeration system:

- The **vacuum pump** is the "straw."
- The **refrigerant system** (evaporator, condenser, hoses, compressor) is the "bottle."
- **Air, moisture, and non-condensable gases** are the "stale air and dust."
- The **refrigerant** is the "fresh cold water."

If we skip this step, the system will suffer — analogous to drinking contaminated water. The compressor will overwork, the cooling will be poor, and the metallic parts will corrode from within.

## Why is Evacuation Specifically Critical in Automotive A/C?

Automotive systems are subjected to **vibrations, temperature swings (-20°C to +80°C), and humidity exposure** (especially when doors are opened on rainy days). This makes them more vulnerable to moisture ingress than stationary systems. Proper evacuation ensures long-term reliability of the compressor, expansion valve, and condenser.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Vacuum Levels Used in Practice:**
> - **Rough vacuum (first stage):** ~ 25,000 microns (25 mm Hg)
> - **Deep vacuum (final target):** **500 microns or less** (0.5 mm Hg / 0.66 mbar)
> - **Boiling point of water at 500 microns:** approximately **0°C** (a key figure for examiners)
> - **Boiling point of water at 1000 microns:** approximately **6-7°C**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Purpose of Evacuation — The "Three Enemies"

A refrigeration system has three invisible enemies that must be removed before charging:

### Enemy #1: **Non-Condensable Gases (NCGs) — Mainly Air (N₂ + O₂)**
- These gases do **not** condense at the operating condenser pressure/temperature.
- They accumulate at the top of the condenser, creating a **partial pressure barrier**.
- Result: **Higher head pressure** → increased compressor work → reduced cooling capacity → higher fuel consumption in cars.

### Enemy #2: **Moisture (Water Vapor)**
- Sources: residual moisture after manufacture, improper service, leakage, and desiccant saturation.
- Critical consequences:
  1. **Ice formation at the expansion valve (TXV/CCOT orifice)** — the most common cause of automotive A/C complaints ("cooling works only for 5-10 minutes, then stops").
  2. **Hydrolysis of refrigerant + oil** → formation of **hydrochloric (HCl), hydrofluoric (HF) acids**.
  3. **Copper plating** on iron/steel surfaces (compressor bearings, valves) — a destructive electrochemical effect.
  4. **Sludge formation** in the compressor oil → lubrication failure → compressor seizure.

### Enemy #3: **Air itself (which carries moisture + oxygen)**
- Oxygen accelerates **oxidation** of copper, steel, and aluminum components.
- Promotes **acid formation** in the presence of refrigerant and oil.

## 2.2 The Physics Behind Evacuation

When pressure is reduced, the **boiling point of water drops dramatically**. By pulling a deep vacuum, we force any trapped liquid water to **boil off at room temperature**, converting it to vapor, which is then pumped out as a gas.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key relationship:** As absolute pressure drops, water's boiling point drops. At 500 microns Hg, water boils at roughly 0°C, ensuring that even moisture trapped in the desiccant bag, oil, and crevices is vaporized and removed.

## 2.3 The Evacuation Procedure — Step-by-Step

The complete evacuation process used in professional automotive workshops:

| Step | Action | Purpose |
|------|--------|---------|
| 1 | Confirm system is leak-free (pressure test with OFN beforehand) | Avoid pulling air into a leaky system |
| 2 | Connect **vacuum pump**, **manifold gauges**, and **micron gauge** | Establish service connection |
| 3 | Open both high-side and low-side manifold valves | Allow pump to pull vacuum on entire system |
| 4 | Run vacuum pump for **15–60 minutes** (depending on system size) | Remove bulk air and moisture |
| 5 | Close manifold valves, **monitor micron gauge** | Check for vacuum rise (indicates leak or moisture) |
| 6 | **Vacuum hold test** — system must hold within **100 microns** over 15–30 minutes | Confirms no leaks and moisture is removed |
| 7 | (Optional) **Triple Evacuation** — break vacuum with dry nitrogen, then re-evacuate | Removes stubborn moisture |
| 8 | Isolate vacuum pump, charge with refrigerant | Begin operation |

## 2.4 Triple Evacuation Method (Frequently Asked in KTU)

A more rigorous procedure used when the system has been open for a long time or contaminated:

1. **First Evacuation** → pull to 500 microns, hold 15 min.
2. **Break vacuum with dry OFN (Oxygen-Free Nitrogen)** to ~ 0 psig → moisture absorbed and displaced.
3. **Second Evacuation** → pull to 500 microns again.
4. **Break vacuum with dry OFN** again.
5. **Third and final Evacuation** → pull to 500 microns, hold 30 min.
6. **Charge with refrigerant.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Why three times?** Each cycle removes moisture from progressively deeper layers (oil, desiccant, internal crevices). The nitrogen break effectively "flushes" the moisture that the vacuum alone cannot dislodge.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Value | Units | Notes |
|---------|-----------------|-------|-------|
| Vacuum Level (Target) | $P_{vac} \le 500$ | microns Hg (µm Hg) | Industry standard for automotive |
| Acceptable Maximum | $P_{vac} \le 1000$ | microns Hg | Used for smaller systems |
| Conversion: 1 mm Hg | $= 1000$ microns | — | Useful for unit conversion |
| Conversion: 1 atm | $= 760$ mm Hg $= 760{,}000$ microns | — | Reference |
| Boiling point of water at 500 µm | $\approx 0$ | °C | Critical for moisture removal |
| Boiling point of water at 1000 µm | $\approx 6$–$7$ | °C | — |
| Vacuum Hold Test — Allowable Rise | $\le 100$ microns in 15 min | microns Hg | Indicates no leak/no moisture |
| Micron Gauge Working Principle | $1\ \text{micron} = 0.001\ \text{mm Hg}$ | — | — |
| Approximate Evacuation Time | $t \approx 30$–$60$ | minutes | For typical car A/C |
| Pressure Conversion | $1\ \text{bar} = 750.06\ \text{mm Hg}$ | — | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Tip:** When asked "What is the recommended vacuum level?", always answer **500 microns or below** and briefly justify: *"At this pressure, water boils near 0°C, ensuring complete removal of moisture from the system."*

## 2.6 Engineering Significance

In modern automotive A/C systems using **HFC-134a** and increasingly **HFO-1234yf**, moisture is even more problematic because:
- HFC-134a is **hygroscopic** (absorbs moisture readily).
- HFO-1234yf is mildly flammable, so contamination must be avoided for safety.
- Modern **PAG oils** and **POE oils** (used with HFO) react with water to form acids more aggressively than older mineral oils.

Proper evacuation directly translates to:
- **Longer compressor life**
- **Stable head pressure** (no NCG accumulation)
- **Consistent cabin cooling**
- **Lower fuel consumption**
- **Warranty compliance**

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Pressure Unit Conversions for Vacuum Levels

KTU examiners often test whether students can convert between **microns, mm Hg, mbar, and psia**. Let us derive these conversions exhaustively.

### Given Standard Relations
$$1\ \text{atm} = 760\ \text{mm Hg} = 1.01325\ \text{bar} = 14.696\ \text{psia}$$

By definition:
$$1\ \text{micron} = 10^{-3}\ \text{mm Hg} = 10^{-3}\ \text{Torr}$$

### Step 1: Convert 500 microns to mm Hg

$$P_{mmHg} = \frac{500}{1000} = 0.5\ \text{mm Hg}$$

This is the standard target absolute pressure.

### Step 2: Convert 0.5 mm Hg to bar

We use the relation $1\ \text{bar} = 750.06\ \text{mm Hg}$ (standard atmospheric approximation):

$$P_{bar} = \frac{0.5}{750.06}$$

$$P_{bar} = 6.667 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{bar}$$

$$P_{bar} \approx 0.667\ \text{mbar}$$

### Step 3: Convert 0.667 mbar to psia

Using $1\ \text{atm} = 14.696\ \text{psia} = 1.01325\ \text{bar}$:

$$P_{psia} = 14.696 \times \frac{0.667 \times 10^{-3}}{1.01325}$$

$$P_{psia} = \frac{14.696 \times 6.67 \times 10^{-4}}{1.01325}$$

$$P_{psia} = \frac{9.802 \times 10^{-3}}{1.01325}$$

$$P_{psia} \approx 9.674 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{psia}$$

$$P_{psia} \approx 0.00967\ \text{psia}$$

This confirms that 500 microns corresponds to a near-perfect vacuum (less than 1% of atmospheric pressure).

### Step 4: Vacuum Level as a Percentage of Atmosphere

$$\% \text{ Vacuum} = \frac{P_{atm} - P_{abs}}{P_{atm}} \times 100$$

$$\% \text{ Vacuum} = \frac{760 - 0.5}{760} \times 100$$

$$\% \text{ Vacuum} = \frac{759.5}{760} \times 100$$

$$\% \text{ Vacuum} \approx 99.934\ \%$$

> **Interpretation:** A 500-micron vacuum is approximately **99.93% vacuum** — extremely close to a perfect vacuum.

## 3.2 Derivation: Boiling Point of Water vs. Pressure (Clausius–Clapeyron Approximation)

The boiling point of water at reduced pressure can be estimated using a simplified form of the **Clausius–Clapeyron equation**:

$$\ln \left( \frac{P_2}{P_1} \right) = -\frac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R} \left( \frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1} \right)$$

Where:
- $P_1 = 760\ \text{mm Hg}$, $T_1 = 373.15\ \text{K}$ (normal boiling point)
- $\Delta H_{vap} = 40{,}656\ \text{J/mol}$ (latent heat of vaporization of water at 100°C)
- $R = 8.314\ \text{J/(mol·K)}$
- $P_2 = 0.5\ \text{mm Hg}$ (target vacuum)
- $T_2 = ?$

### Step 1: Substitute Known Values

$$\ln \left( \frac{0.5}{760} \right) = -\frac{40{,}656}{8.314} \left( \frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{373.15} \right)$$

### Step 2: Evaluate the Left Side

$$\ln(0.5/760) = \ln(6.5789 \times 10^{-4})$$

$$= -7.326$$

### Step 3: Evaluate the Coefficient

$$-\frac{40{,}656}{8.314} = -4890.55$$

### Step 4: Substitute Back

$$-7.326 = -4890.55 \left( \frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{373.15} \right)$$

### Step 5: Solve for the Bracket

$$\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{373.15} = \frac{-7.326}{-4890.55}$$

$$\frac{1}{T_2} - 2.680 \times 10^{-3} = 1.498 \times 10^{-3}$$

### Step 6: Isolate 1/T₂

$$\frac{1}{T_2} = 2.680 \times 10^{-3} + 1.498 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\frac{1}{T_2} = 4.178 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{K}^{-1}$$

### Step 7: Solve for T₂

$$T_2 = \frac{1}{4.178 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$T_2 \approx 239.4\ \text{K}$$

Converting to Celsius:

$$T_2 = 239.4 - 273.15 = -33.75\ ^\circ\text{C}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Implication:** At 0.5 mm Hg (500 microns), pure water would boil at approximately **−34°C**. Even residual moisture in oil and crevices (which has slightly different properties) is forced to vaporize at room temperature during evacuation. This is why deep vacuum is so effective at dehydrating a system.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Effects of Improper Evacuation

| Contaminant | Symptom in Operation | Affected Component | Service Implication |
|-------------|----------------------|--------------------|---------------------|
| Air (NCG) | High head pressure, warm condenser outlet, reduced cooling | Condenser, compressor | Vacuum pump + recharge |
| Moisture | Intermittent cooling, ice at TXV orifice | Expansion valve | Replace desiccant, triple evacuate |
| Oxygen | Acid formation, copper plating | Compressor, hoses | Full system flush + evacuation |
| Residual oil/water sludge | Compressor noise, seizure risk | Compressor bearings | Replace compressor + flush |
| N₂ (from leak test, not removed) | Reduced cooling capacity, higher discharge T | Condenser top | Re-evacuate properly |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Evacuation System Block Diagram (Mermaid Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Vacuum Pump] -->|Suction hose| B[Manifold Gauge Set]
    B -->|High-side yellow hose| C[High-Side Service Port - Receiver Drier]
    B -->|Low-side blue hose| D[Low-Side Service Port - Compressor Suction]
    C --> E[Condenser]
    C --> F[Receiver Drier with Desiccant]
    E --> G[Compressor]
    F --> G
    G --> D
    D --> H[Evaporator]
    H --> I[Expansion Valve or Orifice Tube]
    I --> E
    J[Micron Gauge] --> B
    K[Service Technician] -->|Monitors| J
    L[Dry Nitrogen Cylinder] -.->|Optional: break vacuum| B
    M[Refrigerant Cylinder] -.->|After evacuation| B
```

## 4.2 Triple Evacuation Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Start: System Open or Contaminated] --> S1[Step 1: Connect Vacuum Pump and Micron Gauge]
    S1 --> S2[Step 2: First Evacuation to 500 microns]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Hold Vacuum for 15 minutes]
    S3 --> S4{Q1: Vacuum Rise greater than 100 microns?}
    S4 -->|Yes - leak/moisture present| S5[Step 4: Break Vacuum with Dry Nitrogen to 0 psig]
    S4 -->|No - acceptable| S10[Proceed to Charge]
    S5 --> S6[Step 5: Second Evacuation to 500 microns]
    S6 --> S7[Step 6: Hold Vacuum for 15 minutes]
    S7 --> S8{Q2: Vacuum still rising?}
    S8 -->|Yes| S9[Step 7: Third Evacuation to 500 microns - Final]
    S8 -->|No| S10
    S9 --> S11[Step 8: Final 30-minute Hold Test]
    S11 --> S12{Q3: Holds Steady less than 100 microns rise?}
    S12 -->|Yes| S10[Step 9: Isolate Pump and Charge with Refrigerant]
    S12 -->|No| S13[Investigate Leak or Severe Moisture - Service Required]
    S10 --> S14[End: System Ready for Charging]
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture of an Automotive A/C System (with Evacuation Paths)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HIGH_PRESSURE_SIDE[High Pressure Side]
        COMP[Compressor - Discharges Hot High-Pressure Vapor]
        COND[Condenser - Rejects Heat to Ambient Air]
        RD[Receiver Drier with Desiccant Bag]
    end
    subgraph LOW_PRESSURE_SIDE[Low Pressure Side]
        EXP[Expansion Valve or Fixed Orifice Tube]
        EVAP[Evaporator - Absorbs Cabin Heat]
        ACC[Accumulator - Contains Desiccant]
    end
    subgraph SERVICE_BLOCK[Service Equipment]
        VMP[Vacuum Pump]
        MGS[Manifold Gauge Set]
        MIC[Micron Gauge]
        NIT[Oxygen-Free Nitrogen Cylinder]
        REF[Refrigerant Supply]
    end
    COMP --> COND
    COND --> RD
    RD --> EXP
    EXP --> EVAP
    EVAP --> ACC
    ACC --> COMP
    VMP -.->|Evacuation| MGS
    MGS -.->|Service Ports| RD
    MGS -.->|Service Ports| ACC
    NIT -.->|Pressure Test and Break Vacuum| MGS
    REF -.->|Final Charging| MGS
    MIC -.->|Monitors Absolute Pressure| MGS
```

## 4.4 Cause-and-Effect Diagram: Why Evacuation is Essential

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOT[Improper Evacuation] --> A[Air and NCG Trapped]
    ROOT --> B[Moisture Trapped]
    A --> A1[Higher Condensing Pressure]
    A1 --> A2[Increased Compressor Work]
    A2 --> A3[Higher Fuel Consumption in Car]
    A --> A4[Reduced Condenser Efficiency]
    A4 --> A5[Poor Cabin Cooling]
    B --> B1[Ice Formation at TXV Orifice]
    B1 --> B2[Blocked Refrigerant Flow]
    B2 --> B3[No Cooling at Evaporator]
    B --> B4[Acid Formation HCl and HF]
    B4 --> B5[Corrosion of Copper and Steel]
    B5 --> B6[Copper Plating on Bearings]
    B6 --> B7[Compressor Seizure]
    B --> B8[Sludge in PAG Oil]
    B8 --> B9[Loss of Lubrication]
    B9 --> B7
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]
**"What is the purpose of evacuating an automotive air conditioning system before charging it with refrigerant? List any three consequences if evacuation is not properly done."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Purpose:** Evacuation removes air, moisture (water vapor), and other non-condensable gases from the refrigeration system using a vacuum pump, achieving a deep vacuum of **500 microns or less**, to ensure system reliability and performance.

**Three consequences of improper evacuation:**

1. **High head pressure** due to accumulation of non-condensable gases in the condenser, leading to increased compressor work and reduced cooling capacity.
2. **Ice formation at the expansion valve orifice** due to moisture, causing intermittent or complete loss of cooling.
3. **Acid formation (HCl and HF)** due to hydrolysis of refrigerant with moisture, leading to corrosion, copper plating, and eventual compressor seizure.

*(Award 1 mark for purpose, 1 mark each for any two correct consequences, and 0.5 for the third if mentioned.)*
