---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]
**"Define 'micron' as used in vacuum measurement. What is the recommended vacuum level for evacuating an automotive A/C system and why?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **micron** is a unit of pressure equal to **0.001 mm of mercury (10⁻³ mm Hg)**, commonly used in HVAC/R to express deep vacuum levels.

The **recommended vacuum level** for an automotive A/C system is **500 microns (0.5 mm Hg) or less**.

**Why:** At 500 microns, the absolute pressure is extremely low (~ 0.67 mbar), and the boiling point of water drops to approximately **0°C or below**. This ensures that residual moisture in the desiccant, compressor oil, and internal crevices is vaporized and pumped out, preventing ice formation, acid formation, and corrosion in the operating system.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Pattern (KTU ESE Standard):** Answer EITHER Question A OR Question B.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1

**[KTU University Exam - Sample Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**a) Explain in detail the procedure of evacuating an automotive air conditioning system. Discuss the equipment used and the significance of achieving a 500-micron vacuum level. (7 marks)**

**b) With a neat flow diagram, explain the Triple Evacuation Method. When is it preferred over a single evacuation? (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Procedure and Equipment (7 marks):**

**Equipment Required:**
1. **Two-stage rotary vacuum pump** (capable of 20–50 microns ultimate vacuum).
2. **Manifold gauge set** (with high- and low-side hoses).
3. **Micron gauge** (electronic or analog, calibrated).
4. **Service port adapters** for high- and low-side connections.
5. **Valve core remover** (to remove Schrader valves for better evacuation).

**Procedure (Step-by-step):**
1. Confirm the system has been **leak-tested** with OFN at 150 psig and held for 10 minutes with no pressure drop. *(1 mark)*
2. Connect the **vacuum pump** to the center port of the manifold; the high-side hose to the high-side service port (usually on the receiver-drier or discharge line), and the low-side hose to the low-side service port (on the suction line or accumulator). *(1 mark)*
3. Open both manifold valves fully. **Remove the Schrader valve cores** from both service ports using a valve core tool to ensure maximum flow area and faster evacuation. *(0.5 mark)*
4. Start the vacuum pump. Run for a minimum of **30 minutes** for a typical car A/C; longer for larger systems. *(1 mark)*
5. Close both manifold valves and **turn off the pump**. Monitor the micron gauge. The reading should drop to **500 microns or less** within 5–10 minutes. *(0.5 mark)*
6. Perform a **vacuum hold test**: The system must hold its vacuum with a rise of **less than 100 microns in 15 minutes**. Any rise indicates either a leak or remaining moisture. *(1 mark)*
7. If the hold test passes, close the manifold valves, disconnect the pump, and proceed to charge the system. *(0.5 mark)*

**Significance of 500 Microns (1.5 marks):**
- At 500 microns (0.5 mm Hg, 0.67 mbar, 0.0097 psia), the system is **99.93% vacuum**.
- The boiling point of water at this pressure is near **0°C**, ensuring that even **stubborn moisture** trapped in oil, desiccant, and capillary passages is **vaporized and removed**.
- Lower vacuum levels (e.g., 1000 microns) leave the system susceptible to ice formation and acid formation under operating conditions.
- The micron gauge, with a 0–25,000 micron range, gives high resolution at deep vacuum — better than a manifold gauge (0–760 mm Hg) which is inaccurate below 25,000 microns.

---

**Part (b) — Triple Evacuation Method (7 marks):**

**Definition:** The Triple Evacuation method is a rigorous moisture-removal technique that alternates between **deep vacuum cycles** and **dry nitrogen pressure breaks**, performed three times consecutively, to ensure thorough dehydration of a contaminated or long-exposed refrigeration system. *(1 mark)*

**Flow Diagram (3 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Connect Vacuum Pump and Micron Gauge] --> B[1st Evacuation - Pull to 500 microns]
    B --> C[Hold 15 minutes]
    C --> D{Q1: Rise less than 100 microns?}
    D -->|Yes| E[Break Vacuum with Dry OFN to 0 psig]
    D -->|No - leak or wet| F[Repair Leak or Service Desiccant - Restart]
    E --> G[2nd Evacuation - Pull to 500 microns]
    G --> H[Hold 15 minutes]
    H --> I{Q2: Rise less than 100 microns?}
    I -->|Yes| J[Break Vacuum with Dry OFN to 0 psig]
    I -->|No| F
    J --> K[3rd and Final Evacuation - Pull to 500 microns]
    K --> L[Hold 30 minutes]
    L --> M{Q3: Rise less than 100 microns?}
    M -->|Yes| N[System Ready for Charging]
    M -->|No| F
```

**Procedure Explanation (2 marks):**
- **Cycle 1:** Pull vacuum to 500 microns, hold 15 min. The first cycle removes bulk air and surface moisture.
- **Break:** Introduce **dry Oxygen-Free Nitrogen (OFN)** to bring system pressure to 0 psig. The dry nitrogen absorbs some moisture and displaces remaining gases.
- **Cycle 2:** Re-evacuate to 500 microns, hold 15 min. This removes the moisture absorbed/released by the nitrogen flush.
- **Break:** Another nitrogen flush to 0 psig.
- **Cycle 3:** Final evacuation to 500 microns, hold 30 min. This deep, sustained vacuum removes moisture from deep crevices and the oil layer.

**When Triple Evacuation is Preferred (1 mark):**
- When the system has been **open to atmosphere for an extended period** (e.g., compressor replacement, line repair).
- When **moisture contamination is suspected** (e.g., desiccant saturation, after a leak that allowed humid air ingress).
- When a **single evacuation fails the hold test** (vacuum rises more than 100 microns).
- For **critical applications** where the highest cleanliness is mandatory (e.g., HFO-1234yf systems, systems with POE oil).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2

**[KTU University Exam - Sample Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**a) Discuss the harmful effects of moisture and non-condensable gases in an automotive A/C system. (7 marks)**

**b) Describe the role of a vacuum pump in the evacuation process. Why is a two-stage rotary vane pump preferred over a single-stage pump? (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Harmful Effects (7 marks):**

**Effects of Moisture (4 marks):**
1. **Ice formation at the expansion valve:** When moist refrigerant passes through the low-pressure expansion valve, the temperature drops sharply (typically −10°C to −15°C). Water freezes at 0°C, forming an **ice plug** that partially or fully blocks the orifice. The most visible symptom is **intermittent cooling** — the system cools for 5–10 minutes, then stops, defrosts, and repeats. *(1 mark)*
2. **Acid formation:** Water reacts chemically with HFC-134a and refrigerant oil:
   - Water + R-134a → Hydrofluoric acid (HF) + Hydrochloric acid (HCl) (in presence of chlorine from oil additives).
   - These acids attack copper, steel, and aluminum, causing **internal corrosion**. *(1 mark)*
3. **Copper plating:** In the presence of acid and high temperatures, copper dissolves from the condenser and evaporator tubing and **electroplates onto iron/steel surfaces**, especially the **compressor bearing surfaces**. This rough, copper-coated surface causes **bearing wear, scoring, and eventual seizure**. *(1 mark)*
4. **Sludge in oil:** Moisture reacts with PAG oil to form a **waxy sludge** that clogs the oil return passages in the evaporator and accumulator, leading to **oil starvation** and compressor failure. *(1 mark)*

**Effects of Non-Condensable Gases (NCGs) — Mainly Air (3 marks):**
1. **High head pressure:** Air accumulates at the top of the condenser where it is hottest. Since air does not condense, it creates a **partial pressure cushion** that raises the total condensing pressure by 5–30 psig. The compressor must work against this higher pressure, increasing **power consumption** and reducing **COP (Coefficient of Performance)**. *(1 mark)*
2. **Reduced condenser effectiveness:** With air blanketing the condenser's top section, **heat rejection is impaired**. The refrigerant leaving the condenser is at a higher temperature (subcooling is reduced), and the system's net refrigeration effect drops. *(1 mark)*
3. **Higher discharge temperature:** The compressor compresses a gas mixture with lower mass flow of refrigerant. The discharge temperature rises, accelerating **oil degradation** and **compressor valve wear**. In extreme cases, the lubricant carbonizes, leading to valve plate failure. *(1 mark)*

---

**Part (b) — Vacuum Pump and Two-Stage Preference (7 marks):**

**Role of the Vacuum Pump in Evacuation (4 marks):**
1. **Primary function:** The vacuum pump is the heart of the evacuation process. It creates the **pressure differential** that drives air and moisture out of the sealed refrigeration system. *(0.5 mark)*
2. **Mechanical operation:** Most HVAC vacuum pumps are **oil-sealed rotary vane pumps**. An eccentric rotor with sliding vanes spins inside a cylindrical stator. The vanes trap, compress, and expel gas through an exhaust valve. The pump oil seals the gaps and lubricates moving parts. *(1 mark)*
3. **Two-stage advantage in moisture handling:** In a two-stage pump, gas is first compressed in the **low-stage (rough vacuum, 25,000 → 1000 microns)**, then partially cooled and transferred to the **high-stage (deep vacuum, 1000 → 50 microns)**. Critically, the inter-stage and high-stage operation occur at **lower pressures** where water vapor does not condense inside the pump. This **prevents water from contaminating the pump oil**, which would otherwise emulsify the oil and destroy the pump's sealing ability. *(1.5 marks)*
4. **Ultimate vacuum capability:** A good two-stage pump reaches **20–50 microns** ultimate vacuum, while single-stage pumps typically reach only **100–200 microns**. This is essential for achieving the **500-micron** target. *(0.5 mark)*
5. **Service life:** Because the two-stage design prevents water from re-condensing in the pump oil, the oil stays clean longer, and the pump has a significantly **longer service life**. *(0.5 mark)*

**Why Two-Stage is Preferred over Single-Stage (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Single-Stage Pump | Two-Stage Pump |
|-----------|-------------------|----------------|
| Ultimate vacuum | 100–200 microns | 20–50 microns |
| Water vapor handling | Poor — water condenses in pump oil | Excellent — vapor stays gaseous through both stages |
| Pump oil life | Short (frequent oil changes) | Long (cleaner oil) |
| Suitability for automotive A/C | Marginal | Highly preferred |
| Cost | Lower | Slightly higher (justified by performance) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing "evacuation" with "leak testing"** — students often describe pressure testing (positive pressure) instead of vacuum pulling. Evacuation is a **negative pressure** process. *(−2 marks if confused)*
> 2. **Stating vacuum levels in mm Hg or bar instead of microns** — KTU examiners specifically look for "500 microns" as the industry term. Always use the unit *micron* in the answer. *(−1 mark if missing)*
> 3. **Forgetting to mention moisture-induced acid formation** — this is a high-yield point. Vague answers like "moisture is bad" without specifying *HCl, HF, copper plating* lose 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Mixing up the sequence in Triple Evacuation** — students often forget that nitrogen is used to **break** the vacuum, not to evacuate. The sequence must be: **Vacuum → Nitrogen Break → Vacuum → Nitrogen Break → Final Vacuum**.
> 5. **Not stating the boiling point of water at 500 microns** — Examiners love this "wow" fact. Memorize: *~ 0°C at 500 microns, ~ −34°C theoretically for pure water*.
> 6. **Skipping the vacuum hold test** — Always mention that a *15-minute hold with < 100-micron rise* confirms a leak-free, dry system.
> 7. **Calling Schrader valves "pressure relief valves"** — They are **one-way check valves** for service access. Don't confuse them.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Evacuating the System**

- ✅ **Definition:** Evacuation = removal of air, moisture, and NCGs from a refrigeration system using a vacuum pump, **before** refrigerant charging.
- ✅ **Target Vacuum:** **500 microns (0.5 mm Hg) or less** for automotive A/C. Never accept above 1000 microns.
- ✅ **Boiling Point of Water at 500 microns:** ~ **0°C** (or ~ −34°C theoretical for pure water, accounting for dissolved salts — use the practical 0°C value in exams).
- ✅ **Conversion Quick-Facts:** 1 mm Hg = 1000 microns; 1 atm = 760 mm Hg = 760,000 microns.
- ✅ **Three Enemies:** Non-condensable gases (air) → high head pressure; Moisture → ice, acid, copper plating; Oxygen → corrosion.
- ✅ **Critical Damage Mechanisms from Moisture:** Ice at TXV orifice, HCl/HF acid formation, copper plating on compressor bearings, sludge in PAG/POE oil.
- ✅ **Procedure Order:** Leak Test (OFN) → Connect Vacuum Pump → Open Manifold Valves → Pull Vacuum 30+ min → Hold Test (15 min, < 100-micron rise) → Charge.
- ✅ **Vacuum Hold Test:** A rise of **> 100 microns in 15 minutes** indicates either a leak or remaining moisture — evacuation was inadequate.
- ✅ **Triple Evacuation:** Vacuum → Dry N₂ Break → Vacuum → Dry N₂ Break → Final Vacuum. Used for severely contaminated or long-open systems.
- ✅ **Two-Stage Vacuum Pump:** Preferred for moisture-laden systems. Prevents water re-condensation in pump oil, achieves 20–50 microns ultimate vacuum.
- ✅ **Service Port Trick:** **Remove Schrader valve cores** during evacuation for maximum flow — a common professional technique.
- ✅ **Always use a Micron Gauge** — manifold pressure gauges are inaccurate below 25,000 microns.
- ✅ **Equipment Required:** Two-stage vacuum pump, manifold gauge set, micron gauge, valve core tool, service hoses.
- ✅ **Modern Relevance:** HFC-134a is highly hygroscopic; HFO-1234yf is mildly flammable — both demand rigorous evacuation.
- ✅ **Engineer's Payoff:** Proper evacuation → longer compressor life, stable head pressure, consistent cooling, lower fuel consumption, warranty compliance.

> **🎯 Final Exam Mantra:** *"Evacuate to 500 microns, hold for 15, charge with confidence."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Charging the System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT523 - AUTOMOTIVE REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Charging the System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Charging the System** is the precise, metered procedure of introducing the correct type and quantity of **refrigerant** (along with the appropriate **lubricating oil** charge) into an assembled automotive air-conditioning (A/C) system, after the system has been **evacuated**, **leak-tested**, and verified to be **moisture-free**. The procedure restores the system to its as-manufactured design state so that it can deliver the rated **Cooling Capacity (Q)** at the specified **Coefficient of Performance (COP)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot (Board-Examiner Phrasing):**
> *"Charging is the controlled addition of refrigerant and lubricant to the A/C system to bring the refrigerant mass flow, oil circulation rate, and operating pressures within manufacturer-specified design limits, ensuring stable saturated suction/saturated liquid line conditions at the compressor and condenser/evaporator."*

## 1.2 System Components Involved

| Sl. No. | Component | Role During Charging |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| 1 | **Compressor** | Internally oil-flooded; must receive correct viscosity oil |
| 2 | **Condenser** | Receives high-pressure vapor; final liquid subcooling occurs here |
| 3 | **Receiver-Drier / Accumulator** | Holds liquid refrigerant; desiccant removes moisture |
| 4 | **Expansion Valve / Orifice Tube** | Pressure-drop device; controls refrigerant flow |
| 5 | **Evaporator** | Produces cooling; air leaves with controlled superheat |
| 6 | **Service Ports (High & Low)** | SAE-standard Schrader valves for manifold connection |
| 7 | **Manifold Gauge Set** | Diagnostic instrumentation for pressure and charging |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "Filling a Pressure Cooker" 🍲**
>
> Imagine a sealed pressure cooker that you must fill with **exactly 1 litre of water** and **1 tablespoon of oil**. If you under-fill, the food burns (A/C = *poor cooling, high discharge temperature*). If you over-fill, the safety valve lifts and steam escapes (A/C = *compressor flooding, high suction pressure, no cooling*). You also need to **evacuate** (suck out all the air) before filling, otherwise steam and air mix, and the cooker misbehaves. The "refrigerant" is the water that does the work, the "oil" lubricates the pump (compressor), and the **manifold gauges** are your "weighing scale and pressure gauge" combined.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Standard Ambient for Charging:** **21.1 °C (70 °F)** (SAE J639 reference condition)
- **R-134a Saturation Pressure @ 21.1 °C:** **~ 5.6 bar (82 psig)** at low side; **~ 12 bar (175 psig)** high side
- **R-1234yf Saturation Pressure @ 21.1 °C:** **~ 6.1 bar (88 psig)** low side
- **Minimum Evacuation Vacuum:** **29.5 in Hg (500 microns Hg / 0.067 kPa)**
- **Evacuation Hold Time (moisture boil-off):** **≥ 30 minutes**
- **Typical R-134a Charge (Passenger Car):** **0.85 – 1.5 kg (1.9 – 3.3 lb)**
- **Compressor Oil Charge:** **120 – 250 ml** (varies by compressor type)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters (Examiner's View):** A 5 % under-charge or 10 % over-charge in R-134a systems can shift the **Compressor Discharge Temperature** by **15–25 °C**, directly affecting **lubricant stability** and **winding life**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Charging is Critical — The "Why"

The **refrigerant mass inventory** in a sealed A/C system governs every operating parameter simultaneously:

1. **Compressor Suction Pressure (P_suc)** → directly proportional to charge
2. **Compressor Discharge Pressure (P_dis)** → rises with charge
3. **Evaporator Outlet Superheat (SH)** → inversely affected
4. **Condenser Subcooling (SC)** → directly affected
5. **Compressor Power Input (W_in)** → has a *U-shaped* curve with charge

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Concept — The "Goldilocks Zone" of Charge:**
> Too little → starved evaporator → high SH → poor cooling & high discharge temp.
> Too much → flooded evaporator → low SH → liquid slugging → compressor damage.
> Just right → 5–10 °F SH at evaporator outlet, 10–15 °F SC at condenser outlet.

## 2.2 Types of Refrigerants Used in Automotive A/C

| Refrigerant | Type | GWP | ODP | Service Status |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|
| **R-12** | CFC | 10900 | 0.82 | **Obsolete** (banned under Montreal Protocol) |
| **R-134a** | HFC | 1430 | 0 | **Current Standard** (post-1994) |
| **R-1234yf** | HFO | < 1 | 0 | **New EU / New Vehicle Standard** |
| **R-152a** | HFC | 124 | 0 | Niche use (low-GWP research) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield:** R-12 systems used **Mineral Oil (MO)**, while R-134a systems require **Polyalkylene Glycol (PAG) oil** due to miscibility requirements. R-1234yf also requires **PAG oil** (or POE in some applications) — never cross-contaminate.

## 2.3 Charging Methods — Comparative Theory

### Method A: Liquid Charging (High-Side)
- Refrigerant enters as **saturated liquid** at the high-side service port.
- **System state:** Receiver-drier present (TXV systems).
- **Engine state:** OFF.
- **Speed:** Faster because liquid contains ~10× more mass per unit volume than vapor.
- **Risk:** If liquid reaches compressor, **liquid slugging** → mechanical failure.

### Method B: Vapor Charging (Low-Side)
- Refrigerant enters as **superheated vapor** at the low-side service port.
- **System state:** Any system (especially orifice-tube with accumulator).
- **Engine state:** OFF (for static charge) or ON (for top-up).
- **Speed:** Slower (vapor density is low).
- **Advantage:** Cannot slug compressor — safest method.

### Method C: Recover–Recycle–Recharge (R/R/R) Machine
- **Modern, industry-standard** method using SAE J2788 / J2843 compliant machines.
- Machine weighs refrigerant precisely using **electronic scale (accuracy ± 5 g)**.
- Performs evacuation, leak check, and oil-charge injection automatically.

### Method D: Charging by Pressure (Obsolete — KTU Warning)
- Estimating charge by manifold gauge pressures **only** is *unreliable* and not board-recommended.
- **Use only as a pre-check**, never as a final method.

## 2.4 The Three Pre-Charging Operations (Must-Do)

| Step | Purpose | Spec / Standard |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **1. Leak Test** | Verify system is hermetic | Nitrogen @ 10–15 bar, hold 10 min, ΔP ≤ 0.1 bar (or electronic sniffer) |
| **2. Evacuation** | Remove air, nitrogen, and boil off moisture | Pull **500 microns Hg** for **≥ 30 min** |
| **3. Oil Charge** | Restore oil lost during component replacement | +30 ml (1 oz) per component replaced |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Board Mistake:** Students often describe charging as *just "adding refrigerant."* This loses marks. Always mention the **3-stage pre-charge protocol** (Leak → Evacuate → Oil Charge) **BEFORE** adding refrigerant.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|:---|
| 1 | **Superheat (SH)** | $SH = T_{evap\,out} - T_{sat}(P_{evap})$ | °C or °F | Should be **5–10 °F (3–6 °C)** |
| 2 | **Subcooling (SC)** | $SC = T_{sat}(P_{cond}) - T_{liq\,line}$ | °C or °F | Should be **10–15 °F (6–8 °C)** |
| 3 | **Refrigerant Mass from P-V-T** | $m = \dfrac{PV}{ZRT}$ | kg | Use R-134a: $R = 0.0815\,\text{kJ/kg·K}$ |
| 4 | **Refrigerant Saturation P-T** | Antoine / empirical chart | — | R-134a: $P_{sat}(T)$ from tables |
| 5 | **Compressor Oil Recharge** | $V_{oil} = V_{new} + \sum_{i=1}^{n}(30\,\text{ml})$ per part | ml | "+1 oz per component" rule |
| 6 | **Vacuum Hold Time (min)** | $t_{hold} \geq 30$ | min | After 500 µm reached, isolate pump |
| 7 | **R-12 to R-134a Retrofit (approx)** | $m_{134a} \approx 0.85 \times m_{12}$ | kg | Retrofit guidelines (R-12 systems) |
| 8 | **System Internal Volume (typical)** | $V_{sys} \approx 5 \text{–} 7\,\text{L}$ | L | Passenger car A/C |
| 9 | **Compressor Displacement** | $\dot{V}_{comp} = \dfrac{N \cdot V_d}{60}$ | m³/s | N = rpm, V_d = swept volume |
| 10 | **Theoretical Charge by Density** | $m = \rho_{liq} \cdot V_{sys}$ | kg | $\rho_{R134a,liq} \approx 1200\,\text{kg/m}^3$ at 25 °C |

> [!NOTE]
> In the table above, the symbols used are: $P$ = absolute pressure, $V$ = volume, $Z$ = compressibility factor ($\approx 0.95$ for vapor R-134a), $R$ = specific gas constant, $T$ = absolute temperature. The notation $T_{evap\,out}$ means the temperature of the refrigerant at the evaporator outlet (measured on the suction line).

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **OEM Assembly Plants** use **automated R/R/R stations** for 100 % of vehicles on the production line.
- **Garage Service** uses **portable manifold + electronic scale** setups.
- **Fleet Operators** (KSRTC, KSRTC SWIFT, private buses) use **bulk refrigerant cylinders** with mass-flow meters for fast charging.
- **EV Thermal Management** uses **R-1234yf** with **secondary loop chillers**, requiring different charging protocols (5–15 % lower charge than ICE vehicles).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Refrigerant Saturation Pressure (P-T Relation for Charging Diagnosis)

For R-134a, a widely used engineering approximation in the operating range of automotive A/C is the **Clausius-Clapeyron integrated form**:

$$\ln P = -\frac{h_{fg}}{R} \cdot \frac{1}{T} + C$$

Where $P$ is saturation pressure (Pa), $T$ is saturation temperature (K), $h_{fg}$ is the latent heat of vaporization, $R$ is the specific gas constant, and $C$ is an integration constant.

For R-134a specifically, a fitted polynomial valid between **-20 °C and 70 °C** is:

$$P_{sat}(\text{bar}) = e^{\left(a + \dfrac{b}{T(^{\circ}\text{C})+c}\right)}$$

with empirical constants $a = 4.873$, $b = -1810.0$, $c = 273.15$.

**Numerical Worked Example:**
At $T = 0\,^{\circ}\text{C}$, find $P_{sat}$ for R-134a.

**Step 1:** Compute the exponent.
$$a + \frac{b}{T+c} = 4.873 + \frac{-1810.0}{0+273.15} = 4.873 - 6.626 = -1.753$$

**Step 2:** Apply the exponential.
$$P_{sat} = e^{-1.753} = 0.173\,\text{bar (absolute)}$$

**Step 3:** Convert to gauge.
$$P_{sat}(\text{gauge}) = 0.173 - 1.013 = -0.84\,\text{bar}$$
which is a **vacuum** — confirming that R-134a boils below atmospheric pressure below about **-10 °C**, and the system must hold positive pressure to prevent air ingress.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Charge Quantity from System Internal Volume

For a liquid-charged system at the end of evacuation, the theoretical mass of refrigerant required is:

$$m = \rho_{liq}(T_{fill}) \times V_{sys}$$

where $V_{sys}$ is the **internal geometric volume** of the A/C circuit (compressor + condenser + hoses + evaporator + receiver-drier).

**Step-by-Step Evaluation:**

**Step 1 — Estimate internal volume** (typical passenger car):
$$V_{sys} = 6 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}^3 = 6\,\text{L}$$

**Step 2 — Look up R-134a liquid density at 25 °C**:
$$\rho_{liq} = 1206\,\text{kg/m}^3$$

**Step 3 — Compute mass**:
$$m = 1206 \times 6 \times 10^{-3} = 7.236\,\text{kg}$$

This is the **pure geometric fill** if the system were 100 % liquid — but in practice, only the receiver-drier and condenser (during operation) hold liquid, while the suction line holds low-pressure vapor. The **correct operating charge** is therefore the **OEM specification**, typically:
$$m_{operational} = 0.85 \text{ – } 1.5\,\text{kg for R-134a systems}$$

**Step 4 — Verify using P-V-T method (vapor portion):**
The low-side volume (suction side) at idle contains vapor at $P_{suc} \approx 2.5\,\text{bar}$, $T \approx 5\,^{\circ}\text{C}$.

$$m_{vapor} = \frac{P V}{Z R T} = \frac{250000 \times 2 \times 10^{-3}}{0.95 \times 81.5 \times 278.15}$$
$$m_{vapor} = \frac{500}{21532} = 0.0232\,\text{kg} = 23.2\,\text{g}$$

**Step 5 — Verify using P-V-T method (high-side liquid + vapor):**
$$\rho_{high} = \frac{P}{Z R T} = \frac{1.4 \times 10^6}{0.012 \times 278.15} \approx 419\,\text{kg/m}^3 \text{ (mixed)}$$
$$\rho_{liq,R134a,40°C} \approx 1117\,\text{kg/m}^3$$

**Step 6 — Conclude operating charge:**
$$m_{tot} \approx m_{low, vap} + m_{high, liq} \approx 0.023 + (0.85 \text{ to } 1.45) \approx 0.87 \text{ – } 1.47\,\text{kg}$$

This **matches the OEM range**, validating both methods.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Superheat Diagnosis of Under-Charge vs. Over-Charge

**Given:** Vehicle running at 1500 rpm, ambient 35 °C, $P_{suc} = 1.7\,\text{bar (gauge)}$, $T_{evap\,out} = 8\,^{\circ}\text{C}$.

**Step 1 — Convert to absolute suction pressure:**
$$P_{suc,abs} = 1.7 + 1.013 = 2.713\,\text{bar}$$

**Step 2 — From R-134a saturation table, find $T_{sat}$ at 2.713 bar:**
$$T_{sat}(2.713\,\text{bar}) \approx -1.5\,^{\circ}\text{C}$$

**Step 3 — Compute superheat:**
$$SH = T_{evap\,out} - T_{sat} = 8 - (-1.5) = 9.5\,^{\circ}\text{C}$$
$$SH \approx 9.5\,^{\circ}\text{C (which is } 17\,^{\circ}\text{F)}$$

**Step 4 — Diagnose:**
Since target SH is **3 – 6 °C (5 – 10 °F)**, this is a **mildly over-charged or TXV stuck-closed condition**. If SH were > 18 °C, the system would be **under-charged**.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Vacuum Hold Time for Moisture Removal

The Boiling Point of Water at **500 microns Hg (0.067 kPa)** is approximately **-27 °C**. To remove adsorbed moisture from desiccant and internal surfaces, the system must be held at this vacuum for sufficient time to **boil off trapped water**.

**Moisture removal rate (semi-empirical):**
$$t_{hold} \geq \dfrac{m_{H_2O}}{\dot{m}_{boil}} \approx 15 \text{ to } 45 \text{ minutes}$$

**Worked example for a 6 L system with 50 mg of trapped moisture:**

**Step 1:** Saturated water vapor density at 0.067 kPa, 25 °C:
$$\rho_{v,H_2O} = 0.023\,\text{kg/m}^3$$

**Step 2:** Free volume of system:
$$V_{free} = 0.006\,\text{m}^3$$

**Step 3:** Maximum water that can be removed per cycle:
$$m_{cycle} = 0.023 \times 0.006 = 1.38 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{kg} = 138\,\text{mg}$$

**Step 4:** Since 138 mg > 50 mg trapped, **one evacuation cycle is sufficient** but must be held ≥ 30 minutes for desiccant-bed diffusion.

**Step 5 — Conclusion:**
$$t_{hold} \geq 30\,\text{min} \Rightarrow \text{moisture} < 50\,\text{ppm (acceptable for R-134a)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Analytical Insight:** Triple evacuation (evac → break vacuum with dry N₂ → evac again) reduces moisture **10× faster** than single evacuation, and is the **OEM-procedure** for systems opened for > 1 hour.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Charging Decision Table

| Symptom | Low-Side P | High-Side P | SH | SC | Diagnosis |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|
| **Normal** | 1.4 – 2.4 bar | 10 – 15 bar | 5 – 10 °F | 10 – 15 °F | Correct charge |
| **Under-charged** | LOW | LOW | HIGH | LOW | Add 0.1 – 0.2 kg vapor |
| **Over-charged** | HIGH | HIGH | LOW | HIGH | Recover excess |
| **No refrigerant** | 0 (vacuum) | 0 (vacuum) | — | — | Leak → recharge |
| **Orifice Tube blocked** | LOW | HIGH | HIGH | HIGH | Replace OT / filter |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Manifold Gauge Set & Service Port Connections

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeR[Refrigerant Cylinder R134a]
    nodeM[Manifold Center Block]
    nodeLG[Low Pressure Gauge Blue]
    nodeHG[High Pressure Gauge Red]
    nodeLV[Low Side Service Port]
    nodeHV[High Side Service Port]
    nodeVP[Vacuum Pump]
    nodeSS[A C System Evaporator Condenser]

    nodeR --> nodeM
    nodeM --> nodeLG
    nodeM --> nodeHG
    nodeLG --> nodeLV
    nodeHG --> nodeHV
    nodeM --> nodeVP
    nodeLV --> nodeSS
    nodeHV --> nodeSS
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Blue gauge** → Low side (suction) → 1.4 – 2.4 bar at idle
- **Red gauge** → High side (discharge) → 10 – 15 bar at idle
- **Center hose** → Common — connects to cylinder, vacuum pump, or recovery unit depending on step
- **Hand valves** (red & blue) control which port is open to the center hose

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Complete Charging Procedure Flow (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    step1[Step 1 Visual Inspection and Identification of Refrigerant Type]
    step2[Step 2 Connect Manifold Gauge Set to High and Low Service Ports]
    step3[Step 3 Leak Test with Nitrogen at 10 to 15 bar for 10 minutes]
    step4[Step 4 Connect Vacuum Pump to Center Hose and Evacuate to 500 microns Hg]
    step5[Step 5 Hold Vacuum for Minimum 30 minutes for Moisture Removal]
    step6[Step 6 Isolate Pump and Check Vacuum Stability for 5 minutes]
    step7[Step 7 Charge Correct PAG Oil Quantity if Replaced]
    step8[Step 8 Liquid Charge from High Side with Engine OFF]
    step9[Step 9 Top Up Vapor Charge from Low Side if Required]
    step10[Step 10 Start Engine A C ON Check Low Side and High Side Pressures]
    step11[Step 11 Measure Superheat 5 to 10 F and Subcooling 10 to 15 F]
    step12[Step 12 Disconnect Manifold and Cap Service Ports]
    step13[Step 13 Document Charge Mass and Performance Data]

    step1 --> step2 --> step3 --> step4 --> step5 --> step6 --> step7 --> step8 --> step9 --> step10 --> step11 --> step12 --> step13
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Step 5 is non-negotiable for KTU Board Answers.** The 30-minute vacuum hold is the single most important quality step. Skipping it gives a non-board-acceptable answer.

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Block Diagram of Refrigerant Mass Distribution in a Steady-State System

```mermaid
graph TD
    blockC[Compressor 30 to 50 g of oil and vapor]
    blockCD[Condenser 60 to 70 percent of total refrigerant as liquid]
    blockRD[Receiver Drier 15 to 20 percent of total refrigerant as liquid]
    blockEV[Evaporator 10 to 15 percent of total refrigerant as vapor]
    blockAC[Accumulator 5 to 10 percent of total refrigerant as vapor plus oil return]
    blockSL[Suction Line 5 percent of total refrigerant as vapor]
    blockLL[Liquid Line 5 percent of total refrigerant as liquid]
```

**Key Takeaway:** The **receiver-drier** acts as the **charge reservoir**. In TXV systems, **all excess charge** above the minimum required to circulate gets stored in the receiver-drier as subcooled liquid. In orifice-tube (CCOT) systems, the **accumulator** serves the opposite function — protecting the compressor from liquid carryover.

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Refrigerant State at Each Point of the A/C Cycle (Indicating Charge Quality)

```mermaid
graph LR
    pt1[Point 1 Compressor Discharge 130 to 150 C Superheated Vapor]
    pt2[Point 2 Condenser Outlet 50 to 60 C Subcooled Liquid]
    pt3[Point 3 TXV or OT Inlet 50 to 60 C Subcooled Liquid]
    pt4[Point 4 Evaporator Outlet 5 to 10 C Superheated Vapor]
    pt5[Point 5 Compressor Suction 8 to 12 C Superheated Vapor]
```

**Reading guide for KTU exam:**
- Points 1, 4, 5 → **Vapor state** (sensible heat transfer only)
- Points 2, 3 → **Liquid state** (latent + subcool)
- Δ(Pt1 − Pt2) and Δ(Pt3 − Pt4) determine **system charge adequacy** via SH and SC checks.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023 Style]**
*Define the term "Superheat" as applied to the evaporator outlet of an automotive A/C system. Why is a 5–10 °F superheat considered ideal?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Superheat is the temperature difference between the **refrigerant vapor temperature at the evaporator outlet** and the **saturation temperature corresponding to the low-side pressure** at that point.

$$SH = T_{evap\,out} - T_{sat}(P_{suc})$$

It is measured in **°C or °F**.

A superheat of **5 – 10 °F (3 – 6 °C)** is ideal because:
1. It guarantees that **only vapor** enters the compressor (no liquid slugging).
2. It ensures the **evaporator is fully utilized** (most surface area is producing latent heat transfer).
3. If SH is lower, the compressor may ingest liquid; if higher, the evaporator is starved and cooling capacity drops.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Style]**
*State three differences between liquid-side charging and vapor-side charging of an automotive A/C system.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per point):**

| # | Liquid-Side Charging | Vapor-Side Charging |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| 1 | Refrigerant enters as **saturated liquid** at the high-side port. | Refrigerant enters as **superheated vapor** at the low-side port. |
| 2 | **Faster** because liquid density is ~10× vapor density. | **Slower** because vapor is bulky. |
| 3 | Requires a **receiver-drier** in the system to buffer the charge. | Can be used on any system, **safer for compressor** (no liquid slug risk). |

---

## 📗 PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **complete procedure for charging an automotive A/C system** using a manifold gauge set after the system has been opened for service. Mention the role of evacuation and moisture removal. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A vehicle's A/C system has the following readings:
Low-side pressure = **1.8 bar (gauge)**, Evaporator outlet temperature = **6 °C**, Ambient = **30 °C**, Compressor speed = **1800 rpm**.
Determine the **superheat** and diagnose whether the system is **under-charged, over-charged, or correctly charged**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for A(a) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** State the goal — "to restore the system to OEM-specified refrigerant and oil charge in a hermetic, moisture-free state."

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Describe pre-charge operations:
- **Leak test** with OFN (oxygen-free nitrogen) at 10–15 bar for 10 min, ΔP ≤ 0.1 bar.
- **Evacuation** to 500 microns Hg using a vacuum pump for ≥ 30 min.
- **Hold test** — isolate pump, ensure pressure does not rise by more than 50 microns in 5 min (proves hermeticity and moisture removal).

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Describe the charging method:
- Connect **center hose to R-134a cylinder** (upright for liquid).
- Open the **high-side hand valve** to charge **~80 % of total charge as liquid**, engine OFF.
- Switch to **low-side vapor charging** for the **final 20 %**, with engine running at 1500 rpm, A/C MAX, blower HIGH.

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Verify performance:
- Manifold pressures: Low 1.4 – 2.4 bar; High 10 – 15 bar (at 30 °C ambient).
- Superheat 5 – 10 °F; Subcooling 10 – 15 °F.
- Center vent air temperature: **3 – 6 °C** at the evaporator outlet.

---

#### **Model Solution for A(b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Convert low-side pressure to absolute.
$$P_{suc,abs} = 1.8 + 1.013 = 2.813\,\text{bar}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Find saturation temperature at 2.813 bar using R-134a saturation tables:
$$T_{sat}(2.813\,\text{bar}) \approx 0\,^{\circ}\text{C}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Apply superheat formula.
$$SH = T_{evap\,out} - T_{sat} = 6 - 0 = 6\,^{\circ}\text{C} = 10.8\,^{\circ}\text{F}$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Compare to target band (5 – 10 °F, i.e., 3 – 6 °C).
- Lower bound: 5 °F = 2.8 °C
- Upper bound: 10 °F = 5.6 °C
- Computed SH = 6 °C → **just above the upper limit**.

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Diagnose.
- SH marginally high + low-side pressure at lower bound of normal → **mildly under-charged**, OR **TXV slightly starved** (if TXV system).
- Recommended action: add 50 – 100 g of R-134a vapor, re-measure SH.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the **three methods of charging refrigerant** into an automotive A/C system. State one advantage and one limitation of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A passenger-car A/C system has an **internal volume of 6 L** and is to be charged with **R-134a**. The system normally holds **0.9 kg of liquid R-134a at 25 °C** plus **0.025 kg of low-side vapor at 2.5 bar and 5 °C**. Calculate the **total operating charge in kg** and the **liquid-fill fraction**. Use $R_{R134a} = 0.0815\,\text{kJ/kg·K}$, $Z = 0.95$, $\rho_{liq} = 1206\,\text{kg/m}^3$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for B(a) — 7 marks**

**Method 1 — Vapor-Side Charging (2 marks)**
- *Procedure:* Connect cylinder to low-side service port with engine OFF; open low-side valve to allow refrigerant to flow as vapor.
- *Advantage:* Safe; cannot slug compressor.
- *Limitation:* Slow; large cylinders may take 30 – 60 min to empty.

**Method 2 — Liquid-Side Charging (2 marks)**
- *Procedure:* Invert cylinder (or use liquid-withdrawal valve), connect to high-side port, charge ~80 % of total.
- *Advantage:* Fast; high mass flow.
- *Limitation:* Risk of liquid slugging if receiver-drier absent; cylinder must have liquid-withdrawal tube.

**Method 3 — R/R/R Machine (Recover–Recycle–Recharge) (3 marks)**
- *Procedure:* Connect SAE J2788 machine; machine performs recovery, recycling, evacuation, leak decay, and charging automatically using electronic scale.
- *Advantage:* Most accurate (±5 g); best for modern R-1234yf and HFO systems; mandatory in many service shops.
- *Limitation:* High equipment cost; requires regular machine calibration.

---

#### **Model Solution for B(b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Given data.
- $V_{sys} = 6 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}^3$
- $m_{liquid} = 0.9\,\text{kg}$ at 25 °C
- $m_{vapor} = ?$ at $P = 250\,\text{kPa}$, $T = 278.15\,\text{K}$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Compute vapor mass using ideal-gas-corrected equation.
$$m_{vapor} = \frac{PV}{ZRT} = \frac{250000 \times V_{vap}}{0.95 \times 81.5 \times 278.15}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Total system volume = 6 L; assume receiver holds 1.5 L, condenser 1.0 L, suction side 3.5 L (vapor region).
$$V_{vap} \approx 3.5 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}^3$$
$$m_{vapor} = \frac{250000 \times 3.5 \times 10^{-3}}{0.95 \times 81.5 \times 278.15} = \frac{875}{21530} = 0.0406\,\text{kg}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Total operating charge.
$$m_{tot} = 0.900 + 0.041 = 0.941\,\text{kg}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Liquid-fill fraction.
$$f_{liq} = \frac{m_{liq}}{m_{tot}} = \frac{0.9}{0.941} = 0.956 = 95.6\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** 95.6 % of the charge is in the **liquid phase** at the off-cycle state. This is the **expected behavior** for a TXV system with a healthy receiver-drier; if the fraction drops below ~85 %, the receiver is empty → risk of vapor at TXV inlet → system under-charged.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Mistakes KTU Students Make in Charging Questions (losing 2–5 marks each):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the 30-minute vacuum hold** — Board expects explicit mention of "evacuate to 500 µm and hold ≥ 30 min."
> 2. **Confusing SH and SC signs** — Superheat = $T_{out} - T_{sat,evap}$; Subcooling = $T_{sat,cond} - T_{liq}$. Reversing signs gives negative answers and 0 marks.
> 3. **Not converting gauge to absolute** — Always add **1.013 bar** to gauge pressure before using saturation tables.
> 4. **Using R-12 properties for R-134a problems** — They are different refrigerants with different P-T curves.
> 5. **Skipping the oil charge discussion** — In KTU 2024 scheme, oil charge carries **1.5–2 marks** by itself. Always mention the "+30 ml per component" rule.

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

- **Definition:** Charging = adding correct mass of refrigerant + correct oil quantity to a hermetic, evacuated, leak-free A/C system.
- **Refrigerants in use:** R-12 (obsolete) → R-134a (current) → R-1234yf (new standard for low GWP).
- **Lubricants:** MO for R-12; **PAG** (Polyalkylene Glycol) for R-134a; PAG/POE for R-1234yf.
- **Pre-Charge 3-step:** Leak Test (N₂ @ 10–15 bar) → Evacuate (500 µm Hg, 30 min) → Oil Charge (+30 ml/component).
- **Charging Methods:** Vapor-side (safe, slow), Liquid-side (fast, requires receiver), R/R/R Machine (best, automated).
- **Superheat Target:** 5 – 10 °F (3 – 6 °C) — protects compressor from liquid slug.
- **Subcooling Target:** 10 – 15 °F (6 – 8 °C) — ensures liquid at TXV inlet.
- **Operating Pressures @ 30 °C ambient:** Low = 1.4 – 2.4 bar (g); High = 10 – 15 bar (g).
- **Typical Charge (R-134a, passenger car):** 0.85 – 1.5 kg.
- **Rule of thumb:** A system that is 5 % under-charged loses 8 – 12 % cooling capacity; 10 % over-charged raises discharge temp by 15 – 25 °C.
- **Critical Constants:** R-134a, $R = 0.0815\,\text{kJ/kg·K}$; $T_{sat}$ @ 0 °C ≈ 2.93 bar abs; minimum vacuum 500 µm = 0.067 kPa.
- **Always remember:** The 30-minute vacuum hold is **the single most important quality step** in KTU 2024 board answers.
- **Always state:** P-T diagnosis using **SH and SC**, not just manifold gauge pressures, for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Servicing Heater System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAUT523 - AUTOMOTIVE REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Servicing Heater System ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Automotive Heater System Servicing** is defined as the systematic set of inspection, diagnostic, maintenance, and repair procedures performed on the cabin heating subsystem of a vehicle, which utilizes waste heat recovered from the engine coolant (or, in diesel/combustion heaters, from fuel combustion) to deliver conditioned warm air to the passenger compartment. The heater system is fundamentally a **secondary heat exchanger** (heater core) coupled to the engine's primary cooling circuit. Servicing encompasses: leak testing, coolant flushing, air-bleeding, thermostat calibration, blower motor testing, control valve actuation checks, and HVAC blend-door calibration. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus:** Module 4 deals with the **servicing** aspect — meaning the student must demonstrate diagnostic reasoning, sequence of operations during servicing, and identification of faulty components. Pure design calculations are NOT the focus; **process orientation and troubleshooting logic** are. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition) Think of a car's heater system like a **chai (tea) strainer suspended over boiling water**: - The boiling water in a pot is your **engine coolant** (heated by the engine's combustion). - The strainer (perforated cup) is your **heater core** — it has small tubes through which hot water flows. - The steam and heat radiating from the pot is **waste heat** that you want to capture. - A small fan blowing across the strainer (in our case, the **blower motor** blowing cabin air across the heater core) carries the heat into the room (cabin). - If the strainer is clogged with old tea residue, no steam comes out — this is exactly what happens when a **heater core is clogged with rust or scale**, leading to "no heat" complaints. - "Servicing" the strainer means cleaning it, checking the handle (hoses), and ensuring the fan is working. In real terms: **the heater does NOT generate heat; it merely transfers waste engine heat to the cabin.** ### 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics > [!NOTE] > **Key Standard Values Used in KTU Examinations:** > - Normal engine coolant operating temperature: **80 °C – 95 °C** > - Minimum acceptable heater outlet air temperature: **40 °C – 50 °C** (at blower medium speed) > - Coolant specific heat (water-ethylene glycol 50:50 mix): **C_p ≈ 3.5 kJ/kg·K** > - Coolant flow rate through heater core: typically **4–8 L/min** > - Blower air flow: **150–300 m³/h** for passenger cars ### 1.4 Classification of Automotive Heaters (KTU Module 4 Prerequisite) | Type | Heat Source | Common Application | |------|-------------|-------------------| | **Hot-Water Heater** | Engine coolant | Petrol/Gasoline cars | | **Exhaust Gas Heater** | Exhaust manifold (air-cooled) | Older Volkswagen Beetles, vans | | **Fuel-Fired/Combustion Heater** | Diesel/gasoline burner | Buses, trucks, EVs (modern) | | **Heat-Pump Heater** | Reverse-cycle refrigeration | Hybrid & EV vehicles | | **PTC Heater** | Electric resistive element | EVs, HEVs (supplementary) | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operating Principle — The "Why" Behind the Heater The internal combustion engine is grossly inefficient — only about **25–35%** of fuel energy is converted to mechanical work. The remaining **65–75%** is waste heat, split roughly as: - **30–35%** carried away by exhaust gases - **30–35%** absorbed by the engine coolant (jacket water) A portion of this coolant is diverted through the **heater core** (a small radiator behind the dashboard). A **blower fan** forces cabin air across the heater core fins, and the air picks up heat by convection. This warm air is ducted into the cabin through vents. ### 2.2 Core Components — "How" Each Functions 1. **Heater Core** — A small radiator (usually aluminum or copper-brass) with tubes and fins. Hot coolant flows in; cabin air passes over it; heat is transferred. 2. **Heater Hoses (Inlet & Outlet)** — Reinforced rubber hoses connecting the engine cooling circuit to the heater core. 3. **Heater Control Valve (Tepid Air Valve / Water Valve)** — Regulates the flow of hot coolant to the heater core. Can be **vacuum-operated, cable-operated, or electronically controlled (solenoid)**. 4. **Blower Motor & Fan** — A 12 V DC motor that forces air across the heater core. Speed is regulated by a **resistor pack** (typically 3–4 fixed speeds) or by a **PWM controller** (modern). 5. **Heater Control Panel / HVAC Module** — User interface (knobs, buttons, touch screen). 6. **Air Blend Door (Mix Door)** — A movable flap that proportions airflow between the heater core (hot) and the evaporator/chilled air (cold) to achieve the desired temperature. 7. **Thermostat (in engine cooling system)** — Ensures coolant reaches ~85 °C before opening to the radiator, ensuring the heater has hot fluid available. 8. **Heater Housing/Plenum** — The plastic casing under the dashboard that mounts the heater core, evaporator, blower, and blend doors. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Examination Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > All formulas below are KTU-exam-grade. Memorize units and boundary conditions. | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---------|-------------|-------| | 1 | $Q = \dot{m} \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Sensible heat transfer by coolant (theoretical heat available) | W | | 2 | $Q = \dot{V} \cdot \rho \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Heat transfer using volumetric flow rate | W | | 3 | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}$ | Heat transfer across heater core (LMTD method) | W | | 4 | $\Delta T_{LMTD} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ | Log Mean Temperature Difference | K | | 5 | $P_{blower} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot \dot{V}^3}{2 \cdot A^2}$ | Blower power (turbo-machine scaling) | W | | 6 | $\eta_{heater} = \dfrac{Q_{delivered}}{Q_{coolant}} \times 100\,\%$ | Heater effectiveness | % | | 7 | $\dot{V}_{coolant} = \dfrac{Q}{\rho \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T_{coolant}}$ | Required coolant flow for given heat output | m³/s | | 8 | $\Delta P_{core} = K \cdot \dfrac{\rho v^2}{2}$ | Pressure drop across clogged heater core (clog → ↑ΔP) | Pa | | 9 | $T_{air,out} = T_{air,in} + \dfrac{Q}{\dot{m}_{air} \cdot C_{p,air}}$ | Outlet air temperature across heater | °C | | 10 | $T_{air,out} = T_{coolant,in} - \Delta T_{approach}$ | Approximation when effectiveness ≈ 1 (ΔT_approach ≈ 5–10 K) | °C | **Where:** - $\dot{m}$ = mass flow rate of coolant (kg/s) - $C_p$ = specific heat at constant pressure (J/kg·K) - $\Delta T$ = temperature difference (K or °C) - $U$ = overall heat transfer coefficient (~50–150 W/m²·K for heater cores) - $A$ = heat transfer surface area (m²) - $\rho$ = density (kg/m³) - $\dot{V}$ = volumetric flow rate (m³/s) - $K$ = loss coefficient (dimensionless) > [!NOTE] > **CRITICAL EXAM TIP (KTU):** When asked "Why is the heater not giving heat even though the engine is hot?", the answer involves $\Delta P_{core}$ rising due to clogging — the thermostat remains closed-circuit equivalent; flow is restricted; the heater runs hot at inlet but cold at outlet. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **EV Cabin Heating:** Traditional coolant heaters are inefficient in EVs (no waste engine heat). Engineers now use **heat-pump systems** and **PTC heaters**. The same servicing principles apply: leak testing, coolant purity, sensor calibration. - **Heavy Vehicles:** Buses and trucks often use **fuel-fired heaters (e.g., Webasto, Eberspächer)** — service includes burner inspection, glow plug testing, and exhaust soot removal. - **Hybrid Vehicles:** Heater systems must work even when the ICE is OFF → supplementary PTC or heat-pump heating. Diagnosing this requires understanding of high-voltage interlock systems (servicing is restricted to certified personnel). ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Heat Delivered by the Heater Core **Problem Statement (Typical KTU Numerical):** A car heater uses engine coolant entering at **90 °C** and leaving at **80 °C**. The coolant (50:50 water-glycol) flow rate is **6 L/min**. Density = **1.05 kg/L**, specific heat $C_p$ = **3.5 kJ/kg·K**. Determine: (a) The heat transferred to the cabin air. (b) The required cabin air flow rate to achieve a temperature rise of 35 °C (air $C_{p,air}$ = 1.005 kJ/kg·K, $\rho_{air}$ = 1.2 kg/m³). **Step 1 — Convert volumetric flow to mass flow:** $$ \dot{V} = 6 \,\dfrac{\text{L}}{\min} = \dfrac{6}{1000 \times 60} \,\dfrac{\text{m}^3}{\text{s}} = 1.0 \times 10^{-4} \,\dfrac{\text{m}^3}{\text{s}} $$ $$ \dot{m}_{coolant} = \dot{V} \times \rho = 1.0 \times 10^{-4} \times 1050 \, \dfrac{\text{kg}}{\text{s}} = 0.105 \,\dfrac{\text{kg}}{\text{s}} $$ Conversion logic: 1 L = 10⁻³ m³, and 1 min = 60 s, so we divide the 6 L/min by 1000 × 60 to obtain m³/s, then multiply by density (1050 kg/m³) to get mass flow. **Step 2 — Apply the heat-transfer equation (sensible heat):** $$ Q = \dot{m} \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T $$ $$ Q = 0.105 \,\dfrac{\text{kg}}{\text{s}} \times 3500 \,\dfrac{\text{J}}{\text{kg·K}} \times (90 - 80) \,\text{K} $$ $$ Q = 0.105 \times 3500 \times 10 = 3675 \,\text{W} \approx 3.68 \,\text{kW} $$ Conversion logic: $C_p$ for 50:50 glycol is approximately 3.5 kJ/kg·K = 3500 J/kg·K. $\Delta T$ in Kelvin equals $\Delta T$ in °C (a 10 K temperature drop is the same as a 10 °C drop). **Step 3 — Determine required air mass flow for 35 °C rise:** For the same Q delivered to the cabin air: $$ \dot{m}_{air} = \dfrac{Q}{C_{p,air} \cdot \Delta T_{air}} = \dfrac{3675}{1005 \times 35} = \dfrac{3675}{35175} $$ $$ \dot{m}_{air} = 0.1045 \,\dfrac{\text{kg}}{\text{s}} $$ **Step 4 — Convert air mass flow to volumetric flow:** $$ \dot{V}_{air} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{air}}{\rho_{air}} = \dfrac{0.1045}{1.2} = 0.0871 \,\dfrac{\text{m}^3}{\text{s}} $$ $$ \dot{V}_{air} = 0.0871 \times 3600 = 313.5 \,\text{m}^3/\text{h} $$ **Interpretation:** A blower moving ~314 m³/h of cabin air is required. This matches typical automotive blowers (150–400 m³/h range), confirming the design is feasible. --- ### 3.2 Derivation: Heater Effectiveness & LMTD **Given:** Heater core area $A = 0.04 \,\text{m}^2$, $U = 100 \,\text{W/m}^2\text{·K}$, coolant enters at 90 °C and leaves at 80 °C, air enters cabin at 20 °C. **Step 1 — Determine temperature differences at the two ends of the heat exchanger:** $$ \Delta T_1 = T_{c,in} - T_{a,out} \quad ; \quad \Delta T_2 = T_{c,out} - T_{a,in} $$ Without iteration, assume $T_{a,out} \approx T_{a,in} + 35 = 55 \,°C$ (from §3.1): $$ \Delta T_1 = 90 - 55 = 35 \,\text{K} \quad ; \quad \Delta T_2 = 80 - 20 = 60 \,\text{K} $$ **Step 2 — Compute LMTD:** $$ \Delta T_{LMTD} = \dfrac{35 - 60}{\ln(35/60)} = \dfrac{-25}{\ln(0.5833)} = \dfrac{-25}{-0.5390} $$ $$ \Delta T_{LMTD} \approx 46.4 \,\text{K} $$ Conversion logic: Since $\Delta T_1 < \Delta T_2$, the formula still works because we take the absolute values; the LMTD is always positive. ln(0.5833) is negative, so the negative signs cancel. **Step 3 — Heat transfer using LMTD method:** $$ Q_{LMTD} = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD} = 100 \times 0.04 \times 46.4 = 185.6 \,\text{W} $$ **Note:** This is far less than the 3675 W calculated in §3.1. The discrepancy indicates that the assumed $A = 0.04 \,\text{m}^2$ is unrealistically small. A real heater core has $A \approx 0.5$–$1.5 \,\text{m}^2$. **Step 4 — Heater effectiveness:** $$ \eta_{heater} = \dfrac{T_{a,out} - T_{a,in}}{T_{c,in} - T_{a,in}} = \dfrac{55 - 20}{90 - 20} = \dfrac{35}{70} = 0.50 = 50\,\% $$ Conversion logic: Effectiveness = 1 means the air leaves at the coolant inlet temperature (impossible due to finite area and finite $U$); 50% is typical and acceptable for a compact heater core. --- ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Symptom → Diagnosis → Service Action | # | Symptom (Customer Complaint) | Probable Cause | Diagnostic Test | Service Action | |---|------------------------------|----------------|-----------------|----------------| | 1 | **No hot air from vents** | Air in cooling system / clogged heater core | Squeeze inlet hose — should be hot; outlet slightly cooler | Bleed air; flush heater core; replace if ΔP too high | | 2 | **Insufficient heat, one side of car cold** | Blend door actuator failure / linkage broken | Listen for actuator; scan HVAC module | Recalibrate blend door; replace actuator | | 3 | **Heater works only at high RPM** | Low coolant level / weak water pump | Check expansion tank; check pump flow | Top up coolant; replace water pump | | 4 | **Sweet smell inside cabin; fogging** | Heater core leak (coolant into cabin) | Visible wet carpet; pressure-test cooling system; UV dye | **Replace heater core** (dashboard may need partial removal) | | 5 | **Blower not working on certain speeds** | Blower resistor pack failure | Measure resistance at each tap | Replace resistor pack | | 6 | **Blower not working at all** | Blower motor failure / fuse / relay | 12 V at motor? Click from relay? | Replace fuse/relay/motor | | 7 | **Heater blows cold intermittently** | Sticking thermostat (engine not reaching 85 °C) | OBD-II coolant temp reading | Replace thermostat | | 8 | **Excessive fan noise** | Debris in blower housing / worn motor bearings | Visual + spin the fan by hand | Clean housing; replace blower assembly | | 9 | **Heater very slow to warm up** | Heater control valve stuck closed | Hose temp at heater core outlet | Replace control valve | | 10 | **Steam from vents** | Severe heater core leak | Visible white vapor + coolant smell | Immediate replacement + refill coolant | > [!TIP] > **KTU examiner loves this matrix-style answer.** When a 7-mark question asks "Discuss the troubleshooting of a heater system," reproduce a similar table — it scores full marks efficiently. --- ### 3.4 Standard Servicing Procedure (Step-by-Step) **Phase A — Preliminary Inspection (No Tools)** 1. Verify customer complaint (when does it occur? idle vs. driving? AC on/off?). 2. Visually inspect coolant level in expansion tank (level between MIN and MAX when cold). 3. Check for visible leaks under dashboard (carpet dampness, coolant odor). 4. Start engine, allow to reach operating temperature; confirm upper radiator hose is hot. **Phase B — Diagnostic Testing (Tools Required)** 5. Use an **OBD-II scanner** to read Engine Coolant Temperature (ECT) — should reach 85–95 °C within 10–15 min of idle. 6. With engine at operating temperature, **squeeze the heater inlet hose** — should be hot and firm (pressurized). 7. Squeeze the **outlet hose** — should be slightly cooler, confirming flow. 8. Use an **infrared thermometer** at the vent to verify outlet air temperature (target > 40 °C). 9. Check blower speeds 1, 2, 3, 4 — listen for each, feel for airflow. **Phase C — Active Servicing** 10. If flow is restricted → **flush the heater core**: - Disconnect inlet/outlet hoses. - Use a garden hose with flushing agent (or a pressurized coolant flush gun). - Flush until clear water emerges; reverse-flush if necessary. 11. **Bleed air** from the cooling system: - Open the bleeder screw (located on the heater hose or thermostat housing). - Top up coolant; run engine with heater valve open and radiator cap loose. - Close bleeder when steady stream of coolant (no bubbles) emerges. 12. **Replace thermostat** if ECT stays below 80 °C after 15 min idle. 13. **Replace blower resistor** if specific speeds fail (typically speed 1 fails first, as it's the highest-resistance position). 14. **Replace heater control valve** if hoses show identical temperature (no flow regulation). 15. **Replace heater core** if leak confirmed (UV dye in coolant reveals fluorescence at HVAC outlets). **Phase D — Verification & Documentation** 16. Re-test all functions; record vent temperatures at each blower speed. 17. Document coolant type used (modern OAT, HOAT, or traditional — never mix). 18. Reset HVAC self-calibration routine (if applicable — some cars require scan-tool recalibration of blend doors). ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Heater System — Block-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR ENG["ENGINE BLOCK
Heat Source 80-95 C"] THERM["THERMOSTAT
Opens at 85 C"] RAD["RADIATOR
Primary Cooler"] HPV["HEATER CONTROL VALVE
Regulates Hot Flow"] HC["HEATER CORE
Secondary Heat Exchanger"] BLO["BLOWER MOTOR and FAN
12 V DC"] BRP["BLOWER RESISTOR PACK
Speed Control"] BLN["BLEND DOOR ACTUATOR
Temperature Mix"] VENT["CABIN VENTS
Outlet Air"] CAB["PASSENGER CABIN"] EXP["EXPANSION TANK
Coolant Reservoir"] WPU["WATER PUMP
Circulates Coolant"] ENG --> WPU WPU --> THERM THERM --> RAD THERM --> HPV HPV --> HC HC --> VENT BLO --> HC BRP --> BLO BLN --> VENT VENT --> CAB RAD --> EXP EXP --> WPU ``` ### 4.2 Heater Servicing — Diagnostic Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD S1["Customer Reports
Heater Fault"] S2{"Cabin Air
Temperature"} S3["Check Coolant Level
and Quality"] S4{"ECT Reaches
85 C within 15 min"} S5["Replace Thermostat"] S6{"Heater Inlet Hose
Hot and Pressurized"} S7["Bleed Air and
Refill Coolant"] S8["Flush Heater Core
or Replace"] S9{"Blower Operating
at All Speeds"} S10["Replace Blower Resistor
or Motor"] S11{"Blend Door
Responding"} S12["Recalibrate Actuator
or Replace"] S13["Heater OK
System Restored"] S1 --> S2 S2 -->|Below 40 C| S3 S3 --> S4 S4 -->|No| S5 S4 -->|Yes| S6 S6 -->|No| S7 S6 -->|Yes| S8 S2 -->|Above 40 C| S9 S9 -->|No| S10 S9 -->|Yes| S11 S11 -->|No| S12 S11 -->|Yes| S13 S8 --> S13 S10 --> S13 S12 --> S13 ``` ### 4.3 Coolant Flow Path in Heater Circuit ```mermaid flowchart LR P1["WATER PUMP
Discharge"] P2["ENGINE BLOCK
Hot Coolant 90 C"] P3["THERMOSTAT
Closed Initially"] P4["HEATER HOSE INLET
To Heater Core"] P5["HEATER CORE
Heat Released"] P6["HEATER HOSE OUTLET
Cooled to 80 C"] P7["RETURN TO PUMP
SUCTION SIDE"] P8["RADIATOR
Bypass Path"] P1 --> P2 --> P3 P3 -->|If T below 85 C| P7 P3 -->|If T above 85 C| P8 P4 --> P5 P5 --> P6 --> P7 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Mermaid compilation note for students:** If a diagram fails to render in your local Markdown viewer, it is almost always due to a reserved word being used as a node ID (e.g., `end`, `graph`, `subgraph`). Always prefix node IDs with letters as shown above. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **Q1. What is a heater core? How does it differ from a radiator?** **Model Answer:** A heater core is a small heat exchanger, similar in construction to a radiator, that uses hot engine coolant to warm the cabin air. It consists of tubes carrying coolant and fins that transfer heat to air blown across them by the blower. **The key differences from a radiator are:** - The heater core is **much smaller** (typically 0.5–1.5 m² area). - It **rejects heat to cabin air** (used for comfort), whereas the radiator rejects heat to ambient air (for engine cooling). - Coolant flow is **diverted** through it via a control valve; the radiator is in the **main** coolant circuit. --- > **[KTU University Exam - June 2024]** **Q2. List any four common symptoms indicating a faulty heater system.** **Model Answer:** 1. **No hot air** from vents despite engine reaching operating temperature. 2. **Insufficient heat** — air is warm but not hot enough. 3. **Sweet/coolant smell** inside the cabin (indicates heater core leak). 4. **Steam or fogging** from vents (coolant vapor entering cabin). 5. **Heater works only at high RPM** (low coolant flow / weak water pump). 6. **Blower not working** on one or more speeds (resistor pack failure). --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]** #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **(a)** Explain the construction and working of an automotive **hot-water heater system** with a neat sketch. Discuss the role of the heater control valve, blower motor, and blend door. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A heater core receives coolant at **92 °C** and discharges it at **82 °C**. The coolant (50:50 glycol-water) flow rate is **8 L/min**. Take $\rho$ = 1050 kg/m³, $C_p$ = 3.5 kJ/kg·K. Calculate: - (i) The rate of heat transfer to the cabin air. - (ii) The required blower air flow (in m³/h) to deliver air at **55 °C** when the cabin inlet air is **20 °C**. Take $\rho_{air}$ = 1.2 kg/m³, $C_{p,air}$ = 1.005 kJ/kg·K. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **(a)** List and explain the **major components of an automotive heater system** and describe the **standard servicing procedure** when a customer reports "no hot air from vents." **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss in detail the **troubleshooting matrix** for a heater system. Explain the diagnostic steps for: (i) coolant leak from heater core, (ii) blower resistor failure, (iii) blend door actuator malfunction, and (iv) stuck thermostat. **(7 marks)** --- ### 5.3 Complete Model Solutions #### **Solution to Question A:** **(a) Construction and Working (7 marks):** The hot-water heater system uses engine coolant as the heat source. The major components are: - **Heater Core:** A compact radiator (aluminium or copper-brass) located behind the dashboard, with small tubes through which hot coolant circulates and fins to maximize surface area for heat transfer to cabin air. - **Heater Hoses:** Two reinforced rubber hoses carry hot coolant from the engine to the heater core (inlet) and return the slightly cooled coolant to the water pump (outlet). - **Heater Control Valve:** Mounted in the inlet hose, this valve regulates the flow of hot coolant entering the heater core. It may be **vacuum-operated** (older cars), **cable-operated**, or **solenoid/Electronically controlled** (modern). When the driver selects "cold," the valve closes (or partially closes), limiting heat transfer. - **Blower Motor and Resistor Pack:** A 12 V DC motor drives a centrifugal fan that forces cabin air across the heater core. The resistor pack (3–4 resistors) provides stepped speed control by varying voltage; modern systems use **PWM (Pulse Width Modulation)** for stepless control. - **Blend Door (Mix Door):** A movable flap in the HVAC housing that proportions airflow between hot (post-heater-core) and cold (post-evaporator) air streams, enabling precise temperature control. - **HVAC Control Module:** The user interface (knobs, touchscreen) and the electronic controller that commands the blend door, blower, and AC compressor. **Working:** Hot coolant from the engine block flows through the inlet hose and control valve into the heater core. The blower fan draws cabin air through the heater core, where it picks up heat by convection. The blend door modulates the air temperature. The cooled coolant returns to the water pump. **(7 marks — full marks for a labelled diagram, listing all 6 components, and explaining the working sequence.)** --- **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):** **Given:** - $T_{c,in}$ = 92 °C, $T_{c,out}$ = 82 °C - $\dot{V}_{coolant}$ = 8 L/min, $\rho_{coolant}$ = 1050 kg/m³ - $C_{p,coolant}$ = 3.5 kJ/kg·K = 3500 J/kg·K - $T_{a,in}$ = 20 °C, $T_{a,out}$ = 55 °C - $\rho_{air}$ = 1.2 kg/m³, $C_{p,air}$ = 1.005 kJ/kg·K **Step 1 — Convert coolant volumetric flow to mass flow:** $$ \dot{V}_{coolant} = \dfrac{8}{1000 \times 60} = 1.333 \times 10^{-4} \,\dfrac{\text{m}^3}{\text{s}} $$ $$ \dot{m}_{coolant} = 1.333 \times 10^{-4} \times 1050 = 0.140 \,\dfrac{\text{kg}}{\text{s}} $$ **Step 2 — Heat transferred (i):** $$ Q = \dot{m}_{coolant} \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T = 0.140 \times 3500 \times (92 - 82) $$ $$ Q = 0.140 \times 3500 \times 10 = 4900 \,\text{W} = 4.9 \,\text{kW} $$ **Step 3 — Required air mass flow (ii):** $$ \dot{m}_{air} = \dfrac{Q}{C_{p,air} \cdot \Delta T_{air}} = \dfrac{4900}{1005 \times (55 - 20)} = \dfrac{4900}{35175} $$ $$ \dot{m}_{air} = 0.1393 \,\dfrac{\text{kg}}{\text{s}} $$ **Step 4 — Convert to volumetric flow:** $$ \dot{V}_{air} = \dfrac{0.1393}{1.2} = 0.1161 \,\dfrac{\text{m}^3}{\text{s}} = 0.1161 \times 3600 = 417.9 \,\text{m}^3/\text{h} $$ **Final Answers:** (i) **Q = 4.9 kW**; (ii) **air flow ≈ 418 m³/h** > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mistakes:** > 1. **Unit conversion error:** Students often forget to convert L/min to m³/s, writing 8 kg/s directly. This loses 1–2 marks. > 2. **Mixing °C and K:** The temperature difference is the SAME in K and °C (Δ10 °C = Δ10 K), but students sometimes use the wrong absolute value. > 3. **Density confusion:** Coolant is denser than pure water (≈1.05 kg/L) — using 1000 kg/m³ introduces ~5% error. > 4. **Forgetting the 3600 multiplier** when converting m³/s to m³/h. > 5. **No units in the final answer:** KTU strictly penalizes missing units. --- #### **Solution to Question B:** **(a) Components & Servicing Procedure (7 marks):** [Refer to the six components listed in Question A solution above, and the four-phase servicing procedure from §3.4 — Preliminary Inspection, Diagnostic Testing, Active Servicing, Verification & Documentation. The 7 marks are awarded for listing all components (3 marks) and detailing the 4-phase procedure (4 marks).] **(b) Troubleshooting Matrix (7 marks):** The four diagnostic cases are best presented as a structured table: **(i) Coolant leak from heater core:** - **Symptom:** Sweet coolant smell inside cabin, damp carpet on front passenger side, fogging on windshield. - **Diagnostic:** UV dye in cooling system reveals fluorescence at HVAC vents; pressure-test cooling system at 1.5 bar and watch for drop. - **Service:** Replace heater core (typically requires partial dashboard disassembly). Refill with specified coolant. Bleed air. **(ii) Blower resistor failure:** - **Symptom:** Blower works only on highest speed (or doesn't work at all). - **Diagnostic:** Measure resistance across each tap of the resistor pack with a multimeter. Open circuit at the failed speed = burnt resistor. - **Service:** Replace blower resistor pack (located in the HVAC housing, accessible from engine bay or under dash). Inspect connector for corrosion. **(iii) Blend door actuator malfunction:** - **Symptom:** One side hot, other side cold; temperature knob does not change output; clicking sounds from behind dashboard. - **Diagnostic:** Use scan tool to command blend door actuation; observe actual vs. commanded position. Listen for actuator motor sound. - **Service:** Recalibrate blend door end-stops via scan tool. If actuator is mechanically failed (stripped gear), replace the actuator motor. Inspect the blend door for broken hinges. **(iv) Stuck thermostat:** - **Symptom:** Heater blows cold air; engine temperature gauge reads low even after 15+ minutes of driving. - **Diagnostic:** OBD-II scan tool reads ECT sensor value (should reach 85–95 °C); if it stays below 75 °C, thermostat is stuck open. Infrared thermometer on upper radiator hose confirms low temperature. - **Service:** Drain coolant, remove thermostat housing, replace thermostat with correct temperature rating (typically 82–88 °C). Refill and bleed. --- ### 5.4 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where Students Typically Lose Marks on Heater-System Questions:** > 1. **Confusing heater core with evaporator** — the heater core uses hot coolant (waste heat), the evaporator uses refrigerant to absorb heat. Many students swap these. > 2. **Forgetting that the heater uses WASTE HEAT** — saying "heater generates heat" is wrong. The heater only transfers existing heat. > 3. **Skipping the bleeding step** — in any servicing answer, always mention bleeding the cooling system after refill. > 4. **Ignoring safety** — cooling system is pressurized and HOT. Always depressurize (cool engine) before opening the radiator cap. KTU examiners reward safety-conscious answers. > 5. **Mixing coolant types** — modern OAT (Organic Acid Technology) coolants must NOT be mixed with traditional IAT (Inorganic Additive Technology). This is a frequently asked 3-mark question. > 6. **Skipping the LMTD derivation** — when asked for "heater effectiveness" or "heat transfer calculation," skipping the LMTD method loses 1–2 marks. --- ### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Servicing Heater System (Module 4):** - ☐ The heater system is a **secondary heat exchanger** that uses **waste engine coolant heat**. - ☐ Main components: **heater core, hoses, control valve, blower motor, resistor pack, blend door, HVAC control module**. - ☐ Normal coolant temperature: **80–95 °C**; minimum vent air: **40–50 °C**. - ☐ Key formula: $Q = \dot{m} C_p \Delta T$ (sensible heat transfer). - ☐ LMTD formula: $\Delta T_{LMTD} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)}$. - ☐ Heater effectiveness typically **40–60%** in real systems. - ☐ **Servicing phases:** Inspection → Diagnostics → Active Service → Verification. - ☐ **Top 5 faults:** clogged core, leak, stuck thermostat, failed blower resistor, blend door actuator failure. - ☐ **Always bleed air** after any cooling system work. - ☐ **Never mix coolant types** (OAT/HOAT/IAT). - ☐ Modern EVs/HEVs use **heat-pump or PTC heaters** — no engine coolant available. - ☐ Pressure-test cooling system at **1.5 bar** to detect leaks. - ☐ **Coolant specific heat (50:50 mix):** 3.5 kJ/kg·K; **density:** ~1050 kg/m³. - ☐ **Coolant flow rate** in heater circuit: 4–8 L/min. - ☐ **Blower air flow rate:** 150–400 m³/h. - ☐ Document every servicing step — KTU practical exams require a service log format.

topics.title: Removing and Replacing Components
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAUT523 - AUTOMOTIVE REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Removing and Replacing Components # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Removing and Replacing Components (R&R)** in an automotive air-conditioning system refers to the standardized set of workshop procedures used to safely detach a faulty or serviceable part (compressor, condenser, evaporator, receiver-drier, expansion device, hoses, etc.) from the refrigerant circuit, and subsequently install a new or rebuilt component while preserving the integrity, cleanliness, oil charge, and charge quantity of the closed-loop vapour-compression system. In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, R&R is a **service-level competence** governed by the hierarchy: 1. **Recovery** of refrigerant (per SAE J2788 / J2843 standards). 2. **Depressurization** and isolation of the sub-system. 3. **Mechanical disconnection** (lines, mounting bolts, electrical connectors, drive belts). 4. **Replacement** with OEM-equivalent components. 5. **Evacuation** (vacuum) of the system to a target absolute pressure of ≤ **500 µm Hg (0.5 Torr)**. 6. **Leak testing** and **recharging** with the correct refrigerant grade and lubricant (PAG/POE oil). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (Module 4):** The examiner expects students to correlate every R&R step with the underlying thermodynamic principle (pressure-temperature relationship, saturation curve, lubricant migration) — not merely memorize a tool list. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of an automotive A/C system as the **human circulatory system**: - The **compressor** is the **heart** — it pumps the "blood" (refrigerant). - The **condenser** and **evaporator** are the **lungs and skin** — they exchange heat with the surroundings. - The **receiver-drier** is the **kidney** — it filters contaminants and moisture. - The **expansion valve** is a **valve in a vein** — it controls the flow rate. - The **hoses and O-rings** are the **blood vessels and valves** — they seal the system. Now imagine a doctor has to perform **open-heart surgery** (replacing the compressor). Before cutting, the surgeon must: 1. Drain the blood (refrigerant recovery). 2. Clamp the vessels (line isolation). 3. Replace the organ (compressor swap). 4. Flush air out (evacuation). 5. Refill the blood to the correct volume (recharge). Performing R&R without following this sequence is like operating without a sterile field — the patient (the A/C system) will develop **contamination, moisture ingress, or compressor metal fatigue** within weeks. ## 1.3 Governing Industry Standards & Physical Constants | Standard / Constant | Value | Significance | |---|---|---| | SAE J2788 | Recovery / Recharge Equipment Specification | Mandates ≥ **95% refrigerant recovery efficiency** | | SAE J2843 | R-1234yf Recovery Equipment Specification | For newer low-GWP refrigerants | | EPA Section 82 | U.S. Federal Ventilation Prohibition | No intentional venting of refrigerant | | Target Vacuum (Evacuation) | ≤ **500 µm Hg** (≈ 66.5 Pa absolute) | Removes non-condensables & moisture | | Holding Test (Vacuum Decay) | System must hold vacuum for **15–30 min** | Verifies absence of leaks | | O-ring Lubricant | **PAG oil** (for R-134a) / **POE oil** (for R-1234yf) | Prevents O-ring tearing during installation | > [!NOTE] > The vacuum target of **500 µm Hg** is not arbitrary — at this pressure, water boils at approximately **−18 °C**, ensuring all moisture is driven out of the desiccant and internal passages before charging. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Universal R&R Workflow (Logic Decomposition) Every component replacement in an automotive A/C system follows the same **6-phase logical sequence**. Understanding *why* each phase exists is more important than memorizing the *how*: ### Phase 1 — Refrigerant Recovery - **Why:** Prevents atmospheric release of HFC/HFO refrigerants (ozone-depletion and global-warming compliance). - **How:** Connect a recovery machine to the high-side and low-side service ports. Run the machine until system pressure drops below **0 psig (gauge)** — typically to a vacuum of **−29 in. Hg**. - **Thermodynamic rationale:** Lowering pressure reduces the boiling point, allowing residual liquid refrigerant to vaporize and be captured. ### Phase 2 — System Isolation & Depressurization - **Why:** Mechanical safety — opening a pressurized circuit can shoot O-rings, manifold caps, or even the technician's hands. - **How:** Close shut-off valves (if present in modern parallel-flow systems) and verify zero pressure on both manifold gauges. ### Phase 3 — Disconnection & Physical Removal - **Why:** Each component has unique fasteners, torque requirements, and electrical interfaces. - **How:** Use **flare-nut wrenches** (not open-end wrenches) on line fittings to avoid rounding. Disconnect electrical connectors at the compressor clutch and pressure switches *before* unbolting. ### Phase 4 — Component-Specific Pre-Installation Checks - **Why:** A new compressor must contain the *correct oil charge* (typically **80–250 ml of PAG oil**, depending on vehicle). A new receiver-drier must be sealed until installation to prevent moisture saturation. - **How:** Measure drained oil from old compressor; refill new compressor to match. ### Phase 5 — Evacuation (Vacuum Pulling) - **Why:** Removes non-condensable gases (N₂, O₂) and moisture. Any residual air will: - Raise discharge temperature (compressor overheating). - Cause acid formation in the oil (moisture + refrigerant → HCl, HF). - **How:** Pull vacuum for a minimum of **30 minutes** at ≤ 500 µm Hg.
