A second-stage pump is recommended. ### Phase 6 — Leak Test, Oil Charge, and Refrigerant Charge - **Why:** Confirms seal integrity before introducing expensive refrigerant. - **How:** Perform an electronic leak search or nitrogen pressure test (to **150–200 psig** for R-134a), then charge refrigerant by **weight** (not by gauge pressure alone). ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Typical Value | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | Refrigerant Charge (Weight Method) | $m_{ref} = V_{system} \times \rho_{ref}$ | 0.6 – 1.2 kg (typical car) | kg | R-134a liquid density ≈ 1207 kg/m³ | | Oil Charge Drained from Compressor | $V_{oil,drained}$ | 80 – 250 ml | ml | Refill new compressor to OEM spec | | Evacuation Time (minimum) | $t_{evac} \geq 30$ min | 30 – 45 | min | Longer for larger systems | | Vacuum Holding Test | $\Delta P_{vac} \leq 50\ \mu m\ Hg$ over 15 min | — | µm Hg | Pass criterion | | Leak Test Pressure (High Side) | $P_{test,HS}$ | 150 – 200 | psig | Dry nitrogen only | | Torque — Compressor Bolts | $T_{comp}$ | 20 – 30 | N·m | Per OEM spec | | Torque — Flare Nuts (3/8″) | $T_{flare,3/8}$ | 14 – 18 | N·m | Two-wrench technique | | Torque — Flare Nuts (1/2″) | $T_{flare,1/2}$ | 20 – 25 | N·m | — | | Torque — O-ring Service Port Caps | $T_{cap}$ | 1.5 – 3.0 | N·m | Low torque — brass threads | | Saturation Pressure (R-134a @ 25 °C) | $P_{sat}$ | 6.7 | bar (≈ 97 psig) | Used as “static” reference | | Discharge Temperature Limit | $T_{disch,max}$ | 120 | °C | Above → air ingress suspected | | Suction Superheat | $\Delta T_{sh} = T_{evap,out} - T_{sat}(P_{evap})$ | 5 – 12 | °C | Diagnostic after recharge | | Low-Pressure Cutoff | $P_{LP,cutoff}$ | 1.7 – 2.4 | bar | Cycles compressor off | > [!IMPORTANT] > Always use **two wrenches** in opposing directions (backing wrench + drive wrench) on flare fittings. Using a single wrench transmits torque to the line, causing fatigue failure and micro-leaks. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility The R&R procedure is not merely a workshop skill — it is the **practical embodiment of the Second Law of Thermodynamics** in service engineering: - *Recovery* protects the **environment** (entropy of mixing). - *Evacuation* protects the **compressor** (vapour compression cleanliness). - *Leak testing* protects the **owner** (refrigerant cost ≈ ₹1500–3000/kg for R-134a, ₹4000–6000/kg for R-1234yf). In modern EV and hybrid vehicles, where the A/C system is also a **thermal management loop** for the battery pack, a single contaminated component can degrade battery cooling and range — elevating R&R from a comfort-service task to a *critical-safety* procedure. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Procedures & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Compressor Removal and Replacement The compressor is the **most complex and most frequently replaced** A/C component. KTU examiners frequently target this procedure. ### A. Pre-Removal Diagnostic Checks 1. Verify refrigerant charge via manifold gauges. Normal static pressure at 25 °C ambient: $$P_{static} \approx 6.7\ \text{bar (gauge)}$$ 2. Inspect drive belt tension and clutch air gap (typical gap: **0.4 – 0.8 mm**). 3. Confirm electrical fault codes (P0645, P0530 series). ### B. Step-by-Step Removal | Step | Action | Technical Note | |---|---|---| | 1 | Connect recovery machine to HP and LP service ports | Per SAE J2788 | | 2 | Run recovery until gauge reads ≤ **−29 in. Hg** | Liquid + vapour captured | | 3 | Disconnect battery negative terminal | Electrical safety | | 4 | Remove drive belt (serpentine) using a breaker bar on the tensioner | Relieves tension | | 5 | Disconnect compressor clutch electrical connector | Note routing for reinstallation | | 6 | Unbolt suction and discharge line fittings using **two flare-nut wrenches** | Cap open lines immediately | | 7 | Remove mounting bolts (typically 3 or 4) | Record torque value | | 8 | Lift compressor out; **drain and measure oil volume** | Critical for refill spec | ### C. Oil Charge Calculation The total PAG oil charge in the system is split between: - Oil retained in the new compressor (factory pre-fill). - Oil circulating in the rest of the system. Let the parameters be: - $V_{drained}$ = oil drained from the *old* compressor (ml). - $V_{new,pre}$ = oil already in the *new* compressor (ml, marked on label). - $V_{total,OEM}$ = OEM-specified total oil charge (ml, from service manual). The **net oil to be added to the new compressor** is computed as: $$ \begin{aligned} V_{add} &= V_{total,OEM} - V_{new,pre} \\ &= V_{drained} \quad \text{(if new compressor is shipped dry)} \end{aligned} $$ > **Example (KTU-style numerical):** > A vehicle's OEM oil specification is 220 ml. The old compressor drained 180 ml. The new compressor is shipped with 80 ml pre-fill. > $V_{add} = 220 - 80 = 140\ \text{ml of PAG oil to be added before installation.}$ ### D. Reinstallation Steps 1. Pour the calculated $V_{add}$ of fresh PAG oil into the new compressor suction port. 2. Hand-rotate the shaft **15–20 revolutions** to distribute oil internally. 3. Replace all O-rings with **new, lubricant-coated** ones (compatible with refrigerant type). 4. Bolt the compressor to the bracket; torque to $T_{comp} = 20$–30 N·m in a *criss-cross sequence*. 5. Reconnect discharge line first, then suction line (or vice versa per OEM). 6. Reinstall the drive belt. 7. Proceed to **evacuation and charging** (universal for all component R&R). ### E. Post-Installation Verification - Static pressure after ≥ 1 hour of stabilization: $$P_{static} \approx P_{sat}(T_{amb}) \pm 0.3\ \text{bar}$$ - Run compressor; verify low-side ≈ 1.7–2.4 bar and high-side ≈ 12–16 bar at 1500 RPM, 25 °C ambient. - Measure **suction superheat**: $$\Delta T_{sh} = T_{evap,out} - T_{sat}(P_{LP})$$ Acceptable: $5\ \text{°C} \leq \Delta T_{sh} \leq 12\ \text{°C}$. --- ## 3.2 Condenser Removal and Replacement The condenser is typically located at the **front of the radiator** and is therefore vulnerable to stone-chip damage and corrosion. ### Procedure 1. Recover refrigerant. 2. Remove the front grille and/or radiator fan shroud for access. 3. **Flush the condenser** *before* removal if it is suspected of internal blockage (a blocked condenser is a symptom, not a cause — find the root cause, e.g., debris from a failed compressor). 4. Disconnect inlet (discharge from compressor) and outlet (to receiver-drier) lines. 5. Unbolt condenser from its cradle; tilt it out carefully to avoid bending fins. 6. **Inspect parallel-flow tubes** for micro-leaks; an oil-streaked area indicates leakage. 7. Install new condenser with **new O-rings**; torque line fittings to $T_{flare} = 14$–25 N·m based on size. 8. Add oil compensation: parallel-flow condensers retain **30–60 ml** of oil by design. If replacing, add this amount to the system's oil total. 9. Replace the **receiver-drier** simultaneously (industry best practice — see §3.3 for justification). --- ## 3.3 Receiver-Drier Removal and Replacement The receiver-drier is a **non-repairable** service item containing a desiccant (molecular sieve, typically **3 Å or 4 Å zeolite**) and a filter screen. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Golden Rule:** Whenever the system is opened to atmosphere for more than a few minutes (e.g., condenser replacement), the receiver-drier **must** be replaced. Its desiccant becomes saturated with moisture and cannot be regenerated. ### Procedure 1. Recover refrigerant. 2. Locate the receiver-drier (usually on the liquid line between condenser and expansion valve, often in front of the radiator). 3. Disconnect inlet and outlet lines. 4. Unbolt the mounting clamp. 5. **Note the flow direction arrow** stamped on the new drier body. 6. Install with new O-rings; torque to OEM spec. 7. Evacuate and recharge. ### Desiccant Saturation Logic The water-holding capacity of a typical receiver-drier is approximately: $$m_{H_2O,cap} \approx 5\ \text{grams}$$ Once this threshold is exceeded, free water reacts with PAG oil and refrigerant: $$H_2O + R\text{-}134a \rightarrow HF + HCl + \text{organic acids}$$ These acids attack compressor windings and copper lines — hence the **mandatory replacement** policy. --- ## 3.4 Evaporator Removal and Replacement The evaporator is the **most labour-intensive** component to access, as it is housed inside the HVAC plenum (behind the dashboard). ### Procedure 1. Recover refrigerant. 2. Discharge the HVAC system refrigerant *and* drain the condensate drain. 3. Remove the dashboard or HVAC housing (varies by vehicle — often 2–6 hours of labour). 4. Disconnect expansion valve (TX valve) or orifice tube connections. 5. Disconnect the blower motor wiring (if co-located). 6. Remove the evaporator core from the housing. 7. Inspect the **drain tube** for blockage; clear or replace if needed. 8. Install new evaporator; replace the expansion device **simultaneously** (best practice). 9. Replace all HVAC seals and gaskets. > [!NOTE] > Evaporator R&R is a frequent **Kerala monsoon-season** issue due to microbial growth ("dirty sock syndrome") caused by condensate drainage failure. --- ## 3.5 Expansion Device (TXV / Orifice Tube) Removal and Replacement ### Thermal Expansion Valve (TXV) - Mounted at the evaporator inlet. - **Always replace** the inlet screen/filter when the TXV is serviced. - Add a **bulb clamp** securely on the suction line at the evaporator outlet (poor clamp → hunting → low suction superheat → liquid slugging). ### Orifice Tube (Fixed Restriction) - Located in the liquid line at the evaporator inlet. - Colour-coded by capacity (white, blue, red, etc.). - Inserted *arrow-forward* (in direction of flow). - Always replace O-rings on both ends of the orifice tube housing. --- ## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: When to Replace vs. Repair | Component | Typical Failure Mode | Repair or Replace? | Oil Compensation Required? | |---|---|---|---| | Compressor | Mechanical seizure, clutch burn-out | Replace (rebuild possible but uncommon) | Yes — full $V_{total,OEM}$ adjustment | | Condenser | Internal blockage, corrosion, leak | Replace | Yes — 30–60 ml | | Evaporator | Leak (corrosion), microbial growth | Replace | Yes — 40–80 ml | | Receiver-Drier | Desiccant saturation | **Always replace** when system opened | No | | TXV | Stuck open/closed, hunting | Replace | No | | Orifice Tube | Plugging | Replace | No | | Hoses / Lines | Permeation, abrasion, corrosion | Replace | No (drain residual oil) | | Pressure Switch | Open/short circuit | Replace | No | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Universal R&R Workflow Flowchart The following Mermaid diagram captures the **decision-tree logic** a technician must follow for *any* component R&R. Note the iterative loop on the leak/vacuum test — this is a critical exam point. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: Diagnose Faulty Component] --> B[Identify Refrigerant Type R-134a or R-1234yf] B --> C[Connect Recovery Machine to HP and LP Ports] C --> D[Run Recovery until System Pressure less than -29 in Hg] D --> E{Quality of Recovered Refrigerant Acceptable} E -- No --> F[Discard to Recovery Cylinder for Reclamation] E -- Yes --> G[Store in Reusable Cylinder] F --> H[Disconnect Battery Negative Terminal] G --> H H --> I[Remove Drive Belt and Electrical Connectors] I --> J[Unbolt Component and Disconnect Line Fittings with Two Wrenches] J --> K[Cap All Open Lines Immediately] K --> L[Drain and Measure Oil from Old Component] L --> M[Compare Drained Oil to OEM Specification] M --> N[Install New Component with Fresh O-Rings and Correct Oil Charge] N --> O[Torque All Fittings to OEM Specification] O --> P[Reconnect Electrical and Belt] P --> Q[Connect Manifold and Vacuum Pump] Q --> R[Pull Vacuum to less than 500 micrometre Hg] R --> S[Hold Vacuum for 30 Minutes minimum] S --> T{Vacuum Holds without Pressure Rise} T -- No --> U[Leak Test with Electronic Detector or Nitrogen] U --> V[Repair Leak Point] V --> R T -- Yes --> W[Charge Refrigerant by Weight] W --> X[Leak Test All Fittings] X --> Y[Verify Static and Running Pressures] Y --> Z[Document Service and Return Vehicle] ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the A/C Refrigerant Circuit This diagram shows the *interactions* between components and the R&R access points — a Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow (used as a fallback for the physical schematic, which is too complex to render in Mermaid). ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Refrigerant_Circuit CMP[Compressor
R and R Access: Drive Belt, Clutch, Lines, Bolts] CON[Condenser
R and R Access: Front of Radiator, Inlet and Outlet Lines] RD[Receiver Drier
R and R Access: Liquid Line, Mounting Clamp] EXP[Expansion Device
TXV or Orifice Tube
R and R Access: Evaporator Inlet] EVP[Evaporator
R and R Access: HVAC Plenum, Drain Tube] end CMP -->|Discharge Hot High Pressure Vapour| CON CON -->|High Pressure Subcooled Liquid| RD RD -->|Filtered Dry Liquid| EXP EXP -->|Low Pressure Two Phase Mixture| EVP EVP -->|Low Pressure Superheated Vapour| CMP EXT1[Environment Heat Sink] -.->|Heat Rejected| CON EXT2[Cabin Heat Load] -.->|Heat Absorbed| EVP EXT3[Moisture and Contaminants] -.->|Captured by Desiccant| RD EXT4[Compressor Oil] -.->|Circulates with Refrigerant| CMP classDef rnr fill:#ffe4b5,stroke:#d2691e,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class CMP,CON,RD,EXP,EVP rnr ``` ## 4.3 Component-Specific R&R Access Map (Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph R_and_R_Access_Matrix direction TB N1[Compressor] --- N1a[Tools: Flare Wrench Set, Torque Wrench, Oil Measure] N2[Condenser] --- N2a[Tools: Flare Wrench, Radiator Fan Tools] N3[Receiver Drier] --- N3a[Tools: Line Wrenches, New O-Rings] N4[Expansion Valve] --- N4a[Tools: Allen Keys, Bulb Clamp Tool] N5[Evaporator] --- N5a[Tools: Dashboard Removal Kit, Plenum Tools] N6[Hoses] --- N6a[Tools: Hose Clamp Pliers, Spring Lock Coupler Tool] end ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Why must the receiver-drier be replaced every time the refrigerant system is opened to atmosphere for a service procedure?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The receiver-drier contains a desiccant (molecular sieve) that absorbs moisture from the refrigerant. Once exposed to atmospheric air, the desiccant becomes saturated with water (its capacity is only about **5 grams**). When the system is recharged, this free moisture reacts with R-134a and PAG oil to form **hydrochloric acid (HCl) and hydrofluoric acid (HF)**, which corrode the compressor, copper lines, and metallic seals — leading to premature failure. Additionally, the filter screen inside the drier traps metallic debris from a failing compressor, which would otherwise re-enter the new compressor. Therefore, replacement of the receiver-drier is **mandatory** after any system opening. **(3 marks)** ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **State the standard vacuum level and minimum holding time required during evacuation of an automotive A/C system before charging.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The evacuation must pull the system down to a vacuum of **≤ 500 µm Hg (≈ 0.5 Torr or 66 Pa absolute)**, and this vacuum must be held for a **minimum of 30 minutes** (preferred 45 minutes) with no measurable pressure rise. The vacuum serves three purposes: (i) removal of non-condensable gases such as N₂ and O₂, (ii) removal of residual moisture by lowering the boiling point of water to below 0 °C, and (iii) internal leak-check of the system before introducing expensive refrigerant. A two-stage rotary vane vacuum pump is recommended to achieve sub-atmospheric moisture removal. **(3 marks)** --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice ### Question A (14 Marks) — Compressor-Focused `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 | Module 4 | 14 Marks]` **(a) [7 marks]** Describe, with a logical sequence, the step-by-step procedure for **replacing the compressor** in an automotive R-134a air-conditioning system. Highlight the role of oil charge calculation in your answer. **(b) [7 marks]** A vehicle's OEM-specified total PAG oil charge is **240 ml**. During compressor removal, **180 ml of oil** is drained from the failed compressor. The replacement compressor is supplied with a **pre-fill of 60 ml**. Calculate the volume of additional PAG oil to be added before installation. Also state the consequences of over-oiling and under-oiling the system. --- #### Model Solution to Q-A (a) **Step 1 — Refrigerant Recovery (1 mark):** Connect a recovery machine (SAE J2788 compliant) to both high-side and low-side service ports. Run the machine until manifold pressure reads below **−29 in. Hg**, ensuring ≥ 95% refrigerant capture. This protects the environment and prevents refrigerant loss. **Step 2 — System Isolation (1 mark):** Verify zero pressure on both gauges. Disconnect the vehicle's battery negative terminal to prevent clutch coil energization during work. **Step 3 — Belt and Electrical Disconnection (1 mark):** Release the serpentine belt using a breaker bar on the automatic tensioner. Disconnect the compressor clutch coil electrical connector. Note the routing for reinstallation. **Step 4 — Line Disconnection (1 mark):** Use **two flare-nut wrenches** (a backing wrench on the line nut and a drive wrench on the compressor fitting) to loosen and remove the suction and discharge line fittings. Immediately cap all open lines and ports with clean plastic caps to prevent moisture and dirt ingress. **Step 5 — Compressor Unbolting and Removal (1 mark):** Remove the compressor mounting bolts (typically 3 or 4, torque 20–30 N·m). Lift the compressor out. Position it suction-port-up and drain the oil into a graduated container; **measure and record the volume**. **Step 6 — Oil Charge Adjustment (1 mark):** Calculate the net oil to be added to the new compressor (see Q-A (b) for the calculation method). Add the correct amount of fresh PAG oil through the suction port. **Hand-rotate the shaft 15–20 revolutions** to distribute the oil internally and pre-lubricate the bearings. **Step 7 — Reinstallation and Evacuation (1 mark):** Install with new lubricated O-rings, torque mounting bolts and line fittings in a criss-cross sequence, reconnect clutch wiring and belt. Connect vacuum pump and evacuate to ≤ 500 µm Hg for ≥ 30 minutes. Then leak-test and recharge by weight to OEM specification (typically 600–900 g of R-134a). Run the system and verify low-side 1.7–2.4 bar, high-side 12–16 bar at 1500 RPM and 25 °C ambient. **(Total: 7 marks — 1 mark per step with clear reasoning.)** --- #### Model Solution to Q-A (b) **Step 1 — Identify Given Data:** - Total OEM oil charge: $V_{total,OEM} = 240$ ml. - Oil drained from old compressor: $V_{drained} = 180$ ml. - Pre-fill in new compressor: $V_{new,pre} = 60$ ml. **Step 2 — Apply the Oil Balance Equation:** $$ \begin{aligned} V_{add} &= V_{total,OEM} - V_{new,pre} \\ &= 240\ \text{ml} - 60\ \text{ml} \\ &= 180\ \text{ml} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Cross-Verification:** The volume of oil to be added (180 ml) equals the volume drained from the old compressor (180 ml) because the new compressor was supplied with the *same pre-fill* as the original factory charge. This is a typical coincidence when a like-for-like replacement is performed. **Answer: Add 180 ml of fresh PAG oil to the new compressor before installation.** **Step 4 — Consequences of Incorrect Oil Charge (3 marks split):** - **Under-oiling (oil charge too low):** Insufficient lubrication of compressor bearings → metal-to-metal contact → premature compressor seizure. Oil is also the lubricant for the expansion valve's moving parts (in TXV systems); under-oiling causes valve sticking and erratic superheat control. - **Over-oiling (oil charge too high):** Excess oil circulates with the refrigerant and accumulates in the **evaporator** as a viscous coating, reducing heat-transfer efficiency and producing poor cabin cooling. It can also cause the suction line to foam, leading to liquid slugging of the compressor. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Evacuation and Charging-Focused `[KTU University Exam - May 2023 | Module 4 | 14 Marks]` **(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **six-phase universal workflow** for removing and replacing any major component of an automotive A/C system, with the underlying thermodynamic reasoning at each phase. **(b) [7 marks]** An automotive A/C service station evacuates a system to a vacuum of **400 µm Hg** and holds it for 30 minutes. The pressure rises to **550 µm Hg** within 10 minutes. Diagnose the situation, list the possible causes, and outline the corrective steps. --- #### Model Solution to Q-B (a) **Phase 1 — Refrigerant Recovery (1 mark):** Recovery is mandated by environmental law (EPA Section 82 in the U.S.; equivalent CPCB norms in India) and prevents atmospheric release of high-GWP refrigerants. Thermodynamically, the recovery machine creates a low-pressure environment, lowering the refrigerant's boiling point and forcing it to vaporize and be captured. Recovery must continue until the system pressure falls below **−29 in. Hg**. **Phase 2 — Depressurization & Isolation (1 mark):** Confirming zero gauge pressure ensures no residual stored energy exists in the system. This protects the technician from projectile O-rings or fittings and prevents sudden depressurization events. **Phase 3 — Mechanical Disconnection (1 mark):** Use proper tooling (flare-nut wrenches, line caps, torque wrenches). Each connection point is a potential leak site, so cleanliness and O-ring integrity are critical. Electrical connectors must be disconnected *before* mechanical unbolting to avoid short circuits and clutch damage. **Phase 4 — Pre-Installation Preparation (1 mark):** The new component must be inspected for correct oil pre-fill, undamaged O-ring seats, and protective packaging integrity. Oil compensation (if the new component is shipped dry or under-filled) is performed at this stage. Shaft-rotation distributes oil and pre-lubes bearings. **Phase 5 — Evacuation (1 mark):** A two-stage vacuum pump pulls the system to **≤ 500 µm Hg**. The low absolute pressure causes dissolved water to boil off at room temperature, removing moisture. A 30-minute holding test checks for pressure rise that would indicate either a leak or trapped moisture re-evaporating. **Phase 6 — Leak Test, Oil Compensation & Charge (1 mark):** A nitrogen pressure test (150–200 psig) confirms seal integrity. Refrigerant is then charged **by weight** (not by gauge pressure alone, because ambient temperature strongly affects static pressure). Final verification involves measuring running pressures and superheat to confirm system performance. **Engineering Justification (1 mark):** The entire sequence enforces the **closed-loop assumption** of the vapour-compression cycle. Any breach of the loop (air ingress, moisture, debris) breaks the assumption and degrades COP, life, and reliability. Hence the workflow is not procedural ritual but a thermodynamic necessity. --- #### Model Solution to Q-B (b) **Step 1 — Calculate Pressure Rise:** $$\Delta P = 550 - 400 = 150\ \mu m\ Hg$$ The **acceptable rise over 30 minutes is ≤ 50 µm Hg** (per industry practice). A rise of 150 µm Hg within only 10 minutes is a **strong indicator of either a leak or moisture boil-off**. **Step 2 — Diagnostic Decision Matrix:** | Possible Cause | Diagnostic Test | |---|---| | External air leak (fitting, hose, O-ring) | Pressurize with dry N₂ to 150 psig and use electronic leak detector or soap solution | | Internal leak (compressor shaft seal, evaporator) | Listen for hissing; UV dye inspection | | Trapped moisture boiling off | Conduct a 2-stage evacuation: first pull to ~ 5000 µm Hg for 15 min, then to ≤ 500 µm Hg for 30 min | | Faulty vacuum pump (poor ultimate vacuum) | Test pump with a blanked-off line — should reach ≤ 50 µm Hg | | Contaminated service hoses (permeation) | Replace hoses | **Step 3 — Corrective Actions (2 marks):** 1. Release the vacuum by bleeding in a small amount of dry nitrogen to bring the system to atmospheric pressure. 2. Perform an **electronic leak search** on all fittings, the compressor shaft seal, the condenser, and the evaporator. Repair any identified leak. 3. Re-evacuate using a **two-stage method**: first at 25,000 µm Hg for 15 min to remove bulk moisture, then at ≤ 500 µm Hg for 30 min. Replace the receiver-drier to ensure full moisture absorption capacity. 4. Verify the vacuum holds (≤ 50 µm Hg rise over 30 min). 5. Charge refrigerant by weight and verify running pressures. **Step 4 — Final Note (1 mark):** A leaking system must *never* be charged with refrigerant, as the leak will not only waste expensive refrigerant (₹1500–3000/kg for R-134a) but also violate environmental regulations. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in Module 4 R&R Questions:** > 1. **Omitting refrigerant recovery** in the first step of a replacement procedure (loss of 1 mark). > 2. **Using a single open-end wrench** on flare fittings (loss of 1 mark for the two-wrench technique). > 3. **Forgetting to replace the receiver-drier** when the condenser or evaporator is replaced. > 4. **Charging by gauge pressure** instead of by **weight** (shows thermodynamic misunderstanding). > 5. **Skipping the vacuum holding test** — KTU examiners specifically check for the 30-minute hold and 50 µm Hg criterion. > 6. **Confusing TXV and orifice tube** replacement procedures (TXV requires a bulb clamp; orifice tube is directional). > 7. **Omitting oil compensation** for the condenser (30–60 ml) and evaporator (40–80 ml) replacements. > 8. **Reusing old O-rings** — a strict 0-mark issue in R&R answers. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > 📋 **Rapid Revision Checklist** - **R&R is governed by a strict 6-phase workflow:** Recovery → Isolation → Disconnection → Pre-installation → Evacuation → Leak Test & Charge. - **Refrigerant recovery is mandatory** (SAE J2788 / J2843) and must reach **≤ −29 in. Hg** before opening any system fitting. - **Evacuation target: ≤ 500 µm Hg** for **≥ 30 minutes**; pass criterion is **≤ 50 µm Hg rise** during the hold test. - **Always use two wrenches** on flare fittings (backing + drive) to avoid line fatigue. - **Always use new O-rings**, lubricated with the correct oil grade (PAG for R-134a; POE for R-1234yf). - **Always replace the receiver-drier** when the system is opened; its desiccant is non-regenerable. - **Oil charge balance equation:** $V_{add} = V_{total,OEM} - V_{new,pre}$. - **Compressor mounting torque:** **20–30 N·m**; **flare-nut torque:** **14–25 N·m** (depending on size). - **Charge refrigerant by weight**, not by gauge pressure. - **Under-oil → compressor seizure; over-oil → poor cooling & liquid slugging.** - **Condenser R&R** typically requires **30–60 ml** oil compensation; **evaporator R&R** requires **40–80 ml**. - **TXV R&R** requires a properly clamped sensing bulb on the suction line; **orifice tube** must be installed in the correct flow direction. - **Post-charge verification:** low-side ≈ 1.7–2.4 bar; high-side ≈ 12–16 bar at 1500 RPM, 25 °C ambient. - **Superheat check:** $\Delta T_{sh} = T_{evap,out} - T_{sat}(P_{LP})$; acceptable range is **5–12 °C**. - **R-1234yf systems** require **POE oil**, special low-permeation hoses, and **SAE J2843** recovery equipment. - **Kerala-specific pitfall:** Monsoon-driven microbial growth in evaporators — always inspect and clear the condensate drain tube during R&R. ---

topics.title: Troubleshooting of Air Conditioning System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAUT523 - AUTOMOTIVE REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Troubleshooting of Air Conditioning System # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Troubleshooting of an Air Conditioning System** is the systematic, evidence-based diagnostic process used to identify, isolate, and rectify faults in an automotive vapour-compression refrigeration cycle by correlating measured parameters (suction/discharge pressures, superheat, subcooling, vent air temperatures) with the established thermodynamic and mechanical performance benchmarks of the system. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, troubleshooting integrates **refrigerant cycle diagnostics**, **mechanical component inspection**, and **electrical circuit testing** to restore the system to its design Coefficient of Performance (COP) and deliver specified cabin comfort conditions. The primary diagnostic tools include: - **Manifold Gauge Set** (High-pressure and Low-pressure gauges) - **Thermometer / Thermocouple** (for vent, ambient, and line temperatures) - **Electronic Leak Detector** (for refrigerant escape) - **Multimeter / Test Lamp** (for clutch, relay, and switch circuits) > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping (Module 4):** This topic directly addresses the learning outcomes related to *diagnostic procedures*, *performance evaluation*, and *refrigerant handling safety* in automotive HVAC systems. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Doctor Visit" for an AC System Imagine your car's AC system as a **patient visiting a doctor**: | AC System | Human Body Analogy | |-----------|-------------------| | Refrigerant (R-134a / R-1234yf) | Blood | | Compressor | Heart | | Condenser | Lungs (releases heat) | | Expansion Valve | Liver (regulates flow) | | Evaporator | Skin (absorbs heat) | | Manifold Gauges | Blood Pressure Monitor | | Cabin Vent Temperature | Body Temperature Reading | Just as a doctor checks blood pressure, pulse, and temperature to diagnose an illness, an AC technician measures **high-side pressure, low-side pressure, and vent temperature** to "diagnose" the system's health. **Abnormal readings = Symptoms → Root cause analysis → Repair.** This is the essence of troubleshooting. ## 1.3 Why Troubleshooting is Critical in Automotive HVAC > [!IMPORTANT] > An improperly diagnosed automotive AC fault can lead to **compressor seizure**, **refrigerant contamination**, and **electrical damage** — escalating a ₹2,000 service bill into a ₹25,000+ repair. Systematic troubleshooting prevents cascading failures. ## 1.4 Standard Reference Metrics (Industry-Standard Norms) For a properly charged R-134a automotive AC at **ambient = 35 °C** and blower on **HIGH** with doors/windows closed: - **Low-side (Suction) Pressure:** **25 – 45 psi (≈ 1.7 – 3.1 bar)** - **High-side (Discharge) Pressure:** **150 – 250 psi (≈ 10.3 – 17.2 bar)** - **Center Vent Air Temperature:** **3 °C to 8 °C** (with humidity below 70 %) - **Compressor Clutch Engagement Temperature:** Above **3 °C – 5 °C** ambient # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Diagnostic Triad: Pressure – Temperature – Superheat Every troubleshooting exercise in KTU examinations revolves around the **interdependence of three measurable parameters**. If you understand this triangle, you can diagnose **90 % of AC faults**. ### 2.1.1 Pressure-Temperature (P-T) Relationship Refrigerant saturation temperature is **uniquely determined** by its pressure for a given fluid (R-134a, R-1234yf). A refrigerant's P-T chart (or saturation table) is the technician's "Rosetta Stone." - **If measured pressure = 30 psi → R-134a saturation T ≈ −6 °C** (low side → evaporator condition) - **If measured pressure = 200 psi → R-134a saturation T ≈ 47 °C** (high side → condenser condition) ### 2.1.2 Superheat (SH) $$\text{Superheat} = T_{\text{suction line at compressor inlet}} - T_{\text{saturation at suction pressure}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Why it matters:** Superheat guarantees that **only vapor** enters the compressor (no liquid slugging). Typical target: **5 °C to 10 °C** superheat at the compressor inlet. ### 2.1.3 Subcooling (SC) $$\text{Subcooling} = T_{\text{saturation at condenser pressure}} - T_{\text{liquid line at receiver-drier outlet}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Why it matters:** Subcooling confirms that the **condensate is fully liquid** before reaching the expansion valve. Typical target: **3 °C to 8 °C** subcooling at the receiver-drier outlet. ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Parameter | Formula | Typical Automotive Range | Significance | |---|-----------|---------|--------------------------|--------------| | 1 | **Superheat (SH)** | $SH = T_{\text{line}} - T_{\text{sat}}(P_{\text{low}})$ | **5 – 10 °C** | Prevents liquid slugging of compressor | | 2 | **Subcooling (SC)** | $SC = T_{\text{sat}}(P_{\text{high}}) - T_{\text{line}}$ | **3 – 8 °C** | Confirms fully liquid feed to TXV | | 3 | **Coefficient of Performance** | $COP = \dfrac{Q_L}{W_{\text{comp}}} = \dfrac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$ | **2.5 – 3.5** | System efficiency indicator | | 4 | **Refrigerant Mass Balance** | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{Q_L}{h_1 - h_4}$ | — | Confirms charge is consistent | | 5 | **Condenser Heat Rejection** | $\dot{Q}_H = \dot{m}(h_2 - h_3)$ | — | Energy dumped to ambient | | 6 | **Pressure Ratio** $r_p$ | $r_p = \dfrac{P_{\text{discharge}}}{P_{\text{suction}}}$ | **≈ 5 – 8** | Indicator of compressor load | | 7 | **Volumetric Efficiency** $\eta_v$ | $\eta_v = \dfrac{\dot{m}}{\rho_1 \cdot V_{\text{displaced}}}$ | **70 – 90 %** | Compressor health marker | | 8 | **Actual Refrigeration Effect (ARE)** | $ARE = h_1 - h_4$ | — | Per kg cooling capacity | | 9 | **Cabin Cooling Rate** | $q_{\text{cabin}} = \dot{m}_{\text{air}} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | — | How fast cabin cools | | 10 | **Clutch Air-Gap** | Gap (electromagnetic clutch) | **0.4 – 0.8 mm** | Critical for engagement | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always quote superheat and subcooling **with units (°C)** and explicitly state the reference pressure location (e.g., "at the compressor service port"). Marks are awarded for **methodology**, not just the final number. ## 2.3 The Eight Canonical Faults of an Automotive AC System The KTU 2024 syllabus categorises faults into **8 diagnostic patterns** that every student must memorize: | Fault Code | Symptom Combination | High-Side Pressure | Low-Side Pressure | Superheat | Subcooling | Probable Root Cause | |------------|--------------------|--------------------|-------------------|-----------|------------|---------------------| | **F1** | No Cooling | Low | Low | High | Low | **Undercharge / Leak** | | **F2** | No Cooling | High | High | Normal | High | **Overcharge** | | **F3** | Poor Cooling (intermittent) | High | Low/High | Low | High | **Moisture in system / Restriction** | | **F4** | No Cooling, Clutch Not Engaging | Any | Any | — | — | **Electrical fault** (clutch, relay, fuse, switch) | | **F5** | Cooling OK, then fades | High | High | High | Low | **Compressor inefficiency / Wear** | | **F6** | Frost on suction line | Normal/Low | Low | Very Low | Normal | **TXV stuck open / Low charge** | | **F7** | No airflow from vents | — | — | — | — | **Blower / HVAC box fault** | | **F8** | Musty odour | — | — | — | — | **Evaporator drain blockage / Biofilm** | > [!NOTE] > **Real-world utility:** This 8-fault matrix is the **de facto diagnostic flowchart** used by ASE-certified (Automotive Service Excellence) technicians in the USA and by Bosch Service Centres worldwide. Mastering it makes a student industry-ready. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Framework & Diagnostic Derivations ## 3.1 The KTU-Approved Diagnostic Workflow (11-Step Protocol) A KTU 14-mark theory question typically asks students to **"Explain the systematic troubleshooting procedure for an automotive AC system."** The following 11-step protocol is the **model answer blueprint**. ### Step 1: Information Gathering (Preliminary Interview) - **Ascertain symptoms** from the customer: When did the problem start? Is cooling intermittent or constant? Any unusual noises/smells? - **Review history:** Last service date, refrigerant top-up history, accident history, recent electrical work. ### Step 2: Visual & Tactile Inspection - Inspect **compressor drive belt** for tension, cracks, glazing. - Check **hose and line condition** — look for oil stains (indicates refrigerant leak with oil). - Inspect **condenser** for bent fins, road debris damage, bug blockage. - Verify **condenser fan** operation (where applicable). - Examine **compressor clutch air gap** with feeler gauge. ### Step 3: Verify Electrical Supplies - Battery voltage **≥ 12.4 V** (engine off). - Engine running voltage **13.8 – 14.4 V** (alternator health). - Test **compressor clutch coil resistance** (typically **3.0 – 5.0 Ω** at 25 °C). - Test **relay continuity** and **fuse integrity**. - Verify **thermostatic switch / pressure switch** operation. ### Step 4: Ambient & Vent Temperature Baseline - Measure **ambient (outside) temperature** in shade. - Measure **center vent air temperature** after 5 minutes of operation. - Expected delta: $T_{\text{ambient}} - T_{\text{vent}} \geq 20\ ^\circ\text{C}$ for adequate cooling. ### Step 5: Connect Manifold Gauge Set - Connect **RED** (high-side) hose to the discharge port. - Connect **BLUE** (low-side) hose to the suction port. - Run engine at **1500 rpm** with AC on **MAX COOL** and blower on **HIGH**. - Open vehicle doors/windows to simulate low-load condition for stable readings. ### Step 6: Record Steady-State Pressures - Allow **3 – 5 minutes** for system stabilization. - Note high-side ($P_H$) and low-side ($P_L$) pressure values. ### Step 7: Correlate with P-T Chart - Use the R-134a saturation table to convert pressures to saturation temperatures. - Compare to manufacturer specifications. ### Step 8: Measure Superheat at Compressor Inlet - Wrap suction line near compressor with thermocouple or use clamp thermometer. - Compute: $SH = T_{\text{line}} - T_{\text{sat}}(P_L)$. ### Step 9: Measure Subcooling at Receiver-Drier Outlet - Wrap liquid line near receiver-drier outlet. - Compute: $SC = T_{\text{sat}}(P_H) - T_{\text{line}}$. ### Step 10: Cross-Reference with Fault Matrix - Use the **8-fault table from Section 2.3** to identify root cause. ### Step 11: Confirmatory Testing & Repair - Perform **electronic leak detection** (if F1 suspected). - **Recover refrigerant** (mandatory per F-Gas regulations). - **Evacuate** system to **500 microns Hg** for **30 minutes minimum**. - **Leak test** with OFN (Oxygen-Free Nitrogen) at **1.5× working pressure**. - **Recharge** with exact specified mass of refrigerant **and** PAG oil (typically **120 – 180 ml**). ## 3.2 Worked-Out Numerical Example (KTU 14-Mark Standard) > **Problem:** A Maruti Suzuki Swift's AC is reported to have poor cooling. The technician records the following steady-state values: > - Ambient temperature: **38 °C** > - High-side pressure: **240 psi (gauge)** > - Low-side pressure: **55 psi (gauge)** > - Compressor inlet line temperature: **12 °C** > - Liquid line temperature at receiver-drier outlet: **45 °C** > - R-134a saturation table excerpt: at **55 psig** → $T_{\text{sat}} = 4\ ^\circ\text{C}$ ; at **240 psig** → $T_{\text{sat}} = 51\ ^\circ\text{C}$ > > **Determine:** (a) Superheat, (b) Subcooling, (c) Identify the fault and recommend the remedial action. ### Solution — Part (a): Superheat Calculation **Given:** - Compressor inlet line temperature: $T_{\text{line}} = 12\ ^\circ\text{C}$ - Low-side (suction) pressure: $P_L = 55\ \text{psig}$ → from R-134a table, $T_{\text{sat}} = 4\ ^\circ\text{C}$ **Step 1 — Apply the Superheat formula:** $$ \begin{aligned} SH &= T_{\text{line}} - T_{\text{sat}}(P_L) \\ SH &= 12\ ^\circ\text{C} - 4\ ^\circ\text{C} \\ SH &= 8\ ^\circ\text{C} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Interpretation:** The superheat of **8 °C** lies within the ideal operating window of **5 – 10 °C**. > **[Conclusion for Part (a)]:** Superheat is **NORMAL** (8 °C). The TXV is metering correctly, and no liquid refrigerant is reaching the compressor. --- ### Solution — Part (b): Subcooling Calculation **Given:** - Liquid line temperature: $T_{\text{line,liq}} = 45\ ^\circ\text{C}$ - High-side (discharge) pressure: $P_H = 240\ \text{psig}$ → from R-134a table, $T_{\text{sat}} = 51\ ^\circ\text{C}$ **Step 1 — Apply the Subcooling formula:** $$ \begin{aligned} SC &= T_{\text{sat}}(P_H) - T_{\text{line,liq}} \\ SC &= 51\ ^\circ\text{C} - 45\ ^\circ\text{C} \\ SC &= 6\ ^\circ\text{C} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Interpretation:** The subcooling of **6 °C** lies within the ideal window of **3 – 8 °C**. > **[Conclusion for Part (b)]:** Subcooling is **NORMAL** (6 °C). The condenser is rejecting heat properly, and the receiver-drier is delivering fully liquid refrigerant to the TXV. --- ### Solution — Part (c): Fault Diagnosis **Observations from parts (a) and (b):** - Superheat = 8 °C → **NORMAL** - Subcooling = 6 °C → **NORMAL** - High-side pressure = 240 psig → **HIGH** (spec: 150 – 220 psig) - Low-side pressure = 55 psig → **HIGH** (spec: 25 – 45 psig) - Cooling performance: **POOR** **Cross-reference with Fault Matrix (Section 2.3):** | Parameter | Observed | Spec | Status | |-----------|----------|------|--------| | High-side | 240 psig | 150 – 220 | **HIGH** | | Low-side | 55 psig | 25 – 45 | **HIGH** | | Superheat | 8 °C | 5 – 10 | Normal | | Subcooling | 6 °C | 3 – 8 | Normal | **Diagnostic match:** Both pressures high, superheat and subcooling normal → **Fault F2 (Overcharge)** OR **Condenser air flow restriction** OR **Refrigerant contamination (non-condensable gases).** **Step-by-step further diagnosis:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 1:} \quad &\text{Turn OFF AC. Wait 10 minutes. Restart.} \\ &\text{If pressures normalise briefly, then re-rise} \Rightarrow \text{NCG (air) ingress} \\ \\ \text{Step 2:} \quad &\text{Inspect condenser fins — blockage?} \\ &\text{If clogged} \Rightarrow \text{Clean with low-pressure water + fin comb} \\ \\ \text{Step 3:} \quad &\text{Recover refrigerant and weigh it.} \\ &\text{If mass} > \text{specification (e.g., 550 g} \to \text{spec is 500 g)} \Rightarrow \text{Overcharge} \\ \\ \text{Step 4:} \quad &\text{Evacuate and recharge to exact specification.} \end{aligned} $$ > **[Conclusion for Part (c)]:** The most probable root cause is **non-condensable gases (air) trapped in the condenser** or **slight overcharge**. Recommended remedy: **Recover refrigerant → evacuate to 500 microns Hg for 30 min → recharge to manufacturer-specified mass**. ## 3.3 Compressor Mechanical Diagnostics ### 3.3.1 Compressor Clutch Air-Gap Verification The electromagnetic clutch must engage cleanly without slippage. Air-gap is measured between the clutch plate and the pulley face. $$ \text{Required Air-Gap} = 0.4\ \text{mm to}\ 0.8\ \text{mm} $$ **Procedure:** Insert a feeler gauge at three equispaced points (120° apart). Average the three readings. If the gap is **> 0.8 mm**, shim adjustment or clutch replacement is required. ### 3.3.2 Clutch Coil Resistance Check Set multimeter to **Ω (resistance)**, measure across clutch coil terminals. $$ \begin{aligned} R_{\text{coil, expected}} &\approx 3.0\ \Omega \text{ to}\ 5.0\ \Omega \text{ at}\ 25\ ^\circ\text{C} \\ R_{\text{coil, open}} &= \infty\ (\text{OL}) \Rightarrow \text{Burnt coil — replace clutch} \\ R_{\text{coil, shorted}} &< 1.0\ \Omega \Rightarrow \text{Insulation failure} \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.3.3 Compressor Oil Check (PAG Oil) **PAG (Polyalkylene Glycol) oil** is hygroscopic. If the system has been open to atmosphere for > 30 minutes, the oil must be replaced. $$ \text{Typical PAG oil charge} = 120\ \text{ml to}\ 180\ \text{ml}\ (\text{depends on compressor model}) $$ **Contamination check:** Extract a small oil sample. If the colour is **dark/black** or contains **metallic particles** → **compressor internal wear** → replacement warranted. ## 3.4 Leak Detection Methodology (Quantified) Refrigerant leaks are the **#1 cause** of automotive AC failure. KTU often asks: *"Differentiate between the four methods of leak detection."* | Method | Sensitivity | Application | Cost | KTU Mark Weightage | |--------|------------|-------------|------|--------------------| | **Electronic (He-diode) Sniffer** | **1 g/yr** | General purpose | High | 2 marks | | **Fluorescent Dye + UV Light** | **7 g/yr** | Slow leaks, component ID | Low | 2 marks | | **Soap Bubble Solution** | **28 g/yr** | Field quick check | Negligible | 1 mark | | **Halide Torch (obsolete)** | Low | Legacy only | Low | 1 mark | | **Nitrogen Pressure Test** | **Qualitative** | Post-repair verification | Medium | 2 marks | > [!NOTE] > **Industry Standard:** Modern workshops use **electronic sniffers** calibrated to R-134a (or R-1234yf) with a sensitivity of **1 gram per year**, certified to **SAE J1628 / J2791** standards. ## 3.5 Evacuation and Recharge — The "Recovery Triplet" A KTU 14-mark question may ask the student to "explain the recovery-recharge procedure." The triplet is: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 1: RECOVERY} &\rightarrow \text{Use recovery machine. Recover to} \approx -1\ \text{bar gauge}. \\ \text{Step 2: EVACUATION} &\rightarrow \text{Vacuum pump to} \leq 500\ \text{microns Hg for} \geq 30\ \text{min}. \\ \text{Step 3: RECHARGE} &\rightarrow \text{Charge to OEM spec mass (e.g., 480 g for Swift).} \end{aligned} $$ **Why the 30-minute evacuation?** Moisture boils off from PAG oil and internal surfaces. A triple evacuation (vacuum → break with dry N₂ → vacuum → break → vacuum) is recommended for systems that have been severely contaminated. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 F-Gas Compliance:** Releasing refrigerant into the atmosphere is a **criminal offence** under the Indian Ozone Depleting Substances (ODS) Regulation, 2000 and its 2014 amendment. Always use **certified recovery equipment**. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The KTU-Standard Troubleshooting Decision Tree This Mermaid flowchart is the **canonical visual answer** expected by KTU examiners for the question "Explain with a flowchart how you would troubleshoot a car's AC." ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA([START - Customer Complaint]) --> nodeB{Cooling OK?} nodeB -- No --> nodeC{Compressor Clutch Engaging?} nodeB -- Yes --> nodeD[Check Vent Temp
Target 3-8 deg C] nodeC -- No --> nodeE[Test Electrical Circuit
Fuse - Relay - Clutch Coil - Pressure Switch] nodeC -- Yes --> nodeF[Connect Manifold Gauges] nodeF --> nodeG{Compare Pressures with Spec} nodeG -- Both LOW --> nodeH[F1 - Undercharge / Leak] nodeG -- Both HIGH --> nodeI[F2 - Overcharge / NCG / Condenser Blockage] nodeG -- High HIGH, Low LOW --> nodeJ[F3 - TXV Stuck / Restriction] nodeG -- Both NORMAL --> nodeK[Check Superheat and Subcooling] nodeK -- High SH, Low SC --> nodeL[F1 - Undercharge Confirmed] nodeK -- Low SH, High SC --> nodeM[F6 - TXV Stuck Open] nodeK -- Both NORMAL, Poor Cooling --> nodeN[Compressor Mechanical Issue] nodeE --> nodeO[Repair Electrical Defect] nodeH --> nodeP[Leak Test - Recover - Evacuate - Recharge] nodeI --> nodeP nodeJ --> nodeP nodeL --> nodeP nodeM --> nodeQ[Replace TXV - Recharge] nodeN --> nodeR[Compressor Replacement] nodeD -- Vent Temp 8 to 15 deg C --> nodeS[Weak Cooling - Check Blower and Filter] nodeD -- Vent Temp gt 15 deg C --> nodeF nodeP --> nodeT([END - System Restored]) nodeQ --> nodeT nodeR --> nodeT nodeO --> nodeT nodeS --> nodeT ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Modern Automotive HVAC System The following diagram illustrates the **signal flow and refrigerant flow** for fault-tracing purposes. ```mermaid flowchart LR cabinTemp[Cabin Temp Sensor] --> hvacECU[HVAC ECU / Climate Control Module] ambTemp[Ambient Temp Sensor] --> hvacECU evapTemp[Evaporator Fin Temp Sensor] --> hvacECU sunSensor[Sunload Sensor] --> hvacECU hvacECU -- PWM Signal --> blowerCtrl[Blower Motor Controller] hvacECU -- Relay Drive --> clutchRelay[Compressor Clutch Relay] hvacECU -- Solenoid Drive --> txvSolenoid[TXV Solenoid if EETV] clutchRelay -- 12V Supply --> compClutch[Compressor Clutch Coil] compClutch -- Engages --> compressor[Compressor] blowerCtrl -- V --> blower[Blower Motor] blower --> evapBox[Evaporator Housing] evapTemp --> evapBox compressor -- High P Vapor --> condenser[Condenser] condenser -- Liquid --> recvDrier[Receiver Drier] recvDrier -- Subcooled Liquid --> txv[Thermostatic Expansion Valve] txv -- Low P Mix --> evapBox evapBox -- Low P Vapor --> compressor cabinTemp -.Vent Air Temp Feedback.-> hvacECU ``` ## 4.3 Manifold Gauge Reading Diagnostic Map This **pressure-based diagnostic map** is a high-yield visual aid for the lab component. ```mermaid flowchart TB start([Read High and Low Side Pressures]) --> q1{Both Low?} q1 -- Yes --> f1[F1 Undercharge
Action Leak Test and Recharge] q1 -- No --> q2{Both High?} q2 -- Yes --> f2[F2 Overcharge or NCG
Action Recover, Evacuate, Recharge] q2 -- No --> q3{High HIGH, Low LOW?} q3 -- Yes --> f3[F3a TXV Stuck Closed
F3b Receiver Drier Plugged] q3 -- No --> q4{High NORMAL, Low HIGH?} q4 -- Yes --> f4[F4 Compressor Internal Leak
Action Compressor Replacement] q4 -- No --> q5{Both NORMAL but No Cooling?} q5 -- Yes --> f5[F5 Compressor Mechanical
Action Bench Test and Replace] f1 --> done([Diagnose Complete]) f2 --> done f3 --> done f4 --> done f5 --> done ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set A]** > > **Q1. Define superheat and subcooling in the context of an automotive AC system. State their typical operating ranges.** **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > **Superheat** is the temperature difference between the actual refrigerant vapor temperature at the compressor inlet and the saturation temperature corresponding to the low-side pressure. It ensures that only dry vapor enters the compressor, preventing liquid slugging. The typical operating range is **5 °C to 10 °C**. > > **Subcooling** is the temperature difference between the saturation temperature corresponding to the high-side pressure and the actual liquid refrigerant temperature at the receiver-drier outlet. It ensures the refrigerant is fully liquid before entering the expansion valve. The typical range is **3 °C to 8 °C**. --- > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** > > **Q2. List any three common electrical faults in an automotive AC system and the test instrument used to diagnose each.** **Model Answer:** | # | Electrical Fault | Test Instrument | Procedure | |---|------------------|-----------------|-----------| | 1 | **Burnt clutch coil** | Digital Multimeter (Ω) | Measure resistance; expected 3–5 Ω. Open (OL) = faulty. | | 2 | **Blown fuse** | Test lamp / Multimeter (continuity) | Remove fuse; check continuity. No beep = blown. | | 3 | **Failed pressure switch** | Multimeter (V/Ω) | Disconnect switch; test for continuity at specified pressure. | | 4 | **Faulty relay** | Multimeter + jumper wire | Swap with known-good relay or jump terminals 30 and 87. | --- ## 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Choice (14 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** > > ### **Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks** > > **(a)** Describe the systematic procedure to diagnose "no cooling" complaint in an automotive AC system. Include the role of manifold gauges, P-T relationship, and electrical testing. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** A car's AC system shows: High-side = 230 psi; Low-side = 30 psi; Compressor inlet line temperature = 0 °C; R-134a saturation at 30 psi = −7 °C. Calculate the superheat, identify the fault, and recommend remedial action. **(7 marks)** ### **Model Solution — Question A** **Part (a) — Systematic Procedure (7 marks):** 1. **Information gathering** — customer interview for symptoms, history, and last service. *(1 mark)* 2. **Visual inspection** — drive belt, hoses, condenser fins, compressor clutch gap. *(1 mark)* 3. **Electrical testing** — battery voltage, fuse, relay, clutch coil resistance, pressure switch continuity. *(1 mark)* 4. **Temperature baseline** — record ambient and center vent temperature. *(0.5 mark)* 5. **Manifold gauge connection** — connect to high and low service ports, run engine at 1500 rpm with AC on MAX COOL. *(1 mark)* 6. **Record steady-state pressures** and convert to saturation temperatures using the **P-T chart**. *(1 mark)* 7. **Measure superheat and subcooling** using line thermometers. Cross-reference with the **8-fault diagnostic matrix** to identify the root cause. *(1 mark)* 8. **Perform confirmatory leak detection** (electronic sniffer / dye), then **recover, evacuate (500 microns / 30 min), and recharge to OEM specification**. *(0.5 mark)* **Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):** **Given Data:** $$ \begin{aligned} P_H &= 230\ \text{psi} \\ P_L &= 30\ \text{psi} \\ T_{\text{line,inlet}} &= 0\ ^\circ\text{C} \\ T_{\text{sat}}(P_L) &= -7\ ^\circ\text{C} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 1: Superheat Calculation** $$ \begin{aligned} SH &= T_{\text{line,inlet}} - T_{\text{sat}}(P_L) \\ SH &= 0\ ^\circ\text{C} - (-7\ ^\circ\text{C}) \\ SH &= +7\ ^\circ\text{C} \end{aligned} $$ The superheat of 7 °C is **within the normal range of 5 – 10 °C**. **Step 2: Fault Identification from Pressure Pattern** | Parameter | Observed | Spec Range | Verdict | |-----------|----------|------------|---------| | High-side | 230 psi | 150 – 220 | **Slightly HIGH** | | Low-side | 30 psi | 25 – 45 | **NORMAL (low end)** | **Step 3: Cross-Reference with Fault Matrix** The pattern (high-side elevated, low-side normal-to-low, normal superheat) matches **Fault F3 (partial restriction in receiver-drier or early-stage TXV issue)** OR **slight overcharge**. Most likely cause: **partially plugged receiver-drier desiccant** releasing debris and obstructing flow. **Step 4: Remedial Action** 1. **Recover refrigerant** using certified recovery machine. 2. **Replace receiver-drier** (desiccant is saturated and shedding). 3. **Replace TXV** if inspection shows debris contamination. 4. **Evacuate** to ≤ 500 microns Hg for **30 minutes minimum**. 5. **Leak test** with OFN at 1.5× working pressure. 6. **Recharge** with OEM-specified mass of R-134a and **PAG oil** top-up (typically 60 – 80 ml for receiver-drier replacement). 7. **Verify performance** — vent temperature should drop to **3 – 8 °C** within 5 minutes. > **[End of Question A]** --- > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** > > ### **Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks** > > **(a)** Explain the four methods of refrigerant leak detection in an automotive AC system, with their sensitivities and applications. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** A technician evacuates an AC system to a vacuum of 800 microns Hg. The system capacity is 1.2 L. Calculate (i) the absolute pressure inside, (ii) the boiling temperature of water at this pressure, and (iii) comment on whether this vacuum is sufficient for moisture removal. **(7 marks)** ### **Model Solution — Question B** **Part (a) — Leak Detection Methods (7 marks):** > [!IMPORTANT] > **1. Electronic Sniffer (Heated Diode Detector) — Sensitivity 1 g/year (2 marks)** > A heated sensor element changes resistance when halogenated refrigerant molecules pass over it. Audible alarm and LED indicate leak. Calibrated to R-134a/R-1234yf. SAE J2791 standard. > **2. Fluorescent Dye + UV Lamp — Sensitivity 7 g/year (2 marks)** > Dye (e.g., Spectroline GLO-Stick) is added to refrigerant. UV-A light (365 nm) causes dye to fluoresce yellow-green at leak points. Excellent for slow leaks and component identification. > **3. Soap Bubble Solution — Sensitivity 28 g/year (1.5 marks)** > Brush soapy water on suspected joints; bubbles form at leak. Low-cost, field-use, but limited to larger leaks and to accessible joints. > **4. Nitrogen Pressure Test (OFN — Oxygen-Free Nitrogen) — Qualitative (1.5 marks)** > Pressurise the system with OFN to 1.5× working pressure. Monitor gauge for 15–30 minutes. A pressure drop indicates a leak. Used **post-repair** to verify the repair, and **pre-evacuation** to find gross leaks. **Part (b) — Vacuum Analysis (7 marks):** **Given:** - Vacuum gauge reading: $P_{\text{vac}} = 800\ \text{microns Hg} = 0.800\ \text{mm Hg}$ - System volume: $V = 1.2\ \text{L} = 1.2 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^3$ **Step (i) — Absolute Pressure:** Standard atmospheric pressure: $P_{\text{atm}} = 760\ \text{mm Hg} = 760{,}000\ \text{microns Hg}$. $$ \begin{aligned} P_{\text{abs}} &= P_{\text{atm}} - P_{\text{vac}} \\ P_{\text{abs}} &= 760{,}000\ \text{microns} - 800\ \text{microns} \\ P_{\text{abs}} &= 759{,}200\ \text{microns Hg} \\ P_{\text{abs}} &= 759.2\ \text{mm Hg} \\ P_{\text{abs}} &\approx 0.9989\ \text{bar (absolute)} \approx 99{,}890\ \text{Pa} \end{aligned} $$ **Step (ii) — Boiling Point of Water at this Pressure:** Using the **Antoine equation** for water (valid range 1 – 200 mm Hg): $$ \log_{10} P = A - \dfrac{B}{C + T} $$ with constants $A = 8.07131$, $B = 1730.63$, $C = 233.426$, and $P$ in mm Hg, $T$ in °C. For $P = 759.2\ \text{mm Hg}$: $$ \begin{aligned} \log_{10}(759.2) &= 8.07131 - \dfrac{1730.63}{233.426 + T_{\text{boil}}} \\ 2.8805 &= 8.07131 - \dfrac{1730.63}{233.426 + T_{\text{boil}}} \\ \dfrac{1730.63}{233.426 + T_{\text{boil}}} &= 5.1908 \\ 233.426 + T_{\text{boil}} &= \dfrac{1730.63}{5.1908} \\ 233.426 + T_{\text{boil}} &= 333.40 \\ T_{\text{boil}} &= 100.0\ ^\circ\text{C} \end{aligned} $$ **Step (iii) — Is 800 microns Sufficient?** > [!WARNING] > **No — 800 microns is INSUFFICIENT.** The KTU/Bosch industry standard mandates evacuation to **≤ 500 microns Hg** for at least **30 minutes**. At 800 microns, residual moisture in PAG oil (hygroscopic) will not boil off completely. The water will freeze at the TXV orifice, causing the **Fault F3 (moisture-induced restriction)**. > **Recommendation:** The technician should **continue evacuation** until the micron gauge stabilises at **≤ 500 microns** for at least **30 minutes**, and perform a **15-minute isolation test** (close the manifold valves) — if pressure rises > 100 microns in 15 min, the system has moisture ingress or a leak. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Forgetting units** — Always write "°C" with superheat/subcooling. **(−1 to −2 marks)** > 2. **Not stating the P-T relationship explicitly** — The examiner expects you to say *"From the R-134a saturation table, at 30 psi the saturation temperature is −7 °C."* **(−1 mark)** > 3. **Confusing superheat with subcooling** — Superheat is on the **low side**; subcooling is on the **high side**. Mixing them up is a fatal error. **(−2 marks)** > 4. **Ignoring the 30-minute evacuation rule** — Marks for evacuation procedure are routinely awarded only if duration and micron level are **both** mentioned. **(−1 mark)** > 5. **Not citing F-Gas / ODS regulation** for refrigerant recovery — A single mention of "**refrigerant must be recovered, not vented**" is worth **1 mark** in 14-mark answers. **(−1 mark)** ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Superheat (SH):** $T_{\text{line}} - T_{\text{sat}}(P_L)$ — target **5 – 10 °C**. - **Subcooling (SC):** $T_{\text{sat}}(P_H) - T_{\text{line}}$ — target **3 – 8 °C**. - **Low-side normal:** **25 – 45 psi (R-134a)** at 35 °C ambient. - **High-side normal:** **150 – 250 psi (R-134a)** at 35 °C ambient. - **Clutch coil resistance:** **3 – 5 Ω** at 25 °C. - **Clutch air-gap:** **0.4 – 0.8 mm**. - **Evacuation target:** **≤ 500 microns Hg for ≥ 30 minutes**. - **Leak test pressure:** **1.5× working pressure with OFN**. - **Vent air target temperature:** **3 – 8 °C** at 35 °C ambient, MAX COOL, blower HIGH. - **Always recover refrigerant** — never vent (F-Gas / ODS compliance). - **PAG oil** is hygroscopic — replace oil and receiver-drier if system is open > 30 min. - **Electronic sniffer sensitivity: 1 g/yr** — the gold standard for leak detection. - **8-fault matrix** (F1–F8) — memorize the pressure and SH/SC signatures. - **Both pressures high + normal SH + normal SC = Overcharge or NCG (air) ingress.** - **Both pressures low = undercharge or leak (most common fault).** - **Compressor not engaging = electrical fault first, mechanical second.** - **High-side high, low-side low = restriction (TXV/drier) — the classic F3 fault.** > **End of Module 4 — Topic: Troubleshooting of Air Conditioning System**

topics.title: Compressor Service
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT523 - AUTOMOTIVE REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Compressor Service

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Compressor Service** in an automotive air-conditioning system refers to the systematic diagnosis, maintenance, repair, and replacement procedures performed on the A/C compressor—the prime mover of the refrigeration cycle that compresses low-pressure, low-temperature refrigerant vapor into high-pressure, high-temperature superheated vapor.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PEAUT523 – Module 4), compressor service encompasses **diagnosis of compressor-related faults**, **oil management**, **clutch service**, **suction/discharge valve reconditioning**, **system flushing after burnout**, and **compressor replacement protocols**.

The compressor is mechanically driven by the engine via a **serpentine belt** (or dedicated V-belt) through an **electromagnetic clutch assembly**. Modern vehicles predominantly use:

| Compressor Type | Drive Mechanism | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Swash-Plate (Axial)** | Piston reciprocation via angled plate | Most passenger cars |
| **Scroll (Orbital)** | One fixed, one orbiting scroll | Premium sedans, hybrids |
| **Wobble-Plate** | Piston movement via wobbling disc | Compact cars |
| **Rotary Vane** | Eccentric rotor with sliding vanes | Light commercial vehicles |
| **Electric Scroll (HV)** | High-voltage motor drive | Battery EVs (BEV), HEVs |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> Think of the compressor as the **"heart"** of the A/C system—just as the human heart pumps blood, the compressor pumps refrigerant. Now imagine this heart has a clutch (like a clutch pedal on a manual car), oil as its "lubricant blood," and valves as its "heart valves." Compressor service is essentially a **cardiology check-up**—diagnosing murmurs (noise), checking fluid levels (oil), replacing worn valves (reed/flapper valves), and sometimes performing a complete transplant (compressor replacement).

### Real-World Analogy: The Bicycle Pump

A bicycle pump is the simplest analogy. When you push the pump handle down:
- Low-pressure air is **drawn in** through the intake valve
- It is **compressed** into a smaller volume
- High-pressure air is **pushed out** through the exhaust valve

The automotive compressor operates identically, but with **refrigerant gas** instead of air, and continuously rather than manually. The difference: in the A/C system, the refrigerant doesn't escape—it cycles through the system, absorbing heat indoors and rejecting it outdoors.

## 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics (Bold)

- **Refrigerant Oil Charge:** typically **80 – 240 mL** depending on compressor displacement
- **Suction Pressure (Idle):** **1.5 – 2.5 bar** (gauge)
- **Discharge Pressure (Idle):** **12 – 18 bar** (gauge) at ambient ~35°C
- **Compressor Speed:** typically **600 – 6,000 rpm** (engine-idle to cruise)
- **Oil Type:** **PAG (Polyalkylene Glycol)** for R-134a systems; **POE (Polyolester)** for R-1234yf and hybrid systems
- **Oil Viscosity Grade:** typically **PAG 46 or PAG 100**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Highlight:** Mishandling compressor oil is the **#1 cause of A/C system failure** in automotive applications. PAG oils are highly hygroscopic (absorb moisture) and must be stored in sealed containers with < **30 ppm moisture content**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Compressor Service Operational Breakdown

### Step 1: Initial Diagnosis & Symptom Recognition

Before any service, the technician must classify the fault using the **"Listen – Look – Feel – Measure"** protocol:

- **Listen:** Knocking (worn bearings), squealing (clutch slip), clicking (clutch air-gap)
- **Look:** Oil streaks (leak), frosting on suction line (low charge), oily clutch face
- **Feel:** Clutch face temperature (should not exceed **60°C** under normal operation)
- **Measure:** High-side and low-side pressures, superheat, subcooling

### Step 2: Clutch Service Procedure

The electromagnetic clutch consists of:
1. **Pulley/Hub assembly** (rotates with belt)
2. **Armature plate** (mates to compressor shaft via splines)
3. **Electromagnetic coil** (12V DC in conventional vehicles; high-voltage in EVs)
4. **Bearing** (allows pulley to freewheel when disengaged)

**Air-Gap Specification:** typically **0.4 – 0.8 mm** (0.016" – 0.032"). Measured with feeler gauge between armature and pulley friction surface.

### Step 3: Oil Management & Charging

**Why oil matters:** Compressor oil performs three critical functions:
- **Lubrication** of bearings, pistons, swash-plate
- **Sealing** of clearances (scroll tip seals, piston rings)
- **Heat removal** (carries ~10% of motor heat away)

### Step 4: System Flushing (Post-Burnout)

When a compressor fails catastrophically (seized bearings, broken valves), metallic debris contaminates the entire system. A **system flush** is mandatory using an approved flushing solvent (e.g., **A/C Flush Solvent**, **ZR-Flush**).

### Step 5: Compressor Replacement & Commissioning

After installation, the system must undergo:
1. **Leak test** (pressure decay or electronic sniffer)
2. **Deep vacuum** (minimum 500 microns, 30 minutes)
3. **Moisture removal** via vacuum
4. **Refrigerant charging** (by weight or recovery-recycle machine)
5. **Performance verification**

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are **exam-critical** for Module 4. Memorize units and boundary conditions.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_{oil} = V_{comp} + V_{receiver} \times \alpha$ | Oil charge estimation (α ≈ 0.10) | mL |
| 2 | $\dot{m} = \rho_{suc} \times V_{disp} \times N$ | Refrigerant mass flow rate | kg/s |
| 3 | $\text{SH} = T_{suc,line} - T_{sat}(P_{low})$ | Superheat = measured temp − saturation temp | K or °C |
| 4 | $\text{SC} = T_{sat}(P_{high}) - T_{liq,line}$ | Subcooling = sat temp − liquid line temp | K or °C |
| 5 | $W_{comp} = \dot{m} \times (h_2 - h_1)$ | Compressor work input | kW |
| 6 | $\eta_{vol} = \dfrac{V_{actual}}{V_{disp}} \times 100$ | Volumetric efficiency | % |
| 7 | $COP_{cool} = \dfrac{Q_L}{W_{comp}}$ | Coefficient of Performance (cooling) | — |
| 8 | $t_{vac} \geq 30 \text{ min @ } 500 \,\mu\text{m}$ | Minimum evacuation time | minutes |
| 9 | $Q_{flush} = k \times V_{system}$ | Flushing solvent volume (k ≈ 1.5–2) | mL |
| 10 | $T_{clutch,max} \leq 60\,°C$ | Maximum clutch operating temperature | °C |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Conditions & Standard Assumptions:**
> - Ambient reference for testing: **35 °C (95 °F)** dry bulb
> - Engine speed for diagnostics: **1,500 rpm** (idle + small load)
> - Blower speed: **recirculate, MAX**
> - All doors/windows closed
> - Vehicle on shaded, level surface

## 2.3 Engineering Utility & Real-World Application

Compressor service is the **highest-revenue service category** in any automotive A/C workshop. Approximately **35–40%** of all A/C complaints are compressor-related (industry data from SAE J2765 service guidelines). Mastery of compressor service procedures:

- Prevents warranty disputes (especially in EVs where HV compressors cost **₹80,000 – ₹1,50,000**)
- Ensures compliance with **F-Gas Regulation (EU 517/2014)** for refrigerant recovery
- Reduces **refrigerant emissions** that have GWP impact (R-134a GWP = 1430; R-1234yf GWP = 4)

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Oil Charge Calculation for a Replacement Compressor

When replacing an automotive A/C compressor, the correct oil charge must be determined. New compressors ship **pre-charged** with oil, but the technician must add **make-up oil** to account for oil retained in the system.

**Given Scenario (KTU-style numerical):**
- Compressor displacement: **140 mL**
- New compressor arrives pre-charged with: **180 mL** of PAG 46
- System holds an estimated residual oil: **40 mL** in lines, condenser, and evaporator
- Receiver-drier capacity: **120 mL** of desiccant; oil retention factor α = **0.10**
- System requires total oil charge per OEM spec: **220 mL**

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1:** Identify the new compressor's pre-charge.

$$V_{pre} = 180 \text{ mL}$$

**Step 2:** Calculate oil retained in the receiver-drier.

$$V_{receiver} = 120 \text{ mL}, \quad \alpha = 0.10$$

$$V_{recv,oil} = V_{receiver} \times \alpha = 120 \times 0.10 = 12 \text{ mL}$$

**Step 3:** Sum all oil retained in the system (excluding the compressor).

$$V_{retained} = V_{lines+evap+cond} + V_{recv,oil}$$

$$V_{retained} = 40 + 12 = 52 \text{ mL}$$

**Step 4:** Apply the oil-balance equation.

$$V_{total,system} = V_{pre} + V_{added}$$

$$V_{added} = V_{total,system} - V_{pre}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute known values.

$$V_{added} = 220 - 180 = 40 \text{ mL}$$

**Step 6:** Subtract any oil to be drained from the new compressor (if overshooting).

If the new compressor holds **180 mL** but the system only needs to *retain* **220 - 40 = 180 mL** total in the compressor itself (because **40 mL** will naturally migrate to the rest of the system), then:

$$V_{added} = (V_{total,system} - V_{retained,rest}) - V_{pre}$$

$$V_{added} = (220 - 52) - 180 = 168 - 180 = -12 \text{ mL}$$

Since the result is negative, **drain 12 mL** from the new compressor before installation. No additional oil is needed.

**Step 7:** Verification.

$$\text{Total oil in system} = V_{pre,drained} + V_{retained} = (180-12) + 52 = 168 + 52 = 220 \text{ mL} \quad \checkmark$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Compressor Mass Flow Rate & Theoretical Work

**Given:** A 6-cylinder swash-plate compressor, displacement **150 cc/rev**, running at **2,500 rpm**, with suction density ρ = **4.5 kg/m³** and refrigerant enthalpy change **h₂ − h₁ = 35 kJ/kg**.

**Step 1:** Compute volumetric flow rate per second.

$$V_{disp,total} = 150 \text{ cc/rev} = 150 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3/\text{rev}$$

$$\dot{V} = V_{disp} \times N = 150 \times 10^{-6} \times \frac{2500}{60}$$

$$\dot{V} = 6.25 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 2:** Apply density to find mass flow rate.

$$\dot{m} = \rho_{suc} \times \dot{V} = 4.5 \times 6.25 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\dot{m} = 0.028125 \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 3:** Compute compressor theoretical work.

$$W_{comp} = \dot{m} \times (h_2 - h_1) = 0.028125 \times 35$$

$$W_{comp} = 0.9844 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4:** Convert to horsepower for board examiner familiarity.

$$W_{comp,HP} = \frac{0.9844}{0.7457} \approx 1.32 \text{ HP}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Common Compressor Faults → Service Action

| Symptom | Likely Cause | Diagnostic Method | Service Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| No cold air, low-side high | Compressor not pumping | Pressure test + manifold gauge | Check clutch, drive belt, refrigerant charge |
| Compressor silent, no engagement | Open clutch coil | Measure coil resistance (**3.5–5 Ω** typical) | Replace clutch coil |
| Squealing noise at compressor | Clutch slippage | Visual + thermal scan | Replace clutch assembly; check air-gap |
| Compressor knocking | Worn bearings | Mechanical stethoscope | Replace compressor |
| Metallic debris in sight glass | Burnout | Inspect filter-drier screen | Flush system + replace comp + drier |
| Excessive vibration | Mounting bolts loose | Torque check (**25–35 Nm**) | Re-torque or replace mounts |
| Compressor cycles on/off rapidly (short-cycling) | Low refrigerant, ICL failure | Pressure + amp draw | Recover, repair leak, recharge |
| Frost on suction line at compressor | Excessively low charge | Pressure + T-s chart | Add refrigerant; check for leak |
| Oil streaks at shaft seal | Worn seal | UV dye + electronic sniffer | Replace shaft seal assembly |
| High discharge temp (> 120 °C) | Overcharge, condenser blockage | IR thermometer + pressure | Recover; flush condenser |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture: Compressor Service Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Customer Complaint Intake"] --> nodeB["Pre-Service Symptom Audit"]
    nodeB --> nodeC{"Clutch Engaging?"}
    nodeC -- "No" --> nodeD["Check 12V Supply + Coil Resistance"]
    nodeC -- "Yes" --> nodeE["Measure Low/High Side Pressures"]
    nodeD --> nodeF{"Resistance OK?"}
    nodeF -- "No" --> nodeG["Replace Clutch Coil"]
    nodeF -- "Yes" --> nodeH["Inspect Air-Gap Spec"]
    nodeH --> nodeI{"Gap 0.4 to 0.8 mm?"}
    nodeI -- "No" --> nodeJ["Adjust Shim to Spec"]
    nodeI -- "Yes" --> nodeK["Test Clutch Current Draw"]
    nodeE --> nodeL{"Pressures within Range?"}
    nodeL -- "No" --> nodeM["Diagnose Refrigerant Charge"]
    nodeL -- "Yes" --> nodeN["Measure Superheat Subcool"]
    nodeN --> nodeO{"Superheat 5 to 12 C?"}
    nodeO -- "No" --> nodeP["Adjust TXV or Orifice Tube"]
    nodeO -- "Yes" --> nodeQ["Auditory Check on Compressor"]
    nodeQ --> nodeR{"Knocking Noise?"}
    nodeR -- "Yes" --> nodeS["Bearing Failure Suspected"]
    nodeR -- "No" --> nodeT["Performance Verification"]
    nodeS --> nodeU["Recover Refrigerant by Machine"]
    nodeU --> nodeV["Flush System with Approved Solvent"]
    nodeV --> nodeW["Replace Compressor and Drier"]
    nodeW --> nodeX["Apply Oil Charge Calculation"]
    nodeX --> nodeY["Deep Vacuum 500 micron for 30 min"]
    nodeY --> nodeZ["Recharge by Weight per OEM Spec"]
    nodeZ --> nodeAA["Leak Test with Electronic Sniffer"]
    nodeAA --> nodeAB["Document Service and Handover"]
```

## 4.2 Compressor Sub-Assembly Schematic

```mermaid
graph LR
    compA["Pulley Hub"] --> compB["Electromagnetic Coil"]
    compB --> compC["Armature Plate"]
    compC --> compD["Clutch Bearing"]
    compD --> compE["Front Housing"]
    compE --> compF["Cylinder Block"]
    compF --> compG["Swash Plate"]
    compG --> compH["Piston x N"]
    compH --> compI["Shaft with Seal"]
    compI --> compJ["Rear Housing"]
    compJ --> compK["Suction Port"]
    compJ --> compL["Discharge Port"]
    compM["Oil Sump"] --> compF
```

## 4.3 Compressor Clutch Air-Gap Visual Reference (ASCII Schematic)

```
         BELT GROOVE
        ┌─────────────┐
        │  ┌───────┐  │   ← Pulley (rotates with belt)
        │  │       │  │
        │  │ HUB   │  │
        │  │       │  │
        │  └───┬───┘  │
        └──────┼──────┘
               │
          ◄────X────►   ← AIR GAP (0.4 – 0.8 mm) measured with feeler gauge
               │
        ┌──────┼──────┐
        │      ▼      │   ← Armature plate (drives compressor shaft)
        │   SHAFT     │
        │   SPLINE    │
        └─────────────┘
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**Q: List any three common causes of compressor failure in automotive air-conditioning systems.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

1. **Insufficient lubrication** due to oil loss from leaks or incorrect oil charge, leading to bearing seizure.
2. **Liquid slugging** caused by refrigerant flood-back when the evaporator is overfed (failed TXV), producing hydromechanical shock.
3. **Contamination** from metallic debris after a prior burnout, abrasion of scroll flanks, or piston rings.
4. **Overheat** due to condenser blockage or insufficient airflow across the condenser.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often list "refrigerant leak" as a compressor failure—this is incorrect. Refrigerant leaks are **system** failures, not compressor failures. Stick to mechanical/electrical faults of the compressor itself.

### Question A2
**Q: What is PAG oil and why is it important in automotive A/C compressors?** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**PAG (Polyalkylene Glycol)** is a synthetic lubricant specifically formulated for compatibility with HFC refrigerants such as **R-134a**. It performs three functions:
- **Lubricates** moving parts (bearings, pistons, swash-plate)
- **Seals** small clearances between metal surfaces
- **Carries heat** away from friction zones

PAG oil is **highly hygroscopic**—it readily absorbs atmospheric moisture, which reacts with refrigerant to form corrosive acids. Therefore, opened containers must be sealed immediately. PAG oils are **miscible** with R-134a and circulate throughout the system, returning to the compressor sump via the suction line.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe with a neat sketch the construction and working of an automotive swash-plate compressor. Compare it briefly with a scroll-type compressor. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer:**

**Construction:**
A swash-plate compressor consists of a rotating shaft connected to an angled swash-plate (typically 15°–20° from axial). Multiple pistons (usually 5, 6, 7, or 10) are arranged axially in bores around the swash-plate. Each piston carries shoes that ride against the swash-plate faces. The compressor has:
- Front housing with electromagnetic clutch
- Cylinder block with piston bores
- Rear housing with suction and discharge chambers
- Reed or poppet valves at the head
- Shaft seal at the front

**Working:**
When the shaft rotates, the angled swash-plate imparts reciprocating motion to each piston. As a piston moves away from the head (down-stroke), low-pressure refrigerant vapor from the suction chamber is drawn through inlet ports into the cylinder. On the up-stroke, the vapor is compressed and pushed through the discharge valve into the high-pressure chamber.

The volume swept per revolution depends on the swash-plate angle: a larger angle yields more displacement. Some compressors use a **variable displacement mechanism** with a swash-plate angle control chamber to modulate capacity based on cooling demand.

**Comparison with Scroll Compressor:**

| Feature | Swash-Plate | Scroll |
|---|---|---|
| Moving parts | Multiple pistons | 2 scroll elements |
| Noise | Higher (mechanical) | Lower (continuous compression) |
| Efficiency | Moderate | Higher (~10% better) |
| Reliability | Proven, robust | Sensitive to liquid slugging |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |
| Variable capacity | Easy (angle control) | Requires axial modulation |

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 6-cylinder swash-plate compressor has a swept volume of **150 cc/rev** and operates at **2,800 rpm**. Suction conditions are **1.8 bar** and **5 °C**. Discharge pressure is **15 bar**. Assuming isentropic compression for R-134a, calculate the **compressor work input** in kW. Use the standard enthalpy values: $h_1$ = 247 kJ/kg at suction; $h_2$ = 282 kJ/kg at discharge. Suction density ρ = **4.2 kg/m³**. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Volumetric flow rate.**

$$\dot{V} = V_{disp} \times \frac{N}{60} = 150 \times 10^{-6} \times \frac{2800}{60}$$

$$\dot{V} = 150 \times 10^{-6} \times 46.67 = 7.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 2: Mass flow rate.**

$$\dot{m} = \rho_{suc} \times \dot{V} = 4.2 \times 7.0 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\dot{m} = 0.0294 \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 3: Compressor work input.**

$$W_{comp} = \dot{m} \times (h_2 - h_1) = 0.0294 \times (282 - 247)$$

$$W_{comp} = 0.0294 \times 35 = 1.029 \text{ kW}$$

**Answer: W_comp ≈ 1.03 kW (≈ 1.38 HP)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks)
