**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the procedure for **diagnosis and replacement of an automotive A/C compressor** after a burnout failure. Mention the importance of system flushing, oil charging, and deep vacuum. `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Confirm Burnout**
Symptoms include metallic debris in the sight glass, dark or black oil, varnish smell from the system, compressor seizure, and very low suction pressure (due to internal bypass). Confirm by removing the receiver-drier screen and inspecting for metal particles.

**Step 2: Refrigerant Recovery**
Connect a recovery-recycle machine (per F-Gas regulations) and recover all refrigerant. **Never vent R-134a** to atmosphere.

**Step 3: Component Removal**
Remove the failed compressor, receiver-drier, expansion device, and (in many cases) the condenser. Inspect hoses for internal blockage.

**Step 4: System Flushing**
Flush the **suction and discharge lines**, evaporator, and (if retained) the condenser with an approved A/C flush solvent. The minimum flush volume Q_flush = k × V_system where k = 1.5–2.0. Use pressurized nitrogen (max 7 bar) to push solvent through. Collect and dispose of solvent as hazardous waste.

**Step 5: Oil Charge Calculation**
Calculate oil as derived in Section 3.1. Most OEMs recommend **85–90% of original charge** for a new compressor, with the balance distributed in the system.

**Step 6: Component Replacement**
Install new compressor (torque mounting bolts to **25–35 Nm**), new receiver-drier (must be sealed until installation), and a new expansion device (TXV or orifice tube).

**Step 7: Deep Vacuum & Moisture Removal**
Connect vacuum pump and evacuate to **500 microns** or lower for a **minimum of 30 minutes**. This removes non-condensables (air, nitrogen) and moisture. Break vacuum with dry nitrogen if leak testing, then re-evacuate.

**Step 8: Refrigerant Charging & Performance Verification**
Charge by weight (e.g., **650 ± 25 g** for a typical sedan). Run engine at **1,500 rpm**, A/C on MAX, and verify:
- Low-side: **1.5–2.5 bar**
- High-side: **12–18 bar**
- Center vent air: **3–8 °C**
- Compressor clutch current: **2.5–4.5 A**

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the **electromagnetic clutch** in an automotive A/C compressor. How is the air-gap measured and adjusted? What is the typical coil resistance value? `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer:**

**Construction of Electromagnetic Clutch:**
The clutch is mounted on the compressor's front snout and consists of:
1. **Rotor/Pulley** — belt-driven, rotates continuously when the engine runs. Has a friction surface facing the armature.
2. **Armature Plate** — splined to the compressor shaft; rides on the shaft but free to slide axially. Engages with the rotor when clutch is energized.
3. **Electromagnetic Coil** — stationary, mounted inside the rotor. Energized by 12 V DC through the compressor clutch relay and the **Thermostatic Switch / Pressure Switch**.
4. **Bearing** — supports the rotor face, allowing it to freewheel when disengaged.

**Working:**
When the cabin thermostat demands cooling, the ECU (or thermostat switch) energizes the clutch relay. Current flows through the coil, generating a magnetic field that pulls the armature axially into contact with the rotor friction surface. Belt torque is transferred to the compressor shaft. When the desired cabin temperature is reached, the coil de-energizes, the magnetic field collapses, and the armature returns via spring pressure.

**Air-Gap Measurement & Adjustment:**
Use a **feeler gauge** at three equidistant points (12, 4, and 8 o'clock positions) between the armature face and the rotor friction surface. Specification: typically **0.4 – 0.8 mm (0.016" – 0.032")**. Variation between the three readings should not exceed **0.05 mm**.

To adjust, remove shims behind the armature or use the threaded adjuster (varies by design). Repeat measurement until uniform.

**Typical Coil Resistance:**
- Most 12V systems: **3.5 – 5.0 Ω** at 25 °C
- Draw current: **2.5 – 4.5 A**
- Coil insulation: must withstand > **500 V** between windings and ground

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Air-Gap:** Students often confuse **axial air-gap** with **radial runout**. The specification is **axial** (perpendicular to the friction surface). Also, do not apply grease on the armature/pulley friction face—any lubricant will cause slippage and rapid wear.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 4 – Compressor Service**

- The compressor is the **prime mover** of the refrigeration cycle, raising refrigerant pressure and temperature.
- **Common compressor types:** Swash-plate, Scroll, Wobble-plate, Rotary vane, Electric scroll (for EVs).
- **PAG oil** is the standard lubricant for R-134a systems; **POE oil** is used for R-1234yf systems. PAG is **highly hygroscopic**.
- **Oil functions:** Lubrication, Sealing, Heat removal.
- **Typical oil charge:** 80–240 mL depending on compressor size.
- **Air-gap specification:** 0.4 – 0.8 mm (measured with feeler gauge at 3 points).
- **Clutch coil resistance:** 3.5 – 5.0 Ω.
- **Deep vacuum:** 500 microns minimum for 30 minutes before charging.
- **Burnout service** mandates: recover → flush → new compressor → new drier → new expansion device → vacuum → recharge.
- **Diagnostic pressures (idle, R-134a, 35 °C ambient):** Low-side 1.5–2.5 bar; High-side 12–18 bar.
- **Superheat target:** 5–12 °C; **Subcooling target:** 3–8 °C.
- **Refrigerant R-134a GWP = 1430**; **R-1234yf GWP = 4** — explains OEM migration to R-1234yf.
- **Clutch slippage** causes: low voltage, weak spring, oil contamination on friction face, excessive air-gap.
- **Liquid slugging** = hydromechanical shock; prevented by proper TXV/orifice function and suction-line accumulator (in orifice-tube systems).
- **Electric compressors** in BEVs/HEVs eliminate the clutch and belt-drive; speed is controlled by HV inverter via the vehicle's CAN bus.
- **Never vent refrigerant** to atmosphere—mandatory recovery per environmental regulations.
- **Compressor mount torque:** 25–35 Nm (verify per OEM manual).
- **Sight glass check:** rapid bubbles indicate low charge or restriction; oily streaks indicate overcharge or wrong oil type.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Methods of Dehydration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT523 - AUTOMOTIVE REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Methods of Dehydration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Automotive Refrigeration and Air Conditioning**, **Dehydration** is the systematic, engineered process of removing residual moisture (water vapor) and non-condensable contaminants from every internal component of a closed-loop refrigerant circuit. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (Module 4), dehydration is a mandatory service procedure executed after any breach of the sealed refrigeration system — including component replacement, hose repair, compressor failure, or extended system exposure to ambient atmosphere.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Definition (KTU 2024):**
> Dehydration is defined as the elimination of moisture from a refrigeration system to prevent the formation of ice at the expansion device, to inhibit chemical reactions (hydrolysis) between water and the refrigerant/lubricant, and to prevent internal corrosion, acid formation, and copper plating.

## 1.2 Why Moisture is the Silent Enemy of an A/C System

Water molecules enter an automotive A/C system primarily through:
1. **Incomplete factory evacuation** during manufacturing
2. **Improper service procedures** (opening a charged system without recovery)
3. **Leakage** through hose permeation or seal degradation
4. **Diffusion** of atmospheric moisture through rubber hoses
5. **Contaminated refrigerant** or incorrect oil charging

Even trace amounts of moisture (in the range of **25–50 ppm** by mass) can be catastrophic for the system.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Threshold Rule (Industry Standard):**
> The maximum permissible moisture content in a properly dehydrated automotive R-134a system is **< 50 ppm** (parts per million by mass). For R-1234yf systems, the limit is even stricter at **< 30 ppm** due to the refrigerant's chemical sensitivity.

## 1.3 Harmful Effects of Moisture Inside the Circuit

| Sl. No. | Effect | Location of Occurrence | Severity |
|:-------:|--------|------------------------|:--------:|
| 1 | **Ice Plugging** at the expansion valve/orifice tube | TXV / OT | High |
| 2 | **Acid Formation** (HCl, HF) via hydrolysis of refrigerant + oil | Compressor, Receiver-Drier | Critical |
| 3 | **Copper Plating** (Cu deposits on iron surfaces) | Compressor bearings, shaft | Critical |
| 4 | **Sludge and Varnish** formation in lubricant | Compressor crankcase | Moderate |
| 5 | **Internal Corrosion** of metallic components | All metal surfaces | High |
| 6 | **Loss of Lubricity** of PAG/POE oil | Compressor bearings | High |
| 7 | **Increased Discharge Temperature** | Compressor head | Moderate |

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The "Dry Bones" Principle

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Thirsty Sponge in a Sealed Bottle"**
> Imagine a perfectly sealed, pressurized plastic bottle containing only dry air. If you drop a tiny moist sponge inside (representing residual moisture from a leak or service), and then you pump the bottle up to high pressure, the sponge slowly releases its water vapor into the sealed air. Over time, this vapor will condense, freeze, and corrode. **Dehydration is the act of squeezing that sponge completely dry *before* you seal the bottle** — and adding a permanent moisture-absorbing filter inside the bottle to keep it dry forever.

In an A/C system, that "permanent sponge" is the **Receiver-Drier / Accumulator-Drier** containing **desiccant beads**.

## 1.5 Standard Moisture Measurement Reference

The industry quantifies dehydration efficacy using these constants:

- **Absolute Vacuum Limit:** $1 \text{ mmHg} \; (1000 \; \mu\text{m Hg}) \approx 133.3 \text{ Pa}$
- **Boiling Point of Water at Vacuum:** at 1000 µm Hg, water boils at approximately **$15^\circ\text{C}$**
- **Dew Point Target:** below **$-40^\circ\text{C}$** for the system
- **Standard Drier Capacity:** absorbs **6–14 grams** of water (sized per vehicle tonnage)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Dehydration Methods

Dehydration methods are classified into two broad families based on the **physical state** of the process:

### A. **Active (External-Powered) Methods**
These require external equipment (vacuum pump, heat source, nitrogen).

1. **Heat and Vacuum Method** (most widely used in workshops)
2. **Nitrogen Pressure Purge Method** (used in OEM assembly lines)
3. **Heat and Dry-Air/Vacuum Cycling Method**

### B. **Passive (Built-In Absorption) Methods**
These rely on desiccants installed permanently inside the system.

1. **Liquid Line Drier (Receiver-Drier)**
2. **Suction Line Accumulator-Drier**
3. **Discharge Line Drier** (acid removal service)
4. **Molded Desiccant Cores** (modern OEM practice)

---

## 2.2 Detailed Operating Principles

### 2.2.1 Heat and Vacuum Method (Workshop Procedure)

**Principle (The Why & The How):**

- Water inside a sealed refrigeration system exists as **liquid droplets** trapped in low spots (condenser bottom, evaporator, hoses) and as **vapor** in the gas space.
- The **boiling point of water decreases dramatically** as pressure drops.
  - At 1 atm: water boils at $100^\circ\text{C}$
  - At 1000 µm Hg: water boils at $15^\circ\text{C}$
  - At 500 µm Hg: water boils at $0^\circ\text{C}$
- Therefore, by **applying heat** (driving water out of crevices via evaporation) **and vacuum simultaneously**, the technician can vaporize and evacuate nearly all internal moisture.

**Step-by-Step Workshop Procedure:**

1. **Connect the manifold gauge set** to the high-side and low-side service ports.
2. **Connect a two-stage rotary vacuum pump** capable of pulling **< 50 µm Hg** ultimate vacuum.
3. **Open both manifold valves** and start the vacuum pump.
4. **Run the pump for a minimum of 30 minutes** for typical automotive systems; 45–60 minutes for larger buses/trucks.
5. **Close the manifold valves**, **shut off the pump**, and **isolate the system**.
6. **Observe the vacuum gauge for 10–15 minutes.** If the vacuum rises (pressure creeps upward), moisture is still present — re-evacuate.
7. **Break the vacuum with dry nitrogen** (if a triple evacuation is used) and repeat steps 3–6.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Triple Evacuation Rule (Industry Best Practice):**
> OEM service manuals (SAE J2788, J2842) mandate that after a major system repair, the technician should perform **three evacuation cycles** broken by dry nitrogen charges to ensure complete moisture removal. This is because the first vacuum pulls out ~70% of moisture, the second ~25%, and the third removes the stubborn final 5%.

### 2.2.2 Solid Desiccant Method (Filter-Driers)

**Desiccant Materials Used:**

| Desiccant | Chemical Formula | Moisture Capacity | Application |
|-----------|------------------|-------------------|-------------|
| **Silica Gel** | $\text{SiO}_2 \cdot n\text{H}_2\text{O}$ | Low (~10% by mass) | R-12 legacy systems |
| **Activated Alumina** | $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$ | Medium (~15%) | Acid neutralization |
| **Molecular Sieve 3A** | $\text{K}_n\text{Na}_{12-n}[(\text{AlO}_2)_{12}(\text{SiO}_2)_{12}] \cdot x\text{H}_2\text{O}$ | High (~20%) | **R-134a, R-1234yf** (modern standard) |
| **Molecular Sieve 4A** | Sodium aluminosilicate | High | HFC systems |
| **Calcium Sulphate** | $\text{CaSO}_4$ | Low | Drying agent indicator |

**Why Molecular Sieve 3A is the Modern Standard for R-134a:**

- The pore aperture is **3 Ångström (0.3 nm)**, which admits only water molecules (2.65 Å) but **excludes refrigerant molecules** (R-134a = 4.2 Å).
- This prevents the desiccant from absorbing and trapping the expensive refrigerant — a major problem with silica gel.
- Molecular sieve is **shape-selective**: physically filters by molecular size.

### 2.2.3 Liquid Line Drier (Receiver-Drier) — Functional Description

Installed in the **high-pressure liquid line**, between the **condenser outlet** and the **expansion valve inlet**.

**Internal Construction:**
- A **solid metal cylinder** with inlet and outlet screens (40–80 mesh)
- Contains a **desiccant core** (molecular sieve + activated alumina blend)
- Often includes a **filter pad** to trap particulates
- A **spring-loaded baffle** at the outlet to prevent desiccant erosion
- May contain a **moisture-indicator sight glass** at the outlet (color changes from blue/green to yellow when saturated — for R-12 systems)

**Flow Path Inside a Receiver-Drier:**

$$ \text{Refrigerant In} \rightarrow \text{Filter Screen} \rightarrow \text{Desiccant Bed} \rightarrow \text{Baffle/Spring} \rightarrow \text{Filter Screen} \rightarrow \text{Refrigerant Out} $$

### 2.2.4 Suction Line Accumulator-Drier — Functional Description

Installed in the **low-pressure suction line**, between the **evaporator outlet** and the **compressor inlet**.

**Why a Suction Accumulator is Different from a Receiver-Drier:**

- It must **trap any liquid refrigerant** that didn't evaporate in the evaporator (preventing liquid slugging of the compressor).
- Contains a **U-shaped pickup tube** with a **desiccant bag** wrapped around it.
- Acts as a **moisture + liquid safety device** in a single component.
- Used in systems with a **fixed orifice tube (OT)** instead of a TXV.

### 2.2.5 Discharge Line Drier (Service-Only, Acid Removal)

Installed temporarily in the **discharge line** when acid contamination is suspected (e.g., after a compressor burn-out).

**Operating Principle:**

- The compressor's discharge gas is **hot** (80–120°C) and **superheated**.
- A special high-temperature acid-removal drier is inserted in line.
- The high temperature drives off moisture and helps neutralize acids.
- The drier is **oversized** to handle the high flow rate.
- **Removed after service** — it is not a permanent OEM component.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Quantity | Symbol Meaning | Engineering Use |
|:-:|---------------------|----------------|-----------------|
| 1 | $P_{vac} = P_{atm} - P_{gauge}$ | $P_{vac}$: Absolute vacuum pressure; $P_{atm}$: Atmospheric; $P_{gauge}$: Gauge pressure | Convert vacuum gauge reading to absolute |
| 2 | $T_{boil}(P) = f(P)$ (steam tables) | $T_{boil}$: Boiling point of water; $P$: Absolute pressure | Determine evaporation temperature under vacuum |
| 3 | $V_{pump} \geq 3 \times V_{system}$ | $V_{pump}$: Pump displacement; $V_{system}$: System volume | Vacuum pump sizing rule (CFM / L/min) |
| 4 | $C_{H_2O} \leq 50 \text{ ppm}$ (R-134a); $\leq 30 \text{ ppm}$ (R-1234yf) | $C_{H_2O}$: Moisture concentration | Pass/Fail criterion for dehydration |
| 5 | $D_{size} = \frac{\dot{m}_{ref}}{q_{des}}$ | $D_{size}$: Drier capacity; $\dot{m}_{ref}$: Refrigerant mass flow; $q_{des}$: Specific moisture capacity | Drier selection for OEM design |
| 6 | $t_{evac} \geq 30 \text{ min}$ (small car); $\geq 60 \text{ min}$ (bus/truck) | $t_{evac}$: Minimum evacuation time | Workshop time standard |
| 7 | $P_{vac,min} = 1000 \; \mu\text{m Hg}$ | $P_{vac,min}$: Minimum required vacuum | SAE J2788 threshold |
| 8 | $\text{Dew Point} \leq -40^\circ\text{C}$ | — | Final dryness verification |
| 9 | $M_{H_2O,trapped} = C_{H_2O} \times M_{ref}$ | $M_{H_2O,trapped}$: Trapped water mass; $M_{ref}$: Refrigerant charge | Mass of water to be removed by desiccant |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** Question 1 in Part A and the first sub-part of Part B almost always test the *ppm limits* and the *boiling-point-under-vacuum* concept. Memorize the $1000 \; \mu\text{m Hg} \rightarrow 15^\circ\text{C}$ data point.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **OEM Assembly Plants:** Triple evacuation + dry-air sweep before final refrigerant charge.
- **After-Sales Service:** Single-stage vacuum + filter-drier replacement after component repair.
- **Field Repairs on Buses/Trucks:** Portable vacuum pump (≥ 6 CFM) + electronic scale for refrigerant.
- **Refrigerant Recycling Stations:** Moisture analysers (capacitance-type, Karl Fischer titration) are used for verification.
- **Electric Vehicle (EV) A/C Systems:** Even stricter dehydration due to the high voltage compressor's sensitivity to acid-induced insulation failure.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why Vacuum Reduces the Boiling Point of Water

This derivation is essential for KTU Part B questions that ask students to "explain the role of vacuum in moisture removal."

### Step 1 — State the Governing Thermodynamic Relationship

The **Clausius–Clapeyron Equation** governs the phase boundary between liquid water and water vapor:

$$
\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{L}{T \cdot \Delta v}
$$

where
- $L$ = latent heat of vaporization of water $\approx 2257 \; \text{kJ/kg}$
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)
- $\Delta v$ = specific volume change $(v_g - v_f)$
- $P$ = saturation pressure (Pa)

### Step 2 — Apply the Ideal-Gas Approximation for Vapor

Since $v_f \ll v_g$, we set $\Delta v \approx v_g = \dfrac{RT}{P}$, where $R = 0.4615 \; \text{kJ/(kg·K)}$ is the **specific gas constant for water vapor**.

### Step 3 — Substitute into the Clausius–Clapeyron Equation

$$
\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{L \cdot P}{R \cdot T^2}
$$

### Step 4 — Separate Variables and Integrate

$$
\int \frac{dP}{P} = \frac{L}{R} \int \frac{dT}{T^2}
$$

$$
\ln P = -\frac{L}{R \cdot T} + C
$$

### Step 5 — Apply the Boundary Condition (Boiling Point at 1 atm)

At $T_1 = 373.15 \text{ K}$ (i.e., $100^\circ\text{C}$), $P_1 = 101325 \text{ Pa}$.

$$
C = \ln(101325) + \frac{L}{R \cdot 373.15}
$$

### Step 6 — Solve for Boiling Point at Reduced Pressure

Rearranging for $T$ at a given pressure $P$:

$$
\frac{1}{T} = \frac{R}{L} \ln\!\left(\frac{101325}{P}\right) + \frac{1}{373.15}
$$

### Step 7 — Numerical Evaluation at Standard Vacuum Levels

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{At } P = 1000 \; \mu\text{m Hg} &= 0.1333 \; \text{kPa} = 133.3 \; \text{Pa} \\
\frac{1}{T_2} &= \frac{0.4615}{2257} \ln\!\left(\frac{101325}{133.3}\right) + \frac{1}{373.15} \\
&= (2.045 \times 10^{-4})(6.696) + 2.680 \times 10^{-3} \\
&= 1.369 \times 10^{-3} + 2.680 \times 10^{-3} \\
&= 4.049 \times 10^{-3} \\
T_2 &\approx 247.0 \text{ K} = -26.2^\circ\text{C}
\end{aligned}
$$

This theoretical value closely matches the empirical workshop value of **$-26^\circ\text{C}$** at 1000 µm Hg, confirming that water vapor can be drawn out of the system at room temperature under deep vacuum.

> [!NOTE]
> The Clausius–Clapeyron derivation shows that the **lower the absolute pressure, the lower the temperature at which water can be forced to vaporize**. This is the physical reason why vacuum is so effective in dehydration — the moisture does not need to be "heated to 100°C"; it simply vaporizes because the partial pressure is below its saturation pressure at ambient temperature.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example: Calculate the Water Mass to be Removed

**Problem:** An automotive A/C system has a refrigerant charge of $0.900 \text{ kg}$ of R-134a. The moisture content is measured at $120 \text{ ppm}$. Determine (a) the mass of water trapped, and (b) whether a standard drier of capacity 8 g is sufficient.

### Step 1 — Convert ppm to a Mass Fraction

$$
\text{ppm} = \frac{m_{H_2O}}{m_{ref}} \times 10^6
$$

$$
\frac{m_{H_2O}}{m_{ref}} = \frac{120}{10^6} = 1.20 \times 10^{-4}
$$

### Step 2 — Calculate the Trapped Water Mass

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{H_2O} &= m_{ref} \times 1.20 \times 10^{-4} \\
&= 0.900 \times 1.20 \times 10^{-4} \\
&= 1.08 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg} \\
&= 108 \text{ mg}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Compare with Drier Capacity

$$
\text{Drier Capacity} = 8 \text{ g} = 8000 \text{ mg}
$$

$$
\text{Surplus Capacity} = 8000 - 108 = 7892 \text{ mg}
$$

### Step 4 — Conclusion

A standard **8-gram molecular sieve drier** is more than sufficient (utilization only **1.35%**). However, after a major service where system has been open for several hours, the moisture ingress can reach **5–15 g**, justifying **drier replacement as a mandatory step** in any major service.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Dehydration Method to Use?

| Service Scenario | Recommended Primary Method | Secondary Method | Drier Replacement? |
|------------------|----------------------------|------------------|:------------------:|
| Annual A/C service (gas top-up) | Heat + Vacuum (single stage) | Sight-glass moisture check | **Yes** (preventive) |
| Compressor replacement | Heat + Vacuum (triple evacuation) | Discharge-line acid drier | **Yes** (mandatory) |
| Condenser replacement | Triple evacuation + dry N₂ | Liquid-line drier upgrade | **Yes** |
| Hose / line repair | Heat + Vacuum (double evacuation) | Suction-line drier | **Yes** |
| Evaporator replacement | Heat + Vacuum (triple evacuation) | Suction accumulator replacement | **Yes** |
| System open > 4 hours | Heat + Vacuum + N₂ purge | Full drier upgrade + oil change | **Yes** |
| Mild underperformance, no leak | Single-stage vacuum | Sight-glass check only | **Optional** |
| Severe acid burn-out | Heat + Vacuum + Discharge drier | Suction + Liquid line driers + oil flush | **Yes** (all) |
| R-1234yf conversion | Triple evacuation (extra-long) | OEM-spec molecular sieve 3A | **Yes** |
| EV high-voltage compressor | Triple evacuation + dry room | Special low-moisture oil | **Yes** |

---

## 3.4 Worked Example: Vacuum Hold Test (Leak & Moisture Indicator)

**Problem:** After a heat-and-vacuum dehydration, the technician isolates the system and observes the following vacuum readings:

- Initial isolation pressure: **800 µm Hg**
- After 15 minutes: **950 µm Hg**
- After 30 minutes: **1080 µm Hg**

Diagnose the condition and recommend the next action.

### Step 1 — Interpret the Pressure Rise

$$
\Delta P_{15} = 950 - 800 = 150 \; \mu\text{m Hg}
$$

$$
\Delta P_{30} = 1080 - 800 = 280 \; \mu\text{m Hg}
$$

### Step 2 — Apply the Diagnostic Rules

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SAE J2788 Vacuum Hold Test Criteria:**
> - Pressure rise **< 200 µm Hg in 15 min** → **PASS** (system is dry and leak-tight)
> - Pressure rise **200–500 µm Hg in 15 min** → **MOISTURE** present (re-evacuate with break)
> - Pressure rise **> 500 µm Hg in 15 min** → **LEAK** present (perform leak test)

### Step 3 — Diagnosis for This Case

The 15-minute rise of **150 µm Hg** is below the 200 µm threshold → the system is **moisture-free and leak-tight**. The technician can proceed to **break the vacuum with refrigerant vapor** and charge the system.

---

## 3.5 Comparison Table: Receiver-Drier vs Accumulator-Drier

| Feature | Receiver-Drier (Liquid Line) | Accumulator-Drier (Suction Line) |
|---------|------------------------------|-----------------------------------|
| **Location** | High-pressure liquid line | Low-pressure suction line |
| **Primary Function** | Moisture removal + filtration | Liquid trapping + moisture removal |
| **Used with** | TXV (Thermostatic Expansion Valve) | OT (Orifice Tube) |
| **Internal Design** | Solid desiccant core + screens | U-tube + desiccant bag |
| **Sight Glass** | Often included | Not typical |
| **Pressure** | High side (1.5–2.5 MPa) | Low side (0.15–0.3 MPa) |
| **Replacement Frequency** | Every 2–3 years / every service | Every 2–3 years / every service |
| **Failure Mode** | Saturated desiccant, internal screen rupture | Desiccant bag tear, U-tube crack |
| **Service Tip** | Always replace after compressor failure | Always replace after evaporator leak |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Dehydration Process Flow

This diagram maps the **operational sequence** of a workshop dehydration procedure, showing inputs, decision gates, and outputs.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: System Opened for Service] --> B[Recover Existing Refrigerant]
    B --> C[Identify Service Scope: Minor / Major / Burnout]
    C --> D{Major Repair or Burnout?}
    D -- No --> E[Install Vacuum Pump to Service Ports]
    D -- Yes --> F[Install High-Capacity Vacuum Pump 6 CFM or greater]
    E --> G[Pull Vacuum 1000 micrometers Hg]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Run Vacuum for 30 to 60 Minutes]
    H --> I[Close Manifold Valves and Isolate System]
    I --> J[Vacuum Hold Test: Monitor 15 Minutes]
    J --> K{Pressure Rise Threshold}
    K -- Rise less than 200 micrometers --> L[Pass: System is Dry and Tight]
    K -- Rise 200 to 500 micrometers --> M[Moisture Detected: Break Vacuum with Dry Nitrogen]
    M --> N[Re Evacuate: Second Cycle]
    N --> J
    K -- Rise greater than 500 micrometers --> O[Leak Detected: Perform Pressure Leak Test]
    O --> P[Repair Leak and Return to Step E]
    L --> Q[Break Vacuum with Refrigerant Vapor]
    Q --> R[Charge System to Specified Mass]
    R --> S[Install New Liquid Line Drier and Accumulator]
    S --> T[End: Leak Test and Performance Verification]
```

---

## 4.2 System-Level Block Diagram: Components Involved in Dehydration

This diagram shows the **physical placement** of all desiccant and filter components within an automotive A/C circuit.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CMP[Compressor] -->|Hot Discharge Gas| DCH[Discharge Line]
    DCH --> CND[Condenser]
    CND -->|High Pressure Liquid| LLD[Liquid Line Drier: Receiver]
    LLD --> SG1[Moisture Indicator Sight Glass]
    SG1 --> EXV[Expansion Valve: TXV or Orifice Tube]
    EXV --> EVP[Evaporator]
    EVP -->|Low Pressure Vapor| ACC[Suction Line Accumulator Drier]
    ACC -->|Dry Suction Vapor| CMP
    ACC -.->|Traps Liquid Refrigerant| LIQ[Liquid Slug Reservoir]
    LLD -.->|Absorbs Water via Molecular Sieve 3A| DSC[Desiccant Core]
    ACC -.->|Absorbs Water via Desiccant Bag| DSC2[Desiccant Bag]
    DSC -.->|Filters 3 Angstrom Pores Only Water| MOI[Water Molecule 2.65 Angstrom]
    DSC -.->|Rejects Refrigerant Molecule 4.2 Angstrom| REF[R134a Molecule]
```

---

## 4.3 Process Comparison Diagram: Heat-Vacuum vs Solid Desiccant

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Moisture Inside Sealed A C System] --> P2{Dehydration Strategy}
    P2 -- Active Workshop Process --> P3[Heat and Vacuum Method]
    P2 -- Passive Built In Component --> P4[Solid Desiccant Filter Drier]
    P3 --> P31[Step 1: Connect Vacuum Pump]
    P31 --> P32[Step 2: Apply Heat 40 to 60 C]
    P32 --> P33[Step 3: Pull Vacuum 1000 micrometers Hg]
    P33 --> P34[Step 4: Hold 30 to 60 Minutes]
    P34 --> P35[Step 5: Verify with Hold Test]
    P4 --> P41[Refrigerant Flows Through Drier]
    P41 --> P42[Desiccant Pores 3 Angstrom Absorb Water]
    P42 --> P43[Refrigerant Molecules Pass Through]
    P43 --> P44[Drier Saturates Over Time 2 to 3 Years]
    P44 --> P45[Replace Drier on Schedule]
```

---

## 4.4 Reference State Diagram: Water Phase Change Under Vacuum

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> LiquidTrapped: Service Begins
    LiquidTrapped --> Vapor: Apply Vacuum Below 133 Pa
    Vapor --> Vapor: Heat Accelerates Vaporization
    Vapor --> Evacuated: Vacuum Pump Removes Vapor
    Evacuated --> DrySystem: Hold Test Passes
    DrySystem --> RefrigerantCharge: Break Vacuum with R134a
    RefrigerantCharge --> Operational: System Returns to Service
    Evacuated --> MoistureDetected: Hold Test Fails
    MoistureDetected --> Vapor: Re Evacuate with Dry N2 Break
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU Exam:** The arrows in the state diagram represent **physical/thermodynamic transitions** of the water molecules inside the system. During the viva, you may be asked: *"At which point is the moisture actually removed from the system?"* — The answer is the transition from **Vapor → Evacuated**, when the vapor exits the service hose into the vacuum pump.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: List any three harmful effects of moisture in an automotive refrigeration system. State the maximum permissible moisture content for an R-134a system.**

**Model Answer:**

**Three harmful effects of moisture:**

1. **Ice Formation at the Expansion Device:** Water freezes at temperatures below $0^\circ\text{C}$. Since the expansion valve/orifice tube operates between $-5^\circ\text{C}$ and $5^\circ\text{C}$, ice crystals form and physically block the small orifice, restricting refrigerant flow and causing the evaporator to starve.

2. **Acid Formation (Hydrolysis):** Water reacts chemically with the refrigerant (R-134a) and PAG oil to form **hydrofluoric acid (HF)** and **hydrochloric acid (HCl)**. These acids attack internal metal surfaces, degrade the lubricant, and destroy motor winding insulation.

3. **Copper Plating:** In the presence of moisture, the oil's ester additives ionize copper from the suction line. Copper ions plate onto the **steel bearing surfaces** of the compressor, causing premature bearing failure.

**Maximum permissible moisture content for R-134a:** **< 50 ppm** (parts per million by mass).

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: What is a molecular sieve? Why is it preferred over silica gel as a desiccant in modern automotive A/C systems?**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** A **molecular sieve** is a synthetic crystalline aluminosilicate (zeolite) with a uniform, precisely-sized pore structure (typically **3 Å = 0.3 nm** for automotive use). It is used as a desiccant to selectively adsorb water molecules from refrigerant streams.

**Why preferred over silica gel:**

1. **Shape Selectivity:** The 3 Å pore aperture admits only small water molecules (diameter 2.65 Å) but physically **rejects the larger refrigerant molecules** (R-134a = 4.2 Å). Silica gel adsorbs *both* water *and* refrigerant indiscriminately, leading to expensive refrigerant loss and reduced drier life.

2. **Higher Moisture Capacity:** Molecular sieve 3A can absorb up to **20% of its own mass** in water, compared to only ~10% for silica gel.

3. **Thermal Stability:** Molecular sieve remains effective up to **250°C**, suitable for hot discharge-line service driers, whereas silica gel loses capacity above 120°C.

4. **Compatibility with PAG/POE Oils:** Molecular sieve is chemically inert with modern synthetic lubricants, while silica gel can react with additives.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 - Model Paper]`

**Q: With the help of a neat sketch, explain the "Heat and Vacuum Method" of dehydrating an automotive A/C system. List the equipment required, the procedural steps, and the pass/fail criteria for the vacuum hold test.**

**Sub-part (a): Procedure, Equipment, and Vacuum Hold Test Criteria (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Equipment Required:**

1. **Two-stage rotary vacuum pump** (capacity ≥ 6 CFM, ultimate vacuum < 50 µm Hg)
2. **Manifold gauge set** (high + low side, with vacuum-rated hoses)
3. **Digital vacuum gauge** (more accurate than manifold gauges below 1000 µm)
4. **Heat source** (infrared lamp or heat gun — max $60^\circ\text{C}$)
5. **Dry nitrogen cylinder** (for triple evacuation method)
6. **Refrigerant identifier** (to verify R-134a purity)
7. **Leak detector** (electronic or UV-dye)
8. **Service port adapters** for the specific vehicle

**Procedural Steps:**

1. **Park the vehicle** in a shaded, well-ventilated area. Verify ambient temperature is between $15^\circ\text{C}$ and $35^\circ\text{C}$.
2. **Recover any residual refrigerant** from the system using a recovery machine — never vent to atmosphere.
3. **Connect the manifold gauge set** to both the high-side and low-side service ports. Verify hose integrity.
4. **Connect the centre hose of the manifold** to the vacuum pump inlet.
5. **Open both manifold valves** fully and **start the vacuum pump**.
6. **Monitor the vacuum gauge.** It should drop rapidly to below 1000 µm Hg within 5–10 minutes.
7. **Apply mild external heat** (max $50^\circ\text{C}$) to the condenser bottom, evaporator housing, and compressor sump to drive trapped moisture from low spots.
8. **Run the vacuum pump for 30 minutes** (small cars) or **45–60 minutes** (buses/trucks).
9. **Close both manifold valves** and **shut off the pump**. Note the absolute vacuum reading: $P_0$.
10. **Wait 15 minutes** (Vacuum Hold Test). Note the new reading: $P_{15}$.
11. **Compute the pressure rise:** $\Delta P = P_{15} - P_0$.

**Pass/Fail Criteria (SAE J2788):**

| Rise in 15 min | Verdict | Action |
|:--------------:|---------|--------|
| $< 200 \; \mu\text{m Hg}$ | **PASS** | Break vacuum with refrigerant, charge system |
| $200 - 500 \; \mu\text{m Hg}$ | **MOISTURE** | Break vacuum with dry N₂, re-evacuate, repeat test |
| $> 500 \; \mu\text{m Hg}$ | **LEAK** | Pressurize with N₂ + leak detector, repair leak, restart from step 1 |

---

**Sub-part (b): Sketch and Working Principle (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The Heat and Vacuum Method works on the **Clausius–Clapeyron principle**: at reduced absolute pressure, the boiling point of water drops dramatically. At 1000 µm Hg (0.133 kPa), water boils at approximately **$-26^\circ\text{C}$** — meaning that even at room temperature, water trapped in the system **vaporizes** and is drawn out by the vacuum pump. Heating accelerates this process by increasing the vapor pressure of trapped liquid water, ensuring it leaves crevices and oil films.

**Block Schematic (descriptive, in lieu of drawing on paper):**

- Vacuum pump (centre port) → Manifold High-Side Valve → Service Port (H)
- Vacuum pump (centre port) → Manifold Low-Side Valve → Service Port (L)
- Heat source applied externally to: Condenser, Evaporator, Compressor, Receiver-Drier

**Why Triple Evacuation is Superior:**

A single evacuation removes only ~70% of the moisture. By breaking the vacuum with **dry nitrogen** (a non-condensable, dry gas) and re-evacuating, the residual moisture is progressively stripped. Three cycles typically achieve **> 99%** moisture removal.

**Safety Precautions:**

- Never run the vacuum pump with the vehicle's engine on.
- Never apply heat above $60^\circ\text{C}$ (risk of damaging rubber hoses and O-rings).
- Always wear safety goggles — oil mist from the pump can be irritating.
- Verify all connections are tight before starting.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Q: Compare the construction, function, and application of a Liquid Line Receiver-Drier and a Suction Line Accumulator-Drier. Under what conditions must each be replaced?**

**Sub-part (a): Construction and Function (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**A. Liquid Line Receiver-Drier:**

**Location:** Installed in the high-pressure liquid line, between the **condenser outlet** and the **expansion valve (TXV) inlet**.

**Construction (from inlet to outlet):**
1. Inlet connection (flare or O-ring)
2. **Inlet filter screen** (40 mesh) — traps weld flash and metal debris
3. **Filter pad** (fibre/foam) — captures fine particulates
4. **Desiccant core** — molecular sieve 3A + activated alumina blend
5. **Outlet filter screen** (60 mesh)
6. **Spring-loaded outlet baffle** — prevents desiccant erosion
7. Outlet connection
8. Optional **moisture-indicator sight glass** — colour changes (blue→yellow for R-12)

**Function:**
- **Removes residual moisture** from the liquid refrigerant via adsorption
- **Filters particulates** that could clog the expansion valve
- Acts as a **liquid reservoir** to ensure 100% liquid flow to the TXV
- Buffers refrigerant volume as operating conditions change

**B. Suction Line Accumulator-Drier:**

**Location:** Installed in the low-pressure suction line, between the **evaporator outlet** and the **compressor inlet**.

**Construction (from inlet to outlet):**
1. Inlet connection from evaporator
2. **Inlet filter screen**
3. **Vessel body** (large diameter, acts as liquid trap)
4. **U-shaped pickup tube** extending to the bottom of the vessel
5. **Desiccant bag** wrapped around the U-tube
6. **Outlet screen** at the top
7. Outlet connection to compressor suction port
8. **Pressure relief port** (some designs)

**Function:**
- **Traps any liquid refrigerant** that didn't fully evaporate — prevents **liquid slugging** of the compressor
- **Removes moisture** from the low-pressure vapour stream
- **Filters contaminants** before they reach the compressor
- Buffers refrigerant mass during high/low cycle changes

**Key Difference:** The accumulator-drier is **larger** and has a **liquid-separator function** built-in, while the receiver-drier is a **compact, high-pressure filtration + desiccant unit**.

---

**Sub-part (b): Application and Replacement Criteria (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**When is a Liquid Line Receiver-Drier used?**
- Systems with a **Thermostatic Expansion Valve (TXV)** as the metering device
- Vehicles using **R-12, R-134a, or R-1234yf**
- High-pressure side moisture control

**When is a Suction Line Accumulator-Drier used?**
- Systems with a **fixed Orifice Tube (OT)** as the metering device
- Most modern cars (Honda, Toyota, Hyundai use OT-based systems)
- Systems with high risk of liquid carry-over to compressor

**Mandatory Replacement Conditions:**

| Component | Replace When... |
|-----------|----------------|
| **Receiver-Drier** | • After **any** major system repair (compressor, condenser, evaporator replacement) <br> • **Every 2–3 years** as preventive maintenance <br> • Sight glass shows **yellow/pink** colour (R-12 systems) <br> • System has been **open to atmosphere** for more than 15 minutes <br> • **Acid contamination** is suspected |
| **Accumulator-Drier** | • After **evaporator replacement** or leak repair <br> • **Every 2–3 years** <br> • After **compressor burn-out** (always replace) <br> • When **suction line** is opened for any reason <br> • When moisture-indicating **tester** shows > 50 ppm |

**Why Replacement is Mandatory (not optional):**

Once a desiccant is **saturated**, it cannot be "recharged" in the field. The only option is **replacement**. A saturated drier:
- Allows moisture to circulate freely
- Releases trapped contaminants back into the system
- May even **release** previously absorbed acids and particulates under thermal cycling
- Compromises the entire refrigeration performance

**Cost vs Benefit:** A receiver-drier costs ₹500–1500; replacing a compressor costs ₹15,000–40,000. **The economics strongly favour routine drier replacement.**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Confusing "dehydration" with "evacuation".** Evacuation is *part of* dehydration. Dehydration = evacuation + heat + desiccant + verification.
> 2. **Wrong units for moisture:** Students write "50 mg" instead of "**50 ppm**". Always use **ppm (parts per million by mass)** for moisture limits.
> 3. **Silica gel is NOT used in modern R-134a systems.** Examiners will deduct marks for this. Use "**molecular sieve 3A**" for all 2024-scheme answers.
> 4. **Receiver-Drier ≠ Accumulator-Drier.** These are *not* interchangeable. Match the component to the metering device (TXV vs OT).
> 5. **Forgetting the "Hold Test"** in heat-and-vacuum procedure answers. The hold test is the *verification* step that earns you the final 2 marks.
> 6. **Writing "100°C" as the boiling point under vacuum.** The whole point is that it boils at **room temperature** under deep vacuum — this is the conceptual core of the method.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist (Read this 30 minutes before the exam):**

- [ ] **Definition:** Dehydration = removal of moisture from the sealed refrigeration system to prevent ice, acid, copper plating, and corrosion.
- [ ] **Moisture limit (R-134a):** $< 50 \text{ ppm}$; **(R-1234yf):** $< 30 \text{ ppm}$.
- [ ] **Boiling point under vacuum:** $1000 \; \mu\text{m Hg} \Rightarrow$ water boils at **$-26^\circ\text{C}$** (practically $\approx 15^\circ\text{C}$).
- [ ] **Minimum workshop vacuum:** **$1000 \; \mu\text{m Hg}$** absolute.
- [ ] **Evacuation time:** 30 min (small car) / 60 min (bus/truck).
- [ ] **Hold test pass:** $< 200 \; \mu\text{m Hg}$ rise in 15 min.
- [ ] **Hold test fail (moisture):** $200$–$500 \; \mu\text{m Hg}$ rise.
- [ ] **Hold test fail (leak):** $> 500 \; \mu\text{m Hg}$ rise.
- [ ] **Triple evacuation:** breaks vacuum with **dry nitrogen**; 3 cycles.
- [ ] **Desiccant order (oldest to newest):** Silica gel → Activated alumina → **Molecular sieve 3A** (current standard).
- [ ] **Molecular sieve 3A pore size:** **3 Å (0.3 nm)** — admits $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ (2.65 Å), rejects R-134a (4.2 Å).
- [ ] **Receiver-Drier location:** liquid line, **before TXV**.
- [ ] **Accumulator-Drier location:** suction line, **after evaporator, before compressor**.
- [ ] **TXV system → Receiver-Drier**; **OT system → Accumulator-Drier**.
- [ ] **Drier replacement frequency:** Every **2–3 years** OR after any major service.
- [ ] **Acid burn-out** → discharge-line drier + suction-line drier + liquid-line drier + oil flush.
- [ ] **Acid formation:** $\text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{R-134a/PAG oil} \rightarrow \text{HF} + \text{HCl}$ (corrosive).
- [ ] **Sight glass colour (R-12):** Blue/green = dry; Yellow/pink = wet.
- [ ] **Discharge line drier is service-only**, not a permanent component.
- [ ] **Maximum heat during evacuation:** **$60^\circ\text{C}$** (protect hoses and seals).
- [ ] **Vacuum pump minimum capacity:** **3× system volume** rule (in CFM or L/min).
- [ ] **Always recover refrigerant** before opening the system — never vent to atmosphere (environmental law).
- [ ] **EV/HEV A/C:** stricter dehydration limits; special low-moisture oil required.
- [ ] **Boiling point derivation:** based on **Clausius–Clapeyron equation** — memorize for Part B.
- [ ] **R-1234yf compatibility:** only molecular sieve 3A; silica gel absorbs and degrades this refrigerant.

---

> **End of Module 4 — Methods of Dehydration Notes**
> *Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020) for PEAUT523 — Automotive Refrigeration and Air Conditioning.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Charging and Testing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT523 - AUTOMOTIVE REFRIGERATION AND AIR CONDITIONING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Charging and Testing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Charging** is the precisely controlled process of introducing the correct mass (or volume) of refrigerant — and in many cases the correct quantity of lubricant — into an automotive air conditioning (A/C) system to bring it to its specified operational inventory. **Testing** is the subsequent (and preceding) set of diagnostic procedures used to verify refrigerant integrity, system hermeticity, evacuation quality, and thermodynamic performance before the vehicle is returned to the customer.

In a KTU 2024 scheme context, charging and testing are treated as a **coupled engineering workflow** that links the A/C service equipment, refrigerant handling regulations (e.g., SAE J2843, SAE J1739), and thermodynamic performance verification.

## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Lungs of a Car"**
> Think of your car's A/C system as a *living organism* that needs the exact right amount of "blood" (refrigerant) to function. If you put in too little, the system starves and blows warm air; too much, and the compressor suffocates (high discharge pressure) and gets damaged. **Charging** is like the precise blood transfusion — measured in grams, not "pours." **Testing** is the post-transfusion checkup: we listen for leaks (like stethoscope), check the pulse (pressures), and measure the oxygen saturation (superheat/subcooling) to confirm the patient is healthy.

## Why This Topic Matters in Automotive HVAC

Automotive A/C systems operate under **high vibration, temperature swings (-20 °C to +80 °C ambient), and tight packaging constraints**. Therefore:

- **Undercharging** → poor cooling, compressor oil starvation, clutch cycling.
- **Overcharging** → high head pressure, condenser starvation, compressor overload, increased fuel consumption.
- **Improper testing** → undetected leaks, moisture damage, acid formation, premature compressor failure.

## Standard Metrics in Automotive A/C Servicing

- **R-134a charge quantity (typical sedan):** **600 g to 900 g** (under-hood label dictates exact value).
- **R-1234yf charge quantity (newer vehicles):** **400 g to 600 g** (lower due to GWP regulations).
- **Service port high-side pressure (idle, 35 °C ambient):** **1.4 MPa to 1.7 MPa**.
- **Service port low-side pressure (idle, 35 °C ambient):** **0.15 MPa to 0.25 MPa**.
- **Target evaporator outlet superheat:** **3 °C to 8 °C** (TXV systems).
- **Target liquid line subcooling:** **5 °C to 10 °C** (fixed orifice and TXV systems).
- **PAG oil quantity (post-compressor replacement):** typically **80 mL to 180 mL** (OEM specific).
- **Required evacuation vacuum:** below **500 µm Hg (0.067 kPa)** held for **15–30 minutes**.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Board Tip:** Examiners often award 2 marks simply for stating the *correct charging method* matched to the *correct system type* (TXV vs. fixed orifice). Memorize the four canonical methods.

## Section Map of the Workflow

1. **Pre-charging checks** → system inspection, oil check, leak search.
2. **Recovery** → existing refrigerant must be recovered (legal & environmental).
3. **Evacuation** → remove air and moisture via deep vacuum.
4. **Charging** → introduce correct refrigerant mass via the right method.
5. **Leak testing** → confirm hermeticity.
6. **Performance testing** → confirm thermodynamic correctness.
7. **Documentation & labeling** → update under-hood label and service log.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Canonical Charging Methods

### Method 1 — Charging by Weight (Bench Standard / OEM Specified)

- **Procedure:** Weigh the charging cylinder (or use an electronic refrigerant scale) on a calibrated scale. Zero the scale (tare). Open the cylinder valve and meter refrigerant into the system until the prescribed mass is delivered.
- **Accuracy:** ±5 g — this is the **most accurate method** and is the **OEM-specified** method.
- **When used:** Required when the A/C system has been fully evacuated (e.g., component replacement, new vehicle build, or full discharge).
- **Limitation:** Requires disconnecting the charging hose or using a scale on the charging cylinder; cannot easily top-up a running system.

### Method 2 — Charging by Pressure (Older / Field Method)

- **Procedure:** With the engine OFF and ambient at ~25 °C, introduce refrigerant vapor into the low side until the **static low-side pressure equals the saturation pressure corresponding to the ambient temperature** (per the refrigerant's P–T chart). For R-134a, ambient 25 °C → static low-side ~0.65 MPa.
- **Theory basis:** At static (no flow) conditions, the low side equilibrates to the saturated vapor pressure at the cylinder/ambient temperature.
- **When used:** Legacy field service and rough top-offs.
- **Limitation:** Inaccurate when ambient is far from 25 °C; doesn't account for system oil charge.

### Method 3 — Charging by Superheat (TXV / Thermostatic Expansion Valve Systems)

- **Procedure:** With engine running at 1500 rpm, A/C on MAX, blower on high, doors open:
  1. Measure **evaporator outlet (suction line) temperature** with a clamp thermocouple.
  2. Read **low-side pressure** from manifold gauge → convert to **saturated evaporator temperature** $T_{sat,evap}$ using a P–T chart.
  3. Compute $\text{Superheat} = T_{suction\,outlet} - T_{sat,evap}$.
  4. Compare to target: **3 °C to 8 °C** for automotive TXV.
  5. Add vapor refrigerant in small bursts (50 g) if superheat is too high (undercharge) or recover refrigerant if superheat is too low (overcharge).
- **Engineering rationale:** Superheat is the *direct thermodynamic indicator* of the refrigerant's state entering the compressor. It protects the compressor from liquid slugging.

### Method 4 — Charging by Subcooling (Fixed Orifice / CCOT Systems)

- **Procedure:** With system stabilized:
  1. Measure **liquid line temperature** at condenser outlet.
  2. Read **high-side pressure** → convert to **saturated condensing temperature** $T_{sat,cond}$.
  3. Compute $\text{Subcooling} = T_{sat,cond} - T_{liquid\,line}$.
  4. Target: **5 °C to 10 °C**.
- **Engineering rationale:** Subcooling quantifies how much liquid refrigerant is "guaranteed" at the expansion device inlet — it prevents flash-gas formation in the liquid line, which would starve the evaporator.

### Method 5 — Charging by Sight Glass (Older CCOT Systems with Sight Glass)

- **Procedure:** With system running, observe the sight glass on the high-side liquid line. **Clear flow → no bubbles → adequate charge**; persistent bubbles → undercharge; flashing immediately after receiver-drier → restriction or undercharge.
- **Limitation:** Many modern systems have eliminated the sight glass.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Quick Discrimination Rule:** If the question mentions a **TXV or block-type expansion valve → answer "charging by superheat."** If it mentions a **fixed orifice tube (FOT) or CCOT (Cycling Clutch Orifice Tube) → answer "charging by subcooling."**

## 2.2 The Testing Hierarchy

### Test 1 — Visual & Tactile Inspection
- Inspect hoses, fittings, condenser fins, compressor clutch, drive belt.
- Look for oil streaks (sign of refrigerant leak because oil travels with refrigerant).

### Test 2 — Leak Testing
Performed **after charging, before final delivery**, or during evacuation with trace gas.

| Leak Test Method | Detection Limit | Best Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Soap bubble solution** | ~28 g/yr | Quick spot check at fittings |
| **Electronic (diode) leak detector** | ~7 g/yr | R-134a, R-1234yf service |
| **Heated diode / infrared** | ~3 g/yr | Modern, high sensitivity |
| **Ultrasonic** | ~14 g/yr | Noisy environment, all refrigerants |
| **Fluorescent dye (UV)** | ~7 g/yr | Slow leaks, dye stays in oil |
| **Nitrogen pressure decay** | < 14 g/yr | Evacuated systems, before charging |
| **Mass spectrometer (helium sniff)** | < 1 g/yr | R&D, mass-production leak test |

### Test 3 — Vacuum / Evacuation Test
- Pull system to < 500 µm Hg.
- **Hold for 15–30 minutes** with the pump isolated (valves closed).
- A rising vacuum reading (pressure increasing) indicates either: (a) moisture boiling off, (b) leak, or (c) trapped air pocket in a high spot.
- **Pass criterion:** pressure rise < 50 µm Hg over 15 min hold.

### Test 4 — Performance / Operating Test
- Measure high-side and low-side pressures at idle (1000 rpm) and 1500 rpm with A/C on MAX.
- Compare to OEM pressure chart.
- Measure center vent air temperature → typical target **5 °C to 10 °C** with 35 °C ambient.

### Test 5 — Electronic System Test
- Scan via OBD-II for A/C-related DTCs (e.g., P0530 — A/C refrigerant pressure sensor circuit).
- Verify compressor clutch engagement signal.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains all high-yield formulas for charging & testing numerical problems. Pipes are rendered as $\vert$ or $\mid$ to keep markdown table integrity.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Superheat | $\text{SH} = T_{evap,out} - T_{sat}(P_{evap})$ | °C or K | $T_{sat}$ from P–T chart at low-side pressure |
| 2 | Subcooling | $\text{SC} = T_{sat}(P_{cond}) - T_{liq,out}$ | °C or K | $T_{sat}$ from P–T chart at high-side pressure |
| 3 | Pressure-Temperature (Clausius–Clapeyron form, ideal) | $\ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = \dfrac{h_{fg}}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2}\right)$ | Pa, J/kg, J/(kg·K) | Approximate for refrigerant vapor |
| 4 | Refrigerant Mass (ideal gas) | $m = \dfrac{P\,V}{R_{sp}\,T}$ | kg | $R_{sp}$ = specific gas constant; useful for tank sizing |
| 5 | Total System Charge (mass balance) | $m_{sys} = m_{cond} + m_{evap} + m_{lines} + m_{receiver}$ | kg | OEM publishes $m_{sys}$ on under-hood label |
| 6 | Compressor Oil Circulation Rate (empirical) | $\dot{m}_{oil} \approx 0.02 \cdot \dot{m}_{ref}$ | kg/s | ~2 % of refrigerant mass flow |
| 7 | Pressure Decay Leak Test | $\Delta P_{leak} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{leak}\,R_{sp}\,T}{V}$ | Pa | Used in nitrogen decay test |
| 8 | Vacuum Hold Test Rise Rate | $\dfrac{dP}{dt} < 3 \text{ µm Hg/min}$ | µm Hg/min | Pass criterion over 15-min isolation |
| 9 | Centigrade to Kelvin | $T(K) = T(°C) + 273.15$ | K | Mandatory before ideal-gas calcs |
| 10 | Manifold Pressure Conversion | $1 \text{ atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa} = 14.7 \text{ psia}$ | — | Useful for unit-mix problems |
| 11 | Coefficient of Performance (used in perf. test) | $\text{COP} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{evap}}{\dot{W}_{comp}}$ | — | Lower bound for A/C test |
| 12 | Air-side cooling capacity (sensible) | $\dot{Q}_{evap} = \dot{m}_{air}\,c_{p,air}\,\Delta T_{air}$ | W | Used in air-temp-drop performance test |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **OEM service manuals** mandate specific charging procedures for warranty compliance.
- **SAE J2843** (R-1234yf recovery/recycling equipment) governs the legal workflow that the *charging and testing* procedure must follow.
- **EV thermal management systems** use the same charging/testing principles for the refrigerant loop, but with R-1234yf or R-744 (CO₂).
- **Refrigerant leak rates** are used as a regulatory metric in EU F-Gas Regulation and in India's MoEFCC norms.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Superheat and Subcooling from Measured Quantities

We begin from the **definition of superheat** at the evaporator outlet and apply the **Clausius–Clapeyron** relation between saturation pressure and saturation temperature.

### 3.1.1 Saturation Temperature from Pressure (P–T Relation)

For a refrigerant at saturation, the Antoine-type empirical form (or exact tables) gives $T_{sat}$ as a function of $P_{sat}$. The thermodynamic starting point is the **Clausius–Clapeyron equation** (with assumptions: ideal-gas vapor, $h_{fg}$ constant):

$$
\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{h_{fg}}{T \cdot v_{fg}}
$$

Substituting $v_{fg} \approx v_g = R_{sp}\,T / P$ (ideal gas) and integrating between states $(T_1, P_1)$ and $(T_2, P_2)$:

$$
\int_{P_1}^{P_2} \frac{dP}{P} = \frac{h_{fg}}{R_{sp}} \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \frac{dT}{T^2}
$$

Performing the integrals:

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = -\frac{h_{fg}}{R_{sp}}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)
$$

Rearranging (note the sign flip for clarity):

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = \frac{h_{fg}}{R_{sp}}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)
$$

This is **Equation 3** in the KTU Formula Sheet. For narrow temperature ranges it works well for R-134a and R-1234yf.

### 3.1.2 Superheat Calculation

**Step 1.** Measure low-side gauge pressure $P_L$ (e.g., 0.20 MPa = 200 kPa gauge). Convert to absolute: $P_{evap} = P_L + P_{atm}$.

**Step 2.** Use a P–T chart (or the Antoine equation) to read the saturation temperature at $P_{evap}$. For R-134a at 0.20 MPa gauge (~301 kPa absolute), $T_{sat} \approx 4 \,°C$.

**Step 3.** Measure the actual suction line (evaporator outlet) temperature $T_{evap,out}$ with a clamp thermocouple, e.g., $T_{evap,out} = 9 \,°C$.

**Step 4.** Compute:

$$
\text{SH} = T_{evap,out} - T_{sat,evap} = 9 - 4 = 5 \,°C
$$

**Step 5.** Compare to target band (3 °C to 8 °C). 5 °C is within spec → **charge is correct**.

### 3.1.3 Subcooling Calculation

**Step 1.** Measure high-side gauge pressure $P_H$ (e.g., 1.50 MPa gauge = 1.6013 MPa absolute). 

**Step 2.** Read $T_{sat,cond}$ at $P_{cond}$ from P–T chart. For R-134a at 1.60 MPa abs, $T_{sat,cond} \approx 49 \,°C$.

**Step 3.** Measure the liquid line outlet (condenser-to-TXV/FOT) temperature $T_{liq,out}$ with thermocouple, e.g., $T_{liq,out} = 42 \,°C$.

**Step 4.** Compute:

$$
\text{SC} = T_{sat,cond} - T_{liq,out} = 49 - 42 = 7 \,°C
$$

**Step 5.** Compare to target (5 °C to 10 °C). 7 °C is within spec → **charge is correct**.

## 3.2 Derivation — Refrigerant Mass in a System (Mass Balance)

The total charge of a sealed A/C system is the sum of refrigerant in all internal volumes:

$$
m_{sys} = \sum_{i} \rho_i \, V_i
$$

where for each component $i$ we have a characteristic density $\rho_i$ and internal refrigerant-bearing volume $V_i$. For an automotive system:

$$
m_{sys} = m_{compressor} + m_{condenser} + m_{evaporator} + m_{receiver\text{-}drier} + m_{hoses} + m_{condenser\ fan\ shroud}
$$

Since direct volume measurement is impractical, OEMs publish $m_{sys}$ on the **under-hood label** after empirical testing. For a 1.5 L sedan with R-134a, typical published values are 600 g to 750 g.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Numerical Pattern:** A common exam question gives you component volumes and asks for total charge assuming known density of liquid refrigerant in condenser/receiver and known density of vapor in suction line. Use $\rho_{liquid} \approx 1200 \text{ kg/m}^3$ and $\rho_{vapor} \approx 5 \text{ kg/m}^3$ for R-134a at typical in-system conditions.

## 3.3 Derivation — Pressure-Decay Leak Test (Nitrogen Method)

The **nitrogen pressure decay test** is performed before refrigerant charging to verify hermeticity of an empty system.

**Step 1.** Pressurize the system with **OFN (Oxygen-Free Nitrogen)** to a test pressure, typically **1.5 MPa** for high-side components and **1.0 MPa** for low-side, held for several minutes.

**Step 2.** Isolate the nitrogen source and let the system stabilize thermally.

**Step 3.** Record the initial pressure $P_1$ and the temperature $T_1$. Wait for a dwell time $t_{dwell}$ (typically 10–30 min). Record final pressure $P_2$.

**Step 4.** Correct the final pressure for any temperature change (because $P \propto T$ for an ideal gas at constant volume):

$$
P_{2,corrected} = P_2 \cdot \frac{T_1}{T_2}
$$

**Step 5.** Compute the **pressure drop**:

$$
\Delta P = P_1 - P_{2,corrected}
$$

**Step 6.** If $\Delta P < 1\%$ of $P_1$ over the dwell, the system passes.

For a more physical estimate of the equivalent leak rate, treat the leaked nitrogen at the system mean pressure and temperature as an ideal gas escaping through a small orifice. The mass leaked is:

$$
m_{leak} = \frac{(P_1 - P_2)\,V}{R_{sp,N_2}\,T_{mean}}
$$

with $R_{sp,N_2} = 296.8 \text{ J/(kg·K)}$ and $T_{mean}$ in Kelvin.

**Step 7.** Convert to leak rate:

$$
\dot{m}_{leak} = \frac{m_{leak}}{t_{dwell}}
$$

A typical pass criterion for automotive A/C is $\dot{m}_{leak} < 5 \text{ g/yr}$ when scaled to refrigerant-equivalent leak rate.

## 3.4 Worked Example — Combined Charging Verification

> **Problem (Typical KTU 2-mark sub-part):**
> An automotive A/C system with R-134a is being tested. The manifold gauges read: low side = 0.20 MPa (gauge), high side = 1.50 MPa (gauge). The suction line temperature is 9 °C and the liquid line temperature is 42 °C. Atmospheric pressure is 0.1013 MPa. Determine whether the system is correctly charged for both a TXV and a CCOT configuration.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Convert gauge to absolute:

$$
P_{evap} = 0.20 + 0.1013 = 0.3013 \text{ MPa}
$$

$$
P_{cond} = 1.50 + 0.1013 = 1.6013 \text{ MPa}
$$

Step 2 — Read saturation temperatures from R-134a P–T chart:

$$
T_{sat,evap} \approx 4 \,°C \quad \text{at } 0.3013 \text{ MPa abs}
$$

$$
T_{sat,cond} \approx 49 \,°C \quad \text{at } 1.6013 \text{ MPa abs}
$$

Step 3 — Compute superheat (relevant for TXV):

$$
\text{SH} = T_{evap,out} - T_{sat,evap} = 9 - 4 = 5 \,°C
$$

Since $3 \le 5 \le 8$, **TXV system: correctly charged** ✓

Step 4 — Compute subcooling (relevant for CCOT/FOT):

$$
\text{SC} = T_{sat,cond} - T_{liq,out} = 49 - 42 = 7 \,°C
$$

Since $5 \le 7 \le 10$, **CCOT/FOT system: correctly charged** ✓

Step 5 — Comment on vent air temperature (assuming ambient 35 °C, blower high, center vent): typically 5–10 °C. If measured at this point, the *thermodynamic correctness* of charging is fully validated.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Charging Method vs. System Type

| Expansion Device | Recommended Charging Method | Verification Parameter | Target Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| **TXV (Thermostatic Expansion Valve)** | Charging by superheat | SH | 3 – 8 °C |
| **Fixed Orifice Tube (FOT/CCOT)** | Charging by subcooling | SC | 5 – 10 °C |
| **Automatic Expansion Valve (AXV)** | Charging by static pressure | Low-side $P$ | matches ambient $T_{sat}$ |
| **Electronic Expansion Valve (EXV)** | OEM procedure + scan tool | SH + sensor feedback | OEM spec |
| **H-block (legacy)** | Charging by weight | $m_{sys}$ | OEM label |
| **Evaporator-mounted TXV w/ POA** | Charging by superheat | SH (poa-side) | 5 – 10 °C |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall — Don't confuse "superheat" with "subcooling":**
> Superheat is measured on the **low side (suction line)** and is the temperature of vapor *above* its saturation point.
> Subcooling is measured on the **high side (liquid line)** and is the temperature of liquid *below* its saturation point.
> Students frequently swap them. The mnemonic: **S**uperheat → **S**uction; **S**ubcooling → **S**aturated liquid. Both have an **S**.

    D3 --> D4[Step 10: Measure Suction Line Temperature and Pressure]

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following questions are modeled on the KTU 2024 End-Semester Evaluation (ESE) pattern. All marks (3-mark and 14-mark) follow the KTU ESE marking scheme. Model answers are step-by-step, and examiner pitfalls are flagged at the end.

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, PEAUT523, Module 4]
**"Briefly explain the method of charging an automotive A/C system by superheat. State the target superheat range."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Charging by superheat is used for systems fitted with a **Thermostatic Expansion Valve (TXV)**. The procedure is:

1. With the engine running at 1500 rpm, A/C in MAX mode, blower at high, and doors open, **measure the suction line temperature** at the evaporator outlet using a clamp thermocouple — let it be $T_{evap,out}$.
2. **Read the low-side pressure** from the manifold gauge and convert it to the **saturated evaporator temperature** $T_{sat,evap}$ using the refrigerant's pressure–temperature chart.
3. Compute $\text{Superheat} = T_{evap,out} - T_{sat,evap}$.
4. **Target range for automotive TXV systems is 3 °C to 8 °C.**
5. If superheat is too high (undercharged), add refrigerant vapor in small bursts; if too low (overcharged), recover refrigerant.

A superheat within 3–8 °C ensures the compressor receives only vapor (preventing liquid slugging) and that the evaporator is fully utilized. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2 — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEAUT523, Module 4]
**"List any four methods of leak testing used in automotive A/C servicing and state the best use case for each."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | Method | Best Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Soap bubble solution** | Quick spot-check at threaded fittings and accessible joints. |
| 2 | **Electronic diode leak detector** | Field service for R-134a and R-1234yf; detects leaks down to ~7 g/yr. |
| 3 | **Nitrogen pressure decay test** | Pre-charging verification of an empty system before refrigerant is introduced. |
| 4 | **Fluorescent dye + UV light** | Slow, persistent leaks; dye remains trapped in the lubricating oil. |

(Any four valid methods with appropriate use cases earn 3 marks.)

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Rule:** Each Part B question provides INTERNAL CHOICE. The student must answer EITHER **Question A** OR **Question B**. Both options below are independent and complete.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** Describe in detail the **complete step-by-step procedure** for charging an automotive A/C system after a compressor replacement, including evacuation, leak testing, oil charging, refrigerant charging, and performance verification. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A technician records the following measurements on a running R-134a automotive A/C system with a TXV:
- Low-side gauge pressure: **0.20 MPa (gauge)**
- High-side gauge pressure: **1.50 MPa (gauge)**
- Suction line (evap. outlet) temperature: **9 °C**
- Liquid line (cond. outlet) temperature: **42 °C**
- Atmospheric pressure: **0.1013 MPa**

Calculate the **superheat and subcooling** and comment on whether the system is correctly charged. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

The complete charging procedure after a compressor replacement is:

1. **Recover old refrigerant** (if any) using a recovery machine to comply with environmental regulations. (½ mark)
2. **Replace the receiver-drier** and any liquid-line filter, because moisture contamination is assumed. (½ mark)
3. **Charge the new compressor with PAG oil** as per OEM specification (typically 80–180 mL for an automotive scroll compressor). Pour oil through the suction port. (½ mark)
4. **Replace the line O-rings** at all fitting connections; lubricate with PAG oil and torque to OEM specification. (½ mark)
5. **Connect the manifold gauge set** to the high and low service ports. (½ mark)
6. **Connect the vacuum pump** to the center hose. Open both manifold valves. (½ mark)
7. **Evacuate the system** to below **500 µm Hg** (0.067 kPa). Operate the vacuum pump for at least **30 minutes** to remove air and boil off moisture. (1 mark)
8. **Vacuum hold test**: Close manifold valves, isolate the pump, and monitor the system pressure. The rise should be less than **50 µm Hg in 15 min**. If higher → leak search. (½ mark)
9. **Break vacuum with refrigerant vapor** by opening the high-side valve briefly to introduce ~50 g of vapor, then close. (½ mark)
10. **Charge by weight** using an electronic scale and charging cylinder. Add the OEM-specified mass (e.g., 700 g for a typical sedan). (½ mark)
11. **Leak test** all connections with an electronic leak detector. (½ mark)
12. **Performance test**: Start engine, set 1500 rpm, A/C MAX, doors open, blower high. After 5 min, record high and low-side pressures and center vent temperature. Compare to OEM spec. (½ mark)
13. **Document** the charge quantity and update the under-hood label if necessary. (½ mark)

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

Step 1 — Convert gauge pressures to absolute:

$$
P_{evap} = 0.20 + 0.1013 = 0.3013 \text{ MPa abs}
$$

$$
P_{cond} = 1.50 + 0.1013 = 1.6013 \text{ MPa abs}
$$

Step 2 — From R-134a P–T chart:

$$
T_{sat,evap} \approx 4 \,°C \quad \text{at } 0.3013 \text{ MPa}
$$

$$
T_{sat,cond} \approx 49 \,°C \quad \text{at } 1.6013 \text{ MPa}
$$

Step 3 — Superheat:

$$
\text{SH} = T_{evap,out} - T_{sat,evap} = 9 - 4 = 5 \,°C
$$

Step 4 — Subcooling:

$$
\text{SC} = T_{sat,cond} - T_{liq,out} = 49 - 42 = 7 \,°C
$$

Step 5 — Comment: Both superheat (5 °C ∈ [3, 8] °C) and subcooling (7 °C ∈ [5, 10] °C) are within their target ranges. **The system is correctly charged for both TXV and CCOT configurations.** However, since this system has a TXV, the controlling parameter is superheat, which is in spec. **(2 marks for the comment)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Compare the **five canonical methods of charging** an automotive A/C system. State the principle, accuracy, and the system type for which each method is best suited. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **nitrogen pressure decay leak test** in detail. A 5 L A/C component is pressurized to 1.5 MPa absolute with nitrogen at 25 °C. After a 30-minute hold, the pressure drops to 1.495 MPa absolute and the temperature is 24 °C. Calculate the equivalent mass of nitrogen leaked and decide if the system passes a typical 5 g/yr leak rate criterion. Assume $R_{sp,N_2} = 296.8 \text{ J/(kg·K)}$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

(Presented as a comparison table — 1 mark for each method up to 5 marks + 2 marks for summary comment)

| # | Method | Principle | Accuracy | Best Suited For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **By Weight** | Mass delivered measured by electronic scale; matches OEM label value | ±5 g, highest | Full evacuation, new builds, warranty service |
| 2 | **By Pressure** | Static low-side P equals $P_{sat}$ at ambient | ±50 g, low | Legacy field service, AXV systems |
| 3 | **By Superheat** | $\text{SH} = T_{evap,out} - T_{sat,evap}$ set to 3–8 °C | ±25 g, medium-high | **TXV systems** |
| 4 | **By Subcooling** | $\text{SC} = T_{sat,cond} - T_{liq,out}$ set to 5–10 °C | ±25 g, medium-high | **FOT/CCOT systems** |
| 5 | **By Sight Glass** | Visual: clear flow ⇒ adequate charge | Poor, qualitative | Older CCOT systems with sight glass |

**Comment (2 marks):** Method 1 is the bench standard; methods 3 and 4 are the KTU-2024 syllabus focus because they reflect *thermodynamic correctness*, not just inventory mass. Method 2 is obsolete in modern service practice. Method 5 is rarely encountered on post-2010 vehicles.

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

Step 1 — Convert temperatures to Kelvin:

$$
T_1 = 25 + 273.15 = 298.15 \text{ K}
$$

$$
T_2 = 24 + 273.15 = 297.15 \text{ K}
$$

Step 2 — Temperature-correct the final pressure to the initial temperature (ideal-gas law at constant V):

$$
P_{2,corrected} = P_2 \cdot \frac{T_1}{T_2} = 1.495 \cdot \frac{298.15}{297.15} = 1.4998 \text{ MPa}
$$

Step 3 — Compute the corrected pressure drop:

$$
\Delta P = P_1 - P_{2,corrected} = 1.500 - 1.4998 = 0.0002 \text{ MPa} = 200 \text{ Pa}
$$

Step 4 — Apply the ideal-gas law to find the leaked mass:

$$
m_{leak} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot V}{R_{sp,N_2} \cdot T_{mean}}
$$

with $T_{mean} = (298.15 + 297.15)/2 = 297.65 \text{ K}$ and $V = 5 \text{ L} = 0.005 \text{ m}^3$:

$$
m_{leak} = \frac{200 \cdot 0.005}{296.8 \cdot 297.65} = \frac{1.0}{88348.0} = 1.132 \times 10^{-5} \text{ kg} = 11.3 \text{ mg}
$$

Step 5 — Convert to per-year leak rate:

$$
\dot{m}_{leak} = \frac{11.3 \text{ mg}}{0.5 \text{ h}} = 22.6 \text{ mg/h} = 22.6 \cdot 8760 \text{ mg/yr} = 198 \text{ g/yr (N}_2\text{)}
$$

Step 6 — Decision: 198 g/yr of nitrogen does **not** directly correspond to refrigerant leak rate, but the test is a *relative* hermeticity check. With only a 0.013 % pressure drop in 30 min, the system is exceptionally tight. **Passes the pressure-decay test (≤ 1 % drop criterion).** (1 mark for the conclusion)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Module 4 — Charging & Testing):**
> 1. **Confusing superheat and subcooling zones** — Superheat is always measured on the suction (low) side; subcooling on the liquid (high) side. Swapping them in a numerical problem will cost you all 7 marks in that sub-part.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert gauge → absolute** — Pressure in thermodynamic equations must be absolute. The 0.1013 MPa atmospheric correction is a common 1-mark trap.
> 3. **Not converting °C to K** — Any ideal-gas calculation (e.g., nitrogen leak test, refrigerant mass) requires Kelvin. Forgetting this loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Recommending charging by superheat for a CCOT system** — Always match the method to the expansion device type. This is a "free 2 marks" discriminator on the KTU 2024 paper.
> 5. **Skipping the vacuum hold test** — Many students jump from evacuation to charging. The hold test is a mandatory step; omitting it costs ½ to 1 mark.
> 6. **In Part B (a) compressor-replacement procedure, forgetting PAG oil charge** — Compressor oil is part of the charging workflow and examiners specifically look for it.
> 7. **Omitting the leak test step before performance test** — KTU 2024 follows SAE J2843 / J1739 sequence: charge → leak-test → performance-test. Reverse order = mark deduction.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-density rapid-revision checklist (re-read this section twice before entering the exam hall):**

- ☐ **Charging by Weight** = bench/OEM standard; ±5 g accuracy; use an electronic scale and tare the cylinder.
- ☐ **Charging by Pressure** = legacy/AXV; static low-side $P$ should match $P_{sat}$ at ambient.
- ☐ **Charging by Superheat** = for **TXV** systems; target **3–8 °C**; $\text{SH} = T_{evap,out} - T_{sat,evap}$.
- ☐ **Charging by Subcooling** = for **FOT/CCOT** systems; target **5–10 °C**; $\text{SC} = T_{sat,cond} - T_{liq,out}$.
- ☐ **Charging by Sight Glass** = older CCOT; clear flow ⇒ adequate; bubbles ⇒ undercharge; use only as a secondary indicator.
- ☐ **Workflow order** (memorize this sequence):
  1. Visual inspection → 2. Recover refrigerant → 3. Replace receiver-drier (if opened) → 4. Connect manifold → 5. Evacuate to < 500 µm Hg → 6. Vacuum hold test 15–30 min (rise < 50 µm Hg) → 7. Charge by weight (or appropriate method) → 8. Leak test (electronic detector) → 9. Performance test → 10. Update label.
- ☐ **Leak test methods** (in order of sensitivity): mass spec < IR/diode < electronic diode < ultrasonic < UV-dye < soap < nitrogen decay.
- ☐ **Nitrogen pressure decay test** uses **OFN (oxygen-free nitrogen)** at **1.5 MPa** high-side / **1.0 MPa** low-side, with a 10–30 min dwell and ≤ 1 % pressure drop criterion.
- ☐ **Always convert gauge to absolute** before using $P$ in ideal-gas or Clausius–Clapeyron equations. $P_{abs} = P_{gauge} + P_{atm}$.
- ☐ **Always convert °C to K** for ideal-gas calculations: $T(K) = T(°C) + 273.15$.
- ☐ **PAG oil quantity** on a new compressor: typically 80–180 mL — check the OEM label.
- ☐ **R-1234yf service equipment** must be **dedicated and labeled** — it cannot share hoses/gauges with R-134a due to differing hose permeation and refrigerant oil compatibility.
- ☐ **Superheat rule of thumb** (without a P–T chart): for R-134a, $T_{sat,evap} \approx 0.4 \cdot P_{low,bar} - 12$ (rough linear fit, °C vs. bar gauge, valid 0.5–3 bar).
- ☐ **Subcooling rule of thumb**: for R-134a, $T_{sat,cond} \approx 0.5 \cdot P_{high,bar} + 10$ (rough linear fit, °C vs. bar gauge, valid 8–20 bar).
- ☐ **Common symptom → diagnosis table** to memorize:
  - Low vent temp + low both pressures + low SC → severely **undercharged** + possible leak.
  - Low vent temp + high both pressures → **overcharged** or condenser airflow blockage.
  - Normal vent temp + low low-side P + high high-side P → **expansion valve blockage** or **receiver-drier restriction**.
  - Normal vent temp + low high-side P + low low-side P → **compressor inefficiency** (worn scroll/clutch slip).
- ☐ **Clausius–Clapeyron** form to remember: $\ln(P_2/P_1) = (h_{fg}/R_{sp})(1/T_1 - 1/T_2)$.
- ☐ **KTU 2024 syllabus keywords** that earn easy marks when included in answers: *recovery, evacuation, vacuum hold, PAG oil, leak detector, superheat, subcooling, sight glass, hermeticity, OFN, manifold gauge set*.
